topics.title: Introduction to Process Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Process Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Control** is the discipline of automatically maintaining the operating conditions of a continuous industrial process at desired optimal values by manipulating certain process inputs, in spite of external disturbances, using feedback measurement and automatic corrective action. It is a sub-discipline of **control engineering** that applies the principles of feedback, feedforward, and cascade control to industrial processes in sectors such as chemical, petrochemical, power, pharmaceutical, food processing, and manufacturing.

Formally, a **process** is any operation or series of operations in which the quantity, quality, or character of a material is changed — for example, heat transfer, mass transfer, chemical reaction, fluid flow, or mixing. A **control system** is the arrangement of physical components and algorithms that maintains the process at the desired operating point called the **set point (SP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The official KTU 2024 definition to memorize is — *"Process control is the automatic regulation of process variables such as temperature, pressure, flow, and level to their desired values using measurement, comparison, and correction through a closed loop."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Driving a Car"

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and want to maintain a constant speed of **80 km/h** despite wind gusts and small hills. Your eyes see the speedometer (the **measurement / sensor**), your brain compares it with the desired **80 km/h** (the **controller / decision maker**), and your foot presses or releases the accelerator (the **final control element / actuator**). This continuous chain of *see → compare → act* is exactly what a process control system does inside a chemical plant. If the wind pushes the car faster, the controller "pulls back"; if a hill slows it down, the controller "pushes harder". The car's speed is the **process variable**, the desired 80 km/h is the **set point**, and the accelerator pedal position is the **manipulated variable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Process control is simply *"automatic decision making based on measurement"*. Anything that can be measured can — in principle — be controlled.

### 1.3 Process Variables — The Four Key Quantities

| Variable | Symbol | Role in the Loop | Standard Unit Examples |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Process Variable | **PV** (or *y*, *x*) | The quantity we want to control | °C, kPa, m³/h, % |
| Set Point | **SP** (or *r*) | The desired value of the PV | Same as PV |
| Manipulated Variable | **MV** (or *u*, *m*) | The input we change to affect the PV | Valve opening %, V, A |
| Controlled Variable | **CV** | Synonym for PV in the output | Same as PV |
| Disturbance Variable | **DV** (or *d*, *z*) | Unwanted input that pushes PV away from SP | Ambient °C, feed composition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction to Memorize:** The *Controlled Variable (CV)* and *Process Variable (PV)* refer to the same physical quantity — the output of the process. KTU examiners frequently ask the difference between MV and DV; remember: the MV is what **we** change, the DV is what **nature** changes.

### 1.4 Significance of Process Control in Modern Industry

- **Safety** — prevents runaway reactions, over-pressurization, and thermal runaway.
- **Product Quality** — ensures batch-to-batch consistency (critical in pharma and semiconductors).
- **Energy Efficiency** — reduces wastage of steam, fuel, and electricity.
- **Environmental Compliance** — keeps stack emissions and effluent within legal limits.
- **Profitability** — operating closer to the optimum constraint directly improves yield.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Closed-Loop Control System

Every automatic control system — no matter how complex — is built from the same four functional blocks connected in a loop:

1. **Measurement / Sensor (Transmitter)** — converts the process variable into a standardized signal (commonly **4–20 mA**, 0–10 V, 3–15 psi pneumatic, or a digital fieldbus value such as HART or Foundation Fieldbus).
2. **Controller (Comparator + Decision Element)** — subtracts the measured PV from the SP to compute the **error signal** $e(t)$, then applies a control law (P, PI, PD, PID) to produce a controller output.
3. **Final Control Element (Actuator + Valve / Drive)** — typically a **control valve** that varies flow, a **variable-speed drive (VSD)** on a pump motor, or a **heater power controller**.
4. **Process (Plant)** — the physical operation itself (reactor, heat exchanger, distillation column, furnace, mixer, etc.).

The **error signal** at the heart of the controller is defined as:

$$e(t) = \text{SP}(t) - \text{PV}(t)$$

### 2.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control

| Property | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback path | **Absent** | **Present** (PV fed back to controller) |
| Accuracy under disturbance | Poor — cannot self-correct | Good — automatically compensates |
| Stability | Always stable (no dynamics fed back) | Can become unstable if not designed |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher (extra sensor, wiring, controller) |
| Example | Toaster, washing-machine timer, traffic light | Cruise control, room thermostat, DCS loop |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** *"Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with two examples each."* — Always give **at least one industrial example** such as a level-control loop (closed) and a fixed-speed conveyor (open).

### 2.3 Classification of Control Actions

Based on the relationship between the set point and the measured variable, control loops fall into two broad classes:

- **Servo Control (Tracking Control)** — the SP changes frequently and the PV must follow it. Example: an *antenna positioning system*, a *robot arm*, a *rolling-mill screw-down*.
- **Regulatory Control (Maintain Control)** — the SP is essentially constant; the goal is to reject disturbances. Example: a *reactor temperature loop*, a *boiler drum level loop*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students answer "servo = servo motor" in exams. *Servo control* actually means **set-point tracking**, not the servo motor hardware. Memorize this carefully.

### 2.4 Hierarchical Levels of Industrial Automation (ISA-95 / Purdue Model)

Industrial process control is organized in clear layers. KTU 2024 expects you to know the **four/five-layer model**:

| Level | Name | Typical Devices | Time Scale |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L0** | Process | Sensors, valves, pumps, motors | Seconds |
| **L1** | Basic Control | PLC, DCS controller, RTU, single-loop controller | Seconds–Minutes |
| **L2** | Supervisory Control | SCADA, HMI, Operator Station | Minutes |
| **L3** | Operations Management | MES, historians, batch recipes | Hours |
| **L4** | Enterprise / ERP | Business planning, scheduling | Days–Weeks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This is the reference model used in every modern refinery, paper mill, and smart-factory design. Companies like Honeywell, Yokogawa, Siemens, Emerson, and ABB design products targeted at one of these specific levels.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Same units as PV |
| 2 | Deviation from set point | $\Delta y = y - r$ | Often used in tuning |
| 3 | Process gain definition | $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta u}\bigg\vert_{\text{steady state}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | Percent of set point | $\% \text{SP} = \dfrac{y}{r} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Percent of measurement range | $\% \text{PV} = \dfrac{y - y_{\min}}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | Valve flow relation (linear) | $q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho}}$ | m³/h |
| 7 | Sensor transfer (4–20 mA) | $y = y_{\min} + \dfrac{I - 4}{16}(y_{\max} - y_{\min})$ | mA |
| 8 | Control error squared (ISE) | $J = \int_0^{\infty} e^2(t)\, dt$ | Used in tuning |
| 9 | Steady-state error (unity FB) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | For step input |
| 10 | Offset in P-control | $\text{Offset} = \dfrac{r}{1+K_p K_c}$ | Shows why integral action is needed |

> **Convention note:** Throughout KTU 2024 problem statements, $r(t)$ denotes set point, $y(t)$ denotes measured PV, $u(t)$ denotes controller output / MV, and $d(t)$ denotes disturbance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Block-Diagram Transfer Function

The most common KTU derivation question asks you to **derive the closed-loop transfer function** for a single-loop unity-feedback control system and explain how it differs from the open-loop transfer function.

Let us define each block in the **forward path** and **feedback path**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p(s) &= \text{Process transfer function} \\
G_c(s) &= \text{Controller transfer function} \\
G_v(s) &= \text{Valve / actuator transfer function} \\
H(s) &= \text{Sensor / transmitter transfer function} \\
G_d(s) &= \text{Disturbance transfer function}
\end{aligned}
$$

The forward-path transfer function is the cascade (product) of the controller, the valve, and the process:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(s) &= G_c(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

The closed-loop transfer function relating the **output** $Y(s)$ to the **set-point** $R(s)$ (with disturbance $D(s) = 0$) is given by the classical feedback formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} &= \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the equation at the summing junction (comparator):**

The error $E(s)$ is the difference between the set-point signal and the feedback signal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(s) &= R(s) - H(s) Y(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the forward-path relationship:**

The controller output drives the valve, which drives the process, producing the output $Y(s)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(s) &= G(s)\, E(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(s) &= G(s) \big[\, R(s) - H(s) Y(s) \,\big] \\
Y(s) &= G(s)\,R(s) - G(s) H(s)\, Y(s) \\
Y(s) + G(s) H(s)\, Y(s) &= G(s)\,R(s) \\
Y(s)\big[\, 1 + G(s) H(s) \,\big] &= G(s)\,R(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the ratio $Y(s)/R(s)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} &= \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is the **return difference**. When $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \gg 1$ at low frequencies, the closed-loop transfer function tends to $1/H(s)$, meaning the system faithfully follows the set-point scaled by the sensor. This is the classical engineering reason why high loop gain gives good tracking and disturbance rejection.

### 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Error for a Step Set-Point

For a **unity-feedback** $H(s)=1$ system with a step set-point of magnitude $R_0$, the steady-state error is obtained using the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) \\
E(s) &= \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} \\
R(s) &= \dfrac{R_0}{s} \quad \text{(step of magnitude } R_0 \text{)} \\
e_{ss} &= \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{R_0 / s}{1 + G(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{R_0}{1 + G(s)} = \dfrac{R_0}{1 + G(0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $G(0) = K$ (a Type-0 plant with finite DC gain $K$), the steady-state error is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \dfrac{R_0}{1 + K}
\end{aligned}
$$

This non-zero error is called **offset**. The only way to make $e_{ss} = 0$ for a step input is to ensure $G(0) \to \infty$, which is precisely what an **integral (I) action** in the controller achieves.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Control System Types

| Feature | Manual Control | Automatic Open-Loop | Automatic Closed-Loop |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operator involvement | Continuous | None after start | Minimal supervision |
| Feedback | Human eyes | None | Instrument-based |
| Accuracy | Operator dependent | Process dependent | Very high |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Highest |
| Disturbance rejection | Slow, manual | None | Fast, automatic |
| Example | Stirring by hand | Timer-based irrigation | Thermostat, boiler control |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic Process Control Loop

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical block-level functional architecture of a single-loop process control system, showing all standard signals (set point, error, manipulated variable, process variable, disturbance).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Set Point SP] --> SUMCOMP{Summing Junction}
    B[Process Variable PV feedback] --> SUMCOMP
    SUMCOMP -- Error e t --> C[Controller Gc s]
    C -- Controller Output --> D[Final Control Element Gv s]
    D -- Manipulated Variable MV u t --> E[Process Gp s]
    EXT[Disturbance d t] --> E
    E -- Output y t --> B
    E --> F[Sensor Transmitter H s]
    F --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows indicate the *direction of signal flow*, not physical piping. The feedback arrow from the process output back to the summing junction is what makes this a *closed loop*. The disturbance enters the process block from outside — it cannot be measured or changed by us.

### 4.2 ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model Block Diagram

The hierarchical architecture used in modern DCS (Distributed Control System) and SCADA implementations is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4[L4 Enterprise Planning ERP]
    L3[L3 Manufacturing Operations MES Historian]
    L2[L2 Supervisory Control SCADA HMI]
    L1[L1 Basic Control DCS Controller PLC]
    L0[L0 Field Devices Sensors Valves Actuators]
    L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 --> L0
```

### 4.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN["Open Loop - No Feedback"]
        direction LR
        A1[Set Point] --> A2[Controller] --> A3[Actuator Valve] --> A4[Process]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED["Closed Loop - With Feedback"]
        direction LR
        B1[Set Point] --> B2[Summing Junction] -- Error --> B3[Controller] --> B4[Actuator Valve] --> B5[Process] --> B6[Sensor] --> B2
    end
```

### 4.4 Detailed Example — Temperature Control Loop of a Jacketed CSTR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[SP Temperature deg C] --> CJ{Summing Junction}
    TT[Temperature Transmitter RTD or TC] --> CJ
    CJ -- Error --> TIC[Temperature Indicator Controller PID]
    TIC -- 4-20 mA --> CV[Control Valve on Steam Line]
    CV -- Steam Flow --> REA[Jacketed CSTR Process]
    AMB[Ambient Temperature Disturbance] --> REA
    REA -- Reactor Temperature --> TT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Tag-Name Convention (ISA-5.1):** *TIC* means **T**emperature **I**ndicating **C**ontroller; the first letter describes the *measured variable*, the remaining letters describe the *function*. Always use the standard tag names in KTU answers to score high.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **Define process control. List any four process variables that are commonly controlled in a typical chemical plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Process control is the automatic regulation of process variables to their desired values (set points) using measurement, comparison, and corrective action through a closed loop, in order to maintain safety, product quality, and energy efficiency.
>
> **Four commonly controlled process variables (4 × ½ = 2 marks):**
>
> 1. **Temperature** — e.g., reactor outlet, furnace, distillation column.
> 2. **Pressure** — e.g., column overhead, boiler drum, gas pipeline.
> 3. **Flow** — e.g., feed streams, steam, cooling water.
> 4. **Level** — e.g., reflux drum, boiler drum, tank inventory.
> 5. *(Bonus)* Composition / pH / Humidity.

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* **Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Disturbance handling | Cannot reject | Automatically rejects |
| Accuracy | Poor | High |
| Stability | Always stable | Can be unstable |
| Industrial example | Timer-based street-light control (1) | Thermostat controlling room temperature (1) |

> **Industrial examples (½ + ½ marks):**
> - **Open-Loop:** *Traffic signal controller* — operates on a fixed time cycle, never senses traffic.
> - **Closed-Loop:** *Boiler drum-level control loop* — uses a level transmitter to position a feed-water valve continuously.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a closed-loop process control system and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a *temperature control loop* for a jacketed CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor). Identify the controlled, manipulated, and disturbance variables. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

Refer to the canonical block diagram in **Section 4.1** above. The four essential blocks are:

1. **Measurement / Sensor block (1 mark):** Detects the process variable (e.g., temperature) and converts it into a standard signal (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, 3–15 psi, or HART digital). Example: RTD, thermocouple, orifice plate, DP cell.
2. **Controller block (2 marks):** Receives the set-point and the measured PV, computes the error $e(t) = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$, and applies a control law (P / PI / PID). Outputs a corrective signal to the actuator. The most common is the PID controller whose output is $u(t) = K_c \left[\, e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\int e(t)\,dt + T_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \,\right]$.
3. **Final control element (1 mark):** Receives the controller output and physically changes the manipulated variable. Examples: pneumatic diaphragm control valve, motorised valve, VFD, damper.
4. **Process block (1 mark):** The actual plant — reactor, heat exchanger, column. It has a transfer function $G_p(s)$ that maps MV to PV.
5. **Disturbance input (1 mark):** Any unwanted external input that affects the PV (e.g., ambient temperature, feed composition, coolant temperature). The controller must counteract it.
6. **Feedback path (1 mark):** The arrow from the process output back to the summing junction. It is what makes the loop *closed*.

---

**(b) Jacketed CSTR Temperature Control — Detailed Solution (7 marks):**

Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.4**. The description follows:

- **Process description (1 mark):** An exothermic or endothermic reaction takes place inside the CSTR. Heat must be added or removed through the jacket to maintain the reactor temperature at the value specified by the chemist.
- **Measured / Controlled Variable (1 mark):** Reactor temperature measured by an **RTD (Pt100)** or **thermocouple** inside the vessel.
- **Manipulated Variable (1 mark):** Steam (or cooling water) flow rate through the jacket — adjusted by a **pneumatic control valve** on the steam line.
- **Controller (1 mark):** A **PID temperature controller** with tag *TIC-100*; set-point typically between 60–150 °C depending on the reaction.
- **Disturbance Variables (1 mark):** Ambient temperature, feed temperature, reactant concentration, coolant inlet temperature, fouling of the heat-transfer surface.
- **Operation narrative (1 mark):** If PV rises above SP (e.g., exotherm causes a temperature spike), error becomes negative, controller reduces steam flow, jacket temperature drops, heat is removed from the reactor, PV returns to SP.
- **ISA Tag convention (1 mark):** Write the standard ISA-5.1 tag names: *TT-100* (transmitter), *TIC-100* (controller), *TV-100* (valve), *TJR-100* (jacket temperature recorder, optional).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **ISA-95 / Purdue reference model** for industrial automation. List the four (or five) hierarchical levels and give the time-scale of operations at each level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate clearly between **servo control** and **regulatory control**. For each type, give one industrial example and state the typical control objective. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ISA-95 / Purdue Model (7 marks):**

The **Purdue Reference Model** (also called **ISA-95**) is a standard framework that divides industrial automation into hierarchical levels. Each level performs a defined function and exchanges data with adjacent levels only.

| Level | Name | Typical Functions | Time Scale |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L0** *(1 mark)* | Process / Field | Sensors, valves, motors, actuators | Seconds |
| **L1** *(1 mark)* | Basic Control | PLC, DCS controller, RTU, single-loop controllers | Seconds – minutes |
| **L2** *(1 mark)* | Supervisory Control | SCADA, HMI, alarm management, trending | Minutes |
| **L3** *(1 mark)* | Operations Management | MES, batch recipes, historians, OEE dashboards | Hours |
| **L4** *(1 mark)* | Enterprise | ERP, supply-chain, finance, long-term scheduling | Days – weeks |
| **1 mark** | Data-flow rule | Information flows only between adjacent levels; bypasses break the model and are discouraged for safety-critical data. |

> **Real-world utility (1 mark):** Used by Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV, Yokogawa CENTUM, Siemens PCS 7, and ABB 800xA in every modern refinery and plant design.

---

**(b) Servo vs Regulatory Control (7 marks):**

| Feature | Servo Control | Regulatory Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Set point behaviour** *(1 mark)* | Set point varies continuously with time | Set point is essentially constant |
| **Main objective** *(1 mark)* | Make PV *track* the SP accurately | Make PV *reject* disturbances |
| **Typical tuning** *(1 mark)* | Fast, low overshoot, good tracking | Robust, moderate speed, high disturbance rejection |
| **Industrial example 1** *(1 mark)* | Antenna positioning system tracking a satellite | Boiler drum level control maintaining constant inventory |
| **Industrial example 2** *(1 mark)* | Rolling-mill screw-down positioning a steel strip to a thickness set-point | Reactor temperature control holding the reaction at optimum conversion |
| **Mathematical equivalent** *(1 mark)* | Reference tracking problem $\min \int (r-y)^2 dt$ | Disturbance rejection problem $\min \int d^2 dt$ |
| **Controller preference** *(1 mark)* | Often PD or PID with derivative action | PI (integral action essential to remove offset) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Writing "servo = servo motor"** — servo control means *set-point tracking*, not a hardware servo. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing CV and MV** — the controlled variable is the *output* of the process; the manipulated variable is the *input* we change. Examiners explicitly test this. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the sensor block in the loop diagram** — many students draw only controller, valve, and process. The *measurement block* is mandatory. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Not stating units in numerical answers** — KTU evaluators mark units as a separate half-mark allocation. (-½ mark)
> 5. **Writing "transducer" everywhere** — prefer the precise term *"transmitter"* (a transducer plus a signal conditioner) in KTU answers, as per ISA-5.1. (-½ mark)

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process control = automatic maintenance** of process variables at desired set points using measurement, comparison, and correction.
- **Four pillars of any loop:** Sensor → Controller → Final Control Element → Process, connected in a *closed* feedback loop.
- **Four critical variables:** **PV** (measured), **SP** (desired), **MV** (we change), **DV** (nature changes).
- **Open-loop** has no feedback — cheap but inaccurate; **Closed-loop** has feedback — accurate but can be unstable.
- **Servo control = tracking**; **Regulatory control = disturbance rejection** — these are the two fundamental objectives of any control loop.
- **ISA-95 / Purdue model** has **five levels** (L0–L4) — L0 Field, L1 Basic Control, L2 Supervisory, L3 Operations, L4 Enterprise. Know the time scale of each.
- **The error signal** is universally defined as $e(t) = \text{SP}(t) - \text{PV}(t)$ — never reverse this sign convention in KTU answers.
- **The standard 4–20 mA current loop** is the most common industrial signal — 4 mA represents 0% and 20 mA represents 100% of the calibrated range.
- **The closed-loop transfer function** is $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ — memorise this derivation; it appears in nearly every KTU module.
- **Steady-state offset in pure-P control** is removed only by adding **Integral (I) action** — a high-yield 3-mark factoid.
- **Always use ISA-5.1 tag names** (TIC, TT, TV, FIC, LT, LCV) in diagrams to score the full mark allocation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Variable (PV)** is defined as any measurable physical, chemical, or biological quantity of a process that can be monitored, recorded, and manipulated to achieve a desired operational state. In process control engineering, the process variable is the output of the process whose value is being measured and compared against a setpoint to generate a control action.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **process variable** (also called the **controlled variable** or **measured variable**) is a dynamic quantity that characterizes the state of an industrial process. The set of all such variables defines the **process state vector**, and they are the central objects upon which the entire control loop architecture operates.

### 1.1.1 Standard Industrial Classification

The universally recognized primary process variables in continuous process industries (per **ISA-5.1** and **ISO 3511** standards) are:

- **Temperature (T)** — measured in **K** or **°C**
- **Pressure (P)** — measured in **Pa**, **bar**, or **psi**
- **Flow (F or Q)** — measured in **m³/s** or **kg/s**
- **Level (L or H)** — measured in **m** or **%**
- **Analytical / Composition variables (A or X)** — **pH**, **conductivity (S/m)**, **oxygen concentration (%)**, **refractive index**, etc.
- **Physical properties** — density (**kg/m³**), viscosity (**Pa·s**), humidity (**%RH**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember for KTU Exams:** Temperature, Pressure, Flow, and Level are the "Big Four" classical process variables. Any numerical or derivation problem in Module 1 will almost always reference one or more of these four.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine you visit a doctor for a checkup. The doctor measures your **body temperature**, **blood pressure**, **heart rate**, and **oxygen saturation**. These are your body's *process variables*. They tell the doctor whether your body is in a healthy state or needs intervention.

Similarly, in a chemical plant:
- The **reactor temperature** is like the body's temperature — it tells us if the reaction is running safely.
- The **pipeline pressure** is like blood pressure — if too high, the system may rupture.
- The **flow rate** is like the heart rate — it indicates how fast material is circulating.
- The **tank level** is like fluid volume in a vessel — it must stay within safe limits.

> [!TIP]
> Just like a doctor uses a stethoscope to sense your heart rate, an engineer uses a **sensor / transmitter** to sense the process variable. The control valve (the body's response) then adjusts to bring the variable back to the desired setpoint.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Hierarchy

| Terminology | Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Variable (PV)** | The measured quantity of interest | Heart rate |
| **Setpoint (SP)** | Desired value of the PV | Ideal resting heart rate (72 bpm) |
| **Measured Variable (MV)** | The output of the sensor | Heart-rate monitor reading |
| **Manipulated Variable (MV)** | The variable adjusted by the controller | Medication dosage |
| **Controlled Variable (CV)** | Synonym for Process Variable | Vital sign under control |
| **Error (e)** | SP − PV | Deviation from healthy state |

---

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Process State and Degrees of Freedom

Every industrial process has a finite number of independent process variables. The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** is a foundational concept:

$$
\text{DOF} = (\text{Number of independent variables}) - (\text{Number of independent equations})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For a control system to function, the number of manipulated variables must equal the number of independently controllable process variables. This is called the **square system principle**.

## 2.2 Classification of Process Variables by Function

The variables in a process control loop can be classified into four functional categories:

- **Controlled Variables (CV / PV)** — variables that are maintained at a desired value (e.g., temperature in a reactor).
- **Manipulated Variables (MV)** — variables adjusted by the controller (e.g., cooling water flow).
- **Disturbance Variables (DV)** — variables that cause the PV to deviate from setpoint unintentionally (e.g., ambient temperature).
- **Load Variables** — disturbances related to the throughput or feed conditions (e.g., feed flow rate).

## 2.3 Mathematical Model of a Generic Process Variable

Most process variables obey a **dynamic balance equation** of the form:

$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = f_{in}(t) - f_{out}(t)
$$

where $x(t)$ is the process variable deviation from steady state, $f_{in}$ is the inflow rate and $f_{out}$ is the outflow rate.

For a **first-order process** (which describes most temperature, level, and pressure processes), the standard form is:

$$
\tau \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + x(t) = K_p \cdot u(t)
$$

- $\tau$ — **Time constant (s)** — speed of response
- $K_p$ — **Process gain (process units / % controller output)**
- $u(t)$ — **Manipulated variable deviation (%)**

## 2.4 Linearization of Nonlinear Process Variables

Many process variables (e.g., flow, heat transfer) have nonlinear physical laws. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to perform **Taylor-series linearization** around an operating point.

For a nonlinear function $y = f(x)$ around operating point $x_0$:

$$
y \approx f(x_0) + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{x=x_0}(x - x_0)
$$

Defining deviation variables $\tilde{x} = x - x_0$ and $\tilde{y} = y - f(x_0)$:

$$
\tilde{y} \approx K \cdot \tilde{x}, \quad \text{where} \quad K = \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{x=x_0}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula reference** for any Process Variables problem in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize these — they reappear every semester.

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Gain | $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta \text{PV}}{\Delta \text{MV}}$ | Sensitivity of process to input | process units / % |
| Time Constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Time to reach 63.2% of final value | seconds (s) |
| Dead Time / Delay | $t_d$ | Transport lag before response begins | seconds (s) |
| First-Order ODE | $\tau \dfrac{dx}{dt} + x = K_p u(t)$ | Single-energy-storage process | dimensionless |
| Second-Order ODE | $\tau^2 \dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\tau \dfrac{dx}{dt} + x = K_p u(t)$ | Two-energy-storage process | dimensionless |
| Self-Regulation | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{Open-loop recovery}}{\text{Without control}}$ | Natural return to steady state | dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime (laminar / turbulent) | dimensionless |
| Bernoulli (Flow) | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Volumetric flow from pressure drop | m³/s |
| Heat Transfer | $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Energy flow for temperature processes | W (Watts) |
| Static Pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic level / pressure | Pa |
| Ideal Gas | $PV = nRT$ | Pressure-Temperature-Volume relation | Pa · m³ |
| Linearization Gain | $K = \left.\dfrac{df}{dx}\right\vert_{x_0}$ | Slope at operating point | unit-dependent |

## 2.6 Why Process Variables Matter in Engineering

Process variables are the *lingua franca* of every industrial automation system. Real-world applications include:

- **Petroleum refineries** — controlling temperature in distillation columns to within $\pm 1°C$ for product purity.
- **Pharmaceutical batch reactors** — maintaining pH within a narrow window (typically $\pm 0.1$ units) for drug efficacy.
- **Power plants** — boiler drum level must be controlled to prevent turbine damage.
- **Food & beverage** — flow and temperature control ensures sterilization and consistent taste.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Context:** The phrase *"process variables"* in any exam question almost always expects you to (1) identify the variable, (2) classify its type, and (3) write the governing equation. Practice these three steps systematically.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Model for a Thermal Process Variable (Temperature)

Consider a perfectly mixed, electrically heated tank. Let:
- $\rho$ = liquid density $\bigl(\text{kg/m}^3\bigr)$
- $C_p$ = specific heat $\bigl(\text{J/(kg·K)}\bigr)$
- $V$ = tank volume $\bigl(\text{m}^3\bigr)$
- $Q$ = heat input rate $\bigl(\text{W}\bigr)$
- $T$ = liquid temperature $\bigl(\text{K}\bigr)$
- $T_i$ = inlet temperature $\bigl(\text{K}\bigr)$
- $F$ = volumetric flow rate $\bigl(\text{m}^3/\text{s}\bigr)$

### Step 1: Write the Energy Balance

Energy accumulation = Energy in by flow + Energy in by heating − Energy out by flow

$$
\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_i - T) + Q
$$

### Step 2: Rearrange into Standard Form

Divide through by $F \rho C_p$ (assuming constant physical properties):

$$
\frac{V}{F}\frac{dT}{dt} + T = T_i + \frac{Q}{F \rho C_p}
$$

### Step 3: Identify Physical Parameters

The coefficient of $\dfrac{dT}{dt}$ is the **residence time**, denoted $\tau$:

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{F} \quad [\text{seconds}]
$$

### Step 4: Introduce Deviation Variables

Let $T' = T - T_s$, $T_i' = T_i - T_{i,s}$, $Q' = Q - Q_s$ where subscript $s$ denotes steady-state values.

$$
\tau \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = T_i' + \frac{Q'}{F \rho C_p}
$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form (Standard First-Order ODE)

$$
\boxed{\;\tau \frac{dT'(t)}{dt} + T'(t) = K_1 \, T_i'(t) + K_2 \, Q'(t)\;}
$$

where:
$$
K_1 = 1, \quad K_2 = \frac{1}{F \rho C_p}, \quad \tau = \frac{V}{F}
$$

This is the **standard KTU first-order process model** for the temperature process variable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Self-Regulating vs Non-Self-Regulating Level Process

Consider a tank with inflow $F_{in}$ and outflow $F_{out}$ through a valve. Level $h$ varies with time.

### Step 1: Volumetric Mass Balance

$$
A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out}
$$

### Step 2: Linearize Outflow Around Operating Point

Using $F_{out} = C_v \sqrt{h}$, linearize around $h_s$:

$$
\tilde{F}_{out} \approx \frac{C_v}{2\sqrt{h_s}} \tilde{h} = K_v \tilde{h}
$$

where $K_v$ is the valve coefficient.

### Step 3: Substitute and Rearrange

$$
A \frac{d\tilde{h}}{dt} = \tilde{F}_{in} - K_v \tilde{h}
$$

$$
\frac{A}{K_v}\frac{d\tilde{h}}{dt} + \tilde{h} = \frac{1}{K_v}\tilde{F}_{in}
$$

### Step 4: Identify Time Constant

$$
\boxed{\;\tau \frac{d\tilde{h}}{dt} + \tilde{h} = K_p \, \tilde{F}_{in}\;}
$$

where $\tau = \dfrac{A}{K_v}$ and $K_p = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Insight for KTU:** Because $\tau$ is finite and positive, if the inflow suddenly stops ($\tilde{F}_{in} = 0$), the level will asymptotically fall to zero on its own. This is the definition of a **self-regulating process**. If $K_v = 0$ (a pump with no outflow regulation), $\tau \to \infty$ and the level will integrate indefinitely — this is a **non-self-regulating (integrating) process**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Linearization of Orifice Flow Equation

The flow rate $Q$ through an orifice plate is given by:

$$
Q = C_d A_o \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

Let $Q_0$ and $\Delta P_0$ denote the steady-state values. Define deviation variables $\tilde{Q} = Q - Q_0$ and $\tilde{P} = \Delta P - \Delta P_0$.

### Step 1: Take the Partial Derivative

$$
\left.\frac{\partial Q}{\partial \Delta P}\right|_{\Delta P = \Delta P_0} = C_d A_o \cdot \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho \Delta P_0}} = \frac{Q_0}{2 \Delta P_0}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Linearization Formula

$$
\tilde{Q} \approx \frac{Q_0}{2 \Delta P_0} \tilde{P}
$$

### Step 3: Process Gain Identification

$$
\boxed{\;K_p^{\text{flow}} = \frac{Q_0}{2 \Delta P_0}\;}
$$

This shows that the **process gain of a flow process is proportional to the square root of the operating point**, an important nonlinear behavior examined in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Process Variable | Governing Physical Law | Linearized First-Order Form | Self-Regulating? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Energy balance | $\tau \dfrac{dT'}{dt} + T' = K_p u'$ | Yes (with losses) |
| Pressure (gas) | Mass balance + ideal gas | $\tau \dfrac{dP'}{dt} + P' = K_p u'$ | Yes |
| Pressure (liquid) | Volumetric balance | $\tau \dfrac{dP'}{dt} + P' = K_p u'$ | Yes |
| Level (gravity outflow) | Mass balance | $\tau \dfrac{dh'}{dt} + h' = K_p F_{in}'$ | Yes |
| Level (pump outflow) | Mass balance | $A \dfrac{dh'}{dt} = F_{in}' - F_{out}'$ | No (integrating) |
| Flow | Bernoulli / orifice | $\tau \dfrac{dQ'}{dt} + Q' = K_p \Delta P'$ | Yes (very fast) |
| Composition | Mass balance | $\tau \dfrac{dC'}{dt} + C' = K_p F_{in}'$ | Yes |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define the term "Process Variable." List any four primary process variables used in industrial process control.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Process Variable (PV)** is any measurable physical or chemical quantity that describes the state of an industrial process and whose value is being controlled or monitored. *(1 mark)*

The four primary process variables are: *(2 marks — ½ mark each)*

1. **Temperature (T)**
2. **Pressure (P)**
3. **Flow (Q or F)**
4. **Level (H or L)**

*Additional composition variables like pH, conductivity, and humidity may also be mentioned as secondary variables.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Distinguish between self-regulating and non-self-regulating processes with an example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Self-Regulating | Non-Self-Regulating |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Returns to a new steady state on its own after a disturbance | Requires control to prevent unbounded deviation |
| Time constant | Finite, well-defined | Infinite (integrating) |
| Example | Temperature in a tank with heat loss | Liquid level in a tank with a constant-speed pump |
| Math form | First-order ODE | Pure integrator $dx/dt = Ku$ |

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1 mark for example + 0.5 mark for mathematical form)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, you must attempt **one full question** from Part B. Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. Below are **two alternatives**.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional elements of a process control loop. Identify the process variable, manipulated variable, and disturbance variable in each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A jacketed CSTR is used to maintain a reaction temperature. Derive the first-order mathematical model relating temperature deviation $T'$ to cooling-water flow deviation $F_c'$, given:
- Volume $V = 2 \, \text{m}^3$, density $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 4186 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$
- Steady-state cooling water flow $F_c = 0.01 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Overall heat-transfer coefficient $U = 500 \, \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K)}$ and area $A = 5 \, \text{m}^2$ *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes. Write the explanation in your exam as:

1. **Setpoint generator** issues the desired value of the process variable (e.g., $T_{sp} = 80°C$). *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Sensor** (e.g., RTD, thermocouple) measures the actual temperature $T$. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Transmitter** converts sensor output to a standard signal (4–20 mA or 0–10 V). *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Comparator** computes error $e(t) = T_{sp} - T(t)$. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Controller (PID)** generates corrective action $u(t)$ based on error. *(1 mark)*
6. **Final control element** (control valve) modulates cooling water flow $F_c$. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Process** (reactor) responds, closing the loop. *(0.5 mark)*

**Identification:** *(1 mark each)*
- **Process Variable (PV):** Reactor temperature $T$
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Cooling water flow $F_c$
- **Disturbance Variable (DV):** Feed temperature $T_{feed}$, ambient temperature

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Energy Balance on the Reactor**

$$
\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = Q_{reaction} - U A (T - T_c) + F \rho C_p (T_i - T)
$$

Assuming $Q_{reaction}$ and feed effects are constant for simplification:

$$
\rho V C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = -UA(T - T_c) + \text{constant}
$$

**Step 2: Cooling-Water Side Energy Balance**

For the jacket:
$$
\rho C_p V_c \frac{dT_c}{dt} = \rho C_p F_c (T_{c,in} - T_c) + UA(T - T_c)
$$

**Step 3: Simplify by Assuming Negligible Jacket Dynamics**

Since jacket dynamics are typically much faster than reactor, $T_c$ reaches quasi-steady state:

$$
0 = F_c (T_{c,in} - T_c) + \frac{UA}{\rho C_p}(T - T_c)
$$

**Step 4: Substitute for $T_c$**

Solving for $T_c$ in terms of $F_c$:

$$
T_c = \frac{F_c T_{c,in} + \frac{UA}{\rho C_p} T}{F_c + \frac{UA}{\rho C_p}}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Deviation Variables and Linearize**

After linearization around steady state, we obtain:

$$
\tau \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = -K_p F_c'
$$

**Step 6: Compute Numerical Values**

$$
\tau = \frac{V}{F_c + \frac{UA}{\rho C_p}} = \frac{2}{0.01 + \frac{500 \times 5}{1000 \times 4186}} = \frac{2}{0.01 + 0.000597} \approx 188.7 \, \text{s}
$$

$$
K_p = \frac{T_{c,in} - T_s}{\rho C_p F_c + UA} \cdot \rho C_p = \frac{T_{c,in} - T_s}{4186 \cdot 0.01 + 2500}
$$

If $T_{c,in} - T_s = 20°C$:

$$
K_p = \frac{20}{41.86 + 2500} \approx 7.87 \times 10^{-3} \, °C \, \text{per} \, \%
$$

**Final Model:**

$$
\boxed{\;188.7 \frac{dT'}{dt} + T'(t) = -7.87 \times 10^{-3} F_c'(t)\;}
$$

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Define process gain, time constant, and dead time. Show how these parameters appear in a standard first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) process model. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A liquid-level tank has cross-sectional area $A = 1 \, \text{m}^2$. The outflow is through a valve with resistance $R_v = 2 \, \text{s/m}^2$. The inflow is suddenly changed from $0.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ to $0.7 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Determine:
- (i) The process gain $K_p$
- (ii) The time constant $\tau$
- (iii) The new steady-state level change $\Delta h$
- (iv) The level after 3 time constants *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Definitions** *(2 marks — 1 mark each pair)*:

- **Process Gain ($K_p$):** The ratio of the change in process variable to the change in manipulated variable at steady state. It quantifies the *sensitivity* of the process.
- **Time Constant ($\tau$):** The time required for the process variable to reach **63.2%** of its final steady-state value following a step change. It characterizes the *speed of response*.
- **Dead Time ($t_d$):** The delay between the application of an input change and the first observable response at the output. Also called *transport delay*.

**FOPDT Model** *(3 marks)*:

The standard First-Order Plus Dead Time model is:

$$
\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K_p \, u(t - t_d)
$$

In Laplace domain:

$$
\frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K_p \, e^{-t_d s}}{\tau s + 1}
$$

**Block Diagram** *(2 marks)*:

A standard FOPDT step response shows an initial flat region of duration $t_d$, followed by an exponential rise with time constant $\tau$ toward the new steady-state value $K_p \cdot \Delta u$.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify the Process**

The liquid-level tank is a **self-regulating first-order process** with mass balance:

$$
A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out}
$$

For a linear outflow characteristic: $F_{out} = \dfrac{h}{R_v}$

**Step 2: Substitute and Rearrange**

$$
A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - \frac{h}{R_v}
$$

Multiplying through by $R_v$:

$$
A R_v \frac{dh}{dt} + h = R_v F_{in}
$$

**Step 3: Identify Parameters**

Comparing with $\tau \dfrac{dh}{dt} + h = K_p u$:

- **Time constant:** $\tau = A \cdot R_v = 1 \times 2 = 2 \, \text{s}$ *(1.5 marks)*
- **Process gain:** $K_p = R_v = 2 \, \text{s/m}^2$ *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 4: Compute Steady-State Level Change**

Step change in inflow: $\Delta F = 0.7 - 0.5 = 0.2 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

$$
\Delta h_{ss} = K_p \cdot \Delta F = 2 \times 0.2 = 0.4 \, \text{m} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 5: Level After 3 Time Constants** *(2.5 marks)*

For a first-order step response:

$$
h(t) = \Delta h_{ss} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)
$$

At $t = 3\tau = 6 \, \text{s}$:

$$
h(6) = 0.4 \left(1 - e^{-3}\right) = 0.4 \left(1 - 0.0498\right) = 0.4 \times 0.9502 \approx 0.380 \, \text{m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $K_p$ units:** Always carry units throughout. Many students write $K_p$ without units and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the steady-state value:** After a step change, the *final* level is not 0 — it is $h_{initial} + \Delta h_{ss}$. Show this in your final numerical answer.
> 3. **Mixing up $\tau$ definition:** The time constant is **not** the time to reach steady state; it is the time to reach **63.2%**. Steady state requires ~$5\tau$.
> 4. **Ignoring linearity assumptions:** Many process-variable equations (e.g., $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$) are nonlinear. KTU expects you to **linearize** before applying FOPDT.

---

- [ ] **Self-regulating vs non-self-regulating** difference clear (residence-time argument).
- [ ] **Process Gain formula:** $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta \text{PV}}{\Delta \text{MV}}$ with units.
- [ ] **Time Constant definition:** 63.2% of final value — *not* steady state.
- [ ] **Dead Time concept:** Pure transport delay in pipelines and analyzers.
- [ ] **Linearization skill:** Can linearize $\sqrt{x}$, $x^2$, exponential, and orifice-flow equations using Taylor series.
- [ ] **FOPDT Laplace form:** $\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{K_p e^{-t_d s}}{\tau s + 1}$.
- [ ] **Energy balance derivation** for a temperature-controlled tank.
- [ ] **Mass balance derivation** for a level-controlled tank.
- [ ] **Bernoulli / orifice equation** for flow process variable.
- [ ] **Degrees of Freedom principle** for multi-variable processes.
- [ ] **Typical industrial ranges** remembered (e.g., $-200°C$ to $1800°C$ for T, vacuum to 1000 bar for P).
- [ ] **Block diagram of control loop** can be drawn freehand.
- [ ] **Common interactions** between variables identified (T↔P↔Flow↔Level).
- [ ] **Square-system rule** for pairing controlled and manipulated variables.
- [ ] **Numerical answer must include units** — practice this discipline in every problem.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In any Process Variables question, follow the **3C Framework** —
> 1. **C**lassify the variable (temperature/pressure/flow/level/composition).
> 2. **C**hoose the governing equation (energy/mass/momentum balance).
> 3. **C**onvert to standard form (linearize, deviate, write FOPDT).
>
> Master this framework and every Part B question in this module becomes a **structured 14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Process Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need for Process Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **DEFINITION — PROCESS CONTROL**
> **Process Control** is the discipline of automatically maintaining the operating conditions of a continuous or batch industrial process at optimal values by manipulating selected variables through feedback or feedforward control strategies, while rejecting the effect of external disturbances.

A **process**, in this context, refers to any physical, chemical, or biological operation in which raw materials (inputs) are transformed into useful products (outputs) under a defined set of operating conditions such as **temperature**, **pressure**, **flow**, **level**, and **composition**.

**Process Control Instrumentation** therefore encompasses the measurement, transmission, decision-making, and final actuation hardware and software that work together to keep the process in its desired state.

The principal **economic, safety, and quality-driven objectives** that justify the *need* for process control in any modern plant are summarised in the following ordered list:

1. **Safety of Personnel and Plant Equipment** — preventing runaway reactions, over-pressurization, fire, and toxic release.
2. **Product Quality and Consistency** — maintaining tight specifications (e.g., purity, viscosity, moisture content).
3. **Operating Efficiency and Profitability** — minimising energy, raw material and catalyst consumption.
4. **Environmental Compliance** — keeping emissions, effluents, and waste within statutory limits.
5. **Operational Flexibility** — allowing rapid setpoint changes for grade transitions or product mix.
6. **Information Management and Diagnostics** — continuous process data for trending, auditing, and predictive maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT**
> A student must be able to: (i) distinguish *manual* control from *automatic* control, (ii) articulate at least **five** engineering reasons why automation of an industrial process is indispensable, and (iii) sketch a generic closed-loop block diagram labelling its standard elements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Home Water Tap" Model

Imagine you are taking a shower. You want the water to be at a **comfortable temperature** (say, $40^\circ\text{C}$) and a **comfortable flow** (say, $5$ litres/min). Every time someone opens a tap in the kitchen, the shower temperature suddenly changes — this is a *disturbance*.

- **Manual control**: You stand there, observe the temperature with your skin (sensor), compare it with how warm you *want* it to be (setpoint), and twist the hot/cold knob (actuator) until you are satisfied. This works, but is tiring and inconsistent.
- **Automatic control**: A thermostatic mixing valve senses the water temperature continuously, compares it with a fixed desired value, and automatically moves a plunger to admit more hot or cold water — *without your intervention*.

The shower, in engineering language, is the **process**; your skin, the **sensor**; the comfort temperature, the **setpoint**; the knob, the **final control element**; and your brain, the **controller**. The mixing-valve system shows why we *need* process control: **continuous, repeatable, and disturbance-free operation at a desired condition, 24 hours a day, without human fatigue or error.**

> [!TIP]
> The plant, the instrument, and the operator are always part of a *causal chain*: any small change upstream propagates downstream. Process control exists precisely to **break or attenuate** this chain so that the final product is unaffected.

## 1.3 Why "Need" is the Operative Word

Modern plants (refineries, power stations, pharmaceutical reactors, paper mills) operate at throughputs of **hundreds of tonnes per hour** and at conditions (high temperature, high pressure, toxic, flammable) that are completely inaccessible to a human operator. Manual supervision is therefore physically impossible, economically prohibitive, and safety-wise unacceptable. Process control — first pneumatic, then electronic, now **Digital Distributed Control Systems (DCS)** and **Industrial IoT** — is the only practical answer to this industrial necessity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manual vs. Automatic Control — A Structural Comparison

| Aspect | Manual Control | Automatic Control |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement | Human sense (vision, touch) | Precision instrument (transmitter) |
| Decision | Human brain | Controller (PID, MPC, AI) |
| Action | Human hand on a valve | Final control element (motorised valve) |
| Speed | Seconds to minutes | Milliseconds |
| Accuracy | $\pm 5$ to $\pm 10$ % | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.5$ % |
| Fatigue / Drift | Yes | None |
| Cost of labour | High (continuous) | Low (one-time) |
| Safety | Low | High (with interlocks & alarms) |

## 2.2 The "Need" — Broken Down into Engineering Drivers

### 2.2.1 Driver 1 — Safety
Uncontrolled exothermic reactions can cause **thermal runaway**, where temperature rises cause reaction rate to rise, which causes further temperature rise — a positive feedback loop. Without active cooling control, the reactor may rupture. Similarly, a furnace left without temperature control can melt its own tubes.

> [!WARNING]
> In petrochemical and nuclear plants, the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is legally mandated under IEC 61511. The process-control loop keeps the plant running; the SIS keeps the plant *safe* when control fails.

### 2.2.2 Driver 2 — Product Quality
A semiconductor wafer requires dopant concentration controlled within **parts-per-million** tolerance. A pharmaceutical tablet requires active-ingredient content within $\pm 2$ %. Such tight specifications are unattainable without closed-loop control.

### 2.2.3 Driver 3 — Economic Optimisation
Energy and raw materials typically constitute **60 to 80 %** of operating cost. Optimal control minimises:
- Excess steam or fuel consumption
- Off-spec product that must be re-worked
- Catalyst deactivation due to hot spots
- Compressor surge and pump cavitation

### 2.2.4 Driver 4 — Environmental and Regulatory Compliance
Stack-gas oxygen must be kept within statutory limits (e.g., $\text{NO}_x < 50$ ppm). Effluent pH must lie in $6.5$ to $8.5$. Continuous automatic monitoring and control is the only practical means of compliance.

### 2.2.5 Driver 5 — Information and Plant-Wide Optimisation
A modern DCS produces **thousands of measurements per second**, enabling:
- Real-time accounting of material and energy balances
- Advanced Process Control (APC) and Model Predictive Control (MPC)
- Data historians for root-cause analysis

## 2.3 Generic Block Diagram of a Process Control Loop

Every feedback control loop consists of **four standard functional blocks**:

1. **Process** — the plant whose variable is to be controlled.
2. **Measurement / Sensor / Transmitter (MT)** — senses the controlled variable and converts it to a standard signal.
3. **Controller (C)** — compares the measurement with the setpoint and computes the corrective action.
4. **Final Control Element (FCE)** — typically a control valve that adjusts the manipulated variable.

> [!NOTE]
> The setpoint is the **desired value** of the controlled variable. The difference between setpoint and measured value is the **error** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, which is the fundamental input to every controller.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units | Formula / Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $r(t)$ | Setpoint (reference) | same as $y$ | Desired value of CV |
| $y(t)$ | Controlled Variable (CV) | e.g., $^\circ$C, kPa, m | Measured process output |
| $u(t)$ | Manipulated Variable (MV) | % valve opening | Output of controller |
| $d(t)$ | Disturbance Variable (DV) | e.g., $^\circ$C, kg/s | Unwanted load change |
| $e(t)$ | Error | same as $y$ | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Steady-state offset | same as $y$ | $\varepsilon = r - y_{\infty}$ |
| $K_p$ | Process gain | $\dfrac{\Delta y / \Delta u_{\text{ss}}}{1}$ | Sensitivity of process |
| $\tau$ | Process time constant | seconds | First-order lag time |
| $T_d$ | Dead time / transport delay | seconds | Pure delay in loop |
| $G_p(s)$ | Process transfer function | dimensionless | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-T_d s}}{\tau s + 1}$ |
| $G_c(s)$ | Controller TF | dimensionless | e.g., $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_i s} + \tau_d s\right)$ |
| $G_m(s)$ | Measurement TF | dimensionless | Often $\approx 1$ or first-order |
| $G_v(s)$ | Valve TF | dimensionless | Often $\approx 1$ for fast valves |
| $G_L(s)$ | Disturbance TF | dimensionless | $Y(s) = G_p G_v G_c (1 + G_m G_p G_v G_c)^{-1} R(s) + \ldots$ |
| $L(s)$ | Loop transfer function | dimensionless | $L(s) = G_c G_v G_p G_m$ |
| $\text{IAE}$ | Integral of $\vert e \vert$ | unit$\cdot$s | $\int_0^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert\,dt$ |
| $\text{ISE}$ | Integral of $e^2$ | (unit)$^2\cdot$s | $\int_0^{\infty} e(t)^2\,dt$ |
| $\text{ITAE}$ | Integral of $t \vert e \vert$ | unit$\cdot$s$^2$ | $\int_0^{\infty} t\,\vert e(t) \vert\,dt$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value, written with `\vert` so as not to break the markdown column delimiter. **All transfer-function relations are linearised at the chosen operating point** — a key KTU-exam assumption.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The need for process control is not abstract. It shows up in:

- **Distillation columns** — controlling reflux to keep top and bottom product purities on-spec.
- **Boiler drum level** — three-element control to handle shrink-and-swell.
- **Reactor temperature** — cascade control with jacket temperature as inner loop.
- **pH neutralisation** — highly nonlinear, requires gain-scheduled or adaptive control.
- **Aircraft autopilot** — the aerospace cousin of process control, controlling pitch, roll, yaw.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The General Closed-Loop Relationship

Consider the standard **negative-feedback** control loop in which the process, valve, and measurement are described by their respective transfer functions, and the controller is denoted $G_c(s)$.

**Step 1 — Forward path contribution of setpoint:**
The signal from the setpoint $R(s)$ travels through the controller, the valve, and the process to produce the controlled variable.

$$
Y_{\text{forward}}(s) \;=\; G_c(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_p(s)\,R(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Feedback path contribution:**
The measurement block multiplies the output by $G_m(s)$ and subtracts it from the setpoint, generating the error:

$$
E(s) \;=\; R(s) - G_m(s)\,Y(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve:**
The controller output $U(s) = G_c(s)\,E(s)$, the final control element adjusts the process input, and the process output is $Y(s) = G_p(s)\,G_v(s)\,U(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s)$. Substituting and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(s) &\;=\; G_p G_v\,G_c\,E(s) + G_d D(s) \\
     &\;=\; G_p G_v G_c\bigl[\,R(s) - G_m Y(s)\,\bigr] + G_d D(s) \\
Y(s)\,\bigl[\,1 + G_p G_v G_c G_m\,\bigr] &\;=\; G_p G_v G_c\,R(s) + G_d D(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final closed-loop transfer functions:**

$$
Y(s) \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m}}_{T(s)\,=\,\text{complementary sensitivity}}\,R(s) \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{G_d}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m}}_{S(s)\,=\,\text{sensitivity}}\,D(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the two fundamental transfer functions:**

$$
T(s) \;=\; \dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + L(s)} \quad\text{with}\quad L(s) = G_c G_v G_p G_m
$$

$$
S(s) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 + L(s)}
$$

**Step 6 — Useful identity (Youla’s relation):**

$$
T(s) + S(s) \;=\; 1
$$

This identity states that *setpoint tracking* and *disturbance rejection* are two sides of the same coin: improving one degrades the other. It is the mathematical reason why **controller tuning is always a trade-off**.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is examinable in KTU. A 7-mark sub-question frequently asks the student to derive the closed-loop response for a step change in setpoint OR disturbance.

## 3.2 The Steady-State Error — Why We Need Integral Action

**Step 1 — Definition of steady-state error:**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{ss}} \;=\; \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,E(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Express $E(s)$ in terms of $R(s)$:**

$$
E(s) \;=\; R(s) - Y(s) \;=\; R(s)\bigl[\,1 - T(s)\,\bigr] \;=\; S(s)\,R(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Step change in setpoint** $R(s) = \dfrac{A}{s}$ with a proportional-only controller $G_c = K_c$ on a first-order process $G_p = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$ and unity measurement, unity valve:

$$
T(s) \;=\; \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the steady-state error using the final value theorem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{ss}} &\;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{A}{s}\bigl[1 - T(s)\bigr] \\
&\;=\; A\bigl[1 - T(0)\bigr] \\
&\;=\; A\!\left[\,1 - \dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}\,\right] \\
&\;=\; \dfrac{A}{1 + K_c K_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

- $\varepsilon_{\text{ss}} = 0$ is desired but is *never* achieved with pure proportional action.
- The **offset** depends on the **loop gain** $K_c K_p$.
- Increasing $K_c$ reduces offset but worsens stability.
- **Integral action** is the only systematic way to drive $\varepsilon_{\text{ss}} \to 0$, because it adds a pole at the origin, making the loop **Type-1** or higher, and hence the position-error constant $K_p^{\text{(pos)}}$ infinite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This last derivation is the *mathematical proof* of why we need integral action in a controller — and hence why we need process control at all, because integral action is what guarantees the controlled variable eventually equals the setpoint in the steady state.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The "Five Ws" of Process Control

| Question | Engineering Answer |
|---|---|
| **What** is controlled? | A process variable such as temperature, pressure, level, flow, or composition. |
| **Why** must it be controlled? | Safety, product quality, profitability, environmental, and information reasons. |
| **Where** in the plant? | Anywhere raw materials are converted — reactors, separators, heat exchangers, storage tanks. |
| **When** is it needed? | Always — $24 \times 7$ — for as long as the plant is running. |
| **How** is it done? | Through a closed feedback (or feedforward) loop comprising sensor, controller, and final element. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Closed-Loop Process Control — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR[Setpoint R] --> nodeSumA{Summing junction}
    nodeD[Disturbance D] --> nodeProc
    nodeSumA --> nodeC[Controller Gc]
    nodeC --> nodeV[Final Control Element Valve Gv]
    nodeV --> nodeProc[Process Gp]
    nodeProc --> nodeY[Controlled Variable Y]
    nodeY --> nodeM[Measurement Gm]
    nodeM --> nodeSumA
    nodeY --> nodeOut[Plant output to next stage]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The setpoint $R$ enters the summing junction (circle with $\pm$ signs), where it is compared with the measured feedback $G_m Y$. The error $E = R - G_m Y$ drives the controller $G_c$, whose output moves the valve $G_v$, which manipulates an input to the process $G_p$. The process output $Y$ is the controlled variable. A disturbance $D$ can enter the process at any point and must be rejected by the loop.

## 4.2 Manual vs. Automatic — Comparative Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MAN["MANUAL CONTROL LOOP"]
        m1[Operator observes the process] --> m2[Operator compares with desired value]
        m2 --> m3[Operator decides action]
        m3 --> m4[Operator hand moves the valve]
        m4 --> m1
    end
    subgraph AUTO["AUTOMATIC CONTROL LOOP"]
        a1[Transmitter measures process] --> a2[Controller computes error]
        a2 --> a3[Controller outputs signal]
        a3 --> a4[Actuator moves valve]
        a4 --> a1
    end
```

**Interpretation:** In the manual loop, the human is simultaneously the *sensor, controller, and actuator*. In the automatic loop, dedicated hardware/software performs each function continuously, faster, and more accurately. This is the architectural essence of *why we need process control*.

## 4.3 Hierarchical Plant-Wide Control Structure (Purdue Model — Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL4[Level 4 Enterprise Resource Planning] --> nodeL3
    nodeL3[Level 3 Manufacturing Execution System] --> nodeL2
    nodeL2[Level 2 Supervisory Control DCS SCADA] --> nodeL1
    nodeL1[Level 1 Basic Control loops PID valves] --> nodeL0
    nodeL0[Level 0 Field sensors and actuators]
```

**Interpretation:** Process control is not an isolated discipline. The basic control loops (Level 1) exist to satisfy the setpoints generated by supervisory control (Level 2), which in turn supports MES (Level 3) and ERP (Level 4). The *need* for process control is therefore embedded in the entire business hierarchy.

## 4.4 Process-Control Engineering Workflow — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Step 1 Define operating objectives] --> nodeP2[Step 2 Identify CV MV and DV]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Step 3 Select sensors and FCE]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Step 4 Obtain process model]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Step 5 Choose control strategy]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[Step 6 Tune and simulate]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7[Step 7 Commission and operate]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8[Step 8 Monitor and retune]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **engineering methodology** that justifies the existence of process control. It is also the order in which a KTU examiner typically structures a 14-mark design question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**"List and briefly explain any three reasons why process control is indispensable in a modern chemical plant."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Safety (1 mark):** Uncontrolled reactors, boilers, and compressors can lead to runaway reactions, explosions, or toxic release. Automatic control with interlocks and alarms protects personnel and equipment.
2. **Product quality and consistency (1 mark):** Tight specifications (purity, viscosity, composition) can only be met by continuous automatic regulation of operating conditions.
3. **Economic optimisation (1 mark):** Continuous control minimises energy and raw-material consumption and prevents off-spec product, maximising profit.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]
**"Distinguish between manual control and automatic control with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Manual | Automatic |
|---|---|---|
| Decision-maker | Human | Controller (PID, etc.) |
| Example (1 mark each) | Operator opening a hand valve on a furnace to control temperature | Thermostat-driven heating system / DCS-controlled distillation column reflux |
| Accuracy (1 mark) | Low, drift with fatigue | High, repeatable, $24\times7$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 1 Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT**
> In Part B questions on "Need for Process Control", students **commonly lose marks** in the following ways:
> 1. Confusing *block diagram symbols* — a summing junction must be a **circle** with explicit $\pm$ signs; using a square makes the diagram non-standard and loses one mark.
> 2. Forgetting to **label signals** ($R, E, U, Y, D$); a block diagram without signal names is treated as a drawing, not an engineering sketch, and partial credit is reduced.
> 3. Writing the closed-loop transfer function as $T(s) = G_c G_v G_p$ — this is the *open-loop* relation. The correct closed-loop form is $T(s) = \dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m}$, and many students miss the denominator.
> 4. Stating the closed-loop identity as $T + S = 0$ instead of $T + S = 1$.
> 5. Skipping the link to a **real industrial example** — KTU examiners reward application-oriented answers.

### Question B-A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) "Explain the need for process control in a modern industrial plant. Discuss at least five distinct engineering reasons with one real industrial example for each."** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Process control is the automatic regulation of process variables (temperature, pressure, flow, level, composition) to keep them at desired values in spite of disturbances. The need for it arises from the following five engineering reasons.

1. **Safety (2 marks):** Industrial reactors, boilers, and compressors operate with flammable, toxic, or high-energy materials. A deviation outside the safe envelope (e.g., reactor temperature exceeding $250\,^\circ$C in a nitration reactor) can cause explosion. Automatic control with high-high and low-low trips forms the first line of defence. *Example:* Furnace tube-skin temperature control in an ethylene cracking furnace, where the high-temperature trip prevents tube rupture.

2. **Product Quality (1.5 marks):** Modern products require tight compositional and physical specifications. *Example:* Refinery gasoline research octane number (RON) is controlled by adjusting reformer reactor temperature within $\pm 0.5\,^\circ$C.

3. **Economy and Energy Efficiency (1.5 marks):** Energy is the dominant operating cost. Optimal control minimises fuel, steam, and electricity. *Example:* Minimising excess air in a fired heater by controlling stack-gas $\text{O}_2$ at $2$ to $3$ % improves efficiency by $5$ to $10$ %.

4. **Environmental Compliance (1 mark):** Continuous automatic monitoring of emissions and effluents is mandated by law. *Example:* $\text{NO}_x$ control in a power-plant boiler by staged combustion and flue-gas recirculation.

5. **Information and Plant-Wide Optimisation (1 mark):** Thousands of measurements per second, archived in a historian, enable advanced process control, root-cause analysis, and predictive maintenance.

**(b) "For a unity-feedback system with process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{3s+1}$, valve $G_v(s) = 1$, measurement $G_m(s) = 1$, and proportional controller $G_c(s) = K_c$, derive (i) the closed-loop transfer function, (ii) the steady-state error for a unit step input, and (iii) the value of $K_c$ that gives an offset of $5$ %."** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Closed-loop transfer function (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(s) &\;=\; G_c G_v G_p G_m \;=\; \dfrac{2 K_c}{3s+1} \\
T(s) &\;=\; \dfrac{L(s)}{1+L(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{2K_c}{3s+1+2K_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Steady-state error (2 marks):**

Using the final value theorem with $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{ss}} &\;=\; \lim_{s\to 0}\, s \cdot \dfrac{1}{s}\cdot T(s) \;=\; T(0) \;=\; \dfrac{2K_c}{1+2K_c} \\
\varepsilon_{\text{ss}} &\;=\; 1 - Y_{\text{ss}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1+2K_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Value of $K_c$ for $5$ % offset (2 marks):**

$$
0.05 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1+2K_c} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 1+2K_c = 20 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K_c = 9.5
$$

> [!TIP]
> A common student error is to equate $\varepsilon_{\text{ss}}$ with $K_c$ — the correct relation involves the loop gain $K_c K_p$, here $K_p = 2$.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**(a) "Draw a fully labelled block diagram of a generic closed-loop process control system and explain the function of each block. State the closed-loop transfer function relating the output to the setpoint and to the disturbance."** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The diagram has **four** standard blocks in a unity (or non-unity) feedback path:

- **Process** $G_p(s)$ — the plant whose output $Y(s)$ is to be controlled.
- **Sensor / Transmitter** $G_m(s)$ — measures $Y$ and produces a feedback signal.
- **Controller** $G_c(s)$ — computes the error $E = R - G_m Y$ and produces the corrective signal.
- **Final Control Element** $G_v(s)$ — typically a control valve that adjusts the manipulated variable.

**Functions (2 marks):**
- Process delivers the product.
- Sensor converts the physical variable into a standard pneumatic / electronic / digital signal.
- Controller applies the control law (P, PI, PID, etc.).
- Valve regulates the mass/energy flow into the process.

**Closed-loop transfer functions (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; T(s) \;=\; \dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m}
$$

$$
\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} \;=\; S(s) \;=\; \dfrac{G_d}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m}
$$

where $G_d(s)$ is the disturbance transfer function.

**(b) "With reference to a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR), explain how process control helps in: (i) maintaining temperature, (ii) maintaining level, and (iii) ensuring product composition. Identify the CV, MV, and DV for each case."** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Consider a jacketed CSTR where reactant A is converted to product B, an exothermic, first-order reaction.

**(i) Temperature control (2.5 marks):**
- **CV:** Reactor temperature $T$.
- **MV:** Cooling-water flow rate $F_c$ (or jacket temperature).
- **DV:** Inlet reactant concentration, ambient temperature, feed temperature.
- *How:* A temperature transmitter feeds the controller, which adjusts the cooling-water valve. With cascade control, jacket temperature becomes the inner loop, dramatically improving disturbance rejection.

**(ii) Level control (2 marks):**
- **CV:** Liquid level $h$ in the reactor.
- **MV:** Outlet product flow rate $F_{\text{out}}$.
- **DV:** Inlet feed flow rate $F_{\text{in}}$.
- *How:* Differential-pressure level transmitter drives a controller that throttles the outlet valve. If $F_{\text{in}}$ is the major disturbance, **feedforward control** of $F_{\text{out}}$ based on $F_{\text{in}}$ is added to the feedback loop.

**(iii) Composition control (2.5 marks):**
- **CV:** Concentration of B, $C_B$, often measured by a chromatograph or spectroscopic analyser.
- **MV:** Reactant A feed rate (slowest loop, large dead time).
- **DV:** Inlet $C_A$, coolant temperature, catalyst activity drift.
- *How:* Composition loops are slow (analyser dead time minutes). **Inferential control** using temperature as a secondary measurement, or **cascade** to the feed flow, is commonly used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all three cases, the *need* for process control is justified by the same five reasons: safety (runaway prevention), quality (tight $C_B$), economy (optimum conversion), environment (vent-gas composition), and information (continuous data for plant optimisation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Process control = automatic regulation** of process variables to keep them at desired values despite disturbances.
- **Five reasons to need it:** Safety, Product Quality, Economy, Environment, Information.
- **Manual vs. Automatic:** Manual relies on the human for sensing, decision, action; Automatic replaces each with a dedicated instrument.
- **Four standard blocks:** Process, Measurement, Controller, Final Control Element.
- **Setpoint** $r$ = desired value; **Controlled Variable** $y$ = measured output; **Error** $e = r - G_m y$.
- **Closed-loop transfer function:** $T(s) = \dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m}$.
- **Disturbance transfer function:** $S(s) = \dfrac{G_d}{1 + L(s)}$.
- **Identity:** $T(s) + S(s) = 1$ — setpoint tracking and disturbance rejection are complementary.
- **Steady-state offset with P-control:** $\varepsilon_{\text{ss}} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_c K_p}$ (for unity feedback, step input).
- **Integral action** is required to make $\varepsilon_{\text{ss}} = 0$ — this is a key engineering justification.
- **Purdue model levels:** Field (0) → Basic Control (1) → Supervisory (2) → MES (3) → ERP (4).
- **Standard industrial examples:** Distillation, CSTR, boiler, furnace, pH, compressor surge.
- **Common KTU pitfalls:** Missing denominator in $T(s)$, forgetting signal labels in block diagrams, no real-world example.
- **Always link** a control concept to a **practical reason** (one of the five drivers) in the exam answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Control Actions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Control Actions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Basic Control Actions?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Basic Control Actions* are the fundamental mathematical algorithms (or laws) that a controller executes to convert an **error signal** $e(t)$ — the difference between the **set point (SP)** and the **process variable (PV)** — into a **manipulated variable (MV)** or controller output $m(t)$, in order to maintain the process at its desired operating condition.

Mathematically, the general controller action is expressed as:
$$m(t) = f\bigl(e(t)\bigr)$$

where
$$e(t) = \text{Set Point} - \text{Process Variable} = r(t) - c(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The four canonical control actions are **ON-OFF, Proportional (P), Integral (I), and Derivative (D)**. Combinations like **PI, PD, and PID** are also examined as they form the backbone of industrial process controllers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving a Car" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a cruising speed of **60 km/h** on a highway:

| Control Action | Driving Analogy | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **ON-OFF** | Accelerator either fully pressed or fully released | Crashes between overshoot and undershoot |
| **P** (Proportional) | Press the accelerator *proportionally* to how far you are from 60 km/h | Always stays slightly below 60 (offset) |
| **I** (Integral) | Slowly add extra pressure as long as the error exists | Eliminates the remaining offset |
| **D** (Derivative) | Anticipate hills — press harder *before* speed drops | Dampens sudden changes (improves stability) |
| **PID** | Combine P + I + D | Smooth, fast, accurate cruising |

> [!TIP]
