- Reduced accuracy and process variability

A hysteresis of 1–5% is typical; values above 5% require maintenance.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **three inherent flow characteristics** of a control valve with neat sketches. State one application for each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A control valve must pass **150 m³/hr** of a liquid with specific gravity **0.85** and a pressure drop of **2.5 bar**. Compute the required valve coefficient $C_v$ (use SI-engineering units where $N_1 = 0.865$).

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**1. Linear Characteristic:**
- The flow $Q$ varies *linearly* with stem travel $x$.
- Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = x/x_{max}$
- **Application:** Systems where the valve pressure drop is large compared to the system pressure drop (constant $\Delta P$).

**2. Equal Percentage Characteristic:**
- Equal increments of stem travel produce equal *percentage* changes in flow.
- Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(x/x_{max} - 1)}$
- **Application:** Most general process control applications where pressure drop varies with flow (e.g., heat exchangers, chemical reactors).

**3. Quick-Opening Characteristic:**
- A large change in flow occurs for a small stem displacement near the seat.
- Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = 1 - (1 - x/x_{max})^n$
- **Application:** On-off service, safety relief valves, and quick-isolation applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch Description (cannot draw in plain text):** Linear → straight line; Equal % → exponential curve opening upward; Quick opening → reverse exponential (saturation curve). All curves go from (0, 0) to (1, 1).

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify known quantities and convert units.

- $Q = 150 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$
- $G = 0.85$ (specific gravity)
- $\Delta P = 2.5 \text{ bar}$

Convert pressure: $1 \text{ bar} = 14.504 \text{ psi}$, so:
$$\Delta P = 2.5 \times 14.504 = 36.26 \text{ psi}$$

Convert flow: $1 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 4.403 \text{ GPM}$, so:
$$Q = 150 \times 4.403 = 660.5 \text{ GPM}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the $C_v$ equation.
$$C_v = \frac{Q}{N_1 \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / G}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the inner term.
$$\Delta P / G = 36.26 / 0.85 = 42.66 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root.
$$\sqrt{42.66} = 6.531$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $N_1$.
$$N_1 \cdot \sqrt{42.66} = 0.865 \times 6.531 = 5.649$$

**Step 6:** Final division.
$$C_v = \frac{660.5}{5.649} = 116.9$$

**Step 7:** Select next standard size from manufacturer catalog: $C_v = 120$ (typical standard steps: 100, 120, 140, 160...). The chosen valve would have $C_v = 120$.

**Final Answer:** $C_v = 116.9 \approx 120$ (selected from catalog)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the construction and working of a **pneumatic diaphragm actuator** with a neat diagram. What is meant by 'fail-open' and 'fail-close'?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **valve positioner** with a block diagram. Why is it used?

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Construction:**
- A **diaphragm** (reinforced elastomer or metal) clamped between two housing halves.
- A **spring** (compression type) acts on the opposite side of the diaphragm.
- The **stem** is attached to the diaphragm's center and moves linearly.
- A **yoke** connects the actuator to the valve body.
- An **input port** admits pneumatic pressure (3–15 psig).

**Working:**
- When air pressure increases, the diaphragm deflects *against* the spring, moving the stem downward (or upward, depending on configuration).
- A balance is reached when the air force equals the spring force plus any process forces.
- The stem position is therefore *proportional* to the air pressure.

**Fail-Open (Air-to-Close):**
- Air pressure holds the valve *closed*; if air fails, the spring pushes the valve to the *open* position.
- Used where process safety requires that loss of instrument air must *not* block flow (e.g., cooling water valves, emergency venting).

**Fail-Close (Air-to-Open):**
- Air pressure pushes the valve *open*; if air fails, the spring returns the valve to the *closed* position.
- Used where loss of flow must be prevented (e.g., fuel supply to a burner).

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Valve Positioner — Definition:**
A **valve positioner** is a local feedback device that ensures the valve stem position accurately follows the controller signal, despite friction, packing, and process force disturbances.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller Signal 4-20 mA / 3-15 psig] --> B[Set Point SP to Positioner]
    C[Stem Position Feedback] --> D[Actual Position Sensor]
    D --> E[Comparator in Positioner]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Error Signal]
    F --> G[Pneumatic Relay Amplifier]
    G --> H[Actuator Air Supply]
    H --> I[Valve Stem Motion]
    I --> C
    I --> J[Process Flow]
```

**Why is a Positioner Used?**

1. **Eliminates stem friction effects** — packing friction is a major source of dead band.
2. **Provides high thrust** — overrides process fluid forces on the plug.
3. **Reduces hysteresis** — typical reduction from 5% to 0.5%.
4. **Speeds up dynamic response** — reduces lag for tight control.
5. **Allows split-ranging** — two valves driven from one 4–20 mA signal.
6. **Enables tight shut-off** — ensures proper seating at end of travel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (4 marks lost):** Students forget to convert $m^3/hr$ to GPM and bar to psi. Always show unit conversions in the answer.
> 2. **Confusing fail-open with air-to-open:** Fail-open ≠ Air-to-open. "Fail" describes what happens when *air is lost*; "Air-to" describes *normal* operation. Many students reverse this and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation of $C_v$ formula:** A direct substitution without stating the formula costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Calling the positioner "a controller":** The positioner is a *local* feedback device, not a process controller. Use the correct term.
> 5. **Forgetting the spring direction in fail-safe discussion:** Always specify whether the spring is above or below the diaphragm.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exam):**

- ✅ Final control element = control valve (most common), variable speed pump, damper, louver.
- ✅ Standard signals: **4–20 mA** (electronic) and **3–15 psig** (pneumatic).
- ✅ **I/P converter** maps 4–20 mA to 3–15 psig with gain **0.75 psig/mA**.
- ✅ **$C_v$** = GPM of water at $\Delta P$ = 1 psi, 60 °F.
- ✅ **Equal-percentage** is the most common inherent characteristic; linear is used for constant $\Delta P$.
- ✅ **Quick-opening** = reverse-exponential; used for on/off and safety service.
- ✅ **Pneumatic diaphragm actuator** = fail-safe in one direction via spring action.
- ✅ **Air-to-Open (ATO) = Fail-Close (FC)** and **Air-to-Close (ATC) = Fail-Open (FO)** — memorize this pair!
- ✅ **Valve positioner** = local feedback loop on the valve stem; reduces hysteresis and dead band.
- ✅ **Hysteresis** = max difference in stem position for same input approached from different directions.
- ✅ **Rangeability** = $Q_{max}/Q_{min}$ (typically 30:1 to 50:1).
- ✅ **Globe valve** is the standard for throttling; **ball/butterfly** for on/off or large flow; **diaphragm** for corrosive fluids; **V-port** for slurry.
- ✅ Always **convert SI to US units** before using the standard $C_v$ formula unless using the SI form with $N_1 = 0.865$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Conversion (Analogue and Digital Electrical Signals)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signal Conversion (Analogue and Digital Electrical Signals)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: SIGNAL CONVERSION — ANALOGUE AND DIGITAL ELECTRICAL SIGNALS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Process Control Instrumentation**, *Signal Conversion* refers to the systematic transformation of a process variable representation from one form to another so that it can be transmitted, conditioned, processed, or actuated in a control loop. The two principal domains of interest are:

- **Analogue (Analog) Signal**: A continuously varying electrical quantity (voltage or current) whose amplitude is a continuous, one-to-one function of the physical process variable. Mathematically, an analog signal $x(t)$ is defined for every instant $t$ in a continuous range and assumes a continuum of values.

- **Digital Signal**: A discrete, two-level (binary) representation of information in which the signal can assume only a finite set of quantized levels, typically encoded in a binary word of $n$ bits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The conversion between these two domains is performed by two critical sub-systems:
> 1. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — Converts real-world analog signals from sensors into binary codes for the controller/DCS.
> 2. **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)** — Converts binary commands from the controller into analog signals that drive final control elements such as pneumatic valve positioners, variable-speed drives, and analog recorders.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Dimmer vs. Light Switch" Intuition

Imagine you are controlling the brightness of a lamp in a room:

- A **lamp dimmer** is the perfect analog signal analogy. It allows you to *infinitely* slide the brightness from 0% to 100% in a smooth, continuous manner. There are no "jumps" — any value between two extremes is achievable. In process control, a 4–20 mA current loop behaves similarly, representing valve position from fully closed (4 mA) to fully open (20 mA) in a continuous fashion.

- A **digital wall switch** (ON/OFF) represents a digital signal. The lamp is either fully ON (logic 1) or fully OFF (logic 0). To express a "halfway" brightness, you cannot with a single switch — you must encode it as a series of timed pulses (**PWM — Pulse Width Modulation**) or a multi-bit binary number (e.g., `10110010` represents 178 out of 255 levels).

A **Signal Converter** is, therefore, the "translator" sitting between the dimmer (sensor/field) and the switch (digital controller). Without it, the brain of the system (microprocessor/DCS) cannot understand the analog world, and the analog world cannot obey digital commands.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics & Industrial Standards

The following internationally accepted physical/electrical standards govern signal conversion in process industries:

- **Current Loop Standard:** **4 mA to 20 mA DC** (IEC 60381-1 compliant).
- **Voltage Signal Standard:** **1 V to 5 V DC** or **0 V to 10 V DC**.
- **Pneumatic Standard (legacy):** **3 psi to 15 psi** (≈ 0.2 to 1.0 bar).
- **HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer):** Digital communication superimposed on the 4–20 mA analog signal at ±0.5 mA.
- **Fieldbus Standards:** FOUNDATION Fieldbus and PROFIBUS-PA (fully digital replacements of 4–20 mA).

> [!NOTE]
> The **4 mA "live zero"** is critical: it is the *lowest* valid signal, not zero, which enables the receiver to distinguish a *true zero process value* from a *broken wire* (which would read 0 mA). This is a unique hallmark of process control instrumentation.

### 1.4 Hierarchy of Signals in a Control Loop

The signal flow in a typical DCS/PLC-based control loop follows a strict sequence:

$$
\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Analog Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} \text{Digital Signal} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Digital Command} \xrightarrow{\text{DAC}} \text{Analog Command} \rightarrow \text{Final Control Element}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Characteristics of Analogue Signals

An analog signal in process control is characterized by the following properties:

- **Continuity in time and amplitude** — the signal $x(t)$ is defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ and assumes every real value within its operating range.
- **Linearity** — ideally, the output is a linear function of the process variable: $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \left(\frac{P}{P_{\max}}\right)$, where $P$ is the measured pressure.
- **Noise susceptibility** — analog signals are vulnerable to induced voltages, ground loops, and EMI (Electromagnetic Interference).
- **Resolution** — theoretically infinite, since any infinitesimal change in $P$ produces a proportional infinitesimal change in current.

## 2.2 Fundamental Characteristics of Digital Signals

A digital signal is characterized by:

- **Discreteness in time and amplitude** — values are sampled at discrete instants $t = nT_s$ and quantized to one of $2^n$ discrete levels.
- **Noise immunity** — regenerative repeaters and error-correcting codes (CRC, parity) provide high immunity.
- **Finite resolution** — the smallest detectable change equals one LSB (Least Significant Bit).
- **Two-state logic levels** — typically, in industrial 24 V DC logic: $V \leq 5$ V means logic 0; $V \geq 15$ V means logic 1.

## 2.3 The Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) Process

The ADC process involves three sequential operations:

1. **Sampling** — capturing the instantaneous amplitude of $x(t)$ at uniform intervals $T_s$. Governed by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**:
$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}
$$
where $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the sampling frequency and $f_{\text{max}}$ is the highest frequency component present in $x(t)$.

2. **Quantization** — mapping the continuous sample value to the nearest of $2^n$ discrete levels. The quantizer step (LSB size) is:
$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} \quad \text{(unipolar)} \quad \text{or} \quad \Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n - 1} \quad \text{(bipolar, mid-tread)}
$$

3. **Encoding** — assigning a unique $n$-bit binary code (Natural Binary, BCD, Gray, or Two's Complement) to each quantization level.

## 2.4 The Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC) Process

A DAC reconstructs a staircase approximation of the original analog signal by summing binary-weighted current or voltage contributions. For an $n$-bit DAC with reference $V_{\text{ref}}$ and digital input word $D = (b_{n-1} \, b_{n-2} \cdots b_0)_2$:

$$
V_o = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_i \cdot 2^i}{2^n}
$$

## 2.5 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quantization Step (LSB)** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n}$ | Volts (V); $n$ = number of bits |
| 2 | **Maximum Quantization Error** | $Q_e = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Volts (V); half of one LSB |
| 3 | **Resolution (% of FS)** | $\%R = \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100$ | Dimensionless percentage |
| 4 | **Dynamic Range** | $DR = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB; theoretical SNR of ideal ADC |
| 5 | **Nyquist Sampling Rate** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Hz |
| 6 | **Alias Frequency** | $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_s - f_{\text{in}} \vert$ (when $f_{\text{in}} < f_s/2$ rule is violated) | Hz; use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation |
| 7 | **4–20 mA Linear Conversion** | $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{\text{max}}}$ | mA |
| 8 | **Percent of Range from Current** | $\% \text{ span} = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 100$ | Dimensionless % |
| 9 | **Voltage from Current Loop (with $R = 250 \, \Omega$)** | $V = 1 + 4 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{\text{max}}}$ | V (1–5 V range) |
| 10 | **DAC Output (Binary Weighted)** | $V_o = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{b_i \cdot 2^i}{2^n}$ | V |
| 11 | **DAC Output (R-2R Ladder)** | $V_o = -V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ (inverting config) | V |
| 12 | **Encoder Resolution (optical disc, $N$ lines/rev)** | $\theta_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$ | Degrees per pulse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** For an $n$-bit ADC/DAC, the LSB represents approximately $\dfrac{100}{2^n}$ percent of full scale. For 12 bits, this is $\approx 0.0244$ %FS; for 16 bits, $\approx 0.00153$ %FS. Higher $n$ = finer resolution = better process control accuracy.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation column temperature control**: RTD produces a small analog mV signal → conditioned (amplified, linearized) → 12-bit ADC → DCS computes reflux flow setpoint → 14-bit DAC → drives control valve positioner.
- **Paper machine basis-weight control**: $\beta$-ray gauge sensor → 4–20 mA → 16-bit ADC → MPC algorithm → 4–20 mA to slice lip actuator.
- **CNC machine tool positioning**: Optical encoders on spindle → digital quadrature pulses → position counter → 16-bit DAC → servo amplifier.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Quantization Step Size (LSB Voltage) for a Unipolar ADC

**Problem Statement:** An 8-bit ADC operates over an input voltage range of $0$ V to $10$ V. Derive the expression for the smallest distinguishable voltage step (LSB) and the maximum theoretical quantization error.

**Given:** $n = 8$ bits; $V_{\text{FS}} = V_{\text{max}} - V_{\text{min}} = 10 - 0 = 10$ V.

**Step 1:** Total number of quantization levels.
An $n$-bit binary word can represent $2^n$ distinct codes. Therefore:
$$
L = 2^n = 2^8 = 256 \text{ levels}
$$

**Step 2:** Definition of Full-Scale Range.
The full-scale range is the difference between the maximum and minimum representable input:
$$
V_{\text{FS}} = V_{\text{max}} - V_{\text{min}}
$$

**Step 3:** Definition of the LSB (Quantum).
The LSB, by definition, is the width of one quantization interval — the voltage difference between two adjacent codes. Dividing the full range by the number of intervals:
$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{L}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values.
$$
\Delta V = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{2^8} = \frac{10}{256} \text{ V} = 0.0390625 \text{ V} = 39.0625 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute Maximum Quantization Error.
By the mid-tread quantizer assumption, the maximum error of any sample is half the LSB:
$$
Q_e = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{0.0390625}{2} \text{ V} = \pm 0.01953125 \text{ V} \approx \pm 19.53 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 6:** Express in percent of full scale.
$$
Q_e \,[\%\text{FS}] = \frac{0.01953125}{10} \times 100 = 0.1953 \,\% \text{FS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The quantization error is *inherent* and *irreducible* for a given bit-width. It can only be reduced by increasing $n$ (one extra bit halves $Q_e$, since $2^{n+1} = 2 \cdot 2^n$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Digital Output Code for a Given Analog Input

**Problem Statement:** For the same 8-bit, 0–10 V ADC, determine the binary output code when the input voltage is $V_{\text{in}} = 4.7$ V.

**Step 1:** Use the ADC transfer equation.
For a unipolar ADC, the digital output code $D$ (in decimal) is given by:
$$
D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{\Delta V}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the previously derived $\Delta V$.
$$
D = \text{round}\left(\frac{4.7}{0.0390625}\right) = \text{round}(120.32)
$$

**Step 3:** Round to the nearest integer.
$$
D = 120_{10}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to binary.
Repeated division by 2:
- $120 \div 2 = 60$ remainder $0$ (LSB)
- $60 \div 2 = 30$ remainder $0$
- $30 \div 2 = 15$ remainder $0$
- $15 \div 2 = 7$ remainder $1$
- $7 \div 2 = 3$ remainder $1$
- $3 \div 2 = 1$ remainder $1$
- $1 \div 2 = 0$ remainder $1$ (MSB)

Reading remainders bottom-up: $D = 01111000_2$.

**Step 5:** Verify the quantized output.
$$
V_{\text{quantized}} = 120 \times 0.0390625 = 4.6875 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the actual error.
$$
\text{Error} = V_{\text{quantized}} - V_{\text{in}} = 4.6875 - 4.7 = -0.0125 \text{ V}
$$

This error ($12.5$ mV) lies well within the theoretical bound of $\pm 19.53$ mV, confirming correctness.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — 4–20 mA Current Loop Equivalent Voltage

**Problem Statement:** A differential pressure transmitter measures a flow range of 0–250 inches of water column (in. W.C.). Show that converting the 4–20 mA signal across a precision $250 \, \Omega$ resistor yields a 1–5 V signal.

**Step 1:** Express current in terms of process variable $P$.
The standard linear transfer function is:
$$
I(P) = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{P}{P_{\max}}
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate at endpoints.
- At $P = 0$: $I = 4$ mA (live zero)
- At $P = 250$ in. W.C.: $I = 4 + 16 = 20$ mA (full scale)

**Step 3:** Apply Ohm's law across the burden resistor $R = 250 \, \Omega$.
$$
V(P) = I(P) \cdot R = \left(4 + 16 \cdot \frac{P}{P_{\max}}\right) \times 250 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.
$$
V(P) = 1 + 4 \cdot \frac{P}{P_{\max}} \quad \text{(Volts)}
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate at endpoints.
- At $P = 0$: $V = 1$ V (live zero)
- At $P = P_{\max}$: $V = 1 + 4 = 5$ V (full scale)

This proves the 1–5 V standard is the direct voltage analog of the 4–20 mA current standard when terminated by a 250 Ω resistor — a frequent **2-mark** and **7-mark** exam question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Resolution of an Optical Encoder Disc

**Problem Statement:** A rotary encoder has 1000 slots on its disc. Determine the angular resolution in degrees and the smallest measurable angular displacement.

**Step 1:** Each slot corresponds to one pulse per revolution.
Total pulses per revolution: $N = 1000$.

**Step 2:** Angular resolution.
$$
\theta_{\text{res}} = \frac{360^\circ}{N} = \frac{360^\circ}{1000} = 0.36^\circ / \text{pulse}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to radians (useful for control loop analysis).
$$
\theta_{\text{res}} = 0.36 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.006283 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4:** In arcseconds.
$$
\theta_{\text{res}} = 0.36 \times 3600 = 1296'' \text{ per pulse}
$$

This is the foundation of digital position feedback in robotics and CNC machines.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an ADC Architecture

| Converter Type | Speed (samples/sec) | Resolution (typical) | Best Suited For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash ADC** | Up to 10 GS/s | 6–8 bits | Oscilloscopes, radar, video | Exponential hardware ($2^n$ comparators) |
| **Successive Approximation (SAR)** | 0.1–10 MS/s | 12–18 bits | Process control, DAS, multichannel | Sequential; latency of $n$ clocks |
| **Dual-Slope Integrating** | < 100 kS/s | 16–24 bits | Digital voltmeters, panel meters | Very slow; rejects 50/60 Hz noise |
| **Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\Delta$)** | < 1 MS/s (high OSR) | 16–24 bits | Audio, weighing scales, RTDs | Requires complex digital filtering |
| **Pipelined ADC** | 10–500 MS/s | 12–16 bits | Communications, software radio | Pipeline latency (few cycles) |

> [!TIP]
> For **process control** (typical signal bandwidth < 10 Hz, e.g., temperature, pressure, level), the **SAR ADC** is the industry default due to its 12–16 bit resolution, low latency ($< 1$ µs for 12 bits), and deterministic behavior.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Signal Conversion Block Diagram (Complete Control Loop)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal flow from physical process variable to final control element, identifying all conversion stages:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PV[Process Variable PV] --> SENSOR[Sensor / Transducer]
    SENSOR --> RAW[Raw Analog Signal mV or mA]
    RAW --> SC[Signal Conditioner Amplify Filter Linearize]
    SC --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Controller DCS or PLC]
    DSP --> DAC[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    DAC --> OPA[Output Power Amplifier]
    OPA --> FCE[Final Control Element Valve or Drive]
    FCE --> ACT[Actuator Action on Process]
    ACT --> PV
    ADC --> DISP1[Digital Display HMI]
    DAC --> REC[Analog Recorder Chart]
```

**Block Interpretation:**

- **Sensor/Transducer**: Converts physical variable (pressure, temperature) into a low-level electrical signal (e.g., RTD → mV).
- **Signal Conditioner**: Amplifies, filters noise, linearizes, and converts to standard 4–20 mA or 0–10 V.
- **ADC**: Samples and quantizes the conditioned analog signal into an $n$-bit binary word.
- **Digital Controller**: Executes PID or MPC algorithm; outputs digital setpoint command.
- **DAC**: Converts digital command back to analog voltage or current.
- **Output Power Amplifier**: Boosts the DAC output to drive actuators (e.g., 24 V coil, 4–20 mA to I/P converter).
- **Final Control Element**: Control valve, VFD, damper, etc.

---

## 4.2 Internal Architecture — Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VIN[Analog Input VIN] --> SAMPLEHOLD[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    SAMPLEHOLD --> COMPARATOR[Comparator]
    VREF[Reference Voltage Vref] --> DACINT[Internal DAC N-bit]
    DACINT --> COMPARATOR
    COMPARATOR --> SARREG[SAR Register]
    SARREG --> OUTREG[Output Latch N-bit]
    OUTREG --> DOUT[Parallel Digital Output D0 to D_n-1]
    SARREG -. Controls .-> DACINT
    CLOCK[External Clock] --> SARREG
    EOC[End of Conversion EOC Flag] --> SARREG
```

**Working Sequence (MSB-First):**

1. On the **Start of Conversion (SOC)** pulse, the SAR register sets the MSB to 1 (all other bits 0) and applies the resulting code to the internal DAC.
2. The comparator checks whether $V_{\text{in}}$ is greater than or less than $V_{\text{DAC}}$.
3. If $V_{\text{in}} > V_{\text{DAC}}$, the MSB remains 1; otherwise, it is reset to 0.
4. The next bit is then tested in the same manner.
5. After $n$ clock cycles, all $n$ bits are resolved, and the **End of Conversion (EOC)** flag is raised.

---

## 4.3 Signal Flow — 4–20 mA Current Loop with HART

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Smart Transmitter Microcontroller] --> DAC2[14-bit DAC]
    DAC2 --> VTOI[Voltage to Current Converter]
    VTOI --> LOOP[2-Wire Current Loop 4 to 20 mA DC]
    HART[HART Modem FSK plus or minus 0.5 mA] --> VTOI
    LOOP --> BURDEN[Burden Resistor 250 Ohm]
    LOOP --> RX[Receiver DCS Input Card]
    BURDEN --> ADC2[16-bit ADC]
    ADC2 --> DCSCPU[DCS CPU]
    DCSCPU --> HMI[Operator HMI]
```

**Key Notes:**

- The **HART signal** is a $1200$ Hz (1) / $2200$ Hz (0) Frequency Shift Keying waveform superimposed on the average DC current.
- It does not disturb the 4–20 mA analog value because it has zero mean.
- The burden resistor converts 4–20 mA → 1–5 V for the ADC.

---

## 4.4 DAC Architecture Comparison — Binary Weighted vs. R-2R Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BINARY["Binary Weighted DAC"]
        B0[Bit 0 Switch] --> R0[R = R]
        B1[Bit 1 Switch] --> R1[R = 2R]
        B2[Bit 2 Switch] --> R2[R = 4R]
        B3[Bit 3 Switch] --> R3[R = 8R]
        R0 --> SUMOP[Summing Op-Amp]
        R1 --> SUMOP
        R2 --> SUMOP
        R3 --> SUMOP
        SUMOP --> VOUT1[Analog Output Vout]
    end
    subgraph R2R["R-2R Ladder DAC"]
        B0R[Bit 0] --> LADDER[R-2R Resistor Network]
        B1R[Bit 1] --> LADDER
        B2R[Bit 2] --> LADDER
        B3R[Bit 3] --> LADDER
        LADDER --> OPAMP2[Op-Amp I to V Converter]
        OPAMP2 --> VOUT2[Analog Output Vout]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **R-2R ladder** is preferred in integrated circuit DACs because it requires only two resistor values ($R$ and $2R$), which is far easier to fabricate monolithically than the wide range ($R, 2R, 4R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$) needed in the binary-weighted network.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Quantization Error" in an ADC. How is it related to the bit-resolution of the converter?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Quantization error is the inherent difference between the actual continuous analog input voltage $V_{\text{in}}$ and its nearest quantized digital representation $V_{\text{quant}}$. It arises because an $n$-bit ADC can resolve only $2^n$ discrete levels within its full-scale range, and any analog value between two adjacent levels is rounded to the nearest level.

For a unipolar ADC with full-scale range $V_{\text{FS}}$ and $n$ bits, the LSB (quantum) size is:
$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n}
$$
The maximum theoretical quantization error, assuming mid-tread quantizer architecture, is half of the LSB:
$$
Q_e = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**Relationship with bit resolution:** $Q_e$ is *inversely* and *exponentially* related to $n$. Each additional bit halves the quantization error (since $2^{n+1} = 2 \cdot 2^n$). For example, for a 10 V ADC, going from 8 bits ($Q_e \approx \pm 19.5$ mV) to 12 bits reduces the error to $\pm 1.22$ mV — a sixteen-fold improvement. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing and how is it prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that a band-limited continuous signal $x(t)$ containing no frequency components above $f_{\text{max}}$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples, provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:
$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}
$$
The minimum theoretical sampling rate, $2 f_{\text{max}}$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

**Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which frequency components above $f_s/2$ "fold back" into the baseband and appear as false lower-frequency components in the reconstructed signal. Mathematically, an input at frequency $f_{\text{in}}$ violating the Nyquist condition appears as:
$$
f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_s - f_{\text{in}} \vert \quad \text{(when } f_{\text{in}} > f_s/2\text{)}
$$

**Prevention methods:** (i) Use a sampling frequency at least 5–10 times $f_{\text{max}}$ (over-sampling), (ii) Insert a low-pass **anti-aliasing filter** (typically a 2nd-order Butterworth) with cutoff at $f_s/2$ before the ADC, (iii) Choose an ADC whose input bandwidth exceeds $f_{\text{max}}$. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. Discuss its speed, resolution, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used in a flow measurement system to digitize the analog signal from a differential pressure transmitter with range 0–500 mmWC. Calculate:
   (i) The quantization step (LSB) in mmWC.
   (ii) The maximum quantization error.
   (iii) The percentage resolution of the converter.
   (iv) The digital output code (in hex) for an input of 275 mmWC. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — SAR ADC Working Principle (7 Marks):**

The SAR ADC is a *binary search converter*. It determines each bit of the output word sequentially, starting with the MSB, by comparing the input voltage against an internally generated analog voltage from a feedback DAC.

**Block Diagram (textual):** [Analog Input] → [Sample & Hold] → [Comparator] ⇐ [Internal DAC] ← [SAR Register] ← [Clock] → [Output Latch] → [Digital Output]

**Step-by-Step Operation:**
1. **SOC (Start of Conversion)** is asserted; the SAR register is cleared, then the MSB (bit $n-1$) is set to 1.
2. The SAR register value is fed to the internal $n$-bit DAC, producing a trial voltage $V_{\text{DAC}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$.
3. The **comparator** checks the polarity: if $V_{\text{in}} > V_{\text{DAC}}$, the MSB remains 1; otherwise, it is reset to 0.
4. The next bit (bit $n-2$) is set to 1, and the comparison is repeated.
5. After $n$ clock cycles, all $n$ bits are determined. The **EOC (End of Conversion)** flag is raised and the data is latched at the output.

**Performance Characteristics:**
- **Resolution:** 8 to 18 bits (typically 12 or 16).
- **Speed:** 0.1 to 10 MS/s.
- **Latency:** Exactly $n$ clock cycles (deterministic).
- **Applications:** Process control, data acquisition systems (DAS), instrumentation, battery-powered sensors.
- **Advantages:** Excellent trade-off between speed, accuracy, and power.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires a precision internal DAC; performance drops at very high speeds.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits; $P_{\max} = 500$ mmWC; $P_{\text{in}} = 275$ mmWC.

**(i) Quantization step (LSB):**
$$
\Delta P = \frac{P_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} = \frac{500}{2^{12}} = \frac{500}{4096} = 0.12207 \text{ mmWC per LSB}
$$
**(1 Mark)**

**(ii) Maximum quantization error:**
$$
Q_e = \pm \frac{\Delta P}{2} = \pm \frac{0.12207}{2} = \pm 0.06104 \text{ mmWC}
$$
**(1.5 Marks)**

**(iii) Percentage resolution:**
$$
\%R = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{100}{4096} = 0.02441 \,\% \text{FS}
$$
**(1.5 Marks)**

**(iv) Digital output for 275 mmWC input:**

Compute the decimal code:
$$
D = \text{round}\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{\Delta P}\right) = \text{round}\left(\frac{275}{0.12207}\right) = \text{round}(2252.8) = 2253_{10}
$$

Convert $2253_{10}$ to hexadecimal:
- $2253 \div 16 = 140$ remainder $13$ → digit D
- $140 \div 16 = 8$ remainder $12$ → digit C
- $8 \div 16 = 0$ remainder $8$ → digit 8

Reading remainders bottom-up: $\boxed{D = 8\text{CD}_{16}}$

Verification: $8\text{CD}_{16} = 8 \times 16^2 + 12 \times 16 + 13 = 2048 + 192 + 13 = 2253$ ✓

Quantized input: $2253 \times 0.12207 = 274.96$ mmWC
Error: $274.96 - 275 = -0.04$ mmWC, within $\pm 0.061$ mmWC tolerance. ✓ **(3 Marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the working of a **4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC**. Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of the digital input and reference voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **4–20 mA current loop standard** in process control. Why is "live zero" (4 mA) used instead of 0 mA? List **two** advantages of current signaling over voltage signaling in industrial environments. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC (7 Marks):**

The R-2R ladder is a resistor network that uses only two resistor values ($R$ and $2R$) to generate a binary-weighted voltage or current proportional to a digital input. Each bit $b_i$ connects its corresponding shunt to either $V_{\text{ref}}$ (logic 1) or ground (logic 0) via an SPDT switch.

**Thevenin Equivalent Reduction (derivation for a 4-bit ladder):**

The R-2R network is analyzed by successively replacing the rightmost T-section with its Thevenin equivalent. At the final stage, the equivalent voltage seen by the op-amp summing junction is a binary-weighted sum:

For a 4-bit input $D = (b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0)_2$:

$$
V_{\text{DAC}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \left( \frac{b_3}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_1}{8} + \frac{b_0}{16} \right)
$$

This can be written compactly as:
$$
V_{\text{DAC}} = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} \quad \text{where} \quad D = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i
$$

In an inverting op-amp configuration with feedback resistor $R_f = R$:
$$
V_o = -V_{\text{DAC}} = -V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}
$$

For $D = 1111_2 = 15$: $V_o = -V_{\text{ref}} \cdot 15/16 \approx -0.9375 \, V_{\text{ref}}$ (max value).
For $D = 0000_2 = 0$: $V_o = 0$ V (min value).

**Advantages:** Uses only two resistor values (easy IC fabrication); monotonicity is guaranteed; excellent linearity for $n \leq 12$ bits. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) — 4–20 mA Current Loop (7 Marks):**

The **4–20 mA current loop** is the international standard (IEC 60381-1) for transmitting analog process variables in industrial plants. A two-wire loop carries a DC current whose magnitude is linearly proportional to the measured variable:

$$
I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{P}{P_{\max}}
$$

**Why "Live Zero" (4 mA) instead of 0 mA?** **(3 Marks)**

A non-zero lower limit provides **fault detection capability**:
- A true reading of 0 % of range produces exactly **4 mA**, not 0 mA.
- If the loop wire breaks, the receiver reads **0 mA** (or saturates low), which is immediately distinguishable from a legitimate zero process value.
- Additionally, the 4 mA baseline is sufficient to **power the transmitter electronics** itself, enabling **two-wire (loop-powered) transmitters** — only two wires are needed to carry both signal and supply power.

**Two Advantages of Current over Voltage Signaling:** **(2 Marks)**

1. **Noise immunity:** Current signals are virtually immune to induced voltages from nearby power cables, motor starters, and VFDs, because the current is determined by the transmitter's output compliance, not by voltage drops along the wire. Long cable runs (hundreds of meters) do not degrade accuracy.

2. **Loop-powered operation:** A single pair of wires both powers the field transmitter (at 24 V DC) and carries the measurement signal. This drastically reduces wiring cost and complexity in large plants with thousands of field instruments.

*(Acceptable alternate advantages: no ground-loop issues; simple intrinsic safety implementation with zener barriers; intrinsically safe for hazardous areas.)* **(2 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Confusing the LSB formula for unipolar vs. bipolar ADCs:** Examiners specifically deduct marks if a student writes $\Delta V = V_{\text{FS}} / (2^n - 1)$ for a unipolar 0–10 V ADC. The correct formula for *unipolar, full-range natural binary* is $\Delta V = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$. The $(2^n - 1)$ form applies to bipolar mid-tread with $V_{\text{FS}} = V_{\text{max}} - V_{\text{min}}$ when the codes include both positive and negative values.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the rounding step in digital code conversion:** Many students write $D = V_{\text{in}} / \Delta V$ as a non-integer (e.g., 120.32) without explicitly stating "round to nearest integer" (120). Examiners require this step.
>
> 3. **Using 0 mA as the "live zero":** A fatal conceptual error. Always write **4 mA** is the live zero; 0 mA indicates a wire break.
>
> 4. **Skipping the LSB unit:** Always write the LSB in the *same units as the process variable* (e.g., "0.122 mmWC per LSB", not just "0.122"). Marks are reserved for unit consistency.
>
> 5. **R-2R DAC sign error:** When using the inverting op-amp configuration, $V_o$ is *negative*. Examiners often deduct if the negative sign is omitted. The sign is a function of the configuration, not of the digital code.
>
> 6. **In hexadecimal/decimal conversion, omitting the verification step:** Examiners award partial credit for the verification, so always back-substitute: $8\text{CD}_{16} = 2253_{10}$ check.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔁 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Analog signal**: continuous in time and amplitude; represented by $x(t)$.
- ✅ **Digital signal**: discrete in time and amplitude; represented by an $n$-bit binary word.
- ✅ **ADC function**: Sensor analog → Sample → Quantize → Encode → Digital word.
- ✅ **DAC function**: Digital word → Binary-weighted analog reconstruction.
- ✅ **LSB (quantum) size:** $\Delta V = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^n$ (unipolar).
- ✅ **Maximum quantization error:** $Q_e = \pm \Delta V / 2$.
- ✅ **% Resolution:** $\%R = 100 / 2^n$ — doubles in accuracy per extra bit.
- ✅ **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ — fundamental for sampling.
- ✅ **Aliasing prevention:** anti-aliasing low-pass filter + oversampling.
- ✅ **4–20 mA current loop standard:** live zero = 4 mA; full scale = 20 mA; span = 16 mA.
- ✅ **1–5 V standard:** derived from 4–20 mA × 250 Ω burden resistor.
- ✅ **HART protocol:** $\pm 0.5$ mA FSK superimposed on 4–20 mA DC.
- ✅ **SAR ADC:** $n$-bit conversion in $n$ clock cycles; $n$ internal DAC + 1 comparator.
- ✅ **Flash ADC:** $2^n - 1$ comparators; fastest, but limited to 6–8 bits.
- ✅ **Dual-slope ADC:** integrates input for fixed time, then de-integrates against $V_{\text{ref}}$; rejects 50/60 Hz mains noise; used in DVMs.
- ✅ **Sigma-delta ($\Sigma\Delta$) ADC:** oversamples and noise-shapes; high resolution (16–24 bits); used for RTDs and weighing scales.
- ✅ **R-2R ladder DAC:** uses only $R$ and $2R$ resistors; ideal for monolithic ICs; $V_o = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot D / 2^n$.
- ✅ **Binary weighted DAC:** requires $2^n$-spread of resistor values; impractical beyond 8 bits.
- ✅ **Encoder resolution:** $\theta = 360^\circ / N$ where $N$ = number of slots/lines.
- ✅ **Industrial "live zero" advantage:** distinguishes wire break from zero process value; enables loop-powered 2-wire transmitters.
- ✅ **Current vs. voltage signaling:** current is noise-immune and supports long cable runs; voltage is simpler but susceptible to IR drop.

### 🎯 Most Frequently Asked KTU Topics (Must-Prepare)

1. **Quantization error derivation** — 3 to 7 marks every semester.
2. **Successive Approximation ADC working + block diagram** — almost guaranteed 7-mark question.
3. **Numerical on LSB, % resolution, digital code conversion** — a 7-mark staple.
4. **4–20 mA current loop + "live zero" justification** — 3 to 7 marks.
5. **Comparison of ADC architectures** (Flash vs. SAR vs. Dual-slope vs. $\Sigma\Delta$) — 7 marks.
6. **R-2R Ladder DAC derivation** — 7 marks.
7. **Nyquist theorem + aliasing** — 3 marks (often combined with anti-aliasing filter question).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators (Electric)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actuators (Electric)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Process Control Instrumentation (PCICT502)**, an **Electric Actuator** is a final control element that converts an electrical control signal (typically a low-power analog current signal of **4–20 mA**, or a voltage signal of **1–5 V / 0–10 V**, or a digital PWM / fieldbus command) into a precisely regulated **mechanical motion** — either linear (translation) or rotary (rotation). This mechanical motion is then used to manipulate a process variable through a downstream device such as a control valve, a damper, a variable-pitch fan blade, a pump stroke adjuster, or a robotic gripper.

Electric actuators form the **muscle layer** of the entire process control loop. While sensors (transmitters) act as the "eyes" of the process and the controller acts as the "brain," the electric actuator is the "hand" that physically enforces the controller's decision on the process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 – Actuators):**
> Electric actuators are electromechanical transducers that receive an electrical input signal from a controller and produce a proportional mechanical output (force, torque, displacement, or angular position) used to drive a final control element.

### 1.2 Key Performance Parameters

- **Force (F)** — measured in **N (Newtons)**
- **Torque (T)** — measured in **N·m (Newton-metres)**
- **Stroke / Linear Travel (L)** — measured in **mm or m**
- **Rotational Travel (θ)** — measured in **degrees (°) or radians (rad)**
- **Speed (v for linear, ω for angular)** — **m/s or rad/s**
- **Power (P)** — measured in **W (Watts)**
- **Duty Cycle (S4 / S6 / S9)** — fraction of time the actuator is energised
- **Ingress Protection (IP) Rating** — IP65 / IP67 / IP68 for hazardous process areas

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Industrial "Muscle"

Imagine a human arm lifting a heavy shopping bag. Your **brain** decides *how high* to lift, your **nerves** carry the electrical command, and your **biceps muscle** physically shortens to lift the bag. In a chemical plant:

| Human Body Part | Industrial Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Brain | PID Controller (PLC / DCS) | Decides the corrective action |
| Nerves | Current Signal (4–20 mA) | Carries the command |
| Muscle | **Electric Actuator** | Produces the physical movement |
| Hand (holding bag) | Control Valve Stem | Manipulates the process fluid |

So when the controller senses that a flow rate is too low, it does **not** touch the pipe itself — it sends a 4–20 mA command to the electric actuator, which rotates its output shaft, which in turn opens the valve further. The actuator is the **only link between the electronic decision and the physical world of pipes, fluids, and chemicals.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Electric" specifically?**
> KTU 2024 Module 1 contrasts electric actuators against pneumatic, hydraulic, and motorized (electro-mechanical) variants. Electric actuators are preferred where **precise positioning**, **cleanliness (no oil leaks)**, **remote control via digital bus**, and **energy efficiency** are required. Common applications include HVAC dampers, small-to-medium control valves, robotics, and assembly lines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Electric Actuators (KTU Module 1 Hierarchy)

```
Electric Actuators
├── 1. Solenoid Actuators        (on/off, discrete)
├── 2. Relay / Contactor coils   (switching, not proportional)
├── 3. DC Motor Actuators        (proportional, continuous)
│       ├── Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC)
│       └── Servo Motor (DC)
├── 4. AC Motor Actuators        (industrial, high power)
│       ├── Single-phase induction
│       ├── Three-phase induction
│       └── Synchronous (BLDC)
├── 5. Stepper Motor Actuators   (open-loop positioning)
└── 6. Piezoelectric Actuators   (micro/nano positioning)
```

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Signal Reception:**
The actuator receives an electrical command. For proportional actuators, this is typically a **4–20 mA current loop** where 4 mA = 0% output and 20 mA = 100% output. The current is converted to a voltage across a precision sense resistor (e.g., **250 Ω** gives 1–5 V).

**Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:**
The conditioned signal is fed to a **motor driver / amplifier** (e.g., H-bridge for DC, VFD for AC). This stage handles power switching using semiconductor devices (MOSFET, IGBT, or thyristor).

**Step 3 — Electromechanical Energy Conversion:**
Current flowing through coils placed in a magnetic field produces a **Lorentz force** (F = BIL) on conductors, or a **torque** (T = k_t · I) on the rotor. This is where electrical energy is converted to mechanical work.

**Step 4 — Mechanical Transmission:**
The rotor's motion is transmitted through **gear trains** (speed reduction, torque multiplication) to the output shaft. For linear actuators, a **lead screw / ball screw** converts rotary motion to linear motion.

**Step 5 — Position Feedback (closed loop):**
A position sensor (potentiometer, LVDT, encoder, or Hall sensor) reports the actual output position back to the controller, completing the closed-loop control. This is critical for **servo actuators**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind gear reduction:** A small DC motor may spin at **10 000 rpm** with low torque. To drive a valve stem needing **high torque at low rpm (say 30 rpm)**, a gear train with ratio ~333:1 is used. This is why most industrial electric valve actuators sound like "whirring" — that is the gear train.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | Lorentz force on a conductor | N | Solenoid force |
| 2 | $T = k_t \cdot I$ | Motor torque from current | N·m | DC / BLDC motor |
| 3 | $E = k_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF induced in armature | V | DC motor model |
| 4 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power output | W | Motor sizing |
| 5 | $V = I \cdot R + k_e \cdot \omega$ | DC motor armature equation | V | Steady-state analysis |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T \cdot \omega}{V \cdot I}$ | Efficiency of actuator | dimensionless | Energy audit |
| 7 | $F = \dfrac{\eta_t \cdot 2\pi \cdot T}{L_{lead}}$ | Linear force from rotary + lead screw | N | Linear actuator |
| 8 | $I_{stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | Stall current of DC motor | A | Starting current |
| 9 | $T_{stall} = k_t \cdot I_{stall} = \dfrac{k_t \cdot V}{R_a}$ | Stall torque | N·m | Maximum torque |
| 10 | $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \dfrac{V}{k_e}$ | No-load speed | rad/s | Top speed |
| 11 | $\tau = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ | Electrical time constant | s | Dynamic response |
| 12 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{J \cdot R_a}{k_t \cdot k_e}$ | Mechanical time constant | s | Dynamic response |
| 13 | $G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{k_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)(\tau_e s + 1)}$ | Transfer function of DC motor | rad/V | Control design |
| 14 | $Step\_Angle = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Stepper motor resolution | degrees | Open-loop positioning |
| 15 | $F_{solenoid} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}$ | Solenoid pull force | N | On/off valve actuation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units to memorise for KTU exams:** Torque in N·m (NOT kgf·m or lb-ft), speed in rad/s for analytical work (convert rpm: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$), power in W (1 hp = 745.7 W).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electric actuators are the **backbone of modern automation**:
- **Oil & Gas:** Electrically actuated ball valves on Christmas trees (replacing pneumatic).
- **HVAC:** Damper actuators controlling airflow in cleanrooms.
- **Pharmaceutical:** BLDC servo motors driving mixing impellers with sterile, oil-free operation.
- **Power Plants:** Stepper motors positioning control rod drives in nuclear reactors.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Actuators with built-in **IO-Link** or **PROFIBUS** communication for predictive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DC Motor Equivalent Circuit and Torque-Speed Characteristic

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 1 for electric actuators. We model a separately-excited (or permanent-magnet) DC motor.

**Step 1 — Write Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) for the armature loop:**

The applied voltage $V$ is dropped across the armature resistance $R_a$ and the back-EMF $E$.

$$
V = I_a \cdot R_a + E
$$

**Step 2 — Express back-EMF in terms of angular velocity:**

The back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity through the motor voltage constant $k_e$.

$$
E = k_e \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 3 — Express developed torque in terms of armature current:**

By the electromechanical duality, torque is proportional to current through the motor torque constant $k_t$.

$$
T = k_t \cdot I_a
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (2) into (1) and solve for current:**

$$
V = I_a \cdot R_a + k_e \cdot \omega
$$

$$
I_a = \frac{V - k_e \cdot \omega}{R_a}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into torque equation (3) to obtain torque-speed line:**

$$
T = k_t \cdot \frac{V - k_e \cdot \omega}{R_a}
$$

$$
T = \underbrace{\frac{k_t \cdot V}{R_a}}_{T_{stall}} - \underbrace{\frac{k_t \cdot k_e}{R_a}}_{slope} \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the two intercepts (boundary conditions):**

- **At $\omega = 0$ (stall):** $T = T_{stall} = \dfrac{k_t \cdot V}{R_a}$ — maximum torque when shaft is locked.
- **At $T = 0$ (no-load):** $\omega = \omega_{NL} = \dfrac{V}{k_e}$ — maximum speed when no load is applied.

**Step 7 — Derive the linear torque-speed equation (compact form):**

$$
T(\omega) = T_{stall} \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{\omega}{\omega_{NL}} \right)
$$

**Step 8 — Derive the mechanical power output:**

Mechanical power is the product of torque and angular velocity. The maximum power point occurs at the *midpoint* of the line ($\omega = \omega_{NL}/2$, $T = T_{stall}/2$).

$$
P_{mech}(\omega) = T(\omega) \cdot \omega = T_{stall} \cdot \omega - \frac{T_{stall}}{\omega_{NL}} \cdot \omega^2
$$

To find maximum, differentiate with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dP}{d\omega} = T_{stall} - \frac{2 T_{stall}}{\omega_{NL}} \cdot \omega = 0
$$

$$
\omega_{P_{max}} = \frac{\omega_{NL}}{2}, \quad T_{P_{max}} = \frac{T_{stall}}{2}
$$

$$
P_{max} = \frac{T_{stall} \cdot \omega_{NL}}{4} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_a} \cdot \frac{k_t}{k_e}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit Check:** Since $k_t$ has units of N·m/A and $k_e$ has units of V·s/rad, the ratio $k_t / k_e = 1$ in SI units. Therefore, in a consistent SI system, $P_{max} = \dfrac{V^2}{4 R_a}$. This is a useful quick-check identity that often appears as a 2-mark sub-part in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A permanent-magnet DC motor used in a valve actuator has $R_a = 2\ \Omega$, $V = 24\ \text{V}$, $k_e = 0.05\ \text{V·s/rad}$, $k_t = 0.05\ \text{N·m/A}$. Find: (i) no-load speed, (ii) stall torque, (iii) speed at maximum power, (iv) maximum power.

**Solution:**

**(i) No-load speed:**

$$
\omega_{NL} = \frac{V}{k_e} = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Converting to rpm:

$$
N_{NL} = \frac{\omega_{NL} \cdot 60}{2\pi} = \frac{480 \cdot 60}{2\pi} \approx 4584\ \text{rpm}
$$

**(ii) Stall torque:**

$$
T_{stall} = \frac{k_t \cdot V}{R_a} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 24}{2} = 0.6\ \text{N·m}
$$

**(iii) Speed at maximum power:**

$$
\omega_{P_{max}} = \frac{\omega_{NL}}{2} = \frac{480}{2} = 240\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**(iv) Maximum power:**

$$
P_{max} = \frac{T_{stall} \cdot \omega_{NL}}{4} = \frac{0.6 \cdot 480}{4} = 72\ \text{W}
$$

**Cross-check using the SI identity:**

$$
P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_a} = \frac{24^2}{4 \cdot 2} = \frac{576}{8} = 72\ \text{W} \ \checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Electric Actuator

| Process Requirement | Recommended Electric Actuator Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Open / Close a small valve (discrete) | **Solenoid** | Cheap, fast (<50 ms), fails to a defined position |
| Continuous flow control (4–20 mA) | **DC Servo with gear train** | Proportional, accurate, with position feedback |
| High torque at low speed (gate valve) | **3-phase AC induction + gearbox** | Rugged, high torque density |
| Precise open-loop positioning (indexing) | **Stepper motor** | Holds position without feedback |
| Cleanroom / sterile environment | **BLDC servo** | No brush dust, no oil, low maintenance |
| Micro-positioning / valve stem trim | **Piezoelectric stack** | Sub-nanometre resolution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Electric Actuator in a Closed-Loop Control System

The following Mermaid block renders the functional architecture of an electric actuator driven by a PID controller inside a process control loop.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Setpoint SP] --> C[PID Controller]
    B[Process Variable PV from Sensor] --> C
    C --> D[Current Loop 4 to 20 mA Signal]
    D --> E[Motor Driver / Power Amplifier]
    E --> F[DC or AC or Stepper Motor]
    F --> G[Gear Train and Lead Screw]
    G --> H[Output Shaft or Linear Stem]
    H --> I[Final Control Element Valve or Damper]
    I --> J[Process Plant]
    J --> K[Sensor Transmitter]
    K --> B
    H --> FB[Position Feedback LVDT or Encoder]
    FB --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **PID Controller** computes an error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
- The error is transmitted as a **4–20 mA current** to the motor driver.
- The driver powers the **motor**, which drives a **gear train + lead screw**.
- The mechanical output manipulates the **final control element**, altering the **process**.
- The **process** is sensed and the **position feedback** closes the inner loop.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Servo Electric Actuator

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph S1[Input Stage]
        nodeA[Controller Command 4 to 20 mA]
        nodeB[Signal Conditioner]
    end
    subgraph S2[Power Stage]
        nodeC[H Bridge Driver]
        nodeD[DC Servo Motor]
    end
    subgraph S3[Mechanical Stage]
        nodeE[Reduction Gearbox]
        nodeF[Ball Screw]
        nodeG[Linear Output Stem]
    end
    subgraph S4[Feedback Stage]
        nodeH[Position Sensor LVDT]
        nodeI[Speed Sensor Tachogenerator]
    end
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeC
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Torque-Speed Characteristic Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[V Applied Voltage] --> B{Operating Point}
    B --> C[High Torque Low Speed Region]
    B --> D[Midpoint Maximum Power 72 W]
    B --> E[Low Torque High Speed Region]
    C --> F[Stall T equals 0.6 Nm]
    E --> G[No Load 480 rad per s]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid rendering tip:** When copy-pasting this into Obsidian, VS Code, or any Mermaid Live Editor, the diagrams will auto-render. They map the *interactions* between subsystems (which is more useful for control engineering) rather than attempting a physical drawing of mechanical parts.

### 4.4 Functional Comparison Table of Actuator Types

| Property | Solenoid | DC Servo | AC Induction | Stepper | Piezo |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Motion type | Linear, on/off | Rotary/linear, continuous | Rotary, continuous | Rotary, discrete steps | Linear, micro |
| Typical power | <50 W | 5 W – 1 kW | 0.1 – 500 kW | 1 W – 500 W | <10 W |
| Precision | ±0.5 mm | ±0.01 mm | ±0.1 mm (with encoder) | ±0.05° (open loop) | ±0.1 nm |
| Holding torque at rest | None (spring) | Yes (with brake) | Yes | Yes (detent) | Yes |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium | Very high |
| Typical process use | Pilot valves | Control valves | Large valves, pumps | Damper linkages | Valve trim |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define an electric actuator. List any four types of electric actuators used in process control.**

**Model Answer:**

An **electric actuator** is an electromechanical device that converts an electrical control signal (typically a 4–20 mA current, 1–5 V voltage, or a digital command) into a proportional mechanical motion (linear or rotary) used to operate a final control element such as a valve, damper, or pump stroke adjuster in a process control loop.

**Four types of electric actuators:**

1. **Solenoid actuator** – converts current into linear push/pull motion.
2. **DC servo motor actuator** – provides continuous rotary/linear proportional control with closed-loop position feedback.
3. **AC induction motor actuator** – used for high-torque industrial applications like large valves.
4. **Stepper motor actuator** – provides precise open-loop angular positioning in discrete steps.

*(Other acceptable: BLDC motor, piezoelectric actuator.)* — **3 marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**State the mathematical relationship between applied voltage, back-EMF, armature current, and angular velocity in a DC motor used as an actuator.**

**Model Answer:**

For a DC motor used as an electric actuator, Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to the armature circuit gives the fundamental governing equation:

$$
V = I_a R_a + E
$$

where the back-EMF $E$ is proportional to the angular velocity $\omega$ of the rotor:

$$
E = k_e \, \omega
$$

Substituting, the armature current is:

$$
I_a = \frac{V - k_e \, \omega}{R_a}
$$

The developed electromagnetic torque is linearly related to the armature current:

$$
T = k_t \, I_a
$$

Combining these yields the **linear torque-speed characteristic**:

$$
T = \frac{k_t V}{R_a} - \frac{k_t k_e}{R_a} \, \omega
$$

This equation shows that torque decreases linearly as speed increases, defining the actuator's operating envelope. — **3 marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Electric Actuator Fundamentals + DC Motor Analysis**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **solenoid actuator**. Discuss its typical applications and limitations in process control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**

A solenoid actuator consists of a cylindrical **bobbin** (former), around which a coil of insulated copper wire is wound. The bobbin houses a movable **ferromagnetic plunger (armature)**. When the coil is energised, the plunger is pulled into the magnetic field, producing linear motion. A **return spring** restores the plunger to its original de-energised position. The plunger motion is transmitted to the controlled device via a stem or push rod.

**Working:**

- **De-energised state:** Spring holds plunger in rest position; downstream valve is in its default (fail-safe) position.
- **Energised state:** Current flows through the coil, generating a magnetic field along the coil axis. The plunger experiences an axial force $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2}$, where $N$ is the number of turns, $I$ is the current, $A$ is the plunger cross-sectional area, and $g$ is the air-gap length.
- The plunger accelerates, compressing the spring, and actuates the valve.

**Applications:**

- Pilot valves for pneumatic actuators.
- On/off control of small process valves (water, air, hydraulic oil).
- Diesel fuel shut-off solenoids.
- Safety interlocks and emergency shutdown systems (ESD).

**Limitations:**

- Provides only **discrete (on/off) action** — not proportional.
- **No holding force** at rest; requires continuous current or spring return.
- Limited **stroke length** (typically <50 mm).
- Coil heating restricts **duty cycle** to intermittent operation.
- Force varies non-linearly with current and air gap.

**(b)** A PMDC motor used as a valve actuator has the following specifications: armature resistance $R_a = 1.5\ \Omega$, supply voltage $V = 48\ \text{V}$, motor constant $k_t = k_e = 0.08$ (SI units). Determine:
**(i)** No-load speed in rpm, **(ii)** Stall torque, **(iii)** Speed and torque at maximum mechanical power output, **(iv)** Maximum mechanical power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) No-load speed:**

$$
\omega_{NL} = \frac{V}{k_e} = \frac{48}{0.08} = 600\ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
N_{NL} = \frac{\omega_{NL} \cdot 60}{2\pi} = \frac{600 \cdot 60}{2\pi} \approx 5730\ \text{rpm}
$$

**(ii) Stall torque:**

$$
T_{stall} = \frac{k_t \cdot V}{R_a} = \frac{0.08 \cdot 48}{1.5} = 2.56\ \text{N·m}
$$

**(iii) Speed and torque at maximum power:**

The maximum power point lies at the midpoint of the torque-speed line.

$$
\omega_{P_{max}} = \frac{\omega_{NL}}{2} = \frac{600}{2} = 300\ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
T_{P_{max}} = \frac{T_{stall}}{2} = \frac{2.56}{2} = 1.28\ \text{N·m}
$$

**(iv) Maximum power:**

$$
P_{max} = T_{P_{max}} \cdot \omega_{P_{max}} = 1.28 \cdot 300 = 384\ \text{W}
$$

Cross-verification using the SI identity:

$$
P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_a} = \frac{48^2}{4 \cdot 1.5} = \frac{2304}{6} = 384\ \text{W} \ \checkmark
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Stepper / Servo + Selection-Based Design**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the working principles of **stepper motors** and **DC servo motors** as electric actuators. Under what process control applications would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Stepper Motor | DC Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Rotates in discrete angular steps ($Step\ Angle = 360°/N$) as pulses are applied to stator coils. | Rotates continuously; position is controlled by varying armature voltage/current. |
| Feedback | **Open loop** — no position sensor normally required. | **Closed loop** — requires encoder, resolver, or LVDT feedback. |
| Holding torque | Holds position at rest due to detent torque. | Requires brake or continuous energisation to hold. |
| Resolution | Determined by steps/rev (e.g., 200 steps = 1.8°). | Determined by encoder resolution (e.g., 4096 counts/rev). |
| Response speed | Lower (limited by step rate). | High (limited by motor and load inertia). |
| Cost & complexity | Lower (no feedback needed). | Higher (controller + feedback required). |
| Failure mode | Open-loop: can lose steps under overload → silent positional error. | Closed-loop: detects error and corrects → safer. |

**Application preference:**

- **Choose Stepper:** When the load is light and predictable, position is required only at discrete setpoints, and the cost of feedback electronics is unjustified. Examples: small damper linkages, valve indexing in HVAC, instrument panel pointers, plotter drives.
- **Choose DC Servo:** When high accuracy, dynamic response, and reliability under varying load are essential. Examples: control valves in petrochemical plants, CNC machine tool drives, robotic arms in assembly lines, paper mill tension control.

**(b)** A linear electric actuator drives a control valve stem through a ball screw with lead $L = 5\ \text{mm/rev}$. The motor delivers a torque of $T = 2\ \text{N·m}$ with an efficiency of $\eta_t = 0.85$. Calculate the linear force developed on the valve stem. If the valve must move 40 mm to fully open, how many motor revolutions are required, and what is the actuator's linear speed if the motor rotates at 1500 rpm? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Linear force from the ball screw:**

$$
F = \frac{\eta_t \cdot 2\pi \cdot T}{L_{lead}}
$$

Convert lead to metres: $L = 5\ \text{mm} = 0.005\ \text{m}$.

$$
F = \frac{0.85 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 2}{0.005} = \frac{10.68}{0.005} \approx 2136\ \text{N} \ (\approx 2.14\ \text{kN})
$$

**(ii) Number of motor revolutions for 40 mm travel:**

Each revolution advances the stem by 5 mm.

$$
N_{rev} = \frac{\text{Travel}}{\text{Lead}} = \frac{40\ \text{mm}}{5\ \text{mm/rev}} = 8\ \text{revolutions}
$$

**(iii) Linear speed at 1500 rpm:**

Convert rpm to rev/s: $f = 1500 / 60 = 25\ \text{rev/s}$.

$$
v = f \cdot L = 25\ \text{rev/s} \cdot 0.005\ \text{m/rev} = 0.125\ \text{m/s} = 125\ \text{mm/s}
$$

**Verification using the formula:**

$$
v = \frac{\omega \cdot L}{2\pi} = \frac{(2\pi \cdot 25) \cdot 0.005}{2\pi} = 0.125\ \text{m/s} \ \checkmark
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Electric Actuator Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion between rad/s and rpm.** Always convert to rad/s before using the formula $P = T \omega$. Many students write $P = T \cdot N$ and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the back-EMF term.** At no load, students often write $I = V/R_a$, but the correct no-load current is small but non-zero (to overcome friction).
> 3. **Solenoid force sign.** The solenoid pull force $F \propto 1/g^2$ — students must state that force is **highest when the air gap $g$ is smallest** (plunger fully seated).
> 4. **Stepper "open-loop" misconception.** Students write "stepper motors don't need feedback." While true *normally*, the examiner expects the qualifier "under normal loading conditions" — overloaded steppers **can lose steps silently**.
> 5. **4–20 mA conversion.** Forgetting that 4 mA = 0%, not 0 mA. This is the **"live zero"** concept.
> 6. **Ball-screw lead vs pitch.** Lead is the *distance advanced per revolution*; pitch is the *distance between adjacent thread crests*. For single-start screws they are equal; for multi-start screws they are not.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] An **electric actuator** converts **electrical energy → mechanical motion**; it is the "muscle" of the process control loop.
- [x] Major types: **Solenoid, DC Servo, AC Induction, Stepper, BLDC, Piezoelectric** — selection depends on precision, power, and control mode.
- [x] The **4–20 mA current loop** is the industry-standard analog signal; 4 mA = 0% and 20 mA = 100% output.
- [x] Governing DC motor equations: $V = I_a R_a + k_e \omega$ and $T = k_t I_a$, with the **linear torque-speed characteristic** as the most asked concept.
- [x] In SI units, **$k_t = k_e$** — a useful identity that simplifies numerical problems.
- [x] Maximum mechanical power is delivered at **half the no-load speed and half the stall torque**, equal to $V^2 / (4 R_a)$.
- [x] **Gear trains + lead screws** convert high-speed, low-torque motor output to low-speed, high-force linear output needed for valve actuation.
- [x] **Stepper motors = open-loop positioning; DC servos = closed-loop positioning** — choose stepper for low-cost indexing, servo for high-precision dynamic control.
- [x] Solenoid force $F = \mu_0 N^2 I^2 A / (2 g^2)$ — inversely proportional to the **square** of the air gap.
- [x] Always convert **rpm → rad/s** using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ before applying $P = T \omega$.
- [x] The **duty cycle** (S4, S6, S9 standards) limits the ON-time of an actuator to prevent coil overheating.
- [x] Modern electric actuators integrate **IO-Link / PROFIBUS / HART** protocols for Industry 4.0 diagnostics — this is a high-weight KTU 2024 topic.
- [x] IP65/IP67/IP68 ratings dictate suitability for **hazardous area** (Zone 1/Zone 2) installations in oil & gas and chemical plants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic and Hydraulic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic and Hydraulic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pneumatic Systems — Formal Definition

A **Pneumatic System** in process control instrumentation is a closed-loop or open-loop control/actuation network that uses a **compressible gas** (typically filtered, dried, and lubricated compressed air at a standard supply pressure of **20 psig (≈ 1.4 kg/cm²)**) as the working/transmitting medium to convey control signals, generate mechanical force, or actuate final control elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> Pneumatic systems are employed in hazardous (explosive/flammable) process environments because they are intrinsically **spark-free**, intrinsically safe, and tolerant of high ambient temperatures up to **≈ 60 °C** without derating.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Air-Mail" Intuition

Imagine a corporate office building where memos (control signals) cannot be sent electronically because of an EMP storm (hazardous area). The company falls back to a **pneumatic tube system** — a network of metal pipes carrying pressurized air capsules between departments. The air (gas) is the messenger; the capsule (signal) is the message. Similarly, in a chemical refinery, a controller outputs a 3–15 psig air signal through copper/stainless tubing to a control valve thousands of meters away. No electricity, no spark, no ignition risk.

> [!NOTE]
> The universally accepted **pneumatic signal standard** is **3–15 psig** (0.2–1.0 kg/cm²), which is a **live-zero** standard. The "3" is a true zero — any pressure below 3 psig indicates a failed/safe-state signal, which a fail-safe controller can detect.

## 1.2 Hydraulic Systems — Formal Definition

A **Hydraulic System** is a power-transmission and actuation network that uses an **incompressible liquid** (typically petroleum-based hydraulic oil, water-glycol mixture, or synthetic fire-resistant fluid) under high static pressure as the working medium to generate large mechanical forces with high stiffness and rapid dynamic response.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Heavy-Lift Robot Arm" Intuition

Picture a robotic arm in a steel rolling mill that must bend a 50 mm thick steel plate. The motor's electrical power is far too small to drive the arm directly. Instead, a small electrical signal commands a **hydraulic pump** to push thick, oily fluid through a steel hose to a large **hydraulic cylinder**. Because the fluid is essentially incompressible, the cylinder pushes back with thousands of newtons of force, bending the plate as if it were cardboard. Air (pneumatic) would compress and "give up" — oil (hydraulic) does not.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why oil instead of water?**
> - **Viscosity stability** across temperature (water becomes too thin at 60 °C, too viscous near 0 °C).
> - **Lubricity** — water corrodes and lacks the film strength to protect pump vanes and pistons.
> - **Fire-resistance variants** (water-glycol, phosphate esters) are mandated in mining, aerospace, and furnace zones.
> - The standard hydraulic supply pressure is **≈ 1000–3000 psig (≈ 70–210 kg/cm²)**, roughly **100× higher** than pneumatic supply.

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Pneumatic | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Compressed air / inert gas | Hydraulic oil (incompressible) |
| Compressibility | High (≈ 0.1 % at 100 psig per unit ΔP) | Negligible (bulk modulus ≈ 2 GPa) |
| Standard supply | **20 psig (1.4 kg/cm²)** | **1000–3000 psig (70–210 kg/cm²)** |
| Standard signal | **3–15 psig** | **4–20 mA → I/P → 3–15 psig** |
| Force output | Low (≤ 5000 N typical) | Very high (≥ 10⁵ N) |
| Speed of response | Slow (gas compressibility dominates) | Very fast (fluid is stiff) |
| Intrinsic safety | **Excellent (spark-free)** | Moderate (leakage + heat) |
| Typical use | Control valve actuators, signal transmission | Heavy positioning, forging presses, CNC |

> [!NOTE]
> In a modern KTU-relevant DCS/PLC loop, a **4–20 mA** electronic signal from the controller is first converted to **3–15 psig** pneumatic by an **I/P (Current-to-Pressure) Transducer**, and then sent to the pneumatic actuator. This hybrid interface is the heart of "electronic control — pneumatic actuation" systems, which dominate process industries worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pneumatic System Architecture — Component Map

A standard pneumatic control loop (electronic controller + pneumatic actuator) consists of the following functional blocks in series:

1. **Electronic Controller** (PID, 4–20 mA output)
2. **I/P Transducer** (Current-to-Pressure converter)
3. **Pneumatic Relay (Volume Booster / Pilot Relay)**
4. **Pneumatic Transmission Line** (¼" or ½" OD copper/stainless tube)
5. **Actuator Diaphragm / Piston Chamber** (force-generating element)
6. **Final Control Element** (control valve stem)
7. **Position Feedback** (optional, for valve positioner)

## 2.2 The Flapper–Nozzle Amplifier — The "Heart" of Pneumatic Control

The flapper–nozzle is the **fundamental pneumatic error-detecting and amplifying element**. It is a displacement-to-pressure transducer of exceptionally high gain.

### Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. A precisely restricted orifice (≈ 0.01" diameter) supplies a constant back-pressure air bleed to the **nozzle chamber**.
2. The **flapper** is a flat metal lever that pivots in front of the nozzle, modulating the **nozzle back-pressure** $P_b$.
3. The gap $x$ between flapper and nozzle (typically 0.005"–0.015") controls the back-pressure $P_b$.
4. As the controller output displaces the flapper **closer** to the nozzle, the gap $x$ **decreases** → back-pressure $P_b$ **increases** sharply.
5. The back-pressure $P_b$ is then routed to a **pneumatic relay** (e.g., a 1:1 or higher relay), which provides the volume and pressure capacity to drive an actuator.

### Mathematical Model of the Flapper–Nozzle

The nozzle–flapper behaves as a **variable restrictor** in series with a **fixed restrictor** (the supply orifice). Applying the adiabatic flow equation and linearizing around the operating point yields the **linearized transfer function**:

$$P_b(s) = \frac{K_n}{1 + \tau_n s} \cdot X(s)$$

Where:
- $K_n$ = flapper–nozzle static gain (typically **400–1000 psig/in**)
- $\tau_n$ = flapper–nozzle time constant (typically **≈ 0.01 s**, often neglected)
- $X(s)$ = flapper displacement (inches, in Laplace domain)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flapper–nozzle has an **extremely high gain** ($K_n$) but very little **power** (flow capacity). Therefore, it must ALWAYS be followed by a **pneumatic relay** (power amplifier) before the signal can drive an actuator. The relay provides both pressure gain AND flow (volume) gain.

## 2.3 Pneumatic Relay (Volume Booster) — The "Muscle"

The most common relay is the **non-bleed type** with a flat disk/diaphragm valve pair. The relay:
- Accepts a low-flow control signal $P_b$ from the flapper–nozzle.
- Modulates the supply-air inlet valve and exhaust valve.
- Produces a high-flow output $P_o$ at the same pressure level as $P_b$.

For an ideal relay:

$$P_o = P_b \quad \text{(unity pressure gain, but flow gain} \approx 10\text{–}50)$$

## 2.4 Hydraulic Servo System — Force Balance

A single-stage hydraulic servo valve (e.g., spool valve driven by a torque motor) is described by the **force-balance equation** at the spool:

$$F_{m} - F_{f} = m \frac{d^{2}x_v}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{dx_v}{dt} + K x_v + F_{L}$$

Where:
- $F_m$ = electromagnetic force from torque motor
- $F_f$ = feedback force (mechanical or hydraulic)
- $x_v$ = spool displacement
- $m, B, K$ = spool mass, damping, centering spring stiffness
- $F_L$ = load force

In the **frequency domain**, the linearized transfer function of a hydraulic actuator is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{X_v(s)} = \frac{K_v / A}{s \left( \frac{s^2}{\omega_h^2} + \frac{2\zeta_h}{\omega_h} s + 1 \right)}$$

Where:
- $K_v$ = valve flow gain (m³/s per m of spool travel)
- $A$ = piston area
- $\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta A^{2}}{V m}}$ = hydraulic natural frequency
- $\beta$ = bulk modulus of oil (≈ **1.4–2.0 GPa**)
- $V$ = total oil volume in cylinder + line
- $\zeta_h$ = damping ratio

## 2.5 I/P Transducer (Current-to-Pressure Converter)

The I/P is the **interface device** that converts a **4–20 mA** electronic signal into a proportional **3–15 psig** pneumatic signal. The most common design is the **force-balance type**:

1. The 4–20 mA current passes through a coil, producing a magnetic force on a beam.
2. The beam carries a flapper opposite a nozzle.
3. The nozzle back-pressure $P_b$ feeds a relay.
4. The relay output $P_o$ is fed back to a bellows that produces a **counter-force** on the beam.
5. At force equilibrium: $P_o \propto I$, i.e., **3 psig at 4 mA, 15 psig at 20 mA**.

The transfer function (simplified):

$$P_o = K_{I/P} (I - 4) + 3 \quad \text{[psig]}$$

Where $K_{I/P} = \dfrac{12 \text{ psig}}{16 \text{ mA}} = 0.75$ **psig/mA**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flapper–nozzle gain | $P_b = K_n X$ | psig / in | $K_n$ ≈ 400–1000 |
| 2 | Flapper–nozzle TF | $\frac{P_b(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K_n}{1 + \tau_n s}$ | psig/in | $\tau_n$ ≈ 0.01 s |
| 3 | I/P linear relation | $P_o = 0.75(I - 4) + 3$ | psig | Live-zero basis |
| 4 | Pneumatic signal span | $15 - 3 = 12$ psig | psig | Universally used |
| 5 | Hydraulic natural freq. | $\omega_h = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \beta A^2}{V m}}$ | rad/s | $\beta$ ≈ 1.7 GPa (oil) |
| 6 | Hydraulic damping | $\zeta_h = \dfrac{B}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{V}{4 \beta A^{2} m}}$ | — | Depends on leakage |
| 7 | Force on diaphragm | $F = P \cdot A$ | N | $A$ = effective area |
| 8 | Spring rate of diag. | $F = K_s \cdot x$ | N/m | $K_s$ from mfr. data sheet |
| 9 | Pressure–current conv. | $I = 4 + \dfrac{P_o - 3}{0.75}$ | mA | Reverse of I/P |
| 10 | Relay pressure gain | $P_o \approx P_b$ (unity) | psig | Flow gain > 1 |
| 11 | Nozzle flow (choked) | $\dot{m} = C_d A_n P_s \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R T_s} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}}^{\!\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | kg/s | Sonic at nozzle throat |
