| 12 | Oil bulk modulus | $\beta \approx 1.4$–$2.0$ GPa | Pa | Temperature-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU numericals, the **3–15 psig → 4–20 mA** and **4–20 mA → 3–15 psig** conversions are tested every year. Memorize the slope **0.75 psig/mA** and **1.333 mA/psig**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil refineries & petrochemicals**: 3–15 psig signals are routed to control valves on distillation columns.
- **Steel mills**: Hydraulic servo systems drive rolling-mill stand cylinders (forces up to 10⁷ N).
- **Aerospace**: Hydraulic actuators move flight control surfaces (forces up to 10⁵ N, response < 20 ms).
- **Pharmaceutical**: Pneumatic diaphragm pumps deliver sterile, contamination-free fluids.
- **Nuclear plants**: Pneumatic systems act as the **fail-safe** backup in case of electrical failure (air bleeds to atmosphere, valves close).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Linearized Gain of the Flapper–Nozzle Amplifier

### Step 1: Define the geometry and pressures
Let:
- $P_s$ = supply pressure (constant, **20 psig**)
- $P_b$ = nozzle back-pressure (the controlled output)
- $P_a$ = atmospheric pressure (the nozzle discharges to ambient)
- $x$ = flapper-to-nozzle gap (variable)
- $A_n$ = nozzle throat area
- $A_o$ = supply orifice area (fixed)
- $R_n(x) = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r_n^{4}}$ + turbulent term for the **annular leak** between flapper and nozzle
- $R_o$ = resistance of the fixed supply orifice (constant)

### Step 2: Apply mass / flow conservation
In steady state, mass flow through the supply orifice **equals** mass flow through the flapper-nozzle leak:

$$Q_s = Q_n$$

For isothermal air, expressed in terms of pressures (assuming small pressure drops), the linearized form is:

$$\frac{P_s - P_b}{R_o} = \frac{P_b - P_a}{R_n(x)}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $P_b$
Cross-multiplying:

$$(P_s - P_b) \cdot R_n(x) = (P_b - P_a) \cdot R_o$$

$$P_s R_n(x) - P_b R_n(x) = P_b R_o - P_a R_o$$

$$P_s R_n(x) + P_a R_o = P_b (R_o + R_n(x))$$

$$P_b = \frac{P_s R_n(x) + P_a R_o}{R_o + R_n(x)}$$

### Step 4: Linearize around the operating point
Let the operating point be $P_b = P_{b0}$ at $x = x_0$, with $R_n = R_{n0}$. Taking the partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial P_b}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial R_n}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{R_o (P_s - P_a)}{(R_o + R_n)^2}$$

Define the static gain $K_n$:

$$K_n = \left. \frac{\partial P_b}{\partial x} \right|_{x = x_0} = \frac{R_o (P_s - P_a)}{(R_o + R_{n0})^2} \cdot \frac{\partial R_n}{\partial x}$$

Empirically, this yields $K_n \in [400, 1000]$ **psig/inch**.

### Step 5: Final transfer function (with dynamics)
Including the small pneumatic time constant $\tau_n$ (resistance–capacitance of the nozzle chamber):

$$\frac{P_b(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K_n}{1 + \tau_n s}$$

This completes the linearized derivation. In most KTU problems, $\tau_n$ is neglected and the gain is treated as a pure constant.

## 3.2 Derivation — Hydraulic Natural Frequency $\omega_h$

### Step 1: Continuity equation for oil flow
The flow $Q$ into a hydraulic cylinder of piston area $A$ produces a piston velocity:

$$Q = A \frac{dy}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{1}{A} \int Q\, dt$$

### Step 2: Oil compressibility
For an oil volume $V$ at pressure $P$, the compressibility relation is:

$$\frac{dV}{dP} = -\frac{V}{\beta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta V = -\frac{V}{\beta} \Delta P$$

### Step 3: Total effective flow into the cylinder chamber
Net flow into the chamber = piston displacement flow + compression flow:

$$Q = A \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{V}{\beta} \frac{dP_L}{dt}$$

### Step 4: Force balance on the piston
Pressure $P_L$ acts on the piston area $A$ against the load spring $K$ and damping $B$:

$$P_L A = m \frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{dy}{dt} + K y + F_L$$

### Step 5: Combine and solve
Eliminating $P_L$ and applying the Laplace transform (assuming no load $F_L = 0$ and $K = 0$ for free piston), the transfer function between piston position $Y$ and valve spool displacement $X_v$ (with flow $Q = K_v X_v$) is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{X_v(s)} = \frac{K_v / A}{s \left( \frac{s^{2}}{\omega_h^{2}} + \frac{2 \zeta_h}{\omega_h} s + 1 \right)}$$

Where the **hydraulic natural frequency** is:

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 \beta A^{2}}{V m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **4** comes from the symmetric two-sided piston (pressure acts on both sides of the piston, doubling the effective stiffness contribution from compression of oil on both sides). For a single-rod asymmetric cylinder, replace 4 by 1.

### Step 3.3 — Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A hydraulic actuator has piston area $A = 0.005 \, \text{m}^2$, oil volume $V = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3$, and piston mass $m = 10 \, \text{kg}$. Oil bulk modulus $\beta = 1.7 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$. Calculate the natural frequency.

**Solution:**

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times (1.7 \times 10^9) \times (0.005)^2}{(2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10}}$$

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.7 \times 10^9 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{1.7 \times 10^5}{2 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{8.5 \times 10^7} = 9219 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_h = \frac{\omega_h}{2\pi} \approx 1467 \, \text{Hz}$$

This extremely high natural frequency is why hydraulic servos are prized for **fast, stiff** motion control.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Criterion | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Winner (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of response | Slow (gas compliance) | Fast (oil incompressibility) | **Hydraulic** |
| Force output | Limited | Massive | **Hydraulic** |
| Intrinsic safety | Excellent (no spark) | Moderate (leak/heat) | **Pneumatic** |
| Cost of installation | Low | High (precision machining) | **Pneumatic** |
| Precision positioning | Moderate | High (stiff) | **Hydraulic** |
| Maintenance burden | Low (air is free) | High (oil, filters, seals) | **Pneumatic** |
| Operating temperature | Wider range | Narrower (oil viscosity) | **Pneumatic** |
| Power density | Low | Very high | **Hydraulic** |
| Noise level | Noisy (exhaust) | Quiet | **Hydraulic** |
| Failure mode | Fail-safe (air bleeds) | Fail-as-is (locked) | **Pneumatic** |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Compare pneumatic and hydraulic actuation systems on the basis of working fluid, signal standard, force output, and intrinsic safety.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Working fluid:** Pneumatic — compressed air (compressible, 20 psig supply); Hydraulic — oil (incompressible, 1000–3000 psig supply).
- **Signal standard:** Pneumatic — 3–15 psig; Hydraulic — derived from 4–20 mA via I/P converter.
- **Force output:** Pneumatic — limited (≤ 5000 N); Hydraulic — very high (≥ 10⁵ N).
- **Intrinsic safety:** Pneumatic — excellent (no electrical spark, no ignition source); Hydraulic — moderate (leakage, hot oil hazards).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Explain the role of the flapper–nozzle amplifier in a pneumatic controller. Why must it always be followed by a pneumatic relay?*

**Model Answer:**
- The flapper–nozzle is the **error-detecting and pressure-amplifying** element. A small flapper displacement (inches) modulates the nozzle back-pressure $P_b$ (psig), providing very high static gain ($K_n$ ≈ 400–1000 psig/in) — i.e., a tiny mechanical motion is converted into a large pneumatic pressure change.
- However, the flapper–nozzle has **negligible flow capacity**; it cannot deliver the volume of air needed to move a diaphragm actuator.
- The **pneumatic relay** (volume booster) provides the necessary **flow (volume) gain** (typically 10–50×) to drive the actuator at the same pressure level, making the combination a true **power amplifier**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Question, with Internal Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of an I/P (Current-to-Pressure) transducer of force-balance type. Derive the input-output relationship $P_o = 0.75(I - 4) + 3$.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A pneumatic controller output of 9 psig is to be sent to a diaphragm actuator through 50 m of ¼" OD copper tubing. The actuator diaphragm has effective area $A = 0.005 \, \text{m}^2$ and spring constant $K_s = 5000 \, \text{N/m}$. Calculate the actuator stem displacement. If the I/P transducer receives 12 mA, what will be the displacement?*

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Operation (Block Diagram, see Section 4.2 above):**

1. The 4–20 mA DC current from the controller flows through an electromagnetic coil wound on a flexure beam.
2. The coil produces a magnetic force proportional to $I$, which deflects the beam downward.
3. The beam carries a flapper positioned opposite a nozzle with a fixed supply of 20 psig air.
4. The deflection reduces the flapper–nozzle gap $x$, raising the nozzle back-pressure $P_b$.
5. $P_b$ is routed to a pneumatic relay, which produces a high-capacity output $P_o$.
6. The output $P_o$ is fed back to a bellows that generates a counter-force on the beam, opposing the magnetic force.
7. At force equilibrium: magnetic force = bellows counter-force.

**Derivation:**

Let $F_m = K_1 I$ (electromagnetic force per mA)
Let $F_b = K_2 P_o$ (bellows counter-force per psig)

At equilibrium: $F_m = F_b$

$$K_1 I = K_2 P_o \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_o = \frac{K_1}{K_2} I$$

Applying the **boundary conditions**:
- At $I = 4$ mA, $P_o = 3$ psig
- At $I = 20$ mA, $P_o = 15$ psig

Solving the linear equation through these two points:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{15 - 3}{20 - 4} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75 \text{ psig/mA}$$

$$P_o - 3 = 0.75 (I - 4)$$

$$\boxed{P_o = 0.75 (I - 4) + 3}$$

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — Pneumatic signal at the actuator:**
The pressure at the actuator is given as **9 psig** (we assume no line loss, as is standard in KTU problems unless otherwise stated).

**Step 2 — Convert psig to Pa:**
$$P = 9 \text{ psig} \times 6894.76 \, \text{Pa/psig} = 62{,}052.84 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Force on the diaphragm:**
$$F = P \cdot A = 62{,}052.84 \times 0.005 = 310.26 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Stem displacement (force balance with spring):**
$$F = K_s \cdot x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{F}{K_s} = \frac{310.26}{5000} = 0.062 \, \text{m} = 62 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Displacement when I/P receives 12 mA:**
Using the derived I/P equation:

$$P_o = 0.75 (12 - 4) + 3 = 0.75 \times 8 + 3 = 6 + 3 = 9 \, \text{psig}$$

**Step 6 — Same force and displacement calculation:**
$$x = 0.062 \, \text{m} = 62 \, \text{mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The pneumatic signal of 9 psig (whether from the controller or derived from 12 mA via I/P) produces a **62 mm stem displacement**. This demonstrates the **interchangeability** of pneumatic and electronic signals through the I/P interface.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain with a block diagram the operation of a hydraulic servo system. Derive the expression for the natural frequency $\omega_h = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \beta A^{2}}{V m}}$.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A hydraulic servo system has piston area $A = 0.003 \, \text{m}^2$, oil volume $V = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3$, piston mass $m = 15 \, \text{kg}$, and bulk modulus $\beta = 1.5 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa}$. Calculate the natural frequency $\omega_h$ and natural frequency $f_h$ in Hz.*

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Block Diagram (see Section 4.3 above):** The system consists of:
1. **Command signal** → **Torque motor** → **Spool valve** → **Hydraulic cylinder** → **Load** → **LVDT feedback** → **Error detector** → back to torque motor.

**Derivation of $\omega_h$:**

The continuity equation for oil flow into the cylinder chamber is:

$$Q = A \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{V}{\beta} \frac{dP_L}{dt}$$

The force balance on the piston (assuming no load spring and no load force):

$$P_L A = m \frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + B \frac{dy}{dt}$$

Taking the Laplace transform of the force equation:

$$P_L A = m s^{2} Y + B s Y = Y(m s^{2} + B s)$$

$$P_L = Y \frac{s(m s + B)}{A}$$

Taking the Laplace transform of the continuity equation:

$$Q = A s Y + \frac{V}{\beta} s P_L = s \left( A Y + \frac{V}{\beta} P_L \right)$$

Substituting $P_L$ from the force equation:

$$Q = s \left( A Y + \frac{V}{\beta} \cdot \frac{s(m s + B)}{A} Y \right)$$

$$Q = s Y \left( A + \frac{V s (m s + B)}{\beta A} \right)$$

$$Q = s Y \cdot \frac{\beta A^{2} + V s (m s + B)}{\beta A}$$

Now substituting $Q = K_v X_v$ (valve flow gain × spool displacement):

$$\frac{K_v X_v}{s Y} = \frac{\beta A^{2} + V s (m s + B)}{\beta A}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{Y}{X_v} = \frac{K_v / A}{s \left( \frac{V m}{\beta A^{2}} s^{2} + \frac{V B}{\beta A^{2}} s + 1 \right)}$$

Writing in standard second-order form:

$$\frac{Y}{X_v} = \frac{K_v / A}{s \left( \frac{s^{2}}{\omega_h^{2}} + \frac{2 \zeta_h}{\omega_h} s + 1 \right)}$$

Comparing coefficients of $s^{2}$:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_h^{2}} = \frac{V m}{\beta A^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{\beta A^{2}}{V m}}}$$

(The factor of 4 in some references arises when both chambers of a double-rod symmetric cylinder are considered, doubling the effective oil compliance term. For a single-rod asymmetric cylinder, the form above applies.)

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Given:**
- $A = 0.003 \, \text{m}^{2}$
- $V = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}$
- $m = 15 \, \text{kg}$
- $\beta = 1.5 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa}$

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta A^{2}$:**

$$\beta A^{2} = (1.5 \times 10^{9}) \times (0.003)^{2} = (1.5 \times 10^{9}) \times (9 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.35 \times 10^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V m$:**

$$V m = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 15 = 7.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\omega_h$:**

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\frac{1.35 \times 10^{4}}{7.5 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\omega_h = 1341.64 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $f_h$ in Hz:**

$$f_h = \frac{\omega_h}{2 \pi} = \frac{1341.64}{6.2832} \approx 213.5 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Conclusion:** The hydraulic natural frequency is **≈ 1342 rad/s (≈ 213.5 Hz)**, indicating a very fast, stiff actuation system — characteristic of high-performance hydraulic servos used in aerospace and machine tools.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the live-zero offset** — Writing $P_o = 0.75 I$ instead of $P_o = 0.75(I-4) + 3$ will cost **all 3 marks** allocated to that part.
> 2. **Mixing psig with psia or Pa** — 1 psig = 6894.76 Pa. KTU expects unit conversions to be shown explicitly; missing this loses **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing gain with flow** — The flapper–nozzle has pressure gain but no flow gain. The relay has flow gain but unity pressure gain. Writing "the nozzle drives the actuator" loses **2 marks** (the relay is essential).
> 4. **Skipping the continuity equation** in hydraulic derivations — The derivation of $\omega_h$ MUST begin with the continuity equation $Q = A\dot{y} + (V/\beta)\dot{P}$; starting from "force balance" alone is incomplete.
> 5. **Missing the factor-of-4 clarification** — For a double-rod symmetric cylinder, $\omega_h = \sqrt{4\beta A^2/(Vm)}$; for a single-rod asymmetric cylinder, it is $\sqrt{\beta A^{2}/(Vm)}$. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if the cylinder type is not specified.
> 6. **Ignoring the assumption of no line loss** — In pneumatic transmission, KTU problems typically assume the line has negligible volume and resistance, so that the controller output pressure equals the actuator pressure. Students who add complex line dynamics without prompt lose **2 marks** for over-engineering.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Pneumatic** systems use **compressed air** (compressible) at **20 psig** supply, transmitting **3–15 psig** as the standard control signal — intrinsically safe, fail-safe by venting.
- [x] **Hydraulic** systems use **incompressible oil** at **1000–3000 psig** supply, offering very high force, stiffness, and bandwidth (natural frequencies of 100s of Hz).
- [x] The **flapper–nozzle amplifier** is the universal pneumatic error-detector; its static gain $K_n$ is **400–1000 psig/inch**.
- [x] The **pneumatic relay (volume booster)** MUST follow the flapper–nozzle to provide flow (volume) gain; pressure gain is approximately unity.
- [x] The **I/P transducer** converts **4–20 mA** to **3–15 psig** via the linear equation: $P_o = 0.75 (I - 4) + 3$.
- [x] The **hydraulic natural frequency** is $\omega_h = \sqrt{\beta A^{2} / (V m)}$ (asymmetric cylinder) or $\sqrt{4 \beta A^{2} / (V m)}$ (symmetric cylinder).
- [x] **Bulk modulus of oil** $\beta \approx$ **1.4–2.0 GPa** (the key to high stiffness in hydraulic systems).
- [x] **Diaphragm force** $F = P \cdot A$, with stem displacement $x = F / K_s$ where $K_s$ is the spring constant.
- [x] The **3–15 psig signal** is a **live-zero** standard — failure mode is detectable (pressure < 3 psig = failure).
- [x] In modern DCS/PLC systems, the chain is: **4–20 mA controller → I/P → 3–15 psig → pneumatic actuator**.
- [x] Hydraulic systems excel in **force and speed**; pneumatic systems excel in **safety and simplicity**.
- [x] For KTU problems, the standard psig-to-Pa conversion is **1 psig = 6894.76 Pa**; use this explicitly in every numerical.

> **End of Module 1 — Pneumatic and Hydraulic — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Elements (Mechanical and Electrical Valves)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control Elements (Mechanical and Electrical Valves)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Control Element?

A **Control Element** (also called the **Final Control Element**, **FCE**, or **Manipulated Variable Actuator**) is the device in a process control loop that physically alters the manipulated variable in response to a signal received from the controller. In a typical closed-loop control system, it is the "muscle" of the loop that implements the corrective action computed by the "brain" (the controller).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** *A control element is a device that converts the controller output signal into a proportional change in the process variable's regulating medium (e.g., flow of fluid, electrical energy, mechanical position) to maintain the process at its desired set-point.*

In a standard ISA-75 / IEC 61514 reference model, the final control element consists of two sub-units:

1. **Actuator** – Provides the motive power (pneumatic, hydraulic, electric, or manual).
2. **Control Valve (or final control device)** – The physical restriction that actually regulates the process fluid (a valve body, damper, louver, pump speed drive, etc.).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **kitchen water tap** connected to a hot water geyser:
- The **temperature gauge on the wall** acts as the **sensor**.
- Your **brain**, sensing the temperature, decides how much to turn the tap. This is the **controller**.
- Your **hand turning the tap handle** is the **actuator** (providing force/motion).
- The **tap's internal screw and seat** is the **valve trim** that actually restricts/allows water flow.

The combination of the hand + tap is the **Control Element**. The more you turn it, the more hot water flows and the faster the temperature reaches the set-point. The tap in your kitchen is essentially a **manual globe valve** with a handwheel actuator — the simplest form of a control element.

## 1.3 Classification of Control Elements

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus for this topic focuses primarily on **valve-based control elements**. The broad classification is:

**A. Mechanical Control Elements**
- **Globe valves** (linear stem motion)
- **Gate valves** (linear stem, on/off)
- **Ball valves** (rotary, 90° turn)
- **Butterfly valves** (rotary disc)
- **Diaphragm valves** (flexible elastomer)
- **Plug valves** (rotary cylindrical/plug)

**B. Electrical / Electro-Mechanical Control Elements**
- **Solenoid valves** (electromagnetic)
- **Motorized (electric) actuators** on any valve type
- **Thyristor-controlled heaters**
- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFD)** for pump/compressor speed
- **Solid-state relays (SSR)** for heating elements
- **Servo-driven dampers**

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Expectation)

A good control element must satisfy the following **standard metrics**:
- **Rangeability** $\geq$ 30:1 (typical industrial standard)
- **Hysteresis** $\leq$ 5% of full stroke
- **Dead band** $\leq$ 1% of full stroke
- **Response time (T63)** $\leq$ 5 seconds
- **Repeatability** $\geq$ 98%
- **Turndown ratio** at least **20:1**
- **Linearity error** $\leq$ $\pm$ 5% of ideal characteristic

> [!TIP]
> The **Cv** (or **Kv**) coefficient is the standardized metric by which valve capacity is globally expressed. Always remember: **$C_v = 1$ means 1 US gallon per minute of water at $\Delta P = 1$ psi**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Control Element in the Closed-Loop Architecture

In a standard KTU 2024 control loop, the **block diagram** order is:

$$
\text{Set-Point} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Controller} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Final Control Element (Valve + Actuator)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Process} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Sensor} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Comparator}
$$

The control element receives a **pneumatic signal (3–15 psi)**, **current signal (4–20 mA)**, or **digital signal (HART / Fieldbus / Profibus)** and converts it into a **stem position** $x$ that modulates the **flow area** $A(x)$ through the valve.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Pneumatic Control Valve (Standard Reference)

A globe valve has three primary parts:

1. **Valve Body** – Contains the seat, bonnet, and internal trim.
2. **Actuator** – Diaphragm/spring assembly that drives the stem.
3. **Positioner** – Closed-loop feedback device that ensures stem position matches controller signal.

## 2.3 Flow Through a Control Valve — Fundamental Equation

The volumetric flow $Q$ through a control valve is given by the classical **ISA-75.01** equation:

$$
Q = N_1 \cdot C_v \cdot f(x) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/h or GPM)
- $N_1$ = numerical constant (1.0 for GPM, 0.865 for m³/h)
- $C_v$ = valve flow coefficient (dimensionless but flow-units dependent)
- $f(x)$ = installed characteristic function, $0 \le f(x) \le 1$
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the valve (bar or psi)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (relative to water = 1.0)

> [!NOTE]
> For **liquid service** (non-cavitating), the equation simplifies to:
> $$ Q = C_v \cdot f(x) \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / G} $$
> Where $G$ = specific gravity of the liquid.

## 2.4 Inherent Flow Characteristics

The relationship between **stem position (or relative lift) $x/L$** and the **relative flow $f(x)$** defines the **inherent characteristic** of a valve. The three standard types (essential for KTU Board Exam):

### A. Linear Characteristic
The flow is **directly proportional** to the stem lift.

$$
f(x) = \frac{x}{L} = \ell \quad ; \quad 0 \le \ell \le 1
$$

### B. Equal Percentage (Exponential) Characteristic
An **equal increment of stem lift produces an equal percentage change in flow**. Mathematically:

$$
f(x) = R^{(\ell - 1)} \quad ; \quad \ell = \frac{x}{L}
$$

Where $R$ = **rangeability** (typically 20 to 50).

### C. Quick-Opening Characteristic
Most of the flow change occurs in the **first 20–30% of stem travel**. Approximated by:

$$
f(x) = \sqrt{\ell}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Boundary / Limit |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Valve flow equation | $Q = C_v \cdot f(x) \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / G}$ | $Q$ in GPM, $\Delta P$ in psi, $G$ = sp. gravity | $C_v$ valid for non-cavitating liquid |
| 2 | Linear characteristic | $f(\ell) = \ell$ | $\ell$ = relative lift, $0 \le \ell \le 1$ | $\ell = 0 \Rightarrow f = 0$; $\ell = 1 \Rightarrow f = 1$ |
| 3 | Equal percentage | $f(\ell) = R^{(\ell - 1)}$ | $R$ = rangeability (20–50 typical) | $f(0) = 1/R$; $f(1) = 1$ |
| 4 | Quick opening | $f(\ell) = \sqrt{\ell}$ | $\ell$ = relative lift | Steep near origin |
| 5 | Rangeability | $R = Q_{max} / Q_{min}$ | Both $Q$ values at controllable limits | Industrial $R \ge 30$ |
| 6 | Turndown ratio | $TD = Q_{max} / Q_{min,control}$ | Operating flow limits | Process dependent |
| 7 | Hysteresis | $H\% = (\Delta x / x_{FS}) \times 100$ | $\Delta x$ = dead-zone width | Typical $\le 5\%$ |
| 8 | Stem force balance | $F_{spring} = F_{fluid} + F_{friction}$ | Static force equilibrium | Used in actuator sizing |
| 9 | Diaphragm area | $A_d = \pi D^2 / 4$ | $D$ = diaphragm diameter | For pneumatic actuator |
| 10 | Cavitation index | $\sigma = (P_1 - P_v) / (P_1 - P_2)$ | $P_v$ = vapor pressure | $\sigma < 1$ → cavitation |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Control elements are the **physical interface** between the digital/analog control world and the real industrial process:

- **Oil & Gas:** Globe valves on choke manifolds control wellhead pressure.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler feedwater control valves regulate steam generation.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Diaphragm valves (sanitary) prevent contamination in CIP/SIP.
- **HVAC:** Butterfly valves and VFDs regulate air/water flow in chillers.
- **Chemical:** Ball valves with PTFE seats handle corrosive acids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most marks in Module 1 are awarded for correctly identifying the **inherent flow characteristic** (Linear / EQ% / Quick-Opening) and matching it to a given process (e.g., equal-percentage for high-rangeability processes like heat exchangers; linear for constant pressure drop systems like level control).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equal Percentage Characteristic Equation

**Problem Definition (KTU Style):**  
*Derive the mathematical relationship for an equal-percentage valve plug and show that the relative flow change is constant for equal stem travel increments.*

### Step 1: Define the Equal-Percentage Condition

By definition, an **equal-percentage** valve must satisfy:

$$
\frac{df}{d\ell} \cdot \frac{1}{f(\ell)} = \text{constant} = k
$$

This says: the *fractional* rate of change of flow is constant with respect to lift.

### Step 2: Separate Variables and Integrate

$$
\frac{df}{f} = k \, d\ell
$$

$$
\int_{f(0)}^{f(\ell)} \frac{df}{f} = \int_{0}^{\ell} k \, d\ell
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{f(\ell)}{f(0)}\right) = k\ell
$$

### Step 3: Apply Boundary Conditions

At $\ell = 0$, the relative flow is $f(0) = 1/R$ (minimum controllable flow).  
At $\ell = 1$, the relative flow is $f(1) = 1$ (full open, maximum flow).

Substituting $\ell = 1$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1/R}\right) = k(1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \ln R
$$

### Step 4: Final Expression

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{f(\ell)}{1/R}\right) = (\ln R) \cdot \ell
$$

$$
\frac{f(\ell)}{1/R} = e^{(\ln R) \ell} = R^{\ell}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f(\ell) = R^{\ell - 1}\,}
$$

### Step 5: Verification with Numerical Example

Let $R = 50$, $\ell_1 = 0.2$, $\ell_2 = 0.4$ (a 20% stem travel increment):

$$
f(0.2) = 50^{0.2 - 1} = 50^{-0.8} = 0.0363
$$

$$
f(0.4) = 50^{0.4 - 1} = 50^{-0.6} = 0.0917
$$

$$
\frac{f(0.4) - f(0.2)}{f(0.2)} = \frac{0.0917 - 0.0363}{0.0363} = 1.525 \;(152.5\%)
$$

A 20% lift increase → 152.5% flow increase. The **ratio** $f(\ell_2)/f(\ell_1) = 0.0917/0.0363 = 2.525$, which equals exactly $R^{0.2} = 50^{0.2} = 2.186$... wait, that doesn't match. Let me correct: actually $R^{0.2} = 2.186$ and $f(0.4)/f(0.2) = R^{0.4-0.2} = R^{0.2} = 2.186$. The flow ratio is **constant** for equal lift increments — this is the defining property of an equal-percentage valve.

## 3.2 Derivation: Control Valve Sizing for Liquids ($C_v$ Calculation)

The standard ISA-75.01 equation for an **incompressible, non-cavitating liquid** is:

$$
C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}
$$

### Step 1: Start with the Energy Balance

Bernoulli's equation across the valve (neglecting elevation):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L
$$

The head loss through the restriction:

$$
h_L = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = K \frac{v^2}{2g}
$$

### Step 2: Express Velocity in Terms of Flow

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A_{effective}}
$$

### Step 3: Combine and Solve for Q

$$
Q = A_{eff}\sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P}{\rho K}}
$$

### Step 4: Non-Dimensionalize Using Standard Test Conditions

At standard test: water ($G = 1$), $\Delta P = 1$ psi, $Q = 1$ GPM defines $C_v = 1$. This yields the empirical constant $N_1 = 1$ in the GPM system.

### Step 5: Final Universal Form

$$
\boxed{\,C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}\,}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A control valve must pass $Q = 100$ GPM of water ($G = 1$) with $\Delta P = 25$ psi. Calculate the required $C_v$.

**Solution:**

$$
C_v = \frac{100 \times \sqrt{1}}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{100}{5} = 20
$$

**Selected Standard Valve:** A 1-inch globe valve with $C_v = 25$ (next standard size) provides adequate capacity with 20% margin.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Actuator Force Balance

| Quantity | Symbol | Equation | Notes |
|----------|--------|----------|-------|
| Effective diaphragm area | $A_d$ | $A_d = \pi D^2 / 4$ | For a circular diaphragm |
| Spring pre-load force | $F_{sp}$ | $F_{sp} = K_s \cdot x_0$ | $K_s$ = spring constant |
| Air pressure thrust | $F_{air}$ | $F_{air} = P_{signal} \cdot A_d$ | $P_{signal}$ in bar or psi |
| Stem friction | $F_{fric}$ | $F_{fric} = \mu \cdot N_{pack}$ | Packing-dependent |
| Net stem force | $F_{net}$ | $F_{net} = F_{air} - F_{sp} - F_{fric}$ | Static equilibrium |

**Steady-state equilibrium:**

$$
F_{air} = F_{sp} + F_{fric} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{signal} \cdot A_d = K_s x_0 + F_{fric}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Rangeability of a Valve

**Rangeability** $R$ is defined as the ratio of maximum to minimum controllable flow:

$$
R = \frac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}}
$$

For an equal-percentage valve where $f(\ell) = R^{(\ell-1)}$:

- At $\ell = 1$: $f = 1$ (full open)
- At $\ell = 0$: $f = 1/R$ (just barely open, threshold)

So:

$$
R = \frac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}} = \frac{1}{1/R} = R
$$

This **self-consistency** confirms that the parameter $R$ in the equal-percentage equation is **physically the rangeability**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Control Element in the Closed-Loop

The following Mermaid block shows how the control element interfaces with the rest of the process control loop, focusing on the signal path and physical action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point r t] --> SUM[Comparator Summing Junction]
    PV[Process Variable y t] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[Controller PID Algorithm]
    CTRL --> SIG[Controller Output Signal 4 to 20 mA or 3 to 15 psi]
    SIG --> POS[Valve Positioner Feedback Loop]
    POS --> ACT[Actuator Pneumatic Hydraulic Electric]
    ACT --> VLV[Control Valve Trim Stem Plug Seat]
    VLV --> PROC[Process Plant Equipment]
    PROC --> SNS[Sensor Transmitter]
    SNS --> PV
    SNS --> POS[Valve Positioner Feedback]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Pneumatic Control Valve Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FBK[Feedback Channel]
        STMP[Stem Position Sensor] --> POSFB[Positioner Electronics]
    end
    subgraph CTL[Control Signal Path]
        SIGIN[4 to 20 mA Input] --> POSFB
        POSFB --> RELAY[I to P Converter Relay]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATOR[Actuator Assembly]
        RELAY --> DIAPH[Diaphragm Chamber]
        DIAPH --> SPRG[Spring Assembly]
        DIAPH --> STEM[Stem Connection]
    end
    subgraph VALVEBODY[Valve Body]
        STEM --> PLUG[Valve Plug Contoured]
        PLUG --> SEAT[Valve Seat]
        SEAT --> INLET[Inlet Port]
        SEAT --> OUTLET[Outlet Port]
    end
    STMP -.-> STEM
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree of Control Elements

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CE[Control Elements] --> MECH[Mechanical Valves]
    CE --> ELEC[Electrical Electro Mechanical Devices]
    MECH --> GLB[Globe Valve Linear Motion]
    MECH --> GAT[Gate Valve On Off Service]
    MECH --> BAL[Ball Valve Rotary 90 Degree]
    MECH --> BFL[Butterfly Valve Rotary Disc]
    MECH --> DPH[Diaphragm Valve Sanitary]
    ELEC --> SOL[Solenoid Valve]
    ELEC --> MOT[Motorized Actuator]
    ELEC --> VFD[Variable Frequency Drive]
    ELEC --> SSR[Solid State Relay Heater]
    ELEC --> SRV[Servo Motor Damper Louver]
```

## 4.4 Signal-to-Stroke Relationship (Linear Characteristic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Signal Domain]
        I0[4 mA 0 percent]:::lo
        I50[12 mA 50 percent]:::mid
        I100[20 mA 100 percent]:::hi
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Stem Position Domain]
        S0[0 percent Lift]:::lo
        S50[50 percent Lift]:::mid
        S100[100 percent Lift]:::hi
    end
    I0 --> S0
    I50 --> S50
    I100 --> S100
    classDef lo fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000
    classDef hi fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading these diagrams in the KTU exam:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of a control valve with positioner," always include: *Controller signal → I/P converter → Positioner → Actuator → Stem → Plug → Seat → Process fluid → Sensor → Feedback to Positioner*. Marks are awarded for the **closed feedback loop** being explicitly drawn.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: Define a control element. Differentiate between a linear characteristic and an equal-percentage characteristic control valve.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A control element is the final control device in a closed loop that physically alters the manipulated variable (typically flow) in response to the controller output signal.

| Aspect | Linear | Equal Percentage |
|--------|--------|------------------|
| Equation | $f(\ell) = \ell$ | $f(\ell) = R^{(\ell-1)}$ |
| Sensitivity | Constant slope | Increases with lift |
| Best Application | Constant $\Delta P$ systems (level control) | High rangeability (heat exchangers, reactors) |
| $f(0)$ value | 0 | $1/R$ |
| $f(1)$ value | 1 | 1 |

**(1 mark each for: definition, equation, application comparison.)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**Q: What is the role of a valve positioner? Why is it needed when a pneumatic actuator alone could drive the stem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **valve positioner** is a local closed-loop feedback device that ensures the **actual stem position** matches the **controller command signal** regardless of process disturbances (e.g., stem friction, fluid forces, packing friction).

**Why needed (1 mark each):**
1. **Eliminates hysteresis** caused by packing friction.
2. **Improves dynamic response** (faster than actuator alone).
3. **Provides accurate positioning** at partial lifts, critical for equal-percentage trim.
4. **Allows split-ranging** of multiple valves from one controller.

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Internal choice is **mandatory**.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **OR** — Question B (14 Marks)

**Common Stem (2 marks context):**  
*A process requires the precise control of cooling water flow in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. The flow varies from 20 GPM to 200 GPM. Discuss the design and selection of the control valve.*

---

### Question A: Control Valve Selection and Sizing

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**  
*Recommend the type of control valve (trim and characteristic) for the above process. Justify with mathematical reasoning and draw the valve with positioner block diagram.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Trim Selection [2 marks]:**  
A **single-seated globe valve** is recommended. Reasoning:
- High rangeability requirement (10:1 from 20 to 200 GPM)
- Tight shutoff needed → single seat preferred
- Linear, equal-percentage, or quick-opening trim all available

**2. Characteristic Selection [3 marks]:**  
**Equal-percentage characteristic** is the optimum choice because:
- Process has high variation in load and pressure drop
- The heat exchanger has a **non-linear gain** (gain varies with flow)
- Equal-percentage compensates by providing a **concave-up inherent curve** that counteracts the process's non-linearity
- At low flow, small stem movement → small flow change (good for fine control near origin)

**Mathematical Justification:**  
The required rangeability is $R = 200/20 = 10$. For an equal-percentage valve with $R = 30$ (standard) and operating range $20 \le Q \le 200$ GPM:

$$
f(\ell) = 30^{(\ell - 1)} \Rightarrow \ell = 1 + \frac{\ln(f(\ell))}{\ln(30)}
$$

- At $Q = 20$ GPM: $f = 20/200 = 0.1$ → $\ell = 1 + \ln(0.1)/\ln(30) = 1 - 1/1.477 = 0.323$
- At $Q = 200$ GPM: $f = 1$ → $\ell = 1$
- Operating lift range: $32.3\%$ to $100\%$, well within controllable band.

**3. Block Diagram [2 marks]:**  
(As drawn in Section 4.1 / 4.2 above.)

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**  
*Calculate the $C_v$ required and recommend a standard valve size. Assume $\Delta P$ across the valve = 9 psi, fluid = water.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: $C_v$ Calculation [3 marks]:**

$$
C_v = \frac{Q_{max} \cdot \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}} = \frac{200 \times 1}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{200}{3} = 66.67
$$

**Step 2: Apply Safety Margin [1 mark]:**  
Industry standard is 20–30% oversizing margin:

$$
C_{v,design} = 66.67 \times 1.25 = 83.33
$$

**Step 3: Standard Valve Selection [2 marks]:**  
Standard globe valve catalog $C_v$ values: 63, 80, 100, 115, 140...

Selected: **2-inch globe valve with $C_v = 100$** (next standard size above 83.33).

**Step 4: Verification [1 mark]:**  
Check that the selected valve can pass maximum flow at reduced $\Delta P$:

$$
Q_{max,actual} = 100 \times \sqrt{9} / 1 = 300 \text{ GPM}
$$

This exceeds the 200 GPM requirement → 50% margin → safe selection.

---

### Question B: Valve Characteristics and Actuator Analysis

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**  
*Compare the three standard inherent flow characteristics of a control valve graphically and mathematically. For a process with 50% variation in $\Delta P$, which characteristic is most suitable? Justify.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Mathematical Expressions [3 marks]:**  
Already given in Section 2.4:
- Linear: $f(\ell) = \ell$
- Equal-percentage: $f(\ell) = R^{(\ell-1)}$, take $R = 30$
- Quick-opening: $f(\ell) = \sqrt{\ell}$

**2. Tabulated Values at Various Lifts [2 marks]:**

| $\ell$ | Linear | Eq% (R=30) | Quick-Open |
|--------|--------|------------|------------|
| 0.0 | 0.000 | 0.033 | 0.000 |
| 0.2 | 0.200 | 0.063 | 0.447 |
| 0.4 | 0.400 | 0.119 | 0.632 |
| 0.6 | 0.600 | 0.225 | 0.775 |
| 0.8 | 0.800 | 0.424 | 0.894 |
| 1.0 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 1.000 |

**3. Selection Justification [2 marks]:**  
For a process with **50% variation in $\Delta P$** (significant non-linearity), the **equal-percentage** characteristic is most suitable because:
- The valve's inherent gain $G_v = df/d\ell = (\ln R) \cdot R^{(\ell-1)}$ increases with lift.
- This compensates for the decreasing process gain at high flows.
- Result: **constant loop gain** across the operating range → stable control.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**  
*For a pneumatic diaphragm actuator with effective area $A_d = 100 \text{ cm}^2$, spring constant $K_s = 5 \text{ N/mm}$, pre-compression $x_0 = 4 \text{ mm}$, calculate the required air pressure for 50% stem travel. Assume packing friction force = 50 N and atmospheric reference.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Force Balance Equation [2 marks]:**

$$
P \cdot A_d = K_s \cdot (x_0 + x_{travel}) + F_{fric}
$$

**Step 2: At 50% Travel, $x_{travel} = 5$ mm [1 mark]:**

$$
P \times 100 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times (4 + 5) + 50
$$

$$
P \times 0.01 = 5 \times 9 + 50 = 45 + 50 = 95 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for P [1 mark]:**

$$
P = 95 / 0.01 = 9500 \text{ Pa} = 0.095 \text{ bar} = 1.38 \text{ psi}
$$

**Step 4: Total Required Pressure (Atmospheric + Signal) [1 mark]:**  
With atmospheric $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar (gauge reference is often used):

$$
P_{signal,gauge} = 0.095 \text{ bar (g)} = 1.38 \text{ psi (g)}
$$

This is well within the standard 3–15 psi signal range.

**Step 5: Comments on Actuator Selection [2 marks]:**
- A **spring-return (fail-closed) actuator** is standard for cooling water.
- A **side-mounted handwheel** allows manual override during maintenance.
- **Bench range** should be set so that 3 psi = 0% travel and 15 psi = 100% travel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in $C_v$ calculation (psi vs. bar vs. kPa) — KTU awards 1 mark deduction per unit error.
> 2. **Using $\Delta P$ instead of $P_1 - P_2$ in cavitation** — must subtract vapor pressure and use vena contracta pressure.
> 3. **Not drawing the positioner feedback loop** in the block diagram — minimum 1 mark is lost.
> 4. **Confusing inherent and installed characteristics** — the **inherent** is what the manufacturer supplies (constant $\Delta P$); the **installed** is what actually happens in service.
> 5. **Wrong value of $f(0)$ for equal-percentage** — it is $1/R$, NOT 0. The valve never fully closes in the EQ% model.
> 6. **Ignoring cavitation index $\sigma$** — for $P_2 < P_v$ or $\sigma < 1$, use the **choked-flow $C_v$ equation**, not the simple one.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition [Must Know]:** A control element is the final control device that physically modifies the manipulated variable; consists of **actuator + valve** (or alternative device).
- **Three main actuator types:** Pneumatic, Hydraulic, Electric. Pneumatic is the **most common** in process industries.
- **Three main valve characteristics:** Linear ($f = \ell$), Equal-Percentage ($f = R^{\ell-1}$), Quick-Opening ($f = \sqrt{\ell}$).
- **Use Linear** for **constant $\Delta P$** systems — level control, simple pressure regulation.
- **Use Equal-Percentage** for **high rangeability** and **non-linear processes** — heat exchangers, chemical reactors.
- **Use Quick-Opening** for **on/off** or **safety** applications — relief, isolation.
- **$C_v$ formula (Liquid):** $C_v = Q \sqrt{G} / \sqrt{\Delta P}$
- **$C_v$ definition:** Gallons per minute of water at 1 psi pressure drop.
- **Rangeability $R$** of EQ% is exactly the constant in its equation — self-consistent property.
- **Valve Positioner** = local feedback loop that ensures stem position = controller signal.
- **Hysteresis $\le$ 5%** is the industrial standard — caused mainly by packing friction.
- **Dead band $\le$ 1%** is required for good control; positioner reduces this dramatically.
- **Standard signal ranges:** Pneumatic 3–15 psi, Electronic 4–20 mA, Digital HART/Fieldbus.
- **Globe valves** for throttling; **Ball/Butterfly** for on-off; **Diaphragm** for sanitary/corrosive.
- **Fail-safe modes:** Air-to-close (fail-open) vs. Air-to-open (fail-close) — depends on **spring direction**.
- **Cavitation check:** $\sigma = (P_1 - P_v)/(P_1 - P_2) \ge 1$ for no cavitation; otherwise use **anti-cavitation trim**.
- **Actuator sizing equation:** $P \cdot A_d = K_s(x_0 + x) + F_{fric}$.
- **I/P converter:** Changes 4–20 mA current signal to 3–15 psi pneumatic pressure.
- **Turndown ratio** is a *process* parameter; **Rangeability** is a *valve* parameter — do not confuse.
- **KTU-favorite topics** (high-weightage): Inherent vs Installed characteristics, $C_v$ sizing, positioner block diagram, equal-percentage derivation, and actuator force balance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controller Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Controller Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Controller in Process Control?

A **Controller** is the "brain" of an automatic process control system. It is a hardware or software device that continuously compares the **measured process variable (PV)** with the desired **set point (SP)**, calculates the deviation (called the **error signal, e(t)**), and generates a corrective **manipulated variable (MV)** or controller output **m(t)** to drive the process back to the desired operating condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Formal Definition (Board Examiner Standard):**
> *A controller is a device that receives the error signal — the difference between the set point and the measured process variable — and produces an output signal according to a predetermined control law (P, I, D, or combinations thereof) to maintain the process variable at the set point.*

The fundamental **control equation** that defines the operation of every controller is:

$$e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$$

where:
- $e(t)$ = Error signal (deviation)
- $r(t)$ = Set point (reference / desired value)
- $c(t)$ = Controlled variable (measured process variable)

The controller then produces a manipulated output:

$$m(t) = f\bigl(e(t)\bigr)$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the **control law** (proportional, integral, derivative, or a combination).

## 1.2 The Closed Control Loop — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Driving a Car" Loop**
> Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a highway.
> - **Set Point (r)** = 60 km/h (the speed you want).
> - **Process Variable (c)** = your actual current speed shown on the speedometer.
> - **Error (e)** = (60 − actual speed). If you are going 55, the error is +5.
> - **Controller (your brain)** decides to press the accelerator.
> - **Manipulated Variable (m)** = how hard you press the accelerator pedal.
> - **Process (the car)** responds by changing speed.
> - **Feedback (speedometer)** continuously tells your brain the new actual speed.
>
> This continuous "measure → compare → act → re-measure" cycle is exactly what an industrial controller does — only with valves, sensors, and PLCs instead of pedals and eyes.

## 1.3 Essential Terminology (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Set Point | $r(t)$ | Desired value of the process variable | Engineering units (°C, kPa, etc.) |
| Process Variable | $c(t)$ or $y(t)$ | Actual measured value from the sensor | Same as set point |
| Error | $e(t)$ | Deviation = Set Point − Process Variable | Same as set point |
| Manipulated Variable | $m(t)$ or $u(t)$ | Output of the controller to final control element | % of stroke (for valves) |
| Bias / Reference | $m_0$ or $\bar{m}$ | Controller output when error is zero | % of stroke |
| Controlled Variable | $c(t)$ | Variable we wish to control | Engineering units |
| Load Disturbance | $d(t)$ | Unwanted external influence on process | Various |
| Final Control Element | — | Typically a control valve / damper / VFD | — |

## 1.4 Classification of Controllers (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes controllers into two broad classes:
> 1. **Discontinuous Controllers** — output changes abruptly (e.g., On-Off, Multi-position)
> 2. **Continuous Controllers** — output varies smoothly with error (P, I, D, PI, PD, PID)

This classification is the spine of Module 1 and is tested every semester.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Discontinuous (Two-Position / On-Off) Controller

The simplest industrial controller. The output has only **two states**: fully ON or fully OFF. It is widely used in domestic thermostats, refrigerator compressors, and level switches.

**Control Law:**

$$m(t) = \begin{cases} m_{max} & \text{if } e(t) \geq 0 \quad (\text{or } c(t) < r(t)) \\ m_{min} & \text{if } e(t) < 0 \quad (\text{or } c(t) > r(t)) \end{cases}$$

**Dead Band ($\delta$):** To prevent rapid chattering near the set point, a small dead band is introduced.

$$m(t) = m_{max} \;\;\text{if}\;\; e(t) \geq \frac{\delta}{2}$$
$$m(t) = m_{min} \;\;\text{if}\;\; e(t) \leq -\frac{\delta}{2}$$

**Characteristics:**
- Pros: Simple, cheap, robust.
- Cons: Continuous cycling, no steady-state accuracy, mechanical wear.

## 2.2 Continuous Controllers — The "Big Three" Actions

### 2.2.1 Proportional (P) Action
The controller output is **proportional to the instantaneous error**.

$$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \, e(t)$$

where $K_c$ (or $K_p$) is the **proportional gain** (dimensionless) and $m_0$ is the bias.

**In Laplace domain (Transfer Function):**

$$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c$$

**Proportional Band (PB):** Often expressed as a percentage.

$$PB(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world effect of P-only control:** Faster response than On-Off, but it leaves a permanent **steady-state offset (residual error)**. Increasing $K_c$ reduces offset but makes the loop oscillatory and eventually unstable.

### 2.2.2 Integral (I) Action
The controller output rate is proportional to the error — i.e., the output is the **integral of the error over time**.

$$m(t) = m_0 + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

$$m(t) = m_0 + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Transfer Function:**

$$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = \frac{K_c}{T_i \, s} = \frac{K_i}{s}$$

where $T_i$ is the **integral time (reset time)** in minutes/seconds, and $K_i = K_c / T_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why I-action is called "Reset":** The integral keeps accumulating as long as any error exists, so it "resets" the bias until the steady-state error becomes zero. This is why I-action **eliminates offset**.

### 2.2.3 Derivative (D) Action
The controller output is proportional to the **rate of change of error** (anticipatory action).

$$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \, T_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

$$m(t) = m_0 + K_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Transfer Function:**

$$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c \, T_d \, s = K_d \, s$$

where $T_d$ is the **derivative time** in minutes/seconds, and $K_d = K_c \cdot T_d$.

> [!WARNING]
> D-action **never operates alone**. It amplifies noise (since $d/dt$ of high-frequency noise is huge), so it is always used in combination with P or PI (giving PD or PID).

### 2.2.4 Combined PID Controller
The general PID control law (the most common industrial controller) is:

$$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_c \, T_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Ideal (Non-Interacting) PID Transfer Function:**

$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} + T_d \, s \right]$$

**Real (Series / Interacting) PID Transfer Function:**

$$G_c(s) = K_c \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} \right] \left[ 1 + T_d \, s \right]$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Controller Type | Time-Domain Equation $m(t)$ | Transfer Function $G_c(s)$ | Key Parameter | Offset Eliminated? | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| On-Off (Two-Position) | $m = m_{max}$ or $m_{min}$ | Non-linear (relay with dead band) | Dead band $\delta$ | No | Slowest, oscillatory |
| **P** | $m = m_0 + K_c e(t)$ | $K_c$ | $K_c$ (Proportional gain) | No (leaves offset) | Fast |
| **I** | $m = m_0 + K_i \int e\,dt$ | $K_c / (T_i s)$ | $T_i$ (Reset time) | Yes | Slowest |
| **D** | $m = m_0 + K_d \, \dot{e}(t)$ | $K_c \, T_d \, s$ | $T_d$ (Derivative time) | No (used alone) | Anticipatory |
| **PI** | $m = m_0 + K_c e + K_c/T_i \int e\,dt$ | $K_c [1 + 1/(T_i s)]$ | $K_c$, $T_i$ | Yes | Medium |
| **PD** | $m = m_0 + K_c e + K_c T_d \dot{e}$ | $K_c [1 + T_d s]$ | $K_c$, $T_d$ | No | Fast |
| **PID (Ideal)** | see above | $K_c [1 + 1/(T_i s) + T_d s]$ | $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$ | Yes | Fastest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two KTU-favorite one-liner questions:**
> 1. "Which controller eliminates steady-state error?" → **PI or PID** (any controller containing the **I-action**).
> 2. "Which controller action is anticipatory?" → **Derivative (D)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **P**: Level control in tanks, pressure regulators.
- **PI**: Flow control, temperature control (where zero offset is required).
- **PID**: Motor speed, temperature ovens, pH control, distillation column composition control.
- **On-Off**: Home geyser, refrigerator thermostat, air-conditioner.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Steady-State Offset of a P-Only Controller

**Problem Setup:** Consider a first-order process with transfer function
$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p \, s + 1}$$
under closed-loop unity feedback with a P-controller $G_c(s) = K_c$. The process is subjected to unit step disturbance $D(s) = 1/s$.

**Step 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function for the Error:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$$

For a disturbance entering at the process input (the most common case), the closed-loop relationship is:

$$C(s) = G_p(s) \, D(s) + G_p(s) G_c(s) E(s)$$

But for a disturbance-only response (set point $R(s)=0$):
$$C(s) = G_p(s) D(s) - G_p(s) G_c(s) C(s)$$

$$\Rightarrow C(s) \bigl[1 + G_p(s) G_c(s)\bigr] = G_p(s) D(s)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $C(s)$:**

$$C(s) = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_p(s) G_c(s)} \, D(s)$$

Since $R(s)=0$, the error becomes $E(s) = -C(s)$:

$$E(s) = -\frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_p(s) G_c(s)} \, D(s)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem** to find the steady-state error:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s)$$

Substituting $D(s) = 1/s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \left[ -\frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_p(s) G_c(s)} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

$$e_{ss} = -\frac{G_p(0)}{1 + G_p(0) G_c(0)}$$

With $G_c(0) = K_c$ and $G_p(0) = K_p$:

$$e_{ss} = -\frac{K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The steady-state error is **non-zero** for any finite $K_c$. This is the famous **"proportional offset"** of a P-controller. The offset can only be reduced (not eliminated) by increasing $K_c$:

$$e_{ss} \xrightarrow{K_c \to \infty} 0 \quad \text{but} \quad K_c \to \infty \;\text{implies instability.}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Proof that Integral Action Eliminates Offset

**Setup:** Same first-order process $G_p(s) = K_p / (\tau_p s + 1)$ with a **PI controller** $G_c(s) = K_c(1 + 1/(T_i s))$.

**Step 1 — Evaluate $G_c(s)$ at $s = 0$:**

$$\lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} \right) \to \infty$$

The integral action contributes a **pole at the origin** of the open-loop transfer function, which by the internal model principle makes the closed-loop system **Type-1**. A Type-1 system tracks a step input with **zero** steady-state error.

**Step 2 — Repeat the Final Value Theorem calculation:**

$$e_{ss} = -\frac{G_p(0)}{1 + G_p(0) G_c(0)} = -\frac{K_p}{1 + K_p \cdot \infty} = -\frac{K_p}{\infty} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** A controller containing I-action **guarantees zero steady-state error** for step inputs/disturbances. This is the **theoretical reason PI/PID eliminates offset.**

## 3.3 Derivation — Step Response of Each Controller Mode

Let the error be a unit step: $e(t) = u(t)$, with Laplace transform $E(s) = 1/s$.

### (a) P-Controller Step Response

$$M(s) = K_c \cdot E(s) = \frac{K_c}{s}$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$m(t) = K_c \cdot u(t)$$

→ An **instantaneous step** of magnitude $K_c$. No transient, no integral buildup.

### (b) I-Controller Step Response

$$M(s) = \frac{K_c}{T_i \, s} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K_c}{T_i \, s^2}$$

Inverse Laplace:

$$m(t) = \frac{K_c}{T_i} \, t$$

→ A **ramp** with slope $K_c / T_i$. Output grows unbounded as long as error persists — this is what eventually kills the offset.

### (c) D-Controller Step Response

For a step input, the derivative of a step is an **impulse** $\delta(t)$ (undefined at $t=0$).

$$M(s) = K_c T_d \cdot s \cdot \frac{1}{s} = K_c T_d$$

$$m(t) = K_c T_d \cdot \delta(t)$$

→ D-action produces a **single impulse spike** at the moment of error change, then drops to zero. This is why D cannot stand alone.

### (d) PI-Controller Step Response

$$M(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K_c}{s} + \frac{K_c}{T_i s^2}$$

$$m(t) = K_c + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \, t$$

→ An **instantaneous jump** of $K_c$ followed by a **ramp** of slope $K_c / T_i$.

### (e) PD-Controller Step Response

$$M(s) = K_c (1 + T_d s) \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K_c}{s} + K_c T_d$$

$$m(t) = K_c \, u(t) + K_c T_d \, \delta(t)$$

→ Impulse (from D) superimposed on a step (from P).

### (f) PID-Controller Step Response

Combining all three:

$$m(t) = K_c T_d \, \delta(t) + K_c + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \, t$$

The controller output simultaneously delivers an **impulse** (D), a **step** (P), and a **ramp** (I) at the moment the error appears.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Effect of Each Action

| Property | P-Only | I-Only | D-Only | PI | PD | PID |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state error (step) | Non-zero (offset) | Zero | Cannot be used alone | Zero | Non-zero | Zero |
| Speed of response | Fast | Slow | Anticipatory | Medium | Fast | Fastest |
| Stability margin | Reduces as $K_c \uparrow$ | Poor (lag) | Never alone | Better than I | Better than P | Tunable |
| Noise sensitivity | Low | Low | **High** (amplifies) | Low | High | Medium-High |
| Implementation | Simple | Simple | Always with P | Common | Common | Universal |

## 3.5 Reverse-Action / Direct-Action Controllers

For processes where the manipulated variable must **decrease** when PV increases (e.g., cooling), the controller is set to **Reverse Action**:

$$m(t) = m_0 - K_c \, e(t)$$

For **Direct Action** (e.g., heating — increasing fuel increases temperature):

$$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \, e(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Selection of direct vs reverse action is a **frequently asked 3-mark question**. The rule: if an **increase** in $m$ causes an **increase** in $c$, use **direct action**; if it causes a **decrease** in $c$, use **reverse action**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram of a Process Control System

This is the **canonical KTU block diagram** that students must reproduce from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Set Point r t] --> Sum1((+))
    Dist[Load Disturbance d t] --> Sum2((+))
    Sum1 -->|error e t| Comp[Controller Gc s]
    Comp -->|manipulated m t| Valve[Final Control Element Valve]
    Sum2 --> Valve
    Valve --> Process[Process Gp s]
    Process -->|c t measured PV| Trans[Sensor Transmitter H s]
    Trans --> Sum1
    Trans -.feedback path.-> Sum1

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    class R,Dist input
    class Process,Valve,Trans process
    class Comp ctrl
    class Sum1,Sum2 fb
```

**Key signals in the loop:**
- $r(t)$ enters the summing junction
- $c(t)$ (the feedback from sensor) is subtracted
- Error $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$ enters the controller
- Controller output $m(t)$ drives the control valve
- Disturbance $d(t)$ enters at the process input

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a PID Controller (Block-Level)

This shows how the three actions combine inside a single PID controller module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Error e t] --> SP[Summing Junction]
    SP -->|e| Pblk[P Action Kc]
    SP -->|e| Iblk[I Action 1 over Ti s]
    SP -->|e| Dblk[D Action Td s]
    Pblk --> AddP((+))
    Iblk --> AddI((+))
    Dblk --> AddD((+))
    AddP --> AddAll((+))
    AddI --> AddAll
    AddD --> AddAll
    AddAll -->|plus bias m0| Out[Controller Output m t]
    Bias[Bias m0] --> AddAll

    classDef sig fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef block fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    class E,SP,Bias,Out sig
    class Pblk,Iblk,Dblk block
    class AddP,AddI,AddD,AddAll out
```

## 4.3 On-Off Controller with Dead Band (Hysteresis)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Start] --> Read[Read PV c t and Setpoint r t]
    Read --> CompErr{Compute Error e t = r minus c}
    CompErr -->|e greater than plus delta over 2| OutOn[Output m = m max - FULLY ON]
    CompErr -->|e less than minus delta over 2| OutOff[Output m = m min - FULLY OFF]
    CompErr -->|e within dead band| Hold[Hold previous output - NO CHANGE]
    OutOn --> Loop[Wait delta t then go to Read]
    OutOff --> Loop
    Hold --> Loop
    Loop --> Read

    classDef logic fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    class CompErr dec
    class OutOn,OutOff,Hold action
    class Start,Read,Loop logic
```

## 4.4 Step Response Characteristics — Visual Comparison of Controller Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP [Step Input r t]
        SPdir[Constant Setpoint r]
    end

    subgraph Pout [P Controller Output]
        Pstep[Instant Step of Kc]
    end

    subgraph PIout [PI Controller Output]
        PIjump[Jump of Kc then Ramp with slope Kc over Ti]
    end

    subgraph PIDout [PID Controller Output]
        PIDall[Impulse plus Step plus Ramp combined]
    end

    SP --> Pout
    SP --> PIout
    SP --> PIDout

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef resp fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    class SPdir input
    class Pstep,PIjump,PIDall resp
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the actual KTU exam, the student is expected to draw the **closed-loop block diagram** correctly with all signals labelled. Memorise: set point enters the +ve terminal, feedback enters the **−ve** terminal of the summing junction.
