|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Output drift rate | One fixed magnitude | Multiple discrete magnitudes |
| Reset behaviour | Linear, single slope | Stepped / piecewise linear |
| Error thresholds | None (continuous rate) | Two or more error bands |
| Implementation | Simplest | More common in practice (e.g., 3-speed) |
| Proportional Band | $\infty$ | $\infty$ (still no P-action) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = K_f \cdot e$ | Rate equation of floating controller | %/s |
| 2 | $m(t) = m(0) + K_f \displaystyle\int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Output after time $t$ (zero initial output) | % |
| 3 | $T_i = \dfrac{100}{K_f}$ | Reset time for full-scale traverse | s or min |
| 4 | $K_f = \dfrac{100}{T_i}$ | Floating gain from reset time | %/s |
| 5 | $m(t) = m(0) + K_f \cdot e \cdot t$ | Output for *constant* error $e$ | % |
| 6 | $v_f = K_f \cdot e$ | Instantaneous drift speed | %/s |
| 7 | $\text{PB} = \infty$ | Proportional band (no P-action) | – |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Boundary Condition Rule**
> When error crosses zero, the controller **stops integrating** immediately. Output $m$ stays at whatever value it reached when $e$ became zero. This "memory" is why floating control is also called a **control mode with integral action but no proportional action**.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Floating controllers are favoured where:

1. **Slow, large-capacity processes** dominate (e.g., level control in large tanks, blending operations).
2. **Actuators are inherently rate-limited** (motorised valves with fixed stroking time).
3. **Cost is critical** and full PID is overkill.
4. The process naturally **integrates** the manipulated variable (e.g., a tank filling).
5. **Batch processes** where the setpoint must be approached slowly and held.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Trap**
> Floating control is **NOT the same as integral (I) control**, even though both produce $m \propto \int e\, dt$. The key distinction: in pure I-control the integration is *ideal* (any non-zero error → any output rate); in floating control the rate is **bounded** by $K_f$ — it cannot exceed a finite velocity, which is essential for matching real actuator speeds.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Response for a Step Change in Error

**Problem Setup:** A floating controller has floating gain $K_f = 2\,\%/\text{s}$ per % error. The error is suddenly stepped from $0$ to $e_0 = 10\%$. Find the controller output $m(t)$ for $0 \le t \le 20$ s.

### Step 1 — State the governing differential equation

$$
\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt} = K_f \cdot e(t)
$$

For a constant step error $e(t) = e_0$, this becomes:

$$
\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt} = K_f \cdot e_0
$$

### Step 2 — Recognise that the right-hand side is a constant

Substitute the numerical values: $K_f = 2\,\%/\text{s}$ per % error, $e_0 = 10\%$.

$$
K_f \cdot e_0 = 2 \cdot 10 = 20 \;\dfrac{\%\, \text{output}}{\text{s}}
$$

So the controller output drifts at a steady **20 % per second**.

### Step 3 — Integrate both sides with respect to time

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \dfrac{dm}{dt'}\, dt' = \int_{0}^{t} K_f \cdot e_0 \, dt'
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals

The left side gives $m(t) - m(0)$. The right side gives $K_f \cdot e_0 \cdot t$ (since $K_f e_0$ is constant).

$$
m(t) - m(0) = K_f \cdot e_0 \cdot t
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the initial condition $m(0) = m_0$

$$
\boxed{\;m(t) = m_0 + K_f \cdot e_0 \cdot t\;}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute numbers

$$
m(t) = m_0 + 20 \cdot t \quad [\% \text{ output}]
$$

| $t$ (s) | $m(t)$ (% output, assuming $m_0=0$) |
|--------:|-------------------------------------:|
| 0       | 0                                    |
| 1       | 20                                   |
| 5       | 100 *(saturated)*                    |
| 10      | 100 *(saturated)*                    |

> **Conclusion:** The output rises **linearly at 20 %/s**. After 5 seconds it would nominally be 100 %, after which it is clamped at the actuator limit (saturation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Floating Gain and Reset Time

**Problem Setup:** A pneumatic floating controller has a reset time $T_i = 2$ minutes. Determine its floating gain $K_f$ in %/min and in %/s.

### Step 1 — Definition of reset time

By definition, $T_i$ is the time for the output to traverse 100 % of scale under a step error of 100 %.

$$
100\% = K_f \cdot (100\%) \cdot T_i
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $K_f$

$$
K_f = \dfrac{100\%}{100\% \cdot T_i} = \dfrac{1}{T_i}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $T_i = 2$ min

$$
K_f = \dfrac{1}{2\,\text{min}} = 0.5 \;\dfrac{\%\, \text{output}}{\%\, \text{error}\cdot\text{min}}
$$

If we interpret it as "drift speed per percent error":

$$
K_f = 0.5 \;\dfrac{\%}{\%\, \cdot \text{min}}
$$

For 1 % error, output drifts at 0.5 % per minute. For 100 % error, output drifts at 50 % per minute.

### Step 4 — Convert to %/s

$$
K_f = \dfrac{0.5\,\%/\text{min}}{60\,\text{s/min}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \;\dfrac{\%}{\%\, \cdot \text{s}}
$$

> **Key takeaway for KTU:** Always carry the **unit string** explicitly: %output / (%error · time). Dropping the %error dimension is a common mistake that costs marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Response for a Ramp Error

**Problem Setup:** Error varies linearly as $e(t) = a t$ for $t \ge 0$, with $K_f$ constant. Find $m(t)$.

### Step 1 — Substitute into the rate equation

$$
\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt} = K_f \cdot a t
$$

### Step 2 — Integrate with $m(0) = 0$

$$
m(t) = K_f \cdot a \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} t'\, dt'
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the integral

$$
\int_{0}^{t} t'\, dt' = \dfrac{t^2}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Write the result

$$
\boxed{\;m(t) = \dfrac{1}{2}\, K_f\, a\, t^2\;}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A ramp error produces a **parabolic** output trajectory. The controller accelerates its drift because the error is growing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Floating Control at a Glance

| Property | Floating Control | Pure Integral Control |
|----------|------------------|------------------------|
| Governing law | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = K_f \cdot e$ | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = K_i \cdot e$ |
| Bounded velocity? | **Yes** (limited by $K_f$) | **No** (theoretical $K_i$ unbounded) |
| Effect of zero error | Output floats (constant) | Output remains at last value (same) |
| Proportional band | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| Response to step | Linear ramp until saturation | Linear ramp (unbounded) |
| Eliminates offset? | Yes (after sufficient time) | Yes |
| Match to real actuator? | Excellent | Often poor (saturates too fast) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Speed Floating Controller in a Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> Sum1((+))
    PV[Process Variable PV] --> Sum1
    Sum1 --> E[Error e t]
    E --> FC[Floating Controller Kf]
    FC --> M[Manipulated Variable m t]
    M --> ACT[Actuator Rate Limited]
    ACT --> PROC[Process Plant]
    PROC --> SENS[Sensor Transmitter]
    SENS --> PV
    style Sum1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style FC fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1a5276,stroke-width:2px
    style ACT fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **error** $e(t)$ is fed to the floating controller block labelled $K_f$.
- The controller output is the **velocity** $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = K_f \cdot e$, which is then **integrated** implicitly by the actuator's limited stroke rate.
- The process output is measured, transmitted back as $PV$, and subtracted from $SP$ to form a new error.

## 4.2 State-Transition Flowchart — Floating Controller Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Controller tick]) --> READ[Read current error e]
    READ --> CHK{Is e equal to 0?}
    CHK -- Yes --> HOLD[Hold m t constant]
    CHK -- No --> COMPUTE[Compute drift rate = Kf times e]
    COMPUTE --> SIGN{Sign of e}
    SIGN -- Positive --> INC[m t increases by Kf e dt]
    SIGN -- Negative --> DEC[m t decreases by Kf e dt]
    INC --> LIMIT{m t exceeds limits?}
    DEC --> LIMIT
    HOLD --> OUTPUT[Send m t to actuator]
    LIMIT -- Yes --> CLAMP[Clamp m t to 0 or 100 percent]
    LIMIT -- No --> OUTPUT
    CLAMP --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> WAIT[Wait dt then loop]
    WAIT --> START
    style HOLD fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d
    style INC fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style DEC fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style CLAMP fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#566573
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Floating ≠ Integral

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FLOATING[FLOATING MODE]
        direction TB
        E1[Error e] --> MUL1[Multiplier Kf]
        MUL1 --> LIM[Velocity Limiter plus or minus Vmax]
        LIM --> INT1[Integrator bounded by limiter]
        INT1 --> M1[m t]
    end
    subgraph IDEALI[PURE INTEGRAL MODE]
        direction TB
        E2[Error e] --> MUL2[Multiplier Ki]
        MUL2 --> INT2[Integrator unbounded]
        INT2 --> M2[m t]
    end
    style LIM fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
    style INT1 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d
    style INT2 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d
```

**Architectural insight:** The **velocity limiter** block is the structural element that distinguishes floating control from pure integral control. Without it, the integrator is free to ramp at any speed; with it, the rate is bounded — matching the physical stroking speed of a real valve or damper.

## 4.4 Typical Step Response Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Error step applied]
    E0[Error jumps to 10 percent]:::err
    end
    subgraph B[Controller response]
    M0[m t rises linearly at 20 percent per second]:::out
    end
    subgraph C[Saturation]
    SAT[m t clamps at 100 percent after 5 seconds]:::sat
    end
    A --> B --> C
    classDef err fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1a5276,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sat fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#566573,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` 

**Define floating control mode. Why is it classified as a discontinuous controller mode?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Floating control** is a discontinuous controller mode in which the **controller output changes at a predetermined fixed rate** (the floating speed) whenever the error is non-zero, and **remains constant (floats)** at its last value when the error becomes zero.
>
> **Why discontinuous:** There is no fixed one-to-one functional relationship between the instantaneous error and the controller output. For a given error value, the output can take any value within its range depending on past history — only the **rate of change** $dm/dt$ is uniquely determined by $e$, not the output value $m$ itself.
>
> **Equation:** $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = K_f \cdot e$, with $m(t)$ constant when $e = 0$.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — discontinuous classification justification; 1 mark — equation)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between single-speed and multi-speed floating control modes with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Speed Floating | Multi-Speed Floating |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Drift rate | One fixed magnitude for any non-zero error | Two or more discrete magnitudes depending on error band |
| Error thresholds | None | Defined bands (e.g., small, medium, large error) |
| Complexity | Simplest | Slightly more complex, smoother response |
| Example | Pneumatic controller driving a valve at one stroking speed | 3-speed controller in a level loop: slow / medium / fast drift rates |

*(1 mark — definition of each; 1 mark — comparison; 1 mark — example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern: each Part-B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Answer any ONE full question.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) `[7 marks]`
**Explain the working principle of a single-speed floating controller with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between floating gain and reset time.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. A floating controller continuously monitors the error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
2. If $e \neq 0$, the controller drives its output **up or down at a constant speed** $K_f \cdot \vert e \vert$.
3. The direction of drift is chosen to **reduce the error** (direct or reverse acting).
4. When $e$ reaches **exactly zero**, the controller output is held at its instantaneous value — it "floats".
5. There is **no proportional band**; the controller acts purely as a bounded integrator.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP2[Setpoint] --> SUM2((Sum))
    PV2[Measured PV] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> E2[Error e] --> Kf2[Gain Kf] --> LIM2[Integrator with rate limit] --> M2[m t]
    M2 --> PROC2[Process] --> SENS2[Sensor] --> PV2
```

**Derivation of Floating Gain vs Reset Time (2 marks):**

By definition, reset time $T_i$ is the duration required for the output to traverse 100 % of scale under a 100 % step error.

$$
100\% = K_f \cdot (100\% \text{ error}) \cdot T_i
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\;K_f = \dfrac{100}{T_i} \quad \text{[% per unit time]}\;}
$$

Or equivalently $T_i = 100/K_f$.

---

#### Part (b) `[7 marks]`
**A floating controller has a reset time of 3 minutes. A step error of 8 % is suddenly applied. Calculate (i) the floating gain, (ii) the time required for the controller output to traverse 100 % of its range, and (iii) the controller output after 90 seconds. Assume initial output = 0 %.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_i = 3$ min, $e_0 = 8\,\%$, $m(0) = 0$, $t = 90$ s $= 1.5$ min.

**(i) Floating gain (2 marks):**

$$
K_f = \dfrac{100}{T_i} = \dfrac{100}{3} = 33.33 \;\dfrac{\%\, \text{output}}{\%\, \text{error}\cdot \text{min}}
$$

**(ii) Time for full 100 % traverse under 100 % error (2 marks):**

By definition of reset time, this is exactly $T_i$:

$$
t_{\text{full}} = T_i = 3 \text{ minutes} \;(180 \text{ s})
$$

**(iii) Output after 90 s (3 marks):**

For a constant step error, output rises linearly:

$$
m(t) = m(0) + K_f \cdot e_0 \cdot t
$$

Substituting (using minutes):

$$
m(1.5) = 0 + 33.33 \cdot 8 \cdot 1.5
$$

$$
= 33.33 \cdot 12
$$

$$
= 400 \,\%
$$

But the output cannot exceed 100 % — the actuator saturates at 100 %.

The time to reach 100 % under $e_0 = 8\,\%$ is:

$$
t_{100} = \dfrac{100\%}{K_f \cdot e_0} = \dfrac{100}{33.33 \cdot 8} = \dfrac{100}{266.67} = 0.375 \text{ min} = 22.5 \text{ s}
$$

Since 90 s > 22.5 s, the controller output is **clamped at 100 %**.

$$
\boxed{\;m(90\,\text{s}) = 100\%\;}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) `[7 marks]`
**Compare floating control with two-position (ON–OFF) control and pure integral (I) control. When is floating control preferred over both?**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Two-Position (ON–OFF) | Pure Integral (I) | Floating |
|---------|------------------------|-------------------|----------|
| Output values | Only two discrete positions | Continuous | Continuous |
| Rate of change | Instantaneous | Unbounded (ideal) | **Bounded** by $K_f$ |
| Cycling | Yes, sustained | None (theoretical) | None once $e = 0$ |
| Offset | Always present in the cycling band | Zero (asymptotic) | Zero (after sufficient time) |
| Actuator stress | High (continuous full stroking) | Can be excessive | Low (smooth limited motion) |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Low |

**When floating is preferred (3 marks):**

1. When the actuator has a **finite, fixed stroking speed** (motorised valves, dampers) — floating matches the actuator's natural behaviour, eliminating the unrealistic demand for infinite speed that pure I-control would impose.
2. When a **smooth, slow correction** is needed without cycling (ON-OFF would oscillate, I-control would be too aggressive).
3. Typical applications: **level control in large tanks**, **batch blending**, **temperature control in large thermal masses**.

---

#### Part (b) `[7 marks]`
**A process is controlled by a floating controller with $K_f = 1.5\,\%/\text{min}$ per % error. The error varies as a ramp: $e(t) = 0.5 t$ [%], starting at $t = 0$. Plot the controller output trajectory $m(t)$ for $0 \le t \le 20$ min and compute the output at $t = 10$ min. Take $m(0) = 0$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Governing equation (1 mark):**

$$
\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt} = K_f \cdot e(t) = 1.5 \cdot (0.5 t) = 0.75 t
$$

**Integration (2 marks):**

$$
m(t) = \int_0^t 0.75 t' \, dt' + m(0)
$$

$$
m(t) = 0.75 \cdot \dfrac{t^2}{2} = 0.375 \, t^2 \quad [\%]
$$

**Compute at $t = 10$ min (2 marks):**

$$
m(10) = 0.375 \cdot (10)^2 = 0.375 \cdot 100 = 37.5\;\%
$$

**Trajectory table (2 marks):**

| $t$ (min) | $e(t) = 0.5t$ (%) | $dm/dt = 1.5 e$ (%/min) | $m(t) = 0.375 t^2$ (%) |
|----------:|------------------:|------------------------:|-----------------------:|
| 0         | 0.0               | 0.00                    | 0.0                    |
| 5         | 2.5               | 3.75                    | 9.4                    |
| 10        | 5.0               | 7.50                    | **37.5**               |
| 15        | 7.5               | 11.25                   | 84.4                   |
| 20        | 10.0              | 15.00                   | 150 → **clamped 100**  |

> **Conclusion:** The controller output follows a **parabolic** curve. At $t = 10$ min, $m = 37.5\,\%$. The output would nominally reach 150 % at $t = 20$ min, but it saturates at the actuator limit of 100 %.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfalls & Warning Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Floating Control**
> 1. **Mixing up floating gain and integral gain:** Floating gain $K_f$ carries an implicit "per % error" factor. Pure $K_i$ does not. Writing $K_f$ in units of 1/time without the "%error" dimension loses one full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the saturation clamp:** When $e$ is large, the linear ramp reaches 100 % in finite time. Students who write $m(t) = 200\,\%$ lose 2–3 marks. Always clamp at the actuator's physical limits.
> 3. **Treating floating as identical to ON-OFF:** These are entirely different. ON-OFF has only two output states and exhibits limit cycling. Floating has continuous output and no cycling once $e = 0$.
> 4. **Confusing "float" with "no control":** Floating means *the output holds its last value*, not that the controller has stopped working. It is actively holding the corrective action.
> 5. **Forgetting to state units** in reset-time calculations. Reset time is in *minutes* (pneumatic) or *seconds* (electronic) — pick one and stick with it.
> 6. **Skipping the "memory" feature:** Examiners award a mark for explicitly stating that the output value depends on past history — a defining trait of integral-type actions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Floating Control Mode**
> - ☐ **Definition:** Output drifts at fixed rate when $e \neq 0$; holds constant (floats) when $e = 0$.
> - ☐ **Classification:** **Discontinuous** controller mode (no fixed $e \to m$ mapping).
> - ☐ **Governing law:** $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = K_f \cdot e$ (single-speed form).
> - ☐ **Reset time relation:** $T_i = 100/K_f$, equivalently $K_f = 100/T_i$.
> - ☐ **Output for step error:** $m(t) = m_0 + K_f \cdot e_0 \cdot t$ (linear ramp, clamped at 100 %).
> - ☐ **Output for ramp error:** $m(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} K_f a t^2$ (parabolic).
> - ☐ **Zero offset:** Yes — eliminates steady-state offset in finite time (unlike ON-OFF).
> - ☐ **Proportional band:** **Infinite** (no P-action).
> - ☐ **Memory / integral nature:** Output value depends on past history of error.
> - ☐ **Saturation behaviour:** Output clamps at 0 % or 100 % once limits are reached.
> - ☐ **Difference from I-control:** Bounded velocity (matches real actuator) vs. unbounded theoretical rate.
> - ☐ **Difference from ON-OFF:** Continuous output vs. two discrete states; no limit cycling.
> - ☐ **Typical applications:** Level control in large tanks, slow thermal processes, motorised valve loops.
> - ☐ **Always carry units** of $K_f$ as %output / (%error · time).
> - ☐ **Always check for saturation** after computing $m(t)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Controller Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Continuous Controller Modes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Continuous Controller Modes? **Continuous Controller Modes** are the fundamental mathematical algorithms (or "control laws") that continuously calculate a controller's output signal $m(t)$ as a function of the error signal $e(t)$, where the error is the difference between the setpoint (SP) and the process variable (PV). Unlike discontinuous modes (like on/off or relay control), continuous modes produce a smooth, infinitely variable output within the controller's range, allowing for fine, precise corrective action on the final control element (e.g., a control valve). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A continuous controller mode is a control algorithm whose output $m(t)$ varies continuously as a function of the deviation $e(t)$, such that the controller output can take any value between its minimum and maximum bounds. The standard continuous modes are **P, I, D, PI, PD, and PID**. ## 1.2 The Generic PID Architecture All continuous controller modes are derived from a single, master equation known as the **Parallel (Ideal) PID Equation**: $$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_{0}^{t} e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$ Where: - $m(t)$ = Controller output (sent to final control element) - $m_0$ = Controller bias / initial output at startup - $K_c$ = Proportional gain (dimensionless) - $\tau_I$ = Integral time (also called *reset time*) — measured in **minutes/repeat** - $\tau_D$ = Derivative time (also called *rate time*) — measured in **minutes** - $e(t) = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$ = Error signal By selectively setting $K_c$, $\tau_I$, and $\tau_D$ to specific values, we obtain the six standard continuous modes. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The "Cruise Control" Car Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain exactly **60 km/h** (the setpoint). | Driver Action | Controller Mode | Intuition | |---|---|---| | **Press the accelerator harder the faster the gap grows** | **P (Proportional)** | The harder you fall behind, the harder you push. Like a sprinter reacting to distance lost. | | **Keep a running mental average of past speed errors** | **I (Integral)** | You remember how long you were slow and add steady correction. Eliminates "drifting" below the target. | | **Anticipate future speed based on current acceleration trend** | **D (Derivative)** | If you're closing in too fast, you ease off *before* you overshoot. A defensive, predictive action. | | **Combine any of the above** | **PI, PD, PID** | Real driving mixes reaction, memory, and prediction. | > [!NOTE] > **Concept Hook for KTU Students:** Every continuous controller mode is just a specific "personality" of the same master equation. The exam never asks you to memorize six separate equations — it asks you to derive any one of them by setting two of the three terms ($\tau_I$, $\tau_D$) to **infinity** (effectively disabling them) or **zero** (effectively disabling the P-term). ## 1.4 Classification of Continuous Modes The six canonical continuous controller modes are: 1. **Two-Position / Multiposition** — discontinuous; technically not continuous but forms the baseline. 2. **Proportional (P)** — current error only. 3. **Integral (I)** — accumulated past error only. 4. **Derivative (D)** — predicted future error only. 5. **Proportional + Integral (PI)** — current + past. 6. **Proportional + Derivative (PD)** — current + predicted future. 7. **Proportional + Integral + Derivative (PID)** — full master equation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Reality Check:** The **PI controller** is the workhorse of over **90% of industrial process control loops** (flow, level, temperature in most chemical plants). The **PID** is used where tight, fast tracking is critical. **Pure I, pure D, and pure P** controllers are rarely used alone in practice. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mode-by-Mode Operational Logic ### 2.1.1 Proportional (P) Controller **Time-Domain Equation:** $$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \cdot e(t)$$ **Transfer Function:** $$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c$$ **Operational Logic:** - The output is **directly proportional** to the instantaneous error. - If $e(t) = 0$, output stays at bias $m_0$. - A larger $K_c$ means a more aggressive response. **The "Proportional Band" (PB):** The proportional band is the percentage change in error required to drive the controller output through its full 100% range. It is the **inverse** of the gain expressed in percent: $$\boxed{PB\,\% = \frac{100\%}{K_c}}$$ - **$K_c = 1$** $\Rightarrow$ **PB = 100%** (a "wide" band — gentle action) - **$K_c = 10$** $\Rightarrow$ **PB = 10%** (a "narrow" band — aggressive action) **The Critical Flaw — Offset (Steady-State Error):** Proportional control alone **cannot** eliminate the steady-state error caused by load disturbances. Mathematically, for a load change $\Delta L$ acting on a process with steady-state gain $K_p$: $$e_{ss} = \frac{-\Delta L}{1 + K_c \cdot K_p} \quad \neq 0$$ > [!WARNING] > **The smaller the proportional band (larger $K_c$), the smaller the offset — but the closer the loop gets to instability and sustained oscillation.** This is the central engineering trade-off in P-control. ### 2.1.2 Integral (I) Controller **Time-Domain Equation:** $$m(t) = m_0 + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_{0}^{t} e(t)\,dt$$ **Transfer Function:** $$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = \frac{K_c}{\tau_I \cdot s} = \frac{K_i}{s}$$ where $K_i = K_c / \tau_I$ is the **integral gain**. **Operational Logic:** - The output is proportional to the **time-integral of the error**. - As long as any error exists (positive or negative), the output continues to change — a feature called **"reset action."** - $\tau_I$ (in minutes/repeat) is the time required for the integral action to **repeat** the proportional action. Smaller $\tau_I$ = faster reset. **Elimination of Offset:** Integral action **theoretically drives the steady-state error to zero** because the integrator keeps accumulating output until $e(t) = 0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** $\tau_I$ has the unit **minutes/repeat** (also called "reset time" or "integral time"). The reciprocal $1/\tau_I$ has units of **repeats/minute** and is called the **"reset rate."** ### 2.1.3 Derivative (D) Controller **Time-Domain Equation:** $$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \cdot \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ **Transfer Function:** $$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c \cdot \tau_D \cdot s = K_d \cdot s$$ **Operational Logic:** - The output is proportional to the **rate of change** of the error. - Pure D-control is **never used alone** because if $e(t)$ is constant (even if nonzero), $de/dt = 0$ and the output is zero — useless. - $\tau_D$ is called **"rate time"** or **"derivative time"** in minutes. - Derivative action is **anticipatory**: it acts *before* the error actually grows large. > [!NOTE] > **The "Derivative Kick" Problem:** When the setpoint is changed abruptly, $e(t)$ changes as a step, and $de(t)/dt$ becomes an **impulse**, causing a massive spike in controller output that can damage final control elements. The industry-standard fix is to apply the derivative term to the **PV (measured variable) only**, not to the error. The KTU textbook calls this the **"derivative-on-measurement"** configuration. ### 2.1.4 PI, PD, and PID Controllers (Composite Modes) These are obtained by combining the master PID equation in three combinations: **PI Controller:** $$m(t) = m_0 + K_c\left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt \right] \quad ; \quad G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$$ **PD Controller:** $$m(t) = m_0 + K_c\left[ e(t) + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] \quad ; \quad G_c(s) = K_c(1 + \tau_D s)$$ **PID Controller (Ideal/Parallel Form):** $$m(t) = m_0 + K_c\left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] \quad ; \quad G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$$ ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Mode** | **Controller Output $m(t)$** | **Transfer Function $G_c(s)$** | **Key Parameter** | **Offset?** | |---|---|---|---|---| | P | $m_0 + K_c e(t)$ | $K_c$ | $K_c$ or PB % | **Yes** (non-zero) | | I | $m_0 + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt$ | $\dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I s}$ | $\tau_I$ (min/repeat) | No (theoretically) | | D | $m_0 + K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_c \tau_D s$ | $\tau_D$ (min) | Cannot act alone | | PI | $m_0 + K_c\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt\right]$ | $K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ | $K_c$, $\tau_I$ | No | | PD | $m_0 + K_c\left[e(t) + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$ | $K_c(1 + \tau_D s)$ | $K_c$, $\tau_D$ | Yes (reduced) | | PID | $m_0 + K_c\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$ | $K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$ | $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$ | No | **Universal Relationships:** $$\boxed{PB\,\% = \frac{100}{K_c}} \qquad \boxed{e_{ss}\,\vert_{P} = \frac{-\Delta L}{1+K_c K_p}} \qquad \boxed{\text{Reset rate} = \frac{1}{\tau_I}}$$ ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **P-only** — Used in level control where offset is acceptable (e.g., surge tanks) and where simplicity and stability are paramount. - **I-only** — Used in slow processes like batch temperature ramps; never used for fast loops. - **D-only** — Almost never used alone; the *anticipatory* logic is what makes it valuable *inside* PI or PID. - **PI** — The **default for 90%+ of industrial loops** (flow, level, pressure, most temperature loops). - **PD** — Used where **fast, predictive correction is needed** but long-term error is acceptable (e.g., motion control, servo positioning). - **PID** — Reserved for the most demanding regulation and tracking tasks: temperature control of exothermic reactors, pH control, and motion systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Examiner's Favorite):** For a first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) process with gain $K_p$, time constant $\tau_p$, and dead time $\theta_p$, the textbook (Coughanowr / Stephanopoulos) recommends the following initial tuning settings: $K_c \approx \tau_p / (K_p \theta_p)$ for P-only, and $\tau_I \approx \tau_p$ and $\tau_D \approx \theta_p / 2$ for PID. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Generic Continuous Controller Output Equation The most general continuous controller (the parallel-form PID) is obtained by combining the three basic control actions. ### Step 1: Define the Error Signal $$e(t) = \text{SP}(t) - \text{PV}(t)$$ This is the **negative feedback** error convention used throughout process control. ### Step 2: Define the Proportional Contribution The proportional output is a direct amplification of the error by the controller gain $K_c$, added to the controller bias $m_0$: $$m_P(t) = m_0 + K_c \cdot e(t)$$ **Logic:** A larger error demands a larger corrective output. The bias $m_0$ ensures nonzero output even at zero error (e.g., to hold a valve partially open at startup). ### Step 3: Define the Integral Contribution The integral action is the time-integral of the error, scaled by $K_c$ and divided by the integral time $\tau_I$: $$m_I(t) = m_0 + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau$$ **Logic:** As long as any error persists, the integrator accumulates it and continues to change the output, ensuring that the steady-state error is driven to zero. ### Step 4: Define the Derivative Contribution The derivative action is proportional to the rate of change of the error, scaled by $K_c$ and the derivative time $\tau_D$: $$m_D(t) = m_0 + K_c \cdot \tau_D \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ **Logic:** A rapidly growing error (large $de/dt$) demands an immediate, strong correction — an *anticipatory* action. ### Step 5: Superpose (Add) All Three Contributions The complete parallel-form PID output is the sum: $$\begin{aligned} m(t) &= m_P(t) + m_I(t) + m_D(t) - 2m_0 \\ &= m_0 + K_c\left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] \end{aligned}$$ **Logic:** The $-2m_0$ correction is needed because each of the three sub-equations added its own $m_0$ — we need only one. (This is the textbook pedagogical derivation used in Coughanowr's *Process Systems Analysis and Control*.) ### Step 6: Derive the PID Transfer Function Taking the Laplace transform of the equation above (with $E(s) = \mathcal{L}\{e(t)\}$ and assuming zero initial conditions): $$\begin{aligned} M(s) &= m_0/s + K_c\left[ E(s) + \frac{1}{\tau_I} \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + \tau_D \cdot s E(s) \right] \\ M(s) - m_0/s &= K_c \cdot E(s) \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right] \end{aligned}$$ Dividing by $E(s)$ (treating the bias term as a separate, additive constant): $$\boxed{G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right)}$$ ### Step 7: Derive the Sub-Mode Transfer Functions by Substitution | Mode | Setting | Resulting Transfer Function | |---|---|---| | **P** | $\tau_I \to \infty$, $\tau_D = 0$ | $G_c(s) = K_c$ | | **I** | $K_c = 1$, $\tau_D = 0$ (ideal) | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} = \dfrac{K_i}{s}$ | | **D** | $K_c = 1$, $\tau_I \to \infty$ (ideal) | $G_c(s) = \tau_D s = K_d s$ | | **PI** | $\tau_D = 0$ | $G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ | | **PD** | $\tau_I \to \infty$ | $G_c(s) = K_c(1 + \tau_D s)$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why the limits?** In the P-mode, you "disable" the integral by making $\tau_I$ infinite (so $1/\tau_I = 0$). You "disable" the derivative by setting $\tau_D = 0$. KTU examiners frequently test this substitution logic — *memorize the limits, not the sub-equations.* ## 3.2 Derivation: Proportional Band and Steady-State Offset ### Step 1: Definition of Proportional Band By definition, the proportional band is the range of error (in percent of the error span) required to traverse 100% of the controller output span. So: $$PB\,\% = \frac{\Delta e \,(\%)}{\Delta m \,(\%)} \times 100$$ ### Step 2: Express in Terms of Gain Since the controller gain is defined as $K_c = \Delta m / \Delta e$, and both $\Delta m$ and $\Delta e$ are expressed over their full spans, the ratio of percentage changes is exactly the inverse of $K_c$: $$PB\,\% = \frac{1}{K_c} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{PB\,\% = \frac{100}{K_c}}$$ ### Step 3: Derive Steady-State Offset under Load Disturbance Consider a process with transfer function $G_p(s) = K_p / (\tau_p s + 1)$ subjected to a step load disturbance $L(s) = \Delta L / s$. With a P-controller $G_c(s) = K_c$, the closed-loop response to the load is found by the standard final-value theorem. The control loop equation for the load effect on PV is: $$PV(s) = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} \cdot L(s)$$ The error is $E(s) = -PV(s)$ (since SP = 0 for disturbance-only analysis). Apply the **Final Value Theorem**: $$\begin{aligned} e_{ss} &= \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) \\ &= \lim_{s \to 0} s \left[ -\frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{s} \right] \\ &= -\frac{G_p(0)}{1 + G_c(0)\, G_p(0)} \cdot \Delta L \\ &= -\frac{K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} \cdot \Delta L \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{-K_p \cdot \Delta L}{1 + K_c K_p}}$$ **Verification of offset-free PI behavior:** For a PI controller, $G_c(0) = \infty$ (integrator at DC), so the denominator $\to \infty$, driving $e_{ss} \to 0$. This **proves mathematically** that PI control eliminates offset. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Step-Response Behavior Comparison For a unit-step error input $E(s) = 1/s$ at $t=0$: | Mode | $M(s) = G_c(s) \cdot (1/s)$ | $m(t)$ (time-domain) | Step-Response Character | |---|---|---|---| | **P** | $K_c / s$ | $K_c \cdot u(t)$ | Instantaneous jump to $K_c$, holds steady. **No decay, no growth.** | | **I** | $K_c / (\tau_I s^2)$ | $\dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I} \cdot t$ | Continuous ramp; output grows linearly forever as long as error persists. | | **D** | $K_c \tau_D$ | $K_c \tau_D \cdot \delta(t)$ | Impulse at $t=0$ (Dirac delta), then zero. **Cannot sustain a nonzero output.** | | **PI** | $\dfrac{K_c}{s} + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I s^2}$ | $K_c + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I} t$ | Initial jump to $K_c$, then ramps. Eliminated offset. | | **PD** | $K_c + K_c \tau_D$ | $K_c(1 + \tau_D \delta(t))$ | Initial impulse spike plus steady $K_c$. Reduced offset (proportional to $\tau_D$ growth). | | **PID** | $\dfrac{K_c}{s} + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I s^2} + K_c \tau_D$ | $K_c + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I} t + K_c \tau_D \delta(t)$ | Spike, jump, then ramp. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** The most common 7-mark sub-part in Module 2 is: *"Sketch the response of a P, PI, and PID controller to a unit-step error input and explain the role of each term."* The table above is the answer-key foundation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master PID Block Diagram (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR spNode["Setpoint SP"] --> sumA(("+")) pvFbk["Process Variable PV"] --> sumB(("−")) sumA --> errNode["Error e(t) = SP − PV"] errNode --> gainP["Proportional Path
Kc × e(t)"] errNode --> gainI["Integral Path
Kc over tauI × integral e dt"] errNode --> gainD["Derivative Path
Kc × tauD × de dt"] biasNode["Controller Bias m0"] --> sumC(("+")) gainP --> sumC gainI --> sumC gainD --> sumC sumC --> outNode["Controller Output m(t)"] outNode --> fce["Final Control Element
e.g., Control Valve"] fce --> process["Process Plant Gp(s)"] process --> sensor["Sensor / Transmitter"] sensor --> pvFbk ``` ## 4.2 PI Controller Block Diagram (Industrial Workhorse) ```mermaid flowchart LR spA["SP"] --> sumA1(("+")) pvA["PV"] --> sumA2(("−")) sumA1 --> errA["e(t)"] errA --> kcA["Kc"] errA --> intA["1 over tauI × s
Integrator"] kcA --> sumA3(("+")) intA --> sumA3 m0A["Bias m0"] --> sumA3 sumA3 --> outA["m(t) to Valve"] outA --> procA["Process"] procA --> transA["Transmitter"] transA --> pvA ``` ## 4.3 PD Controller Block Diagram (Anticipatory Action) ```mermaid flowchart LR spB["SP"] --> sumB1(("+")) pvB["PV"] --> sumB2(("−")) sumB1 --> errB["e(t)"] errB --> kcB["Kc"] errB --> devB["tauD × s
Differentiator"] kcB --> sumB3(("+")) devB --> sumB3 m0B["Bias m0"] --> sumB3 sumB3 --> outB["m(t)"] outB --> procB["Process"] procB --> transB["Transmitter"] transB --> pvB ``` ## 4.4 Mode Selection Decision Tree (Engineer's Guide) ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Start: Choose Controller Mode"]) --> q1{"Is zero
steady-state
offset required?"} q1 -- "No (e.g., surge tank level)" --> pOnly["Use P-Only Controller
Tune Kc"] q1 -- "Yes" --> q2{"Is the loop fast
and prone to
overshoot?"} q2 -- "Yes (e.g., motion, servo)" --> pdOnly["Use PD Controller
Tune Kc and tauD"] q2 -- "No" --> q3{"Is the process slow
or have significant
dead time?"} q3 -- "Yes (e.g., temperature, pH)" --> piMode["Use PI Controller
Tune Kc and tauI
90% of industrial use"] q3 -- "Extremely slow or complex" --> pidMode["Use PID Controller
Tune Kc, tauI, tauD"] piMode --> end1(["Implement and test with
Ziegler-Nichols or Cohen-Coon"]) pidMode --> end1 pOnly --> end1 pdOnly --> end1 ``` ## 4.5 Open-Loop Step-Response Comparison (Mermaid Graph View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P_Mode["P Mode Output m(t)"] pA["Kc × step input"]:::pClass --> pB["Steady value Kc
No ramp, no decay"] end subgraph PI_Mode["PI Mode Output m(t)"] piA["Kc + Kc over tauI integral"]:::piClass --> piB["Initial jump then
linear ramp until e=0"] end subgraph PD_Mode["PD Mode Output m(t)"] pdA["Kc plus impulse
spike at t=0"]:::pdClass --> pdB["Steady value Kc
plus transient spike"] end subgraph PID_Mode["PID Mode Output m(t)"] pidA["Kc + ramp + impulse"]:::pidClass --> pidB["Spike, jump, then
ramp until e=0"] end classDef pClass fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#1F77B4 classDef piClass fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D4A017 classDef pdClass fill:#E2F0D9,stroke:#548235 classDef pidClass fill:#FBE5D6,stroke:#C55A11 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Hand-Drawn Schematics?** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **functional understanding** of how each controller term is computed and combined, rather than the exact P&ID symbols. These block diagrams clearly show the parallel decomposition of the master PID equation and the feedback signal flow — which is the focus of the 14-mark KTU questions. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define proportional band. How is it related to the controller gain $K_c$?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The proportional band (PB) is defined as the range of error (expressed as a percentage of the error span) required to drive the controller output through 100% of its range. It is mathematically the inverse of the controller gain, expressed in percent: $$PB\,\% = \frac{100}{K_c}$$ For example, a controller with $K_c = 5$ has a proportional band of 20%, meaning a 20% change in the error span causes a full 100% swing in the output. A narrow PB indicates an aggressive (high-gain) controller, while a wide PB indicates a gentle response. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark example).* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is "reset windup" in a PI controller, and how is it prevented in practice?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Reset windup (also called **integral windup**) is the phenomenon in which the integral term of a PI or PID controller continues to accumulate (integrate) the error even after the controller output has reached its saturation limits (e.g., 0% or 100% valve position). When the error finally changes sign, the accumulated integral value is so large that it takes a long time to "unwind," causing severe overshoot and poor recovery. **Prevention methods include:** (i) **Anti-windup (back-calculation)** — feeding back the difference between the actual and computed output to discharge the integrator; (ii) **Clamping the integral term** between output limits; (iii) **Conditional integration** — stopping integration when output is saturated; and (iv) **Setpoint ramping** to avoid large instantaneous errors. *(1 mark problem, 2 marks solutions).* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]` **a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, derive the transfer function of a parallel-form (ideal) PID controller. Show how PI, PD, and P-only controllers are obtained from the master equation. **(7 Marks)** **b)** A first-order process with transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{5s+1}$ is controlled by a proportional controller of gain $K_c = 4$. A unit-step load disturbance enters the loop. Calculate the steady-state offset and the proportional band. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]` **a)** Derive the time-domain response of a **PI controller** to a unit-step error input. Sketch the response and explain why PI control eliminates the steady-state offset present in pure P-control. **(7 Marks)** **b)** A temperature control loop uses a PID controller in **derivative-on-measurement** mode to avoid the "derivative kick" problem. Explain this configuration, derive its transfer function, and state one practical situation where PD-only control is preferred over PI-only control. **(7 Marks)** --- ## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions ### Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Master PID equation in time domain (2 marks)** The general parallel-form (ideal) PID controller output is the sum of three parallel actions, added to the controller bias $m_0$: $$m(t) = m_0 + K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 2: Take the Laplace transform (1 mark)** Assuming zero initial conditions and treating the bias $m_0$ as an additive constant $m_0/s$: $$M(s) = \frac{m_0}{s} + K_c E(s)\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$$ **Step 3: Solve for the transfer function (1 mark)** $$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$$ **Step 4: Derive sub-mode transfer functions (3 marks)** | Mode | Setting | Resulting $G_c(s)$ | |---|---|---| | **P** | $\tau_I \to \infty$, $\tau_D = 0$ | $K_c$ | | **PI** | $\tau_D = 0$ | $K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ | | **PD** | $\tau_I \to \infty$ | $K_c(1 + \tau_D s)$ | **Block Diagram (already covered in SECTION_4.1):** Reference the master PID block diagram with three parallel branches (P, I, D) feeding into a summing junction that adds the bias $m_0$ before sending the output to the final control element. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{5s+1}$, $K_c = 4$, load $\Delta L = 1$ (unit step). **Step 1: Find the steady-state offset using the final-value theorem (3 marks)** The closed-loop error response to a load $L(s) = 1/s$ is: $$E(s) = -\frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$ Substituting $G_c(s) = K_c = 4$ and $G_p(s) = 2/(5s+1)$: $$E(s) = -\frac{\dfrac{2}{5s+1}}{1 + \dfrac{8}{5s+1}} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = -\frac{2}{(5s+1) + 8} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = -\frac{2}{5s+9} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$ Applying the Final Value Theorem: $$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \left(-\frac{2}{s(5s+9)}\right) = -\frac{2}{9} = -0.222$$ **Offset = $-0.222$ units (or 22.2% of the load magnitude).** ✓ **Step 2: Find the proportional band (1 mark)** $$PB\,\% = \frac{100}{K_c} = \frac{100}{4} = 25\%$$ **Step 3: Verification and discussion (3 marks)** - The offset is **nonzero**, confirming the inherent flaw of P-only control. - To reduce the offset, $K_c$ must be increased (e.g., $K_c = 19$ would give $e_{ss} = -0.1$). - However, increasing $K_c$ reduces the proportional band and may cause oscillation or instability if the loop is not properly damped. - This is why **PI control is preferred in practice** — it achieves the offset elimination that P-control cannot. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Question A:** > 1. **Forgetting the bias term $m_0$** in the PID equation: students often write $m(t) = K_c[\dots]$ without the bias, losing 0.5 mark. > 2. **Sign of the offset:** Some students write a positive offset; always re-derive carefully. The negative sign indicates the PV falls *below* the setpoint under a positive load. > 3. **Confusing $K_c$ with $K_p$:** The process gain is $K_p = 2$, the controller gain is $K_c = 4$. They are not the same. > 4. **Final value theorem must use the closed-loop transfer function**, not just the open-loop process. --- ### Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: PI controller equation (1 mark)** A PI controller has transfer function $G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$. **Step 2: Unit-step error input (1 mark)** For a unit-step error, $E(s) = 1/s$. **Step 3: Compute controller output in Laplace domain (1 mark)** $$M(s) = G_c(s) E(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{K_c}{s} + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I s^2}$$ **Step 4: Take the inverse Laplace transform (2 marks)** Using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/s\} = u(t)$ (unit step) and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/s^2\} = t$ (unit ramp): $$\boxed{m(t) = K_c \cdot u(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \cdot t}$$ **Step 5: Response sketch description (1 mark)** - At $t = 0^+$: $m(t)$ jumps **instantaneously** to $K_c$ (the proportional kick). - For $t > 0$: $m(t)$ increases **linearly** with slope $K_c/\tau_I$ (the ramp from the integral action). - This continues **until $e(t) = 0$**, at which point the ramp stops (slope becomes zero) and $m(t)$ holds its final value. **Step 6: Explanation of offset elimination (1 mark)** For pure P-control, the output reaches a constant $K_c \cdot e_{ss}$ at steady state, leaving a residual error $e_{ss} = -\Delta L/(1+K_c K_p)$. With the PI controller's ramp term, the output continues to change **as long as any error exists**, so the only steady state consistent with a constant output is $e = 0$. Therefore, integral action drives the offset to zero. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: The "derivative kick" problem (1.5 marks)** When the setpoint is changed abruptly (e.g., SP step change), the error $e(t)$ changes as a step function. The derivative of a step is an **impulse (Dirac delta)**. Multiplied by $K_c \tau_D$, this impulse appears as a huge spike in the controller output that can damage valves, cause actuator saturation, and create unsafe transients in processes like exothermic reactors. **Step 2: The derivative-on-measurement fix (1.5 marks)** Instead of differentiating the error $e(t) = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$, the controller differentiates **only the PV**. Mathematically, the controller output becomes: $$m(t) = m_0 + K_c e(t) - K_c \tau_D \frac{d\text{PV}(t)}{dt}$$ **Note the negative sign:** Because the derivative term originally was $+K_c \tau_D (de/dt)$ and $de/dt = -d\text{PV}/dt$ (SP being constant), the sign flips. The setpoint step no longer affects the derivative branch, eliminating the kick. **Step 3: Transfer function of the modified controller (2 marks)** Taking the Laplace transform with $\text{SP}(s)$ and $\text{PV}(s)$ treated as separate inputs: $$M(s) = K_c E(s) - K_c \tau_D s \cdot \text{PV}(s)$$ Splitting $E(s) = \text{SP}(s) - \text{PV}(s)$: $$M(s) = K_c \text{SP}(s) - K_c(1 + \tau_D s) \text{PV}(s)$$ In transfer function form (treating SP as input): $$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = K_c\left(1 + \tau_D s\right) \quad \text{(for the load-response path)}$$ But the **effective open-loop path** for disturbance rejection uses the modified signal flow. The PV-derivative branch is a **separate feedback loop** that does not respond to SP changes — this is the essence of derivative-on-measurement. **Step 4: When is PD preferred over PI? (2 marks)** PD control is preferred in: - **Servo and motion control systems** where setpoint tracking is frequent and the speed of response (bandwidth) is critical. - **Process loops with significant dead time**, where the derivative action can "look ahead" and begin correction before the error grows large. - **Systems where integral action is undesirable** because the slow integrator would cause windup (e.g., batch processes with frequent actuator saturation). In contrast, **PI** is preferred for **regulatory control** (rejecting load disturbances at a constant setpoint) in flow, level, and pressure loops. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Question B:** > 1. **The sign of the derivative term** in the modified equation is **negative** ($-K_c \tau_D \cdot d\text{PV}/dt$), but many students write it positive. This is the single most common error in this topic. > 2. **Forgetting to explain the *why***: students define derivative-on-measurement but don't mention that it eliminates the impulse response to SP changes. > 3. **Confusing PD and PI selection criteria**: PD is not "better" than PI — they are different tools. The exam expects you to articulate the trade-off. > 4. **Units of $\tau_D$:** Always **minutes** (or seconds, depending on the problem's time base). The KTU 2024 scheme accepts both, but be consistent. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuous Controller Modes** - ☐ The **master PID equation** is the source of all six continuous modes. Memorize it as $m(t) = m_0 + K_c[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}]$. - ☐ The **parallel (ideal) PID transfer function** is $G_c(s) = K_c(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s)$. - ☐ **Proportional Band** $PB\,\% = 100/K_c$ — inverse relationship; small PB = aggressive, large PB = gentle. - ☐ **P-only control** has a **nonzero steady-state offset** under load: $e_{ss} = -K_p \Delta L / (1+K_c K_p)$. - ☐ **Integral action** (when active) **eliminates offset** but introduces **reset windup** if output saturates. - ☐ **Derivative action** is **anticipatory** but **cannot be used alone** because $de/dt = 0$ at constant error. - ☐ **Derivative-on-measurement** is the industrial solution to the **derivative kick** problem. - ☐ **$\tau_I$** has units of **minutes/repeat**; its reciprocal is the **reset rate** in **repeats/minute**. - ☐ **$\tau_D$** has units of **minutes** (or seconds, depending on time base). - ☐ **PI is the workhorse** of industrial control (90%+ of loops); **PID** is reserved for demanding applications; **PD** is for fast servo systems. - ☐ Industrial tuning typically follows **Ziegler-Nichols** or **Cohen-Coon** methods (referenced for the next module). - ☐ For a unit-step error input: - P gives an instantaneous jump (constant) - I gives a continuous ramp - D gives an impulse (then zero) - PI = jump + ramp - PD = impulse + jump - PID = impulse + jump + ramp > **End of Notes — Module 2, Continuous Controller Modes**

topics.title: Proportional Controller Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proportional Controller Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Proportional Controller Mode (P-Mode)** is a fundamental continuous (analog) control action in which the controller output **m(t)** is linearly proportional to the deviation of the controlled variable (process variable, PV) from the desired set point (SP). The magnitude of this deviation is termed the **error signal e(t)**.

The most widely used mathematical representation (used universally in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations) is:

$$
m(t) = m_0 + K_c \, e(t)
$$

Where:
- $m(t)$ = Controller output (manipulated variable, MV) at time *t*
- $m_0$ = Controller output bias (also called *set-point value of output* or *manual reset value*)
- $K_c$ = **Proportional Gain** (dimensionless, but carries engineering unit conversion: %/% for electronic controllers)
- $e(t)$ = Error signal, generally taken as $e(t) = SP - PV$ for a **reverse-acting** controller

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — The Cruise Control Analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and want to maintain a speed of **80 km/h**. As the road inclines (a load disturbance), the car slows down. You instinctively press the accelerator **proportionally** to how much the speed has dropped from 80. The harder the car slows down, the harder you press. This "pressing the accelerator in proportion to the error" is exactly what a **P-controller** does to a final control element (valve, heater, motor).

The proportional controller is essentially a **variable amplifier**: it amplifies the error by a factor $K_c$ and adds it to a fixed bias $m_0$ to drive the final control element.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Standardized Metric:** In industrial process control instrumentation, the controller output and PV are usually normalized to a **standard pneumatic range of 3–15 psi** or an **electronic range of 4–20 mA / 0–100%**. This standardization is essential for KTU numerical problems.

- **Proportional Band (PB)** is defined as the percentage change in error required to drive the controller output through its full range (100%).

$$
PB\,(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}
$$

- **Proportional Gain** $K_c$ is unitless in pure form, but in industrial practice it is expressed in **%/% (output percent per input percent)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The proportional controller operates on a strict **closed-loop negative feedback** principle. The following structured sequence describes its operation:

1. **Measurement:** The sensor/transmitter measures the process variable (PV) and sends it to the controller.
2. **Error Generation:** The controller computes the deviation: $e(t) = SP - PV$.
3. **Proportional Action:** The error is multiplied by the gain $K_c$. This is the **only** corrective action — there is no integral or derivative term.
4. **Biasing:** The amplified error is added to the bias $m_0$ (a pre-set operating point) to produce the controller output.
5. **Actuation:** The output $m(t)$ adjusts the final control element (e.g., valve opening, heater power).
6. **Correction:** The process responds, PV changes, new error is computed, and the loop continues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Proportional" and not "Bang-Bang"?** Unlike a two-position (ON/OFF) controller, the P-controller produces a **continuously varying** output proportional to the error. This eliminates the destructive cycling associated with relay-type controllers.

### 2.2 Characteristics of Proportional Control

- **Continuous output** — output is smooth, not discrete.
- **No anticipation** — controller cannot predict future errors.
- **Self-regulating** within the proportional band.
- **Introduces offset** under load disturbance (a permanent, steady-state error).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $m(t) = m_0 + K_c e(t)$ | Basic P-controller equation | Output in % or psi or mA |
| 2 | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Error definition (reverse acting) | Same as PV |
| 3 | $PB(\%) = \dfrac{100}{K_c}$ | Proportional Band | Percent (%) |
| 4 | $K_c = \dfrac{100}{PB}$ | Gain from PB | dimensionless |
| 5 | $G_c(s) = K_c$ | Transfer function of P-controller | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Offset} = \dfrac{\Delta D}{1 + K_c K_p K_v}$ | Steady-state offset for a load change $\Delta D$ | Same as PV |
| 7 | $m(t) = K_c e(t)$ | If bias $m_0$ is taken as zero reference | — |
| 8 | $K_p K_c \gg 1$ | Condition for small offset | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE problems, you will be given a **pneumatic** (3–15 psi) or **electronic** (4–20 mA) controller and asked to compute $K_c$ or $PB$. Always **convert ranges to percentage** before using the formulas.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Temperature control** in furnaces, ovens, and reactors.
- **Level control** in tanks and boilers.
- **Pressure control** in distillation columns and gas pipelines.
- **Flow control** in chemical and petrochemical plants.
- **Speed control** of DC motors in industrial drives.

P-only control is preferred in processes where **large capacity** and **slow response** dominate, and where a small steady-state offset is acceptable (e.g., tank level, surge vessels).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Proportional Band and Gain

**Given:** A controller output range of 0–100% and an input (error) range of 0–100%.

**Step 1:** Express the proportional action linearly.

$$
m(t) - m_0 = K_c \cdot e(t)
$$

**Step 2:** Define the proportional band $PB$ as the error range (in % of input span) that produces a **full 100% swing** in the controller output.

$$
100\% \, \text{output} = K_c \cdot (PB\% \, \text{of input})
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the percent values directly.

$$
100 = K_c \cdot PB
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for proportional band.

$$
PB\,(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the inverse.

$$
K_c = \frac{100}{PB}
$$

> This proves that **proportional band and gain are reciprocally related** — a high gain implies a narrow PB, meaning a small error produces a large output change (aggressive control).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Offset (Load Disturbance)

**Given:** A first-order process with transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$, a P-controller $G_c(s) = K_c$, and a load disturbance $D(s) = \dfrac{\Delta D}{s}$.

**Step 1:** The closed-loop transfer function for the load response (assuming $R(s)=0$).

$$
\frac{PV(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) G_v(s)}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Final Value Theorem** to find steady-state error under a step disturbance $\Delta D$.

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \left[ \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) G_v(s)} \cdot D(s) \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $D(s) = \dfrac{\Delta D}{s}$.

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \left[ \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) G_v(s)} \cdot \Delta D \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the transfer functions at $s \to 0$.

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{-K_p \cdot \Delta D}{1 + K_c K_p K_v}
$$

**Step 5:** The **magnitude of steady-state offset** is therefore.

$$
\text{Offset} = \frac{\Delta D \cdot K_p}{1 + K_c K_p K_v}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Offset is **inversely proportional to controller gain $K_c$**. Increasing $K_c$ reduces offset, but it also makes the system more oscillatory and may lead to instability. This is the **fundamental trade-off of proportional control**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Closed-Loop Step Response for Set-Point Change

**Given:** A unity feedback system with $G_c(s) = K_c$ and $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$, and a final control element $G_v = 1$.

**Step 1:** Forward path transfer function.

$$
G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}
$$

**Step 2:** Closed-loop transfer function.

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p}
$$

**Step 3:** Normalize by dividing numerator and denominator by $(1 + K_c K_p)$.

$$
T(s) = \frac{\frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}}{\frac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p} s + 1}
$$

**Step 4:** For a unit step set-point change, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$.

$$
PV(s) = T(s) \cdot R(s) = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p} s + 1}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply inverse Laplace transform.

$$
PV(t) = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} \left( 1 - e^{-\frac{1+K_c K_p}{\tau_p} t} \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Steady-state value.

$$
PV_{ss} = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}
$$

**Step 7:** Offset for a unit set-point change (assuming $PV$ and $SP$ have the same scale).

$$
e_{ss} = 1 - \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}
$$

> This derivation proves that **P-control cannot achieve zero steady-state error** for a step set-point change (only an integrating process, like a level with no outflow restriction, can overcome this naturally).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Model):** A pneumatic proportional controller has an input range of 3–15 psi and output range of 3–15 psi. The set point is 9 psi and the measured PV is 10.5 psi. The bias $m_0$ = 9 psi and the proportional band is 50%. Calculate the controller output.

**Step 1: Convert inputs to percent of span.**

$$
SP(\%) = \frac{9 - 3}{15 - 3} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

$$
PV(\%) = \frac{10.5 - 3}{15 - 3} \times 100 = 62.5\%
$$

**Step 2: Compute error in percent.**

$$
e(\%) = SP - PV = 50 - 62.5 = -12.5\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute $K_c$.**

$$
K_c = \frac{100}{PB} = \frac{100}{50} = 2
$$

**Step 4: Compute output in percent.**

$$
m(\%) = m_0(\%) + K_c \cdot e(\%) = 50 + 2 \times (-12.5) = 25\%
$$

**Step 5: Convert output to psi.**

$$
m = 3 + (0.25)(15 - 3) = 3 + 3 = 6 \, \text{psi}
$$

> **Answer:** Controller output is **6 psi**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Proportional Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Set Point SP] --> A1[Summing Junction]
    A1 --> E[Error e t]
    E --> C[P Controller Kc]
    C --> M[Controller Output m t]
    M --> V[Final Control Element Valve]
    V --> P[Process Gp s]
    P --> PV[Process Variable PV]
    PV --> H[Feedback Sensor H s]
    H --> A1
    style R fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22
    style V fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
    style P fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B
    style PV fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Components of a P-Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Signal SP and PV] --> B[Error Detector e t = SP - PV]
    B --> C[Gain Amplifier Kc]
    D[Bias Setting m0] --> C
    C --> E[Output m t = m0 + Kc e t]
    E --> F[Final Control Element Valve / Heater / Motor]
    F --> G[Process Plant]
    G --> H[Measurement Transmitter]
    H --> A
    style B fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
```

### 4.3 Step Response Waveform — Effect of Increasing $K_c$ on Offset

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[LOW GAIN Small Kc]
        A11[Slow response with LARGE offset]
    end
    subgraph A2[MEDIUM GAIN]
        A21[Faster response with MODERATE offset]
    end
    subgraph A3[HIGH GAIN Large Kc]
        A31[Fast response with SMALL offset but OSCILLATORY]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3
    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style A2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style A3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The progression above visually summarizes the **P-controller trade-off curve**. KTU examiners often ask students to *qualitatively* sketch or describe this behavior in 3-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Proportional Controller Mode. Write its mathematical equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Proportional Controller Mode is a continuous control action in which the controller output $m(t)$ is linearly proportional to the error signal $e(t)$. The governing equation is:

$$
m(t) = m_0 + K_c \, e(t)
$$

- **$m(t)$** → Controller output (manipulated variable).
- **$m_0$** → Bias / manual reset value (controller output when error is zero).
- **$K_c$** → Proportional gain (sensitivity of the controller).
- **$e(t)$** → Error signal = $SP - PV$.

The significance is that the corrective action is **instantaneous and proportional** to the magnitude of the deviation, making it faster than integral action but introducing a steady-state offset.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Proportional Band? How is it related to Proportional Gain?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Proportional Band (PB)** is defined as the **percentage change in error (input) required to cause a 100% change in the controller output**.

It is **inversely proportional** to the controller gain:

$$
PB(\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}
$$

- A **small PB** (large $K_c$) → aggressive control, smaller offset, but risk of oscillation.
- A **large PB** (small $K_c$) → sluggish control, large offset, but stable.

This trade-off is fundamental to P-controller tuning.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state offset expression for a proportional controller subjected to a step load disturbance in a unity feedback first-order process. Discuss why the offset cannot be eliminated by increasing gain indefinitely. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature process has a range of 100–500°C. The set point is set to 350°C and the PV is currently 380°C. A pneumatic controller (3–15 psi) has a proportional band of 40% and a bias of 9 psi. Determine the controller output in psi. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Write the process transfer function (first order).

$$
G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the controller and final control element transfer functions.

$$
G_c(s) = K_c, \quad G_v(s) = K_v
$$

**Step 3:** For a step load disturbance $\Delta D$ at the process input, the closed-loop response (with $R(s) = 0$) is:

$$
\frac{PV(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) G_v(s)}
$$

**Step 4:** Error under disturbance:

$$
E(s) = -PV(s) = \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) G_v(s)} \cdot D(s)
$$

**Step 5:** Final Value Theorem with $D(s) = \dfrac{\Delta D}{s}$:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \left[ \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) G_v(s)} \cdot \Delta D \right]
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $s \to 0$:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{-K_p \Delta D}{1 + K_c K_p K_v}
$$

**Step 7:** Magnitude of offset.

$$
\text{Offset} = \frac{K_p \Delta D}{1 + K_c K_p K_v}
$$

**Discussion (1 Mark):** Although increasing $K_c$ reduces offset, practical limitations (valve saturation, sensor noise amplification, system instability, and process nonlinearity) prevent $K_c \to \infty$. At very high gain, the closed-loop poles cross into the right-half plane, making the system unstable and oscillatory.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert set point to percent of span.**

$$
SP(\%) = \frac{350 - 100}{500 - 100} \times 100 = 62.5\%
$$

**Step 2: Convert PV to percent of span.**

$$
PV(\%) = \frac{380 - 100}{500 - 100} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute error.**

$$
e(\%) = 62.5 - 70 = -7.5\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute $K_c$ from PB.**

$$
K_c = \frac{100}{PB} = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5
$$

**Step 5: Convert bias to percent.**

$$
m_0 = \frac{9 - 3}{15 - 3} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step 6: Compute output in percent.**

$$
m(\%) = 50 + 2.5 \times (-7.5) = 50 - 18.75 = 31.25\%
$$

**Step 7: Convert output to psi.**

$$
m = 3 + 0.3125 \times (15 - 3) = 3 + 3.75 = 6.75 \, \text{psi}
$$

> **Answer:** Controller output = **6.75 psi**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the operation of a closed-loop proportional control system. Identify each block and state the function of the summing junction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A proportional controller with $K_c = 4$ and bias $m_0 = 50\%$ is used to control a process. The set point changes from 40% to 50%. Find the controller output (a) immediately after the change, and (b) at the new steady state, assuming the process has unity gain and the final control element has unity gain. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram Description:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Set Point (SP)** | Desired value of the controlled variable |
| **Process Variable (PV)** | Actual measured value of the process |
| **Summing Junction** | Computes $e(t) = SP - PV$ (subtractive feedback) |
| **P-Controller ($K_c$)** | Amplifies the error by gain $K_c$ |
| **Bias $m_0$** | Adds manual reset value to controller output |
| **Final Control Element** | Adjusts manipulated variable (valve, heater) |
| **Process** | Plant whose PV must be regulated |
| **Sensor/Transmitter** | Converts PV into feedback signal (H(s)) |

**Function of Summing Junction (2 Marks):** The summing junction algebraically subtracts the feedback signal (PV) from the reference input (SP) to produce the error $e(t)$. The negative sign at the feedback path is what makes the system **closed-loop negative feedback** — essential for self-correcting behavior and stability.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute new error immediately after the change.**

Initial PV (at old steady state) = 40% (since process gain = 1 and previous SP was 40%).

New SP = 50%, PV still = 40% (immediately after change).

$$
e_{initial} = 50 - 40 = 10\%
$$

**Step 2: Controller output immediately after change.**

$$
m_{initial} = 50 + 4 \times 10 = 90\%
$$

**Step 3: At the new steady state, the process output equals the set point (process gain = 1).**

$$
PV_{ss} = 50\%, \quad e_{ss} = 50 - 50 = 0\%
$$

**Step 4: Controller output at new steady state.**

$$
m_{ss} = 50 + 4 \times 0 = 50\%
$$

> **Answer:** (a) Immediately after the change: **90%** (b) At the new steady state: **50%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Students often forget to convert psi to % or °C to %. Always normalize to **0–100%** before applying P-controller equations.
> 2. **Sign convention confusion** — KTU adopts the **reverse-acting** convention $e = SP - PV$. If the process is direct-acting, the controller action must be reversed.
> 3. **Forgetting the bias $m_0$** — In some problems, the bias is given as a percentage, in others as engineering units. Mismatch of units loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusion between set-point change offset and load-disturbance offset** — The formulas are different. Be careful which scenario the question addresses.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Proportional Controller** output is a **linear function of the error**: $m(t) = m_0 + K_c e(t)$.
- ✅ **Proportional Band (PB)** and **Gain $K_c$** are reciprocally related: $PB = 100/K_c$.
- ✅ **Bias $m_0$** is the controller output when error = 0; it allows the controller to operate within an active range.
- ✅ P-control produces a **steady-state offset** for both set-point changes and load disturbances.
- ✅ Offset magnitude = $\dfrac{\Delta D \cdot K_p}{1 + K_c K_p K_v}$ (load disturbance case).
- ✅ Increasing $K_c$ → smaller offset but **higher oscillation and instability risk**.
- ✅ P-control is preferred for **slow, large-capacity processes** (level, surge tanks, blending).
- ✅ P-control **cannot** track ramp inputs (non-zero steady-state error).
- ✅ Standard industrial ranges: **pneumatic 3–15 psi**, **electronic 4–20 mA**, **digital 0–100%**.
- ✅ Transfer function: $G_c(s) = K_c$ (purely real, no dynamics).
- ✅ **Two-position (ON/OFF)** is **NOT** proportional — P-mode is continuous.
- ✅ Real-world analogy: **cruise control / accelerator pressing proportional to speed drop**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integral Controller Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integral Controller Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Integral Controller Mode** (also known as the **Reset Mode** or **Floating Mode**) is a continuous control action in which the rate of change of the controller output, $p(t)$, is proportional to the integral of the actuating error signal, $e(t)$, over time. Mathematically, the controller output is the time-integral of the error, scaled by the integral action gain, $K_I$ (or equivalently, divided by the integral time $T_I$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCICT502 – Module 2):** Integral Mode is one of the **three basic continuous controller modes** (along with Proportional and Derivative). Its defining property is the **complete elimination of offset (steady-state error)** in the closed-loop response, a property that Proportional-only control fundamentally cannot provide.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath-Tub Filling" Intuition

Imagine you are filling a bath tub to an exact water level of **30 cm**, but the tap drips away slowly:

- A **Proportional (P) controller** would be like adjusting the tap flow proportional to *how far below* 30 cm the water currently is. The closer you get, the smaller the flow — but you would always end up a few millimetres short because the drip continuously steals water. That permanent shortfall is the **offset**.
- An **Integral (I) controller** instead keeps a *running memory* of how much shortfall has accumulated over time. The longer the water has been below 30 cm, the more aggressively it opens the tap. It will not stop "accumulating concern" until the error has been exactly zero for a sustained period. This is why integral action is also called **automatic reset** — it *resets* the controller output until offset vanishes.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance

| Attribute | Value for Integral Mode |
| :--- | :--- |
| Primary Benefit | **Eliminates steady-state offset** |
| Primary Drawback | Increases overshoot, slows response, can cause oscillation |
| Typical Use | Always combined with Proportional → **PI Controller** |
| Industry Term | "Reset Action" or "Automatic Reset" |
| Tuning Knob | **Integral Time $T_I$** (minutes/repeat) or **Reset Rate $K_I$** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** A *pure* Integral controller has **no knowledge of the present** — only of the **past accumulated error**. This is why it is almost never used alone in industry; it is virtually always paired with Proportional action to give a controller that is aware of both the present and the past.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The integral controller operates on a single governing philosophy: **"the longer an error persists, the harder the controller must push."**

1. **Sample the Error:** The controller continuously measures the difference between the set-point (SP) and the process variable (PV): $e(t) = \text{SP} - \text{PV}(t)$.
2. **Integrate Over Time:** The error is fed into an integrator block. The integrator's output is the *area under the error curve* from time $0$ up to the present time $t$.
3. **Scale the Output:** This accumulated area is multiplied by the **Integral Gain $K_I$** (units: % output per % error per minute).
4. **Drive the Final Control Element:** The result is sent to the valve/pump/heater to manipulate the process.
5. **Self-Correcting Loop:** As long as any non-zero error exists — no matter how tiny — the integral term continues to grow, pushing the output further. This is the mechanism by which **offset is forced to zero**.

### 2.2 Why the Controller "Remembers"

Unlike the P-mode, the I-mode has **memory**. Mathematically, integration is a *summation* operation. Each instant of error contributes a small rectangle to the cumulative area. The controller's response at any moment $t$ depends on the **entire history** of $e(\tau)$ for $\tau \in [0, t]$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** under KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) for PCICT502.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Boundary / Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $p(t) = K_I \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + p_0$ | $K_I$ = Integral gain, $p_0$ = initial output | (%/%)·min or 1/min | Continuous-time form |
| 2 | $p(t) = \dfrac{1}{T_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + p_0$ | $T_I$ = Integral time (reset time) | min | Equivalent to (1); $K_I = 1/T_I$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dp(t)}{dt} = K_I \cdot e(t)$ | Rate form of integral action | – | Fundamental differential form |
| 4 | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{P(s)}{E(s)} = \dfrac{K_I}{s}$ | Controller Transfer Function | – | Laplace-domain form |
| 5 | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1}{T_I\,s}$ | Using integral time | – | Equivalent form |
| 6 | $p_{ss} = p_0 + K_I \int_0^{\infty} e(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Steady-state output | – | For step disturbance rejection |
| 7 | $K_p K_I \lim_{s \to 0}\left[\dfrac{1}{s}\cdot G_p(s)\right] = \infty$ | Open-loop pole at origin | – | Explains zero offset |
| 8 | $\text{Reset Rate} = \dfrac{1}{T_I}$ | Industrial tuning term | repeats/min | $T_I$ in min/repeat |

> [!NOTE]
