# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern**: Part A questions are 3-mark direct conceptual questions, typically answerable in 3–4 sentences with a diagram or equation.

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between feedback and feedforward control with suitable examples.** (3 Marks)

#### Model Answer

| Aspect | Feedback Control | Feedforward Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Measured Variable** | Process Variable (PV) | Disturbance Variable (DV) |
| **Action Triggered By** | Error `e = SP − PV` | Measured disturbance `D` |
| **Nature of Action** | Reactive (waits for deviation) | Proactive (acts before deviation) |
| **Model Required** | Not essential | Essential (`G_p` and `G_d`) |
| **Steady-State Error** | Eliminates (with integral action) | May have offset if model is wrong |
| **Example** | Temperature control using a PID | Pre-heating inlet air to a furnace when feed flow changes |

> Feedback control **waits for the error to develop**, while feedforward control **acts on the disturbance directly** to prevent the error. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **What is ratio control? State its industrial applications.** (3 Marks)

#### Model Answer

**Ratio control** is a multiple-loop control strategy in which the flow rate of one stream (the *wild stream* `F_w`) is measured, multiplied by an operator-set ratio `R`, and used as the setpoint for the controller that regulates the *controlled stream* `F_c`. The control law is `F_c = R · F_w`. *(2 marks)*

**Industrial Applications** *(1 mark)*:
1. **Air-fuel ratio** control in boilers and combustion furnaces.
2. **Blending** of two liquids in fixed proportion (e.g., ethanol-water mixing).
3. **Reflux-to-distillate** ratio control in distillation columns.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern**: Part B questions are 14 marks with **internal choice**. The two sub-parts (a) and (b) are each 7 marks. Solutions require a diagram plus a full derivation or design procedure.

---

### Question B-A

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of **Cascade Control**. State the **advantages** and the **essential conditions** for its application. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A heat exchanger heats a process stream using steam. The outlet temperature is controlled by manipulating the steam flow. The steam flow itself is subject to pressure variations in the supply line. Propose a **cascade control** scheme and explain how it improves disturbance rejection compared to a single-loop PID. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Cascade Control Architecture (7 Marks)

**Definition** *(1 mark)*: Cascade control is a multiple-loop strategy in which the output of a *primary* (master) controller sets the setpoint of a *secondary* (slave) controller. The two loops are nested — the secondary loop lies *inside* the primary loop.

**Block Diagram** *(3 marks)*:

```
   SP_primary → (+) → [GC1] → SP_secondary → (+) → [GC2] → Valve
                  (-)                              (-)
                  ↑                                  ↑
                  │                                  │
                  └─ PV_primary ← Process ← PV_secondary
```

In Mermaid form (as drawn in Section 4.1).

**Advantages** *(2 marks)*:
1. **Disturbance Rejection**: Inner-loop disturbances are rejected by the slave loop *before* propagating to the outer PV.
2. **Improved Linearization**: The slave loop linearizes part of the process dynamics.
3. **Higher Effective Gain**: The primary loop can be tuned more aggressively because the inner loop has already stabilized the actuator dynamics.
4. **Reduced Sensitivity to Nonlinearities**: The inner loop can be tuned to handle valve nonlinearities (e.g., equal-percentage trim via positioner).

**Essential Conditions** *(1 mark)*:
1. The **secondary loop must be 3 to 5 times faster** than the primary loop.
2. The **secondary PV must be measurable** and reflect the actual MV reaching the process.
3. There must be a **causal relationship** between the secondary PV and the manipulated variable (e.g., flow → valve stem position → flow).

### Part (b) — Heat Exchanger Cascade Design (7 Marks)

**Single-Loop Problem** *(1 mark)*: In a single-loop scheme, the temperature controller (TIC) directly commands the steam valve. When steam supply pressure varies, the actual steam flow changes, but the controller doesn't "see" this until the outlet temperature drifts — leading to a sluggish, oscillatory response.

**Proposed Cascade Scheme** *(3 marks)*:

- **Primary Loop (Master)**: `TIC-100` measures the **outlet temperature** of the process stream. Its output sets the setpoint of `FIC-101`.
- **Secondary Loop (Slave)**: `FIC-101` measures the **steam flow** (using an orifice + DP transmitter). Its output drives the **steam control valve**.

```
   SP_T → TIC-100 → SP_F → FIC-101 → Steam Valve
              ↑                       ↑
              │                       │
              T_outlet                F_steam
```

**Why it Improves Performance** *(3 marks)*:
1. **Disturbance Compensation**: If steam supply pressure drops, the steam flow `F_steam` starts to fall. `FIC-101` *immediately* opens the valve further to restore `F_steam` to its setpoint. The **outlet temperature never deviates significantly** because the steam flow is maintained.
2. **Faster Loop Response**: The steam flow loop (typically a few seconds) is much faster than the temperature loop (tens of seconds). The inner loop acts as a *fast servo* for the outer loop.
3. **Valve Nonlinearity Compensation**: The inner loop with a flow sensor linearizes the valve characteristic; the outer loop sees an effectively linear plant.
4. **Quantitative Improvement**: Settling time can reduce by a factor of **3 to 5**; integral of squared error (ISE) can reduce by **50–80%** compared to a single-loop PID.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> - Students often **forget the condition** that the inner loop must be 3–5× faster than the outer loop. Lose 1 mark.
> - Many describe cascade control as "two PID controllers" without explaining the *setpoint cascading* relationship. Always state that the *primary output is the secondary setpoint*.
> - In part (b), do not just repeat the block diagram; explain the **causal sequence of events** during a disturbance.

---

### Question B-B (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **(a)** Derive the **ideal feedforward controller transfer function** for a process with measurable disturbance. State the **limitations** of pure feedforward control. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A 2×2 multivariable process has the steady-state gain matrix:
> $$K = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Compute the **Relative Gain Array (RGA)** and recommend the **best input-output pairing**. Justify your answer. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Feedforward Controller Derivation (7 Marks)

**Process Model** *(1 mark)*: For a SISO process with a single measurable disturbance:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) \cdot M(s) + G_d(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Feedforward Action** *(1 mark)*: The feedforward controller operates on `D(s)` to produce:

$$M(s) = G_{FF}(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Substitution and Solution** *(3 marks)*:

Substituting into the process equation:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) \cdot G_{FF}(s) \cdot D(s) + G_d(s) \cdot D(s)$$

For perfect disturbance rejection `Y(s) = 0`:

$$G_p(s) \cdot G_{FF}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\,G_{FF}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}\,}$$

**Limitations of Pure Feedforward** *(2 marks)*:
1. **Requires an exact process model** (`G_p` and `G_d`). Model mismatch causes offset and imperfect rejection.
2. **Disturbance must be measurable** — not all disturbances have reliable sensors.
3. **Cannot handle unmeasured disturbances** — the controller is "blind" to anything not explicitly modeled.
4. **Static feedforward is often used in practice** (ignoring dynamics) because it still provides 80–90% of the benefit with much less complexity.
5. **Steady-state offset may persist** if the model gains are slightly off — solved by combining with feedback trim.

### Part (b) — RGA Computation and Pairing (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the determinant of K** *(1 mark)*:

$$\det(K) = K_{11} \cdot K_{22} - K_{12} \cdot K_{21} = (4)(6) - (1)(2) = 24 - 2 = 22$$

**Step 2: Compute `\lambda_{11}`** *(1 mark)*:

$$\lambda_{11} = \dfrac{K_{11} \cdot K_{22}}{\det(K)} = \dfrac{(4)(6)}{22} = \dfrac{24}{22} = \dfrac{12}{11} \approx 1.091$$

**Step 3: Apply RGA symmetry** *(1 mark)*: For a 2×2 system, the RGA has the form:

$$\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & 1-\lambda \\ 1-\lambda & \lambda \end{bmatrix}$$

So:

$$\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 1.091 & -0.091 \\ -0.091 & 1.091 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Pairing recommendation** *(2 marks)*:

- The diagonal elements are `1.091 \approx 1` (close to unity) → **diagonal pairing preferred**.
- The off-diagonal elements are `-0.091` (negative and close to 0) → **avoid the off-diagonal pairing**.
- Recommended pairing: pair `m_1` with `y_1`, and `m_2` with `y_2` (i.e., keep the natural input-output assignment).

**Step 5: Justification** *(2 marks)*:
1. **\lambda near 1**: Indicates that the gain from `m_1` to `y_1` is *almost the same* whether loop 2 is open or closed — meaning there is **minimal interaction** between the loops in the natural pairing.
2. **Negative off-diagonal `\lambda`**: A negative relative gain means the *alternative* pairing would cause **inverse response** and potential instability.
3. **Industrial rule**: When `0.67 < \lambda < 1.5`, the pairing is acceptable. Here, `\lambda_{11} = 1.091` falls in this range, confirming the diagonal pairing is robust.
4. **Practical implication**: The two loops can be tuned **independently** as if they were SISO loops — no decoupler is needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — RGA Problems**
> - **Always compute `\det(K)` first** — many students skip this step and lose 1 mark.
> - **Use `\lambda` notation** carefully — do not confuse the relative gain `\lambda_{ij}` with the index variable `i, j`.
> - **State the pairing rule explicitly** — quoting the rule without applying it loses 1 mark.
> - **Check the sign of off-diagonal terms** — a negative sign is a strong indicator of *instability* in the alternative pairing; this is the most critical insight and worth 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Control Systems with Multiple Loops**

### A. Cascade Control
- Definition: Output of primary = Setpoint of secondary. Two nested loops.
- **Golden Rule**: Inner loop **3 to 5 times faster** than outer loop.
- Tuning order: **Inner first, then outer**.
- Advantage: Rejects inner-loop disturbances *before* they affect outer PV.
- Industrial example: Reactor temperature via jacket flow.

### B. Ratio Control
- Definition: Maintains a fixed ratio between two flow rates.
- Equation: `F_c = R · F_w`.
- Two schemes: (A) wild uncontrolled, (B) both controlled.
- Applications: Air-fuel mixing, blending, reflux/distillate ratio.

### C. Feedforward Control
- Definition: Acts on *measured disturbance* before it affects PV.
- Ideal FF controller: `G_FF(s) = -G_d(s) / G_p(s)`.
- Negative sign = opposite action to cancel disturbance effect.
- Limitation: Requires exact model; cannot handle unmeasured disturbances.
- Industrial practice: **Always combine FF + FB** (FF for fast correction, FB for trim).

### D. Selective (Override) Control
- Definition: Two or more controllers share one MV; a *selector* picks the active output.
- **High Selector** → maximum constraint enforcement (e.g., over-temperature).
- **Low Selector** → minimum constraint enforcement (e.g., anti-surge, anti-cavitation).
- Example: Compressor anti-surge, boiler drum level high-low control.

### E. Split-Range Control
- Definition: One controller output drives *multiple* valves, each over a sub-range.
- Split point divides the `0–100%` controller output among actuators.
- Common split: `0–50%` heating, `50–100%` cooling (or with overlap).
- Example: Jacketed reactor with both heating and cooling valves.

### F. Multivariable (MIMO) Control
- Definition: Multiple inputs and outputs with cross-coupling.
- **RGA**: `\lambda_{ij} = K_{ij} \cdot K_{ji}^{CL-inverse}`; for 2×2, `\lambda_{11} = K_{11} K_{22} / (K_{11} K_{22} - K_{12} K_{21})`.
- RGA property: Row and column sums equal **1**.
- Pairing rule: Pair where `\lambda \approx 1`; avoid where `\lambda < 0`.
- **Decoupler** mathematically cancels cross-coupling.

### G. Universal High-Yield Points (Every KTU Paper)
1. Always draw a **block diagram** in cascade, ratio, and selective control questions.
2. Always show the **sign convention** in feedforward derivations.
3. Always state the **essential conditions** (e.g., inner-loop speed for cascade).
4. Always mention **model assumptions** (linearity, time-invariance) in derivations.
5. Always quote **RGA rules** with numerical evidence when justifying pairings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy**
> For a 14-mark Part B question, allocate your writing as: 2 marks for diagram, 4 marks for derivation/formula, 4 marks for numerical/computational work, 2 marks for justification/conclusion, 2 marks for conditions/limitations. This ensures full marks coverage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Forward Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Forward Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Feedforward Control** is an advanced process control strategy in which the manipulated variable (MV) is adjusted proactively based on real-time measurements of *disturbance variables* (load variables) — **before** those disturbances can propagate through the process and cause a deviation in the controlled variable (CV). It is, therefore, a *measurement-of-disturbance* and *anticipatory* control paradigm.

Mathematically, a feedforward controller is a transfer function block, $G_{ff}(s)$, that processes the measured disturbance $D(s)$ and produces a corrective action in the manipulated variable path, such that the net effect of the disturbance on the output is ideally nullified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCICT502 – Module 2):**
> Feedforward control is the natural counterpart to feedback control. KTU expects you to be able to *derive*, *design*, and *critically evaluate* a feedforward controller and to know its limitations, especially the necessity of pairing it with feedback (cascade/trim) for practical implementation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a wet, icy road. Two distinct mental models exist:

| Driving Mental Model | Control Philosophy |
|---|---|
| You see a pedestrian stepping onto the road **before** you reach them, and you **already press the brake pedal**. | **Feedforward Control** – Action based on a *perceived future threat* (the measured disturbance). |
| You see the pedestrian only when they are 2 m away, and you brake **after** realizing the gap is closing. | **Feedback Control** – Action based on the *observed error* between desired and actual position. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> - **Feedback control = "Wait and react."** It corrects errors *after* they have occurred at the output. The error $e(t) = \text{Setpoint} - \text{Output}$ must be non-zero for action to begin.
> - **Feedforward control = "Anticipate and prevent."** It acts the moment the disturbance is sensed, so the controlled variable ideally never deviates from the setpoint at all.
> - In real industrial plants, the two are almost always combined: feedforward provides the *bulk correction*, while feedback provides the *trim*.

A second classic analogy is a **shower**: when someone else in the house flushes a toilet (the disturbance), the cold-water pressure drops. A feedforward system would detect the toilet flush (disturbance sensor) and immediately open the hot-water valve a little more, *preventing* the shower temperature from changing at all. A feedback system would only react *after* the shower water becomes noticeably colder.

### Key Standard Metrics / Constants

- **Ideal Cancellation Condition**: $G_{d}(s) + G_{ff}(s) \cdot G_{p}(s) = 0$
- **Static Gain Ratio**: $K_{ff} = -K_{d} / K_{p}$  *(negative of the ratio of disturbance-path gain to process-path gain)*
- **Lead-Lag Compensator Time Constants**: $T_{1}$ (lead) and $T_{2}$ (lag) — both in **seconds**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Feedforward Control

A feedforward control loop contains the following functional blocks:

1. **Disturbance Sensor (Transmitter):** Measures the load variable (e.g., feed flow, inlet temperature, inlet composition).
2. **Feedforward Controller ($G_{ff}(s)$):** A lead-lag compensator (most common industrial form) that converts the measured disturbance signal into a corrective manipulated-variable signal.
3. **Final Control Element (Valve / VFD / Heater):** Executes the corrective action on the process.
4. **Process ($G_{p}(s)$):** The plant whose output we wish to keep on setpoint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works (the 'How' and 'Why'):**
> The disturbance $D(s)$ propagates to the output $C(s)$ through two parallel paths:
> - The **disturbance path** $G_{d}(s)$: $D(s) \rightarrow C(s)$ — this is *unwanted*.
> - The **control path** $G_{ff}(s) \cdot G_{p}(s)$: $D(s) \rightarrow M(s) \rightarrow C(s)$ — this is the *counteracting* effect.
> If the two paths are made equal in magnitude and opposite in sign at every frequency, their sum at the output is zero. Hence, *perfect disturbance rejection*.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Cancellation

1. **Measure** the disturbance with a fast, accurate sensor.
2. **Compute** the corrective MV using the feedforward controller transfer function $G_{ff}(s)$.
3. **Apply** the MV change through the same final control element used by the feedback loop.
4. **Hope** that the process model used to design $G_{ff}(s)$ is accurate — because any model error leaves a residual error in the output.
5. **Trim** the residual error using a slow feedback (PI/PID) loop — this is the reason feedforward is *almost always* paired with feedback in practice.

### 2.3 The Lead-Lag Compensator (Industrial Standard)

In real plants, perfect cancellation is impossible because the true $G_{p}(s)$ and $G_{d}(s)$ may be of high order, non-linear, or have time delays. The *practical* feedforward controller is therefore reduced to a first-order lead-lag block:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -K_{ff} \cdot \frac{T_{1} s + 1}{T_{2} s + 1}$$

where:
- $K_{ff}$ is the **static feedforward gain** (dimensionless or with engineering units as needed).
- $T_{1}$ is the **lead time constant** (provides *dynamic anticipation*).
- $T_{2}$ is the **lag time constant** (provides *dynamic smoothing*).

| Condition | Compensator Type | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{1} > T_{2}$ | **Lead** | The MV responds *faster* than the disturbance path — used when the process is slower than the disturbance. |
| $T_{1} < T_{2}$ | **Lag** | The MV responds *slower* than the disturbance — used when the process is faster than the disturbance (less common). |
| $T_{1} = T_{2}$ | **Static** (gain only) | A pure proportional feedforward — simplest, but ignores all dynamics. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{ff,\text{ideal}}(s) = -\dfrac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{p}(s)}$ | Ideal feedforward transfer function (perfect cancellation). | dimensionless |
| 2 | $G_{ff}(s) = -K_{ff}\,\dfrac{T_{1} s + 1}{T_{2} s + 1}$ | Practical lead-lag compensator (industrial standard). | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K_{ff} = -\dfrac{K_{d}}{K_{p}}$ | Static feedforward gain (steady-state ratio of disturbance to process gain). | depends on signal levels |
| 4 | $T_{1} = \tau_{p}$ | Lead time constant = process dominant time constant. | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $T_{2} = \tau_{d}$ | Lag time constant = disturbance-path dominant time constant. | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $\dfrac{C(s)}{D(s)}\big\vert_{ff} = G_{d}(s) + G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s)$ | Net effect of disturbance on output (should $\rightarrow 0$). | process output units |
| 7 | $\text{IAE}_{ff} < \text{IAE}_{fb}$ | Integral Absolute Error is reduced compared to feedback alone. | unitless metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never write the absolute-value bars `| |` inside a table cell — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, otherwise the markdown table will break.** *(KTU students frequently lose marks when their study tables are corrupted.)*

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation columns**: feedforward on feed flow rate + feed composition → reduces product composition swings dramatically.
- **Boiler drum level**: feedforward on steam flow → prevents "swell" and "shrink" transients that pure feedback cannot handle.
- **HVAC systems**: feedforward on outdoor temperature and humidity.
- **Chemical reactors**: feedforward on feed concentration to maintain yield.
- **Rolling mills**: feedforward on incoming strip thickness in a thickness-control system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Feedforward Transfer Function

We start with the two parallel paths from disturbance $D(s)$ to controlled output $C(s)$.

**Path 1 — The disturbance path (unwanted):**
$$C_{1}(s) = G_{d}(s)\,D(s)$$

**Path 2 — The control path (counteracting):**
$$C_{2}(s) = G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s)\,D(s)$$

**Net output deviation due to the disturbance:**
$$C(s) = C_{1}(s) + C_{2}(s) = \big[\,G_{d}(s) + G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s)\,\big]\,D(s)$$

For **perfect disturbance rejection**, the bracketed term in square brackets must equal zero:

$$G_{d}(s) + G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s) = 0$$

Solving algebraically for the feedforward controller:

$$G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s) = -G_{d}(s)$$

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{p}(s)}$$

> **Interpretation:** The feedforward controller must, at every frequency $\omega$, have a magnitude equal to the ratio $\vert G_{d}(j\omega)/G_{p}(j\omega) \vert$ and a phase equal to $\pi - \angle G_{d}(j\omega) + \angle G_{p}(j\omega)$.

### 3.2 Design of a Lead-Lag Compensator from Process Models

Let the process and disturbance transfer functions be of the standard first-order-plus-dead-time form (very common in chemical plants):

$$G_{p}(s) = \frac{K_{p}\,e^{-\theta_{p} s}}{\tau_{p} s + 1}, \qquad G_{d}(s) = \frac{K_{d}\,e^{-\theta_{d} s}}{\tau_{d} s + 1}$$

The **ideal** feedforward controller is then:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{p}(s)} = -\frac{K_{d}}{K_{p}} \cdot \frac{\tau_{p} s + 1}{\tau_{d} s + 1} \cdot e^{-(\theta_{d} - \theta_{p}) s}$$

For a **physically realizable** compensator (no negative time delays), the dead-time term $e^{-(\theta_{d}-\theta_{p})s}$ is **dropped** (we keep the larger of the two dead times as a residual). The result is the lead-lag block:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{K_{d}}{K_{p}} \cdot \frac{\tau_{p} s + 1}{\tau_{d} s + 1} = -K_{ff}\,\frac{T_{1} s + 1}{T_{2} s + 1}$$

where we identify:
- $K_{ff} = K_{d}/K_{p}$ *(the sign depends on whether the action is direct or reverse)*
- $T_{1} = \tau_{p}$ *(lead time = process time constant)*
- $T_{2} = \tau_{d}$ *(lag time = disturbance time constant)*

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A heat exchanger has the following transfer functions. The process is the steam-flow $\rightarrow$ outlet-temperature path, and the disturbance is the inlet-feed-flow $\rightarrow$ outlet-temperature path.

$$G_{p}(s) = \frac{1.2}{3s + 1}, \qquad G_{d}(s) = \frac{0.8}{1.5s + 1}$$

**Step 1 — Static feedforward gain:**

$$K_{ff} = \frac{K_{d}}{K_{p}} = \frac{0.8}{1.2} = 0.667$$

**Step 2 — Lead and lag time constants:**

$$T_{1} = \tau_{p} = 3 \text{ s} \,(\text{lead}), \qquad T_{2} = \tau_{d} = 1.5 \text{ s} \,(\text{lag})$$

Since $T_{1} > T_{2}$, this is a **lead compensator** (because the process is slower than the disturbance path — common in heat exchangers where the steam jacket responds slowly).

**Step 3 — Final feedforward controller transfer function:**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -0.667 \cdot \frac{3s + 1}{1.5s + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of perfect cancellation at steady state:**

$$\lim_{s \to 0} \big[\,G_{d}(s) + G_{ff}(s) G_{p}(s)\,\big] = 0.8 + (-0.667)(1.2) = 0.8 - 0.8 = 0 \;\; \checkmark$$

At steady state, the disturbance is completely rejected.

### 3.4 Why Feedforward Alone is Insufficient (Mathematical Justification)

A pure feedforward controller has **no integral action**. Any unmeasured disturbance, any model mismatch, or any sensor bias will produce a **steady-state offset** in the output. This is why industrial practice *always* pairs feedforward with feedback:

$$\text{Total Control} = \underbrace{G_{ff}(s)\cdot D(s)}_{\text{feedforward (fast, model-based)}} \;+\; \underbrace{G_{c}(s)\cdot \big[\,R(s) - C(s)\,\big]}_{\text{feedback (slow, trims residual error)}}$$

The feedback path is the only one capable of providing **zero steady-state error** (via integral action) for unmeasured disturbances. The feedforward path simply handles the *measured* bulk of the disturbance, freeing the feedback loop from most of the work.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Feedforward Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dvar[Disturbance D] --> dsens[Disturbance Sensor]
    dsens --> gff[Feedforward Controller Gff s]
    gff --> summing[Summer]
    fbc[Feedback Controller] --> summing
    summing --> fce[Final Control Element Valve]
    fce --> proc[Process Gp s]
    dvar --> gddist[Disturbance Path Gd s]
    gddist --> outputs[Output Summer]
    proc --> outputs
    outputs --> pv[Controlled Variable C]
    pv --> fbnode[Measurement]
    fbnode --> fbc
```

> **Reading guide:** Trace the disturbance `D` — it has two arrows. The right-hand arrow goes through the disturbance path `Gd` directly to the output, *unwanted*. The left-hand arrow goes through `Gff` → valve → `Gp`, *counteracting*. The feedback loop sits on the right and trims the residual.

### 4.2 Comparison: Feedback vs Feedforward Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FB["FEEDBACK CONTROL"]
        FB1[Setpoint R] --> FB2[Summing Junction]
        FB3[Output C] --> FB4[Sensor H]
        FB4 --> FB2
        FB2 --> FB5[Controller Gc]
        FB5 --> FB6[Process Gp]
        FB6 --> FB3
    end
    subgraph FFW["FEEDFORWARD CONTROL"]
        FF1[Disturbance D] --> FF2[Sensor]
        FF2 --> FF3[Gff s]
        FF3 --> FF4[Summer with FB trim]
        FF4 --> FF5[Process Gp]
        FF5 --> FF6[Output C]
        FF1 --> FF7[Gd s]
        FF7 --> FF8[Summer with output]
        FF8 --> FF6
    end
```

### 4.3 Lead-Lag Compensator Block (Internal View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Disturbance Signal] --> B[Static Gain Kff]
    B --> C[Lead Branch T1 s plus 1]
    C --> D[Numerator]
    D --> E[Summer]
    F[Lag Branch T2 s plus 1] --> E
    E --> G[Output Manipulated Variable]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: When to Use Lead, Lag, or Static FF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Disturbance Dynamics vs Process Dynamics] --> Q1{Which is faster?}
    Q1 -->|Disturbance is faster| Lead[Use Lead Compensator T1 greater than T2]
    Q1 -->|Process is faster| Lag[Use Lag Compensator T1 less than T2]
    Q1 -->|Equal dynamics| Static[Use Static FF Gain only T1 equals T2]
    Lead --> Use[Implement in industrial PLC DCS]
    Lag --> Use
    Static --> Use
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tuning Rule of Thumb (used in Honeywell and Yokogawa DCS systems):**
> - Set $K_{ff}$ first using the static gain ratio $K_{d}/K_{p}$.
> - Adjust $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ to minimize the integral of the absolute error (IAE) during step disturbances logged on the historical trend.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define feedforward control. State any two advantages and two limitations of feedforward control over feedback control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* Feedforward control is a control strategy in which the manipulated variable is adjusted based on continuous measurement of one or more disturbance variables, in order to prevent the disturbance from affecting the controlled variable, rather than correcting it after the fact.

*Advantages (1 mark — any two):*
1. It takes corrective action **before** the output deviates from the setpoint — no error has to develop first.
2. It is highly effective for **slow loops with long dead times** (e.g., composition control, pH control), where feedback alone gives sluggish response.
3. It significantly reduces the **Integral Absolute Error (IAE)** for known, measurable disturbances.

*Limitations (1 mark — any two):*
1. Requires an **accurate mathematical model** of the process and disturbance paths; any model mismatch leaves a steady-state offset.
2. Cannot compensate for **unmeasured disturbances** (no integral action).
3. Requires **additional expensive sensors** for the disturbance variable.
4. Implementation is more complex than plain feedback and is sensitive to sensor noise.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a lead-lag compensator? Under what condition is it called a "lead" compensator in feedforward control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Lead-lag compensator (2 marks):* A lead-lag compensator is the most common industrial form of the feedforward controller. It is a first-order transfer function of the form

$$G_{ff}(s) = -K_{ff}\,\frac{T_{1} s + 1}{T_{2} s + 1}$$

where $T_{1}$ is the lead time constant and $T_{2}$ is the lag time constant. It allows the engineer to independently tune the static gain (steady-state ratio) and the dynamic response (shape of the time-domain correction).

*Lead compensator condition (1 mark):* The compensator is called a **lead compensator** when $T_{1} > T_{2}$, i.e., the zero in the numerator is *closer to the origin* in the s-plane than the pole in the denominator. In the time domain, this means the manipulated variable responds *faster* than the disturbance path — typical when the process is slower than the disturbance.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, derive the transfer function of an ideal feedforward controller for a process subjected to a measurable disturbance.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Block diagram (2 marks):* The feedforward control block diagram contains two parallel paths from the disturbance $D(s)$ to the output $C(s)$:

- **Disturbance path:** $D(s) \rightarrow G_{d}(s) \rightarrow C(s)$ — this is the unwanted propagation of the disturbance.
- **Feedforward control path:** $D(s) \rightarrow G_{ff}(s) \rightarrow$ (summer) $\rightarrow G_{p}(s) \rightarrow C(s)$ — this is the corrective path.

*Derivation (5 marks):* The total effect of the disturbance on the output is the sum of the two parallel paths.

Step 1: Output due to the disturbance path alone:

$$C_{1}(s) = G_{d}(s)\,D(s)$$

Step 2: Output due to the feedforward control path alone (the manipulated variable $M(s) = G_{ff}(s) D(s)$ passes through the process $G_{p}(s)$):

$$C_{2}(s) = G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s)\,D(s)$$

Step 3: Total output deviation:

$$C(s) = C_{1}(s) + C_{2}(s) = \big[\,G_{d}(s) + G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s)\,\big]\,D(s)$$

Step 4: For **perfect disturbance rejection**, the transfer function from $D(s)$ to $C(s)$ must be zero. Hence the bracket must vanish:

$$G_{d}(s) + G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s) = 0$$

Step 5: Solving for the feedforward controller:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{p}(s)}$$

This is the *ideal* feedforward controller. It is rarely realizable in practice due to time delays, high-order dynamics, and model uncertainty, so we reduce it to a lead-lag form.

---

**(b)** A chemical reactor has the following identified transfer functions:

$$G_{p}(s) = \frac{2.0}{4s + 1}, \qquad G_{d}(s) = \frac{1.5}{2s + 1}$$

Design a practical feedforward controller (lead-lag form) and verify that it achieves zero steady-state offset for a step disturbance.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Identify static feedforward gain (2 marks):*

$$K_{ff} = \frac{K_{d}}{K_{p}} = \frac{1.5}{2.0} = 0.75$$

*Step 2 — Identify lead and lag time constants (2 marks):*

$$T_{1} = \tau_{p} = 4 \text{ s (lead)}, \qquad T_{2} = \tau_{d} = 2 \text{ s (lag)}$$

Since $T_{1} > T_{2}$, this is a **lead compensator** (the reactor process is slower than the disturbance path — typical for exothermic reactions where cooling water disturbances are quick).

*Step 3 — Final feedforward controller (2 marks):*

$$G_{ff}(s) = -0.75 \cdot \frac{4s + 1}{2s + 1}$$

*Step 4 — Steady-state verification (1 mark):* Apply the Final Value Theorem to the closed-loop disturbance response:

$$\lim_{s \to 0}\big[\,G_{d}(s) + G_{ff}(s)\,G_{p}(s)\,\big] = 1.5 + (-0.75)(2.0) = 1.5 - 1.5 = 0 \;\; \checkmark$$

The steady-state offset is zero — the design is correct.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain, with the help of a heat-exchanger example, how feedforward control is implemented in practice. Why is it always paired with feedback in industrial plants?

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Heat-exchanger example (4 marks):* Consider a shell-and-tube heat exchanger in which the outlet temperature of the process fluid is to be controlled by adjusting the steam flow rate on the shell side. The disturbances are the **inlet process-fluid flow rate** and the **inlet process-fluid temperature**.

- A flow transmitter is installed on the inlet line to measure the disturbance (feed flow).
- A temperature transmitter is installed on the inlet line to measure the disturbance (inlet temperature).
- A temperature transmitter is installed on the outlet line to measure the controlled variable.
- The feedforward controller $G_{ff}(s)$ (a lead-lag block implemented in the DCS) takes the disturbance signals and computes a steam-flow setpoint adjustment.
- The final control element is the steam control valve.
- The PID feedback controller (also in the DCS) takes the outlet-temperature error and trims the steam flow setpoint.

The combined signal — feedforward (fast, disturbance-based) plus feedback (slow, error-based) — is sent to the steam valve.

*Why pair with feedback (3 marks):*

1. **Model mismatch:** No feedforward model is perfect; errors in $K_{p}$, $K_{d}$, $\tau_{p}$, $\tau_{d}$ leave a residual offset that only feedback can remove.
2. **Unmeasured disturbances:** Other disturbances (fouling, ambient temperature, steam pressure fluctuations) are not sensed by the feedforward path; only feedback can handle them.
3. **Setpoint tracking:** The user changes the setpoint, not the disturbance. Feedforward by definition cannot respond to setpoint changes; feedback can.
4. **Drift correction:** Sensor drifts and valve stiction would cause a permanent offset in a feedforward-only system; integral action in feedback eliminates this.

---

**(b)** A distillation column has the following identified transfer functions for the top-product composition loop:

$$G_{p}(s) = \frac{0.8\,e^{-2s}}{5s + 1}, \qquad G_{d}(s) = \frac{0.6\,e^{-s}}{3s + 1}$$

Design the practical feedforward controller and discuss the limitation introduced by the dead times.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Static feedforward gain (1.5 marks):*

$$K_{ff} = \frac{K_{d}}{K_{p}} = \frac{0.6}{0.8} = 0.75$$

*Step 2 — Lead and lag time constants (1.5 marks):*

$$T_{1} = \tau_{p} = 5 \text{ s (lead)}, \qquad T_{2} = \tau_{d} = 3 \text{ s (lag)}$$

Lead compensator ($T_{1} > T_{2}$).

*Step 3 — Dead-time term (2 marks):* The ideal controller is

$$G_{ff,\text{ideal}}(s) = -0.75 \cdot \frac{5s + 1}{3s + 1} \cdot e^{+(2-1)s} = -0.75 \cdot \frac{5s + 1}{3s + 1} \cdot e^{s}$$

The factor $e^{+s}$ is a **negative time delay** (anticipation), which is **physically unrealizable**. We must drop this term and accept the larger of the two dead times ($\theta_{p} = 2$ s) as an irreducible delay in the feedforward path.

*Step 4 — Practical controller (1.5 marks):*

$$G_{ff}(s) = -0.75 \cdot \frac{5s + 1}{3s + 1}$$

*Step 5 — Limitation discussion (0.5 mark):* The 2-second irreducible dead time means that for the first 2 seconds after a disturbance step, the controlled variable will *still deviate* from setpoint. The feedforward action can only fully cancel the disturbance after $t > 2$ s. This residual transient is what the feedback trim must handle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in $K_{ff}$:** Many students write $K_{ff} = -K_{d}/K_{p}$ and forget the additional minus sign from the derivation $G_{ff} = -G_{d}/G_{p}$. The convention in most textbooks (Coughanowr, Stephanopoulos) is to absorb the sign into the *action* (direct/reverse) of the controller — be consistent.
> 2. **Forgetting to drop negative dead times:** A common 2-mark loss. Negative dead times are physically impossible and must be discarded.
> 3. **Confusing lead and lag:** A compensator is "lead" if $T_{1} > T_{2}$ (numerator time constant larger), *not* if the numerator sits "ahead" in the formula. The terminology comes from the *phase lead* the zero contributes at high frequencies.
> 4. **Omitting the feedback pairing in design questions:** KTU examiners specifically look for a discussion of model mismatch and unmeasured disturbances — failing to mention these loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong sign of disturbance path:** Always re-verify with a step test on the plant trend; sign errors in $G_{d}$ propagate directly into the wrong $K_{ff}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Feedforward control is anticipatory, not corrective** — it acts on the disturbance, not on the output error.
- **Ideal controller:** $G_{ff}(s) = -G_{d}(s)/G_{p}(s)$.
- **Industrial practical form:** $G_{ff}(s) = -K_{ff}(T_{1}s+1)/(T_{2}s+1)$.
- **Static gain:** $K_{ff} = K_{d}/K_{p}$ (sign handled by direct/reverse action).
- **Lead compensator** when $T_{1} > T_{2}$; **lag compensator** when $T_{1} < T_{2}$.
- **Drop negative dead times** when designing — they are physically unrealizable.
- **Feedforward alone has no integral action** — it cannot remove steady-state offset from unmeasured disturbances or model errors.
- **Always pair with feedback** (PI/PID) in real plants — feedforward handles the bulk, feedback handles the trim.
- **Impossible to implement** if the disturbance cannot be measured reliably or if the process model is too uncertain.
- **KTU focus areas** for Module 2: derivation of $G_{ff}$, design of lead-lag from first-order-plus-dead-time models, numerical examples, block diagrams, and the feedback-pairing justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Aspects of the Design of Feed Forward Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Practical Aspects of the Design of Feed Forward Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedforward Control** is an advanced process control strategy in which the principal disturbance(s) affecting a process are measured, and a corrective control action is computed and applied *before* the disturbance can produce any appreciable deviation in the controlled variable. Mathematically, the feedforward controller transfer function is designed to satisfy an **ideal cancellation criterion**, where the effect of the measured disturbance acting through the disturbance path is exactly cancelled by the effect of the manipulated variable acting through the process path.

Unlike **Feedback Control**, which reacts *after* an error has already occurred in the process output, feedforward control acts *anticipatively* (proactively), making it a fundamentally **open-loop compensation technique** based on the principle of **disturbance compensation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Practical aspects emphasize the *inability* to achieve perfect cancellation in real plants, leading to the requirement of a **Lead–Lag Compensator** and the typical pairing of feedforward with a residual **Feedback (Trim) Controller** in industrial practice.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **mariner steering a small boat** across a river:
- A **feedback helmsman** would see that the boat has already drifted downstream due to the current, and only then turn the rudder to correct the course.
- A **feedforward helmsman**, however, measures the strength and direction of the river current **upstream** and tilts the rudder *in advance* by exactly the right angle to counter the anticipated drift.

In a chemical plant:
- A classic example is a **distillation column reflux drum**. If the *feed flow rate* (a major disturbance) suddenly increases, a **feedback temperature controller** would only react after the tray temperatures have already shifted. A **feedforward controller**, sensing the rise in feed flow, *immediately* increases the reflux flow proportionally to keep the composition on target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** The key performance metric in feedforward design is the **Disturbance Rejection Ratio (DRR)**, ideally approaching **unity (1.0)** for perfect cancellation. A practical, achievable value in real plant conditions is typically in the range of **0.70 – 0.90**.

### 1.3 Key Distinctions at a Glance

| Parameter | Feedback Control | Feedforward Control |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Trigger Source** | Error in controlled variable (CV) | Measured disturbance (DV) |
| **Reaction Time** | Reactive (after-the-fact) | Anticipative (before-the-fact) |
| **Mathematical Nature** | Closed-loop | Open-loop compensation |
| **Requirement** | Knowledge of set point | Accurate process + disturbance model |
| **Residual Error** | Drives error → 0 in steady state | Leaves residual error → needs FB trim |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophy and Operating Principle

The fundamental philosophy of feedforward control rests on three engineering imperatives:

1. **Disturbance Identification:** Not all disturbances need feedforward compensation. The most economically significant disturbances (those with the largest impact on the controlled variable) are the candidates.
2. **Disturbance Measurement:** A reliable, fast, and accurate sensor must be available to measure the disturbance. *You cannot control what you cannot measure.*
3. **Process Model Inversion:** The feedforward controller is essentially the **inverse** of the process dynamic relationship between the manipulated variable and the controlled variable, scaled by the disturbance dynamics.

### 2.2 The Three Foundational Issues in Practical Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Emphasis:** When asked "Why is pure feedforward control rarely used alone in industry?", the answer must address the **three practical issues** below.

**Issue 1 — Imperfect Process Model:** Real processes are non-linear, time-varying, and exhibit unknown dynamics. A model identified today may be invalid next week due to fouling, catalyst deactivation, or raw-material variability.

**Issue 2 — Imperfect Disturbance Measurement:** Sensors have finite bandwidth, measurement noise, dead time, and installation errors. If the disturbance is unmeasured (e.g., ambient humidity, catalyst poison), feedforward is impossible.

**Issue 3 — Unmeasured Disturbances Persist:** Even when one major disturbance is compensated, other unmeasured disturbances will still cause offset in the controlled variable. This is the fundamental reason for pairing feedforward with a feedback **trim controller**.

### 2.3 Derivation of the Ideal Feedforward Controller

Consider the canonical block diagram of a single-input-single-disturbance (SISO-D) plant:

- $D(s)$ = Measured disturbance
- $C(s)$ = Controlled variable (process output)
- $M(s)$ = Manipulated variable (controller output)
- $G_d(s)$ = Disturbance transfer function (path from $D$ to $C$)
- $G_p(s)$ = Process transfer function (path from $M$ to $C$)
- $G_v(s)$ = Control valve / final control element transfer function
- $G_m(s)$ = Measurement transmitter transfer function
- $G_f(s)$ = Feedforward controller transfer function (to be designed)

For **ideal cancellation**, the net effect of the disturbance on $C$ must be zero:

$$C(s) = G_d(s) \cdot D(s) + G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_f(s) \cdot G_m(s) \cdot D(s) = 0$$

Solving for the ideal feedforward controller:

$$G_f^{ideal}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_m(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** is the essence of feedforward: the feedforward action must be **opposite in direction** to the disturbance action so that the two effects cancel at the process output. The **sign convention** must be verified carefully during commissioning — a sign error is the single most common feedforward commissioning failure.

### 2.4 The Lead–Lag Compensator Form

In practice, $G_f^{ideal}(s)$ is rarely implementable because it may contain:
- **Right-Half-Plane (RHP) zeros** (non-minimum phase processes),
- **Derivative terms** (which are physically unrealizable),
- **Excessively high gains** at high frequency (noise amplification).

Hence, the practical feedforward controller takes the **Lead–Lag** form:

$$G_f(s) = -K_{ff} \cdot \frac{(1 + \tau_{lead} \cdot s)}{(1 + \tau_{lag} \cdot s)}$$

where:
- $K_{ff}$ = Static feedforward gain (steady-state tuning parameter)
- $\tau_{lead}$ = Lead time constant (speeds up the response)
- $\tau_{lag}$ = Lag time constant (slows down the response)

### 2.5 Static Feedforward Design (Steady-State Basis)

The **static feedforward gain** is computed from a steady-state material/energy balance at the design operating point. For a process with linear steady-state gains $K_d$ and $K_p$:

$$K_{ff} = \frac{K_d}{K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_m}$$

where each $K$ is the steady-state gain of the corresponding transfer function block.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $G_f^{ideal}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s) G_v(s) G_m(s)}$ | Ideal cancellation of disturbance | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | $G_f(s) = -K_{ff} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \tau_{lead} s}{1 + \tau_{lag} s}$ | Practical Lead–Lag compensator | s (time) for $\tau$ |
| 3 | $K_{ff} = \dfrac{K_d}{K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_m}$ | Static (steady-state) feedforward gain | Mixed (gain ratio) |
| 4 | $e_{ff}(t) = C_{ff}(t) - C_{fb}(t)$ | Feedforward residual error | Same as CV |
| 5 | $G_c(s) = G_{fb}(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ | Closed-loop feedback transfer | (rad/s)^n |
| 6 | $\tau_{lead} \approx \tau_d$ (dominant disturbance pole) | Lead time matches disturbance lag | seconds (s) |
| 7 | $\tau_{lag} \approx \tau_p$ (dominant process pole) | Lag time matches process lag | seconds (s) |
| 8 | $DRR = 1 - \dfrac{\vert C_{ff+fb} \vert}{\vert C_{fb-only} \vert}$ | Disturbance Rejection Ratio | Dimensionless (0–1) |

> [!TIP]
> **Sign Convention Rule (KTU Board Trick):** If an *increase* in disturbance $D$ causes the controlled variable $C$ to *increase*, AND an *increase* in the manipulated variable $M$ causes $C$ to *increase*, then $K_{ff}$ is **positive** (direct action feedforward). If the action on $C$ is opposite, $K_{ff}$ is **negative** (reverse action).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Feedforward control is industrially deployed in:
- **Distillation columns** — feed flow and feed composition disturbances
- **Boiler drum level control** — steam demand and feedwater flow disturbances
- **Heat exchangers** — inlet temperature and flow disturbances
- **pH neutralization** — reagent concentration disturbances
- **HVAC systems** — outdoor temperature and humidity disturbances

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Ideal Feedforward Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Identify all paths from $D(s)$ to $C(s)$.**
There are exactly two parallel paths:
- **Path 1 (Disturbance path):** $D \xrightarrow{G_d(s)} C$
- **Path 2 (Feedforward compensation path):** $D \xrightarrow{G_m(s)} G_f(s) \xrightarrow{G_v(s)} G_p(s) C$

**Step 2 — Apply the Superposition Principle.**
The total effect of $D$ on $C$ is the sum of contributions from both paths:

$$C(s) = D(s) \cdot G_d(s) + D(s) \cdot G_m(s) \cdot G_f(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)$$

**Step 3 — Factor $D(s)$ to isolate the control structure.**

$$C(s) = D(s) \cdot \left[ G_d(s) + G_m(s) \cdot G_f(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Impose the Ideal Cancellation Criterion.**
For perfect disturbance rejection, the term in brackets must equal zero:

$$G_d(s) + G_m(s) \cdot G_f(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $G_f(s)$.**

$$G_m(s) \cdot G_f(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) = -G_d(s)$$

$$G_f(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_m(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The feedforward controller is the **negative ratio** of the disturbance-path dynamics to the manipulated-variable-path dynamics. The negative sign enforces the **opposing action** needed for cancellation.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Static Feedforward Gain from Material Balance

**Step 1 — Define the controlled variable deviation equation at steady state.**
For a linearized process with a single disturbance and single manipulated variable, in the vicinity of the operating point:

$$\Delta C = K_p \cdot \Delta M + K_d \cdot \Delta D$$

**Step 2 — Apply the perfect cancellation condition $\Delta C = 0$ at steady state.**

$$0 = K_p \cdot \Delta M_{ff} + K_d \cdot \Delta D$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required change in manipulated variable.**

$$\Delta M_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p} \cdot \Delta D$$

**Step 4 — Include the valve and transmitter steady-state gains** $K_v$ and $K_m$:

$$\Delta M_{ff} = \frac{1}{K_v} \cdot \left( -\frac{K_d}{K_p} \cdot \frac{\Delta D}{K_m} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the static feedforward gain.**

$$K_{ff} = \frac{K_d}{K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_m}$$

This completes the static design — dynamics (lead-lag) are added in the next step.

### 3.3 Lead–Lag Time Constant Selection (Analytical Matrix)

| Step | Observation | Mathematical Rule | Practical Heuristic |
|------|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Determine disturbance dead time $\theta_d$ | From step test on $D$ → $C$ | Often small or zero |
| 2 | Determine process dead time $\theta_p$ | From step test on $M$ → $C$ | Often small or zero |
| 3 | Determine disturbance lag $\tau_d$ | Fit first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) | $\tau_d \approx 0.5 \cdot t_{63\%}$ |
| 4 | Determine process lag $\tau_p$ | Fit FOPDT | $\tau_p \approx 0.5 \cdot t_{63\%}$ |
| 5 | Set $\tau_{lead} = \tau_d$ | Lead compensates for disturbance lag | Speeds up the correction |
| 6 | Set $\tau_{lag} = \tau_p$ | Lag compensates for process lag | Smooths the action |
| 7 | If $\tau_d < \tau_p$: $\tau_{lead} < \tau_{lag}$ | Feedforward becomes a **lag** block | Action is delayed |
| 8 | If $\tau_d > \tau_p$: $\tau_{lead} > \tau_{lag}$ | Feedforward becomes a **lead** block | Action is anticipatory |
| 9 | If $\theta_d < \theta_p$: pure feedforward fails | Disturbance arrives before correction | **Add feedback trim** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** *"When does feedforward provide no benefit over feedback?"* — Answer: When the disturbance dynamics are *faster* than the process dynamics AND the disturbance cannot be measured *earlier* than the controlled variable responds. In that pathological case, the **dead time ratio $\theta_p / \theta_d$** is the diagnostic metric.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Combined Feedforward–Feedback (FF + FB) Design

The combined scheme augments feedforward with a feedback trim controller $G_{fb}(s)$. The total controller output is:

$$M(s) = G_{fb}(s) \cdot E(s) + G_f(s) \cdot D(s)$$

**Design logic:**

| Component | Purpose | Tuning Approach |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| Feedforward $G_f$ | Reject measured disturbance | Tune $K_{ff}$ first, then lead-lag |
| Feedback $G_{fb}$ | Reject unmeasured disturbance + model error | Tune last (PI/PID) using Z-N or IMC |
| $G_{fb}$ interaction with $G_f$ | Must be stable when both are active | Use SIMC or lambda tuning |

The closed-loop transfer function from disturbance to output under FF+FB is:

$$\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_d(s) + G_m \cdot G_f \cdot G_v \cdot G_p(s)}{1 + G_{fb}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}$$

When $G_f$ is chosen ideally, the **numerator** → 0, leaving the denominator's feedback term to handle residual error — this is the design ideal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Canonical Feedforward Control Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeD["DISTURBANCE D(s)"] --> nodeGd["Gd(s) Disturbance Path"]
    nodeD --> nodeGm["Gm(s) Transmitter"]
    nodeGm --> nodeGf["Gf(s) FEEDFORWARD CONTROLLER"]
    nodeGf --> nodeSumFB["Summing Junction m(t)"]
    nodeGv["Gv(s) Control Valve"] --> nodeSumFB
    nodeSumFB --> nodeMValveOutput["Manipulated Variable m(t)"]
    nodeMValveOutput --> nodeGp["Gp(s) PROCESS"]
    nodeGd --> nodeProcessOut["C(s) Controlled Variable"]
    nodeGp --> nodeProcessOut
    nodeSP["Set Point R(s)"] --> nodeErrSum["Error Junction e(t)"]
    nodeProcessOut --> nodeErrSum
    nodeErrSum --> nodeGfb["Gfb(s) FEEDBACK CONTROLLER"]
    nodeGfb --> nodeGv
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The disturbance $D$ splits into two parallel paths. The lower path goes through $G_m \to G_f \to G_v$ and arrives at the process as a *compensating* manipulated action. The upper path is the natural disturbance propagation through $G_d$. When $G_f$ is tuned ideally, the two arrivals at $C(s)$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign → **disturbance rejection**.

### 4.2 Mermaid Functional Architecture: Combined FF + FB (Industrial Standard)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSP1["Set Point SP"] --> nodeErr1["Summing Junction e"]
    nodeCV1["C(t) Controlled Variable"] --> nodeErr1
    nodeErr1 --> nodeFBctrl["PID Feedback Controller Gfb"]
    nodeDistMeas["Disturbance Sensor"] --> nodeFFctrl["Lead-Lag Feedforward Gf"]
    nodeFBctrl --> nodeSumMVC["Manipulated Variable Summing Junction m"]
    nodeFFctrl --> nodeSumMVC
    nodeSumMVC --> nodeValve["Control Valve Gv"]
    nodeValve --> nodePlant["Process Gp"]
    nodeDist["Unmeasured + Measured Disturbance"] --> nodePlant
    nodePlant --> nodeCV1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Practical Lead–Lag Feedforward Implementation

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeDsrc["DISTURBANCE D"] --> nodeTrans["Transmitter Gm"]
    nodeTrans --> nodeKff["Static Gain Kff"]
    nodeKff --> nodeLag["Lag Block 1 / 1 plus tau_lag s"]
    nodeLag --> nodeLead["Lead Block 1 plus tau_lead s"]
    nodeLead --> nodeSign["Sign Block plus or minus"]
    nodeSign --> nodeFFout["FF Output added to FB Output"]
    nodeFBpid["PID Output"] --> nodeFFout
    nodeFFout --> nodeValvePos["Valve Position"]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Practical Commissioning Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Step 1 Identify Major Disturbance"] --> nodeB["Step 2 Install / Verify Disturbance Sensor"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 3 Perform Open-Loop Step Test on Gd and Gp"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 4 Compute Kff from Steady-State Gains"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 5 Tune Static FF with Feedback in Manual"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 6 Add Lead Time Constant tau_lead"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step 7 Add Lag Time Constant tau_lag"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Step 8 Verify Disturbance Rejection"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Step 9 Re-Engage Feedback in Auto"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Step 10 Document and Monitor DRR"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
*Define feedforward control. State two key practical difficulties that prevent its use as a standalone strategy in industrial processes.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Feedforward control is an anticipatory control strategy in which the principal disturbance(s) affecting a process are measured, and the manipulated variable is adjusted so as to maintain the controlled variable at its set point without waiting for an error to develop. It operates on the **disturbance-cancellation principle**.

Two key practical difficulties:
1. **Imperfect process model:** Real processes are non-linear and time-varying; a model derived today may be invalid under future operating conditions.
2. **Imperfect disturbance measurement:** Sensors have finite bandwidth, noise, and dead time. Unmeasured secondary disturbances will still cause offset, requiring a **feedback trim controller**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*What is the role of the static feedforward gain $K_{ff}$? How is it computed from the steady-state gains of the disturbance path and the process path?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The static feedforward gain $K_{ff}$ determines the **magnitude** of the corrective action applied by the feedforward controller for a unit change in the measured disturbance, in the steady state. It is computed as:

$$K_{ff} = \frac{K_d}{K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_m}$$

where $K_d$, $K_p$, $K_v$, and $K_m$ are the steady-state gains of the disturbance path, process path, control valve, and transmitter, respectively. A correct $K_{ff}$ ensures **zero steady-state offset** for the measured disturbance; dynamic compensation is added separately via the lead–lag block.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Choice Pattern]**
*(a)* Derive the expression for the ideal feedforward controller transfer function $G_f(s)$ for a single-input single-disturbance (SISO-D) process, clearly stating the cancellation criterion. Discuss why the derived $G_f(s)$ may be physically unrealizable in practice. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A heat exchanger is heated by a steam flow rate $M$ (manipulated variable). The inlet process fluid flow rate $D$ (disturbance) varies between 1 and 5 kg/s. From open-loop step tests, the following steady-state data are obtained:

- When $D$ increases from 2 to 3 kg/s (with $M$ held constant), the outlet temperature increases by **+4 °C**. Hence $K_d = +4$ °C/(kg/s).
- When $M$ increases from 100 to 120 kg/h (with $D$ held constant), the outlet temperature increases by **+6 °C**. Hence $K_p = +0.30$ °C/(kg/h) per unit.
- The control valve and transmitter have steady-state gains $K_v = 0.5$ (kg/h per % stroke) and $K_m = 1.0$ (% per kg/s).

Compute the static feedforward gain $K_{ff}$ and explain the required **sign** of the feedforward action. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Step 1: Identify the two paths from disturbance $D$ to controlled variable $C$ — the direct path through $G_d(s)$ and the compensation path through $G_m \to G_f \to G_v \to G_p$.

Step 2: Apply superposition:

$$C(s) = D(s) \cdot G_d(s) + D(s) \cdot G_m(s) \cdot G_f(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)$$

Step 3: Factor and impose $C(s) = 0$:

$$G_d(s) + G_m(s) \cdot G_f(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) = 0$$

Step 4: Solve:

$$G_f^{ideal}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_m(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}$$

**Reasons for physical unrealizability (3 sub-points for 3 of the 7 marks):**
- The ideal form may contain **RHP zeros** (non-minimum-phase processes), which are non-causal.
- It may contain **derivative (numerator) terms** of order higher than denominator — physically impossible to implement.
- It assumes **perfect linearity and time-invariance**, which is never true in real plants.
- It assumes the disturbance transfer function $G_d(s)$ is exactly known and invertible.

**Part (b) — Static Feedforward Gain Computation (7 Marks):**

Step 1: Apply the static feedforward gain formula:

$$K_{ff} = \frac{K_d}{K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_m}$$

Step 2: Substitute values (with consistent unit handling):

$$K_{ff} = \frac{+4 \text{ °C/(kg/s)}}{+0.30 \text{ °C/(kg/h)} \times 0.50 \text{ (kg/h)/%} \times 1.0 \text{ %/(kg/s)}}$$

$$K_{ff} = \frac{4}{0.30 \times 0.50 \times 1.0} = \frac{4}{0.15} = +26.67$$

Step 3: Sign interpretation:
- An *increase* in disturbance $D$ causes $C$ to *increase* ($K_d > 0$).
- An *increase* in $M$ also causes $C$ to *increase* ($K_p > 0$).
- Therefore, when $D$ increases, we must *increase* $M$ to compensate → $K_{ff}$ is **positive (direct action)**.

Step 4: The feedforward output per unit disturbance change is $\Delta M = K_{ff} \cdot \Delta D = 26.67 \cdot \Delta D$ (in % valve stroke), ensuring steady-state offset is eliminated for the measured disturbance.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Alternate Choice]**
*(a)* With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **combined feedforward–feedback** control strategy. State two specific roles played by the feedback controller in such a configuration. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) disturbance path is identified as $G_d(s) = \dfrac{2.0 \cdot e^{-2s}}{1 + 5s}$ and a process path is identified as $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot e^{-3s}}{1 + 8s}$. The valve and transmitter gains are $K_v = 1.0$ and $K_m = 1.0$. Design a **Lead–Lag** feedforward compensator of the form $G_f(s) = -K_{ff} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \tau_{lead} s}{1 + \tau_{lag} s}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Combined FF + FB (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (4 marks):**
- The measured disturbance $D$ is fed to the feedforward controller $G_f$.
- $G_f$ output is summed with the PID feedback controller $G_{fb}$ output.
- The combined signal drives the control valve $G_v$ and then the process $G_p$.
- The controlled variable $C$ is compared with the set point $R$ to generate the error $E$.
- The feedback loop drives the residual error to zero.

**Two roles of the feedback controller:**
1. **Rejection of unmeasured disturbances:** Any disturbance not included in $G_f$ (e.g., ambient temperature, catalyst poison) is automatically corrected by the feedback loop.
2. **Correction of model error:** Even if $G_f$ is imperfect (wrong $K_{ff}$ or wrong lead-lag), the feedback loop drives the long-term offset to zero by integrating the residual error.

**Part (b) — Lead–Lag Compensator Design (7 Marks):**

Step 1: Compute the static feedforward gain:

$$K_{ff} = \frac{K_d}{K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_m} = \frac{2.0}{1.5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0} = 1.333$$

Step 2: Apply the sign rule. Both $K_d$ and $K_p$ are positive → both disturbance and manipulated variable affect $C$ in the *same* direction → $K_{ff}$ is **positive (direct action)**.

Step 3: Select the lead time constant. The disturbance lag is $\tau_d = 5$ s → set $\tau_{lead} = \tau_d = 5$ s.

Step 4: Select the lag time constant. The process lag is $\tau_p = 8$ s → set $\tau_{lag} = \tau_p = 8$ s.

Step 5: Note the disturbance dead time $\theta_d = 2$ s is *less* than the process dead time $\theta_p = 3$ s → feedforward has a chance to act before the disturbance fully propagates. This is a **favorable** case for feedforward.

Step 6: Final compensator expression:

$$G_f(s) = -1.333 \cdot \frac{1 + 5s}{1 + 8s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Sign Error (–2 marks):** The negative sign in the ideal formula is often omitted. Always state the **direction of action** explicitly: direct vs reverse.
> - **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** $K_d$ and $K_p$ must be in **consistent units** for the same controlled variable. A 4 °C/(kg/s) gain and a 0.3 °C/(kg/h) gain must be normalized to a common time basis (kg/s or kg/h) before division.
> - **Confusing Lead with Lag (–2 marks):** A student wrote "$\tau_{lead} = \tau_p$" and "$\tau_{lag} = \tau_d$" — the correct mapping is $\tau_{lead} \to \tau_d$ and $\tau_{lag} \to \tau_p$.
> - **Forgetting the Trim Controller (–1 mark):** A stand-alone feedforward design without a feedback trim loses 1 mark because the examiner expects discussion of the FF+FB combination in a 14-mark question.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

- **Definition:** Feedforward is an **anticipative**, **open-loop-compensation**, **model-based** control strategy.
- **Ideal Formula (MUST memorize):** $G_f^{ideal}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_m(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}$
- **Lead–Lag Form (MUST memorize):** $G_f(s) = -K_{ff} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \tau_{lead} s}{1 + \tau_{lag} s}$
- **Static Gain Formula:** $K_{ff} = \dfrac{K_d}{K_p K_v K_m}$
- **Lead Time Selection:** $\tau_{lead} = \tau_d$ (dominant disturbance lag)
- **Lag Time Selection:** $\tau_{lag} = \tau_p$ (dominant process lag)
- **Sign Rule:** If both $D \uparrow$ and $M \uparrow$ cause $C \uparrow$ → $K_{ff}$ is **positive** (direct). If they act in **opposite** directions on $C$ → $K_{ff}$ is **negative** (reverse).
- **Three Practical Issues:** Imperfect model, imperfect measurement, unmeasured disturbances.
- **Industrial Solution:** Always combine feedforward with a **feedback (trim) controller** (PI or PID).
- **DRR (Disturbance Rejection Ratio):** Practical target = **0.70 – 0.90**.
- **Pathological Case:** If $\theta_d < \theta_p$ and disturbance cannot be measured *upstream*, feedforward offers little benefit — feedback alone is preferred.
- **Two Paths in the Block Diagram:** Disturbance path ($G_d$) and compensation path ($G_m \to G_f \to G_v \to G_p$).
- **Examination Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always draw the **block diagram** and explicitly state the **cancellation criterion** before substituting into the formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Forward - Feedback Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Forward - Feedback Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Feed Forward–Feedback Control (FF–FB Control)** is a hybrid control architecture that combines the proactive disturbance-rejection capability of a **Feed Forward (FF) loop** with the corrective accuracy and robustness of a **Closed-Loop Feedback (FB) controller**. The feedforward path compensates for *measurable* (or *load*) disturbances *before* they propagate through the process, while the feedback path eliminates the residual error arising from *unmeasured* disturbances, modelling inaccuracies, and feedforward imperfections.

Mathematically, the controller output $m(t)$ is the algebraic sum of two parallel control actions:

$$m(t) = m_{FB}(t) - m_{FF}(t)$$

where $m_{FB}(t)$ depends on the error signal $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$, and $m_{FF}(t)$ depends on the measured load variable $u(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Feed Forward–Feedback control is categorized as a **"Combined / Composite Control Strategy"** under Module 2. It is the **industry-preferred architecture** for regulating processes such as heat exchangers, distillation columns, and chemical reactors, where dominant disturbances can be measured on-line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control with Radar" Intuition

Imagine driving a car on a hilly highway. The **feedback (FB) controller** is your *speedometer and throttle* — you keep glancing at the actual speed and correcting it whenever it deviates from the set value (e.g., 80 km/h). By the time you react, the car has already slowed down on an uphill.

Now, add a **radar system that detects the slope of the hill *in advance***. As soon as the radar senses an incline, it tells the throttle to push harder *before* the car actually loses speed. This proactive action is the **feedforward (FF) action**.

The **combined FF–FB system** uses *both*: the radar (FF) does the heavy lifting on predictable disturbances, and the speedometer-throttle loop (FB) cleans up anything the radar missed (sensor noise, wind gusts, imperfect road-data). The result is a smoother, faster, and more accurate speed regulation than either system could achieve alone.

## 1.3 Disturbance Classification in Process Control

| Disturbance Type | Description | Handled By |
|---|---|---|
| **Measurable / Load Disturbance $u(t)$** | Can be sensed on-line (e.g., inlet flow, inlet temperature) | **Feed Forward** loop |
| **Unmeasurable Disturbance $d(t)$** | Cannot be sensed directly (e.g., catalyst deactivation, fouling) | **Feedback** loop |
| **Set-Point Change $r(t)$** | Desired value alteration | **Feedback** loop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics to Remember:**
> - **IAE (Integral of Absolute Error):** $IAE = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ — used to benchmark FF–FB vs. pure FB.
> - **IAE Reduction** with proper FF tuning typically lies in the range **30% – 70%** for dominant load disturbances.
> - Standard FF controller implementation is **lead-lag** form: $G_{f}(s) = -K_{f} \dfrac{\tau_{1}s + 1}{\tau_{2}s + 1}$ (the negative sign accounts for the *negative* feedback convention of the load path).

## 1.4 Why Combine Feed Forward and Feedback?

A standalone feedforward controller is **blind** to model error, drift, and unmeasured upsets. A standalone feedback controller is **slow** to react to large, measured disturbances. Combining them yields:

- **Speed:** FF reacts instantly to measured loads.
- **Accuracy:** FB drives the steady-state error to zero.
- **Robustness:** FB corrects the FF controller's imperfections.
- **Practicality:** Real processes always have both kinds of disturbances.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the FF–FB Architecture

The combined controller can be decomposed into **four functional blocks** interacting in real time:

1. **Measurement of Load Variable $u(t)$**
   - A dedicated sensor (flow transmitter, temperature probe) acquires the *upstream* disturbance *before* it perturbs the controlled variable.

2. **Feed Forward Compensator $G_{f}(s)$**
   - Computes the *anticipatory* correction $m_{FF}(s) = G_{f}(s)\,U(s)$.
   - Designed to *invert* the disturbance dynamics: ideally $G_{f}(s) = -\dfrac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}$.

3. **Feedback Compensator $G_{c}(s)$**
   - Computes the *corrective* action $m_{FB}(s) = G_{c}(s)\,[R(s) - C(s)]$.
   - Conventionally a **PI** or **PID** controller for zero steady-state error.

4. **Final Control Element $G_{v}(s)$**
   - The control valve (or variable-speed pump) whose position is the *summed* output $M(s) = M_{FB}(s) - M_{FF}(s)$.

## 2.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Functions (Master Set)

Let the process be modelled as a **Two-Input, Single-Output (TISO)** system:

$$C(s) = G_{p}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,M(s) + G_{d}(s)\,U(s)$$

The controller structure:

$$M(s) = G_{c}(s)\,[R(s) - C(s)] - G_{f}(s)\,U(s)$$

Substituting and solving gives the canonical **closed-loop transfer functions**:

### 2.2.1 Set-Point Transfer Function

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{c}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}{1 + G_{c}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}$$

> **Key Insight:** The feedforward path **does not appear** in the set-point response. Set-point tracking depends only on the feedback loop.

### 2.2.2 Disturbance Transfer Function

$$\frac{C(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{G_{d}(s) - G_{f}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}{1 + G_{c}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}$$

> **Key Insight:** The numerator $G_{d}(s) - G_{f}(s)G_{v}(s)G_{p}(s)$ represents the *residual disturbance*. If we set this to zero, **perfect disturbance rejection** is achieved.

### 2.2.3 Ideal Feed Forward Controller

Setting the numerator to zero yields:

$$\boxed{\,G_{f,\text{ideal}}(s) = -\frac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}\,}$$

The negative sign is mandatory because the load and control paths enter the process with *opposite* effects (e.g., an increase in inlet temperature *raises* the outlet temperature, while an increase in steam flow *raises* it too — but the controller must *anticipate*, not *double-react*).

## 2.3 Realistic Feed Forward Controller (Lead–Lag Form)

Since ideal $G_{f}(s)$ is rarely physically realizable (it has pure derivatives and may be improper), engineers use a **static gain + lead–lag** approximation:

$$G_{f}(s) = -K_{f}\,\frac{\tau_{1}s + 1}{\tau_{2}s + 1}$$

where:
- **$K_{f}$** = static gain match: $K_{f} = \dfrac{K_{d}}{K_{v} K_{p}}$
- **$\tau_{1}$** = dominant time constant of the *disturbance* path
- **$\tau_{2}$** = dominant time constant of the *control* path

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Equation** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units / Boundary Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(s) = G_{p}G_{v}M + G_{d}U$ | Process model (TISO) | — |
| 2 | $M(s) = G_{c}[R-C] - G_{f}U$ | Composite controller | — |
| 3 | $\dfrac{C}{R}\big\vert_{U=0} = \dfrac{G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}}$ | Servo (set-point) response | Stable iff all poles in LHP |
| 4 | $\dfrac{C}{U}\big\vert_{R=0} = \dfrac{G_{d} - G_{f}G_{v}G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}}$ | Regulatory (load) response | Set numerator = 0 for ideal |
| 5 | $G_{f,\text{ideal}}(s) = -\dfrac{G_{d}}{G_{v}G_{p}}$ | Perfect disturbance cancellation | Requires exact model |
| 6 | $G_{f}(s) = -K_{f}\dfrac{\tau_{1}s+1}{\tau_{2}s+1}$ | Realistic lead–lag compensator | Proper & physically realizable |
| 7 | $K_{f} = \dfrac{K_{d}}{K_{v}K_{p}}$ | Static-gain balance | Dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | $e_{ss}\big\vert_{\text{step }U} = \lim_{s\to 0} s\,\dfrac{C(s)}{U(s)}\cdot\dfrac{1}{s} = 0$ | Steady-state error (with FF+PI) | Requires integrator in $G_{c}$ |
| 9 | $IAE = \int_{0}^{\infty} \mid e(t) \mid \,dt$ | Performance benchmark | Minimised by FF–FB tuning |
| 10 | $\text{Det}(I + G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}) = 0$ | Closed-loop characteristic equation | Roots in LHP $\Rightarrow$ stable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern:** Problems on FF–FB control almost always require students to (a) draw the block diagram, (b) derive $C(s)/U(s)$, and (c) state the ideal $G_{f}(s)$ condition. Memorizing entries #3, #4, and #5 of this table is **non-negotiable** for a full-score answer.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Heat Exchangers:** FF compensates for inlet flow/temperature changes; FB maintains outlet temperature.
- **Distillation Columns:** FF pre-acts on feed composition changes; FB regulates top/bottom product purity.
- **Boiler Drum Level:** FF–FB (called *three-element control* when combined with steam flow) prevents shrink–swell.
- **pH Neutralization:** FF on influent flow, FB on effluent pH.
- **HVAC Systems:** FF on outdoor air temperature, FB on zone temperature.
