# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Disturbance Transfer Function $C(s)/U(s)$

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 2. We derive it from first principles, line-by-line.

**Step 1 — Write the process equation (TISO model).**
The process output depends on two inputs: the manipulated variable $M(s)$ and the disturbance $U(s)$.

$$C(s) = G_{p}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,M(s) + G_{d}(s)\,U(s)$$

> *Logic:* Output is the *superposition* of two effects — one flowing through the valve and process, the other coming in directly through the disturbance path.

**Step 2 — Write the composite controller equation.**
The total controller output is the algebraic sum of the feedback action (proportional to error) and the feedforward action (proportional to the measured load).

$$M(s) = G_{c}(s)\,\bigl[R(s) - C(s)\bigr] - G_{f}(s)\,U(s)$$

> *Logic:* The minus sign on $G_{f}$ reflects the *anticipatory* nature of feedforward — it must *oppose* the disturbance.

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.**

$$C(s) = G_{p}\,G_{v}\,\bigl\{G_{c}\,[R - C] - G_{f}\,U\bigr\} + G_{d}\,U$$

**Step 4 — Expand the braces.**

$$C(s) = G_{p}\,G_{v}\,G_{c}\,R(s) - G_{p}\,G_{v}\,G_{c}\,C(s) - G_{p}\,G_{v}\,G_{f}\,U(s) + G_{d}\,U(s)$$

**Step 5 — Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$C(s) + G_{p}\,G_{v}\,G_{c}\,C(s) = G_{p}\,G_{v}\,G_{c}\,R(s) + \bigl[G_{d}(s) - G_{p}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{f}(s)\bigr]\,U(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor $C(s)$ on the left.**

$$C(s)\,\bigl[1 + G_{c}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)\bigr] = G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}\,R(s) + \bigl[G_{d} - G_{f}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}\bigr]\,U(s)$$

**Step 7 — Isolate $C(s)$ by dividing both sides by the bracketed term.**

$$C(s) = \frac{G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}\,R(s) + \frac{G_{d} - G_{f}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}\,U(s)$$

**Step 8 — Read off the two transfer functions.**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg\vert_{U=0} = \frac{G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\frac{C(s)}{U(s)}\bigg\vert_{R=0} = \frac{G_{d} - G_{f}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}\,}$$

> **Explanatory Note on Derivation Rigor:** Every algebraic manipulation above is *exact*. No approximations, no omissions. This is the KTU 2024 "full-marks" expected derivation — students who skip Steps 5 and 6 typically lose 2–3 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ideal Feed Forward Controller

**Step 1 — Begin from the disturbance transfer function:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{G_{d}(s) - G_{f}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}{1 + G_{c}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}$$

**Step 2 — For *perfect* disturbance rejection, the *output must be zero* for any non-zero $U(s)$.** This forces the *numerator* to be identically zero (the denominator is the closed-loop characteristic polynomial and is non-zero for a stable system).

$$G_{d}(s) - G_{f}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $G_{f}(s)$:**

$$\boxed{\,G_{f,\text{ideal}}(s) = \frac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}\,}$$

> *Sign convention note:* The negative sign often cited in textbooks arises because the *additive* convention $M = M_{FB} - M_{FF}$ is used (negative feedback), giving $G_{f,\text{ideal}} = -G_{d}/(G_{v}G_{p})$ algebraically. The boxed result above assumes $M = M_{FB} + M_{FF}$ convention. **Always state your sign convention explicitly in the KTU answer script** to avoid examiner ambiguity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Error Under a Unit-Step Disturbance

**Step 1 — Write the error $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$.** For the regulatory case, $R(s) = 0$, so:

$$E(s) = -C(s) = -\frac{G_{d} - G_{f}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}\,U(s)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Final Value Theorem for a unit-step disturbance, $U(s) = 1/s$:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,\left[-\frac{G_{d} - G_{f}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}\,G_{v}\,G_{p}} \cdot \frac{1}{s}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $s$ and evaluate the limit:**

$$e_{ss} = -\frac{G_{d}(0) - G_{f}(0)\,G_{v}(0)\,G_{p}(0)}{1 + G_{c}(0)\,G_{v}(0)\,G_{p}(0)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the gains.** Let $K_{d}$, $K_{f}$, $K_{v}$, $K_{p}$, $K_{c}$ be the DC gains. If $G_{c}$ contains a single integrator (PI/PID), then $K_{c} \to \infty$ and the denominator $\to \infty$:

$$e_{ss} = -\frac{K_{d} - K_{f}\,K_{v}\,K_{p}}{1 + \infty} \to 0$$

> *Conclusion:* The steady-state error is **zero** regardless of whether the feedforward controller is "ideal," as long as the feedback path contains an **integral action**. This is the second critical role of the feedback loop in the FF–FB architecture.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stability Condition (Routh–Hurwitz)

**Step 1 — The closed-loop characteristic equation is:**

$$1 + G_{c}(s)\,G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s) = 0$$

> *Note:* The feedforward controller $G_{f}(s)$ does **not** enter the characteristic equation. Therefore, **adding feedforward never destabilizes a stable feedback loop** (a crucial KTU 2024 viva question).

**Step 2 — For a first-order process and PI controller:**

$$G_{p} = \frac{K_{p}}{\tau_{p}s + 1}, \quad G_{v} = K_{v}, \quad G_{c} = K_{c}\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_{i}s}\right)$$

The characteristic polynomial becomes:

$$\tau_{p}\,\tau_{i}\,s^{2} + \tau_{i}\,s + K_{c}\,K_{v}\,K_{p}\,\tau_{i}\,s + K_{c}\,K_{v}\,K_{p} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply Routh–Hurwitz:** all coefficients positive $\Rightarrow$ stable.

$$\tau_{p}\,\tau_{i} > 0, \quad \tau_{i}(1 + K_{c}K_{v}K_{p}) > 0, \quad K_{c}K_{v}K_{p} > 0$$

Since all gains and time constants are positive, the system is **unconditionally stable** for first-order lag + PI.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What Controller

| **Process Characteristic** | **Recommended Strategy** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Slow process, dominant measurable load | **FF only** may suffice | FB loop too sluggish |
| Fast process, large unmeasured upsets | **FB (PID) only** | FF cannot model unknown disturbances |
| Mixed load + unmeasured disturbances | **FF–FB (this topic)** | Combines speed and accuracy |
| Process with large dead time | **FB (Smith Predictor)** + FF | Dead time defeats naive FF |
| Non-linear, multi-variable | **FF–FB + Gain-Scheduling** | Linear FF model fails at operating extremes |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Feed Forward–Feedback Control System

The figure below captures the **complete signal flow** of the FF–FB architecture. The summing junction produces the composite controller output, which drives the final control element; the feedback path closes the loop, while the feedforward path injects a parallel, anticipatory correction.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[R(s) Set Point] --> SB1((+))
    SB1 --> CFB[Feedback Controller Gc]
    CFB --> SB2((+))
    SB2 --> SS[(+ Sum)]
    SS --> VAL[Final Control Element Gv]
    VAL --> PROC[Process Gp]
    PROC --> OUT[C(s) Controlled Output]
    OUT --> SB1
    OUT --> FBK[Measurement Hm]
    FBK --> SB1
    LOAD[U(s) Measurable Disturbance] --> SENS[Load Sensor Hf]
    SENS --> FF[Feed Forward Compensator Gf]
    FF --> SB2
    DIST2[Unmeasured Disturbance D s] --> PROC
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram above is best interpreted as a **functional pipeline** (left to right):

| **Stage** | **Block** | **Function** | **Signal Handled** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `R(s)` | Reference input | Set-point |
| 2 | `SB1` | Error detection | $e = r - c$ |
| 3 | `Gc` | Feedback law | PI / PID computation |
| 4 | `Gf` | Feedforward law | Lead–lag of measured load |
| 5 | `SB2` | Composite summing | $m = m_{FB} - m_{FF}$ |
| 6 | `Gv` | Final control element | Valve / pump |
| 7 | `Gp` | Process dynamics | Plant transfer function |
| 8 | `Hm` | Measurement | Sensor + transmitter |
| 9 | `Hf` | Load measurement | Flow / T transmitter |
| 10 | `D(s)` | Unmeasured disturbance | Enters process directly |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The summing junction `SB2` is the **heart of the FF–FB architecture**. The *opposing* algebraic sign of the feedforward term (relative to the feedback term) is what gives the strategy its name — *feed forward minus feedback*. Many students draw both terms with the *same* sign, leading to an *additive* and ultimately *unstable* control law. Always verify the sign convention of the load path before tuning.

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Comparison: Pure FB vs. Pure FF vs. FF–FB

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FB[Pure Feedback]
        R1[R s] --> E1((e))
        E1 --> C1[Gc]
        C1 --> P1[Gv Gp]
        P1 --> Y1[C s]
        Y1 --> E1
    end
    subgraph FF[Pure Feedforward]
        R2[U s Measured Load] --> FFC[Gf]
        FFC --> P2[Gv Gp]
        P2 --> Y2[C s]
    end
    subgraph FFFB[Combined FF FB]
        R3[R s] --> E3((e))
        E3 --> C3[Gc]
        C3 --> SUM3((+))
        U3[U s] --> FFC3[Gf]
        FFC3 --> SUM3
        SUM3 --> P3[Gv Gp]
        P3 --> Y3[C s]
        Y3 --> E3
    end
```

| **Aspect** | **Pure FB** | **Pure FF** | **FF–FB** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disturbance rejection | *After* error occurs | *Before* error occurs | *Both* |
| Steady-state error | Zero (with I-action) | Non-zero (model error) | Zero |
| Robustness to model error | High | Low | High |
| Sensor requirement | Only controlled variable | Only load variable | Both |
| Stability impact of FF | N/A | N/A | None (FF out of CE) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Feed Forward–Feedback control. Mention two situations where this strategy is preferred over pure feedback control. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Feed Forward–Feedback control is a composite control strategy in which a feedforward controller compensates for *measurable* load disturbances in advance, while a parallel feedback controller eliminates the *residual* error arising from unmeasured disturbances and model inaccuracies. The total controller output is $m = m_{FB} - m_{FF}$.

**Two situations (1 mark):**
1. When the dominant disturbance (e.g., feed flow rate) is **measurable on-line** and the process has **significant lag/delay** between the disturbance and its effect on the controlled variable.
2. When **stringent regulatory performance** is required and a pure feedback loop is too slow (e.g., heat-exchanger outlet temperature under varying inlet conditions).

---

### Q2. State the ideal feedforward controller transfer function. Why is it rarely implemented in practice? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Equation (2 marks):**

$$G_{f,\text{ideal}}(s) = -\frac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{v}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}$$

**Reasons (1 mark):**
- The required transfer function is often **improper** (more zeros than poles), demanding pure derivatives which are physically unrealizable and noise-amplifying.
- It demands a **perfect process model**, which is never available in practice due to nonlinearity, aging, and unmodelled dynamics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a Feed Forward–Feedback control system for a process with one measurable disturbance. Clearly label all transfer functions, signals, and summing junctions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{U(s)}$ for the FF–FB system and obtain the condition for *perfect disturbance rejection*. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

The block diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** above. Key features to be drawn (1 mark each):

- Set-point $R(s)$ entering the positive terminal of summing junction $\Sigma_{1}$.
- Controlled output $C(s)$ fed back through the measurement block $H_{m}$ to the negative terminal of $\Sigma_{1}$.
- Error $E(s)$ entering the feedback controller $G_{c}(s)$.
- Measured load $U(s)$ passing through load-sensor $H_{f}$ and entering the feedforward compensator $G_{f}(s)$.
- The output of $G_{f}$ entering a second summing junction $\Sigma_{2}$ with a *negative* sign relative to $G_{c}$.
- The summed signal entering the final control element $G_{v}(s)$, then the process $G_{p}(s)$.
- Unmeasured disturbance $D(s)$ entering the process directly.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1:** Process equation:

$$C(s) = G_{p}G_{v}M(s) + G_{d}U(s) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2:** Controller equation:

$$M(s) = G_{c}[R(s) - C(s)] - G_{f}U(s) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute (ii) into (i):

$$C(s) = G_{p}G_{v}\{G_{c}[R - C] - G_{f}U\} + G_{d}U$$

**Step 4:** Expand:

$$C(s) = G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}R - G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}C - G_{f}G_{v}G_{p}U + G_{d}U$$

**Step 5:** Collect $C(s)$:

$$C[1 + G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}] = G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}R + [G_{d} - G_{f}G_{v}G_{p}]U$$

**Step 6:** Isolate $C/U$ (with $R=0$):

$$\frac{C(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{G_{d}(s) - G_{f}(s)G_{v}(s)G_{p}(s)}{1 + G_{c}(s)G_{v}(s)G_{p}(s)}$$

**Step 7:** Condition for *perfect disturbance rejection* — set numerator to zero:

$$G_{d}(s) - G_{f}(s)G_{v}(s)G_{p}(s) = 0 \implies G_{f,\text{ideal}}(s) = \frac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{v}(s)G_{p}(s)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students frequently *forget* to set $R(s) = 0$ before taking the ratio $C(s)/U(s)$. Doing so contaminates the derivation with set-point terms and costs **2 marks**. Always state the assumption explicitly.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** The negative sign in $G_{f} = -G_{d}/(G_{v}G_{p})$ depends on the *sign convention* of the summing junction. State your convention **before** writing the equation, or you will lose **1 mark** for ambiguity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Feed Forward–Feedback control system applied to a **heat exchanger** where the controlled variable is the outlet temperature, the manipulated variable is the steam flow rate, and the measurable disturbance is the inlet process flow rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order process has $G_{p}(s) = \dfrac{2}{5s+1}$ and $G_{v}(s) = 1$. The disturbance path is $G_{d}(s) = \dfrac{1.5}{3s+1}$. Design the ideal feedforward controller $G_{f}(s)$ and the realistic lead–lag approximation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Block diagram description (3 marks):** Draw the FF–FB loop (see Section 4.1) with the following process-specific labels:

- $R(s)$ = desired outlet temperature
- $C(s)$ = actual outlet temperature
- $U(s)$ = measured inlet process flow
- $M(s)$ = steam valve position
- $G_{c}(s)$ = PI controller on temperature error
- $G_{f}(s)$ = feedforward compensator on flow
- $G_{v}(s)$ = steam valve dynamics
- $G_{p}(s)$ = heat-exchanger process dynamics
- $G_{d}(s)$ = disturbance path from inlet flow to outlet temperature

**Operation (4 marks):**
1. An increase in inlet flow $U$ is sensed instantly by the flow transmitter.
2. $G_{f}$ computes the required *anticipatory* increase in steam valve opening.
3. Steam flow rises *before* the outlet temperature drops, maintaining it at set-point.
4. The feedback controller $G_{c}$ corrects any *residual* error due to imperfect model or ambient upsets.
5. The two actions are summed at the second junction and sent to the valve.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the ideal feedforward controller:**

$$G_{f,\text{ideal}}(s) = -\frac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{v}(s)G_{p}(s)} = -\frac{\dfrac{1.5}{3s+1}}{1 \cdot \dfrac{2}{5s+1}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$G_{f,\text{ideal}}(s) = -\frac{1.5}{3s+1} \cdot \frac{5s+1}{2} = -\frac{0.75\,(5s+1)}{3s+1}$$

**Step 3 — Identify static gain:**

$$K_{f} = \frac{K_{d}}{K_{v}K_{p}} = \frac{1.5}{1 \times 2} = 0.75$$

**Step 4 — Identify lead–lag time constants:**
- $\tau_{1} = 5$ s (numerator — from the *control* path $G_{p}$)
- $\tau_{2} = 3$ s (denominator — from the *disturbance* path $G_{d}$)

**Step 5 — Realistic lead–lag form:**

$$\boxed{\,G_{f}(s) = -0.75 \cdot \frac{5s+1}{3s+1}\,}$$

This is *proper*, physically realizable, and matches both the static gain and the dominant time-constant dynamics of the ideal compensator.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** In numerical problems, students often *interchange* $\tau_{1}$ and $\tau_{2}$ (placing the process time constant in the denominator and vice versa). This yields an *inverse* compensator that *amplifies* the disturbance instead of cancelling it. **Always verify:** the $\tau$ from the *disturbance* path goes in the *denominator* of $G_{f}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Feed Forward–Feedback Control**

- **Architecture:** FF path handles *measurable* loads; FB path handles *unmeasured* disturbances and *model error*.
- **Composite Output:** $M(s) = M_{FB}(s) - M_{FF}(s)$ — *opposing* signs are mandatory.
- **Master Equation:** $\dfrac{C(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{G_{d} - G_{f}G_{v}G_{p}}{1 + G_{c}G_{v}G_{p}}$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Ideal FF Controller:** $G_{f,\text{ideal}}(s) = -\dfrac{G_{d}(s)}{G_{v}(s)G_{p}(s)}$ — numerator set to zero for perfect rejection.
- **Realistic Form:** $G_{f}(s) = -K_{f}\dfrac{\tau_{1}s+1}{\tau_{2}s+1}$, where $K_{f} = \dfrac{K_{d}}{K_{v}K_{p}}$.
- **Stability Theorem:** $G_{f}(s)$ is **outside** the closed-loop characteristic equation. **Adding FF never destabilizes a stable FB loop.**
- **Steady-State Error:** $e_{ss} = 0$ for step disturbance when $G_{c}$ contains an integrator (PI/PID), *regardless* of FF accuracy.
- **Set-Point Response:** FF plays **no role** in $C(s)/R(s)$; servo tracking depends solely on the feedback loop.
- **Practical Lead–Lag Rule:** $\tau_{1}$ = process time constant; $\tau_{2}$ = disturbance time constant (numerator matches the *control* path; denominator matches the *load* path).
- **Industry Names:** "Three-element boiler control," "Ratio control," and "Inlet-forwarded heat-exchanger control" are all real-world embodiments of the FF–FB concept.
- **Examiner Tip:** When asked to "design" $G_{f}$, always present **both** the *ideal* form and the *realistic lead–lag* approximation. KTU 2024 awards 2 marks for the practical realizability discussion.
- **Common Mistake:** Drawing the FF summing junction with the *same* sign as the FB term — this creates a *positive* feedforward that *amplifies* the disturbance. Always use the *opposing* sign.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ratio Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ratio Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ratio Control** is a specialized multivariable control strategy in which two (or more) process variables are automatically maintained at a fixed, predetermined proportion to one another. The variable whose flow is independently set is called the **Wild Flow** ($F_W$) or **Master Flow**, and the variable whose set point is dynamically computed to follow the wild flow is called the **Controlled Flow** ($F_C$) or **Slave Flow**.

Mathematically, the governing relationship is:

$$R = \frac{F_C}{F_W}$$

where $R$ is the dimensionless **Ratio Set Point** (the constant of proportionality), $F_C$ is the controlled (slave) flow rate, and $F_W$ is the wild (master) flow rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Ratio control is a classical building block of advanced regulatory control. The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between the *blend-header (parallel metering)* and *ratio-station (serial multiplier)* implementation strategies and to sketch the corresponding block diagrams with proper signal-flow annotations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chef's Measuring Cup

Imagine a chef preparing a curry that **must** contain exactly **2 parts tomato** for every **1 part onion** by mass, no matter how much curry is being made. Whether the chef is cooking for 2 people or 200, the ratio never changes. The chef measures the tomato first (the *wild flow* — driven by the recipe size) and then uses a calibrated measuring cup that automatically dispenses exactly half that amount of onion (the *controlled flow*).

- If the chef suddenly has to scale up the recipe → tomato increases → onion automatically doubles.
- If the recipe is scaled down → tomato decreases → onion automatically halves.

The chef never explicitly tells anyone "give me 4 kg of onion." Instead, the instruction is *"give me half of whatever tomato you get."* This implicit, dynamic set-point generation is the essence of **Ratio Control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering equivalence:** In a combustion process, the air flow is the wild flow, the fuel flow is the slave flow, and the ratio $R = F_{fuel} / F_{air}$ must be tightly regulated to maintain efficient and clean burning.

## 1.3 Standard Nomenclature & Key Terms

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Wild Flow | $F_W$ | The independent, free-running process variable whose value is determined by upstream conditions. |
| Controlled Flow | $F_C$ | The dependent flow that is automatically regulated to satisfy the ratio. |
| Ratio Set Point | $R$ | The desired dimensionless ratio $F_C / F_W$. |
| Ratio Relay / Station | — | The computing element that multiplies $F_W$ by $R$ to generate $F_{C,sp}$. |
| Blend Header | — | A piping manifold where two streams are physically mixed; orifices or valves meter the ratio. |
| Lead-Lag Compensator | — | Optional dynamic element used to compensate for transport delays between the two flow loops. |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU textbooks, the wild flow is often alternatively called the **basic flow** or **master flow**, while the controlled flow is also called the **dependent flow** or **slave flow**. All four terms are interchangeable in examination answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Ratio Control is Needed — Engineering Motivation

Many industrial processes are not concerned with the absolute magnitude of a single variable, but with the **relative proportion** of two streams being combined. Some canonical applications:

1. **Blending Operations** — Mixing of petroleum fractions, paints, beverages, pharmaceutical syrups.
2. **Combustion Control** — Maintaining a precise air-to-fuel ratio in boilers and furnaces.
3. **Neutralization / pH Control** — Mixing acid and base streams in a stoichiometric proportion.
4. **Distillation Reflux** — Setting the reflux-to-distillate ratio to control product purity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just use two independent PI controllers?** Because the set point of the slave loop is **not fixed** — it must *track* the wild flow. A ratio controller computes the slave set point *online* from a real-time measurement of the wild flow.

## 2.2 Two Principal Implementation Strategies

### Strategy A — Blend-Header (Parallel Metering) Implementation

In this architecture, two control valves are mounted on parallel branches entering a common mixing vessel (the *blend header*). Each valve has its own flow loop, and the *physical* opening of the valves (via characterized trims or split-ranged valve positioners) determines the ratio. The ratio is adjusted mechanically by changing the **valve trim characteristics** (equal-percentage, linear) or by adjusting a mechanical linkage.

### Strategy B — Ratio Station (Multiplier-Based) Implementation

This is the **modern, electronic, and KTU-favoured** architecture. The wild flow is measured by a flow transmitter (FT), and its signal is sent to a dedicated **ratio relay** (or computing station) that multiplies it by the desired ratio $R$. The output of the ratio relay becomes the **set point of the slave flow controller (FC)**, while the slave's own flow transmitter (FT) closes its own feedback loop.

## 2.3 Detailed Block Diagram — The Ratio Station Method

The signal flow for the ratio-station architecture is:

$$\text{Wild Flow } (F_W) \;\xrightarrow{\text{FT}}\; I_W \;\xrightarrow{\text{Ratio Station } (R)}\; F_{C,sp} \;\xrightarrow{\text{FC}}\; I_C \;\to\; \text{Slave Valve} \;\to\; F_C$$

The slave flow controller FC uses the dynamically-generated $F_{C,sp}$ as its set point, ensuring that $F_C / F_W = R$ at all times.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = F_C / F_W$ | Defining equation of the ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $F_{C,sp} = R \cdot F_W$ | Slave set point generated by ratio station | same as $F_W$ |
| 3 | $e(t) = F_{C,sp}(t) - F_C(t)$ | Error fed to the slave PI controller | same as flow units |
| 4 | $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ | PI controller transfer function | dimensionless |
| 5 | $G_v(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$ | Control-valve first-order transfer function | — |
| 6 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ | Process (flow) first-order transfer function | — |
| 7 | $G_m(s) = K_m$ | Flow transmitter (essentially instantaneous) | — |
| 8 | $F_C(s) = \dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m}\,F_{C,sp}(s)$ | Closed-loop slave transfer function | — |
| 9 | $F_C(s) = R \cdot \dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m}\,F_W(s)$ | Overall ratio-station transfer function | — |
| 10 | Steady-state: $F_C^{ss} = R \cdot F_W^{ss}$ | Ideal ratio achieved in steady state | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical formatting note for your answer script:** When writing the absolute value of a transfer function (e.g., $\vert G(j\omega)\vert$), always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, never a raw vertical pipe `|`, which can confuse the markdown table renderer in KTU's online valuation portal.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Ratio control sits at the heart of **Model-Prediction-Free (Modelfree) Advanced Process Control** (APC). The vast majority of APC layer-1 deployments in refineries, ethylene plants, and pulp & paper mills consist of *cascaded* ratio controllers. Mastering this topic is the gateway to understanding **split-range control**, **override control**, and **model-predictive control (MPC)** in higher semesters.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Slave Loop)

Let the wild flow be measured by a flow transmitter of gain $K_{mW}$, and let the slave loop consist of a PI controller $G_c(s)$, a valve $G_v(s)$, and a flow process $G_p(s)$, with a slave flow transmitter of gain $K_{mC}$. The ratio station is a pure multiplier of gain $R$.

**Step 1 — Wild flow measurement.**
The measured wild-flow signal entering the ratio station is:

$$I_W(s) = K_{mW} \cdot F_W(s)$$

**Step 2 — Ratio station multiplication.**
The ratio station multiplies this signal by the user-set ratio $R$ to generate the slave set point:

$$F_{C,sp}(s) = R \cdot I_W(s) = R \cdot K_{mW} \cdot F_W(s)$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is a static, algebraic multiplication; no dynamics are introduced by the ratio station.

**Step 3 — Slave controller error.**
The error signal feeding the PI controller is the difference between the set point and the measured slave flow:

$$E(s) = F_{C,sp}(s) - K_{mC} \cdot F_C(s)$$

**Step 4 — Manipulated variable (valve output).**
The PI controller drives the valve through:

$$M(s) = G_c(s) \cdot E(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)\!\left[F_{C,sp}(s) - K_{mC} F_C(s)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Process response.**
The valve manipulates the process, producing the controlled flow:

$$F_C(s) = G_v(s)\,G_p(s)\,M(s)$$

**Step 6 — Combine Steps 4 and 5.**

$$F_C(s) = G_v(s) G_p(s) K_c \!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)\!\left[R K_{mW} F_W(s) - K_{mC} F_C(s)\right]$$

**Step 7 — Collect $F_C$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$F_C(s)\!\left[1 + G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s) K_{mC}\right] = G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s) R K_{mW} F_W(s)$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function.**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{F_C(s)}{F_W(s)} = \frac{R \, K_{mW}\, G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s)}{1 + K_{mC}\, G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s)}\;}$$

**Step 9 — Steady-state simplification.**
For a perfect flow loop in which the slave follows its set point with zero steady-state error, substitute $s = 0$ and $G_c(0) \to \infty$ (integral action) and $G_m(0) = K_{mC}$:

$$F_C^{ss} = R \cdot \frac{K_{mW}}{K_{mC}} \cdot F_W^{ss}$$

> If both transmitters are calibrated to the same engineering units ($K_{mW} = K_{mC}$), the steady-state ratio reduces to the ideal $F_C^{ss} = R \cdot F_W^{ss}$, confirming that **ratio control is asymptotically exact** in the steady state.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tuning the Slave Loop for Ratio Tracking

The slave loop must respond **faster** than the wild loop, or ratio excursions will occur whenever the wild flow changes. The KTU heuristic (Coughanowr, *Process Systems Analysis and Control*, 3rd ed., Ch. 16) is:

| Parameter | Recommended Rule | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Slave loop $K_c$ | $0.5 \times K_{c,u}$ (half of ultimate gain) | Robust stability margin |
| Slave loop $\tau_I$ | $0.5 \times P_u$ (half of ultimate period) | Avoids sluggish ratio recovery |
| Slave loop bandwidth | $\geq 3 \times$ wild loop bandwidth | Slave must track wild |

> [!NOTE]
> A ratio loop is essentially a **cascade control** structure in which the outer "ratio controller" is replaced by a static multiplier. The slave loop tuning rules therefore mirror cascade-control inner-loop tuning.

## 3.3 Lead-Lag Dynamic Compensation

In practice, the wild and slave flow loops have **different dynamics** (different valve sizes, pipe lengths, transport delays). A pure multiplier maintains the *steady-state* ratio but not the *dynamic* ratio. To synchronize the dynamic responses, a **lead-lag block** is often inserted in the wild-flow signal path before the ratio station:

$$G_{LL}(s) = \frac{\tau_{lead}\,s + 1}{\tau_{lag}\,s + 1}$$

The lag time $\tau_{lag}$ is set equal to the dead time of the slower loop, and the lead time $\tau_{lead}$ to the dead time of the faster loop, so that both manipulated variables change in unison.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ratio Station (Multiplier Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Wild Flow FW"] --> B["Flow Transmitter FTW"]
    B --> C["Ratio Relay Station R"]
    C --> D["Set Point FC sp for Slave"]
    D --> E["Slave Flow Controller FC PI"]
    F["Measured Slave Flow FC"] --> G["Flow Transmitter FTC"]
    G --> E
    E --> H["Slave Control Valve V"]
    H --> I["Process Piping"]
    I --> F
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Schematic of a Combustion Air–Fuel Ratio System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Boiler Steam Demand"] --> B["Master Flow Controller on Air"]
    B --> C["Air Damper Position"]
    C --> D["Combustion Chamber"]
    E["Fuel Supply Line"] --> F["Slave Flow Controller on Fuel"]
    F --> G["Fuel Valve Position"]
    G --> D
    D --> H["Flue Gas O2 Analyzer"]
    H -->|"Trim Signal"| F
    style A fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 State-Flow Diagram — Modes of Ratio Control

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AutoRatio
    AutoRatio --> ManualRatio : Operator disables multiplier
    ManualRatio --> AutoRatio : Operator re-enables multiplier
    AutoRatio --> Override : Slave measurement out of range
    Override --> AutoRatio : Condition clears
    ManualRatio --> [*]
    state AutoRatio {
        A["Read Wild Flow FW"] --> B["Compute sp = R times FW"]
        B --> C["Drive Slave FC"]
    }
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Blend-Header (Parallel Metering) Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Stream A Inlet"] --> V1["Valve 1 with Characterized Trim"]
    B["Stream B Inlet"] --> V2["Valve 2 with Characterized Trim"]
    V1 --> M["Blend Header Mixing Manifold"]
    V2 --> M
    M --> P["Product Outlet"]
    style V1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style V2 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **blend-header** method, no electronic multiplier is needed; the ratio is set by the *relative valve characteristics* (e.g., one linear, one equal-percentage) and by *fixed orifice plates* sized to deliver the desired flow split at a given header pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *ratio control*. Distinguish between the *wild flow* and the *controlled flow* with one industrial example.

> **Model Answer:**
> Ratio control is a regulatory control strategy that maintains two (or more) process variables in a fixed, pre-defined proportion. The **wild flow** $F_W$ is the independent, free-running variable whose value is dictated by upstream conditions. The **controlled flow** $F_C$ is the dependent variable whose set point is dynamically computed as $F_{C,sp} = R \cdot F_W$ so that the ratio $F_C/F_W = R$ is preserved.
> *Example:* In a boiler, air flow is wild (driven by steam demand) and fuel flow is controlled (slave) to maintain a stoichiometric air–fuel ratio of $\approx 10:1$. **(3 marks)**

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is a *lead-lag compensator* used in the wild-flow signal path of a ratio control loop? State the rule of thumb for setting its time constants.

> **Model Answer:**
> A lead-lag block is inserted to equalize the dynamic response of the slave loop to that of the wild loop, so that the ratio is preserved *dynamically* and not only in the steady state. The rule of thumb is:
> $$\tau_{lag} = \text{dead time of the slower loop}, \qquad \tau_{lead} = \text{dead time of the faster loop}$$
> This ensures the slave and wild manipulated variables change in unison when the wild flow ramps. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** With a neat block diagram, explain the *ratio-station (multiplier)* method of implementing ratio control. Derive the closed-loop transfer function between the wild flow and the slave flow.

#### (a) Block Diagram and Explanation — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

1. The **wild flow** $F_W$ is sensed by a flow transmitter FT, producing a pneumatic/electronic signal $I_W = K_{mW} F_W$. *(1 mark)*
2. This signal is fed to a dedicated **ratio relay station** that multiplies it by the operator-adjusted ratio $R$ to obtain $F_{C,sp} = R \cdot I_W$. *(1 mark)*
3. $F_{C,sp}$ becomes the **set point of the slave flow controller (FC)**, which compares it with the measured slave flow $I_C = K_{mC} F_C$. *(1 mark)*
4. The controller output drives a **slave control valve** that manipulates the controlled flow $F_C$. *(1 mark)*
5. The slave loop, being a closed flow loop, is fast-acting, so any change in $F_W$ is reflected as a corresponding change in $F_{C,sp}$, and the slave controller tracks it. *(1 mark)*
6. Lead-lag compensation $G_{LL}(s)$ may be added in the wild-flow path for dynamic matching. *(1 mark)*
7. A typical industrial example: air–fuel ratio in a furnace, where air is wild and fuel is controlled. *(1 mark)*

#### (b) Closed-Loop Transfer Function Derivation — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:** The detailed algebraic derivation is presented in §3.1 (Steps 1–9). The final result is:

$$\frac{F_C(s)}{F_W(s)} = \frac{R \, K_{mW}\, G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s)}{1 + K_{mC}\, G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s)}$$

> With $G_c(s) = K_c(1 + 1/\tau_I s)$, $G_v(s) = K_v/(\tau_v s + 1)$, $G_p(s) = K_p/(\tau_p s + 1)$, and $K_{mW} = K_{mC}$, the steady-state response is $F_C^{ss} = R \cdot F_W^{ss}$, confirming exact ratio tracking. **(Full marks: 7)**

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alt]** Compare the *blend-header* and *ratio-station* methods of ratio control. For a furnace in which fuel flow $F_{fuel}$ must be maintained at 1/10 of air flow $F_{air}$, calculate the ratio set point $R$ and the expected fuel flow when the air flow ramps to $F_{air} = 25{,}000\;\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$.

#### (a) Comparison Table — 7 Marks

| Criterion | Blend-Header (Parallel) | Ratio Station (Multiplier) |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Mechanical (valve trims, orifice plates) | Electronic / pneumatic multiplier |
| Ratio adjustment | Difficult — requires changing trim or orifice | Easy — dial $R$ on the relay |
| Dynamic accuracy | Static (steady-state) only | Can include lead-lag for dynamic matching |
| Typical use | Simple blending, low-cost | Combustion, distillation, modern DCS |
| Reliability | High (no electronics) | Moderate (single point of failure: relay) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Maintenance | Trim erosion over time | Calibration of ratio station |

*(Full marks awarded for a 5–6 row table with clear entries.)*

#### (b) Numerical Calculation — 7 Marks

**Given:**

$$F_{fuel} = \frac{1}{10} F_{air} \;\Rightarrow\; R = \frac{F_C}{F_W} = \frac{F_{fuel}}{F_{air}} = 0.1$$

**Computation:**

$$F_{fuel} = R \cdot F_{air} = 0.1 \times 25{,}000\;\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

$$\boxed{\;F_{fuel} = 2{,}500\;\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}\;}$$

*(7 marks: 3 for ratio identification, 2 for substitution, 2 for final answer with units.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Ratio Control Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing up wild and controlled flows** — In a furnace, air is the *wild* flow (driven by steam demand) and fuel is the *controlled* flow. Writing the inverse loses at least 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the ratio-station gain $R$** in the transfer function — Examiners expect to see the factor $R \cdot K_{mW}$ multiplying the numerator; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not distinguishing between *blend-header* and *ratio-station* methods** — A 14-mark question often explicitly asks for the comparison; conflating them indicates weak conceptual understanding.
> 4. **Skipping the lead-lag justification** — Whenever you mention dynamic ratio control, you *must* justify why a lead-lag compensator is needed (loop dead-time mismatch). Merely naming it scores zero.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Always carry the engineering units (Nm³/h, kg/h, L/min) into the final numerical answer; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core definition:** Ratio control maintains a fixed proportion $R = F_C / F_W$ between a *wild* (master) and *controlled* (slave) flow.
- **Wild flow** is free-running and dictated by upstream demand; **controlled flow** tracks it via a dynamically computed set point.
- **Two methods:** *Blend-header* (mechanical, static) and *Ratio-station* (electronic/digital, dynamic). Ratio-station is the KTU-favoured modern implementation.
- **Closed-loop transfer function** of the slave loop is $\dfrac{R\,K_{mW}\,G_c G_v G_p}{1 + K_{mC}\,G_c G_v G_p}$; in steady state, $F_C^{ss} = (R\,K_{mW}/K_{mC})\,F_W^{ss}$.
- **Tuning rule:** The slave loop must be **3× faster** than the wild loop, with $K_c \approx 0.5 K_{c,u}$ and $\tau_I \approx 0.5 P_u$.
- **Lead-lag compensation** equalizes loop dead times; $\tau_{lag}$ = dead time of the slow loop, $\tau_{lead}$ = dead time of the fast loop.
- **Applications:** blending, combustion (air–fuel), pH neutralization, distillation reflux, and chemical dosing.
- **Ratio control is essentially cascade control** with a static multiplier replacing the outer PI controller.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** always specify which stream is *wild* and which is *controlled*; never write the ratio equation upside down.
- **Industrial relevance:** forms Layer-1 of Advanced Process Control (APC) hierarchies in refineries and chemical plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Controller** is an error-actuated, solid-state controller that employs active devices (operational amplifiers, transistors, and passive RC networks) to generate a continuous analog control signal by implementing the Proportional (P), Integral (I), Derivative (D), and combined (PI, PD, PID) control laws in the analog electronic domain.

The controller accepts a **DC error voltage** $e(t) = V_{sp} - V_{pv}$ (or $e(t) = V_{pv} - V_{sp}$ in some sign conventions) and produces a manipulated variable $m(t)$ that drives the final control element. In KTU 2024 scheme nomenclature, electronic controllers are classified as **analog continuous controllers**, distinct from pneumatic and digital counterparts.

## 1.2 Operational Amplifier as the Heart of the Electronic Controller

The **operational amplifier (op-amp)** is the foundational building block. When configured in an **inverting mode** with a generalized input impedance $Z_i(s)$ and feedback impedance $Z_f(s)$, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{Z_i(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single, elegant relationship is the **master equation** of all electronic controller design. Every P, I, D, PI, PD, and PID controller is realized by merely substituting the appropriate RC network for $Z_i$ and $Z_f$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cruise Control of a Car (PID Analogy):**
> - **Proportional (P)** action is like a human driver pressing the accelerator **harder in proportion** to how far the car speed is below the setpoint. The bigger the error, the bigger the corrective push.
> - **Integral (I)** action is like the driver **remembering past errors** — if the car has been slow for a long time, the driver presses the accelerator more and more. It eliminates steady-state offset.
> - **Derivative (D)** action is like a driver **anticipating future error** by observing how fast the car is approaching the desired speed. If the car is closing in fast, the driver eases off the accelerator to prevent overshoot.
> - **Electronic implementation** is the **robot chauffeur** — it does all three simultaneously with op-amp circuits, faster and more precisely than any human.

## 1.4 Why Prefer Electronic Controllers over Pneumatic?

| Feature | Electronic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of response | **High** ($\mu$s range) | Slow (seconds) |
| Accuracy | High (mV resolution) | Moderate |
| Signal transmission | Easy, noise-immune (4-20 mA) | Susceptible to leaks |
| Integration with DCS | Seamless | Requires I/P converters |
| Cost of implementation | Low (IC-based) | High (compressors, tubing) |

**Standard signal range** in industrial electronic control: **4–20 mA** current loop or **1–5 V / 0–10 V** voltage standard. The **3–15 psi** pneumatic equivalent is replaced by **4–20 mA** for the same process variable range (Span = **16 mA**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Op-Amp Inverting Configuration — Foundation Stone

An ideal op-amp obeys two golden rules:
1. **Virtual short:** $V^+ = V^-$ at the inverting input (since open-loop gain $A \to \infty$).
2. **No current into input terminals:** $I^+ = I^- = 0$ (since input impedance $Z_{in} \to \infty$).

Applying KCL at the inverting node $V^-$ (which is held at virtual ground):

$$\frac{V_i - 0}{Z_i} + \frac{V_o - 0}{Z_f} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_o}{V_i} = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_i}$$

This is the **master transfer function** from which every controller is derived.

## 2.2 Realization of Each Control Mode

### (a) Proportional (P) Controller
- $Z_i = R_1$, $Z_f = R_f$
- $G_c(s) = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = -K_c$

### (b) Integral (I) Controller
- $Z_i = R_1$, $Z_f = \dfrac{1}{C \cdot s}$
- $G_c(s) = -\dfrac{1/Cs}{R_1} = -\dfrac{1}{R_1 C s} = -\dfrac{K_i}{s}$

### (c) Derivative (D) Controller (Ideal — not used in practice)
- $Z_i = \dfrac{1}{C s}$, $Z_f = R_f$
- $G_c(s) = -R_f \cdot C s = -K_d s$

### (d) PI Controller
- $Z_i = R_1$, $Z_f = R_2 + \dfrac{1}{C s}$
- $G_c(s) = -\left[\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_1 C s}\right] = -K_c\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right]$

### (e) PD Controller
- $Z_i = R_1 \parallel \dfrac{1}{C s} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 C s + 1}$, $Z_f = R_2$
- $G_c(s) = -K_c \left[1 + T_d s\right]$

### (f) PID Controller (Inverting Form)
- $Z_i = R_1 \parallel \dfrac{1}{C_1 s}$, $Z_f = R_2 + \dfrac{1}{C_2 s}$
- $G_c(s) = -K_c\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right]$ (ideal case)

## 2.3 The Practical "Real" Derivative (Derivative Filter)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure derivative action is physically unrealizable** because it amplifies high-frequency noise infinitely. Therefore, a **first-order lag** is added in series with the D-action to produce the **"real" derivative**:
> $$D_{real}(s) = \dfrac{T_d s}{1 + \dfrac{T_d}{N} s}$$
> where $N$ is typically **8 to 20** (often chosen as $N = 10$). The high-frequency gain is **bounded by $N$**, preventing noise amplification.

## 2.4 Anti-Windup Mechanism (Bumpless Transfer)

> [!WARNING]
> **Integral Windup** is a critical failure mode in electronic PID controllers. When the actuator saturates (e.g., valve fully open/closed), the integrator keeps accumulating error, causing large overshoot when the error sign reverses. **Solution:** Use a **back-calculation** or **clamping** anti-windup circuit — feedback the difference between the controller output and the saturated actuator output to discharge the integrator capacitor.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formulas — Memorize These:**

| Control Mode | Time-Domain Equation | Transfer Function $G_c(s)$ | Tuning Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| P (Proportional) | $m(t) = K_c \, e(t)$ | $K_c$ | $K_c$ (Gain) |
| I (Integral) | $m(t) = \dfrac{K_c}{T_i} \displaystyle\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$ | $\dfrac{K_c}{T_i s}$ | $K_c,\, T_i$ |
| D (Derivative) | $m(t) = K_c T_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_c T_d s$ | $K_c,\, T_d$ |
| PI | $m(t) = K_c \left[e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i} \displaystyle\int e \, dt \right]$ | $K_c \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right]$ | $K_c,\, T_i$ |
| PD | $m(t) = K_c \left[e(t) + T_d \dfrac{de}{dt} \right]$ | $K_c [1 + T_d s]$ | $K_c,\, T_d$ |
| PID (Ideal) | $m(t) = K_c \left[e + \dfrac{1}{T_i} \int e \, dt + T_d \dfrac{de}{dt} \right]$ | $K_c \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right]$ | $K_c,\, T_i,\, T_d$ |
| PID (Real with Filter) | — | $K_c \left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + \dfrac{T_d s}{1 + (T_d/N) s} \right]$ | $K_c,\, T_i,\, T_d,\, N$ |

| Component | Transfer Function Contribution |
|---|---|
| Resistor $R$ | $Z_R = R$ |
| Capacitor $C$ | $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{Cs}$ |
| Inverting Op-Amp | $G = -\dfrac{Z_f}{Z_i}$ |

> **Proportional Band** $PB$ (in %): $PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c}$ (when $K_c$ is dimensionless). $PB = 100\%$ corresponds to $K_c = 1$.

> **Derivative filter constant** $N$: typically **8 to 20** (industry default $N = 10$).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — PI Controller Transfer Function

**Circuit Description:** Non-inverting input grounded. Input signal $V_i$ applied through $R_1$ to the inverting node. Feedback path contains $R_2$ in **series** with capacitor $C$.

**Step 1 — Identify the impedances:**

$$Z_i(s) = R_1$$

$$Z_f(s) = R_2 + \frac{1}{C s} = \frac{R_2 C s + 1}{C s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the inverting amplifier transfer function:**

$$G_c(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{Z_i(s)} = -\frac{(R_2 C s + 1) / (C s)}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 — Split the expression into two terms:**

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{1}{R_1} \cdot \frac{R_2 C s + 1}{C s} = -\frac{1}{R_1} \left[ R_2 + \frac{1}{C s} \right]$$

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_1 C s}$$

**Step 4 — Express in standard PI form $G_c(s) = -K_c \left[ 1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} \right]$:**

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{R_2 C s} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Identify the design parameters (this is what KTU examiners love):**

$$K_c = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \quad \text{(Proportional Gain, dimensionless)}$$

$$T_i = R_2 \cdot C \quad \text{(Integral Time, in seconds)}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation:** Increasing $R_2$ raises both gain $K_c$ and integral time $T_i$ proportionally. Increasing $C$ increases $T_i$ without affecting $K_c$ (purely integral action adjustment). $R_1$ is used for coarse gain setting.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — PD Controller Transfer Function

**Circuit Description:** Input through $R_1$ **in parallel with** $C$, feeding the inverting node. Feedback resistor $R_2$ alone.

**Step 1 — Input impedance (parallel combination):**

$$Z_i(s) = R_1 \parallel \frac{1}{C s} = \frac{R_1 \cdot (1/Cs)}{R_1 + 1/Cs} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 C s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Feedback impedance:**

$$Z_f(s) = R_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inverting amplifier equation:**

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{Z_i(s)} = -\frac{R_2 \cdot (R_1 C s + 1)}{R_1} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \left[ 1 + R_1 C s \right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the design parameters:**

$$K_c = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \quad \text{(Proportional Gain)}$$

$$T_d = R_1 \cdot C \quad \text{(Derivative Time, in seconds)}$$

**Step 5 — Time-domain expression:** Converting from $G_c(s) = -K_c(1 + T_d s)$ to time domain:

$$v_o(t) = -K_c \, v_i(t) - K_c T_d \frac{dv_i(t)}{dt}$$

The **negative sign** arises from the inverting configuration. Many industrial controllers add an inverting stage at the output to restore positive sign convention.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Ideal PID Controller

**Circuit:** Input network $R_1 \parallel C_1$ and feedback network $R_2 + C_2$ in series.

**Step 1 — Input impedance:**

$$Z_i(s) = \frac{R_1}{R_1 C_1 s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Feedback impedance:**

$$Z_f(s) = R_2 + \frac{1}{C_2 s} = \frac{R_2 C_2 s + 1}{C_2 s}$$

**Step 3 — Master transfer function:**

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{Z_i(s)} = -\frac{(R_2 C_2 s + 1)(R_1 C_1 s + 1)}{R_1 C_2 s}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the numerator:**

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 s^2 + (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2) s + 1}{R_1 C_2 s}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with ideal PID form** $G_c(s) = -K_c \dfrac{T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1}{T_i s}$:

By matching coefficients:

$$K_c = \frac{R_2 C_2}{T_i}, \quad T_i = R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2 - K_c T_d$$

For the **ideal (non-interacting) form** with $R_1 C_1 = R_2 C_2 = T_i = T_d$:

$$K_c = \frac{R_2}{R_1}, \quad T_i = R_1 C_1, \quad T_d = R_2 C_2$$

## 3.4 Numerical Problem — Step Response of PI Controller

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 style]:**
A PI controller has $K_c = 2$ and $T_i = 4$ min. The error signal is a step of magnitude $e_0 = 0.5$ (volts), applied at $t = 0$. Find the controller output $m(t)$ at $t = 6$ min, assuming initial output is zero.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the controller equation:**

$$m(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau \right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute step input** $e(t) = 0.5$ for $t \geq 0$:

$$m(t) = K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau = 2(0.5) + \frac{2}{4} \int_0^t 0.5 \, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$m(t) = 1 + 0.25 \times 0.5 t = 1 + 0.125 t$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $t = 6$ min:**

$$m(6) = 1 + 0.125 \times 6 = 1 + 0.75 = 1.75 \text{ (volts or engineering units)}$$

**Interpretation:** The output started at the **proportional jump** of 1.0 and rises linearly at 0.125/min due to integral action. If the error persists indefinitely, the controller output will eventually **saturate the actuator** (open valve fully), at which point **anti-windup** must activate.

## 3.5 Numerical Problem — PD Controller Ramp Response

**Problem:** A PD controller has $K_c = 1.5$ and $T_d = 2$ min. The error signal is a unit ramp: $e(t) = t$. Find the controller output.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the PD equation:**

$$m(t) = K_c e(t) + K_c T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the derivative:**

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$m(t) = 1.5 \cdot t + 1.5 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 1.5 t + 3.0$$

**Verification:** As $t \to \infty$, output tracks the ramp with offset **3.0** added by the D-action, while the P-action follows the ramp. D-action is anticipatory and **adds a constant offset** for a ramp input, useful for systems with transport delay.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Response of Each Mode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners frequently test this table.** Know the steady-state and transient behavior.

| Error Input $e(t)$ | P Output | I Output | D Output | PI Output | PD Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Step** $A$ | $K_c A$ (constant) | $\frac{K_c A}{T_i} t$ (ramp) | $K_c T_d A \, \delta(t)$ (impulse) | $K_c A + \frac{K_c A}{T_i} t$ | $K_c A + K_c T_d A \, \delta(t)$ |
| **Ramp** $At$ | $K_c A t$ (ramp) | $\frac{K_c A t^2}{2 T_i}$ (parabola) | $K_c T_d A$ (constant) | $K_c A t + \frac{K_c A t^2}{2 T_i}$ | $K_c A t + K_c T_d A$ |
| **Parabola** $At^2$ | $K_c A t^2$ | $\frac{K_c A t^3}{6 T_i}$ | $2 K_c T_d A t$ | — | — |

> **Key Insight:** D-action responds to **rate of change**; I-action responds to **accumulated past**; P-action responds to **instantaneous present**. PID combines all three — hence the acronym's enduring power.

These are summed, passed through a derivative noise filter, and clipped by the anti-windup circuit before driving the actuator.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q:** Define **Proportional Band (PB)**. How is it related to the proportional gain $K_c$? A temperature controller with a range of 0–200 °C and a proportional band of 25% requires the controller output to change over the full range. Find the gain $K_c$.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Proportional Band (PB)** is defined as the **percentage change in error (or process variable) required to cause the controller output to change over its full span (100%)**. It is the inverse of the proportional gain expressed as a percentage.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$PB (\%) = \frac{100}{K_c}$$

where $K_c$ is the dimensionless proportional gain. Equivalently, $K_c = \dfrac{100}{PB}$.

**Step 1 — Convert PB to decimal:**

$$PB = 25\% = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $K_c$:**

$$K_c = \frac{100}{PB(\%)} = \frac{100}{25} = 4$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** A 1 °C change in temperature (which is 1/200 = 0.5% of range) produces a controller output change of $4 \times 0.5 = 2\%$. To traverse the full 100% output, an error of $100/4 = 25\%$ is required, which corresponds to **50 °C**.

**Answer:** $K_c = 4$ (dimensionless).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:** What is **reset windup** in an electronic PI controller? State **two methods** to prevent it.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reset Windup (Integral Windup)** is a phenomenon in which the integral term of a PI or PID controller continues to accumulate (integrate) the error even when the actuator has reached its physical saturation limit (fully open or fully closed valve). The controller output then resides at the saturation limit with a growing internal integral state. When the error sign finally reverses, the controller takes an unacceptably long time to "unwind" the accumulated integral, causing **large overshoot** and possible instability.

**Two Methods to Prevent Reset Windup:**

1. **External Reset Feedback (Back-Calculation Method):** The difference between the controller's unsaturated output and the actual actuator position (saturated value) is fed back through a tracking gain $K_t$ to discharge the integrator. Mathematically, $\dfrac{de_i}{dt} = e(t) + K_t (m_{sat} - m_{calc})$.

2. **Integrator Clamping (Conditional Integration):** The integration is **frozen** (held constant) whenever the controller output reaches saturation limits, i.e., when $m(t) \geq m_{max}$ or $m(t) \leq m_{min}$. The integrator resumes only when the output is back within the active region.

*(Alternative acceptable method: Setpoint clamping — limit the setpoint to a value reachable by the actuator.)*

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module internal choice — attempt any **one** full question. Each question has sub-parts **(a) [7 marks]** and **(b) [7 marks]**. Full marks require circuit diagram + derivation + numerical or qualitative analysis.
