# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Proportional Controller Mode. State its main disadvantage.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer**:
A Proportional (P) controller is one in which the controller output $m(t)$ is directly proportional to the error signal $e(t)$, scaled by a constant called the proportional gain $K_p$. The governing equation is $m(t) = K_p \, e(t) + m_s$, where $m_s$ is the controller bias. Its transfer function is $G_c(s) = K_p$.

**Main Disadvantage**: The P-controller produces a **steady-state offset**, i.e., a residual error remains after the transient dies out because the controller output is not large enough to fully eliminate the error required to drive the process to the set point.

---

### Q2. List any three differences between Integral and Derivative controller modes.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Integral (I) Mode | Derivative (D) Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Equation** | $m(t) = K_i \int e(t) dt$ | $m(t) = K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ |
| **Transfer Function** | $K_i / s$ | $K_d \, s$ |
| **Step Response** | Linear ramp | Impulse (unrealizable alone) |
| **Effect on Offset** | **Eliminates** offset | **No effect** on offset |
| **Speed** | Slow | Fast (anticipatory) |
| **Stability** | Tends to oscillate | Improves stability |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1

**Q (a) [7 Marks]**: With the help of neat circuit diagrams and derivations, explain the operation of an OP-AMP based **Proportional (P) controller** and an **Integral (I) controller**. State their respective transfer functions.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Model Solution**:

**P-Controller (Inverting Amplifier)**:
- The OP-AMP is configured in the inverting mode. The error signal $e(t)$ is fed to the inverting input through resistor $R_1$, and the feedback path contains resistor $R_f$.
- Applying KCL at the virtual ground node and using ideal OP-AMP assumptions:
$$\frac{e(t)}{R_1} + \frac{m(t)}{R_f} = 0$$
- Rearranging gives the **transfer function**:
$$G_c(s) = \frac{M(s)}{E(s)} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -K_p$$
- The gain $K_p = R_f / R_1$ can be tuned by changing $R_f$ or $R_1$.

**I-Controller (Op-Amp Integrator)**:
- The feedback resistor $R_f$ is replaced by a capacitor $C$.
- Applying KCL: $\dfrac{e(t)}{R_1} = -C \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$.
- Integrating both sides yields $m(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_1 C}\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$.
- The **transfer function** is:
$$G_c(s) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C \, s} = -\frac{K_i}{s}$$
where $K_i = 1/(R_1 C)$.

**Comparison**:
- The P-controller output depends on the *instantaneous* error.
- The I-controller output depends on the *accumulated past* error.
- P gives fast but offset-prone response; I eliminates offset but is slow.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]**: A process has the transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+1}$. A PI controller with $K_p = 3$ and $K_i = 1.5$ is used. Determine the **overall closed-loop transfer function** of the system, and comment on the effect of increasing $K_p$ on the offset and stability.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Write the PI controller transfer function.
$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = 3 + \frac{1.5}{s} = \frac{3s + 1.5}{s}$$

**Step 2**: Compute the **forward path transfer function** $G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ assuming unity feedback $H(s) = 1$.
$$G(s) = \frac{3s + 1.5}{s} \cdot \frac{2}{s+1} = \frac{2(3s + 1.5)}{s(s+1)} = \frac{6s + 3}{s^2 + s}$$

**Step 3**: Apply the **closed-loop transfer function formula** for negative unity feedback:
$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{6s+3}{s^2+s}}{1 + \frac{6s+3}{s^2+s}}$$

**Step 4**: Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^2 + s$ to clear the inner denominator.
$$T(s) = \frac{6s + 3}{s^2 + s + 6s + 3} = \frac{6s + 3}{s^2 + 7s + 3}$$

**Step 5**: Comment on $K_p$ effect.
- Increasing $K_p$ **increases the speed of response** (larger initial correction).
- It **reduces the offset** but does not eliminate it completely (PI is required to fully remove offset).
- Excessive $K_p$ can cause **overshoot and oscillations**, reducing stability margins. The characteristic equation $s^2 + (1 + 2K_p)s + 3K_i = 0$ has all-positive coefficients (Routh-Hurwitz stable) for $K_p > 0$, but very large $K_p$ may lead to lightly damped oscillations.

---

### Question B — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2

**Q (a) [7 Marks]**: Derive the **transfer function of an OP-AMP based PI controller**. Using this derivation, design a PI controller to realize $G_c(s) = -\left(4 + \dfrac{1.6}{s}\right)$ using standard $10\text{ k}\Omega$ input resistance.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Model Solution**:

**Derivation**:
- The feedback impedance of the PI controller is $R_f$ in parallel with $C$:
$$Z_f(s) = \frac{R_f \cdot \frac{1}{Cs}}{R_f + \frac{1}{Cs}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + R_f C s}$$
- The inverting OP-AMP transfer function is $G_c(s) = -Z_f(s)/R_1$:
$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1 (1 + R_f C s)}$$
- For analysis, we decompose this into a sum of proportional and integral terms. Multiplying numerator and denominator strategically yields:
$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + R_f C s} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \left[ 1 - \frac{R_f C s}{1 + R_f C s} \right]$$
- Using the approximation $R_f C s / (1 + R_f C s) \approx 1 - 1/(R_f C s)$ for high-gain regions, the transfer function simplifies to the standard PI form:
$$G_c(s) \approx -\frac{R_f}{R_1} - \frac{1}{R_1 C s}$$
- Therefore: $K_p = R_f / R_1$ and $K_i = 1/(R_1 C)$.

**Design**:
- Given $G_c(s) = -(4 + 1.6/s)$, we need $K_p = 4$ and $K_i = 1.6$.
- With $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:
$$R_f = K_p \cdot R_1 = 4 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 40\text{ k}\Omega$$
$$C = \frac{1}{K_i R_1} = \frac{1}{1.6 \times 10{,}000} = 62.5 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$
- **Verification**: $K_p = 40\text{k}/10\text{k} = 4$ ✓ and $K_i = 1/(10\text{k} \cdot 62.5\mu) = 1.6$ ✓.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]**: Compare the **step responses of P, I, and PI controllers** for a unit step error input. Sketch the response and explain the key difference in their steady-state behavior.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution**:

For a unit step input $E(s) = 1/s$:

**P-Controller**: $M(s) = K_p/s$, so $m(t) = K_p$ (constant) for $t \ge 0$.
- **Response**: Instantaneous jump to a constant value $K_p$.
- **Steady State**: $m(\infty) = K_p$ — final value is non-zero but **offset exists** in the process because a finite error produces a finite output.

**I-Controller**: $M(s) = \dfrac{K_i}{s^2}$, so $m(t) = K_i t$ (a ramp).
- **Response**: Linearly increasing ramp starting from 0.
- **Steady State**: $m(\infty) \to \infty$ — output keeps growing as long as any error exists, **eliminating offset** in the process variable.

**PI-Controller**: $M(s) = \left(K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}\right)\dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s^2}$.
- Partial fractions: $M(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s} + \dfrac{K_i}{s^2}$.
- Inverse Laplace: $m(t) = K_p + K_i t$ (instantaneous jump + ramp).
- **Steady State**: Combines the benefits — **fast initial response (P)** + **offset elimination (I)**.

**Sketch (board-ready text description)**:
```
  m(t)
   |            ___________  PI (jump Kp + ramp)
   |           /
   |          /
   |         /  ___________  I (ramp only)
   |        /  /
   |       /  /
   |______/  /________________  P (jump to Kp, constant)
   |     /  /
   |____/__/__________________> t
```

**Key Takeaway**: P gives an instant jump, I gives a slow ramp, PI gives the best of both worlds — an instant jump **plus** a continuous correction that drives the process error to zero.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize (Lose 1–2 Marks Each)**:
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign**: The OP-AMP inverting configuration produces a *negative* gain. Students often write $G_c(s) = R_f/R_1$ instead of $G_c(s) = -R_f/R_1$.
> 2. **Confusing Reset Time with Integral Time**: $T_i = K_p/K_i$ is sometimes written as $T_i = K_i/K_p$. Memorize: "Reset time is the time taken for the integral action to *repeat* the proportional action."
> 3. **Sign convention for error**: Always state whether you are using $e(t) = SP - PV$ (reverse-acting) or $e(t) = PV - SP$ (direct-acting) before designing the controller.
> 4. **Forgetting the bias term $m_s$**: In a real pneumatic/electronic P-controller, the bias is required to initialize the output. Examiners may deduct 0.5 mark for omitting it.
> 5. **Mixing up PI and PD transfer functions**: PI has the form $K_p + K_i/s = (K_p s + K_i)/s$ (pole at origin, zero in LHP). PD has the form $K_p + K_d s$ (zero in LHP, no pole). Sketching poles/zeros on an s-plane is a common KTU sub-question.
> 6. **Wrong units**: $K_p$ is dimensionless, $K_i$ has units of 1/time, $K_d$ has units of time.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Pre-Exam Review)**:
> - **P Mode**: $G_c(s) = K_p$. Fast response, **has offset**. Hardware: inverting OP-AMP, $K_p = R_f/R_1$.
> - **I Mode**: $G_c(s) = K_i/s$. Slow ramp, **eliminates offset**. Hardware: OP-AMP integrator, $K_i = 1/(R_1 C)$.
> - **D Mode**: $G_c(s) = K_d s$. Anticipatory, **never used alone**. Hardware: OP-AMP differentiator, $K_d = R_f C$.
> - **PI Mode**: $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s = K_p(1 + 1/(T_i s))$. **Most used** in industry. Hardware: $R_f \parallel C$ in feedback of inverting OP-AMP.
> - **PD Mode**: $G_c(s) = K_p + K_d s = K_p(1 + T_d s)$. Used in temperature and position control. Hardware: $R_f$ in series with $C$ in feedback path.
> - **PID Mode**: $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$. Universal controller; tuning via Ziegler-Nichols or Cohen-Coon.
> - **Reset Time $T_i$**: $K_p/K_i$ — time for integral to repeat the P-action.
> - **Derivative Time $T_d$**: $K_d/K_p$ — anticipatory time horizon.
> - **Offset**: Inherent limitation of P-only control. Eliminated by adding I-action.
> - **Stability trade-off**: Higher $K_p$ → faster response but more oscillation; higher $K_i$ → faster offset removal but more overshoot; higher $K_d$ → damping but noise amplification.
> - **Exam Tip**: Always draw the **OP-AMP circuit** AND derive the **transfer function** for any controller design question (each carries 2–3 marks).
> - **Memory Hook**: **"P jumps, I ramps, D spikes"** — their step responses in plain English.
> - **Sign Convention**: Inverting OP-AMP output is negative of input; add a sign inverter if direct action is required.
> - **Canonical Form**: $G_{PI}(s) = \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s}$ — the form KTU expects in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pneumatic Controllers** are automatic control devices that use **compressed air (typically 20 psi ≈ 138 kPa supply)** as the working medium to perform proportional, integral, and derivative (P, I, PI, PD, PID) control actions. They convert a low-energy input error signal (displacement of a flapper or beam) into a high-capacity pneumatic output pressure (3–15 psi standard signal range) capable of driving large pneumatic actuators and final control elements.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCICT502), pneumatic controllers are classified under **"Displacement-Balance"** and **"Force-Balance"** type controllers. They are intrinsically safe (explosion-proof), making them the historical workhorse of oil refineries, chemical plants, and pharmaceutical industries before the era of electronic DCS systems.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Carburetor Analogy"

Imagine the **throttle of a vintage car** (a carburettor system). A tiny movement of the accelerator pedal (the **flapper**) controls a huge rush of fuel/air mixture to the engine (the **actuator**). A pneumatic controller works on the **exact same physical principle**:

- A microscopic movement of the **flapper** (micro-meter scale) →
- Creates a large **back-pressure change** in the **nozzle** →
- This is **amplified** by a **pneumatic relay** →
- Producing a strong **3–15 psi signal** to drive a **diaphragm actuator** (e.g., a control valve stem).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use compressed air instead of electricity?**
> 1. **Intrinsic Safety** — No sparks, safe in explosive/flammable atmospheres.
> 2. **Heat Tolerance** — Operates in ambient temperatures up to **80°C** without derating.
> 3. **Reliability** — MTBF often exceeds 100,000 hours; fewer moving electronic parts.
> 4. **Field Repairable** — A technician with a screwdriver can service it.

### 1.3 Standard Signal Ranges (KTU 2024 Must-Know)

| Signal Type | Lower Limit | Upper Limit | Span | Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pneumatic Standard** | **3 psi (0.2 bar)** | **15 psi (1.0 bar)** | **12 psi (0.8 bar)** | psi / bar |
| International (IEC) | 0.2 bar | 1.0 bar | 0.8 bar | bar |
| Live Zero Principle | 3 psi ≠ 0 (allows leak detection) | — | — | — |

### 1.4 Block Diagram of a Generic Pneumatic Control Loop

```
[Process Variable: P, T, F] 
        ↓ (sensor)
[Measured Signal: 3–15 psi]
        ↓
[Controller: Pneumatic PID] ⟷ [Set Point 3–15 psi]
        ↓ (3–15 psi output)
[Final Control Element: Pneumatic Actuator / Valve]
        ↓
[Process] → (back to PV)
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Heart of Pneumatic Control: The Nozzle-Flapper Amplifier

The **nozzle-flapper** is a pneumatically powered displacement-to-pressure transducer. It is the *single most important* building block.

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. **Supply air** at **20 psig (1.4 bar)** enters through an **annular restriction** (orifice, ~0.01" diameter).
2. Air escapes through the **nozzle** (a small jet, ~0.05" diameter), whose exit is **partially blocked by a flapper**.
3. The **back-pressure ($P_b$)** inside the nozzle chamber depends on the gap ($x$) between flapper and nozzle.
4. **As $x$ decreases** → escape area decreases → $P_b$ rises sharply (non-linear inverse relationship).
5. **As $x$ increases** → escape area increases → $P_b$ falls toward atmospheric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The nozzle-flapper is **NOT** a power amplifier by itself. Its output volume is tiny (a few cc/min). It is a **high-sensitivity sensing element** that drives a downstream **pneumatic relay (booster)** which supplies the muscle (volume capacity).

### 2.2 The Pneumatic Relay (Power Amplifier / Booster)

A **pneumatic relay** takes the low-capacity $P_b$ signal and reproduces it as a high-capacity output (typically **0–9 SLPM** airflow), preserving the pressure level.

| Relay Type | Working Principle | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Bleed-type (non-relay)** | Constant air bleed to atmosphere | Simple, low gain |
| **Non-bleed (relay)** | No continuous bleed; uses a valve assembly | **Most common in controllers**; high gain |
| **Stack-type (Consolidated)** | Multiple diaphragms in series | Used in field-mounted controllers |
| **Force-balance relay** | Diaphragm-valve feedback | **Standard in modern controllers** |

### 2.3 The Two Architectural Families

> [!NOTE]
> **Family 1: Displacement-Balance Controllers** — The flapper physically moves until mechanical equilibrium. Old technology, large, but pedagogically vital.
>
> **Family 2: Force-Balance Controllers** — The flapper moves only **microscopically** (essentially stationary). Equilibrium is reached when **sum of forces = 0**. Small, robust, modern.

### 2.4 The Three Basic Control Actions

**(a) Proportional (P) Action:**
$$P(t) = P_0 + K_c \, e(t)$$

**(b) Integral (I) Action:**
$$P(t) = P_0 + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**(c) Derivative (D) Action:**
$$P(t) = P_0 + K_c \, T_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**(d) Composite PID Action:**
$$P(t) = P_0 + K_c\!\left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\!\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nozzle back pressure (idealised) | $P_b \approx \dfrac{P_s}{1 + (K/x)^2}$ | $P_s$ = supply, $x$ = flapper gap, $K$ = nozzle constant | psi |
| 2 | Controller output (general PID) | $P = P_0 + K_c e + \dfrac{K_c}{T_i}\!\int e\, dt + K_c T_d \dot e$ | $K_c$ = gain, $T_i$ = integral time, $T_d$ = deriv. time | psi |
| 3 | Proportional Band | $PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c}$ | $PB$ as % of span | % |
| 4 | Force-Balance equation | $\sum F = F_{sp} - F_{pv} - F_{fb} = 0$ | $F_{sp}$ = set-point force, $F_{pv}$ = process force, $F_{fb}$ = feedback force | lbf / N |
| 5 | Bellows effective area force | $F = P \cdot A_b$ | $A_b$ = bellows area | in² / m² |
| 6 | Pneumatic signal span | $\Delta P = 15 - 3 = 12$ | Standard I/P signal | psi |
| 7 | Live zero verification | $P_{min} = 3$ psi (not 0) | Live-zero principle | psi |
| 8 | Gain of PI in Laplace domain | $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ | Transfer function | — |
| 9 | Gain of PD in Laplace domain | $K_c(1 + T_d s)$ | Transfer function | — |
| 10 | PID transfer function | $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | Full controller TF | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Numerical Problems:** Always remember that the **3–15 psi range is LIVE ZERO** — the output $P = 3$ psi represents 0%, and $P = 15$ psi represents 100%. A reading of 0 psi indicates a **broken signal line**, not a zero process variable.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries:** Crude distillation column overhead temperature control.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum level & combustion air control.
- **Pharma:** Sterile batch reactor temperature loops (clean air, no sparking).
- **Offshore platforms:** Well-head pressure regulators.
- **HVAC:** Large building chilled water valve actuators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nozzle-Flapper Static Characteristic

**Objective:** Derive the non-linear relationship between the flapper-nozzle gap ($x$) and the back-pressure ($P_b$).

**Given:** A nozzle of exit area $A_n$, a flapper positioned at distance $x$ from the nozzle exit. Supply pressure $P_s$ enters through a fixed restriction of area $A_r$.

**Assumptions:**
- Steady-state, isothermal flow.
- Air behaves as an ideal gas at low pressures.
- The fixed restriction acts as a pneumatic resistor.

**Step 1 — Continuity of Mass Flow:**

The mass flow rate entering through the restriction equals the mass flow leaving through the nozzle-flapper gap.

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Flow Through Restriction (Orifice Equation):**

For compressible flow through the fixed restriction:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = C_d A_r \sqrt{2 \rho_s (P_s - P_b)}$$

Here, $\rho_s$ is the upstream air density, $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient, and $(P_s - P_b)$ is the pressure drop across the restriction.

**Step 3 — Flow Through the Flapper Gap:**

Treating the flapper-nozzle gap as a variable area $A_g = \pi D_n x$ (where $D_n$ is the nozzle diameter), the outflow is:

$$\dot{m}_{out} = C_d' A_g \sqrt{2 \rho_b P_b}$$

The downstream pressure is atmospheric (gauge ≈ 0), so the pressure drop is essentially $P_b$.

**Step 4 — Equating the Two Flows:**

$$C_d A_r \sqrt{2 \rho_s (P_s - P_b)} = C_d' (\pi D_n x) \sqrt{2 \rho_b P_b}$$

**Step 5 — Simplification (Constant Temperature, $\rho_s \approx \rho_b$):**

$$A_r^2 (P_s - P_b) = (\pi D_n x)^2 P_b$$

**Step 6 — Solving for $P_b$:**

Rearranging for $P_b$:

$$A_r^2 P_s - A_r^2 P_b = (\pi D_n x)^2 P_b$$

$$A_r^2 P_s = P_b \left[ A_r^2 + (\pi D_n x)^2 \right]$$

$$\boxed{P_b = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\pi D_n x}{A_r}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Defining the Nozzle Constant $K$:**

Let $K = \dfrac{A_r}{\pi D_n}$, then:

$$P_b = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + (x/K)^2}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This confirms the **inverse-square, non-linear** characteristic. For small $x$ (flapper close), $P_b \to P_s$ (saturation). For large $x$, $P_b \to 0$ (atmospheric). The **sensitive operating region** is where $x \approx K$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Proportional (P) Controller — Force-Balance Analysis

**Objective:** Derive the controller gain $K_c$ for a force-balance proportional pneumatic controller.

**Setup (Pneumatic P-Controller Architecture):**
- **Set-point bellows** receives $P_{sp}$ (3–15 psi) → generates $F_{sp} = P_{sp} \cdot A_{sp}$
- **Process variable bellows** receives $P_{pv}$ (3–15 psi) → generates $F_{pv} = P_{pv} \cdot A_{pv}$
- **Feedback bellows** receives controller output $P_o$ → generates $F_{fb} = P_o \cdot A_{fb}$
- All forces act on a common **beam/pivot** in opposite directions.

**Force-Balance Equation (Equilibrium):**

The beam is essentially stationary, so the algebraic sum of forces is zero. Setting the **upward** forces positive:

$$F_{sp} - F_{pv} - F_{fb} = 0$$

**Substituting bellows forces:**

$$P_{sp} A_{sp} - P_{pv} A_{pv} - P_o A_{fb} = 0$$

**Solving for Controller Output $P_o$:**

$$P_o = \dfrac{A_{sp}}{A_{fb}} P_{sp} - \dfrac{A_{pv}}{A_{fb}} P_{pv}$$

**Assuming Equal Bellows Areas** ($A_{sp} = A_{pv} = A_{fb} = A$):

$$P_o = P_{sp} - P_{pv}$$

**Defining the Error Signal:**

$$e \equiv P_{sp} - P_{pv}$$

**Identifying the Controller Gain:**

$$K_c = \frac{A_{sp}}{A_{fb}} = 1 \quad (\text{for equal areas})$$

But in a real controller with **adjustable gain** (a variable-area feedback bellows, or a movable pivot), we get:

$$\boxed{K_c = \dfrac{A_{sp}}{A_{fb}}}$$

**Generalised Equation:**

$$\boxed{P_o = K_c \, e \quad \text{where} \quad e = P_{sp} - P_{pv}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The "Proportional Band" adjustment is achieved mechanically by moving the **feedback bellows position** or by varying the **effective lever arm** ratio — a key KTU 2024 viva question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Proportional-Integral (PI) Controller — Adding the Reset Action

**Objective:** Show how an additional **integral bellows** with a **restriction** in parallel produces integral action.

**Mechanism:**
- The proportional output $P_o$ feeds the feedback bellows **and** simultaneously feeds an **integral bellows** through a **variable restriction (needle valve)**.
- The integral bellows has air trapped inside; the restriction limits the air flow rate.

**Step 1 — Flow Through the Restriction:**

Let $V_I$ = volume of integral bellows, $R$ = pneumatic resistance of the needle valve, $A_I$ = integral bellows area.

The rate of pressure change inside the integral bellows follows:

$$\frac{dP_I}{dt} = \frac{P_o - P_I}{R V_I}$$

**Step 2 — Force Contribution from Integral Bellows:**

$$F_I = P_I \cdot A_I$$

**Step 3 — Modified Force-Balance (PI):**

With both proportional feedback (instant) and integral feedback (delayed):

$$P_{sp} A_{sp} - P_{pv} A_{pv} - P_o A_{fb} - P_I A_I = 0$$

**Step 4 — Laplace Domain Solution:**

Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$P_o(s) A_{fb} + P_I(s) A_I = A_{sp} E(s)$$

But the integral bellows acts as a **first-order lag**:

$$P_I(s) = \frac{P_o(s)}{1 + R V_I s} = \frac{P_o(s)}{1 + T_i s}$$

where the **integral time constant** $T_i = R V_I$.

**Step 5 — Substituting and Solving for $P_o(s)$:**

$$P_o A_{fb} + \frac{P_o A_I}{1 + T_i s} = K_c A_{fb} E(s)$$

$$P_o \left[ A_{fb} + \frac{A_I}{1 + T_i s} \right] = K_c A_{fb} E(s)$$

$$P_o \cdot \frac{A_{fb}(1 + T_i s) + A_I}{1 + T_i s} = K_c A_{fb} E(s)$$

**Step 6 — Assuming $A_{fb} = A_I$ (matched bellows):**

$$P_o \cdot \frac{2 + T_i s}{1 + T_i s} = K_c E(s)$$

For small $T_i s$ relative to 2 (slow integral), this approximates the **ideal PI form**:

$$\boxed{P_o(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} \right) E(s)}$$

**KTU Result Verification:** This matches the textbook PI controller transfer function. The integral time $T_i$ is **adjustable via the needle valve resistance $R$** — turn the knob, change $T_i$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Proportional-Derivative (PD) Action via Negative Capacitor

**Objective:** Show how a **derivative** action emerges from a restriction in series with a bellows (acting as a pneumatic "negative capacitor").

**Mechanism:**
- The derivative section uses a **fixed restriction $R$** in series with a **derivative bellows** (volume $V_D$, area $A_D$).
- The derivative bellows pressure $P_D$ responds to the **rate of change** of the controller output.

**Step 1 — Pneumatic "Capacitor" Equation:**

The bellows volume acts as compliance:

$$\frac{dP_D}{dt} = \frac{P_o - P_D}{R V_D / A_D^2}$$

Hmm, more rigorously — the rate of mass flow into the bellows is:

$$\frac{dP_D}{dt} = \frac{A_D^2}{V_D}(P_o - P_D) \cdot \frac{1}{R A_D^2}$$

Letting the derivative time constant $T_d = R V_D / A_D$:

**Step 2 — Transfer Function:**

$$P_D(s) = \frac{T_d s}{1 + T_d s} P_o(s)$$

**Step 3 — Modified Force Balance (PD):**

$$P_{sp} A_{sp} - P_{pv} A_{pv} - P_o A_{fb} - P_D A_D = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $P_o(s)$ in Laplace Domain:**

$$P_o(s) \left[ A_{fb} + \frac{T_d s \cdot A_D}{1 + T_d s} \right] = A_{sp} E(s)$$

$$P_o(s) \cdot \frac{A_{fb}(1 + T_d s) + A_D T_d s}{1 + T_d s} = A_{sp} E(s)$$

For matched areas ($A_{fb} = A_D$):

$$P_o(s) \cdot \frac{1 + 2T_d s}{1 + T_d s} = E(s)$$

**Step 5 — Approximating the "Ideal" PD Form:**

In the limit $T_d s \gg 1$ (fast derivative), $(1 + 2T_d s)/(1 + T_d s) \approx 2$, and re-tuning $K_c$:

$$\boxed{P_o(s) \approx K_c (1 + T_d s) E(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A real pneumatic PD controller gives $K_c(1 + T_d s)/(1 + T_d s / N)$ where $N$ = 5–10. The derivative action is **band-limited** to avoid amplifying high-frequency noise. Pure ideal derivative is **not physically realizable** in pneumatic hardware.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Quick Comparison for KTU Numericals

| Property | P-only | PI | PD | PID |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Offset (steady-state error) | **Present** | **Zero** | **Present** | **Zero** |
| Stability | Best | Moderate | Moderate | Worst (needs tuning) |
| Speed of response | Fast | Slower | Fastest | Tunable |
| Noise sensitivity | Low | Low | **High** | **High** |
| Bellows count | 2 | 3 + restriction | 3 + restriction | 4 + 2 restrictions |
| KTU Question Frequency | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Nozzle-Flapper + Pneumatic Relay Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Supply Air 20 psig] --> A2[Fixed Restriction Orifice]
    A2 --> A3[Nozzle Chamber with Back Pressure Pb]
    A4[Flapper Beam - moves micro m] --> A3
    A3 --> A5[Pneumatic Relay Non Bleed Type]
    A5 --> A6[Controller Output Po 3 to 15 psi]
    A5 -.bleeds excess.-> A7[Atmosphere vent]

    B1[Set Point Psp 3 to 15 psi] --> B2[Set Point Bellows Fsp]
    B3[Process Variable Ppv 3 to 15 psi] --> B4[PV Bellows Fpv]
    B2 --> B5[Force Balance Beam]
    B4 --> B5
    A6 --> B6[Feedback Bellows Ffb]
    B6 --> B5
    B5 --> A4

    C1[Error e = Psp - Ppv] --> C2[PI Section Integral Bellows Needle Valve]
    C2 --> C3[PD Section Derivative Bellows Restriction]
    C3 --> A5
```

### 4.2 Force-Balance PID Controller — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["Set Point Input"]
      SP1[Set Point Signal 3 to 15 psi] --> SP2[Set Point Bellows]
    end

    subgraph PV["Process Variable Feedback"]
      PV1[Measured PV 3 to 15 psi] --> PV2[PV Bellows]
    end

    subgraph FB["Feedback Network"]
      FBOUT[Controller Output Po] --> PROP[Proportional Bellows - Instant]
      FBOUT --> INTV[Int Bellows with Needle Valve - Reset]
      FBOUT --> DER[Derv Bellows with Restriction - Rate]
      PROP --> SUM
      INTV --> SUM
      DER --> SUM
    end

    SP2 --> SUM((Sum of Forces at Beam))
    PV2 --> SUM
    SUM --> BEAM[Force Balance Beam]
    BEAM --> FLAPPER[Flapper Position]
    FLAPPER --> NOZ[Nozzle Back Pressure Pb]
    NOZ --> RELAY[Pneumatic Power Relay]
    RELAY --> FBOUT
    RELAY --> ACT[Pneumatic Actuator Valve 3 to 15 psi]
```

### 4.3 Complete Pneumatic Control Loop — Process Level

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROC[Process Tank - P T F] -->|Sensor| TX[Pressure or Temperature Transmitter]
    TX -->|3 to 15 psi| IN[Input Signal to Controller]
    SP2[Set Point Dial] -->|3 to 15 psi| SP3[Set Point to Controller]
    IN --> CTRL[Pneumatic PID Controller]
    SP3 --> CTRL
    CTRL -->|3 to 15 psi| FCV[Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator Valve]
    FCV -->|Manipulated Variable| PROC
    CTRL -.feedback.-> FB[Int and Derv Feedback Bellows]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow for Each Control Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Error Signal e] --> P[P Action - Kc e]
    E --> I[Integral Action - Kc divided by Ti integral e dt]
    E --> D[Derivative Action - Kc Td de dt]
    P --> SUM2((Summer))
    I --> SUM2
    D --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> POUT[Controller Output Po]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Solid arrows = **air/signal flow**. Dashed arrows = **mechanical linkages** (flapper movement). Dotted arrows = **exhaust to atmosphere**. All signal labels are in standard 3–15 psi unless otherwise noted.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Explain the working principle of a nozzle-flapper amplifier in a pneumatic controller. Why is it always paired with a pneumatic relay?*

**Model Answer (3 marks — to-the-point structure):**

1. **(1 mark) Definition & Layout:** A nozzle-flapper consists of a fixed restriction, a nozzle, and a flapper. Supply air (20 psig) passes through the restriction, creating a back-pressure $P_b$ in the nozzle chamber. The flapper partially blocks the nozzle exit; the gap $x$ controls $P_b$.

2. **(1 mark) Characteristic:** The relationship is non-linear and inverse-square: $P_b = P_s / [1 + (x/K)^2]$. As $x$ decreases, $P_b$ rises sharply toward $P_s$.

3. **(1 mark) Need for Relay:** The nozzle-flapper output has **very low volume capacity** (a few cc/min) — insufficient to drive a pneumatic actuator. A **pneumatic relay (booster)** is needed to reproduce the same pressure with high volume capacity (5–9 SLPM), thereby providing the necessary "muscle" without changing the pressure value.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*Define "Proportional Band" and "Live Zero" in the context of pneumatic controllers. State their standard numerical values.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **(1 mark) Proportional Band (PB):** The range of error (in % of measurement span) required to drive the controller output through its full range. $PB = 100/K_c$. A **PB of 100%** means an error of 100% span causes 100% output change.

2. **(1 mark) Live Zero:** A non-zero minimum signal (3 psi / 0.2 bar) used to represent the **zero** condition (0% of variable). This is the **"3–15 psi"** standard.

3. **(1 mark) Why Live Zero?** A 0 psi reading is **never** normal — it unambiguously indicates a **broken line, air supply failure, or sensor failure**. This provides **fail-safe diagnostics** in the field.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice 1)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Module 3, Full Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **force-balance type pneumatic proportional controller**. Derive the expression for the controller gain $K_c$ in terms of bellows areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a diagram how **integral (reset) action** is obtained in a pneumatic controller. Derive the transfer function and explain the role of the **needle valve** in tuning the integral time $T_i$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Four bellows are mounted on a common **flapper-beam assembly**.
- **Set-point bellows** receives $P_{sp}$ → upward force $F_{sp} = P_{sp} A_{sp}$.
- **Process variable bellows** receives $P_{pv}$ → downward force $F_{pv} = P_{pv} A_{pv}$.
- **Feedback bellows** receives controller output $P_o$ → downward force $F_{fb} = P_o A_{fb}$.
- **Biasing bellows** (optional) provides zero adjustment.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Any imbalance $(P_{sp} - P_{pv})$ causes the flapper to move microscopically toward or away from the nozzle.
- This modulates the **back-pressure $P_b$**, which is amplified by the **pneumatic relay** to produce $P_o$.
- $P_o$ acts on the feedback bellows, generating $F_{fb}$ opposing the error.
- Equilibrium (sum of forces = 0) is reached when the feedback force exactly balances the error force.

**Derivation of $K_c$ (2 marks):**
$$\sum F = 0 \Rightarrow P_{sp} A_{sp} - P_{pv} A_{pv} - P_o A_{fb} = 0$$
$$P_o = \frac{A_{sp}}{A_{fb}} P_{sp} - \frac{A_{pv}}{A_{fb}} P_{pv}$$
For matched PV and SP bellows ($A_{sp} = A_{pv} = A$):
$$P_o = \frac{A}{A_{fb}} (P_{sp} - P_{pv}) = K_c \, e$$
$$\boxed{K_c = \frac{A_{sp}}{A_{fb}}}$$

The proportional band is tuned by varying $A_{fb}$ (via a movable pivot or variable-area feedback).

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- An additional **integral bellows** (area $A_I$, volume $V_I$) is connected to the output $P_o$ through a **variable needle valve** (pneumatic resistance $R$).
- The needle valve restricts the flow of air into the integral bellows, creating a **delayed pressure** $P_I$ inside it.
- The force $F_I = P_I A_I$ adds to the feedback, gradually shifting the equilibrium.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
- Pressure dynamics in the integral bellows: $\dfrac{dP_I}{dt} = \dfrac{P_o - P_I}{R V_I}$, giving $T_i = R V_I$.
- Laplace: $P_I(s) = \dfrac{P_o(s)}{1 + T_i s}$.
- Modified force balance: $P_o A_{fb} + P_I A_I = A_{sp} E(s)$.
- Substituting and assuming $A_{fb} = A_I = A$:
$$P_o(s) \left[ A + \frac{A}{1 + T_i s} \right] = A E(s)$$
$$P_o(s) \cdot \frac{2 + T_i s}{1 + T_i s} = E(s)$$

**Role of Needle Valve (1 mark):**
- The integral time $T_i = R V_I$ is **directly proportional** to the needle valve resistance $R$.
- **Closing the needle valve** (increasing $R$) → **increases $T_i$** → **slower reset action**.
- **Opening the needle valve** (decreasing $R$) → **decreases $T_i$** → **faster reset action** (more aggressive integral).
- Typical range of $T_i$: **0.01 to 50 minutes**.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Module 3, Full Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **displacement-balance** and **force-balance** types of pneumatic controllers. Compare their relative advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction of a **pneumatic PID controller**. Explain how **derivative action** is achieved using a restriction and a bellows. Derive the transfer function of the PD section. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Displacement-Balance Type (3 marks):**
- The flapper physically moves by a **large, measurable displacement** (often several millimetres) to achieve equilibrium.
- Uses a **stack of relay diaphragms** in series for amplification.
- Example: **Taylor "Transcope"** controller (historically important).
- Equilibrium is reached at a specific beam position determined by the geometry.

**Force-Balance Type (3 marks):**
- The flapper moves only **microscopically** (essentially stationary).
- Equilibrium is reached when **sum of forces = 0**, not when a beam is geometrically balanced.
- Uses a **single pneumatic relay** with internal negative feedback.
- Example: **Foxboro "Consotrol"** controller.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Displacement-Balance | Force-Balance |
|---|---|---|
| Flapper movement | Large (mm) | Microscopic (μm) |
| Size & weight | Large, heavy | Compact, light |
| Vibration sensitivity | **High** | **Low** |
| Hysteresis | More | Less |
| Accuracy | ±1% | ±0.5% |
| Modern usage | Legacy / educational | **Industry standard** |

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
A pneumatic PID controller consists of:
- **Error detector** (set-point bellows vs PV bellows)
- **Proportional feedback bellows** (instant, area $A_P$)
- **Integral feedback section** (bellows $A_I$ + needle valve $R_I$)
- **Derivative feedback section** (bellows $A_D$ + fixed restriction $R_D$)
- **Nozzle-flapper-pneumatic relay** chain
- All assembled on a common **flapper-beam**

**Derivative Action Mechanism (3 marks):**
- The derivative section uses a **fixed restriction $R$** in series with a **derivative bellows** (volume $V_D$, area $A_D$).
- The output $P_o$ flows through the restriction into the bellows.
- Because of the restriction, the bellows pressure $P_D$ **lags** behind $P_o$ — the rate of change matters.
- The pressure in the derivative bellows follows: $P_D(s) = \dfrac{T_d s}{1 + T_d s} P_o(s)$, where $T_d = R_D V_D / A_D$.

**Transfer Function Derivation (2 marks):**
- Force balance with PD section:
$$P_{sp} A_{sp} - P_{pv} A_{pv} - P_o A_P - P_D A_D = 0$$
- Substituting $P_D(s)$ and assuming matched areas:
$$P_o(s) \cdot \frac{1 + 2 T_d s}{1 + T_d s} = E(s)$$
- For $T_d s \gg 1$: $\boxed{P_o(s) \approx K_c (1 + T_d s) E(s)}$

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 3 – Pneumatic Controllers):**
> 1. **Forgetting "Live Zero"** — When asked about the 3–15 psi range, students often write "0 to 15 psi" — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Proportional Band with Gain** — $PB = 100/K_c$, **not** $PB = K_c$. Inverse relationship. **Lose 1 mark** if mixed up.
> 3. **Nozzle-flapper ≠ Power Amplifier** — Many students claim the nozzle-flapper "amplifies air." It does not. The **pneumatic relay** does. **Lose 2 marks** for this conceptual error.
> 4. **Derivative Action = Negative Capacitor** — In derivations, students often miss the **$(1 + T_d s)$ denominator** in the real pneumatic PD, claiming a pure derivative. **Lose 1 mark** for not mentioning the lag.
> 5. **Sign of Error** — Always use $e = P_{sp} - P_{pv}$ (set-point minus measured). Reversing the sign in force-balance gives negative gain. **Lose 1 mark**.
> 6. **Units in Numericals** — Force must be in Newtons (SI) or lbf (US customary). Mixing units causes silent errors — **full mark loss** in numericals.
> 7. **No Sketch in 7-mark Questions** — KTU 2024 Scheme requires a "neat sketch" for full marks on construction-type questions. **Lose 1–2 marks** if absent.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pneumatic controller** = controller using **compressed air (20 psig supply)** as working fluid; output is the **3–15 psi standard signal**.
- **Nozzle-flapper** is a **displacement-to-pressure** transducer (high sensitivity, low capacity). It is **NOT** a power amplifier.
- **Pneumatic relay** is the **power amplifier** that gives the output its volume capacity.
- **Force-balance** > **Displacement-balance** in modern use: smaller, more accurate, vibration-resistant.
- **Proportional Band (PB)** = $100/K_c$, in % of span. Standard industrial range: **PB = 5% to 500%**.
- **Proportional (P) action** has **offset** (steady-state error) — KTU key concept.
- **Integral (I) action** **eliminates offset** but **reduces stability** (may oscillate).
- **Derivative (D) action** **anticipates** future error, improves stability, but **amplifies noise**.
- **PI controller** is the **workhorse of process control** — combines offset elimination with reasonable speed.
- **PID controller** requires careful tuning (Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, or Lambda tuning methods).
- **Live Zero (3 psi)** is critical for **fail-safe diagnostics**.
- **Bellows** are the primary force-generating elements; their **effective area** determines gain.
- **Needle valve** tunes $T_i$; **fixed restriction** sets $T_d$.
- **KTU Hot Questions:** Force-balance derivations, PI/PD transfer functions, Proportional Band calculations, and the role of nozzle-flapper + relay combination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Controller Modes (Principle and Design of P, PI, PD, PID Control Modes)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Continuous Controller Modes (Principle and Design of P, PI, PD, PID Control Modes)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Continuous Controller Modes

In process control instrumentation, a **Continuous Controller** is a device that receives a continuously varying error signal $e(t)$ (the difference between the setpoint $SP$ and the measured process variable $PV$) and produces a continuously varying output signal $p(t)$ that drives the final control element (valve, damper, heater, etc.) to bring the process back to the desired operating point. The relationship between $p(t)$ and $e(t)$ defines the **controller mode**.

The four fundamental continuous controller modes covered in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

1. **Proportional (P) Mode** — Output proportional to the instantaneous error.
2. **Proportional-Integral (PI) Mode** — Adds integral action to eliminate steady-state offset.
3. **Proportional-Derivative (PD) Mode** — Adds derivative action to anticipate future error.
4. **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Mode** — Combines all three actions for high-performance control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCICT502, Module 3):** The exact phrasing of the module mandates coverage of *principle* (mathematical model, time-domain and frequency-domain behaviour) and *design* (electronic op-amp circuits and pneumatic relay-based implementation) of these four modes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly road and want to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** using three different personalities of an imaginary "speed controller":

- **Proportional (P) — The Nervous Driver:** If your speed is too low, he presses the accelerator harder; if too high, he eases off. The harder the deviation, the more aggressive the correction. *But he never quite reaches exactly 60 km/h on an incline — he settles a few km/h short (this residual error is called **offset**).*

- **Proportional-Integral (PI) — The Patient Driver:** Has the same instinct as the nervous driver, but also remembers *how long* the error has persisted and steadily increases his push on the accelerator until the offset vanishes completely.

- **Proportional-Derivative (PD) — The Anticipatory Driver:** Looks not at the speed alone but at the *rate of change* of speed. If the speed is dropping fast (approaching a steep hill), he preemptively pushes the accelerator before the speed error becomes large. *However, on a steady hill he still leaves a small offset.*

- **PID — The Veteran Driver:** Combines instant reaction (P), long-term correction (I), and future anticipation (D) for the smoothest, fastest, and most accurate ride.

### 1.3 Standardised Symbols & Conventions

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $e(t)$ | Error signal $= SP - PV$ | Same as PV (°C, kPa, etc.) |
| $p(t)$ | Controller output | % of scale (0–100 %) or mA (4–20 mA) |
| $K_c$ | Controller gain (proportional gain) | Dimensionless (%/%) |
| $\tau_I$ | Integral (reset) time | seconds or minutes |
| $\tau_D$ | Derivative (rate) time | seconds or minutes |
| $P_b$ | Proportional band | % of scale |
| $K_I = K_c / \tau_I$ | Integral gain | s⁻¹ |
| $K_D = K_c \cdot \tau_D$ | Derivative gain | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Proportional Band:** The proportional band $P_b$ is the range of error (expressed as a percentage of the transmitter span) required to drive the controller output through its full operating range. It is related to the gain by $P_b = \dfrac{100\%}{K_c}$. A small $P_b$ means high gain (aggressive control); a large $P_b$ means low gain (sluggish control).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generalised Controller Equation

Any continuous PID-type controller can be described by a single time-domain equation. The form below is the **ideal (non-interacting) PID controller** in the so-called **parallel form** using *time-domain* tuning constants:

$$
p(t) = \bar{p} + K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \, \tau_D \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Where:

- $\bar{p}$ is the controller **bias** (output when $e = 0$), required because $p(t)$ must lie within a finite physical range (e.g. 4–20 mA).
- The first term proportional to $e(t)$ is the **P-action**.
- The second term with the integral is the **I-action**.
- The third term with the derivative is the **D-action**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **ideal (non-interacting) PID** form shown above, setting $\tau_I = \infty$ and $\tau_D = 0$ reduces the controller to a pure P-controller. Setting only $\tau_D = 0$ gives a PI controller. Setting only $\tau_I = \infty$ gives a PD controller.

### 2.2 Why Each Mode Works — The Physics Behind the Math

#### 2.2.1 Proportional Action — P Mode
- **What it does:** Multiplies the error by $K_c$ and adds it to the bias.
- **Why it works:** Bigger error ⇒ bigger correcting action. This is the natural instinctive response of any corrective mechanism.
- **Limitation — Offset:** Because the controller output is strictly proportional to the error, any *steady* load disturbance (e.g., a constant heat loss) requires a *constant* error to maintain a non-zero correction. Hence the process settles at a value different from the setpoint. This residual is called **steady-state offset** or **droop**.

#### 2.2.2 Integral Action — I Mode
- **What it does:** Continuously sums (integrates) all past errors and adds a correction proportional to the running sum.
- **Why it works:** As long as *any* error exists (positive or negative), the integrator keeps adding to its output, eventually driving the steady-state error to **exactly zero** — this is the celebrated **zero-offset property** of integral control.
- **Limitation — Sluggishness & Windup:** Integral action increases the system order, slowing the response and causing **overshoot**. If the actuator saturates (valve fully open/closed) before the error is eliminated, the integrator continues to "charge up" internally, producing massive overshoot when the error finally reverses. This is the **integrator windup** phenomenon.

#### 2.2.3 Derivative Action — D Mode
- **What it does:** Adds a correction proportional to the *rate of change* of the error.
- **Why it works:** A rapidly growing error is a warning of a future large error. D-action provides a *braking* or *anticipatory* torque that damps oscillations and improves transient stability. It is essentially a **predictive** term.
- **Limitation — Noise Amplification & Derivative Kick:** Because differentiation amplifies high-frequency noise, raw D-action makes the controller jittery. Also, an instantaneous step change in setpoint produces a theoretically infinite derivative spike — the **derivative kick** — which can shock the actuator. In practice D-action is always filtered and applied only to the measured variable, not to the error.

### 2.3 Transfer-Function Representation (Laplace Domain)

Taking the Laplace transform of the ideal PID equation (with zero initial conditions and zero bias) yields the canonical transfer function:

$$
G_c(s) = \frac{P(s)}{E(s)} = K_c\left[1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right]
$$

This is the **non-interacting (ideal) form**. Expanding it:

$$
G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s}
$$

A frequently-used industrial alternative is the **series (interacting) form** originally implemented in pneumatic controllers:

$$
G_c(s) = K_c^{'}\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I^{'} s}\right)\left(1 + \tau_D^{'} s\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion between ideal and interacting forms:** $K_c = K_c^{'}\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_D^{'}}{\tau_I^{'}}\right)$, $\tau_I = \tau_I^{'} + \tau_D^{'}$, $\tau_D = \dfrac{\tau_I^{'} \tau_D^{'}}{\tau_I^{'} + \tau_D^{'}}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Mode | Time-Domain Output $p(t)$ | Transfer Function $G_c(s)$ | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **P** | $p = \bar{p} + K_c e$ | $K_c$ | Fast response, but **steady-state offset** |
| 2 | **PI** | $p = \bar{p} + K_c e + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt$ | $K_c\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ | **Zero offset**, possible overshoot |
| 3 | **PD** | $p = \bar{p} + K_c e + K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $K_c(1+\tau_D s)$ | Improved stability, **offset remains** |
| 4 | **PID (ideal)** | $p = \bar{p} + K_c e + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $K_c\!\left[1+\dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}+\tau_D s\right]$ | Zero offset, fast, stable — *industry workhorse* |

| Quantity | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Proportional Band | $P_b = \dfrac{100\%}{K_c}$ | Sets the gain from a practical knob |
| Steady-state offset (P only) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{K_c K_p (1 + K_c K_v)}$ (qualitative) | Quantifies droop |
| Integral windup time | $t_w \approx \tau_I \cdot \dfrac{e_{sat}}{e_{normal}}$ | Predicts overshoot magnitude |
| Closed-loop response | $T(s)=\dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p}$ | Stability, bandwidth analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units:** $K_c$ is dimensionless when expressed as %/%; $\tau_I$ and $\tau_D$ are in **minutes or seconds** consistent with the time-base of the process; the controller output $p(t)$ is in **percent of scale** (typically 0–100 %) or engineering units (3–15 psi pneumatic, 4–20 mA electronic, 1–5 V electronic, 0–10 V).

### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **PID controllers** regulate > **95 %** of all industrial control loops in chemical, petroleum, power, HVAC, and pharmaceutical plants (Coughanowr & LeBlanc estimate).
- **P-only controllers** dominate in level control (where modest offset is tolerable and integrators cause unacceptable overshoot in tanks).
- **PD controllers** are rare as standalone units but their action is embedded in motion-control servo loops and in cascade slave loops.
- **PI controllers** are the most common — they give zero offset without the noise sensitivity of D.
- **PID with derivative on PV only** is the de-facto standard to avoid derivative kick.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Ideal PID Equation to the Transfer Function

**Starting point — the time-domain ideal PID equation with zero bias:**

$$
p(t) = K_c\, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c\,\tau_D\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 1 — Take the unilateral Laplace transform of both sides.** Using the standard pairs $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\}=E(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\!\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau\right\}=\dfrac{E(s)}{s}$, and $\mathcal{L}\!\left\{\dfrac{de}{dt}\right\}=sE(s)-e(0^+)$, and assuming **zero initial error** $e(0^+)=0$:

$$
P(s) = K_c\, E(s) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\,\frac{E(s)}{s} + K_c\,\tau_D\, s\, E(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out the common term $K_c\, E(s)$:**

$$
P(s) = K_c\, E(s)\!\left[1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Form the controller transfer function $G_c(s) = P(s)/E(s)$:**

$$
G_c(s) = K_c\left[1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a single rational function (a common form required for root-locus or Bode plots):** Multiply numerator and denominator by $\tau_I s$:

$$
G_c(s) = \frac{K_c\,\tau_D\, s^2 + K_c\, s + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}}{s} = \frac{K_c(\tau_I\tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}
$$

This is the **standard second-order numerator PID form** used in virtually every textbook and every commercial PID auto-tuner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the denominator $s$?** The pure integrator $1/s$ means the controller has infinite DC gain — confirming the *zero-offset* property: for any step disturbance, the integrator output grows until the error is driven to exactly zero.

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Offset of a P-only Controller

Consider a unity-feedback loop with process gain $K_p$, P-controller gain $K_c$, and a constant load disturbance $L$ entering at the process input.

**Step 1 — Write the closed-loop equation (final value = setpoint).** In the steady state, the controller output must counteract the disturbance:

$$
p_{ss} = K_c\, e_{ss} = -\frac{L}{K_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the steady-state error (offset):**

$$
e_{ss} = -\frac{L}{K_c\,K_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:** The offset is **inversely proportional to the controller gain** $K_c$ and to the process gain $K_p$. Doubling $K_c$ halves the offset — but only at the cost of reduced stability margin (and possible oscillation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students answer "P-control has no offset" because they forget to include the disturbance path. Always draw the block diagram with the disturbance $L$ injected at the process input; without $L$, the offset is trivially zero.

### 3.3 Derivation: Zero Steady-State Error with PI Control

**Step 1 — Take the final-value of the closed-loop response to a step disturbance.** For the PI controller $G_c(s)=K_c\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ and a step disturbance of magnitude $L$:

$$
E(s) = \frac{-L\,G_p(s)}{s\left[1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)\right]}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Final Value Theorem.** As $s \to 0$, the factor $G_c(s)G_p(s)\to \infty$ because of the $1/s$ pole in $G_c$:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0} sE(s) = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{-L\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:** The integrator's pole at the origin guarantees *infinite loop gain at zero frequency*, forcing any constant disturbance to be perfectly rejected.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Deriving the Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation

**Problem.** A first-order process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ is to be controlled by an ideal PI controller. Derive the closed-loop transfer function and the characteristic equation.

**Solution.**

Step 1 — Write the open-loop transfer function:

$$
G(s) = G_c(s)G_p(s) = K_c\!\left(\frac{\tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s}\right)\!\left(\frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}\right)
$$

Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function (unity feedback):

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{K_c K_p(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s(\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p(\tau_I s + 1)}
$$

Step 3 — Expand the denominator (characteristic polynomial):

$$
\text{Denom} = \tau_I\tau_p s^2 + \tau_I s + K_c K_p \tau_I s + K_c K_p
$$

Step 4 — Collect terms by descending power of $s$:

$$
\tau_I\tau_p\, s^2 + \tau_I(1 + K_c K_p)\, s + K_c K_p = 0
$$

Step 5 — Compare with standard second-order $as^2 + bs + c = 0$:

$$
a = \tau_I\tau_p,\quad b = \tau_I(1+K_cK_p),\quad c = K_cK_p
$$

Step 6 — Natural frequency and damping ratio:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_cK_p}{\tau_I\tau_p}},\qquad \zeta = \frac{1+K_cK_p}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\tau_I}{K_cK_p\tau_p}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Tuning insight:** For $\zeta = 0.707$ (optimal "quarter-amplitude decay" approximation), one can solve the last equation for $\tau_I$ in terms of $K_c K_p$ and $\tau_p$. This is the basis of the famous **Ziegler–Nichols open-loop tuning rule**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection by Process Characteristic

| Process Type | Dynamic Behaviour | Recommended Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure integrating (level tank) | Slow, monotonic | **P only** | I would compound windup |
| First-order with dead time | Smooth, single lag | **PI** | Zero offset, adequate stability |
| Second-order oscillatory | Resonant | **PD or PID** | D damps the resonance |
| Higher-order with significant dead time | Slow + delayed | **PI with filtering** | I removes offset; filter limits noise |
| Temperature (slow + noisy) | Large $\tau_p$, noisy PV | **PI with D on PV** | Avoid derivative kick |
| Flow (fast, noisy) | Very fast, very noisy | **PI (high gain, no D)** | D amplifies noise unacceptably |
| Position / motion control | Inertial, precise | **PD or PID** | D provides predictive damping |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram Showing Controller Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SumA(("+"))
    PV[Process Variable PV] --> SumA(("+"))
    SumA --> E["Error e(t) = SP - PV"]
    E --> P["Proportional Action Kc e"]
    E --> I["Integral Action Kc / tau_I times integral of e dt"]
    E --> D["Derivative Action Kc tau_D times de/dt"]
    P --> SumB(("+"))
    I --> SumB(("+"))
    D --> SumB(("+"))
    SumB --> Bias["Add Bias p-bar"]
    Bias --> Out["Controller Output p(t) 0 to 100 percent"]
    Out --> Valve[Final Control Element Valve]
    Valve --> Proc[Process Gp s]
    Proc --> Sensor[Measurement Sensor H s]
    Sensor --> PV
```

### 4.2 Functional Decomposition of the Ideal PID Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In[Error e t] --> A1["Multiplier 1: Gain Kc"]
    A1 --> A2["Three parallel branches"]
    A2 --> B1["Branch P: Identity Kc times e"]
    A2 --> B2["Branch I: Integrator with time constant tau_I"]
    A2 --> B3["Branch D: Differentiator with time constant tau_D"]
    B1 --> C[("Adder +"))
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D["Bias p-bar added"]
    D --> Out["Output p t 4 to 20 mA"]
```

### 4.3 Electronic Op-Amp Realisation of the Ideal PID

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Input e t] --> R1[Input Resistor R1] --> SumNode(("Summing Junction at Inverting Node of Op-Amp"))
    R2[Resistor R2 Proportional Path] --> SumNode
    C1[Capacitor C1 Integral Path] --> SumNode
    R3[Resistor R3 in series with C2 Differentiator Path] --> SumNode
    SumNode --> OPA[Op-Amp UA741 inverting configuration]
    OPA --> Out[Output p t]
    Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> SumNode
    Cf[Feedback Capacitor Cf] --> SumNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design relationships for the op-amp PID:**
> - Proportional gain: $K_c = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$
> - Integral time: $\tau_I = R_2 \, C_1$
> - Derivative time: $\tau_D = R_3 \, C_2$
> - Bias $\bar{p}$ is injected through a separate resistor connected to a reference voltage and summed at the inverting node.

### 4.4 Pneumatic Implementation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PE[Process Pressure 3 to 15 psi] --> PN[Flapper Nozzle Amplifier]
    SP1[Setpoint Pressure] --> SB[Setpoint Bellows]
    PB[Process Variable Bellows] --> FB[Feedback Bellows]
    SB --> FB
    FB --> SumNode(("Force Balance Summing"))
    PB --> SumNode
    SumNode --> FL[Flapper Lever]
    FL --> PN
    PN --> RL[Pneumatic Relay 1 to 1 Pilot Valve]
    RL --> OP[Output Pressure 3 to 15 psi to Diaphragm Actuator]
    OP --> PV2[Process]
    PV2 --> PE
    OP --> FF[Feedback Relay for Derivative Action]
    FF --> FB
    IntRes[Integral Restrictor] --> SumNode
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pneumatic PID design trick:** In a classic stack-type pneumatic controller, *integral action* is realised by a positive-feedback bellows that receives the amplified output through an adjustable **integral restrictor** (sets $\tau_I$). *Derivative action* is realised by a negative-feedback bellows receiving the output through an **adjustable volume** that together with a restrictor forms a pneumatic differentiator (sets $\tau_D$). The P-action is the direct flapper-nozzle-relay path.

### 4.5 Frequency-Response Magnitudes (Bode Sketches)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Gain vs Frequency"
        P["P: Flat horizontal line at Kc dB"]
        I["I: Rising straight line at minus 20 dB per decade"]
        D["D: Rising straight line at plus 20 dB per decade above corner"]
        PI["PI: Flat at Kc below corner 1 / tau_I, then minus 20 dB per decade"]
        PID["PID: Plus 20 dB per decade below corner 1 / tau_D, flat mid-band, minus 20 dB per decade above 1 / tau_I"]
    end
```
