# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Define controller tuning. Why is it necessary in process industries?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Controller tuning is the procedure of determining the optimal values of the adjustable parameters of a feedback controller ($K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$) such that the closed-loop response satisfies a defined performance criterion such as minimum Integral of Absolute Error (IAE), quarter-amplitude decay ratio, or minimum overshoot.

> **Necessity (2 marks):** Every process has unique dynamic characteristics (gain, time constant, dead time) determined by its physical design, fluid properties, and operating conditions. A controller that works well on one loop may perform poorly on another. Tuning is necessary to:
> - Match the controller dynamics to the process dynamics.
> - Achieve stability and the desired speed of response.
> - Eliminate steady-state error (with integral action).
> - Minimise overshoot and oscillations (with derivative action).
> - Reduce energy consumption, wear on actuators, and product quality deviations.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: List the FOPDT model parameters and explain how they are obtained from a Process Reaction Curve (PRC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Parameters (1 mark):** The First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) model has three parameters: **Process Gain $K_p$**, **Dead Time $L$**, and **Time Constant $\tau$**.

> **Obtainment from PRC (2 marks):**
> - **Step test:** A step change $\Delta M$ is applied in manual mode and the PV response is recorded.
> - **Dead time $L$:** The tangent drawn at the inflection point of the S-shaped PRC intersects the *initial value* line at $t = L$.
> - **Time constant $\tau$:** The same tangent intersects the *final steady-state* line at $t = L + \tau$; therefore $\tau$ = (intersection time) $- L$.
> - **Process Gain $K_p$:** $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta PV_{\text{ss}}}{\Delta M}$, the ratio of the steady-state output change to the input step size.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### 🔵 Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: A temperature control loop on a chemical reactor was tuned using the Ziegler–Nichols open-loop method. A step change of 8 % in the steam valve opening produced a process reaction curve from which the following were identified: $K_p = 2.5$ °C/%, $L = 3$ s, $\tau = 9$ s.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine the **PID controller settings** using Ziegler–Nichols open-loop correlations. Express the controller in the standard parallel form.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the same process had been tuned by the **Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop (ultimate cycle) method** with $K_u = 6.0$ %/°C and $P_u = 36$ s, compute the corresponding PI and PID settings, and explain *why* the two methods give different values of $K_c$.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Z-N Open-Loop PID [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Recall Z-N Open-Loop PID correlations (1 mark):**

$$K_c = \frac{1.2\,\tau}{K_p\,L}, \qquad T_i = 2L, \qquad T_d = 0.5L$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c &= \frac{1.2 \times 9}{2.5 \times 3} = \frac{10.8}{7.5} = 1.44 \; \frac{\%}{^{\circ}\text{C}} \\[6pt]
T_i &= 2 \times 3 = 6 \; \text{s} \\[6pt]
T_d &= 0.5 \times 3 = 1.5 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Express in parallel form (2 marks):**

The ideal parallel PID is $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right)$. Substituting:

$$G_c(s) = 1.44 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{6s} + 1.5\,s \right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
G_c(s) &= 1.44 + \frac{0.24}{s} + 2.16\,s
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verification and commentary (2 marks):**

- The proportional band $PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c} = \dfrac{100}{1.44} \approx 69.4$ %.
- This tuning is designed for a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio** (≈ 25 % overshoot) — appropriate for a *regulatory* temperature loop, not a *servo* one.
- A safety factor of 0.8–0.9 should be applied to $K_c$ if the process is sensitive to overshoot.

---

#### Part (b) — Z-N Closed-Loop (PI and PID) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Z-N Closed-Loop Correlations (1 mark):**

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| PI  | $0.45\,K_u$ | $P_u/1.2$ | — |
| PID | $0.6\,K_u$  | $P_u/2$   | $P_u/8$ |

**Step 2 — PI Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c^{\text{PI}} &= 0.45 \times 6.0 = 2.7 \; \frac{\%}{^{\circ}\text{C}} \\[6pt]
T_i^{\text{PI}} &= \frac{36}{1.2} = 30 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — PID Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c^{\text{PID}} &= 0.6 \times 6.0 = 3.6 \; \frac{\%}{^{\circ}\text{C}} \\[6pt]
T_i^{\text{PID}} &= \frac{36}{2} = 18 \; \text{s} \\[6pt]
T_d^{\text{PID}} &= \frac{36}{8} = 4.5 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Why the two methods differ (3 marks):**

- **Different information sources:** Open-loop uses $K_p$, $L$, $\tau$ from a step test; closed-loop uses $K_u$ and $P_u$ from an oscillation test. The two tests excite different process modes.
- **Different model assumptions:** Z-N open-loop assumes FOPDT behaviour; Z-N closed-loop makes *no explicit model* assumption, treating the process as a "black box" characterised only by its stability margin.
- **Different design targets:** Both target 1/4 decay ratio, but the *correlations* are different empirical regressions tuned on a different ensemble of pilot-plant processes.
- **Effect of derivative action:** In the closed-loop method, the PID allows $K_c = 0.6\,K_u$, which is *greater* than the PI's $0.45\,K_u$, because the derivative term contributes **phase lead** that adds stability margin, permitting more aggressive proportional action.
- **Dead-time dominance sensitivity:** Cohen–Coon is preferred for high $L/\tau$, but Z-N correlations are typically used for moderate $L/\tau$ (< 1).

---

### 🟢 Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop (continuous cycling) tuning method** in detail. List the correlations for P, PI, and PID controllers, and state the design criterion that the original Z-N correlations were designed to achieve.

**(b) [7 marks]** A level-control loop has FOPDT parameters $K_p = 1.2$, $L = 2$ s, $\tau = 8$ s. Compute the **Cohen–Coon PID settings** and compare them with the Z-N open-loop PID settings. Discuss which method is more appropriate when $L/\tau > 1$.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Z-N Closed-Loop Method Explained [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Place the controller in **P-mode only** (set $T_i = \infty$ to disable integral, $T_d = 0$ to disable derivative).
2. With the loop in **automatic**, give a small set-point change.
3. **Gradually increase $K_c$** in small increments.
4. After each increment, observe the closed-loop response. If oscillations decay, increase $K_c$ further.
5. Continue until the response shows **sustained, undamped oscillations** of constant amplitude and period.
6. Record the gain at which this occurs as the **Ultimate Gain $K_u$**, and the period of the oscillation as the **Ultimate Period $P_u$**.
7. Calculate the controller parameters using the Z-N closed-loop correlations (table below).
8. Implement the controller and **fine-tune** if necessary, typically by reducing $K_c$ by 10–20 % for safety margin.

**Step 2 — Z-N Closed-Loop Correlations (2 marks):**

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P   | $0.5\,K_u$  | — | — |
| PI  | $0.45\,K_u$ | $P_u/1.2$ | — |
| PID | $0.6\,K_u$  | $P_u/2$ | $P_u/8$ |

**Step 3 — Design Criterion (2 marks):**

The original Ziegler–Nichols (1942) correlations were designed empirically to produce a closed-loop response with a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio** (successive overshoots decay by a factor of 1/4). This corresponds approximately to:
- Damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.21$
- Overshoot $\approx 25$ % (first overshoot for a step input)
- Phase margin $\approx 30°$ (approx.)

This is acceptable for **regulatory / industrial process control** (e.g., temperature, level) but is *too oscillatory* for high-performance **servo** applications (e.g., motion control).

---

#### Part (b) — Cohen–Coon vs Z-N Comparison [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Cohen–Coon PID Formulas (1 mark):**

$$K_c = \frac{1}{K_p} \cdot \frac{\tau}{L} \cdot \frac{1 + \dfrac{L}{3\tau}}{1 + \dfrac{L}{12\tau}} \cdot \frac{4}{3}$$

$$T_i = L \cdot \frac{32 + 6\,\dfrac{L}{\tau}}{13 + 8\,\dfrac{L}{\tau}}, \qquad T_d = L \cdot \frac{4}{11 + 2\,\dfrac{L}{\tau}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Ratio $L/\tau$ (0.5 mark):**

$$\frac{L}{\tau} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 \quad (< 1)$$

This is **NOT** dead-time-dominant, so the question's emphasis on $L/\tau > 1$ refers to a *hypothetical* alternative process.

**Step 3 — Cohen–Coon Computation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c \cdot K_p &= \frac{8}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + \dfrac{2}{24}}{1 + \dfrac{2}{96}} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \\[6pt]
&= 4 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.0833}{1 + 0.0208} \cdot 1.3333 \\[6pt]
&= 4 \cdot \frac{1.0833}{1.0208} \cdot 1.3333 \\[6pt]
&= 4 \cdot 1.0613 \cdot 1.3333 = 5.6603
\end{aligned}$$

$$K_c^{\text{CC}} = \frac{5.6603}{1.2} = 4.717$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_i^{\text{CC}} &= 2 \cdot \frac{32 + 6(0.25)}{13 + 8(0.25)} = 2 \cdot \frac{33.5}{15} = 2 \times 2.233 = 4.467 \; \text{s} \\[6pt]
T_d^{\text{CC}} &= 2 \cdot \frac{4}{11 + 2(0.25)} = 2 \cdot \frac{4}{11.5} = 2 \times 0.3478 = 0.6957 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Z-N Open-Loop PID Computation (1.5 marks):**

$$K_c^{\text{ZN}} = \frac{1.2 \times 8}{1.2 \times 2} = 4.0, \qquad T_i = 4 \; \text{s}, \qquad T_d = 1 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Recommendation (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Z-N Open-Loop | Cohen–Coon |
|---|---|---|
| $K_c$ | 4.0 | 4.72 |
| $T_i$ | 4.0 s | 4.47 s |
| $T_d$ | 1.0 s | 0.70 s |

- Cohen–Coon gives a **slightly higher $K_c$** and **shorter $T_d$**, reflecting its tuning of the **dominant dead time** by explicit $\dfrac{L}{\tau}$ dependence.
- **For $L/\tau > 1$** (dead-time-dominant), Cohen–Coon is more appropriate because it uses the dead time as a primary tuning variable, whereas Z-N's simple $\tau/(K_p L)$ correlation becomes too conservative and gives sluggish response.
- For $L/\tau \ll 1$ (integrating or fast processes), Z-N open-loop is simpler and adequate.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in tuning questions:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up the two Z-N methods.** Open-loop uses $K_p, L, \tau$; closed-loop uses $K_u, P_u$. Examiners explicitly look for the correct set of correlations to be applied to the right data.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Always state the unit of $K_c$ (e.g., %/°C, mA/(kg/s)) and the unit of $T_i, T_d$ (seconds). Marks are awarded for unit declaration.
> 
> 3. **Using Cohen–Coon formulas incorrectly when $L = 0$.** The formulas have $L/\tau$ in the denominator and become undefined. State the assumption that the process has non-zero dead time.
> 
> 4. **Sign errors in computing $K_p$.** $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta PV}{\Delta M}$, not the reverse. A wrong sign gives a controller with reversed action, which is dangerous.
> 
> 5. **Not specifying the controller *form*.** Always state whether the final expression is **parallel (ideal)**, **series**, or **standard industrial (with filter)** form. The parallel form is the default in KTU exams.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the design-criterion comment.** Always mention that Z-N targets a 1/4 decay ratio — examiners award at least 1 mark for this insight.
> 
> 7. **Ignoring practical issues.** A 14-mark answer that omits "fine-tuning" / "safety factor of 0.8 on $K_c$" / "saturation of integral term (anti-windup)" loses application-level marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Controller tuning** = selecting $K_c, T_i, T_d$ to match controller dynamics to process dynamics.
- [ ] **FOPDT model:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p\,e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$ is the *universal* model underlying most classical tuning.
- [ ] **Process Reaction Curve (PRC):** tangent-at-inflection gives $L$ and $\tau$; steady-state ratio gives $K_p$.
- [ ] **Z-N Open-Loop (PRC) PID:** $K_c = \dfrac{1.2\,\tau}{K_p\,L}$, $T_i = 2L$, $T_d = 0.5L$.
- [ ] **Z-N Closed-Loop (Ultimate) PID:** $K_c = 0.6\,K_u$, $T_i = P_u/2$, $T_d = P_u/8$.
- [ ] **Cohen–Coon** is best for **dead-time-dominant** processes ($L/\tau \geq 1$).
- [ ] **Z-N design criterion** = **quarter-amplitude decay ratio** (≈ 25 % overshoot).
- [ ] **Parallel form** of PID: $G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$.
- [ ] **Derivative action** adds phase lead, allowing higher $K_c$ in PID vs PI.
- [ ] **Continuous cycling test** can be hazardous — prefer **relay auto-tuning** for production plant.
- [ ] **Performance indices** IAE, ISE, ITAE are used in optimisation-based (model-based) tuning.
- [ ] **Final step:** always **fine-tune in the field**; the calculated values are *starting points*, not absolutes.
- [ ] **Anti-windup** must be enabled for integral action to prevent integrator saturation during actuator limits.
- [ ] **Units check:** $K_c$ has units of *controller output / process variable*; $T_i, T_d$ are in seconds (or minutes — check the problem!).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for the Z-N numbers — "0.5, 0.45, 0.6 / 0, 1.2, 2 / 0, 0, 8":**
> Think of it as: P → 0.5·Ku, PI → 0.45·Ku with Ti = Pu/1.2, PID → 0.6·Ku with Ti = Pu/2 and Td = Pu/8. The denominators 1.2, 2, 8 increase as you go from PI to PID because adding more *dynamics* (integral then derivative) lets the controller handle a tighter response.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coon Method of Tuning (Process Reaction Curve Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coon Method of Tuning (Process Reaction Curve Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coon Method of Tuning (Cohen–Coon Tuning Rule):**
> The Cohen–Coon (commonly referred to as the **Coon method**) is an *open-loop, offline PID controller tuning technique* based on the **Process Reaction Curve (PRC)**. The process is excited by a **step change (ΔM)** in the manipulated variable, the resulting open-loop step response (S-shaped curve) is plotted, and three first-order-plus-dead-time (**FOPDT**) parameters are extracted: **Process Gain (K_p)**, **Dead Time / Transport Delay (L)**, and **Process Time Constant (T)**. These parameters are then substituted into empirical **Cohen–Coon correlations** to compute the optimum **K_c, τ_I, and τ_D** such that the closed-loop response exhibits a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio (1/4 decay)**.

### FOPDT Model (Basis of the Coon Method)

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-Ls}}{Ts + 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Terms to Remember:**
> - **Process Reaction Curve (PRC):** Open-loop response of the controlled variable (CV) to a step input in the manipulated variable (MV).
> - **FOPDT:** First-Order Plus Dead Time — the simplified process model used for tuning.
> - **Quarter-Amplitude Decay:** Successive peaks of the closed-loop error decay to one-fourth of the previous peak value.
> - **Cohen–Coon:** Empirical rule developed by G.H. Cohen and G.A. Coon (1953) for chemical process tuning.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Cricket Coach" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a cricket coach. Before the match, you don't know the bowler's speed, grip, or reaction time. So you ask the bowler to deliver **one practice ball** (this is the **step input / bump test**). You then carefully observe:
> 1. **How much delay** passes before the ball reaches the batsman → **Dead Time (L)** — the "lag" before the system reacts.
> 2. **How fast the batsman swings the bat** → **Time Constant (T)** — the system's natural response speed.
> 3. **How far the ball travels for a fixed arm swing** → **Process Gain (K_p)** — the "sensitivity" of the output to the input.
>
> Based on these three observations (L, T, K_p), you can now decide **how hard, how soon, and for how long** the bowler should bowl — exactly analogous to setting **K_c (controller gain), τ_I (integral time), and τ_D (derivative time)** in a PID controller. The Cohen–Coon formulas are like the coach's pre-match playbook derived from data.

### Why "Open-Loop"?
Because the test is performed **without any controller in the loop** — the loop is broken, a step is applied, and the natural behavior of the process is recorded.

### Why Called "Process Reaction Curve"?
Because we are observing the **reaction** (response) of the **process** itself — not the closed-loop system.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Typical Range (Process Industry) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Gain | **K_p** | % / % (dimensionless) | 0.1 to 10 |
| Dead Time | **L** | seconds (s) or minutes (min) | 1 s to 60 min |
| Time Constant | **T** | seconds (s) or minutes (min) | 5 s to 60 min |
| Controller Gain | **K_c** | dimensionless | 0.01 to 100 |
| Integral Time | **τ_I** | seconds (s) | L < τ_I < 4T |
| Derivative Time | **τ_D** | seconds (s) | 0.1L < τ_D < 0.5L |
| Decay Ratio | **DR** | dimensionless | **1/4 (quarter-amplitude)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quarter-Amplitude Decay Ratio (QADR):** The defining performance benchmark used by Cohen–Coon. The 2nd overshoot peak = 1/4 × (1st overshoot peak). This gives ~20–30% overshoot in the closed-loop step response.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory — The Logic Flow

The Coon method is a **model-based empirical tuning** procedure. It follows a strict **five-step logical chain**:

1. **Open the Control Loop** — Place the controller in **MANUAL** mode (or disconnect it entirely).
2. **Apply a Step Bump (ΔM)** — Introduce a small, deliberate step change (typically 3–10%) in the controller output (manipulated variable).
3. **Record the Process Reaction Curve (PRC)** — Plot the controlled variable (PV) versus time. The curve will exhibit a characteristic **S-shape** with three identifiable regions:
   - **Region I — Dead Time (L):** No response for an initial time interval `L`.
   - **Region II — Maximum Slope Region:** The steepest tangent line is drawn through the inflection point.
   - **Region III — Final Steady State:** Output settles to a new equilibrium value.
4. **Extract the FOPDT Parameters (K_p, L, T)** from the S-curve:
   - **K_p (Process Gain):** Ratio of steady-state output change to input step change.
   - **L (Dead Time):** x-intercept of the **steepest tangent line**.
   - **T (Time Constant):** Difference between the x-intercept and the point where the tangent line intersects the **final steady-state** value.
5. **Apply the Cohen–Coon Empirical Correlations** to compute K_c, τ_I, and τ_D.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cohen–Coon Works Well for "Lag-Dominant" Processes:**
> The Cohen–Coon tuning rules were developed specifically for processes where the **dead time (L)** is comparable to or greater than the time constant (T) — typical of chemical process industries (distillation columns, heat exchangers, chemical reactors). For very fast processes (L << T), the **Ziegler–Nichols** method is preferred.

### The Three Governing Assumptions

- The process can be reasonably approximated by an **FOPDT model** (valid for >80% of industrial processes).
- The process is **open-loop stable** and **self-regulating**.
- The open-loop PRC is approximately **S-shaped** (sigmoidal), enabling tangent-line construction.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cohen–Coon Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are derived for FOPDT process** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-Ls}}{Ts+1}$ **and target a Quarter-Amplitude Decay Response.**

| Controller Type | Controller Gain (K_c) | Integral Time (τ_I) | Derivative Time (τ_D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P-only** | $\dfrac{T}{K_p \, L} \left(1 + \dfrac{L}{3T}\right)$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $\dfrac{T}{K_p \, L} \left(0.9 + \dfrac{L}{12T}\right)$ | $L \cdot \dfrac{30 + 3(L/T)}{9 + 20(L/T)}$ | — |
| **PD** | $\dfrac{T}{K_p \, L} \cdot 1.25$ | — | $0.25 \, L$ |
| **PID** | $\dfrac{T}{K_p \, L} \left(\dfrac{4}{3} + \dfrac{L}{4T}\right)$ | $L \cdot \dfrac{32 + 6(L/T)}{13 + 8(L/T)}$ | $\dfrac{4L}{11 + 2(L/T)}$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Mark-Loss Alert:** Many KTU answer scripts incorrectly use the **Ziegler–Nichols PRC formulas** in Cohen–Coon questions. Remember: Z–N uses $(1 + L/3T)$ for PI and $(1.2 + L/3T)$ for PID with no decay specification. **Cohen–Coon** uses the constants **0.9, 1.25, 4/3, etc.,** tuned for **quarter-amplitude decay**.

### Boundary Conditions & Validity

| Condition | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| **L / T ratio** | 0.1 ≤ L/T ≤ 1.0 (ideal); method breaks down if L/T > 1.0 |
| **Process Type** | Best for **self-regulating** processes with measurable dead time |
| **Loop Type** | Open-loop test; do NOT use on integrating or runaway processes |
| **Decay Performance** | Quarter-amplitude decay (≈ 20–25% overshoot) |
| **Step Magnitude (ΔM)** | 3–10% of MV span to avoid process upset |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Industry:** Tuning temperature loops in distillation columns where dead time dominates due to transport lag.
- **HVAC & Building Automation:** Tuning thermal comfort loops in large buildings with significant air-transport delay.
- **Paper & Pulp Mills:** Level and consistency control where dead time is comparable to process lag.
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors:** pH and temperature control where dead time varies with stirrer speed and viscosity.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Chemical dosing (chlorine, coagulant) loops where the L/T ratio is high.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cohen–Coon tuning gives a **more aggressive** response than Ziegler–Nichols for lag-dominant processes, because it accounts for the *degree* of dead time (L/T ratio) rather than treating L as a fixed percentage. This results in **faster disturbance rejection** — at the cost of slightly higher initial overshoot.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — FOPDT Parameter Extraction from the PRC

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A step input of magnitude **ΔM (%)** is applied to the open-loop process at t = 0. The output (PV) changes from an initial steady state **PV₀** to a final steady state **PV_ss** after some time. The PRC exhibits a sigmoidal (S-shaped) response.

### Step 1 — Compute the Process Gain (K_p)

$$K_p = \frac{\Delta PV}{\Delta M} = \frac{PV_{ss} - PV_0}{\Delta M}$$

- **Unit:** dimensionless (%/% in industrial instrumentation).
- **Sign Convention:** Positive K_p → direct-acting process. Negative K_p → reverse-acting process (flip K_c sign).

### Step 2 — Draw the Steepest Tangent Line at the Inflection Point

Locate the **inflection point (P)** on the PRC — the point of **maximum slope (dPV/dt = max)**. Draw a tangent line through P.

### Step 3 — Compute the Dead Time (L)

The **dead time L** is the time at which the tangent line **intersects the initial steady-state value (PV₀)** on the time axis.

$$L = t_{tangent \cap PV_0} - t_{step}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Time Constant (T)

The **time constant T** is the time required for the tangent line to traverse the **total change in PV**, measured from the dead-time point to the point where the tangent line meets the **final steady-state value (PV_ss)**.

$$T = t_{tangent \cap PV_{ss}} - t_{tangent \cap PV_0}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into Cohen–Coon Formulas

For a **PID controller** (the most common exam target):

$$K_c = \frac{T}{K_p \, L} \left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{L}{4T}\right)$$

$$\tau_I = L \cdot \frac{32 + 6(L/T)}{13 + 8(L/T)}$$

$$\tau_D = \frac{4L}{11 + 2(L/T)}$$

---

## 3.2 Fully Solved Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Module 3, KTU Pattern):**
> An open-loop bump test is performed on a process tank level control loop. The controller output is stepped from 40% to 50% (ΔM = 10%). The level (PV) initially at 35% rises and settles to 47% after a long time. The S-shaped response has a steepest tangent line that intersects the initial PV = 35% at t = 2 s, and the final PV = 47% at t = 12 s. Design a **PID controller** using the **Cohen–Coon method**. Verify the quarter-amplitude decay design.

### Step 1 — Extract K_p

$$K_p = \frac{\Delta PV}{\Delta M} = \frac{47 - 35}{50 - 40} = \frac{12}{10} = 1.2 \, (\% /\%)$$

### Step 2 — Extract L (Dead Time)

$$L = t_{tangent \cap PV_0} - t_{step} = 2 \, s - 0 \, s = 2 \, s$$

### Step 3 — Extract T (Time Constant)

$$T = t_{tangent \cap PV_{ss}} - t_{tangent \cap PV_0} = 12 \, s - 2 \, s = 10 \, s$$

### Step 4 — Compute the L/T Ratio

$$\frac{L}{T} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Cohen–Coon PID Formulas

**Controller Gain (K_c):**

$$
K_c = \frac{T}{K_p \, L} \left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{L}{4T}\right)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
K_c = \frac{10}{(1.2)(2)} \left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{2}{4 \times 10}\right)
$$

$$
K_c = \frac{10}{2.4} \left(\frac{4}{3} + 0.05\right)
$$

$$
K_c = 4.1667 \times \left(1.3333 + 0.05\right)
$$

$$
K_c = 4.1667 \times 1.3833
$$

$$
\boxed{K_c = 5.764}
$$

**Integral Time (τ_I):**

$$
\tau_I = L \cdot \frac{32 + 6(L/T)}{13 + 8(L/T)}
$$

Substituting L = 2 s, L/T = 0.2:

$$
\tau_I = 2 \cdot \frac{32 + 6(0.2)}{13 + 8(0.2)}
$$

$$
\tau_I = 2 \cdot \frac{32 + 1.2}{13 + 1.6}
$$

$$
\tau_I = 2 \cdot \frac{33.2}{14.6}
$$

$$
\tau_I = 2 \cdot 2.274
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_I = 4.548 \, s}
$$

**Derivative Time (τ_D):**

$$
\tau_D = \frac{4L}{11 + 2(L/T)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\tau_D = \frac{4(2)}{11 + 2(0.2)}
$$

$$
\tau_D = \frac{8}{11 + 0.4}
$$

$$
\tau_D = \frac{8}{11.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_D = 0.7018 \, s}
$$

### Step 6 — Express the PID Controller Transfer Function

$$G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right) = 5.764 \left(1 + \frac{1}{4.548s} + 0.7018s\right)$$

### Step 7 — Verification of Quarter-Amplitude Decay

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2nd overshoot peak will be approximately **1/4 (25%) of the 1st overshoot peak**, and the overall response will exhibit **~22% overshoot** with a **decay ratio of 0.25**. This is the **defining performance signature** of Cohen–Coon tuning.

### Result Summary Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Gain | K_p | 1.2 | %/% |
| Dead Time | L | 2 | s |
| Time Constant | T | 10 | s |
| L/T Ratio | L/T | 0.2 | — |
| Controller Gain | K_c | **5.764** | — |
| Integral Time | τ_I | **4.548** | s |
| Derivative Time | τ_D | **0.7018** | s |
| Target Decay Ratio | DR | **1/4** | — |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Cohen–Coon vs. Ziegler–Nichols (PRC Variant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks "Compare Coon method with Z-N method." Use this table for full marks.

| Parameter | Cohen–Coon (Coon) | Ziegler–Nichols (PRC) |
|---|---|---|
| Year Developed | 1953 | 1942 |
| Target Decay Ratio | **1/4 (quarter-amplitude)** | **1/4 (quarter-amplitude)** |
| PID K_c Formula | $\frac{T}{K_p L}\left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{L}{4T}\right)$ | $\frac{1.2 \, T}{K_p \, L}$ |
| PID τ_I Formula | $L \cdot \frac{32 + 6(L/T)}{13 + 8(L/T)}$ | $2L$ |
| PID τ_D Formula | $\frac{4L}{11 + 2(L/T)}$ | $0.5L$ |
| Optimal Process Range | **0.1 < L/T < 1.0** (lag-dominant) | L < 0.5T (fast processes) |
| Overshoot Performance | ~20–25% | ~25–30% |
| Disturbance Rejection | **Faster (more aggressive)** | Slower |
| Setpoint Tracking | Moderate | Moderate |
| Loop Aggressiveness | **High** | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Reaction Curve (PRC) with Tangent-Line Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Block-Level Schematic of the Cohen–Coon Open-Loop Tuning Procedure**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Place Controller in MANUAL Mode] --> B[Step 2: Open the Control Loop]
    B --> C[Step 3: Apply a Step Bump to Manipulated Variable MV, magnitude DELTA M]
    C --> D[Step 4: Record the Process Reaction Curve PRC - S-shaped Response]
    D --> E[Step 5: Identify the Inflection Point P on PRC - Point of Maximum Slope]
    E --> F[Step 6: Draw the Steepest Tangent Line Through Point P]
    F --> G[Step 7: Locate the X-Intercept of Tangent - This Gives Dead Time L]
    G --> H[Step 8: Locate Intersection of Tangent with Final Steady State - This Gives Time Constant T]
    H --> I[Step 9: Compute Process Gain Kp equals DELTA PV divided by DELTA M]
    I --> J[Step 10: Substitute Kp, L, T into Cohen-Coon Correlations]
    J --> K[Step 11: Compute Kc Controller Gain]
    J --> L[Step 12: Compute tau_I Integral Time]
    J --> M[Step 13: Compute tau_D Derivative Time]
    K --> N[Step 14: Implement PID Controller and Switch to AUTO]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Step 15: Verify Quarter Amplitude Decay Response]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Cohen–Coon PID Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Setpoint SP] --> E1[Comparator, plus and minus]
    E1 --> E2[Error e of t]
    E2 --> C[PID Controller, Kc, tau_I, tau_D]
    C --> MV[Manipulated Variable MV]
    MV --> P[FOPDT Process, Kp, L, T]
    P --> PV[Process Variable PV]
    PV --> FB[Feedback Path]
    FB --> E1
```

## 4.3 Parameter Extraction from the PRC — Annotated Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time Axis t] --> B[t equals 0, Step Applied]
    B --> C[Region I, Dead Time L, No Response]
    C --> D[Region II, Inflection Point P, Max Slope]
    D --> E[Region III, Final Steady State, PV_ss]
    A1[Tangent Line at P] --> D
    A1 --> A2[X Intercept equals L]
    A1 --> A3[Intersection with PV_ss gives T]
    P1[Process Gain Kp] --> A4[Kp equals DELTA PV divided by DELTA M]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The **tangent line at the inflection point** is the most important geometric construction in Cohen–Coon tuning. Without it, L and T cannot be determined. In KTU exams, **always draw the tangent line** on the PRC even if the question does not explicitly ask for it — it is worth **2–3 marks** by itself.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Cohen–Coon method of controller tuning. What is the underlying process model and the target decay ratio?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Cohen–Coon method** (also called the Coon method) is an **open-loop, offline PID tuning technique** based on the **Process Reaction Curve (PRC)**.

**(1 Mark — Definition; 1 Mark — Model; 1 Mark — Decay)**

- **Underlying Process Model:** FOPDT (First-Order Plus Dead Time)

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-Ls}}{Ts + 1}$$

- **Parameters Extracted from PRC:** K_p (process gain), L (dead time), T (time constant).
- **Target Decay Ratio:** **Quarter-amplitude decay (1/4)** — successive peaks of the closed-loop error reduce to 25% of the previous peak.
- **Application:** Best suited for **lag-dominant processes** (0.1 < L/T < 1.0).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the three FOPDT parameters extracted from a Process Reaction Curve and explain how each is obtained geometrically.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Geometric Construction (1 Mark each) |
|---|---|
| **K_p (Process Gain)** | Ratio of steady-state PV change to MV step change: $K_p = \Delta PV / \Delta M$ |
| **L (Dead Time)** | x-intercept of the **steepest tangent line** drawn at the inflection point of the PRC, measured from t = 0 |
| **T (Time Constant)** | Time between the tangent line's x-intercept (PV₀) and the point where the tangent line crosses the final steady-state PV_ss |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Part B Module 3 Pattern:**
> - Each Part B question = **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.
> - Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Cohen–Coon PID Design with Full PRC Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Cohen–Coon method of PID controller tuning** with a neat labeled sketch of the Process Reaction Curve (PRC). Show how the parameters **K_p, L, and T** are obtained from the PRC. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A thermal heating tank exhibits the following open-loop step response data:
- Initial PV (level) = 25 °C
- Step change in MV (heater power) = ΔM = 15%
- Final steady-state PV = 55 °C
- Tangent line x-intercept (with PV₀) = 5 s
- Tangent line intersection with PV_ss = 35 s

**Design a PI controller using the Cohen–Coon method. State the closed-loop performance expected.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Explanation (7 Marks):**

1. **Definition (1 Mark):** The Cohen–Coon method is an open-loop empirical tuning technique for PID controllers, developed for **lag-dominant FOPDT processes**. It uses the **PRC** to extract K_p, L, T and applies empirical correlations targeting **quarter-amplitude decay**.

2. **Procedure (3 Marks):**
   - Place controller in MANUAL; open the loop.
   - Apply a step change ΔM in the MV.
   - Record the PRC (S-shaped curve).
   - At the **inflection point P**, draw the **steepest tangent line**.
   - **L** = x-intercept of tangent (where it meets PV₀).
   - **T** = time difference between tangent's intersection with PV₀ and PV_ss.
   - **K_p** = (PV_ss − PV₀) / ΔM.
   - Substitute into Cohen–Coon formulas for K_c, τ_I, τ_D.

3. **Neat Labeled PRC Sketch (2 Marks):** Show t-axis, PV-axis, step at t = 0, initial PV₀, inflection point P, tangent line, dead time L, time constant T, final steady-state PV_ss.

4. **Performance Target (1 Mark):** Quarter-amplitude decay (DR = 1/4), ~20–25% overshoot.

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute K_p (1 Mark):**

$$K_p = \frac{55 - 25}{15} = \frac{30}{15} = 2.0 \, (^{\circ}C /\%)$$

**Step 2 — Extract L and T (1 Mark):**

$$L = 5 \, s, \quad T = 35 - 5 = 30 \, s$$

**Step 3 — Compute L/T Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{L}{T} = \frac{5}{30} = 0.1667$$

**Step 4 — Apply Cohen–Coon PI Formulas (3 Marks):**

$$K_c = \frac{T}{K_p \, L} \left(0.9 + \frac{L}{12T}\right)$$

$$
K_c = \frac{30}{(2.0)(5)} \left(0.9 + \frac{5}{12 \times 30}\right) = 3.0 \left(0.9 + 0.0139\right) = 3.0 \times 0.9139
$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 2.742}$$

$$\tau_I = L \cdot \frac{30 + 3(L/T)}{9 + 20(L/T)}$$

$$
\tau_I = 5 \cdot \frac{30 + 3(0.1667)}{9 + 20(0.1667)} = 5 \cdot \frac{30 + 0.5}{9 + 3.333} = 5 \cdot \frac{30.5}{12.333}
$$

$$\boxed{\tau_I = 12.366 \, s}$$

**Step 5 — State Expected Performance (1 Mark):** Quarter-amplitude decay (DR = 1/4), ~22% overshoot, faster disturbance rejection than Z–N.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Comparative + Cohen–Coon PD Design

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Module Choice]**

**Q. (a)** Compare the **Cohen–Coon (Coon) method** with the **Ziegler–Nichols PRC method** of PID tuning. Discuss the suitability of each for different L/T ratios. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A chemical reactor temperature control loop has FOPDT parameters K_p = 0.8 °C/%, L = 4 s, T = 16 s. Design a **PID controller** using the Cohen–Coon method and verify the design point. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Comparison Axis (1–2 Marks each) | Cohen–Coon | Ziegler–Nichols PRC |
|---|---|---|
| **Year / Origin** | 1953 (Cohen & Coon) | 1942 (Ziegler & Nichols) |
| **Target Decay** | 1/4 (quarter amplitude) | 1/4 (quarter amplitude) |
| **PID K_c Formula** | $\frac{T}{K_p L}\left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{L}{4T}\right)$ | $\frac{1.2 \, T}{K_p \, L}$ |
| **PID τ_I Formula** | $L \cdot \frac{32 + 6(L/T)}{13 + 8(L/T)}$ | $2L$ |
| **PID τ_D Formula** | $\frac{4L}{11 + 2(L/T)}$ | $0.5L$ |
| **Best L/T Range** | 0.1 < L/T < 1.0 (lag-dominant) | L < 0.5T (fast processes) |
| **Loop Aggressiveness** | More aggressive, faster disturbance rejection | Less aggressive, more conservative |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Cohen–Coon is preferred for **chemical processes with significant dead time** (distillation, heat exchangers), while Z–N PRC is preferred for **fast processes with negligible dead time** (flow, pressure loops).

**Part (b) — Numerical PID Design (7 Marks):**

**Given:** K_p = 0.8 °C/%, L = 4 s, T = 16 s.

**Step 1 — L/T Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{L}{T} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Compute K_c (2 Marks):**

$$
K_c = \frac{T}{K_p \, L} \left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{L}{4T}\right) = \frac{16}{(0.8)(4)} \left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{4}{4 \times 16}\right)
$$

$$
K_c = 5.0 \left(1.3333 + 0.0625\right) = 5.0 \times 1.3958
$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 6.979}$$

**Step 3 — Compute τ_I (2 Marks):**

$$
\tau_I = L \cdot \frac{32 + 6(L/T)}{13 + 8(L/T)} = 4 \cdot \frac{32 + 6(0.25)}{13 + 8(0.25)} = 4 \cdot \frac{33.5}{15}
$$

$$\boxed{\tau_I = 8.933 \, s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute τ_D (1 Mark):**

$$
\tau_D = \frac{4L}{11 + 2(L/T)} = \frac{4(4)}{11 + 2(0.25)} = \frac{16}{11.5}
$$

$$\boxed{\tau_D = 1.391 \, s}$$

**Step 5 — Design Verification (1 Mark):** The L/T ratio (0.25) lies in the **recommended range (0.1–1.0)**, so the Cohen–Coon design is valid. Expected performance: **quarter-amplitude decay**, **~22% overshoot**, and **faster disturbance rejection** than Z–N.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost**
> 1. **Confusing Cohen–Coon with Z–N PRC Formulas** — Cohen–Coon uses constants 0.9, 1.25, 4/3, etc. Z–N uses 1.2, 2L, 0.5L. Mixing them = **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Draw the Tangent Line on the PRC** — In part (a) questions, the tangent line construction is worth **2 marks** by itself. Without it, full marks cannot be awarded.
> 3. **Sign Error in K_p** — If the PV *decreases* for a positive MV step, K_p is **negative** (reverse acting). The student must then **reverse the K_c sign** when implementing on the controller.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency** — If MV is in % and PV is in °C, then K_p is in °C/%, **not dimensionless**. Controllers handle this automatically via engineering-unit scaling, but students should report units correctly in derivations.
> 5. **Using Cohen–Coon for Integrating or Runaway Processes** — The method requires an **S-shaped PRC**. Integrating processes give a *ramp* response, and the formulas are **invalid**. This is a **favourite 2-mark trick question** in KTU.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Coon method = Cohen–Coon** = **open-loop** PRC-based PID tuning for **FOPDT** processes.
- ✅ **FOPDT model:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-Ls}}{Ts+1}$.
- ✅ **PRC** = open-loop step response (S-shaped). Tangent at inflection point gives **L** and **T**; **K_p** = ΔPV/ΔM.
- ✅ **Target:** **Quarter-amplitude decay (DR = 1/4)**, ≈ 22% overshoot.
- ✅ **Best for:** Lag-dominant processes (**0.1 < L/T < 1.0**).
- ✅ **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
   - **PID K_c:** $\frac{T}{K_p L}\left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{L}{4T}\right)$
   - **PI K_c:** $\frac{T}{K_p L}\left(0.9 + \frac{L}{12T}\right)$
   - **PID τ_I:** $L \cdot \frac{32 + 6(L/T)}{13 + 8(L/T)}$
   - **PID τ_D:** $\frac{4L}{11 + 2(L/T)}$
- ✅ **NOT valid for:** Integrating, runaway, or very-fast (L/T < 0.1) processes.
- ✅ **Advantage over Z–N PRC:** More aggressive tuning, faster disturbance rejection for lag-dominant loops.
- ✅ **Always draw the tangent line** on the PRC diagram for full marks.
- ✅ **L/T ratio check** is mandatory — outside 0.1–1.0 range, choose a different method (Z–N closed-loop or direct synthesis).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultimate Cycle Method (Ziegler Nichol's Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultimate Cycle Method (Ziegler Nichol's Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Ultimate Cycle Method** (also called the **Closed-Loop Tuning Method** or **Ziegler-Nichols Continuous Cycling Method**) is an *in-situ* experimental procedure used to determine the optimal tuning parameters of a **PID controller** for a stable process whose open-loop transfer function is either unknown or too complex to model analytically.

The method forces the closed-loop system into **sustained (continuous) oscillations** by switching the controller to **P-mode** (with $T_i = \infty$ and $T_d = 0$) and gradually increasing the proportional gain $K_p$ until the system output just begins to oscillate with a **constant amplitude and fixed period**. The critical gain at which this occurs is called the **Ultimate Gain ($K_u$)**, and the corresponding oscillation period is the **Ultimate Period ($P_u$)**. These two empirical constants are then substituted into empirical Ziegler-Nichols correlations to compute $K_p$, $T_i$, and $T_d$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Ultimate Cycle Method is the *only* purely closed-loop empirical tuning technique typically asked in KTU 2024 Scheme PCICT502 Module 3. It is fundamentally different from the **Process Reaction Curve Method** (open-loop), which is also from the same Z-N family but uses a different experimental procedure.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Shower-Tap Tuning" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to set the hot-water tap in a shower to a *perfect* warm temperature using only a **single knob** (the proportional gain $K_p$).

- **Knob turned too gently (low $K_p$):** The water slowly creeps toward warm but never quite arrives. You are *under-damped* — sluggish response, large steady-state error.
- **Knob turned perfectly (ideal $K_p$):** The water reaches "warm" quickly, overshoots slightly, and settles — a well-tuned system.
- **Knob cranked too hard (high $K_p$):** The water swings between *scalding* and *freezing* in never-ending oscillations. The system is *marginally stable* — this is exactly the **Ultimate Gain $K_u$**.

Ziegler and Nichols said: *"Don't guess the perfect knob position. Instead, first find the knob position where it just starts swinging forever ($K_u$ and $P_u$). Then, mathematically, take a fraction of that position."* It is a brilliant **"calibrate-at-the-edge-of-instability"** strategy.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **$K_u$** — **Ultimate Gain** (dimensionless, controller proportional gain at marginal stability).
- **$P_u$** — **Ultimate Period** (in **seconds** or **minutes**), the time for one complete oscillation cycle.
- **$K_p$** — Actual proportional gain to be used.
- **$T_i$** — Integral time (reset time), in **seconds/minutes**.
- **$T_d$** — Derivative time, in **seconds/minutes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Ultimate Cycle Method applies **only to stable, minimum-phase processes** where the open-loop response does not already contain an integrator (otherwise the system will never produce bounded sustained oscillations under pure P-control — it will either saturate or drift).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Breakdown of the Method

The procedure is executed in **five rigid steps**. The KTU examiner will award partial credit for correctly listing these steps even if the final numerical answer is wrong.

1. **Switch the controller to P-mode only.** Disable the integral action by setting $T_i = \infty$ (or its maximum value), and disable the derivative action by setting $T_d = 0$.
2. **Set the set-point to the desired operating value** and let the process reach a steady state. Record the steady-state output.
3. **Gradually increase the proportional gain $K_p$** in small increments. After each increment, disturb the loop (e.g., apply a small set-point step) and observe the response on the recorder.
4. **Identify the critical (ultimate) gain $K_u$** — the value of $K_p$ for which the closed-loop response just becomes a **sustained oscillation of constant amplitude** (marginally stable, characteristic equation roots on the imaginary axis).
5. **Measure the corresponding period $P_u$** of the sustained oscillation from the time-response plot. Plug $K_u$ and $P_u$ into the Z-N empirical correlation table to compute the recommended controller settings.

### The "Why" Behind the Method (Stability Theory Viewpoint)

The closed-loop characteristic equation of a unity-feedback system with a P-controller is:

$$1 + K_p G_p(s) G_v(s) G_m(s) = 0$$

As $K_p$ increases from 0, the closed-loop poles migrate from their open-loop positions. For most open-loop stable processes, the dominant complex-conjugate poles eventually cross the imaginary axis. The value of $K_p$ at this crossing is $K_u$, and the imaginary part of the pole location at that instant determines $P_u$ via:

$$P_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_u}$$

where $\omega_u$ is the **ultimate frequency** (rad/s). Once $K_u$ is known, the **gain margin** of the open-loop system is by definition exactly **1 (0 dB)**.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Tuning Correlations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule for the Table:** All absolute-value/pipe symbols are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| Controller Type | Proportional Gain $K_p$ | Integral Time $T_i$ | Derivative Time $T_d$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **P-Controller** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | — | — |
| **PI-Controller** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{1.2}$ | — |
| **PD-Controller** | $0.8 \, K_u$ | — | $\dfrac{P_u}{8}$ |
| **Classic PID** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $0.5 \, P_u$ | $0.125 \, P_u$ |
| **Some-overshoot PID** | $\dfrac{K_u}{3}$ | $0.5 \, P_u$ | $0.125 \, P_u$ |
| **No-overshoot PID** | $0.2 \, K_u$ | $0.5 \, P_u$ | $0.125 \, P_u$ |

### Related Equations

The PID controller output equation used in KTU problems is the **ideal parallel (non-interacting) form**:

$$u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

For the **series (interacting) form**, the conversion is:

$$K_p' = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{T_d}{T_i} \right), \quad T_i' = T_i + T_d, \quad T_d' = \frac{T_i \, T_d}{T_i + T_d}$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The Ultimate Cycle Method is widely used in **refinery distillation column temperature loops**, **chemical reactor jacketed-cooling loops**, and **HVAC damper-control loops** where the process dynamics are non-linear, time-varying, or simply too complex to model from first principles. The method is *plant-friendly* because it requires only a P-controller (the most common industrial controller mode) and a recorder. However, it is *not* recommended for **integrating processes** (e.g., level control in a tank without a pump) or for **open-loop unstable** processes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $P_u$ from the Marginal-Stability Condition

Consider a stable open-loop process with transfer function $G(s)$ and a P-controller of gain $K_p$ in a unity-feedback loop. The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$
1 + K_p \, G(s) = 0
$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$ for a purely imaginary root (sustained oscillation condition):

$$
1 + K_p \, G(j\omega) = 0
$$

$$
K_p \, G(j\omega) = -1
$$

For this to hold, the **magnitude condition** is:

$$
\vert K_p \, G(j\omega) \vert = 1
$$

And the **angle condition** is:

$$
\angle G(j\omega) = -180^\circ
$$

Let $\omega_u$ be the frequency at which $\angle G(j\omega_u) = -180^\circ$. Then the critical gain is:

$$
K_u = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_u) \vert}
$$

The sustained oscillation is a sinusoid of angular frequency $\omega_u$ rad/s, so its **time period** is:

$$
P_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_u} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: First-Order Plus Dead-Time (FOPDT) Verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A very common KTU 2024 question: *"Verify the Z-N tuning correlations for a first-order plus dead-time (FOPDT) process with transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K \, e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$."*

**Step 1 — Apply the angle condition.**
We need $\angle G(j\omega) = -180^\circ$. For an FOPDT process:

$$
G(j\omega) = \frac{K \, e^{-jL\omega}}{1 + j\omega\tau}
$$

$$
\angle G(j\omega) = -L\omega - \tan^{-1}(\omega\tau) = -180^\circ = -\pi
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\omega_u$.**

$$
L\omega_u + \tan^{-1}(\omega_u \tau) = \pi
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the magnitude condition.**

$$
K_u = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_u \tau)^2}}{K}
$$

This result shows that $K_u$ depends on the **dead time $L$** and the **time constant $\tau$**. As $L \to 0$, $K_u \to \infty$ (no marginal instability achievable). As $L$ increases, $K_u$ decreases — making tuning easier (and oscillation more likely).

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style Problem).**

Suppose $K = 1$, $\tau = 1$ min, $L = 1$ min. Solve numerically:

$$
\omega_u + \tan^{-1}(\omega_u) = \pi
$$

Iterating: $\omega_u \approx 2.029$ rad/min.

$$
P_u = \frac{2\pi}{2.029} \approx 3.096 \text{ min}
$$

$$
K_u = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2.029)^2}}{1} = \sqrt{1 + 4.117} = \sqrt{5.117} \approx 2.262
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Z-N correlations for a PI controller.**

$$
K_p = 0.45 \, K_u = 0.45 \times 2.262 \approx 1.018
$$

$$
T_i = \frac{P_u}{1.2} = \frac{3.096}{1.2} \approx 2.58 \text{ min}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Process Behavior to Tuning Choice

| Process Characteristic | Recommended Z-N Variant | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slow process, large time constant | Classic PID | Derivative helps anticipate long-term drift |
| Fast process with small dead time | Some-overshoot PID | Less aggressive $K_p$ prevents ringing |
| Process with actuator wear concern | No-overshoot PID | Conservative gain protects valve/pump |
| Highly non-linear / multi-variable | PI (with auto-tuning) | Derivative amplifies measurement noise |
| Integrating process (level) | **Method not applicable** | Pure P-mode yields drift, not oscillation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classic Z-N criterion (Quarter-Amplitude Decay) intentionally produces a **~25% overshoot** response. For modern processes where set-point overshoot is unacceptable, modified correlations (Tyreus-Luyben, Cohen-Coon, or Skogestad's SIMC rules) are preferred. KTU questions may ask for the *trade-off* between the two.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Tuning Procedure

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the closed-loop interaction during the Ultimate Cycle Method, showing how the controller, process, and recorder are interconnected and how the operator's adjustments feed back into the loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Process at steady state]) --> mode[Set controller to P-mode only]
    mode --> tiact[Set Ti = infinity]
    mode --> tdact[Set Td = zero]
    tiact --> initkp[Set Kp = small value]
    tdact --> initkp
    initkp --> disturb[Apply small set-point step]
    disturb --> observe{Response observation}
    observe -->|Oscillations decay| inc1[Increase Kp slightly]
    inc1 --> disturb
    observe -->|Oscillations grow| dec1[Decrease Kp slightly]
    dec1 --> disturb
    observe -->|Sustained constant-amplitude oscillation| critical[Record critical values]
    critical --> ku[Note Ku = current Kp value]
    critical --> pu[Measure period Pu from recorder]
    ku --> table[Refer to Z-N correlation table]
    pu --> table
    table --> ptype{Controller type?}
    ptype -->|P-only| pcalc[Kp = 0.5 Ku]
    ptype -->|PI| picalc[Kp = 0.45 Ku, Ti = Pu / 1.2]
    ptype -->|PID| pidcalc[Kp = 0.6 Ku, Ti = 0.5 Pu, Td = 0.125 Pu]
    pcalc --> verify[Implement and verify response]
    picalc --> verify
    pidcalc --> verify
    verify --> done([Tuning complete])
```

### 4.2 Time-Response Schematic (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
graph LR
    a["Time t = 0: Set-point step applied"] --> b["Output decays smoothly: Kp less than Ku"]
    b --> c["Output grows without bound: Kp greater than Ku"]
    c --> d["Output oscillates with constant amplitude: Kp equals Ku — MARGINAL STABILITY"]
    d --> e["Measure Pu from one complete cycle on the chart recorder"]
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Block Diagram (Standard Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r[R_s: Set-point] --> sum((Sum junction))
    d[Disturbance D_s] --> sum
    sum --> e[E_s: Error]
    e --> c[Controller Gc: P-mode with gain Kp]
    c --> u[Control signal U_s]
    u --> plant[Process Gp]
    plant --> y[Process output Y_s]
    y --> fb((Feedback))
    d2[D_s] --> fb
    fb --> sum
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagram is a *closed-loop unity feedback* representation. In the Ultimate Cycle Method, the **only gain that the operator manipulates** is $K_p$ inside the controller block `Gc`. The process `Gp` is fixed and unknown.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q1.** What is meant by the *Ultimate Gain ($K_u$)* and *Ultimate Period ($P_u$)* in the context of Ziegler-Nichols closed-loop tuning?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Ultimate Gain ($K_u$)** is defined as the value of the proportional gain $K_p$ (with the controller in pure P-mode, i.e., $T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$) at which the closed-loop system response just becomes a **sustained oscillation of constant amplitude**, i.e., the system is **marginally stable**. (1.5 marks)
The **Ultimate Period ($P_u$)** is the **time period** of this sustained oscillation, measured in seconds/minutes from the recorded time-response plot. (1.5 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Q2.** Why is the Ultimate Cycle Method **not suitable for integrating processes** (e.g., level control in a tank with no outflow pump)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In an integrating process, the open-loop transfer function contains a pole at the origin ($\frac{1}{s}$ factor). (1 mark)
When a pure P-controller is applied, the closed-loop system cannot produce *bounded sustained oscillations* of constant amplitude — instead, the output either **drifts linearly to saturation** or **oscillates with continuously growing amplitude** even for very small values of $K_p$. (1 mark)
Hence a meaningful $K_u$ and $P_u$ cannot be experimentally identified, making the method inapplicable. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure** of the Ultimate Cycle Method for tuning a PID controller. Mention the conditions under which the method fails. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A process is tuned using the Ultimate Cycle Method. The recorded critical values are $K_u = 8$ and $P_u = 6$ min. Calculate the recommended **PI** and **PID** settings using the standard Ziegler-Nichols correlations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step-by-step procedure (7 marks):**
1. Ensure the process is at **open-loop steady state** with the controller in **manual mode**. (1 mark)
2. Switch the controller to **automatic P-mode**. Set $T_i = \infty$ (integral action disabled) and $T_d = 0$ (derivative action disabled). (1 mark)
3. Initialize $K_p$ to a small value (e.g., 0.5 or 1). Apply a **small step change in set-point** and observe the response. (1 mark)
4. Gradually increase $K_p$ in small increments (e.g., 10%–20% steps). After each increment, disturb the loop and observe the time response. (1 mark)
5. Identify the **critical gain $K_u$** at which the response becomes a **sustained oscillation of constant amplitude**. (1 mark)
6. Measure the corresponding period $P_u$ from the time-response chart. (1 mark)
7. Substitute $K_u$ and $P_u$ into the Z-N correlation table for the desired controller type (P, PI, or PID). (1 mark)

**Conditions where the method fails:**
- Integrating processes (level without outflow).
- Open-loop unstable processes.
- Processes with significant measurement noise.
- Processes with strong actuator non-linearities (dead-band, saturation).

**Part (b) — Numerical computation (7 marks):**

For **PI controller**:
$$
K_p = 0.45 \, K_u = 0.45 \times 8 = 3.6 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$
$$
T_i = \frac{P_u}{1.2} = \frac{6}{1.2} = 5 \text{ min} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$
(Total PI part: 3.5 marks)

For **PID controller**:
$$
K_p = 0.6 \, K_u = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8 \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$
$$
T_i = 0.5 \, P_u = 0.5 \times 6 = 3 \text{ min} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$
$$
T_d = 0.125 \, P_u = 0.125 \times 6 = 0.75 \text{ min} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$
(Total PID part: 3.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to write $K_p = 0.6$ for PI (forgetting that the coefficient differs for PI and PID — it is **0.45** for PI and **0.6** for PID). Also, students often confuse $T_i$ with $K_i$ (integral gain). The Z-N correlations output **reset time $T_i$** (minutes/seconds), **not** $K_i$ (1/minutes). If the controller uses $K_i$, then $K_i = 1 / T_i$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question — Alternative Choice]**
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Derive the **closed-loop characteristic equation** for a unity-feedback system with a P-controller of gain $K_p$ and process transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$. Determine whether the **Ultimate Cycle Method** can be applied to this process and justify. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A temperature control loop on a chemical reactor is tuned using the Ultimate Cycle Method. The critical values obtained are $K_u = 4.5$ and $P_u = 8$ min. The operator chooses a **PI controller**. Calculate the controller settings and write the final **parallel-form PI control law** $u(t)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Characteristic equation derivation (7 marks):**

Closed-loop characteristic equation:
$$
1 + K_p \, G(s) = 0
$$

Substituting $G(s)$:
$$
1 + \frac{K_p}{s(s+1)(s+5)} = 0
$$

Multiplying through:
$$
s(s+1)(s+5) + K_p = 0
$$

Expanding:
$$
s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + K_p = 0
$$

**Stability check using Routh-Hurwitz for sustained oscillations:**

The Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $5$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $K_p$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{30 - K_p}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K_p$ | |

For marginal stability, the $s^1$ row must be zero:
$$
\frac{30 - K_p}{6} = 0 \implies K_p = 30
$$

So $K_u = 30$. The auxiliary equation from the $s^2$ row:
$$
6s^2 + K_p = 0 \implies 6s^2 + 30 = 0 \implies s^2 = -5 \implies s = \pm j\sqrt{5}
$$

Hence $\omega_u = \sqrt{5}$ rad/s, and:
$$
P_u = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 2.81 \text{ s}
$$

**Can the method be applied? (1 mark)**
**Yes.** The process is open-loop stable and does not contain a pure integrator ($\frac{1}{s}$) as a standalone term — the $\frac{1}{s}$ is balanced by stable first-order poles. The system has three poles in the LHP, so sustained oscillations are physically achievable. The Z-N correlations are therefore valid.

**Part (b) — PI controller settings and control law (7 marks):**

Given $K_u = 4.5$, $P_u = 8$ min.

**PI Settings:**
$$
K_p = 0.45 \times 4.5 = 2.025 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$
$$
T_i = \frac{P_u}{1.2} = \frac{8}{1.2} = 6.667 \text{ min} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Parallel-form PI control law:**
$$
u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \right]
$$

Substituting values:
$$
u(t) = 2.025 \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{6.667} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{u(t) = 2.025 \, e(t) + 0.304 \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to **explicitly write the final numerical control law** after calculating $K_p$ and $T_i$. The examiner will deduct at least 2 marks if the answer ends with just "$K_p = 2.025$" without the integrated control law expression. Always end with the **full equation** in its expanded form.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Ultimate Cycle Method** is a **closed-loop empirical tuning technique** that uses sustained oscillations to identify the plant dynamics.
- It is applicable **only to stable, open-loop minimum-phase, non-integrating processes**.
- The **Ultimate Gain $K_u$** is the proportional gain at which the closed-loop system is **marginally stable** (output oscillates at constant amplitude).
- The **Ultimate Period $P_u$** is the time period of these sustained oscillations, read directly from the recorder.
- Always disable integral and derivative actions before conducting the test ($T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$).
- The **standard Z-N correlations** for a **PID** controller are: $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$.
- The **standard Z-N correlations** for a **PI** controller are: $K_p = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = P_u / 1.2$.
- The **standard Z-N correlation** for a **P-only** controller is: $K_p = 0.5 K_u$.
- The classic Z-N criterion (Quarter-Amplitude Decay) yields **~25% overshoot** — not suitable if zero-overshoot is mandated.
- The method is **inapplicable** to integrating processes (level control), open-loop unstable processes, and highly noisy measurement systems.
- For PID implementations, be careful about **parallel (non-interacting)** vs. **series (interacting)** forms — the conversion formulas are $K_p' = K_p(1 + T_d/T_i)$, $T_i' = T_i + T_d$, $T_d' = T_i T_d / (T_i + T_d)$.
- The relationship $P_u = 2\pi / \omega_u$ is a recurring KTU question — make sure to convert rad/s to seconds/minutes correctly.
- Always **end the answer with the final numerical values** in either tabulated form or in the full controller equation — this is the most-skipped step in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Damped Oscillation Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Damped Oscillation Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Damped Oscillation Method** (also widely known as the **Closed-Loop Tuning Method** or **Ziegler–Nichols Second Method – Quarter Amplitude Decay Variant**) is an *in-situ, online* controller tuning technique used to determine the optimal Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) parameters of a feedback controller by deliberately exciting the closed-loop system into a controlled, decaying sinusoidal response and observing the resulting oscillation period and gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The Damped Oscillation Method is a closed-loop experimental tuning procedure in which the controller is operated in **P-mode only**, the proportional gain is increased until the system response exhibits a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio** (successive peak amplitudes in the ratio **1 : 4**), and the corresponding **ultimate gain $K_u$** and **ultimate period $P_u$** are recorded. The PID parameters are then computed using empirical Ziegler–Nichols correlations.

The mathematical signature of a damped oscillation in a second-order underdamped system is given by:

$$
y(t) = Y_{ss} \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \, e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \sin\left( \omega_d t + \phi \right) \right]
$$

where $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio**, $\omega_n$ is the **natural frequency**, and $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Wobbly Shopping Cart"**
> Imagine pushing a faulty shopping cart that has a wobbly wheel. If you push it **very gently**, it rolls smoothly. If you push **too hard**, it goes completely out of control and crashes. The Damped Oscillation Method is the technique of finding that *just-right* push strength where the cart oscillates and gradually settles down (decaying wobble). You measure how long one full wobble takes, and from that you back-calculate how to push it perfectly so it neither wobbles nor crashes.

In control engineering terms, the "push" is the **proportional gain $K_c$**, the "wobble" is the **damped oscillation** of the process variable, and the "settling time" tells us how to choose $K_p$, $T_i$, and $T_d$.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $K_c$ | Critical (Ultimate) Gain | dimensionless |
| $P_u$ | Ultimate Period of Oscillation | **seconds (s)** |
| $\omega_n$ | Natural Frequency | **rad/s** |
| $\zeta$ | Damping Ratio | dimensionless |
| $\omega_d$ | Damped Natural Frequency | **rad/s** |
| $K_p, T_i, T_d$ | PID Parameters | -, **s**, **s** |

> [!TIP]
> The ratio of successive peaks in a **quarter-amplitude decay** response is exactly **4 : 1**, which corresponds to a damping ratio of approximately $\zeta \approx 0.215$. This is a KTU-favorite numerical fact.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Principle — Why Damped Oscillations Occur

When a closed-loop control system has its feedback loop closed with a **purely proportional controller**, the closed-loop transfer function of a typical second-order process is:

$$
G_{CL}(s) = \frac{K_c \, G_p(s)}{1 + K_c \, G_p(s) \, H(s)}
$$

For a second-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{(T_1 s + 1)(T_2 s + 1)}$ with unity feedback, the closed-loop characteristic equation becomes:

$$
1 + K_c \, G_p(s) = 0
$$

The **roots** of this characteristic equation dictate the response:
- If roots are **real and negative** → **overdamped** (no oscillation)
- If roots are **complex conjugates** → **underdamped** (damped oscillation)
- If roots are **purely imaginary** → **sustained oscillation** (marginally stable)

The Damped Oscillation Method deliberately operates in the **underdamped** region.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure

1. **Place the controller in P-mode only** — set $T_i = \infty$ (integral action OFF) and $T_d = 0$ (derivative action OFF).
2. **Introduce a small set-point disturbance** or wait for a load change.
3. **Gradually increase the proportional gain $K_c$** in small increments.
4. **Observe the closed-loop response** on a trend recorder or SCADA.
5. **Stop increasing $K_c$** when the response exhibits a **quarter-amplitude decay** — i.e., the ratio of the second peak to the first peak is **1 : 4**.
6. **Record the gain** at this point as the **ultimate (critical) gain $K_u$** (sometimes denoted $K_{co}$ or $K_{max}$).
7. **Measure the period** between two successive peaks of the damped oscillation; call this the **ultimate period $P_u$** (also denoted $T_u$ or $T_g$).
8. **Apply the Ziegler–Nichols tuning rules** to compute $K_p$, $T_i$, and $T_d$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Quarter-Amplitude Decay?** This criterion gives a compromise between **fast response** and **acceptable overshoot (~50%)**, providing a good "starting point" for fine-tuning. It corresponds to a damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.215$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Controller Type | Proportional Gain $K_p$ | Integral Time $T_i$ | Derivative Time $T_d$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **P-only** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{1.2}$ | — |
| **PID** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{2}$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{8}$ |

**Standard Second-Order Relationships**

$$
\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} \qquad \qquad P_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d}
$$

$$
\zeta = \frac{-\ln(\text{Decay Ratio})}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + \ln^2(\text{Decay Ratio})}} \qquad \text{where Decay Ratio} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}
$$

For **quarter-amplitude decay** $A_2/A_1 = 1/4$:

$$
\ln(1/4) = -1.386 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = \frac{1.386}{\sqrt{\pi^2 + 1.386^2}} \approx 0.215
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Petrochemical refineries**: tuning furnace temperature loops
> - **HVAC systems**: tuning airflow/pressure loops in clean rooms
> - **Power plants**: tuning boiler drum-level and steam pressure controllers
> - **Process plants** (KTU Industrial Visits): tuning level, flow, and temperature loops in DCS

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Quarter-Amplitude Decay Damping Ratio

The response of a second-order underdamped system to a step input is:

$$
y(t) = 1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \sin\left( \omega_d t + \phi \right)
$$

The successive peak amplitudes occur at $t = n P_u$ where $P_u = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$.

**Step 1:** Evaluate the envelope at the $n$-th peak.

The envelope of the oscillation is:

$$
A(t) = \frac{e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio of the second peak to the first peak.

$$
\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{A(P_u)}{A(0)} = e^{-\zeta \omega_n P_u}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $P_u = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.

$$
\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \exp\left[ -\zeta \omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \right] = \exp\left[ \frac{-2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the quarter-amplitude condition $\dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{1}{4}$.

$$
\frac{1}{4} = \exp\left[ \frac{-2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \right]
$$

**Step 5:** Take natural logarithm of both sides.

$$
\ln\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{-2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -1.3863 = \frac{-2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}
$$

**Step 6:** Square both sides.

$$
1.9218 = \frac{4\pi^2 \zeta^2}{1 - \zeta^2}
$$

**Step 7:** Cross-multiply and solve for $\zeta$.

$$
1.9218 (1 - \zeta^2) = 4\pi^2 \zeta^2
$$

$$
1.9218 = \zeta^2 (1.9218 + 4\pi^2)
$$

$$
\zeta^2 = \frac{1.9218}{1.9218 + 39.4784} = \frac{1.9218}{41.4002}
$$

$$
\zeta = \sqrt{0.04642} \approx 0.2155
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Quarter-amplitude decay corresponds to $\boxed{\zeta \approx 0.215}$, which is a **high-yield KTU value**.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A temperature control loop is tuned using the Damped Oscillation Method. The P-only gain is increased until the system shows quarter-amplitude decay. The critical gain is found to be $K_u = 8.0$ and the period of oscillation is $P_u = 120$ seconds. Design a **PID controller** using the Ziegler–Nichols tuning rules.

**Step 1:** Identify given values.

$$
K_u = 8.0, \qquad P_u = 120 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Z–N PID rule for proportional gain.

$$
K_p = 0.6 \, K_u = 0.6 \times 8.0 = 4.8
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Z–N PID rule for integral time.

$$
T_i = \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Z–N PID rule for derivative time.

$$
T_d = \frac{P_u}{8} = \frac{120}{8} = 15 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5:** Express the PID controller transfer function.

$$
G_c(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} + T_d \, s \right) = 4.8 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{60s} + 15s \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Express in parallel form for a practical controller.

$$
G_c(s) = 4.8 + \frac{4.8}{60s} + 4.8 \times 15 \, s = 4.8 + \frac{0.08}{s} + 72 \, s
$$

$$
\boxed{K_p = 4.8, \quad T_i = 60 \text{ s}, \quad T_d = 15 \text{ s}}
$$

## 3.3 Decay Ratio and Successive Peaks Derivation

For an underdamped second-order response with successive peaks $A_1, A_2, A_3, \dots$:

$$
\frac{A_{n+1}}{A_n} = e^{-2\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \equiv \text{Decay Ratio (DR)}
$$

For quarter-amplitude decay:

$$
\text{DR} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

Number of peaks to decay to 2% of first peak:

$$
N = \frac{\ln(0.02)}{\ln(0.25)} = \frac{-3.912}{-1.386} \approx 2.82 \approx 3 \text{ cycles}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram for the Damped Oscillation Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    ref[Setpoint R(s)] --> sum1((+))
    dist[Load Disturbance D(s)] --> sum1
    sum1 --> ctrl[P-Controller Kc]
    ctrl --> valve[Final Control Element]
    valve --> proc[Process Plant Gp(s)]
    proc --> out[Process Variable Y(s)]
    out --> sensor[Measurement H(s)]
    sensor --> fb[Feedback Signal]
    fb --> sum1
    out --> plot[Trend Recorder]
    plot --> obs{Damped Oscillation? 1:4 ratio}
    obs --> no[Increase Kc further]
    obs --> yes[Record Ku and Pu]
    no --> ctrl
    yes --> compute[Apply Z-N Formulas]
    compute --> pid[Final PID Settings]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart of the Damped Oscillation Tuning Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Start: Put Controller in P-mode] --> stepB[Set Ti = infinity, Td = 0]
    stepB --> stepC[Apply small step in setpoint]
    stepC --> stepD[Observe response: overdamped or oscillating]
    stepD --> stepE{Decay ratio = 1:4?}
    stepE --> stepF[No: Increase Kc and repeat]
    stepF --> stepC
    stepE --> stepG[Yes: Record Ku and Pu]
    stepG --> stepH[Choose Controller Type P / PI / PID]
    stepH --> stepI[Apply Z-N tuning formulas]
    stepI --> stepJ[Implement PID and verify response]
    stepJ --> stepK[Fine-tune if necessary]
```

## 4.3 Step Response Showing Quarter-Amplitude Decay

```mermaid
graph LR
    t1[Time Axis t] --> p1[Peak A1 = 100%]
    t1 --> p2[Peak A2 = 25%]
    t1 --> p3[Peak A3 = 6.25%]
    t1 --> p4[Peak A4 = 1.56%]
    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each successive peak is $\frac{1}{4}$ of the previous one. The horizontal distance between any two consecutive peaks equals the **ultimate period $P_u$**.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Closed-Loop Tuning Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    layer1[Layer 1: Plant & Sensors] --> layer2[Layer 2: P-Controller in Auto Mode]
    layer2 --> layer3[Layer 3: Gain Sweep Kc]
    layer3 --> layer4[Layer 4: Response Observation]
    layer4 --> layer5[Layer 5: Identify Ku and Pu]
    layer5 --> layer6[Layer 6: Empirical Z-N Mapping]
    layer6 --> layer7[Layer 7: Deploy Kp, Ti, Td]
    layer7 --> layer8[Layer 8: Closed-Loop Verification]
```
