# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Define the Damped Oscillation Method of controller tuning. Mention any two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
The Damped Oscillation Method is a closed-loop experimental tuning technique in which the controller is operated in **P-mode only**. The proportional gain is gradually increased until the system response exhibits a **quarter-amplitude decay** (successive peak ratio = **1 : 4**). The corresponding **ultimate gain $K_u$** and **ultimate period $P_u$** are noted, and PID parameters are calculated using Ziegler–Nichols empirical correlations.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. It is performed on the **actual operating plant**; hence it accounts for real nonlinearities and load disturbances.
2. The procedure is **simple and systematic**, requiring only a P-controller and trend recording.
3. It gives a **good starting point** for fine-tuning of PID controllers.
4. It is suitable for processes where **open-loop tests are impractical** (e.g., integrating or unstable processes).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`**
**Q: What is meant by quarter-amplitude decay? What is the corresponding damping ratio?**

**Model Answer:**
Quarter-amplitude decay refers to a closed-loop response in which the amplitude of each successive peak is **one-fourth** of the previous peak (i.e., decay ratio = **1 : 4** or 0.25).

For a second-order underdamped system, this criterion corresponds to a damping ratio of:

$$
\zeta = \frac{-\ln(0.25)}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \ln^2(0.25)}} = \frac{1.386}{\sqrt{39.48 + 1.92}} \approx 0.215
$$

This value gives a response with moderate overshoot (~50%) and reasonable settling time, forming the basis of the Ziegler–Nichols tuning rules.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure of the **Damped Oscillation Method** for tuning a PID controller. Include the criteria used for identifying the critical gain and ultimate period. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process control loop is tuned using the Damped Oscillation Method. The P-mode gain is gradually increased and the critical gain at which quarter-amplitude decay is observed is $K_u = 6.0$. The corresponding period of oscillation is $P_u = 90$ seconds. Calculate the **PID controller settings** using Ziegler–Nichols tuning rules. Verify whether the resulting response will be acceptable for a process with allowable overshoot of 20%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Controller Configuration:** Place the controller in **P-mode only** — disable integral action ($T_i = \infty$) and derivative action ($T_d = 0$).

2. **Initial Setup:** Operate the loop in automatic mode with a small value of $K_c$. Introduce a small set-point step or wait for a load disturbance.

3. **Gain Sweep:** Gradually increase $K_c$ in small increments. After each increment, observe the closed-loop response on a trend recorder.

4. **Identification of Critical Point:** The moment the response exhibits a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio** ($A_2 / A_1 = 1/4$), record:
   - The **critical gain $K_u$** (value of $K_c$ at this point)
   - The **ultimate period $P_u$** (time between two consecutive peaks)

5. **Controller Selection:** Choose the desired controller type — P, PI, or PID.

6. **Apply Z–N Empirical Formulas:**

$$
\text{P:} \; K_p = 0.5 K_u
$$

$$
\text{PI:} \; K_p = 0.45 K_u, \; T_i = P_u/1.2
$$

$$
\text{PID:} \; K_p = 0.6 K_u, \; T_i = P_u/2, \; T_d = P_u/8
$$

7. **Implementation:** Switch the controller to PID-mode with the calculated values and verify the response.

8. **Fine-Tuning:** Adjust $K_p$, $T_i$, $T_d$ around the calculated values to meet the desired performance criteria.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
$$
K_u = 6.0, \qquad P_u = 90 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 1 — Proportional Gain:**

$$
K_p = 0.6 \times K_u = 0.6 \times 6.0 = 3.6
$$

**Step 2 — Integral Time:**

$$
T_i = \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{90}{2} = 45 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivative Time:**

$$
T_d = \frac{P_u}{8} = \frac{90}{8} = 11.25 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Controller Transfer Function:**

$$
G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right) = 3.6 \left(1 + \frac{1}{45 s} + 11.25 s \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Overshoot Verification:**

Z–N tuned PID gives a damping ratio of $\zeta \approx 0.215$, which produces an overshoot of approximately:

$$
\%OS = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi (0.215)/\sqrt{1-0.215^2}} \approx 50.4\%
$$

Since the **actual overshoot (~50%) exceeds the allowable 20%**, the Z–N settings are **not directly acceptable** and require fine-tuning. The recommended adjustments are:
- **Reduce $K_p$** to $\sim 1.8$ (half the Z–N value)
- **Increase $T_i$** to $\sim 90$ s
- **Reduce $T_d$** to $\sim 5$ s

These adjustments will raise $\zeta$ towards 0.6–0.7, giving an overshoot around 5–10%, which meets the 20% requirement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **closed-loop response** observed during the Damped Oscillation Method. Define the terms **decay ratio**, **ultimate gain**, and **ultimate period**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A level control loop on a chemical reactor is tuned using the Damped Oscillation Method. The ultimate gain is $K_u = 4.5$ and the ultimate period is $P_u = 60$ s. Determine the **PI controller settings** using Z–N rules. If the closed-loop process transfer function is approximated as a second-order system with $\omega_n = 0.1$ rad/s, determine the **resulting damping ratio** and the **percentage overshoot** for the Z–N tuned PI controller. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Closed-Loop Response Sketch Description:**

The time response shows the process variable starting from an initial steady-state value, overshooting the set-point to a **first peak $A_1$**, undershooting, then rising to a **second peak $A_2$**, and so on. Each successive peak is smaller in amplitude, producing a **decaying sinusoidal envelope** until the response settles at the set-point.

**Definitions:**

- **Decay Ratio (DR):** The ratio of the amplitudes of two successive peaks of the same sign, i.e., $DR = A_2 / A_1$. In the Damped Oscillation Method, $DR = 1/4 = 0.25$.

- **Ultimate Gain ($K_u$):** The value of the proportional-only controller gain at which the system response just exhibits the desired decay ratio (or sustained oscillation in the ultimate cycle method). It represents the limit of stable P-control.

- **Ultimate Period ($P_u$):** The time interval between two consecutive peaks of the damped oscillation, also called the **period of sustained oscillation** at the stability limit.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
$$
K_u = 4.5, \quad P_u = 60 \text{ s}, \quad \omega_n = 0.1 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 1 — PI Controller Settings (Z–N):**

$$
K_p = 0.45 \times K_u = 0.45 \times 4.5 = 2.025
$$

$$
T_i = \frac{P_u}{1.2} = \frac{60}{1.2} = 50 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Damped Natural Frequency from $P_u$:**

$$
\omega_d = \frac{2\pi}{P_u} = \frac{2\pi}{60} = 0.1047 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Damping Ratio:**

Since $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:

$$
\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = \frac{\omega_d}{\omega_n} = \frac{0.1047}{0.1} = 1.047
$$

$$
1-\zeta^2 = 1.096 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta^2 = -0.096
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ Critical Insight — Examiner's Pitfall:** A negative $\zeta^2$ is **physically impossible**, indicating that the given $\omega_n = 0.1$ rad/s is **inconsistent** with $P_u = 60$ s. The correct $\omega_n$ should be $\omega_n = \dfrac{\omega_d}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ with assumed $\zeta \approx 0.215$:

$$
\omega_n = \frac{0.1047}{\sqrt{1-0.215^2}} = \frac{0.1047}{0.9766} \approx 0.1072 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Overshoot (with corrected $\zeta = 0.215$):**

$$
\%OS = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi(0.215)/0.9766}
$$

$$
\%OS = 100 \cdot e^{-0.6915} \approx 100 \times 0.5008 \approx 50.1\%
$$

**Final PI Settings:**

$$
\boxed{K_p = 2.025, \quad T_i = 50 \text{ s}, \quad \text{Overshoot} \approx 50\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to disable integral and derivative actions** before starting the test. The controller MUST be in P-mode only.
> 2. **Confusing $K_u$ with $K_p$:** $K_u$ is the *critical* gain at quarter-amplitude decay; $K_p$ is the *final* gain after applying Z–N correlations.
> 3. **Ignoring units of $P_u$ and $T_i$:** Both must be in **seconds**.
> 4. **Assuming Z–N settings are final:** Z–N gives a starting point; the **quarter-amplitude decay yields ~50% overshoot**, which is often unacceptable in practice.
> 5. **Sign errors in $\ln(1/4)$:** It is $-1.386$, not $+1.386$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **Damped Oscillation Method** = closed-loop tuning in **P-mode only**.
> - **Quarter-amplitude decay ratio** = $1 : 4 \Rightarrow \zeta \approx 0.215$.
> - **Ultimate gain $K_u$** is the P-gain at which the system exhibits this decay.
> - **Ultimate period $P_u$** is the time between two consecutive peaks.
> - **Z–N PID:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = P_u / 2$, $T_d = P_u / 8$.
> - **Z–N PI:** $K_p = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = P_u / 1.2$.
> - **Z–N P:** $K_p = 0.5 K_u$.
> - **Z–N tuned response gives ~50% overshoot** — always fine-tune for tighter specifications.
> - **Decay Ratio formula:** $DR = e^{-2\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
> - **Overshoot formula:** $\%OS = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
> - **Damped frequency:** $\omega_d = 2\pi / P_u = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
> - **Method assumes** the plant is **stable and open-loop stable**, with no integrator in the loop.
> - **Integral action must be disabled** ($T_i = \infty$); **derivative action disabled** ($T_d = 0$).
> - **Procedure is online** — the plant is in operation during tuning.
> - **Suitable for:** temperature, level, pressure, and composition loops.
> - **Not suitable for:** integrating processes, open-loop unstable systems, or processes with severe time delay > process time constant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Response Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Response Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Compliant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency Response Method** is a classical analysis technique used to study the steady-state behaviour of a linear time-invariant (LTI) control system when subjected to a sinusoidal input signal of varying frequency. It characterises the system by its magnitude ratio (gain) and phase shift as a function of the input frequency $\omega$.

Mathematically, if the input to a stable, linear process is:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t)$$

then, after the transient response dies out, the steady-state output is:

$$y_{ss}(t) = Y \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

where:
- $Y$ = amplitude of the steady-state output
- $\phi$ = phase shift (phase lag, since output lags input in a typical process)
- $\omega$ = angular frequency in **rad/s**

The transfer function evaluated at $s = j\omega$ completely defines the frequency response:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{Y(j\omega)}{X(j\omega)} = \vert G(j\omega) \vert \angle G(j\omega)$$

where:
- $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ = **Magnitude Ratio (MR)** or **Amplitude Ratio (AR)**
- $\angle G(j\omega) = \phi(\omega)$ = **Phase Angle**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The frequency response is essentially the **transfer function evaluated on the imaginary axis** $s = j\omega$. The s-plane's imaginary axis is the boundary between stability and instability, making frequency response a powerful tool for stability studies.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **shaking the end of a long rope tied to a pole**, and you watch the wave that travels down the rope:

1. If you shake it **very slowly** (low frequency), the wave travels down the rope with almost the same amplitude and no noticeable delay. The rope "follows" your hand perfectly.

2. If you shake it **very fast** (high frequency), the wave becomes very small (amplitude dies down) and there is a noticeable **time lag** — by the time the wave reaches the other end, your hand has already moved on to the next shake.

3. The **rope is a system**, your hand motion is the **input**, and the motion at the far end is the **output**. The way the rope attenuates and delays signals of different frequencies is its **frequency response**.

> In process control, a **temperature sensor** placed in a stirred tank behaves the same way:
> - It tracks slow temperature changes (low $\omega$) faithfully.
> - It attenuates and lags rapid changes (high $\omega$).
> The frequency response method quantifies exactly *how much* attenuation and *how much* delay occurs at every frequency.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Angular frequency | $\omega$ | **rad/s** |
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hz** (with $\omega = 2\pi f$) |
| Magnitude Ratio | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | dimensionless (or dB) |
| Decibel Gain | $A_{dB}$ | **dB** ($20 \log_{10} \vert G \vert$) |
| Phase angle | $\phi$ | **degrees** or **radians** |
| Resonant frequency | $\omega_r$ | rad/s |
| Bandwidth | $\omega_b$ | rad/s |
| Gain Margin | $GM$ | dB |
| Phase Margin | $PM$ | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **decibel** is universally used in frequency response plots. A magnitude of $1 \Rightarrow 0$ dB, a magnitude of $10 \Rightarrow 20$ dB, and a magnitude of $0.1 \Rightarrow -20$ dB.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Use the Frequency Response Method?

The frequency response method is preferred over the time-domain (transient) method for several compelling reasons:

- **Stability Assessment Without Root Finding:** Stability margins (gain & phase margin) are read directly from Bode/Nyquist plots — no need to compute closed-loop poles.
- **Robustness Insight:** It quantifies how close a system is to instability.
- **Design Tuning:** Controllers (P, PI, PID) can be designed by shaping the open-loop frequency response.
- **Noise Rejection:** High-frequency roll-off indicates how well the system filters measurement noise.
- **Experimental Identification:** Easy to obtain from physical tests on a real process (sinusoidal tests, pulse tests, PRBS).

> [!NOTE]
> A sinusoidal input of any shape can be constructed using **Fourier synthesis** of sinusoids. Therefore, knowing the response to one sinusoid at every frequency allows prediction of the response to *any* periodic input.

---

## 2.2 The Sinusoidal Forcing Function — Step-by-Step Logic

For a linear system with transfer function $G(s)$, an input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ has Laplace transform $X(s) = \dfrac{X\omega}{s^{2}+\omega^{2}}$.

The output in s-domain is:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot \frac{X\omega}{s^{2}+\omega^{2}}$$

Using partial fractions:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot \left[ \frac{X\omega/2}{s-j\omega} + \frac{X\omega/2}{s+j\omega} \right]$$

After inverse Laplace transform, the **steady-state** portion (the term oscillating at $\omega$) yields:

$$y_{ss}(t) = X \cdot \vert G(j\omega) \vert \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

> **Key Insight:** The system's response to a sinusoid is *another sinusoid of the same frequency* but with scaled amplitude and phase shift — provided the system is linear and stable.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G(j\omega) = \left.G(s)\right\vert_{s=j\omega}$ | Frequency response from transfer function |
| 2 | $MR = \vert G(j\omega) \vert = \sqrt{\{\text{Re}\}^{2}+\{\text{Im}\}^{2}}$ | Magnitude ratio |
| 3 | $\phi = \angle G(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}}{\text{Re}}\right)$ | Phase angle |
| 4 | $A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | Decibel gain |
| 5 | $f = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | Hz ↔ rad/s conversion |
| 6 | $\omega_{CO}$ | Gain crossover: $\vert G(j\omega_{CO}) \vert = 1$ (0 dB) |
| 7 | $\omega_{PC}$ | Phase crossover: $\angle G(j\omega_{PC}) = -180^{\circ}$ |
| 8 | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert}$ | Gain margin (numeric) |
| 9 | $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert$ | Gain margin in dB |
| 10 | $PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{CO})$ | Phase margin |
| 11 | $\omega_{BW}$ | Bandwidth: $\vert G(j\omega_{BW}) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of DC gain ($-3$ dB) |
| 12 | $M_{r}$ | Resonant peak (max of $\vert T(j\omega) \vert$ for closed-loop) |
| 13 | $\omega_{r}$ | Resonant frequency (where $M_{r}$ occurs) |
| 14 | $M_{r} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ | Resonant peak (2nd-order system) |
| 15 | $\omega_{r} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-2\zeta^{2}}$ | Resonant frequency (2nd-order) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** Memorize the substitutions $s = j\omega$ for each term of the transfer function:
> - $(1+sT) \rightarrow (1+j\omega T)$ — magnitude: $\sqrt{1+(\omega T)^{2}}$, phase: $\tan^{-1}(\omega T)$
> - $(1+sT)^{2} \rightarrow$ magnitude: $1+(\omega T)^{2}$, phase: $2\tan^{-1}(\omega T)$
> - $\dfrac{1}{s} \rightarrow \dfrac{1}{j\omega}$ — magnitude: $1/\omega$, phase: $-90^{\circ}$
> - $\dfrac{1}{s^{2}} \rightarrow -\dfrac{1}{\omega^{2}}$ — magnitude: $1/\omega^{2}$, phase: $-180^{\circ}$

---

## 2.4 Bode Plot — Magnitude & Phase vs Frequency

A **Bode plot** consists of two semi-log graphs:
- **Magnitude plot:** $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ in dB vs $\log_{10}\omega$
- **Phase plot:** $\angle G(j\omega) \vert$ in degrees vs $\log_{10}\omega$

The plot is constructed using **asymptotic approximations** (straight lines) and then corrected at corner frequencies.

| Term in $G(j\omega)$ | Slope of Asymptote (dB/decade) | Phase Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| Constant $K$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $K/s$ | $-20$ | $-90^{\circ}$ |
| $K/s^{2}$ | $-40$ | $-180^{\circ}$ |
| $(1+j\omega T)$ | $+20$ (high freq.) | $0 \rightarrow +90^{\circ}$ |
| $1/(1+j\omega T)$ | $-20$ (high freq.) | $0 \rightarrow -90^{\circ}$ |
| $(1+j\omega T)^{2}$ | $+40$ (high freq.) | $0 \rightarrow +180^{\circ}$ |

**Corner (Break) Frequency:** $\omega_{c} = \dfrac{1}{T}$

At the corner frequency, the actual magnitude is **3 dB above the asymptote**, and the phase is exactly half its final value.

---

## 2.5 Nyquist Plot — Polar Plot

A **Nyquist plot** traces $\vert G(j\omega) \vert e^{j\phi}$ in the complex plane as $\omega$ goes from $0$ to $\infty$ (and symmetry for $-\infty$ to $0$).

- Distance from origin = magnitude ratio.
- Angle from positive real axis = phase angle.
- The **critical point** is $(-1, j0)$.

> If the Nyquist contour encircles the $(-1, j0)$ point, the closed-loop system is **unstable**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Chemical plants** | Tuning PI controllers on distillation columns by bandwidth specification |
| **Aerospace** | Aircraft autopilot design — phase margin ensures robustness to gain variations |
| **Audio engineering** | Equaliser design — filters are characterised by their frequency response |
| **Biomedical** | Pacemaker response characterisation to varying heart signal frequencies |
| **Power systems** | Grid stability analysis under varying load oscillations |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Frequency Response of a First-Order System

> **Problem:** Find the frequency response of a first-order process with transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ in the transfer function.**

We replace the Laplace variable $s$ with the imaginary number $j\omega$ to get the frequency response function. This is the fundamental step that converts the s-domain description into a frequency-domain description.

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega\tau + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Rationalise by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate.**

To separate real and imaginary parts, multiply both the numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator, which is $1 - j\omega\tau$. This eliminates the imaginary term from the denominator.

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(1 - j\omega\tau)}{(1 + j\omega\tau)(1 - j\omega\tau)}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator.**

The denominator becomes the sum of squares because $(a+b)(a-b) = a^{2} - b^{2}$ with $a=1$ and $b=j\omega\tau$. Note that $(j\omega\tau)^{2} = -\omega^{2}\tau^{2}$, so subtracting a negative adds.

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(1 - j\omega\tau)}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Identify real and imaginary parts.**

Splitting the numerator reveals the real part as $K$ and the imaginary part as $-K\omega\tau$, both divided by the common real denominator $1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}$.

$$G(j\omega) = \underbrace{\frac{K}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}}_{\text{Real part}} \; - \; j\underbrace{\frac{K\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}}_{\text{Imaginary part}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the magnitude ratio.**

The magnitude is the square root of the sum of squares of the real and imaginary parts. After combining under one common denominator, the numerator simplifies to $K^{2}(1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2})$, and the square root cancels with the denominator.

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \sqrt{\left(\frac{K}{1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{K\omega\tau}{1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2}}\right)^{2}}$$

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}\sqrt{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the phase angle.**

The phase is the arctangent of the negative imaginary part divided by the real part. Both have the same positive denominator which cancels, leaving a simple ratio.

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{K\omega\tau/(1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2})}{K/(1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2})}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$$

### **Final First-Order Frequency Response Equations**

$$\boxed{\;\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2}}} \quad,\quad \phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)\;}$$

**Step 7 — Limiting cases (high-yield for KTU exams).**

- As $\omega \rightarrow 0$ (low frequency): $\vert G \vert \rightarrow K$ and $\phi \rightarrow 0^{\circ}$ → system tracks DC.
- As $\omega \rightarrow \infty$ (high frequency): $\vert G \vert \rightarrow K/(\omega\tau) \rightarrow 0$ and $\phi \rightarrow -90^{\circ}$ → system blocks high frequencies.
- At $\omega = 1/\tau$ (corner frequency): $\vert G \vert = K/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., $-3$ dB) and $\phi = -45^{\circ}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency Response of a Second-Order System

> **Problem:** For $G(s) = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$, derive $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ and $\phi(\omega)$.

**Step 1 — Substitute $s = j\omega$.**

Since $s^{2} = (j\omega)^{2} = -\omega^{2}$, the denominator takes a real and an imaginary part.

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{-\omega^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}(j\omega) + \omega_{n}^{2}} = \frac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{(\omega_{n}^{2} - \omega^{2}) + j(2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitude.**

Square the real and imaginary parts of the denominator, sum them, and take the square root. The numerator is real and positive, so it remains outside.

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{\sqrt{(\omega_{n}^{2}-\omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)^{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Express in normalised form by dividing numerator and denominator by $\omega_{n}^{2}$.**

The standard form makes it clear how the ratio $\omega/\omega_{n}$ (normalised frequency) controls the response.

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{\left[1-(\omega/\omega_{n})^{2}\right]^{2} + \left[2\zeta(\omega/\omega_{n})\right]^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the phase.**

The phase of the numerator is zero; the phase of the denominator is the arctangent of the imaginary over the real part, with a negative sign in front because the denominator is in the denominator.

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega}{\omega_{n}^{2}-\omega^{2}}\right) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta(\omega/\omega_{n})}{1-(\omega/\omega_{n})^{2}}\right)$$

### **Final Second-Order Frequency Response Equations**

$$\boxed{\;\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{\left[1-(\omega/\omega_{n})^{2}\right]^{2} + \left[2\zeta(\omega/\omega_{n})\right]^{2}}}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta(\omega/\omega_{n})}{1-(\omega/\omega_{n})^{2}}\right)\;}$$

**Step 5 — Resonant Peak and Resonant Frequency.**

To find $\omega_{r}$, differentiate $\vert G \vert^{2}$ with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero. Let $u = (\omega/\omega_{n})^{2}$.

$$\frac{d}{d\omega}\left[(1-u)^{2} + 4\zeta^{2}u\right] = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -2(1-u) + 4\zeta^{2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; u = 1 - 2\zeta^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\omega_{r} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-2\zeta^{2}} \quad (\text{real only if } \zeta < 1/\sqrt{2})\;}$$

Substituting back gives the resonant peak amplitude:

$$\boxed{\;M_{r} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Response Comparison Table

| Feature | First-Order | Second-Order (under-damped) | Pure Integrator | Pure Differentiator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transfer Function | $\dfrac{K}{\tau s+1}$ | $\dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2}+2\zeta\omega_{n}s+\omega_{n}^{2}}$ | $\dfrac{K}{s}$ | $Ks$ |
| Magnitude $\vert G \vert$ | $\dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2}}}$ | $\dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{\sqrt{(\omega_{n}^{2}-\omega^{2})^{2}+4\zeta^{2}\omega_{n}^{2}\omega^{2}}}$ | $\dfrac{K}{\omega}$ | $K\omega$ |
| Phase $\phi$ | $-\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$ | $-\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega}{\omega_{n}^{2}-\omega^{2}}\right)$ | $-90^{\circ}$ (constant) | $+90^{\circ}$ (constant) |
| Slope (high $\omega$, dB/dec) | $-20$ | $-40$ | $-20$ | $+20$ |
| Final Phase (high $\omega$) | $-90^{\circ}$ | $-180^{\circ}$ | $-90^{\circ}$ | $+90^{\circ}$ |
| Resonant Peak | None | $M_{r}=\dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ | None | None |
| Bandwidth ($-3$ dB) | $\omega_{b}=1/\tau$ | $\omega_{b}=\omega_{n}\sqrt{(1-2\zeta^{2})+\sqrt{4\zeta^{4}-4\zeta^{2}+2}}$ | $\omega_{b}=K/\sqrt{2}$ | $\omega_{b}=1/(\sqrt{2}K)$ |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Phase Margin and Gain Margin Definitions

Consider the open-loop transfer function $G(s)$ in a unity-feedback loop. Define:

**Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{CO}$:** The frequency at which the magnitude of $G(j\omega)$ is exactly unity (0 dB).

$$\vert G(j\omega_{CO}) \vert = 1$$

**Phase Margin $PM$:** The additional phase lag (beyond $-180^{\circ}$) at the gain crossover frequency required to bring the system to the verge of instability.

$$PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{CO})$$

- $PM > 0^{\circ}$ ⇒ **stable**
- $PM = 0^{\circ}$ ⇒ **marginally stable**
- $PM < 0^{\circ}$ ⇒ **unstable**

**Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{PC}$:** The frequency at which the phase of $G(j\omega)$ is exactly $-180^{\circ}$.

$$\angle G(j\omega_{PC}) = -180^{\circ}$$

**Gain Margin $GM$:** The factor by which the gain can be increased before the system becomes unstable.

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert} \quad \text{or} \quad GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb for Design:** A well-designed control system should have:
> - $PM \geq 30^{\circ}$ (typically $45^{\circ}$ to $60^{\circ}$)
> - $GM \geq 6$ dB (typically 8 to 12 dB)


**Description:** The frequency response of the **open-loop transfer function** $G_{OL}(s) = G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)H(s)$ is used to predict closed-loop behaviour. The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)}{1 + G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)H(s)}$$

For unity feedback ($H(s)=1$):

$$T(j\omega) = \frac{G(j\omega)}{1 + G(j\omega)}$$
