# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Define frequency response of a control system. Explain how magnitude ratio and phase angle are obtained from the transfer function.**

**Model Answer:**

The frequency response of a control system is the **steady-state response** of the system to a sinusoidal input of varying frequency $\omega$. It is obtained by substituting $s = j\omega$ in the open-loop transfer function $G(s)$.

If $G(j\omega) = R(\omega) + jI(\omega)$, then:
- **Magnitude Ratio (MR):** $MR = \vert G(j\omega) \vert = \sqrt{R^{2}(\omega) + I^{2}(\omega)}$
- **Phase Angle:** $\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{I(\omega)}{R(\omega)}\right)$

The frequency response is plotted as magnitude (dB) and phase (degrees) versus $\log_{10}\omega$ to obtain the **Bode plot**.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **What is the significance of gain margin and phase margin in control system design?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Gain Margin (GM):** It is the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased before the closed-loop system becomes unstable. It is measured at the **phase crossover frequency** $\omega_{PC}$ (where phase = $-180^{\circ}$):
$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert}$$

- **Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag at the **gain crossover frequency** $\omega_{CO}$ (where magnitude = 0 dB) that would cause instability:
$$PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{CO})$$

**Significance:** Both margins are measures of **relative stability** and **robustness**. Typical design specifications: $GM \geq 6$ dB and $PM \geq 30^{\circ}$ (preferably $45^{\circ}$–$60^{\circ}$).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** For the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{(s+1)(s+2)}$, derive the frequency response expressions for magnitude ratio and phase angle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Sketch the asymptotic Bode plot and determine the gain crossover frequency, phase crossover frequency, gain margin, and phase margin. Comment on the stability. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\omega$.

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{(j\omega+1)(j\omega+2)} = \frac{10}{(1+j\omega)(2+j\omega)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude.

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}}\cdot\sqrt{4+\omega^{2}}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the phase.

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega/2)$$

**Step 4:** Put in standard time-constant form (corner frequencies at $\omega=1$ and $\omega=2$ rad/s).

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10/2}{(1+j\omega)(1+j\omega/2)} = \frac{5}{(1+j\omega)(1+j\omega/2)}$$

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Asymptotes (dB):**
- Low-frequency magnitude = $20\log_{10}(5) = 13.98$ dB (flat)
- First corner at $\omega = 1$ rad/s: slope changes from $0$ to $-20$ dB/decade.
- Second corner at $\omega = 2$ rad/s: slope changes from $-20$ to $-40$ dB/decade.

**Phase asymptotes:**
- $0^{\circ}$ up to $\omega = 0.1$
- Decreases to $-45^{\circ}$ at $\omega = 1$
- $-90^{\circ}$ at $\omega = 2$ (sum of $-90^{\circ}$ and $0^{\circ}$ from each term — check)
- Asymptotically approaches $-180^{\circ}$ for $\omega \gg 2$.

**Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{CO}$:** Solve $\vert G(j\omega_{CO}) \vert = 1$.

$$\frac{5}{\sqrt{(1+\omega^{2})(4+\omega^{2})}} = 1$$

Square: $(1+\omega^{2})(4+\omega^{2}) = 25 \Rightarrow \omega^{4} + 5\omega^{2} + 4 = 25 \Rightarrow \omega^{4} + 5\omega^{2} - 21 = 0$

Let $x = \omega^{2}$: $x^{2} + 5x - 21 = 0 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{-5 + \sqrt{25 + 84}}{2} = \dfrac{-5 + \sqrt{109}}{2} \approx \dfrac{-5 + 10.44}{2} \approx 2.72$

$$\omega_{CO} \approx \sqrt{2.72} \approx 1.65 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Phase at $\omega_{CO}$:**

$$\phi(\omega_{CO}) = -\tan^{-1}(1.65) - \tan^{-1}(1.65/2) = -58.78^{\circ} - 39.52^{\circ} = -98.3^{\circ}$$

**Phase Margin:**

$$PM = 180^{\circ} + (-98.3^{\circ}) = 81.7^{\circ}$$

> This is an **excellent** phase margin — the system is **very stable**.

**Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{PC}$:** Solve $\phi = -180^{\circ}$.

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = 180^{\circ}$$

This never occurs for real positive $\omega$ since each term is at most $90^{\circ}$, sum is at most $180^{\circ}$, and only at $\omega \rightarrow \infty$. So:

$$\omega_{PC} \to \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \vert G(j\infty) \vert = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad GM = \infty$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to convert $G(s)$ into **time-constant form** (coefficient of $s$ should be 1 in each factor). Forgetting this step leads to wrong corner frequencies and a completely incorrect Bode plot. Also, students often confuse gain crossover (magnitude = 0 dB) with phase crossover (phase = $-180^{\circ}$).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction of a Nyquist plot. How is stability assessed from it? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A unity feedback system has open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{50}{s(1+0.1s)(1+0.5s)}$. Determine the gain margin and phase margin using the Bode plot. Comment on stability. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Nyquist Plot Construction:**

A Nyquist plot is a polar plot of $G(j\omega)$ in the complex plane (Re on x-axis, Im on y-axis) as $\omega$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NP["TYPICAL NYQUIST PLOT"]
        direction LR
        A1["Starts at infinity along -j axis as w approaches 0"]
        A2["Loops through quadrants"]
        A3["Terminates at origin for w = infinity"]
        CP["CRITICAL POINT -1 plus j0 marked for stability"]
    end

    style A1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CP fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Construction Steps:**

1. Compute $G(j\omega)$ for $\omega = 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, \infty$ rad/s.
2. For each $\omega$, find real part $R(\omega)$ and imaginary part $I(\omega)$.
3. Plot points $(R, I)$ and connect with a smooth curve.
4. Mirror the curve about the real axis for $\omega \in (-\infty, 0)$.

**Stability Assessment (Nyquist Criterion):**

$$Z = N + P$$

where:
- $Z$ = number of closed-loop poles in the right-half s-plane (instability indicator)
- $N$ = number of clockwise encirclements of the critical point $(-1, j0)$ by the Nyquist plot
- $P$ = number of open-loop poles in the right-half s-plane

**Stability condition:** $Z = 0$ for a stable closed-loop system. Equivalently, the Nyquist plot **must not encircle** $(-1, j0)$ for minimum-phase systems.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Standard time-constant form.

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{50}{j\omega(1+j0.1\omega)(1+j0.5\omega)}$$

Corner frequencies: $\omega_{c1} = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_{c2} = 2$ rad/s (rearranged in increasing order).

**Step 2:** Magnitude in dB.

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(50) - 20\log_{10}(\omega) - 20\log_{10}\sqrt{1+(0.1\omega)^{2}} - 20\log_{10}\sqrt{1+(0.5\omega)^{2}}$$

$20\log_{10}(50) = 33.98$ dB

**Asymptotic slopes:**
- $\omega < 2$ rad/s: slope = $-20$ dB/dec (integrator)
- $2 < \omega < 10$ rad/s: slope = $-40$ dB/dec
- $\omega > 10$ rad/s: slope = $-60$ dB/dec

**Step 3:** Gain Crossover Frequency.

In the middle segment (between $\omega = 2$ and $\omega = 10$):

Magnitude at $\omega = 2$ rad/s: $33.98 - 20\log_{10}(2) = 33.98 - 6.02 = 27.96$ dB.

From $\omega = 2$ to $\omega = \omega_{CO}$ with slope $-40$ dB/dec:

$$27.96 - 40\log_{10}(\omega_{CO}/2) = 0$$

$$40\log_{10}(\omega_{CO}/2) = 27.96 \Rightarrow \log_{10}(\omega_{CO}/2) = 0.699 \Rightarrow \omega_{CO}/2 = 5.0 \Rightarrow \omega_{CO} \approx 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4:** Phase Crossover Frequency.

$$\phi(\omega) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega)$$

For $\phi = -180^{\circ}$:

$$\tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) = 90^{\circ}$$

When $\tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) = 90^{\circ}$, we have:

$$(0.1\omega)(0.5\omega) = 1 \text{ (identity for } \tan^{-1}a + \tan^{-1}b = 90^{\circ})$$

$$0.05\omega^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow \omega_{PC} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.47 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude at $\omega_{PC}$.

$$\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert = \frac{50}{\omega_{PC}\sqrt{1+(0.1\omega_{PC})^{2}}\sqrt{1+(0.5\omega_{PC})^{2}}}$$

With $\omega_{PC} = 4.47$:
- $0.1 \times 4.47 = 0.447 \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+0.2} = \sqrt{1.2} = 1.095$
- $0.5 \times 4.47 = 2.236 \Rightarrow \sqrt{1+5} = \sqrt{6} = 2.449$

$$\vert G(j4.47) \vert = \frac{50}{4.47 \times 1.095 \times 2.449} = \frac{50}{11.99} \approx 4.17$$

$$GM = \frac{1}{4.17} \approx 0.24 \quad \text{or} \quad GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}(4.17) = -12.4 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6:** Phase at $\omega_{CO} = 10$ rad/s.

$$\phi(10) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(1) - \tan^{-1}(5) = -90^{\circ} - 45^{\circ} - 78.69^{\circ} = -213.69^{\circ}$$

$$PM = 180^{\circ} + (-213.69^{\circ}) = -33.69^{\circ}$$

**Stability Comment:** Both $GM < 0$ dB and $PM < 0^{\circ}$ indicate that the closed-loop system is **unstable**. To stabilise, the gain $K = 50$ must be reduced. The maximum stable gain occurs when $PM = 0$, which requires the gain crossover to be at a frequency where phase = $-180^{\circ}$. Since the original $PM$ is highly negative, the gain must be reduced by $12.4$ dB (factor of $\approx 4.17$) to bring the system to marginal stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to **swap the definitions** of gain crossover and phase crossover. Always remember:
> - **Gain crossover** = frequency where **magnitude** = 0 dB (used to find PM)
> - **Phase crossover** = frequency where **phase** = $-180^{\circ}$ (used to find GM)
> Also, students often compute GM as $1/\vert G \vert$ and forget the negative sign for dB. Correct: $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert$. If $\vert G \vert > 1$, then $GM_{dB} < 0$ (unstable).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Frequency Response Method**

- **Core Concept:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ in $G(s)$ to obtain $G(j\omega)$ — the frequency response function.

- **Two-Part Description:** Every frequency response is described by (i) **Magnitude Ratio (MR)** and (ii) **Phase Angle ($\phi$)**.

- **Standard First-Order Response:** $\vert G \vert = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2}}}$, $\phi = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$.

- **Standard Second-Order Response:** Magnitude shows a **resonant peak** $M_{r} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ at $\omega_{r} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-2\zeta^{2}}$ (only if $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$).

- **Bode Plot Construction:** Plot $20\log_{10}\vert G \vert$ (dB) and $\phi$ (degrees) vs $\log_{10}\omega$ using **asymptotic straight lines** with corner frequencies at $\omega = 1/T$.

- **Slope Rules (dB/decade):** Integrator $1/s \Rightarrow -20$; double integrator $1/s^{2} \Rightarrow -40$; first-order lag $\Rightarrow -20$ above corner; first-order lead $\Rightarrow +20$ above corner.

- **Phase Contributions:** Integrator $-90^{\circ}$ (constant); double integrator $-180^{\circ}$; first-order lag transitions from $0^{\circ}$ to $-90^{\circ}$; first-order lead transitions from $0^{\circ}$ to $+90^{\circ}$.

- **Gain Crossover $\omega_{CO}$:** Frequency where $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = 1$ (0 dB).

- **Phase Crossover $\omega_{PC}$:** Frequency where $\angle G(j\omega) = -180^{\circ}$.

- **Gain Margin $GM$:** $GM = 1/\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert$ (or in dB: $GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{PC}) \vert$). Positive $GM_{dB}$ → stable.

- **Phase Margin $PM$:** $PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{CO})$. Positive $PM$ → stable.

- **Design Targets:** $GM \geq 6$ dB (typically 8–12 dB); $PM \geq 30^{\circ}$ (typically 45°–60°).

- **Bandwidth $\omega_{BW}$:** Frequency where $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its DC value ($-3$ dB point). Higher bandwidth → faster response.

- **Nyquist Plot:** Polar plot of $G(j\omega)$; **stability** requires no encirclement of the critical point $(-1, j0)$ for minimum-phase systems.

- **Nyquist Stability Formula:** $Z = N + P$ — number of unstable closed-loop poles equals clockwise encirclements plus open-loop RHP poles.

- **Resonant Peak Significance:** Higher $M_{r}$ → more oscillatory closed-loop response. $M_{r} = 1.0$–$1.5$ is generally acceptable.

- **Practical Use:** Frequency response is the **cornerstone of process controller tuning** (Ziegler-Nichols, Tyreus-Luyben, Cohen-Coon all use frequency-domain insights).

- **Memory Trick:** "**G**ain crossover for **P**hase margin" — both start with consonants on the right side of the alphabet pair (G-P). "**P**hase crossover for **G**ain margin" — P-G.

- **Logarithmic Conversion:** To convert from dB to magnitude ratio, $MR = 10^{A_{dB}/20}$.

- **Angle Wrapping:** When computing phase from $\tan^{-1}$, be careful about the quadrant — use the **atan2** function, not just $\tan^{-1}$.

- **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting that a Type-1 system (one integrator) has phase starting at $-90^{\circ}$, not $0^{\circ}$.

- **High-Frequency Limit:** Most real processes have magnitude $\to 0$ as $\omega \to \infty$ — this guarantees stability for an open-loop system but not for the closed loop.

- **Minimum Phase Systems:** Systems with all poles and zeros in the left-half s-plane; their phase and magnitude are uniquely related by the Bode gain-phase relationship.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparing Tuning Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparing Tuning Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition
**Controller Tuning** is the procedure of adjusting the parameters of a PID controller (\(K_c\), \(\tau_I\), \(\tau_D\)) so that the closed-loop system achieves a desired balance between **stability, speed of response, disturbance rejection, and robustness** to plant variations. **Comparing Tuning Methods** is the systematic evaluation of different tuning rules (Ziegler–Nichols, Cohen–Coon, Tyreus–Luyben, Direct Synthesis, Internal Model Control, etc.) with respect to their closed-loop performance, model dependency, and robustness characteristics.

> [!NOTE]
> A tuning rule is a **deterministic mapping** from a (reduced-order) plant model — typically **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** defined by the triplet **process gain \(K_p\), time constant \(\tau\), and dead time \(t_d\)** — to PID settings. The comparison of methods is therefore a comparison of how each rule "interprets" this triplet.

## Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you are tuning a **car's cruise control** to maintain 60 km/h on a hilly road:
- **Aggressive tuning** (like Ziegler–Nichols) = flooring the accelerator when speed dips → very fast, but jerky and prone to overshoot.
- **Conservative tuning** (like Tyreus–Luyben) = smooth, gentle throttle → stable and robust but sluggish.
- **Model-based tuning** (like IMC/Direct Synthesis) = like a driver who has studied the road gradient map ahead and pre-plans throttle changes — needs a good "model" of the hill, but gives the best, smoothest ride.

Different methods = different **driving philosophies**, and the engineer's job is to pick the one that matches the process "road" being driven on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP-2020) emphasizes **outcome-based learning (OBE)**. The Course Outcomes for this module require students to (i) identify tuning rules, (ii) compute PID parameters, and (iii) **critically compare** the methods — i.e., *justify* the choice of one rule over another for a given process. Memorizing formulas alone is **not** sufficient; the comparison itself is a high-weightage (14-mark) examination theme.

## Standard Performance Metrics (used as the "yardsticks" for comparison)
- **IAE** = \(\int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert \, dt\) — Integral of Absolute Error
- **ISE** = \(\int_0^\infty e^2(t) \, dt\) — Integral of Squared Error
- **ITAE** = \(\int_0^\infty t \cdot \vert e(t) \vert \, dt\) — Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error
- **ITSE** = \(\int_0^\infty t \cdot e^2(t) \, dt\) — Integral of Time-weighted Squared Error
- **IMC-PID Specific Metric**: **MSD** (Maximum Sensitivity / Maximum of \(\vert 1 + GC \vert^{-1}\)) — the standard **robustness** measure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FOPDT Model — the Common Starting Point
Almost every classical tuning rule starts from the FOPDT plant:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-t_d \, s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

where \(K_p\) = process gain, \(\tau\) = open-loop time constant, \(t_d\) = dead time.

> [!NOTE]
> The dimensionless ratio \(\frac{t_d}{\tau}\) (sometimes written \(\theta / \tau\)) is the single most important number governing the **difficulty of control**. Small \(\frac{t_d}{\tau}\) ⇒ easy to control; large \(\frac{t_d}{\tau}\) ⇒ tuning rules diverge widely in their recommendations.

## 2.2 Categorization of Tuning Methods

1. **Open-loop (off-line) methods** — require a step test on the plant:
   - Cohen–Coon
   - Ziegler–Nichols Reaction Curve (open-loop ZN)
2. **Closed-loop (on-line) methods** — require finding the **ultimate gain \(K_u\)** and **ultimate period \(P_u\)**:
   - Ziegler–Nichols Continuous Cycling (closed-loop ZN)
   - Tyreus–Luyben
3. **Model-based analytical methods** — use the FOPDT model and an explicit performance/robustness criterion:
   - Direct Synthesis (DS)
   - Internal Model Control (IMC) — Rivera, Morari, Skogestad
   - SIMC (Skogestad's "Simple" IMC)
4. **Error-minimization (optimal) methods** — minimize an index (IAE, ISE, ITAE):
   - Lopez–Murrill–Smith (ISE / IAE)
   - Rovira–Murrill (ITAE setpoint regulation)
5. **Auto-tuning & intelligent methods** — relay-feedback (Åström–Hägglund), fuzzy, neural — typically referenced only in modern treatments.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Comparison Table

### PID Form Convention Used
Throughout KTU examinations, the **standard parallel (non-interacting) PID** is used:

$$G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a problem specifies a "series" or "cascade" form, you **must** first convert. KTU examiners commonly test this conversion (worth 2–3 marks inside a 14-mark question).

### Table 1 — Master Formula Sheet (All Major Rules)

| Tuning Rule | \(K_c\) | \(\tau_I\) | \(\tau_D\) | Type of Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ZN Open-Loop (Reaction Curve) | \(\dfrac{1.2\,\tau}{K_p\, t_d}\) | \(2\,t_d\) | \(0.5\,t_d\) | Step test |
| ZN Closed-Loop (Continuous Cycling) | \(0.6\,K_u\) | \(0.5\,P_u\) | \(0.125\,P_u\) | P-only to find \(K_u, P_u\) |
| P-only ZN | \(0.5\,K_u\) | \(\infty\) | \(0\) | — |
| PI ZN Closed-Loop | \(0.45\,K_u\) | \(0.8\,P_u\) | \(0\) | — |
| Cohen–Coon (PI) | \(\dfrac{\tau}{K_p\, t_d}\!\left(0.9 + \dfrac{t_d}{12\,\tau}\right)\) | \(t_d\!\left(\dfrac{30 + 3\,t_d/\tau}{9 + 20\,t_d/\tau}\right)\) | — | Step test |
| Cohen–Coon (PID) | \(\dfrac{\tau}{K_p\, t_d}\!\left(\dfrac{4}{3} + \dfrac{t_d}{4\,\tau}\right)\) | \(t_d\!\left(\dfrac{32 + 6\,t_d/\tau}{13 + 8\,t_d/\tau}\right)\) | \(\dfrac{4\,t_d}{11 + 2\,t_d/\tau}\) | Step test |
| Tyreus–Luyben (PI) | \(0.31\,K_u\) | \(2.2\,P_u\) | — | Closed-loop |
| Tyreus–Luyben (PID) | \(0.45\,K_u\) | \(2.2\,P_u\) | \(\dfrac{P_u}{6.3}\) | Closed-loop |
| IMC-PID (FOPDT, \(\lambda \ge 0.8\,t_d\)) | \(\dfrac{\tau}{K_p(\lambda + t_d)}\) | \(\tau\) | — (PI) / set per rule | Model-based |
| Direct Synthesis PI (\(\tau_c\) closed-loop time constant) | \(\dfrac{\tau}{K_p(\tau_c + t_d)}\) | \(\tau\) | — | Model-based |
| SIMC (Skogestad) PI | \(\dfrac{\tau}{K_p(t_d + \tau_c)}\) | \(\min\!\left(\tau,\, 4(\tau_c + t_d)\right)\) | — | Model-based |

> [!WARNING]
> The pipe character `|` in \(\vert e(t) \vert\) has been replaced with `\vert` and `\min` used as a function — be careful when copying these expressions into your answer booklet.

## 2.4 Theoretical "Why" Behind Each Family

### Ziegler–Nichols (ZN)
- **Philosophy**: Quarter-amplitude decay (QAD) — successive peaks of the closed-loop error have ratio **1 : 1/4**.
- **Strength**: Quick, simple, widely accepted as a *baseline*.
- **Weakness**: Very aggressive for high \(t_d/\tau\) processes; gives excessive overshoot, large IAE, poor robustness. **Not recommended** for ratio \(t_d/\tau > 1\).

### Cohen–Coon
- **Philosophy**: Match the first three moments of the open-loop step response — emphasizes **load disturbance rejection**.
- **Strength**: Excellent for **dominant dead-time** (lag-dominated) processes with \(0.1 \le t_d/\tau \le 1.0\).
- **Weakness**: Yields very aggressive PI and may be unstable for \(t_d/\tau < 0.1\); also gives high overshoot in setpoint tracking.

### Tyreus–Luyben
- **Philosophy**: Derived from the ZN continuous-cycling test but with much **more conservative** multipliers.
- **Strength**: Highly robust; recommended for **integrating and marginally stable** processes (e.g., level control, batch reactors).
- **Weakness**: Slower response than ZN — penalty for the safety margin.

### Internal Model Control (IMC) & SIMC
- **Philosophy**: Invert the model inside an IMC structure; add a **tuning filter constant \(\lambda\)** (or \(\tau_c\)) that explicitly trades performance for robustness.
- **Strength**: **Single tuning knob (\(\lambda\))**; explicit robustness via the MSD constraint \(\lambda \ge 0.8\,t_d\) (Rivera) or \(\lambda \ge t_d\) (Skogestad).
- **Weakness**: Performance depends strongly on **model accuracy**; poor model ⇒ detuned response.

### Direct Synthesis (DS)
- **Philosophy**: Specify a desired closed-loop transfer function (typically first-order with time constant \(\tau_c\)) and **algebraically solve** for the controller.
- **Strength**: Transparent — the engineer chooses the desired closed-loop time constant directly.
- **Weakness**: Does not explicitly address robustness; for plants with severe dead-time, the resulting controller may require a **Smith Predictor**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
| Industry Sector | Dominant Process Character | Recommended Rule (typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical / petrochemical (mixing tanks, heat exchangers) | Moderate \(t_d/\tau\) (0.3 – 0.7) | ZN or Cohen–Coon for first-pass; SIMC for fine-tuning |
| Distillation column temperature | Long dead time, integrating | Tyreus–Luyben or SIMC |
| Paper / pulp flow loops | Short \(t_d/\tau\) (< 0.1) | DS or IMC (ZN is too aggressive) |
| Pharmaceutical batch reactors | Robustness-critical | Tyreus–Luyben |
| Power plant boiler drum level | Integrating | Tyreus–Luyben or SIMC |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark comparison questions, the above industry mapping is a **high-scoring observation** that examiners love to see — it shows the engineer knows the practical "why," not just the math.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Comparison Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: IMC-PID Rule for an FOPDT Plant

This derivation is the **single most common 14-mark question** in this module. We will derive it rigorously.

### Step 1 — Write the plant as an FOPDT

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1} \, e^{-t_d s}$$

### Step 2 — Factor the plant into invertible and non-invertible parts
IMC theory splits \(G_p(s)\) into \(G_p(s) = G_p^+(s) \cdot G_p^-(s)\), where:
- \(G_p^-(s)\) contains all **delays and right-half-plane zeros** (non-invertible part).
- \(G_p^+(s)\) is **invertible and stable** (the minimum-phase part).

For the FOPDT plant:

$$G_p^+(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}, \qquad G_p^-(s) = e^{-t_d s}$$

### Step 3 — Form the IMC controller
The IMC controller is the invertible part, inverted and passed through a **low-pass filter \(f(s)\)** for properness and robustness:

$$\tilde{G}_c(s) = \frac{1}{G_p^+(s)} \cdot f(s) = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K_p} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda s + 1}$$

The **single tuning parameter** is \(\lambda > 0\). (Higher \(\lambda\) ⇒ slower but more robust.)

### Step 4 — Convert IMC to classical feedback (parallel) form
The classical feedback controller is obtained from:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{\tilde{G}_c(s)}{1 - G_p(s)\,\tilde{G}_c(s)}$$

Substituting:

$$1 - G_p(s)\,\tilde{G}_c(s) = 1 - \frac{K_p\,e^{-t_d s}}{\tau s + 1}\cdot\frac{\tau s + 1}{K_p(\lambda s + 1)} = 1 - \frac{e^{-t_d s}}{\lambda s + 1}$$

Combine into a single fraction:

$$1 - \frac{e^{-t_d s}}{\lambda s + 1} = \frac{\lambda s + 1 - e^{-t_d s}}{\lambda s + 1}$$

So:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{\dfrac{\tau s + 1}{K_p(\lambda s + 1)}}{\dfrac{\lambda s + 1 - e^{-t_d s}}{\lambda s + 1}} = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K_p\,[\lambda s + 1 - e^{-t_d s}]}$$

### Step 5 — Use a 1st-order Padé approximation for the dead time
The exponential \(e^{-t_d s}\) is replaced by a first-order Padé approximation:

$$e^{-t_d s} \approx \frac{1 - \tfrac{t_d}{2}s}{1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s}$$

Then the denominator becomes:

$$\lambda s + 1 - \frac{1 - \tfrac{t_d}{2}s}{1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s} = \frac{(\lambda s + 1)(1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s) - (1 - \tfrac{t_d}{2}s)}{1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s}$$

Expand the numerator:

$$(\lambda s + 1)\!\left(1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s\right) = 1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s + \lambda s + \lambda\tfrac{t_d}{2}s^2 = 1 + \left(\tfrac{t_d}{2} + \lambda\right)s + \lambda\tfrac{t_d}{2}s^2$$

Subtract \((1 - \tfrac{t_d}{2}s)\):

$$\text{Numerator} = 1 + \left(\tfrac{t_d}{2} + \lambda\right)s + \lambda\tfrac{t_d}{2}s^2 - 1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s = \left(\lambda + t_d\right)s + \lambda\tfrac{t_d}{2}s^2$$

So:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{(\tau s + 1)(1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s)}{K_p\left[(\lambda + t_d)s + \lambda\tfrac{t_d}{2}s^2\right]}$$

### Step 6 — Realize a practical PI form
Choosing \(\lambda \ge 0.8\,t_d\) and neglecting the \(s^2\) denominator term under the standard IMC-PID assumption (or by setting the derivative filter), the result collapses to the **classical IMC-PI rule**:

$$\boxed{\,G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right), \quad K_c = \frac{\tau}{K_p(\lambda + t_d)}, \quad \tau_I = \tau\,}$$

This is the **IMC-PI tuning formula** listed in the master table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 boards frequently ask: *"*Derive the IMC-based PI rule for a FOPDT process*".* The above six steps, with the Padé approximation clearly stated, constitute a **complete 14-mark answer**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (the "exam-table" you must memorize)

### Table 2 — Property-by-Property Comparison

| Property | ZN Open-Loop | ZN Closed-Loop | Cohen–Coon | Tyreus–Luyben | IMC / SIMC | Direct Synthesis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Closed-loop speed** | Fast (aggressive) | Fastest of all | Very fast | Slow | Tunable via \(\lambda\) | Tunable via \(\tau_c\) |
| **Overshoot (%)** | 40 – 60 | 40 – 60 | 30 – 50 | 5 – 15 | 0 – 10 (with \(\lambda\) large) | 0 – 10 |
| **Robustness (MSD)** | Poor (1.6 – 2.5) | Poorest (~2.0) | Poor | Excellent (1.3 – 1.6) | Explicitly tuned | Implicit |
| **Load disturbance rejection** | Good | Best for setpoint | Excellent (best of classical) | Fair | Tunable | Tunable |
| **Setpoint tracking** | Aggressive, oscillatory | Oscillatory | Oscillatory | Smooth | Smooth | Smooth |
| **Model dependency** | Low (only \(K_p, t_d, \tau\)) | Lowest (no model) | Moderate | Low (only \(K_u, P_u\)) | **High** — needs accurate model | High |
| **Tuning knob(s)** | None (formula) | None (formula) | None (formula) | None (formula) | **One — \(\lambda\)** | **One — \(\tau_c\)** |
| **Tuning knob physical meaning** | — | — | — | — | Closed-loop speed ↔ robustness | Desired closed-loop time constant |
| **Test required** | Step test | Sustained oscillation (P-only) | Step test | Sustained oscillation | Step test (for FOPDT fit) | Step test |
| **Recommended \(t_d/\tau\) range** | 0.1 – 0.6 | 0.1 – 0.6 | 0.1 – 1.0 | 0.1 – 1.0 | \(\ge 0.1\) | \(\ge 0.1\) |
| **Risk on integrating plants** | Dangerous | Dangerous | Dangerous | Safe & recommended | Possible with IMC-PID for integrators | Needs extension |
| **KTU 14-mark weightage** | High | High | High | Moderate | **Highest** | High |

### Table 3 — Performance vs. Robustness Verdict (qualitative, no absolute numbers)

| Method | Performance score (1 = poor, 5 = excellent) | Robustness score (1 = poor, 5 = excellent) |
|---|---|---|
| Ziegler–Nichols (both variants) | 4 | 2 |
| Cohen–Coon | 4 | 2 |
| Tyreus–Luyben | 2 | 5 |
| IMC / SIMC | 3 (adjustable to 5 with small \(\lambda\)) | 4 (adjustable) |
| Direct Synthesis | 3 (adjustable) | 3 (depends on \(\tau_c\)) |

> [!NOTE]
> "Performance" here = IAE / ISE / ITAE for a step load disturbance (lower ⇒ better). "Robustness" = inverse of the MSD (lower MSD ⇒ better).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Comparison (for a "given" plant — typical 14-mark framing)

> **Given**: A temperature sensor in a mixing tank gives the FOPDT model
> \(G_p(s) = \dfrac{2.0\,e^{-1.2 s}}{3.0\,s + 1}\).
> **Find**: PID settings using **(i)** ZN Reaction-Curve, **(ii)** Cohen–Coon, **(iii)** IMC-PI. **Compare** the closed-loop performance using IAE and overshoot. Assume \(\lambda = t_d\) for IMC.

### Solution

**Identification of parameters:** \(K_p = 2.0\), \(\tau = 3.0\), \(t_d = 1.2\).

**(i) ZN Reaction-Curve (Open-Loop):**

$$K_c = \frac{1.2\,\tau}{K_p\, t_d} = \frac{1.2 \times 3.0}{2.0 \times 1.2} = 1.5$$

$$\tau_I = 2\,t_d = 2.4 \text{ min}, \qquad \tau_D = 0.5\,t_d = 0.6 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Cohen–Coon (PID form):**

$$K_c = \frac{\tau}{K_p\,t_d}\!\left(\frac{4}{3} + \frac{t_d}{4\tau}\right) = \frac{3.0}{2.0 \times 1.2}\!\left(1.333 + \frac{1.2}{12}\right) = 1.25 \times 1.433 = 1.79$$

$$\tau_I = t_d \cdot \frac{32 + 6\,t_d/\tau}{13 + 8\,t_d/\tau} = 1.2 \times \frac{32 + 2.4}{13 + 3.2} = 1.2 \times \frac{34.4}{16.2} = 2.55 \text{ min}$$

$$\tau_D = \frac{4\,t_d}{11 + 2\,t_d/\tau} = \frac{4.8}{11 + 0.8} = \frac{4.8}{11.8} = 0.407 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) IMC-PI (with \(\lambda = t_d = 1.2\)):**

$$K_c = \frac{\tau}{K_p(\lambda + t_d)} = \frac{3.0}{2.0 \times 2.4} = 0.625$$

$$\tau_I = \tau = 3.0 \text{ min}, \qquad \tau_D = 0$$

### Comparison Table (Numerical)

| Method | \(K_c\) | \(\tau_I\) (min) | \(\tau_D\) (min) | Approx. Overshoot | Approx. IAE (norm.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ZN Reaction-Curve | 1.50 | 2.4 | 0.6 | ~45 % | 1.00 (reference) |
| Cohen–Coon PID | 1.79 | 2.55 | 0.41 | ~35 % | 0.85 |
| IMC-PI (\(\lambda = t_d\)) | 0.625 | 3.0 | 0 | ~8 % | 1.4 (slower) |

> [!WARNING]
> Cohen–Coon produces the **largest gain** (most aggressive), ZN a close second, while IMC is **clearly the most conservative**. The IAE of ZN-Cohen–Coon is *smaller* (faster) but the **overshoot and robustness are far worse** — this is the central trade-off that KTU examiners want you to articulate.

### Engineering Verdict (typical KTU examiner's expected answer)
- Choose **ZN/Cohen–Coon** for **fast, lag-dominated** processes where aggressive control is acceptable.
- Choose **IMC/SIMC** for **noise-sensitive** or **model-accurate** processes; tune \(\lambda\) upward to gain robustness.
- Choose **Tyreus–Luyben** when **stability and robustness are paramount** (e.g., integrating, batch, or near-unstable plants).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Tuning Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Tuning of PID Controller"] --> B["Model Required?"]
    B -- "No, Open-loop step test" --> C["Reaction Curve Methods"]
    B -- "No, Closed-loop oscillation" --> D["Ultimate Cycle Methods"]
    B -- "Yes, FOPDT or higher" --> E["Model-Based Methods"]
    B -- "Yes, FOPDT + IAE/ITAE criterion" --> F["Optimal Index Methods"]

    C --> C1["Ziegler-Nicholas Reaction Curve"]
    C --> C2["Cohen-Coon"]

    D --> D1["Ziegler-Nicholas Continuous Cycling"]
    D --> D2["Tyreus-Luyben"]
    D --> D3["Astrom-Hagglund Relay Auto-Tune"]

    E --> E1["Direct Synthesis DS"]
    E --> E2["Internal Model Control IMC"]
    E --> E3["SIMC Skogestad"]

    F --> F1["Lopez-Murrill IAE ISE"]
    F --> F2["Rovira Murrill ITAE Setpoint"]
    F --> F3["Zhuang-Atherton ITSE"]

    C1 --> G["QAD 1 to 4 Decay Criterion"]
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> H["Conservative Quarter Decay"]
    E1 --> I["Closed Loop Time Constant Tau_c"]
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    E2 --> J["Tuning Filter Lambda Trades Performance for Robustness"]
    E3 --> J
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — How to *Choose* a Tuning Method (Decision Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start Have you identified a process model?"] --> M1{"Is step-test data available?"}
    M1 -- "Yes" --> M2["Fit FOPDT model: Kp, tau, td"]
    M1 -- "No closed-loop possible" --> M3["Apply relay-feedback or sustained P-only to find Ku and Pu"]

    M2 --> R1{"Ratio td by tau?"}
    R1 -- "Less than 0.1" --> R2["Use Direct Synthesis or IMC with small lambda"]
    R1 -- "Between 0.1 and 1.0" --> R3{"Priority?"}
    R1 -- "Greater than 1.0" --> R4["Use Tyreus-Luyben or SIMC consider Smith Predictor"]

    R3 -- "Fast disturbance rejection" --> R5["Cohen-Coon PID"]
    R3 -- "Balanced speed and robustness" --> R6["IMC or SIMC with lambda greater than 0.8 td"]
    R3 -- "Maximum robustness" --> R7["Tyreus-Luyben via M3 closed-loop test"]

    M3 --> M4["Compute ZN or Tyreus-Luyben settings from Ku and Pu"]
    M4 --> M5{"Process is integrating?"}
    M5 -- "Yes" --> M6["Use Tyreus-Luyben only never ZN or Cohen-Coon"]
    M5 -- "No" --> M7["Use ZN as starting point then re-tune with IMC"]

    R2 --> X["Recommended Final Settings"]
    R5 --> X
    R6 --> X
    R7 --> X
    M6 --> X
    M7 --> X
```

## 4.3 Comparative Performance vs. Robustness "Trade-off" Chart (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X1["Aggressive End"]
        ZN1["Ziegler Nichols Open-Loop"]
        ZN2["Ziegler Nichols Closed-Loop"]
        CC["Cohen Coon"]
    end

    subgraph X2["Balanced Middle"]
        DS["Direct Synthesis"]
        IMC["IMC with lambda approx t_d"]
        SIMC["SIMC with lambda equal to t_d"]
    end

    subgraph X3["Conservative End"]
        TL["Tyreus Luyben"]
        IMC2["IMC with lambda greater than 2 t_d"]
    end

    ZN1 -->|"Trade Performance for Robustness"| ZN2
    ZN2 -->|"Increase lambda"| DS
    DS --> IMC
    IMC --> SIMC
    SIMC -->|"Slow down further"| TL
    TL --> IMC2

    Y1["Performance: high to low"] -.-> ZN1
    Y1 -.-> TL
    Y2["Robustness: low to high"] -.-> ZN1
    Y2 -.-> TL
```

> [!TIP]
> In your KTU 14-mark answer, drawing this **aggressive → balanced → conservative** spectrum and placing each method on it is the **single highest-impact visual** you can produce. Most toppers include such a sketch (even a hand-drawn one) in their answer booklet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List the two Ziegler–Nichols tuning methods and state one limitation of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Method 1 — Reaction Curve (Open-Loop):** Apply a step change to the open-loop plant, identify the FOPDT parameters \(K_p, t_d, \tau\) from the S-shaped response, and use the ZN table. **Limitation:** Requires a step test (process must be brought to a steady state first) and gives aggressive settings with high overshoot (≈45 %).
- **Method 2 — Continuous Cycling (Closed-Loop):** With a P-controller, gradually increase \(K_c\) until sustained oscillations occur; record the **ultimate gain \(K_u\)** and **ultimate period \(P_u\)**, then use the ZN table. **Limitation:** Pushes the process to its stability boundary, which is **unsafe for integrating or open-loop unstable plants**, and also produces aggressive (high-overshoot) settings.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is the IMC-based tuning rule preferred over the Ziegler–Nichols rule for a process where the dead time is comparable to the time constant (\(t_d / \tau \approx 1\))?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. For high \(t_d/\tau\), ZN rules give a **very aggressive \(K_c\)** (proportional to \(\tau/t_d\)) and **very small \(\tau_I\) and \(\tau_D\)**, leading to large overshoot and poor robustness.
2. IMC provides a **single tuning knob \(\lambda\)** that the engineer can increase to deliberately slow the response and gain robustness, with the **explicit constraint** \(\lambda \ge 0.8\,t_d\) (Rivera) ensuring stability for any \(t_d/\tau\).
3. IMC directly relates \(\lambda\) to **closed-loop robustness (MSD)** and performance, making the design transparent, whereas ZN gives no such knob.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each — Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 regulations, the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Derive the IMC-based PI tuning rule for a First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) process, clearly stating the Padé approximation used. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** For the FOPDT process

$$G_p(s) = \frac{1.5\,e^{-2.0\,s}}{4.0\,s + 1}$$

compute the PI settings using **(i)** IMC (with \(\lambda = t_d\)) and **(ii)** Ziegler–Nichols Reaction Curve. Compare the two in terms of expected overshoot and disturbance rejection (IAE trend). **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
1. Plant: \(G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p\,e^{-t_d s}}{\tau s + 1}\). Factor: \(G_p^+(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}\), \(G_p^-(s) = e^{-t_d s}\).
2. IMC controller: \(\tilde{G}_c(s) = \dfrac{\tau s + 1}{K_p(\lambda s + 1)}\).
3. Convert to classical feedback: \(G_c(s) = \dfrac{\tilde{G}_c}{1 - G_p \tilde{G}_c} = \dfrac{\tau s + 1}{K_p[\lambda s + 1 - e^{-t_d s}]}\).
4. Apply first-order Padé: \(e^{-t_d s} \approx \dfrac{1 - \tfrac{t_d}{2}s}{1 + \tfrac{t_d}{2}s}\).
5. Expand denominator: \((\lambda + t_d)s + \lambda\tfrac{t_d}{2}s^2\).
6. With the standard IMC simplification (set the \(s^2\) filter to unity), obtain:

$$K_c = \frac{\tau}{K_p(\lambda + t_d)}, \qquad \tau_I = \tau$$

(Full algebraic steps were shown in **Section 3.1** above.)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**
- **Identification:** \(K_p = 1.5\), \(\tau = 4.0\), \(t_d = 2.0\).
- **(i) IMC-PI (\(\lambda = t_d = 2.0\)):**

$$K_c = \frac{4.0}{1.5 \times (2.0 + 2.0)} = \frac{4.0}{6.0} = 0.667, \qquad \tau_I = 4.0 \text{ min}$$

- **(ii) ZN Reaction Curve:**

$$K_c = \frac{1.2 \times 4.0}{1.5 \times 2.0} = \frac{4.8}{3.0} = 1.60, \qquad \tau_I = 2\,t_d = 4.0 \text{ min}, \quad \tau_D = 0.5\,t_d = 1.0 \text{ min}$$

- **Comparison table:**

| Metric | IMC-PI | ZN Reaction Curve |
|---|---|---|
| \(K_c\) | 0.667 | 1.60 |
| \(\tau_I\) (min) | 4.0 | 4.0 |
| \(\tau_D\) (min) | 0 | 1.0 |
| Expected overshoot | ≈ 5 – 10 % | ≈ 40 – 50 % |
| Expected IAE (load) | Higher (slower) | Lower (faster) |
| Robustness (MSD) | Better (≈ 1.4) | Worse (≈ 2.0) |

- **Verdict (1 mark):** ZN gives faster disturbance rejection (lower IAE) but IMC gives smaller overshoot and is more robust — the **classic performance–robustness trade-off**.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**(a)** With the help of a block-level flow diagram, **compare** Ziegler–Nichols (Continuous Cycling), Cohen–Coon, Tyreus–Luyben, and IMC-based tuning methods on the basis of: (i) test required, (ii) tuning philosophy, (iii) speed of response, (iv) robustness, and (v) best-suited process type. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A FOPDT process has \(K_p = 3.0\), \(\tau = 5.0\) min, and \(t_d = 1.0\) min. Compute the **PI settings** using Cohen–Coon and Tyreus–Luyben (assume \(K_u = 2 K_{c,ZN}\) and \(P_u = 4 t_d\) as approximate ultimate values for this plant). Which rule is preferred if the process is **integrating in nature (e.g., a level loop)**? Justify in **two sentences**. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Criterion | ZN Continuous Cycling | Cohen–Coon | Tyreus–Luyben | IMC / SIMC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Test required | P-only to find \(K_u, P_u\) | Open-loop step test | P-only to find \(K_u, P_u\) | Open-loop step test → FOPDT fit |
| Tuning philosophy | QAD (1:¼) of closed-loop error | Match first 3 moments of step response | Conservative QAD | Invert model + low-pass filter |
| Speed of response | Fastest | Very fast | Slow | Adjustable (via \(\lambda\)) |
| Robustness (MSD) | Poor (~2.0) | Poor (~1.9) | Excellent (~1.4) | Tunable, good (~1.5) |
| Best-suited process | First-pass baseline; non-integrating | Lag-dominated (\(0.1 \le t_d/\tau \le 1.0\)) | Integrating, batch, near-unstable | Model-accurate, well-understood plants |

A block diagram must accompany this table (use the decision-logic flow from Section 4.2 above). Marks are distributed: 1 mark per criterion × 5 criteria = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for the diagram and overall synthesis.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**
- **Identification:** \(K_p = 3.0\), \(\tau = 5.0\), \(t_d = 1.0\). Given \(K_u = 2 \times K_{c,ZN}\) where \(K_{c,ZN} = \dfrac{1.2\,\tau}{K_p\,t_d} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 5.0}{3.0 \times 1.0} = 2.0\), so \(K_u = 4.0\) and \(P_u = 4\,t_d = 4.0\) min.

- **Cohen–Coon PI:**

$$K_c = \frac{\tau}{K_p t_d}\!\left(0.9 + \frac{t_d}{12\tau}\right) = \frac{5.0}{3.0 \times 1.0}\!\left(0.9 + \frac{1.0}{60}\right) = 1.667 \times 0.9167 = 1.53$$

$$\tau_I = t_d \cdot \frac{30 + 3 t_d/\tau}{9 + 20 t_d/\tau} = 1.0 \times \frac{30 + 0.6}{9 + 4.0} = \frac{30.6}{13.0} = 2.35 \text{ min}$$

- **Tyreus–Luyben PI:**

$$K_c = 0.31\,K_u = 0.31 \times 4.0 = 1.24$$

$$\tau_I = 2.2\,P_u = 2.2 \times 4.0 = 8.8 \text{ min}$$

- **Comparative settings:**

| Parameter | Cohen–Coon PI | Tyreus–Luyben PI |
|---|---|---|
| \(K_c\) | 1.53 | 1.24 |
| \(\tau_I\) (min) | 2.35 | 8.8 |

- **Verdict (2 marks, two sentences):**
  For an integrating process such as a level loop, **Tyreus–Luyben is preferred** because its conservative gains and large \(\tau_I\) prevent the "reset windup" hazard that aggressive Cohen–Coon rules create on integrating plants. Cohen–Coon's derivation assumes the plant **returns to a steady state** after each step — an assumption that fails for true integrators — whereas Tyreus–Luyben is derived from the closed-loop **ultimate cycle** and remains valid for both self-regulating and integrating processes.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks in "Comparing Tuning Methods" questions (KTU 2024 pattern):**
> 1. **Unit errors:** \(\tau_I\) and \(\tau_D\) must be in the **same time unit as \(t_d\) and \(\tau\)**. Mixing seconds and minutes inside one formula is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Form confusion:** Using series-form ZN formulas but feeding them into a parallel-form PID structure. KTU 2024 requires explicit **form conversion** or a clarifying note.
> 3. **Forgetting the Padé approximation statement:** An IMC derivation without the statement "using 1st-order Padé: \(e^{-t_d s} \approx (1 - t_d s/2)/(1 + t_d s/2)\)" is **incomplete** — deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **No qualitative comparison:** A purely numerical answer loses up to 3 marks. KTU wants a **judgement statement** ("preferred because...").
> 5. **Confusing \(K_p\) (plant gain) with \(K_c\) (controller gain).** This is the single most common handwriting-induced mistake. Circle the symbols clearly.
> 6. **Recommending ZN for an integrating plant** — auto-zero. Examiners will deduct 2 marks for this violation of process-control fundamentals.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "5-must-know" takeaways for "Comparing Tuning Methods":**
>
> 1. **FOPDT model is the universal starting point** — every classical rule reduces the plant to \(K_p, \tau, t_d\). Always identify these first.
>
> 2. **ZN gives aggressive, fast, low-IAE but high-overshoot settings.** It is the **baseline** — never the final answer for a critical process.
>
> 3. **Cohen–Coon is best for dominant-dead-time (lag-dominated) processes** (\(0.1 \le t_d/\tau \le 1.0\)) when **load disturbance rejection** is the priority.
>
> 4. **Tyreus–Luyben is the safe choice** for integrating, batch, or near-unstable processes — slow but extremely robust. The only rule that works safely on a level-control loop.
>
> 5. **IMC/SIMC and Direct Synthesis are model-based "one-knob" methods** — \(\lambda\) or \(\tau_c\) is the **single trade-off parameter** between performance and robustness, with the explicit constraint \(\lambda \ge 0.8\,t_d\).
>
> **Bonus tip:** Always close your 14-mark answer with a **trade-off summary sentence** (e.g., "*ZN maximizes speed at the cost of robustness; Tyreus–Luyben maximizes robustness at the cost of speed; IMC provides an explicit, tunable balance between the two*"). Examiners reserve 1–2 marks specifically for this **synthesis statement**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC):** A **PLC** is a ruggedized, solid-state, digital computing device specifically designed for the real-time control of industrial processes and machinery in harsh factory environments. It operates by continuously monitoring inputs from sensors, executing a user-defined logic program stored in its non-volatile memory, and producing corresponding outputs to actuators — all within a deterministic, cyclic scan time measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.

The term was coined by **Modicon (Bedford Associates)** in **1968**, with the Modicon 084 being the world's first commercial PLC, designed to replace complex electromechanical relay panels in the American automotive industry (notably at the Hydramatic Division of General Motors).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition for Exams:**
> *"A PLC is a microprocessor-based controller that uses programmable memory to store instructions and implement functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations to control machines and processes."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a PLC as a **digital, software-driven replacement for a wall full of electromechanical relays, timers, and counters** that traditionally controlled a manufacturing line.

### The "Smart Junction Box" Analogy

Imagine you have a big industrial machine (say, a bottling plant conveyor). Traditionally, you would wire hundreds of relays, timers, and counters together physically — a wiring nightmare that takes weeks to rewire if you want to change even a single operation.

A PLC simplifies this dramatically:

| Traditional Relay Panel | PLC Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Physical relay coils and contacts | Software "contacts" in memory |
| Wiring changes for new logic | Program changes in laptop |
| Mechanical wear and tear | No moving parts in logic |
| Difficult troubleshooting | Online monitoring & diagnostics |
| Large footprint | Compact DIN-rail module |

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Intuition:** A PLC is to a relay cabinet what a **smartphone app** is to a wall-mounted rotary telephone dial. Both achieve communication/control, but the programmable, software-based device is exponentially more flexible, compact, and intelligent.

---

## 1.3 Physical & Operational Characteristics

- **Operating Temperature Range:** **0 °C to 55 °C** (standard industrial grade)
- **Supply Voltage:** **24 V DC** or **120/230 V AC**
- **Scan Time:** Typically **1 ms to 20 ms** per logic scan
- **Memory Types:** RAM (volatile), EEPROM/Flash (non-volatile)
- **Standard:** Governed by **IEC 61131-3** for programming languages

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** PLCs are the backbone of **SCADA**, **DCS**, and modern **Industry 4.0 / IIoT** architectures. They sit at the lowest control level (Level 0/1) of the ISA-95 automation pyramid.

---

## 1.4 Why PLCs Were Invented — The "Ten Problems" of Relay Logic

The automotive industry (GM Hydramatic, 1968) issued an "Ideal Specification" listing 10 problems with relay-based control:

1. **Frequent relay failures** due to mechanical wear
2. **Difficult troubleshooting** — finding a failed relay among hundreds
3. **Time-consuming wiring changes** for new product variants
4. **Long changeover times** between production runs
5. **High cost of spare relays and maintenance**
6. **Inability to communicate** with central control systems
7. **Large physical footprint** of relay cabinets
8. **No data logging** capability
9. **No arithmetic or complex math** capability
10. **High downtime** and unreliable sequencing

The PLC was engineered to solve **all 10 problems simultaneously**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PLC Architecture — The Five Major Subsystems

A PLC is structurally composed of **five primary functional blocks**. KTU examiners frequently ask for a labeled block diagram — memorize this.

### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU)

The CPU is the **"brain"** of the PLC. It consists of:

- **Microprocessor / Microcontroller:** Executes the logic program. Common families: ARM Cortex, Intel x86, or proprietary RISC cores.
- **System Memory Address Registers**
- **Accumulator (ACC)** for arithmetic operations
- **Program Counter (PC)** — tracks current scan instruction
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** — handles subroutine calls and interrupts
- **Status Register / Flags** — Zero (Z), Carry (C), Negative (N), Overflow (V) flags

> [!NOTE]
> The CPU operates based on a crystal oscillator clock, typically **8 MHz to 1 GHz** depending on PLC class (micro vs. modular).

### 2.1.2 Memory Subsystem

PLC memory is partitioned into distinct functional regions:

| Memory Region | Function | Typical Type | Volatility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **System ROM / Firmware** | Stores OS, boot loader, interpreter | Flash / ROM | Non-volatile |
| **User Program Memory** | Stores ladder logic / FBD / ST program | Flash / EEPROM | Non-volatile |
| **Data Memory (V-Memory)** | Holds variables, I/O states, timers | RAM (battery-backed) | Volatile but backed up |
| **I/O Image Table** | Mirror of physical input/output states | RAM | Volatile but backed up |
| **Working Scratchpad** | Temporary calculations | RAM | Volatile |

> [!TIP]
> **Battery Backup:** Most PLCs use a **lithium battery** (typical life **5–10 years**) to retain RAM contents during power loss. The I/O Image Table and retentive timers are preserved this way.

### 2.1.3 Input/Output (I/O) Section — The "Senses and Muscles"

The I/O section interfaces the digital/analog world with the low-voltage CPU logic.

**A. Digital (Discrete) I/O Modules**
- **Input Voltages:** 24 V DC, 120 V AC, 230 V AC
- **Output Types:**
  - **Transistor outputs** (DC, fast switching, 0.5 A typical)
  - **Triac outputs** (AC, zero-crossing switching)
  - **Relay outputs** (dry contact, up to 2 A, isolated)

**B. Analog I/O Modules**
- **Input Signals:** **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V DC**, **±10 V DC**, RTD, Thermocouple (mV)
- **Resolution:** **8-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit** ADC
- **Output Signals:** **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V DC** for valve actuators and VFDs

**C. Specialty Modules**
- High-Speed Counters (HSC) — for encoder feedback, up to **1 MHz**
- Motion Control Modules — for servo/stepper control
- Communication Modules — Profibus, Profinet, Modbus, EtherNet/IP
- Safety Modules — for SIL-rated emergency stop circuits

### 2.1.4 Power Supply Unit (PSU)

Converts mains AC to regulated DC voltages required by internal electronics:

- **CPU Logic Rails:** **5 V DC** and **3.3 V DC** for digital ICs
- **Sensor Power:** **24 V DC** for field device excitation
- **Isolation:** Provides **galvanic isolation** (typically **1500 V AC** for 1 minute) between field and logic sides
- **Redundancy:** Critical applications use **1+1 redundant** PSUs with hot-swap capability

### 2.1.5 Programming Device (PG) / Human-Machine Interface (HMI)

- **Programming Device:** Laptop/PC with manufacturer-specific software (e.g., TIA Portal for Siemens, RSLogix for Allen-Bradley, EcoStruxure for Schneider).
- **HMI:** Touchscreen panel for operator interaction.
- **Communication:** USB, Ethernet, RS-232, RS-485, MPI/Profibus.

---

## 2.2 PLC Scan Cycle — The Heartbeat of Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PLC does **NOT** execute continuously. It operates in a strict, repeating **SCAN CYCLE**, also called the **Cyclic Executive**. The entire cycle typically completes in **5–50 ms** depending on program size and CPU speed.

### The Four (or Five) Phases of a Scan Cycle

1. **Input Scan (Read Phase)**
   - CPU reads the status of **all physical input modules** (limit switches, sensors, push-buttons).
   - These states are copied into the **Input Image Table (IIT)** in RAM.
   - Duration: typically **1–3 ms**.

2. **Program Scan (Execute Phase)**
   - CPU executes the user program **top-to-bottom, left-to-right** (in ladder logic).
   - Reads inputs from the **Image Table (not physical inputs)** — this is called the "snapshot" principle.
   - Writes intermediate results directly to the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.
   - Duration: **1–30 ms** (program-length dependent).

3. **Output Scan (Write Phase)**
   - Contents of the **Output Image Table** are transferred to the **physical output modules** simultaneously.
   - All actuators (contactors, solenoid valves, lamps) update at once.
   - Duration: **1–3 ms**.

4. **Housekeeping / Overhead Phase**
   - Communication with programming device
   - Diagnostics and self-tests
   - Updating internal timers/clocks
   - Memory integrity checks (checksums, watchdogs)

5. *(Optional)* **Interrupt Handling**
   - High-priority events (emergency stop, HSC overflow) are serviced immediately, regardless of scan position.

### Scan Time Formula

The total scan time $T_{scan}$ is the sum of all phase durations:

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house} + T_{int}
$$

For a typical mid-range PLC with a 1000-instruction ladder program:

$$
T_{scan} \approx 2\,\text{ms} + 10\,\text{ms} + 2\,\text{ms} + 1\,\text{ms} = 15\,\text{ms}
$$

This implies an effective **scan frequency**:

$$
f_{scan} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{0.015\,\text{s}} \approx 66.67\,\text{Hz}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "**input update rate**" with "**scan frequency**". A process variable may change at **1 kHz**, but if the scan time is **10 ms**, the PLC only "sees" that input at **100 Hz** — the Nyquist aliasing risk is real in high-speed applications.

---

## 2.3 IEC 61131-3 Programming Languages

The **IEC 61131-3** international standard defines **5 programming languages** for PLCs. KTU exams routinely ask to name and briefly describe each.

| # | Language | Acronym | Type | Visual? | KTU Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ladder Diagram** | LD | Graphical | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Most asked) |
| 2 | **Function Block Diagram** | FBD | Graphical | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Sequential Function Chart** | SFC | Graphical | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Structured Text** | ST | Textual | ❌ No | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Instruction List** | IL | Textual | ❌ No | ⭐⭐ (Legacy) |

### Brief Description of Each

**1. Ladder Diagram (LD)** — *The "Relay Logic" of PLCs*
- Resembles an electrical ladder with two vertical "rails" (L1 and L2) and horizontal "rungs".
- Contacts (NO/NC) on the left, coils on the right.
- Most intuitive for electricians; easiest to troubleshoot.
- **Example:** `---[ ]---[/]---( )---` means "Start AND NOT Stop energize Motor"

**2. Function Block Diagram (FBD)**
- Uses graphical blocks (AND, OR, TON, CTU, PID) connected by signal lines.
- Excellent for **continuous process control** (PID loops, math).
- Signal flow is left-to-right.

**3. Sequential Function Chart (SFC)**
- Used for **batch processes** with distinct steps and transitions.
- Components: **Steps (actions)**, **Transitions (conditions)**, **Directed Links**.
- Resembles a state-machine / flowchart.

**4. Structured Text (ST)**
- High-level, Pascal-like language: `IF Temperature > 100 THEN Heater := FALSE; END_IF;`
- Best for **complex math, loops, string handling, data manipulation**.

**5. Instruction List (IL)**
- Assembly-like mnemonics: `LD Start, ANDN Stop, OUT Motor`.
- Declared legacy/withdrawn in IEC 61131-3 Edition 3, but still in use.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scan Time** | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{overhead}$ | ms | Sum of 4 phases |
| **Scan Frequency** | $f_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Hz | Inverse of scan time |
| **Maximum Throughput Delay** | $T_{max} = 2 \cdot T_{scan}$ | ms | Worst-case input-to-output |
| **Timer Preset (TON)** | $T_{preset} = \text{count} \times T_{timebase}$ | s | For example, 100 × 0.01s = 1s |
| **Counter Capacity (16-bit)** | $N_{max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ | counts | Standard |
| **Counter Capacity (32-bit)** | $N_{max} = 2^{32} - 1 \approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ | counts | High-speed |
| **Analog Resolution (12-bit)** | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^{12}}$ | mA or V | e.g., $\dfrac{16\,\text{mA}}{4096} = 3.9\,\mu\text{A}$ |
| **PID Output (Standard)** | $u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\!\!\int e(t)\,dt + K_{d}\,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | % | 0–100% valve position |
| **Nyquist Limit (PLC sampling)** | $f_{sampling} \geq 2 \cdot f_{signal,max}$ | Hz | To avoid aliasing |
| **I/O Scan Memory Size** | $\text{bytes} = 2 \times N_{I/O\,points} \times \text{word size}$ | bytes | e.g., 256 DI + 256 DO = 1024 bytes |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** *"I-P-O-H"* = **I**nput scan → **P**rogram execution → **O**utput scan → **H**ousekeeping. Memorize this acronym — it's the single most-asked scan cycle sequence.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive:** Body shop robots, paint shops, assembly line sequencing
- **Oil & Gas:** Wellhead control, pipeline SCADA, ESD (Emergency Shutdown) systems
- **Power Plants:** Turbine start-up sequencing, boiler drum level control
- **Water Treatment:** Pump alternation, chemical dosing, filter backwash
- **Food & Beverage:** Batch mixing, CIP (Clean-In-Place), packaging lines
- **Cement & Steel:** Kiln control, conveyor coordination, batching plants

The PLC's value is its **determinism** — for a given input sequence, the output response is **guaranteed within a bounded time**, which is critical for safety instrumented functions (SIL-1/2/3).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Scan Time and Maximum Throughput Delay

**Problem Statement:** A Siemens S7-1200 PLC is configured with:
- 32 digital inputs, 32 digital outputs
- A ladder program of 800 instructions
- Each digital I/O scan takes **0.5 µs per point**
- Each instruction executes in **0.1 µs**
- Housekeeping phase takes **1.5 ms**

**Compute:** (a) Total scan time, (b) Scan frequency, (c) Worst-case throughput delay.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the Input Scan Time $T_{in}$**

The Input Scan reads all 32 inputs sequentially.

$$
T_{in} = N_{in} \times t_{in} = 32 \times 0.5\,\mu\text{s} = 16\,\mu\text{s} = 0.016\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Output Scan Time $T_{out}$**

The Output Scan writes to all 32 outputs.

$$
T_{out} = N_{out} \times t_{out} = 32 \times 0.5\,\mu\text{s} = 16\,\mu\text{s} = 0.016\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Program Execution Time $T_{prog}$**

$$
T_{prog} = N_{inst} \times t_{inst} = 800 \times 0.1\,\mu\text{s} = 80\,\mu\text{s} = 0.080\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4: Sum All Phases**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 0.016 + 0.080 + 0.016 + 1.5 = 1.612\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Scan Frequency**

$$
f_{scan} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{1.612 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{scan} \approx 620.3\,\text{Hz}}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Maximum (Worst-Case) Throughput Delay**

A signal arriving **just after** the Input Scan must wait **one full scan cycle** before its effect appears at the output.

$$
T_{max} = 2 \times T_{scan} = 2 \times 1.612\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{max} \approx 3.224\,\text{ms}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means the plant sees an effective control loop delay of ~3.2 ms, which is more than adequate for most thermal and mechanical processes, but might be insufficient for high-speed packaging (typically requiring < 1 ms response).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Analog Input Resolution

**Problem Statement:** A 12-bit ADC module is used to read a **4–20 mA** signal from a pressure transmitter spanning **0–10 bar**. Find:

(a) The least significant bit (LSB) in mA
(b) The LSB in pressure units (bar)
(c) The digital code corresponding to **7.5 bar**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Determine the Number of Quantization Levels**

For an $n$-bit ADC, the total number of discrete codes is:

$$
L = 2^{n}
$$

For $n = 12$:

$$
L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ codes}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the LSB in mA (Resolution in Current)**

The full-scale current span is:

$$
\Delta I_{FS} = 20\,\text{mA} - 4\,\text{mA} = 16\,\text{mA}
$$

The current resolution per LSB:

$$
\text{LSB}_I = \frac{\Delta I_{FS}}{L} = \frac{16\,\text{mA}}{4096}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LSB}_I = 3.90625\,\mu\text{A}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the LSB in bar (Resolution in Pressure)**

$$
\text{LSB}_{P} = \frac{\Delta P_{FS}}{L} = \frac{10\,\text{bar} - 0\,\text{bar}}{4096}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LSB}_{P} \approx 2.441 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{bar} = 2.441\,\text{mbar}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Digital Code for 7.5 bar**

First, convert 7.5 bar into the corresponding current using the linear transfer function:

$$
I = 4\,\text{mA} + \left(\frac{7.5}{10}\right) \times 16\,\text{mA} = 4 + 12 = 16\,\text{mA}
$$

Now, compute the code offset from the 4 mA zero:

$$
\text{Code} = \frac{I - 4\,\text{mA}}{\text{LSB}_I} = \frac{16 - 4}{3.90625 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\text{Code} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{3.90625 \times 10^{-3}} = 3072
$$

In binary (12-bit): $3072 = 110000000000_2$

$$
\boxed{\text{Digital Code} = 3072 \text{ (decimal)} = 0xC00 = 1100\,0000\,0000_2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** For a 4–20 mA signal spanning $R$ engineering units, the code for value $V$ is:

$$
\text{Code} = \text{round}\!\left[ \frac{V}{R} \times 4096 \right]
$$

> For 7.5/10 of full scale: $\frac{7.5}{10} \times 4096 = 3072$ — direct calculation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: On-Delay Timer (TON) Behavior

**Problem Statement:** A TON timer has a preset of **150 counts** with a time base of **0.1 s**. The enable input is asserted at $t = 0$.

**Compute:** (a) Total time delay, (b) The timer Done bit status at $t = 8\,\text{s}$, (c) Behavior if enable is removed at $t = 5\,\text{s}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Total Preset Duration**

$$
T_{preset} = N_{preset} \times T_{base} = 150 \times 0.1\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{preset} = 15\,\text{s}}
$$

**Step 2: Status at $t = 8\,\text{s}$**

Accumulated value (ACC) at $t = 8\,\text{s}$:

$$
\text{ACC} = \frac{8\,\text{s}}{0.1\,\text{s/count}} = 80\,\text{counts}
$$

Compare with preset:

$$
\text{ACC} = 80 < 150 = \text{PRE}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Done Bit} = \text{FALSE (Timer still counting)}}
$$

**Step 3: Behavior on Loss of Enable at $t = 5\,\text{s}$**

At $t = 5\,\text{s}$:

$$
\text{ACC}_{5s} = \frac{5}{0.1} = 50\,\text{counts}
$$

When enable goes FALSE at $t = 5\,\text{s}$:
- ACC resets to **0**
- Done bit resets to FALSE
- Timer becomes "de-energized"

If enable re-asserted later, counting **restarts from zero** (non-retentive timer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Retentive Timer (RTO) alternative:** Unlike TON, an RTO **remembers** the accumulated value when enable is removed — useful for totalizing run-time hours.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PLC vs. Other Controllers

| Parameter | **PLC** | **Microcontroller (μC)** | **DCS** | **PC-Based Control** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Environment** | Industrial, rugged | Lab/consumer | Plant-wide | Office-grade |
| **Reliability** | MTBF > 100,000 h | Variable | MTBF > 200,000 h | Lower |
| **Programming** | Ladder, FBD, ST | C, Assembly | Function blocks, ST | Custom software |
| **I/O Count** | Hundreds | Few (10s) | Thousands | Variable |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | Very High | Medium |
| **Scan Determinism** | **Deterministic (cyclic)** | Non-deterministic | Deterministic | Non-RT unless special OS |
| **Redundancy** | Hot standby CPUs | None native | Built-in triple modular | Custom |
| **Best For** | Discrete & batch control | Embedded products | Continuous process (refineries) | Lab/visualization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mantra:** *"PLCs are for **discrete/batch** control, DCS is for **continuous process** control, and PCs/SCADA are for **supervisory** visualization — never confuse the three levels of the ISA-95 pyramid."*

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — PLC Scan Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> POWERON

    POWERON --> SELFTEST : Power Applied
    SELFTEST --> HOUSEKEEPING : Diagnostics Pass
    SELFTEST --> FAULT : Diagnostics Fail
    FAULT --> [*] : STOP Mode

    HOUSEKEEPING --> INPUTSCAN : Read Sensors
    INPUTSCAN --> PROGRAMEXEC : Update Image Table
    PROGRAMEXEC --> OUTPUTSCAN : Run Ladder Logic
    OUTPUTSCAN --> HOUSECOMMS : Write to Actuators
    HOUSECOMMS --> HOUSEKEEPING : Service Comms + Timers

    note right of INPUTSCAN
        Copy all physical inputs
        into Input Image Table IIT
    end note

    note right of PROGRAMEXEC
        Execute user program
        top to bottom left to right
    end note

    note right of OUTPUTSCAN
        Copy OIT to physical
        output modules simultaneously
    end note
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Ladder Logic Example — Motor Start/Stop with Seal-In

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RUNG1["Rung 1: Motor Control Logic"]
        L1["L1 Power Rail 24 VDC"]
        SB1["SB1 Start NO Contact"]
        SB2["SB2 Stop NC Contact"]
        OL["OL Overload NC Contact"]
        M_AUX["M Auxiliary NO Contact Seal-In"]
        COIL["M Motor Contactor Coil"]
        L2["L2 Neutral Rail"]
    end

    L1 --> SB1
    SB1 --> SB2
    SB2 --> OL
    OL --> M_AUX
    M_AUX --> COIL
    COIL --> L2

    M_AUX -.->|Seal-In Path Parallel to SB1| SB2
```

**Logical Equation for the Coil:**

$$
M = (SB_1 + M_{aux}) \cdot \overline{SB_2} \cdot \overline{OL}
$$

Where:
- $M$ = Motor contactor coil
- $SB_1$ = Start push button (NO)
- $SB_2$ = Stop push button (NC)
- $OL$ = Overload relay contact (NC)
- $M_{aux}$ = Auxiliary contact of motor contactor (NO)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC). List any four advantages of PLCs over conventional relay-based control systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A PLC is a solid-state, microprocessor-based digital controller that uses programmable non-volatile memory to store user-defined instructions for implementing specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations to control industrial machines and processes in real time.

**Any Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Compact and space-saving** — Replaces hundreds of relays with a single compact unit
2. **Easy to modify** — Logic changes are made in software, not rewiring
3. **High reliability** — No moving parts (in solid-state outputs); MTBF > 100,000 hours
4. **Built-in diagnostics** — Online monitoring of I/O status, fault detection, troubleshooting aids
5. **Communication capability** — Native support for Profinet, Modbus, EtherNet/IP for SCADA integration
6. **Lower long-term cost** — Reduced wiring, maintenance, and downtime
7. **Faster scan time** — Response in milliseconds, much faster than relay coil energization

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Explain the four phases of a PLC scan cycle with the help of a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The PLC executes its program in a **cyclic scan** with the following four phases (mnemonic: **I-P-O-H**):

**1. Input Scan (Read Phase):** (0.75 marks)
- CPU reads the ON/OFF status of **all physical input devices** connected to the input modules.
- These states are stored in the **Input Image Table (IIT)** in RAM.

**2. Program Scan (Execute Phase):** (0.75 marks)
- CPU executes the user program **top-to-bottom, left-to-right**.
- Inputs are read from the IIT (not the physical input) — ensuring consistency.
- Intermediate results are written to the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.

**3. Output Scan (Write Phase):** (0.75 marks)
- The contents of the OIT are transferred **simultaneously** to all physical output modules.
- Actuators (contactors, valves, lamps) update at the same instant.

**4. Housekeeping Phase:** (0.75 marks)
- Handles communication with programming device, updates internal clocks, performs self-diagnostics, memory checks, and serves interrupts.

**Equation:**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

**Q: (a)** Draw the block diagram of a PLC and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat ladder diagram and logical equation, explain the **motor start-stop control with overload protection and seal-in contact** using a PLC. Also state the role of the seal-in contact. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a): Block Diagram and Functions (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

A PLC consists of the following major functional blocks (draw the standard five-block diagram with arrows indicating data flow):

**Functions (4 marks — 1 each for first four, 0.5 for fifth):**

**1. CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Contains the microprocessor, accumulator, program counter, and stack pointer. It controls and supervises all PLC operations, executes the user program, and performs arithmetic/logic operations.

**2. Memory Unit:** Partitioned into:
- System memory (ROM) — stores OS and firmware
- User memory (RAM/Flash) — stores the user program
- Data memory — stores variables, timer/counter values, I/O image tables
- Battery backup protects RAM during power loss

**3. Input/Output (I/O) Section:**
- **Input modules** accept signals from field sensors (push-buttons, limit switches, RTDs, 4-20 mA transmitters) and provide **galvanic isolation** between field and CPU.
- **Output modules** drive actuators (contactors, solenoids, lamps, VFDs) using relays, transistors, or triacs.
- Analog modules use **ADC/DAC** with 12-bit or 16-bit resolution.

**4. Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Converts 230 V AC mains into regulated 24 V DC, 5 V DC, and 3.3 V DC rails for the CPU, I/O modules, and sensor excitation. Provides **1500 V AC isolation** for noise immunity.

**5. Programming Device / HMI:** External laptop or HMI panel used to write, upload, download, and monitor the user program via Ethernet, USB, or serial link.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b): Motor Start-Stop with Seal-In (7 marks)**

**Logical Equation (2 marks):**

$$
M = (SB_{start} \;\;\bigvert\;\; M_{aux}) \cdot \overline{SB_{stop}} \cdot \overline{OL}
$$

**Ladder Diagram (3 marks):**

Rung 1:
```
    L1                                 L2
    |                                   |
    +---[ SB_START ]---+               |
    |                  |               |
    +---[ M_AUX    ]---+---[ SB_STOP ]---[ OL ]---( M )---+
                                       (NC)         (NC)    |
                                                            |
    +-----[ M_AUX ]------------------------------------------+
       (Seal-in path parallel to SB_START)
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**

- **PB Start (SB_START — NO):** Momentary push button to start the motor.
- **PB Stop (SB_STOP — NC):** Momentary push button to stop the motor.
- **OL (Overload — NC):** Overload relay contact opens on motor overcurrent, de-energizing the coil.
- **M (Contactor Coil):** Energizes the motor and the M_AUX auxiliary contact.
- **M_AUX (Seal-in Contact — NO):** This is the **memory element**. When M is energized, M_AUX closes and creates a parallel path around SB_START. This means the operator can release the start button, but the motor continues to run because the seal-in contact "latches" the circuit.

**Role of Seal-In Contact:**
The seal-in contact provides **latching memory** — equivalent to the holding contact in a conventional relay circuit. It allows momentary start inputs to produce a sustained output until the stop button or overload opens the circuit.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **IEC 61131-3** standard for PLC programming. List and briefly describe all five programming languages specified by the standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature transmitter outputs **4–20 mA** for a range of **0–200 °C**. The signal is fed to a 12-bit ADC of a PLC. Compute:
- (i) The current corresponding to **125 °C**
- (ii) The LSB resolution in mA and in °C
- (iii) The digital code (decimal and binary) for **125 °C** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a): IEC 61131-3 and the Five Languages (7 marks)**

**Introduction to IEC 61131-3 (2 marks):**

IEC 61131-3 is the **international standard** published by the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** that defines the **programming languages, data types, and software model** for PLCs. It was first published in **1993** and revised in **2003** and **2013**. The goal is to provide vendor-neutral, portable programming across different PLC brands.

**The Five Languages (5 marks — 1 each):**

| # | Language | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ladder Diagram (LD)** | Graphical | Rung-based, resembles electrical relay logic; most widely used in industry |
| 2 | **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** | Graphical | Uses graphical function blocks (timers, counters, PID) connected by signal lines; suitable for continuous control |
| 3 | **Structured Text (ST)** | Textual | High-level Pascal-like language; supports loops, conditionals, complex math |
| 4 | **Instruction List (IL)** | Textual | Low-level assembly-like mnemonics; legacy, deprecated in Edition 3 |
| 5 | **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** | Graphical | Step-and-transition based; used for batch and state-machine processes |

**Additional Concepts (carry marks):**
- **POU (Program Organization Unit):** The basic building block — a function, function block, or program.
- **Data Types:** BOOL, INT, REAL, STRING, ARRAY, STRUCT.
- **Software Model:** Configuration → Resources → Tasks → Programs → POUs.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b): ADC Scaling Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Sensor range: **0–200 °C**
- Current output: **4–20 mA**
- ADC resolution: **12-bit** → $L = 2^{12} = 4096$ codes

**Step 1: Current corresponding to 125 °C (2 marks)**

Using the linear transfer function:

$$
I = 4\,\text{mA} + \left(\frac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max} - T_{min}}\right) \times (20 - 4)\,\text{mA}
$$

$$
I = 4 + \left(\frac{125 - 0}{200 - 0}\right) \times 16
$$

$$
I = 4 + 0.625 \times 16 = 4 + 10
$$

$$
\boxed{I = 14\,\text{mA}}
$$

**Step 2: LSB Resolution in mA (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{LSB}_I = \frac{I_{FS} - I_{zero}}{L} = \frac{20 - 4}{4096} = \frac{16}{4096}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LSB}_I = 3.90625\,\mu\text{A/count}}
$$

**Step 3: LSB Resolution in °C (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{LSB}_T = \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{L} = \frac{200 - 0}{4096}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LSB}_T \approx 0.04883\,°\text{C/count}}
$$

**Step 4: Digital Code for 125 °C (2 marks)**

Current offset from 4 mA:

$$
\Delta I = 14 - 4 = 10\,\text{mA}
$$

Number of LSBs:

$$
\text{Code} = \frac{\Delta I}{\text{LSB}_I} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{3.90625 \times 10^{-6}} = 2560
$$

**Verification (using percentage method):**

$$
\text{Code} = \frac{125}{200} \times 4096 = 0.625 \times 4096 = 2560 \;\checkmark
$$

In binary (12-bit): $2560 = 101000000000_2$

$$
\boxed{\text{Code} = 2560_{10} = 0xA00 = 1010\,0000\,0000_2}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps in PLC Questions (KTU Valuation Room):**
>
> 1. **Confusing I/O scan with physical reading:** Marks are lost when students say *"the CPU reads the input"* without specifying that it reads from the **Image Table during program execution**, and from the **physical module only during the Input Scan phase**. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Housekeeping phase:** Examiners specifically look for all **four phases**. Omitting housekeeping is a guaranteed -1 mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Drawing a ladder with missing power rails:** L1 and L2 rails are MANDATORY in ladder diagrams. A rung without rails is incomplete.
>
> 4. **Wrong seal-in logic placement:** The M_AUX contact must be **parallel to the START button**, not the STOP button. This is a classic marker trap.
>
> 5. **Mixing up scan time with throughput delay:** Students write $T_{throughput} = T_{scan}$. Correct answer is $T_{throughput,max} = 2 \cdot T_{scan}$ because a signal arriving just after the input scan must wait for **one full additional cycle** to appear at the output.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to convert 4 mA to 0 mA in ADC problems:** The "zero" of the ADC is at **4 mA**, not 0 mA. Forgetting this offset yields codes that are **25% too low** (4096 codes lost out of 16384 if 16-bit).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **PLC = Industrial, Ruggedized, Real-Time, Microprocessor-Based Digital Controller**
- [x] Invented by **Modicon (1968)** for **GM Hydramatic**
- [x] Standard: **IEC 61131-3** (programming), **IEC 61131-2** (hardware)
- [x] Five subsystems: **CPU, Memory, I/O, PSU, Programming Device**
- [x] Memory types: **ROM, RAM (battery-backed), Flash/EEPROM**
- [x] Scan cycle acronym: **I-P-O-H** = **I**nput → **P**rogram → **O**utput → **H**ousekeeping
- [x] **T_scan = T_in + T_prog + T_out + T_overhead**
- [x] **Max throughput delay = 2 × T_scan**
- [x] Five programming languages: **LD, FBD, SFC, ST, IL**
- [x] **LD (Ladder)** is the most exam-favored language
- [x] **Scan frequency = 1 / T_scan**
- [x] 12-bit ADC → **4096 codes**; 16-bit ADC → **65536 codes**
- [x] 4–20 mA: zero at **4 mA**, span = **16 mA**
- [x] **LSB_I = 16 mA / 2^n**; **LSB_Eng = Span / 2^n**
- [x] **Seal-in contact = latching memory element** (parallel to START)
- [x] **TON timer = on-delay, non-retentive**; **RTO = retentive**
- [x] Digital I/O types: **Transistor (DC), Triac (AC), Relay (universal)**
- [x] Analog I/O standards: **4-20 mA, 0-10 V, ±10 V, RTD, TC**
- [x] **PLC scan is DETERMINISTIC** — bounded worst-case response time
- [x] Nyquist: PLC must sample at least **2× the highest signal frequency**
- [x] **MTBF of PLC > 100,000 hours**; operating temp **0–55 °C**
- [x] Battery backup typical life: **5–10 years** (lithium)
- [x] Hot-swappable, modular, rack-mounted I/O
- [x] Communication protocols: **Profinet, Profibus, Modbus RTU/TCP, EtherNet/IP**

### 🎯 KTU Exam-Ready "One-Liners"
