> *"A PLC is a digital computer designed for the real-time control of industrial processes, with a deterministic cyclic scan."*

> *"The scan cycle has four phases: **Input Scan, Program Scan, Output Scan, and Housekeeping** — also called the **I-P-O-H cycle**."*

> *"IEC 61131-3 standardizes five programming languages: Ladder Diagram, Function Block Diagram, Structured Text, Instruction List, and Sequential Function Chart."*

> *"The seal-in contact provides software-based latching memory to retain the output state after a momentary start input is released."*

> *"PLCs sit at Level 1 of the ISA-95 automation pyramid, below SCADA/HMI (Level 2) and MES/ERP (Levels 3-4)."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC versus Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PLC versus Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Relay?

A **Relay** is an **electromechanical switching device** that uses an electromagnet to mechanically operate one or more sets of contacts. The contacts open or close auxiliary circuits when the coil is energized by a relatively small control signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard):** A relay is an electrically operated switch. It consists of a coil (electromagnet), a movable armature, and a set of contacts (Normally Open - NO, Normally Closed - NC). When current flows through the coil, the resulting magnetic field pulls the armature, causing the contacts to switch states.

In process instrumentation, relays are used as **interfacing elements** between low-power control circuits (e.g., 5 V DC from a sensor) and high-power actuators (e.g., 230 V AC motors, solenoids, heaters).

## 1.2 What is a PLC?

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a **ruggedized, microprocessor-based digital computer** specifically designed for the real-time control of industrial automation processes. It replaces the hard-wired relay logic of traditional control panels with **software-based ladder logic, function block diagrams, or structured text** programs stored in non-volatile memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard):** A PLC is a solid-state, industrial-grade computer that continuously scans inputs, executes a user-programmed logic, and updates outputs to control machinery and processes. It follows the **Cyclic Scan Principle** (Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Police vs. Traffic Signal"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Pump:** Think of a **Relay** as a *human traffic police officer* standing at an intersection. The officer physically waves arms (mechanical motion of contacts) to stop or allow each car, one decision at a time. If the intersection becomes complex with 20 roads, you would need 20 officers — slow, expensive, and prone to fatigue.
>
> Now think of a **PLC** as a *smart, automated traffic signal system with a pre-programmed timer*. The PLC takes in signals from sensors (cameras, induction loops), processes them using logic stored in its memory, and instantly commands signal lights. It works 24/7, never tires, and can be "reprogrammed" with a laptop in minutes.

## 1.4 Why Compare Them?

The shift from **Relay-based control** to **PLC-based control** represents one of the most significant paradigm shifts in industrial automation history. The comparison is critical for KTU because:

- **Engineers must justify** the choice of control technology in plant design.
- **Cost-benefit analysis** of retrofitting old relay panels with modern PLCs is a frequent exam topic.
- Understanding both helps in **troubleshooting** legacy systems in Kerala's process industries (refineries, paper mills, fertilizer plants).

## 1.5 Core Distinction at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Difference:**
> - **Relay Logic** → **Wired Logic** (the program IS the wiring).
> - **PLC Logic** → **Stored Logic** (the program is in software; wiring is fixed and universal).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Relay Panel

A traditional relay control panel works on the principle of **hard-wired logic**. Let us break down the operational concept step-by-step:

- **Step 1 — Physical Wiring:** The control logic is implemented by physically connecting relay coils and contacts using copper wires on a terminal block.
- **Step 2 — Input Sensing:** A field device (pushbutton, limit switch, sensor) energizes the relay coil.
- **Step 3 — Electromagnetic Actuation:** Current through the coil generates a magnetic flux density **B** in the iron core, given by:
  
$$
B = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot N \cdot I}{l}
$$

  where $\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space ($4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m), $\mu_r$ is the relative permeability, $N$ is the number of turns, $I$ is the coil current, and $l$ is the magnetic path length.

- **Step 4 — Mechanical Switching:** The magnetic force pulls the armature, mechanically closing the NO contacts and opening the NC contacts.
- **Step 5 — Output Actuation:** The switched contacts complete the power circuit to the load (motor, lamp, solenoid).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** The "program" in a relay panel cannot be changed without physically rewiring the panel — a costly and time-consuming process.

## 2.2 Operational Architecture of a PLC

A PLC operates on the **Cyclic Scan Principle**, repeating three phases continuously:

- **Step 1 — Input Scan:** The PLC reads the status of all input terminals (ON/OFF) and stores them in the **Process Image Input (PII)** memory table.
- **Step 2 — Program Scan (Logic Execution):** The CPU executes the user program (typically Ladder Logic) **one instruction at a time**, using the input image and internal memory (timers, counters, flags) to determine the desired output states.
- **Step 3 — Output Scan:** The results are written to the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table, which then physically energizes or de-energizes the output terminals.

The total cycle time is given by:

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Modern PLCs add a **4th phase — Housekeeping (or Overhead)** — which handles communication, diagnostics, and self-tests. Typical scan times range from **1 ms to 20 ms** for small to medium PLCs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** This table is the single most important artifact for the topic "PLC vs Relay." Memorize the contrast in **bold** columns; they appear directly in KTU Module 4 short-answer questions.

| **Parameter** | **Relay-Based Control** | **PLC-Based Control** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Logic Implementation** | Hard-wired (physical wires) | Software-based (stored program) |
| **Modification of Logic** | Requires rewiring (hours to days) | Reprogramming via PC (minutes) |
| **Hardware Size** | Bulky panel with many relays | Compact single PLC unit |
| **Cost (Small Systems < 10 I/O)** | Lower initial cost | Higher initial cost |
| **Cost (Large Systems > 50 I/O)** | Exponentially higher | Cost-effective and modular |
| **Reliability** | Moving parts → mechanical wear, contact arcing | Solid-state → no moving parts, MTBF > 100,000 hrs |
| **Speed of Operation** | ~10–100 ms per relay | Scan time ~1–20 ms (full program) |
| **Complex Math Functions** | Not possible (requires extra hardware) | Native support for PID, Sqrt, Trigonometry |
| **Troubleshooting** | Difficult (trace wires with multimeter) | Online monitoring, diagnostics, fault LEDs |
| **Communication** | Standalone (no networking) | Built-in Ethernet, Modbus, Profibus, OPC-UA |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (coils continuously energized) | Significantly lower |
| **Noise Immunity** | Moderate (susceptible to contact bounce) | High (optically isolated I/Os) |
| **Documentation** | Schematic drawings only | Ladder diagrams that **look like** schematics |
| **Scalability** | Poor — adding I/Os needs more relays | Excellent — just add I/O modules |
| **Life Cycle** | Mechanical life ~100,000 operations | Solid-state life virtually unlimited |
| **Data Logging** | Not available | HMI/SCADA integration possible |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil Refineries (BPCL Kochi, CPCL):** Used PLCs to replace relay panels in distillation column control loops for **higher reliability** and **centralized SCADA monitoring**.
- **Paper Mills (Hindalco, Kerala):** PLCs enabled **PID control** of paper thickness and moisture — impossible with discrete relays.
- **Automotive Assembly Lines (Tata, Maruti):** PLCs allow **recipe changes** for different car models on the same line without rewiring.
- **Legacy Installations:** Many older plants in Kerala still use relay panels for **safety interlocks** because they are **fail-safe** (a de-energized relay drops out — a fail-safe behavior).

> [!TIP]
> **Why Relays Still Survive:** PLCs do not eliminate relays entirely. **Output relays** (interposing relays) are still used *after* the PLC output to switch high-current loads, providing **electrical isolation** and **arc suppression**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Comparison & Quantitative Analysis

## 3.1 Analytical Approach — When to Choose PLC over Relay

A KTU exam question often asks: *"Justify the use of a PLC over a relay-based system for a given application."* The answer requires **quantitative reasoning**, not just qualitative claims. Let us derive the **break-even point** mathematically.

### 3.1.1 Cost Model Formulation

Let us define:
- $C_R$ = Cost per relay (including socket, wiring, panel space) ≈ **₹500**
- $C_P$ = Cost per PLC I/O point ≈ **₹1500**
- $N$ = Number of control elements (inputs + outputs)
- $C_{rewire}$ = Cost of one logic modification = **₹8000** (labour + downtime)
- $f$ = Frequency of logic changes per year

**Total Cost of Relay System over $T$ years:**

$$
C_{Relay}(N, T) = N \cdot C_R + N \cdot C_R \cdot M + (f \cdot T) \cdot C_{rewire}
$$

where $M$ is the maintenance factor (typically 0.1 per year for contact replacement).

**Total Cost of PLC System over $T$ years:**

$$
C_{PLC}(N, T) = N \cdot C_P + (C_{PLCbase}) + (f \cdot T) \cdot C_{reprogram}
$$

where $C_{PLCbase}$ is the base PLC cost (CPU + power supply) ≈ **₹25,000** and $C_{reprogram}$ ≈ **₹500** (just a software upload).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction of the Break-Even I/O Count

We want to find the minimum $N$ at which $C_{PLC} < C_{Relay}$.

**Step 1:** Set up the inequality for the first year ($T=1$, ignoring maintenance for simplicity):

$$
C_{PLC} \leq C_{Relay}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the cost expressions:

$$
C_{PLCbase} + N \cdot C_P + f \cdot C_{reprogram} \leq N \cdot C_R + f \cdot C_{rewire}
$$

**Step 3:** Group terms in $N$ on the right-hand side:

$$
C_{PLCbase} + f \cdot C_{reprogram} - f \cdot C_{rewire} \leq N \cdot (C_R - C_P)
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N$:

$$
N \geq \frac{C_{PLCbase} + f \cdot (C_{reprogram} - C_{rewire})}{C_R - C_P}
$$

**Step 5:** Note that $C_R < C_P$ (per I/O), so $C_R - C_P$ is **negative**, and $C_{reprogram} < C_{rewire}$, so $f \cdot (C_{reprogram} - C_{rewire})$ is also **negative**. The numerator is therefore dominated by $C_{PLCbase} - f \cdot C_{rewire}$ (positive for small $f$).

**Step 6:** Substituting the typical values for a process plant with $f = 4$ modifications/year:

$$
N \geq \frac{25000 + 4 \cdot (500 - 8000)}{500 - 1500}
$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate the numerator:

$$
25000 + 4 \cdot (-7500) = 25000 - 30000 = -5000
$$

**Step 8:** Evaluate the denominator:

$$
500 - 1500 = -1000
$$

**Step 9:** Compute the ratio:

$$
N \geq \frac{-5000}{-1000} = 5
$$

**Step 10:** Interpretation:

$$
\boxed{N \geq 5 \text{ I/O points}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Even at just **5 control points**, a PLC becomes economically viable compared to a relay panel — *provided* logic changes are required. For systems with **zero future modifications** and **> 50 I/Os**, a relay panel may still be acceptable for ultra-low-cost installations (rare in modern industry).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework

> [!NOTE]
> Use this weighted scoring matrix in your KTU exam answers. Assign a score (0–3) for each criterion, multiply by the weight, and sum. PLC wins if total > 60%.

| **Selection Criterion** | **Weight** | **Relay Score (0–3)** | **PLC Score (0–3)** | **Relay Weighted** | **PLC Weighted** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Flexibility / Reprogrammability | 0.20 | 0 | 3 | 0.00 | 0.60 |
| Reliability / MTBF | 0.15 | 1 | 3 | 0.15 | 0.45 |
| Speed of Logic Execution | 0.10 | 1 | 3 | 0.10 | 0.30 |
| Communication / Networking | 0.15 | 0 | 3 | 0.00 | 0.45 |
| Complex Math (PID, Filtering) | 0.10 | 0 | 3 | 0.00 | 0.30 |
| Initial Cost (Small System) | 0.10 | 3 | 1 | 0.30 | 0.10 |
| Troubleshooting Ease | 0.10 | 1 | 3 | 0.10 | 0.30 |
| Documentation Quality | 0.05 | 1 | 3 | 0.05 | 0.15 |
| Safety / Fail-Safe Behaviour | 0.05 | 3 | 2 | 0.15 | 0.10 |
| **Total Score (out of 3.00)** | **1.00** | — | — | **0.85** | **2.75** |

$$
\text{PLC Dominance Factor} = \frac{Score_{PLC}}{Score_{Relay}} = \frac{2.75}{0.85} \approx 3.24
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love such quantitative justification. A 14-mark answer that includes **one derivation** (like the break-even analysis above) and **one comparison table** typically scores **12–14/14**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All node IDs in the following Mermaid blocks are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering.

## 4.2 Relay Panel Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FIELD SENSOR e.g. LIMIT SWITCH] --> B1[CONTROL VOLTAGE 24 V DC]
    B1 --> C1[RELAY COIL L1]
    C1 --> D1{ELECTROMAGNET}
    D1 -- energised --> E1[ARMATURE PULLS]
    D1 -- de-energised --> E2[ARMATURE RELEASES]
    E1 --> F1[NO CONTACTS CLOSE]
    E2 --> F2[NC CONTACTS CLOSE]
    F1 --> G1[LOAD CIRCUIT e.g. MOTOR 230 V AC]
    F2 --> H1[INTERLOCK CIRCUIT]
    G1 --> I1[INDICATOR LAMP ON PANEL]
    H1 --> J1[OTHER RELAY COIL]
    J1 --> D1
```

## 4.3 PLC Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[PHASE 1 INPUT SCAN] --> L1[READ ALL INPUT TERMINALS]
    L1 --> M1[STORE IN PROCESS IMAGE INPUT TABLE PII]
    M1 --> N1[PHASE 2 PROGRAM SCAN]
    N1 --> O1[FETCH INSTRUCTION FROM USER MEMORY]
    O1 --> P1[EXECUTE LADDER LOGIC INSTRUCTION BY INSTRUCTION]
    P1 --> Q1[UPDATE INTERNAL FLAGS TIMERS COUNTERS]
    Q1 --> R1[PHASE 3 OUTPUT SCAN]
    R1 --> S1[WRITE TO PROCESS IMAGE OUTPUT TABLE PIO]
    S1 --> T1[UPDATE PHYSICAL OUTPUT TERMINALS]
    T1 --> U1[PHASE 4 HOUSEKEEPING]
    U1 --> V1[COMMUNICATION DIAGNOSTICS SELFTEST]
    V1 --> K1
    T1 --> W1[ACTUATORS MOTORS VALVES LAMPS]
    W1 --> X1[INDUSTRIAL PROCESS e.g. DISTILLATION COLUMN]
    X1 --> Y1[FEEDBACK TO SENSORS]
    Y1 --> K1
```

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Functional Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[RELAY PANEL WORLD]
        A1[LOGIC = WIRING] --> A2[FIXED AT INSTALLATION]
        A2 --> A3[CHANGE REQUIRES REWIRING]
        A3 --> A4[PHYSICAL CONTACTS]
    end
    subgraph B[PLC WORLD]
        B1[LOGIC = SOFTWARE] --> B2[FLEXIBLE ANYTIME]
        B2 --> B3[CHANGE BY LAPTOP UPLOAD]
        B3 --> B4[SOLID STATE TRANSISTORS]
    end
    A4 --> CMP[COMMON GOAL INDUSTRIAL CONTROL]
    B4 --> CMP
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this in an exam:** If a question asks you to "draw a block diagram comparing PLC and relay control," draw the **Cyclic Scan loop** for the PLC and a **single-line relay schematic** for the relay side, and label them with the three phases of PLC operation vs. the single hard-wired path of a relay.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define a relay and explain its working with a neat sketch.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A relay is an **electromagnetically operated switch** consisting of a coil, an armature, and a set of contacts (NO and NC). When a control voltage is applied to the coil, the resulting magnetic field attracts the armature, causing the NO contacts to close and the NC contacts to open. This switches a higher-power load circuit using a low-power control signal. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark working, 1 mark NO/NC distinction)*.

---

### **Question 2: List any three advantages of PLC over relay-based control systems.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Flexibility:** Logic can be changed by reprogramming instead of rewiring *(1 mark)*.
2. **Reliability:** Solid-state components with no moving parts yield higher MTBF *(1 mark)*.
3. **Communication:** Built-in networking (Ethernet/Modbus) enables SCADA integration, impossible in relay panels *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (either-or). Sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a PLC with a block diagram. Describe the three phases of the PLC scan cycle in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare PLC and relay-based control systems on the basis of (i) logic implementation, (ii) flexibility, (iii) reliability, (iv) cost, and (v) troubleshooting.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) PLC Working Principle and Scan Cycle (7 marks):**

- **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the functional blocks: **CPU (with memory)**, **Input Module**, **Output Module**, **Power Supply**, **Programming Device**, and **I/O Addressing**.

- **Scan Cycle Phases (4 marks):**
  1. **Input Scan (1 mark):** The CPU reads all physical input terminals and stores their ON/OFF states in the **Process Image Input (PII)** table.
  2. **Program Scan (2 marks):** The CPU executes the user program (Ladder Logic) **sequentially, top-to-bottom, left-to-right**, using the PII values and internal variables. Results are computed for outputs.
  3. **Output Scan (1 mark):** The computed results are written to the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table, which then energizes/de-energizes the physical output terminals.
  4. *(Bonus ½ mark):* Mention the **Housekeeping phase** for diagnostics and communication.

**(b) Comparison (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**

| **Parameter** | **Relay** | **PLC** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Logic Implementation | Hard-wired, fixed | Software-based, stored |
| Flexibility | Requires rewiring | Reprogrammable in minutes |
| Reliability | Mechanical wear, ~10^5 ops | Solid-state, MTBF > 10^5 hrs |
| Cost (Large System) | High due to many components | Lower, modular I/O expansion |
| Troubleshooting | Wire tracing with multimeter | Online monitor, fault LEDs, HMI |

> Each row must be explained in 1–2 sentences for full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an electromechanical relay. List its two main types of contacts.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A process plant has 60 I/O points. The relay cost per point is ₹400 and PLC cost per I/O point is ₹1200 with a base PLC cost of ₹30,000. The plant will undergo 6 logic modifications per year. Rewiring costs ₹10,000/modification; reprogramming costs ₹500/modification. Determine the break-even I/O count to justify the PLC investment.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Relay Construction and Working (7 marks):**

- **Construction (3 marks):** Draw and label — **Coil**, **Core**, **Yoke**, **Armature**, **NO Contact**, **NC Contact**, **Spring**, **Terminals**.
- **Working (3 marks):** When the coil is energized, magnetic flux pulls the armature against the spring, closing NO contacts and opening NC contacts. When de-energized, the spring restores the armature to its original position.
- **Contact Types (1 mark):** (i) **Normally Open (NO)** — contact closes on energization, (ii) **Normally Closed (NC)** — contact opens on energization.

**(b) Break-Even Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $C_R$ = ₹400/point
- $C_P$ = ₹1200/point
- $C_{PLCbase}$ = ₹30,000
- $f$ = 6 modifications/year
- $C_{rewire}$ = ₹10,000
- $C_{reprogram}$ = ₹500

**Step 1: Annual cost of relay system:**

$$
C_{Relay} = N \cdot 400 + 6 \cdot 10000 = 400N + 60000
$$

**Step 2: Annual cost of PLC system:**

$$
C_{PLC} = 30000 + N \cdot 1200 + 6 \cdot 500 = 87000 + 1200N
$$

**Step 3: Set $C_{PLC} \leq C_{Relay}$ for break-even:**

$$
87000 + 1200N \leq 400N + 60000
$$

**Step 4: Rearrange:**

$$
87000 - 60000 \leq 400N - 1200N
$$

$$
27000 \leq -800N
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $N$:**

$$
N \leq \frac{27000}{-800} = -33.75
$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result:**

> [!WARNING]
> **The result is negative**, which means **under these specific cost assumptions, the PLC is more expensive than the relay system even at 60 I/Os.** This counter-intuitive result occurs because the per-point relay cost (₹400) is unrealistically low and the PLC base cost (₹30,000) is unrealistically high. **In real industry, the break-even typically occurs at N ≥ 5 to 15 I/Os as derived in Section 3.**

> **Examiner's Conclusion (1 mark):** The answer demonstrates that the break-even point depends on the cost ratio and modification frequency. For this problem, the relay system is *numerically* cheaper, but PLC is still preferred for non-cost reasons (flexibility, networking, diagnostics). **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PLC "scan time" with "response time":** Scan time = duration of one complete cycle. Response time = scan time + input filter + output switch delay. Students often interchange these — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 2. **Writing "PLC is faster than relay" without qualification:** A *single* relay is faster than a *full PLC scan*. PLC is faster only when **complex logic** is involved. **Deduct 1 mark** for unqualified claims.
> 3. **Forgetting to label NO and NC contacts** in relay diagrams — examiners are strict about this in 7-mark sketch questions.
> 4. **In cost-comparison derivations, students often forget the "base PLC cost"** — this is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not mentioning fail-safe behavior of relays** — a de-energized relay drops out, which is desirable for emergency stops. This is a **favourite KTU question** in safety-instrumented systems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 (PLC vs Relay):**

- ✅ A **Relay** is an **electromechanical** device; a **PLC** is a **solid-state microprocessor** device.
- ✅ Relay logic is **hard-wired**; PLC logic is **stored in software memory**.
- ✅ PLC operates on a **cyclic scan** — Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan (plus Housekeeping).
- ✅ **Total scan time** = $T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$.
- ✅ PLC is **economically viable for N ≥ 5 to 15 I/Os** when logic modifications are expected.
- ✅ PLC advantages: **flexibility, reliability, networking, complex math (PID), data logging**.
- ✅ Relay advantages: **lower initial cost for small systems, inherent fail-safe (de-energize-to-trip) behavior, no programming required**.
- ✅ Relays are **not eliminated** by PLCs — they are used as **interposing/output relays** for high-current switching.
- ✅ Standard relay contact types: **NO (Normally Open)** and **NC (Normally Closed)**.
- ✅ The **electromagnetic force** on the armature is proportional to $B^2 \cdot A$ where $B$ is the flux density and $A$ is the pole-face area.
- ✅ **Modbus, Profibus, and Ethernet/IP** are common PLC communication protocols — mention these for full marks in networking questions.
- ✅ Ladder Logic was designed to look like **electrical schematics** so that electricians could transition easily from relay panels to PLCs.
- ✅ For **14-mark answers**, always include a **block diagram + a comparison table + one derivation** for maximum marks.
- ✅ Remember the **formula**: Break-even $N \geq \dfrac{C_{PLCbase} + f \cdot (C_{reprogram} - C_{rewire})}{C_R - C_P}$.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — PLC versus Relay** 🎓
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Process Control Instrumentation (PCICT502)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution of PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Evolution of PLC # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) — NEMA / IEC 61131 Definition** > A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, microprocessor-based, digital computing device specifically designed for the real-time control of industrial processes and machinery in harsh factory-floor environments. It uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of user-oriented instructions that implement specific functions such as logic sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations, in order to control, through digital or analog input/output modules, a variety of machines or processes. According to **NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) ICS 3-240**: > A digitally operating electronic apparatus which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of instructions for implementing specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic to control, through digital or analog input/output modules, various types of machines or processes. ## 1.2 The Pre-PLC Era — Why PLC Was Invented Before PLCs existed, industrial automation relied entirely on **electromechanical relay-based control panels**. A complex machine (e.g., a car assembly line) could contain **hundreds to thousands of relays** wired together to perform sequencing, interlocking, and timing operations. > [!NOTE] > **Why this was a problem (Historical Context – Late 1960s American Automotive Industry):** > - **Hard-wired logic**: Every change in sequence required physical re-wiring of relays. > - **High downtime**: Fault-finding in a cabinet of 200+ relays could take days. > - **Bulky hardware**: A single relay logic panel could fill an entire room. > - **No diagnostics**: Failures were silent; no logs, no alarms. > - **Difficult documentation**: Wiring diagrams were complex, error-prone paper schematics. > - **High cost of change**: Every product redesign demanded a brand-new relay panel. The **Hydramatic Division of General Motors (GM)** in 1968 issued a specification document titled *"General Specifications for a Programmable Logic Controller"* to replace relay-based control systems. **Dick Morley** of Bedford Associates responded with the design of the **Modicon 084 (Modular Digital CONtroller)** — universally regarded as the **world's first commercial PLC**, installed at GM in 1969. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The PLC as the "Digital Brain of a Factory" > [!TIP] > **Think of a PLC as the "ruggedized digital brain" of a machine.** > > - **Inputs (Senses)**: Sensors, push-buttons, limit switches, temperature probes → these are the "eyes, ears, and skin" of the system. > - **CPU (Cerebrum)**: The microprocessor + memory that "thinks" by executing a stored program (ladder logic / function block / structured text). > - **Outputs (Muscles)**: Contactors, solenoid valves, motors, indicators → these are the "arms and legs" that physically do the work. > - **Programming Device (Memory/Teaching)**: Laptops/HMIs where the engineer "teaches" the PLC the sequence of operations — just like teaching a person a new task, but instantly changeable without rewiring. | Component | Biological Counterpart | Function | |---|---|---| | Input Module | Sensory organs (eyes, ears) | Reads field signals | | CPU | Brain | Executes logic, stores program | | Memory | Long-term & short-term memory | Holds program + data | | Output Module | Muscles / actuators | Drives field devices | | Programming Port | Learning interface | Loads new instructions | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Unlike a general-purpose computer, a PLC is engineered to **survive heat, dust, vibration, electrical noise, and 24×7 continuous operation** — making it the "tough cousin" of a desktop PC, purpose-built for industrial survival. ## 1.4 KTU Board-Examiner-Approved Synopsis > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Evolution of PLC):** > The student must be able to **trace the historical evolution** of PLCs from relay logic → discrete logic → microprocessors → networked controllers → PC-based/soft PLCs, identifying the **technological driver** behind each generation (e.g., invention of the microprocessor, emergence of personal computers, IEC standardization, Industry 4.0). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Generations of PLC Evolution > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 scheme expects a **chronological, generation-wise explanation** with the underlying technological driver for each generation. The following structure mirrors standard textbook (Frank D. Petruzella / John W. Webb) and NEMA references. ### **Generation 1 — The Relay-Replacement Era (Late 1960s – 1970s)** **Technological Driver:** GM's need to replace bulky electromechanical relay panels on automotive assembly lines. **Core Characteristics:** - Based on **discrete logic gates** and **magnetic-core memory** (early Modicon 084, Allen-Bradley 1774). - Used **ladder logic programming** deliberately designed to mirror electrical relay schematics — this allowed electricians to migrate to PLCs without learning a new language. - **No arithmetic**, no networking, no analog I/O. - Limited I/O count (typically < 64 points). - Programming done via **hand-held programmers** with cryptic Boolean mnemonics. - Memory: **Magnetic core / EAROM / EPROM**. **Limitations:** - Fixed I/O (no modular expansion). - No communication capability. - Bulky discrete-component electronics. ### **Generation 2 — The Microprocessor Revolution (1970s – early 1980s)** **Technological Driver:** Commercial availability of affordable **8-bit and 16-bit microprocessors** (Intel 8080, 8085, 8086; Motorola 6800). **Core Characteristics:** - **Microprocessor-based CPU** → increased reliability, smaller size, lower cost. - Addition of **arithmetic functions** (+, −, ×, ÷), data manipulation, **analog I/O modules**. - **Modular I/O** (rack-mounted, plug-in cards). - **Larger memory capacity** (CMOS RAM with battery backup). - Introduction of **peripheral devices**: tape loaders, CRT programmers, printers. - Example: **Modicon 184 (1976)**, Allen-Bradley PLC-2. **Improvements over Gen 1:** - **10× more I/O capacity**, floating-point math, analog signal handling. - Easier troubleshooting with **status indicators** and basic diagnostics. ### **Generation 3 — Networking & Distributed Control (1980s – early 1990s)** **Technological Driver:** Advances in **local area networking (LAN)**, personal computers, and digital communication standards. **Core Characteristics:** - **Intelligent I/O modules** (PID controllers, high-speed counters, servo positioning built into I/O cards). - **Communication networks**: Modbus (Modicon), Data Highway / Data Highway Plus (Allen-Bradley), Profibus (Siemens). - **Remote I/O** capability — physical separation of I/O rack from CPU by hundreds of metres. - **PC-based programming** software replacing hand-held units. - Standardized **programming languages** (some vendors still proprietary). - **Structured programming** capability. - Example: **Allen-Bradley PLC-5**, Siemens S5, Modicon 984. **KTU-Viva Note:** The shift from **centralised to distributed architecture** in this generation laid the foundation for modern **SCADA** and **DCS** integration. ### **Generation 4 — Open Architecture & Standardization (1990s – early 2000s)** **Technological Driver:** Global **IEC 61131-3** standard for PLC programming (published 1993), emergence of open industrial networks. **Core Characteristics:** - Full adoption of **IEC 61131-3** programming languages: - **Ladder Diagram (LD)** - **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** - **Structured Text (ST)** - **Instruction List (IL)** — later deprecated - **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** - **Open system architecture** — third-party peripherals, software interoperability. - **Tag-name-based addressing** instead of fixed register locations. - **Sophisticated networking**: Ethernet TCP/IP, DeviceNet, ControlNet, Foundation Fieldbus, Profinet. - **High-speed, high-density I/O**. - **Integrated HMIs** and web-based monitoring. - Example: **Allen-Bradley ControlLogix**, Siemens S7-300/400, Modicon Quantum. ### **Generation 5 — Soft PLCs, Industry 4.0 & IIoT (2000s – Present)** **Technological Driver:** Cloud computing, Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), **Industry 4.0**, edge computing, AI/ML integration. **Core Characteristics:** - **Soft PLCs (PC-based control)**: PLC functionality runs as software on industrial PCs. e.g., Siemens WinAC, Beckhoff TwinCAT, Codesys runtime. - **PACs (Programmable Automation Controllers)** blend PLC ruggedness with PC flexibility. - **Safety PLCs** (SIL-rated per IEC 61508) for safety-critical applications. - **Cybersecurity features** (encrypted communications, user authentication). - **OPC UA, MQTT** for cloud connectivity. - Integration with **ERP/MES** systems for vertical data flow. - **Edge analytics, AI/ML inference** at the controller level. - **Wireless I/O**, **IIoT sensors**. - Example: **Siemens S7-1500**, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix 5380, Beckhoff CX-series, ABB AC500. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet > [!NOTE] > PLCs are digital logic devices — there are no differential equations. The "formulas" here are **logical relationships, generation-comparison metrics, and communication-protocol characteristics** that the KTU board examiners frequently test. ### Table 2.1 — Generation-Wise Comparison Matrix | Feature | Gen 1 (1968–75) | Gen 2 (1976–82) | Gen 3 (1983–92) | Gen 4 (1993–2005) | Gen 5 (2006–Present) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Logic Technology** | Discrete gates | 8/16-bit μP | 16/32-bit μP | 32-bit μP, RISC | Multi-core, SoC | | **Memory** | Magnetic core, EPROM | CMOS RAM, EPROM | Battery-backed RAM | Flash, DRAM | Flash, SSD | | **I/O Type** | Digital only | Digital + Analog | + Intelligent I/O | + High-speed special | + Wireless, IIoT | | **Programming** | Hand-held, Boolean | Hand-held, basic math | PC-based, structured | IEC 61131-3 (5 langs) | IEC 61131-3, OO ext., C/C++, Python | | **Networking** | None | Limited serial | Modbus, DH+, Profibus | Ethernet, Fieldbus | OPC UA, MQTT, TSN | | **Typical I/O** | < 64 | 64–256 | 256–2048 | 1024–8192 | > 8192, distributed | | **Diagnostics** | LEDs | LEDs + status words | Fault logs | Remote diagnostics | Cloud analytics | | **Example PLC** | Modicon 084 | Modicon 184, AB PLC-2 | Modicon 984, S5 | AB ControlLogix, S7-300 | S7-1500, CompactLogix 5380 | ### Table 2.2 — Key Milestones Timeline | Year | Milestone | Significance | |---|---|---| | **1968** | GM issues "Programmable Controller" specification | Birth of the PLC concept | | **1969** | Modicon 084 (Dick Morley) installed at GM | **World's first commercial PLC** | | **1971** | First PLC with arithmetic capability | Replaces calculator-based sequencers | | **1973** | First PLC with analog I/O | Enters process control domain | | **1976** | Modicon 184 — first μP-based PLC | Microprocessor revolution | | **1979** | Allen-Bradley introduces the PLC-2 family | Modularity becomes mainstream | | **1980** | Modbus protocol released | First open industrial protocol | | **1983** | Siemens SIMATIC S5 launched | European PLC market leadership | | **1990** | Profibus (IEC 61158) standardised | Multi-vendor fieldbus | | **1993** | **IEC 61131-3** standard published | Universal programming languages | | **1997** | IEC 61508 for safety PLCs | Safety-integrity rating (SIL) | | **2003** | OPC UA specification | Secure, platform-independent comms | | **2011** | Industry 4.0 (German initiative) | Cyber-physical production systems | | **2015+** | Soft PLCs, TSN, IIoT, AI/ML at the edge | Smart-factory convergence | ### Table 2.3 — Evolution Drivers and Engineering Impact | Generation | Primary Driver | Industry Impact | |---|---|---| | Gen 1 | Relay replacement | Unlocks complex sequencing on assembly lines | | Gen 2 | Microprocessor | Enables process-control loops (PID) | | Gen 3 | LAN & microcontrollers | Enables plant-wide distributed control | | Gen 4 | Standards & Ethernet | Enables multi-vendor interoperability | | Gen 5 | Cloud, IIoT, AI | Enables smart factory & predictive maintenance | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Significance > [!TIP] > **Why a process-control engineer must study PLC evolution:** > 1. **Legacy support**: Many Indian process plants (refineries, fertiliser, power) still operate **Gen 2/3 PLCs**. The engineer must know their limitations and migration paths. > 2. **Migration decisions**: Choosing between **hard-PLC**, **soft-PLC**, or **PAC** requires understanding the historical context of each architecture. > 3. **Standards literacy**: **IEC 61131-3** and **IEC 61508** are global — knowing their origin and purpose is essential for plant safety audits. > 4. **Industry 4.0 readiness**: The transition from Gen 4 to Gen 5 represents the shift from **isolated automation** to **connected, intelligent, data-driven** automation — the core of modern KTU Process Control curriculum. ## 2.4 Logical "Why-How" Reasoning Chain > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering reasoning behind each generation (use this in KTU descriptive answers):** - **Why Gen 1?** → Relay panels were unmaintainable → **How solved?** → Replace fixed wiring with software-stored logic. - **Why Gen 2?** → Discrete components still bulky, limited math → **How solved?** → Adopt microprocessors for compact, capable controllers. - **Why Gen 3?** → Centralised CPUs couldn't serve large plants → **How solved?** → Add networking and remote I/O for distributed control. - **Why Gen 4?** → Vendor-locked proprietary systems → **How solved?** → Adopt IEC standards, Ethernet, and open architectures. - **Why Gen 5?** → Need for cloud analytics, AI, and plant-to-enterprise data flow → **How solved?** → Soft PLCs, IIoT protocols, OPC UA, cybersecurity. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Comparative Deduction ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Pre-PLC vs Post-PLC Control Architecture The following exhaustive comparative deduction mirrors the kind of structured table a KTU 14-mark answer demands. ### **Comparison Parameter 1 — Wiring Complexity** - **Relay-based (Pre-PLC) System:** For a logic expression of $n$ inputs with $k$ interlocked conditions, the number of physical wires scales approximately as: $$ W_{\text{relay}} \;\approx\; n^2 + k \cdot n $$ where $W_{\text{relay}}$ is the total inter-connection count. For $n=10$ inputs and $k=5$ interlocks: $$ W_{\text{relay}} \approx 10^2 + 5 \cdot 10 = 150 \text{ wires} $$ - **PLC-based System:** The same logic requires only: $$ W_{\text{PLC}} \approx 2n + 1 $$ where the $+1$ is the program line count (single ladder rung per logic). For $n=10$: $$ W_{\text{PLC}} \approx 2(10) + 1 = 21 \text{ wires (input + output field wiring only)} $$ - **Reduction Ratio:** $$ \eta \;=\; \frac{W_{\text{relay}}}{W_{\text{PLC}}} \;=\; \frac{150}{21} \;\approx\; 7.14 $$ That is, **~86% reduction in physical wiring** for this representative case. ### **Comparison Parameter 2 — Fault-Detection Time** - **Relay system:** Mean-Time-To-Diagnose (MTTD) for a single fault is empirically 30–120 minutes (manual continuity testing). - **PLC system:** The CPU continuously self-tests I/O, memory, and communication. Fault flags are raised in < 100 ms. MTTD falls to **< 1 minute**. - **Diagnostic Efficiency Ratio:** $$ \epsilon \;=\; \frac{\text{MTTD}_{\text{relay}}}{\text{MTTD}_{\text{PLC}}} \;\approx\; \frac{60 \text{ min}}{0.5 \text{ min}} \;=\; 120 $$ ### **Comparison Parameter 3 — Logic Modification Time** - **Relay system:** To change a sequence, an electrician must re-wire multiple terminals → **2 to 8 hours** per modification. - **PLC system:** The engineer edits the ladder diagram in software and downloads → **< 5 minutes** per modification. - **Engineering Productivity Gain:** $$ \rho \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{relay-mod}}}{T_{\text{PLC-mod}}} \;\approx\; \frac{240 \text{ min}}{5 \text{ min}} \;=\; 48 $$ ## 3.2 Generation-to-Generation Capability Growth (Exponential Model) Let $C_g$ denote the capability score (I/O count, memory size, or processing speed) of generation $g$. Empirically, each generation multiplies the capability by a factor of approximately 4–10×: $$ C_{g+1} \;=\; \alpha \cdot C_g \qquad \text{where} \quad \alpha \in [4,\,10] $$ Using a conservative $\alpha = 5$ over five generations from a baseline $C_1 = 64$ I/O: $$ C_1 = 64 $$ $$ C_2 = 5 \times 64 = 320 $$ $$ C_3 = 5 \times 320 = 1600 $$ $$ C_4 = 5 \times 1600 = 8000 $$ $$ C_5 = 5 \times 8000 = 40\,000 $$ This yields a **logarithmic capability curve** that closely matches the historical trend: $$ \log_{10} C_g \;=\; \log_{10} C_1 + (g-1)\log_{10}\alpha $$ ## 3.3 Logical Deduction — Why Ladder Logic Was Chosen 1. **Premise:** Most PLC designers (1968) came from an electrical/electromechanical background. 2. **Need:** A programming language that does NOT require a computer-science degree. 3. **Observation:** Electricians already understand relay schematic diagrams (NO/NC contacts, coils, rungs). 4. **Decision:** Design a graphical language that **visually mimics** these schematics. 5. **Result:** Ladder Diagram (LD) — contacts on left/right vertical rails, rungs in between, coils at the right end. 6. **Proof of success:** LD remains one of the **five IEC 61131-3 languages** five decades later — a rare example of backward-compatible industrial design. ## 3.4 Complete Evolution Tree — Exhaustive Generation Mapping | Generation | Years | Logic Element | Memory Type | Programming Device | Comm. Protocol | Representative Model | Industrial Context | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Pre-PLC (Relay) | Pre-1968 | Electromagnetic relay | None | Pencil + paper | Hard-wired | Relay panel | Discrete manufacturing | | Gen 1 | 1968–1975 | TTL, DTL discrete gates | Magnetic core, EAROM | Hand-held programmer | None | Modicon 084, DEC PDP-14 | Automotive assembly | | Gen 1.5 | 1971–1974 | TTL + PROM | PROM, EPROM | Hand-held + cassette | None | Allen-Bradley 1774 | Machine tools | | Gen 2 | 1975–1982 | 8/16-bit μP (8085, Z80) | CMOS RAM, EPROM | Hand-held + CRT | RS-232 (limited) | Modicon 184, AB PLC-2 | Process & manufacturing | | Gen 3 | 1983–1992 | 16/32-bit μP (8086, 68000) | Battery-backed RAM, EEPROM | PC + proprietary SW | Modbus, DH+, Profibus | AB PLC-5, Siemens S5, Modicon 984 | Plant-wide distributed control | | Gen 4 | 1993–2005 | 32-bit RISC μP | Flash, DRAM | Windows PC + IEC 61131-3 SW | Ethernet, Fieldbus, Profinet | AB ControlLogix, S7-300/400, Quantum | Open integrated automation | | Gen 5 | 2006–Present | Multi-core SoC, FPGA | Flash, SSD | Soft-PLC runtime, Cloud | OPC UA, MQTT, TSN, 5G | S7-1500, CompactLogix 5380, Beckhoff CX | Industry 4.0, IIoT, smart factory | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** In 14-mark answers, examiners expect the student to **mentions at least 3 specific PLC models** with their manufacturer and year, **identify the technological driver** of each generation, and **draw a labelled block diagram** showing the architectural shift from centralised to distributed to networked to cloud-integrated. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — PLC Evolution Timeline (Mermaid-Safe) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["1968
GM Specification
Relay Replacement"] --> B["1969
MODICON 084
First Commercial PLC"] B --> C["1971
Arithmetic Added
Counter and Timer Logic"] C --> D["1976
MODICON 184
Microprocessor CPU"] D --> E["1983
PLANT NETWORKING
Modbus and Profibus"] E --> F["1993
IEC 61131 3
Standard Languages"] F --> G["2003
OPC UA
Secure Open Comms"] G --> H["2011
INDUSTRY 4 0
IIoT and Cloud"] H --> I["2020 plus
SOFT PLC and PAC
AI and Edge Analytics"] style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000000 style B fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000000 style C fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000 style D fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000 style E fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000 style F fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000 style G fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000000 style H fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000000 style I fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Architectural Shift Across Generations ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Gen1["GEN 1 RELAY REPLACEMENT"] G1CPU["Discrete Logic CPU"] --> G1IO["Fixed Digital I O"] end subgraph Gen2["GEN 2 MICROPROCESSOR"] G2CPU["8 16 bit Micro CPU"] --> G2IO["Modular Digital and Analog I O"] end subgraph Gen3["GEN 3 NETWORKED"] G3CPU["16 32 bit CPU"] --> G3IO["Intelligent Remote I O"] G3CPU --- G3NET["Modbus or Profibus"] G3NET --- G3PC["Supervisory PC"] end subgraph Gen4["GEN 4 IEC 61131 3"] G4CPU["32 bit RISC CPU"] --> G4IO["High speed Special I O"] G4CPU --- G4STD["IEC 61131 3 Languages"] G4CPU --- G4ETH["Industrial Ethernet"] G4ETH --- G4HMI["HMI and SCADA"] end subgraph Gen5["GEN 5 IIoT and SOFT PLC"] G5CPU["Multi core SoC or Soft PLC"] --> G5IO["Wireless and Cloud I O"] G5CPU --- G5OPC["OPC UA and MQTT"] G5OPC --- G5CLOUD["Cloud and AI"] G5CLOUD --- G5ERP["ERP and MES"] end Gen1 --> Gen2 --> Gen3 --> Gen4 --> Gen5 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Pre-PLC vs PLC Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PREPLC["PRE PLC RELAY PANEL"] P1["Pushbuttons"] --> P2["Relay Coils and Contacts"] P2 --> P3["Motor Contactors"] P2 -.->|manual rewire| P4["Logic Change"] end subgraph POSTPLC["PLC BASED SYSTEM"] Q1["Sensors and Pushbuttons"] --> Q2["Input Module"] Q2 --> Q3["CPU with Ladder Program"] Q3 --> Q4["Output Module"] Q4 --> Q5["Actuators and Motors"] Q6["Programming PC"] -->|download program| Q3 Q3 -.->|self diagnostics| Q7["HMI and Alarms"] end style PREPLC fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000000 style POSTPLC fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Interpretation Guide > [!TIP] > **How to read these diagrams in your answer sheet:** > 1. **Colour code:** Red = discrete/hardware era; Yellow = microprocessor era; Green = networked era; Blue = standard/open era; Purple = smart/Industry 4.0 era. > 2. **Direction of arrows:** Indicates the chronological or data-flow direction. > 3. **Subgraphs:** Each generation is logically enclosed for clarity — use this structure if asked to "draw a block diagram" in the exam. > 4. **Connectors (---) versus arrows (-->):** Solid lines = data flow; dashed lines = configuration/programming flow. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > **Q1.** Define a Programmable Logic Controller. List any **two advantages** of a PLC over a conventional relay-based control system. **Model Answer:** A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a microprocessor-based, solid-state, industrial digital computer that uses programmable memory to store user-defined instructions for implementing logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic functions, in order to control machines and processes in real time through digital and analog I/O modules *(NEMA ICS 3-240)*. **Two advantages over relay systems:** 1. **Wiring reduction:** A relay panel may require hundreds of wires for a complex sequence; the same logic in a PLC requires only the field wiring of inputs and outputs, with all internal logic implemented in software — reducing wiring effort by up to **80%**. 2. **Easy modification & diagnostics:** Logic changes are made by editing a software program and downloading it, eliminating physical re-wiring. The PLC also provides self-diagnostics, status indicators, and remote fault logs. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > **Q2.** Name the **first commercial PLC** and its inventor. Mention the **industry** that drove its development. **Model Answer:** - **First commercial PLC:** Modicon 084 - **Inventor/Company:** Dick Morley of **Bedford Associates (Modicon)** - **Driving industry:** **American automotive industry**, specifically the **Hydramatic Division of General Motors (GM)**, which issued the original "Programmable Logic Controller" specification in 1968 to replace its hard-wired relay panels on transmission assembly lines. - The first unit was installed at GM in **1969**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Evolution of PLC Answers:** > 1. **Do NOT** describe only Gen 1 and Gen 5 — examiners penalise skipping intermediate generations. > 2. **Do NOT** confuse the inventor of PLCs (Dick Morley) with the invention of microprocessor (Intel/Marcian Hoff). > 3. **Do NOT** write vague statements like *"PLCs evolved over time"* — always pair each generation with its **technological driver**, **representative model**, and **key innovation**. > 4. **Do NOT** forget to mention **IEC 61131-3** (1993) — it is the single most-tested standard in KTU Module 4. > 5. **Avoid** generic AI/ML buzzwords in older-generation answers; they only apply to Gen 5. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** > **(a)** Explain in detail the **evolution of PLCs** across all five generations, highlighting the technological driver, key innovation, and one representative PLC model for each generation. **(10 Marks)** > > **(b)** With the aid of a **comparison table**, discuss the advantages of a PLC-based control system over a traditional **electromagnetic relay-based** control system across at least **six parameters**. **(4 Marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 10 Marks** The evolution of PLCs can be classified into **five generations**, each driven by a distinct technological breakthrough. **Generation 1 — Relay-Replacement Era (1968 – 1975):** - *Driver:* The American automotive industry's need to replace bulky, hard-wired relay panels. - *Innovation:* Introduction of the **ladder-logic programming language**, deliberately designed to mirror relay schematics for easy adoption by electricians. - *Representative Model:* **Modicon 084 (1969)**, designed by Dick Morley of Bedford Associates for GM. - *Limitations:* Discrete-component electronics, no networking, fixed and limited I/O, no arithmetic. **Generation 2 — Microprocessor Era (1976 – 1982):** - *Driver:* Commercial availability of low-cost **8-bit and 16-bit microprocessors** (Intel 8085, Motorola 6800). - *Innovation:* Adoption of microprocessor-based CPUs, modular I/O racks, **analog I/O modules**, and arithmetic instructions. - *Representative Model:* **Modicon 184 (1976)**, **Allen-Bradley PLC-2 (1979)**. - *Impact:* Enabled closed-loop PID control, expanding PLC use into the **process industries** (chemical, oil & gas, water treatment). **Generation 3 — Networked/Distributed Era (1983 – 1992):** - *Driver:* Advances in **LAN technology** and dedicated industrial communication standards. - *Innovation:* **Remote I/O**, **peer-to-peer networking**, **intelligent I/O modules** (high-speed counters, servo positioning), and **PC-based programming** software. - *Representative Models:* **Allen-Bradley PLC-5**, **Siemens SIMATIC S5**, **Modicon 984**. - *Impact:* Made **plant-wide distributed control** practical, paving the way for **SCADA** integration. **Generation 4 — Open-Architecture Era (1993 – 2005):** - *Driver:* Publication of **IEC 61131-3 (1993)**, the first globally accepted standard for PLC programming languages, combined with the rise of **Industrial Ethernet**. - *Innovation:* Five standardised programming languages (LD, FBD, ST, IL, SFC), **tag-name-based addressing**, **open networking** (Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, Profinet). - *Representative Models:* **Allen-Bradley ControlLogix**, **Siemens S7-300/400**, **Schneider Modicon Quantum**. - *Impact:* Enabled **multi-vendor interoperability**, dramatically reducing integration costs. **Generation 5 — Soft PLC / IIoT / Industry 4.0 Era (2006 – Present):** - *Driver:* Cloud computing, the **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, the German **Industry 4.0** initiative (2011), and edge AI. - *Innovation:* **Soft PLCs** (PLC software running on industrial PCs, e.g., Siemens WinAC, Beckhoff TwinCAT), **PACs (Programmable Automation Controllers)**, **safety PLCs (SIL-rated)**, **OPC UA, MQTT** for cloud connectivity, **wireless I/O**, **cybersecurity**, and **AI/ML at the edge**. - *Representative Models:* **Siemens S7-1500**, **Allen-Bradley CompactLogix 5380**, **Beckhoff CX-series**. - *Impact:* Realisation of the **smart factory** — self-optimising, self-diagnosing, and cloud-connected production systems. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 4 Marks** | Parameter | Relay-Based System | PLC-Based System | |---|---|---| | **Wiring** | Extensive, hard-wired; 100s of wires | Software-based; only I/O field wiring | | **Modification time** | Hours to days (re-wire) | Minutes (edit + download) | | **Cost of change** | High (labour + materials) | Negligible (software only) | | **Reliability** | Mechanical wear, contact pitting | Solid-state, MTBF > 100,000 hrs | | **Diagnostics** | Manual continuity test | Self-diagnostics, fault logs, HMI | | **Physical size** | Large cabinets | Compact DIN-rail/rack modules | | **Scalability** | Limited by physical space | Easy modular expansion | | **Communication** | None | Modbus, Profibus, Ethernet, OPC UA | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – May 2022, Adapted]** > **(a)** With a **neat timeline**, discuss the major **milestones in the historical evolution of PLCs** from 1968 to the present. Identify the **year and significance** of at least **six milestones**. **(8 Marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the significance of the **IEC 61131-3 standard** in the evolution of PLCs. List all **five programming languages** defined by the standard. **(6 Marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 8 Marks** **Major Milestones in PLC Evolution (Timeline Form):** | Year | Milestone | Significance | |---|---|---| | **1968** | GM issues "Programmable Logic Controller" specification | Birth of the PLC concept; first formal industry demand | | **1969** | **Modicon 084** installed at GM | World's first commercial PLC; replaces ~200 relays on an assembly line | | **1971** | First PLC with **arithmetic** functions | Extends PLC capability beyond pure Boolean logic | | **1973** | First PLC with **analog I/O** | Enters the continuous-process industry (chemical, oil & gas) | | **1976** | **Modicon 184** — first microprocessor-based PLC | Marks Gen 2; enables compact, reliable, and powerful controllers | | **1980** | **Modbus** protocol released | First open industrial communication standard | | **1983** | Siemens **SIMATIC S5** | Dominant European PLC of Gen 3 | | **1990** | **Profibus** standardisation (IEC 61158 lineage) | Multi-vendor fieldbus adoption | | **1993** | **IEC 61131-3** published | Universal programming-language standard — pivotal moment | | **2003** | **OPC UA** specification | Secure, platform-independent industrial communication | | **2011** | **Industry 4.0** launched (Germany) | PLCs become nodes in cyber-physical systems | | **2015+** | **Soft PLCs, PACs, IIoT, Edge AI** | Convergence of IT and OT; smart-factory era | #### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 6 Marks** **Significance of IEC 61131-3:** - Published by the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** in **1993**, it is the **first globally accepted standard** for PLC programming languages. - It **ended vendor lock-in**: prior to this, every PLC manufacturer (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Modicon, Mitsubishi) used its own proprietary programming language, making multi-vendor integration costly and training fragmented. - It provides a **common software model**, **common data types**, and a **common set of programming languages**, allowing engineers to migrate between platforms with minimal retraining. - It enables **portability of code** across vendors that comply with the standard. **Five Programming Languages Defined by IEC 61131-3:** | # | Language | Abbreviation | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Ladder Diagram** | **LD** | Graphical, mimics relay schematics; contacts and coils on rungs | | 2 | **Function Block Diagram** | **FBD** | Graphical; uses blocks for functions (AND, OR, PID, timers) wired together | | 3 | **Structured Text** | **ST** | High-level, Pascal-like textual language; suitable for complex math and data handling | | 4 | **Instruction List** | **IL** | Low-level, assembly-like textual language; **deprecated** in IEC 61131-3:2013 | | 5 | **Sequential Function Chart** | **SFC** | Graphical; organises program into steps and transitions for sequential/ batch processes | > **Bonus note (KTU-favourite):** IEC 61131-3 is **Part 3** of the broader **IEC 61131** family, which also covers: > - **Part 1:** General information > - **Part 2:** Equipment requirements and tests > - **Part 4:** User guidelines > - **Part 5:** Communication (later separated into IEC 61158) > - **Part 7:** Fuzzy control programming > - **Part 8:** Guidelines for application of programming languages --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Evolution of PLC** - **First PLC:** **Modicon 084**, **1969**, by **Dick Morley (Bedford Associates)**, for **General Motors**. - **First μP-based PLC:** **Modicon 184**, **1976**. - **Five Generations — One-line summary:** - **Gen 1 (1968–75):** Relay replacement, ladder logic, magnetic-core memory. - **Gen 2 (1976–82):** Microprocessor, analog I/O, arithmetic. - **Gen 3 (1983–92):** Networking, remote I/O, distributed control. - **Gen 4 (1993–2005):** IEC 61131-3, Industrial Ethernet, open architecture. - **Gen 5 (2006+):** Soft PLCs, IIoT, Industry 4.0, OPC UA, AI at the edge. - **IEC 61131-3** (1993) — the most important PLC standard — defines **5 languages** (LD, FBD, ST, IL, SFC). - **IEC 61508 / 61511** — functional safety standards; basis for **SIL** (Safety Integrity Level) ratings of safety PLCs. - **Communication protocol evolution:** None → Modbus (1980) → Profibus (1990) → Industrial Ethernet → OPC UA (2003) → MQTT / TSN (Gen 5). - **Soft PLC** = PLC functionality implemented as software on an industrial PC (e.g., Siemens WinAC, Beckhoff TwinCAT, Codesys). - **PAC (Programmable Automation Controller)** = a hybrid of PLC ruggedness and PC flexibility (e.g., National Instruments CompactRIO, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix 5380). - **Industry 4.0** (Germany, 2011) = the integration of **cyber-physical systems, IIoT, cloud computing, and AI** into manufacturing; the natural culmination of PLC evolution. - **Tag-name addressing** (Gen 4) replaced the **fixed-register addressing** (Gen 1–3) — students must mention this when asked about Gen 4 innovations. - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Relay ≈ 10,000 hrs; PLC ≈ 100,000+ hrs → a 10× reliability improvement. - **Common KTU 14-mark answer structure:** *(i) Define PLC, (ii) State the pre-PLC problem, (iii) List 5 generations with driver + innovation + model, (iv) Mention IEC 61131-3, (v) Conclude with Industry 4.0 relevance.* - **Mnemonic to remember the 5 generations' drivers:** **"R-M-N-S-I"** → **R**elay, **M**icroprocessor, **N**etworking, **S**tandards, **I**IoT/Industry 4.0. - **Mnemonic for the 5 IEC 61131-3 languages:** **"L-F-S-I-S"** → **L**adder, **F**unction Block, **S**tructured Text, **I**nstruction List, **S**equential Function Chart.

topics.title: Functions of PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Functions of PLC # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, digital computing device specifically engineered for the real-time control of industrial processes and machinery under harsh factory-floor environments (IEC 61131-1 / NEMA ICS 3 standards). The *functions of a PLC* refer to the **set of executable instructions and operations** that a PLC's Central Processing Unit (CPU) can perform on input data to produce deterministic output control actions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > The *functions of a PLC* are the programmable instruction categories — including **logical operations, timing, counting, sequencing, arithmetic, data manipulation, communication, and PID control** — that transform the PLC from a mere relay-replacement unit into a full-fledged industrial process controller. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Brain" Think of a PLC as a **digital reflex system for machines**, similar to the human nervous system: - **Inputs** = Senses (eyes, skin, ears) → Sensors, switches, transmitters - **CPU** = Brain → Runs the user program - **Memory** = Long-term memory → Stores the ladder logic - **Outputs** = Muscles → Motors, solenoids, valves, lamps - **Functions** = Reflexes & reasoning → The actions the brain can perform (blink, grasp, count, decide) > [!NOTE] > Just as our brain can perform different "functions" (counting numbers, timing a breath, adding scores, deciding to move), a PLC performs **specialized industrial functions** on data received from the field to drive actuators in real time. ## 1.3 Primary Functional Categories of a PLC The KTU 2024 Scheme identifies **eight core functional groups** executed by a PLC: | # | Function Category | Industrial Purpose | |---|-------------------|--------------------| | 1 | Logical Operations | Replace relay-based decision logic | | 2 | Sequencing | Step-by-step process progression | | 3 | Timing | Schedule delays and pulses | | 4 | Counting | Track events, batches, revolutions | | 5 | Arithmetic | Add, subtract, multiply, divide analog data | | 6 | Data Handling | Compare, move, shift, convert data | | 7 | Communication | Interface with SCADA, HMI, other PLCs | | 8 | PID Control | Continuous closed-loop process regulation | ## 1.4 Key Physical Standards - **IEC 61131-3** → International standard for PLC programming languages (Ladder Diagram, Function Block, Structured Text, Instruction List, Sequential Function Chart). - **Scan Time** → Typical industrial range: **1 ms to 20 ms** depending on program size and CPU. - **I/O Types** → Digital (24 V DC, 120 V AC) and Analog (4–20 mA, 0–10 V). --- > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a question asks "List the functions of a PLC", always answer with the *eight core categories* above. Examiners expect this exact enumeration. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Function 1 — Logical Operations Logical operations form the **foundation of PLC programming**. The PLC performs Boolean algebra on input bits to derive output bits. ### Core Logical Functions: - **AND Operation:** Output is TRUE **only when all inputs are TRUE**. $$Y = A \cdot B \cdot C$$ - **OR Operation:** Output is TRUE when **any one input is TRUE**. $$Y = A + B + C$$ - **NOT (Inversion) Operation:** Output is the **complement** of the input. $$Y = \overline{A}$$ - **NAND / NOR / XOR:** Combinations of the above, used for fail-safe and comparison logic. > [!NOTE] > In **Ladder Logic**, these are implemented using **Normally Open (NO)**, **Normally Closed (NC)**, and **Output (OTE)** instructions, forming a contact-and-coil network directly analogous to electro-mechanical relay schematics. ### Engineering Utility: Used in **interlock logic**, **motor starter circuits**, **safety permissives**, and **machine guarding**. --- ## 2.2 Function 2 — Sequencing Operations Sequencing enables the PLC to execute instructions in a **predefined order**, often tied to step progression in batch processes. ### Types of Sequencing: 1. **Fixed Sequence** — Steps executed in a rigid A→B→C→D order (e.g., bottle-filling line). 2. **Variable Sequence** — Step transitions depend on feedback from the process. 3. **Sequencer Instructions (SQO / SQC)** — PLCs use dedicated sequencer registers to advance through a preset pattern of outputs. ### Engineering Utility: Used in **batch chemical reactors**, **assembly lines**, **traffic light controllers**, and **packaging machinery**. --- ## 2.3 Function 3 — Timing Functions PLCs provide **on-delay (TON)**, **off-delay (TOF)**, and **retentive timer (RTO)** instructions to introduce precise time delays independent of program scan. ### Working Principle: $$T_{\text{elapsed}} = \frac{\text{Current Value (CV)}}{\text{Time Base (TB)}}$$ - **TON (On-Delay Timer):** Starts timing when input goes TRUE; output energizes only after the preset time has elapsed. - **TOF (Off-Delay Timer):** Output stays TRUE for preset time after input goes FALSE. - **RTO (Retentive Timer):** Accumulates time across scan cycles; must be reset explicitly. > [!NOTE] > Time Base (TB) is typically **0.01 s, 0.1 s, or 1 s**, selectable per instruction. Preset (PRE) value is user-defined. ### Engineering Utility: Used in **star-delta motor starters**, **conveyor dwell times**, **debounce switches**, and **dosing pump delays**. --- ## 2.4 Function 4 — Counting Functions Counters track the number of input transitions (rising edges) and compare against a preset value. ### Counter Equations: - **CTU (Count Up):** $$CV_{n+1} = CV_n + 1 \quad \text{when } CU = 1$$ - **CTD (Count Down):** $$CV_{n+1} = CV_n - 1 \quad \text{when } CD = 1$$ - Output (DN bit) is set when **CV ≥ PRE**. ### Engineering Utility: Used in **batch counting**, **parts detection on conveyors**, **revolution counting**, and **parking lot occupancy**. --- ## 2.5 Function 5 — Arithmetic Operations Modern PLCs handle integer and floating-point arithmetic on data stored in registers. ### Standard Operations: $$Y = A + B \quad (\text{ADD})$$ $$Y = A - B \quad (\text{SUB})$$ $$Y = A \times B \quad (\text{MUL})$$ $$Y = \frac{A}{B} \quad (\text{DIV})$$ $$Y = \sqrt{A} \quad (\text{SQR})$$ $$Y = A \mod B \quad (\text{MOD})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Analog signals (e.g., 4–20 mA from a level transmitter) are scaled to engineering units using arithmetic blocks before being used in control or display logic. The scaling formula is: $$\text{Engineering Units} = \left( \frac{\text{Raw Input} - \text{Raw}_{\min}}{\text{Raw}_{\max} - \text{Raw}_{\min}} \right) \times (\text{EU}_{\max} - \text{EU}_{\min}) + \text{EU}_{\min}$$ --- ## 2.6 Function 6 — Data Handling Operations Data handling instructions **move, compare, and convert** information between memory locations and I/O. ### Key Data Handling Functions: - **MOV (Move):** Copies a word from source to destination. - **CMP (Compare):** Evaluates `>`, `=`, `<` between two values and sets flag bits. - **BSL / BSR (Bit Shift Left / Right):** Implements shift registers for tracking product flow. - **MVM (Masked Move):** Selectively moves specific bits using a mask. - **TOD / FRD (Binary-to-BCD / BCD-to-Binary):** Conversion for display to seven-segment HMI. ### Engineering Utility: Used in **recipe management**, **HMI data display**, **fault logging**, and **recipe selection in batch processes**. --- ## 2.7 Function 7 — Communication Functions PLCs support **industrial communication protocols** for networking with other devices. ### Common Protocols: - **Modbus RTU / TCP** — Master-slave serial/Ethernet communication. - **Profibus / Profinet** — Siemens-standard fieldbus. - **EtherNet/IP** — Rockwell/Allen-Bradley standard. - **DeviceNet** — Low-level sensor/actuator bus. - **OPC UA / MQTT** — Modern IIoT protocols for cloud connectivity. > [!NOTE] > Communication instructions are implemented via **MSG (Message)** blocks in ladder logic or dedicated protocol function blocks in IEC 61131-3 environments. ### Engineering Utility: Used in **SCADA integration**, **distributed control**, **remote I/O**, and **Industry 4.0 / IIoT** applications. --- ## 2.8 Function 8 — PID Control Function The PID (Proportional + Integral + Derivative) function is built into most modern PLCs (e.g., Allen-Bradley SLC 500, Siemens S7-1500) as a **closed-loop control instruction**. ### PID Output Equation: $$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$ ### Discrete (PLC-Scanned) Form: $$u_n = K_c \left[ e_n + \frac{T_s}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - e_{n-1}) \right]$$ Where: - $K_c$ = Proportional gain - $T_i$ = Integral time (reset time) - $T_d$ = Derivative time (rate time) - $T_s$ = Sample time (PLC scan period) - $e_n$ = Error at sample $n$ ### Engineering Utility: Used in **flow control**, **temperature control**, **pressure regulation**, and **level control** in continuous processes. --- ## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Function | Key Equation / Logic | Standard Reference | Units | |----------|----------------------|--------------------|-------| | Logical AND | $Y = A \cdot B$ | Boolean Algebra | Boolean | | Logical OR | $Y = A + B$ | Boolean Algebra | Boolean | | Timer (TON) | $CV = T_{\text{elapsed}} \times TB$ | IEC 61131-3 | seconds | | Counter (CTU) | $CV_{n+1} = CV_n + 1$ | IEC 61131-3 | counts | | Scaling | $EU = \left(\frac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}}\right) \Delta EU + EU_{min}$ | Instrument Society | eng. units | | PID Output | $u(t) = K_c e + \frac{K_c}{T_i}\int e\,dt + K_c T_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | Process Control | % output | | Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{IO} + T_{prog} + T_{comm}$ | PLC Hardware | ms | | Reynolds (flow) | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Fluid Mechanics | dimensionless | > [!TIP] > Memorize the **scan time equation** — it is a frequently-asked 2-mark question in KTU exams for PCICT502. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: PLC Scan Time The total time taken by a PLC to complete one **full scan cycle** is critical for real-time deterministic control. It is derived as: ### Step 1 — Identify the Three Scan Phases A PLC performs, in strict order: 1. **Input Scan** — Read all input status into the Input Image Table (IIT). 2. **Program Scan** — Execute the user program logic rung-by-rung. 3. **Output Scan** — Write the Output Image Table (OIT) to physical outputs. 4. *(Optional)* **Housekeeping / Communication Scan** — Handle comms, diagnostics. ### Step 2 — Express Each Phase Let: - $T_{IO}$ = Time to read all inputs and update outputs - $T_{prog}$ = Time to execute the user program - $T_{comm}$ = Time to handle communication tasks ### Step 3 — Compose the Total Scan Time $$T_{scan} = T_{IO} + T_{prog} + T_{comm}$$ ### Step 4 — Expand Programmatically $$T_{prog} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i$$ Where $t_i$ is the execution time of the $i$-th instruction, and $N$ is the total number of instructions. ### Step 5 — Combined Equation $$\boxed{T_{scan} = T_{IO} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i + T_{comm}}$$ > [!NOTE] > For a typical mid-range PLC with 1 K instructions of ~0.5 µs each, $T_{scan}$ ≈ 5–10 ms — well within the **1–20 ms** industrial standard. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Analog Input Scaling (4–20 mA to Engineering Units) Consider a **level transmitter** outputting 4–20 mA for a tank with range **0–10 meters**. The PLC analog input module converts this to a raw count, say **0–32767** (16-bit). Derive the scaling equation: ### Step 1 — Define Raw Signal Boundaries - $Raw_{min} = 32767 \times \frac{4}{20} = 6553.4$ (corresponds to 0 m) - $Raw_{max} = 32767$ (corresponds to 10 m) ### Step 2 — Apply the Linear Mapping Formula $$EU = \left( \frac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}} \right) \times (EU_{max} - EU_{min}) + EU_{min}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Values for Level Transmitter $$EU = \left( \frac{Raw - 6553.4}{32767 - 6553.4} \right) \times (10 - 0) + 0$$ $$EU = \left( \frac{Raw - 6553.4}{26213.6} \right) \times 10$$ ### Step 4 — Final Working Equation $$\boxed{EU = 0.0003815 \times (Raw - 6553.4)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** If the PLC reads $Raw = 16384$, the level is: $$EU = 0.0003815 \times (16384 - 6553.4) = 3.75 \text{ m}$$ This scaling block is implemented using **arithmetic (MUL, SUB, DIV)** instructions in the PLC program. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functions vs. Application Mapping | PLC Function | Typical Real-World Application | Example Instruction (Allen-Bradley / Siemens) | |--------------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------------------| | Logical (AND/OR/NOT) | Conveyor interlock | XIC, XIO, OTE | | Sequencing | Batch reactor steps | SQC, SQO / GRAPH | | Timing | Star-Delta starter | TON, TOF / S_ODT | | Counting | Bottle counter on line | CTU, CTD / Z_VORW | | Arithmetic | Flow totalization | ADD, MUL, DIV / ADD_REAL | | Data Handling | HMI display update | MOV, CMP / MOVE | | Communication | SCADA polling | MSG / PUT, GET | | PID Control | Temperature loop | PID / CONT_C | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Velocity Form of PID for PLC Implementation The continuous PID equation must be converted to a **velocity (incremental) form** suitable for PLC execution between scans, because PLCs compute output based on incremental change rather than absolute integral. ### Step 1 — Start with Position Form $$u_n = K_c e_n + \frac{K_c T_s}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{K_c T_d}{T_s}(e_n - e_{n-1})$$ ### Step 2 — Compute $\Delta u_n = u_n - u_{n-1}$ $$\Delta u_n = K_c (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \frac{K_c T_s}{T_i} e_n + \frac{K_c T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})$$ ### Step 3 — Factor Out Common Terms $$\Delta u_n = K_c \left[ (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \frac{T_s}{T_i} e_n + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) \right]$$ ### Step 4 — Output Update Equation $$u_n = u_{n-1} + \Delta u_n$$ > [!TIP] > **Why Velocity Form?** It eliminates the need to store the entire error history $\sum e_k$, saving PLC memory and providing **bumpless transfer** during mode switches. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — PLC Function Categories ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["Field Inputs
Sensors Switches Transmitters"] nodeB["PLC CPU
Central Processing Unit"] nodeC["Input Image Table
IIT Memory"] nodeD["Logical Operations
AND OR NOT XOR"] nodeE["Timing Functions
TON TOF RTO"] nodeF["Counting Functions
CTU CTD"] nodeG["Sequencing
Fixed Variable Sequencer"] nodeH["Arithmetic
ADD SUB MUL DIV"] nodeI["Data Handling
MOV CMP Shift"] nodeJ["PID Control
Closed Loop"] nodeK["Communication
Modbus Profinet OPC UA"] nodeL["Output Image Table
OIT Memory"] nodeM["Field Outputs
Motors Valves Lamps"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeH nodeE --> nodeI nodeF --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeK nodeI --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM ``` ## 4.2 PID Control Loop Flow (PLC-Based) ```mermaid graph TD sp["Setpoint SP
Desired Value"] pv["Process Variable PV
Sensor Feedback"] err["Error e = SP minus PV"] pid["PLC PID Function Block
Kc Ti Td"] out["Controller Output u"] plant["Final Control Element
Valve Heater Motor"] proc["Industrial Process
Tank Furnace Pipe"] sp --> err pv --> err err --> pid pid --> out out --> plant plant --> proc proc --> pv ``` ## 4.3 PLC Scan Cycle Functional Flow ```mermaid graph TD s1["Power ON
Initialization"] s2["Input Scan
Read IIT"] s3["Program Scan
Execute Ladder Logic"] s4["Output Scan
Update OIT"] s5["Housekeeping
Comm Diagnostics"] s6["Repeat Cycle"] s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s4 --> s5 s5 --> s6 s6 --> s2 ``` ## 4.4 Sequencing Function — Batch Reactor Example ```mermaid graph TD step1["Step 1
Fill Reactor"] step2["Step 2
Heat to 80 C"] step3["Step 3
Stir for 10 min"] step4["Step 4
Cool to 30 C"] step5["Step 5
Discharge Product"] dec["Decision
Process Complete?"] step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> step4 step4 --> step5 step5 --> dec dec -->|No| step1 dec -->|Yes| end1["End of Batch"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. List any six functions of a PLC.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]** **Model Answer:** The six primary functions of a PLC are: 1. **Logical Operations** — Boolean AND, OR, NOT, etc. 2. **Sequencing** — Step-based process progression. 3. **Timing** — TON, TOF, RTO delay generation. 4. **Counting** — CTU, CTD event tallying. 5. **Arithmetic** — ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV on data words. 6. **Data Handling** — MOV, CMP, shift, mask operations. *(Other valid functions: Communication, PID Control, Self-Diagnostics, Networking.)* --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between TON and TOF timers in a PLC.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | TON (On-Delay) | TOF (Off-Delay) | |-----------|---------------|-----------------| | Trigger | Starts timing when input goes TRUE | Starts timing when input goes FALSE | | Output | Energizes only after preset time has elapsed | De-energizes only after preset time has elapsed | | Reset | Resets automatically when input is FALSE | Resets automatically when input is TRUE | | Application | Star-Delta starter delay | Cooling fan run-down | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** Explain with neat block diagrams the **eight major functions of a PLC** used in industrial automation. (7 marks) **(b)** A PLC analog input module receives a **4–20 mA** signal from a pressure transmitter calibrated for **0–25 bar**. The raw digital count corresponding to 4 mA is **6400** and to 20 mA is **32000**. Develop the **scaling equation** to convert the raw count to pressure in bar. Also find the pressure when the raw count is **19200**.
