## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into sub-parts of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one** of the two choices provided.

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

#### `(a)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **7 Marks**

**Q: Explain the operation of a Conditional Jump Instruction in a PLC with the help of a ladder logic diagram. Discuss how the skipped rungs' outputs behave during the bypass period.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Conditional Jump:**
A Conditional Jump Instruction evaluates a Boolean rung condition. If TRUE, it diverts the PLC scan to a label (`LBL`), skipping the rungs in between. If FALSE, the scan proceeds normally through those rungs.

**Step 2 — Ladder Logic Diagram (textual representation):**

```
|---[ A ]---[ B ]---( JMP 100 )---|        ← Rung 1: Conditional Jump
|---[ X ]---[ Y ]---( C )---------|        ← Rung 2 (skipped when JMP active)
|---[ Z ]---( D )-----------------|        ← Rung 3 (skipped when JMP active)
...
|--- LBL 100 ----------------------|        ← Rung 20: Label target
|---[ P ]---( E )------------------|        ← Rung 21: Resumes normal scan
```

**Step 3 — Operation Logic:**
- When $A \cdot B = 1$ (both contacts TRUE), `JMP 100` is energized. Scan jumps to Rung 20.
- When $A \cdot B = 0$, `JMP 100` is de-energized. Scan executes Rungs 2 and 3 normally.

**Step 4 — Output Behavior in Skipped Rungs:**
Outputs $C$ and $D$ are **not re-evaluated** during the bypass. They **retain their last value** (memory hold). This is **latch-like retention**, not a forced OFF. If the user requires forced OFF, an MCR zone or explicit de-energize logic must be used.

**Step 5 — Engineering Application:**
In a bottling plant, during *CIP (Clean-in-Place)* mode, the conveyor and filling station logic must be bypassed, but the operator HMI must still show the *last known* speeds. A Conditional Jump with a `CIP_Mode_Active` input enables this behavior perfectly.

#### `(b)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **7 Marks**

**Q: A PLC has a base scan time of 30 ms. It contains four independent jump-inhibitable blocks with execution times $t_1 = 5$ ms, $t_2 = 4$ ms, $t_3 = 6$ ms, $t_4 = 3$ ms. If only blocks 1 and 3 are currently jumped, calculate the effective scan time. Comment on its engineering significance for a turbine control application.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$T_{scan,\text{effective}} = T_{scan,\text{base}} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i \cdot \delta_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values ($\delta_1 = 1$, $\delta_2 = 0$, $\delta_3 = 1$, $\delta_4 = 0$):**

$$T_{scan,\text{effective}} = 30 - \left[(5)(1) + (4)(0) + (6)(1) + (3)(0)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$T_{scan,\text{effective}} = 30 - (5 + 0 + 6 + 0) = 30 - 11 = 19 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{scan,\text{effective}} = 19 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Comment:**
A reduction of 11 ms (≈ 36.7% faster) is **critical for turbine governor applications**, which require scan times below 20 ms to maintain stable frequency control. Without the jumps, the 30 ms scan could lead to **frequency oscillation and trip events** during load transients.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks)

#### `(a)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — **7 Marks**

**Q: Explain the JSR (Jump to Subroutine), SBR, and RET instructions in PLCs. Describe the stack-based mechanism and give one industrial example.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions:**
- **JSR (Jump to Subroutine):** When its rung is energized, the PLC pushes the current scan address (return address) onto a LIFO stack and jumps to the subroutine label.
- **SBR (Subroutine):** The first rung inside the subroutine; an optional block that can pass parameters from the main program.
- **RET (Return):** The last instruction in the subroutine. It pops the return address from the stack and resumes the main program at the instruction following the JSR.

**Step 2 — Stack Mechanism (LIFO):**

$$\text{Stack: } [\text{RET}_{\text{addr1}}, \text{RET}_{\text{addr2}}, \text{RET}_{\text{addr3}}]$$

The most recent call's return address sits at the **top** of the stack. RET pops this top element first.

**Step 3 — Industrial Example:**
In a **refinery Distillation Column control system**, the main program calls:
- `JSR PID_TC_101` (Temperature Control)
- `JSR PID_LC_201` (Level Control)
- `JSR PID_FC_301` (Flow Control)

Each subroutine contains a **reusable PID block** with different process variable and setpoint tags. This modular approach allows engineers to tune each loop independently from a single, validated code base.

**Step 4 — Nesting Limit Note:**
Most PLCs limit subroutine nesting to **8 levels**. Exceeding this limit causes a stack overflow fault and PLC STOP.

#### `(b)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — **7 Marks**

**Q: With a neat ladder diagram, illustrate the use of the Master Control Reset (MCR) instruction. Differentiate its output behavior from a JMP zone.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — MCR Ladder Diagram:**

```
|---[ START_PB ]---( MCR )-----------------|        ← Rung 1: MCR Coil
|---[ M1 ]---( PUMP_1 )---------------------|        ← Rung 2: Inside MCR Zone
|---[ M2 ]---( PUMP_2 )---------------------|        ← Rung 3: Inside MCR Zone
|---[ M3 ]---( HEATER_1 )-------------------|        ← Rung 4: Inside MCR Zone
|---( MCR END )----------------------------|        ← Rung 5: Paired End
```

**Step 2 — MCR Behavior:**
- When `START_PB` = 1 (MCR coil energized), all rungs 2–4 are **enabled** and scanned normally.
- When `START_PB` = 0 (MCR coil de-energized), all rungs 2–4 are **still scanned**, but all **non-retentive** outputs (`PUMP_1`, `PUMP_2`, `HEATER_1`) are **forced OFF**.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | JMP | MCR |
|---|---|---|
| Rungs Scanned? | **No** (skipped) | **Yes** (scanned) |
| Output Behavior When Active | Holds last value | Non-retentive **forced OFF** |
| Retentive Outputs Affected? | No (they hold) | No (they hold) |
| Use Case | Inhibit logic execution | Emergency isolation of zone |

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
JMP is best for **performance optimization and selective logic inhibit**, while MCR is best for **safety-critical zone isolation** where outputs must be reliably de-energized.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing JMP with MCR:** Many students write that JMP "resets outputs" — this is **wrong**. JMP *holds* outputs; MCR *resets non-retentive* outputs. Expect **2–3 mark deductions** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting retentive behavior:** Retentive outputs (e.g., motor contactor with seal-in contact) **retain** their state under both JMP and MCR-disable. Mentioning this is a **+2 mark bonus point**.
> 3. **Miscounting JSR nesting:** When asked about subroutine depth, students often forget that **JSR inside a JSR inside a JSR** counts as **depth 3**, not 1. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Units and labels:** Always quote scan time in **ms** and label numbers must be **unique** within a routine.
> 5. **Mermaid in exam:** You are not required to draw Mermaid diagrams in the exam. Use **text-based ladder** representation like the diagrams above. The Mermaid here is for **conceptual understanding** only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — Jump Instructions**

- **Definition:** Jump Instructions are *program control instructions* that alter the PLC's normal scan cycle by transferring execution to a target label.
- **Two Main Categories:**
  - *Unconditional Jump (JMP / JU)* — always active.
  - *Conditional Jump (JMP / JC / JCN)* — active only when Boolean input is TRUE.
- **Companion Instructions:**
  - `LBL` (Label) — passive target marker, must be unique.
  - `JSR` (Jump to Subroutine) — uses LIFO stack for return addresses.
  - `SBR` (Subroutine) — optional parameter-passing first rung.
  - `RET` (Return) — pops stack, resumes main program.
  - `MCR` (Master Control Reset) — zone-based output reset, not a true skip.
- **Key Formula:**
  $$T_{scan,\text{effective}} = T_{scan,\text{base}} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{block,i} \cdot \delta_i$$
- **Output Behavior — MUST remember:**
  - **JMP** → outputs in skipped zone **hold last value** (memory retention).
  - **MCR** → non-retentive outputs **forced OFF**; retentive outputs hold.
- **Stack Limit:** Most PLCs permit **8 to 32 nested subroutine levels**; exceeding this causes STOP.
- **Standard Reference:** **IEC 61131-3** governs the syntax and semantics of these instructions.
- **Industrial Applications:**
  - Phased plant startup and shutdown sequences
  - Maintenance and commissioning bypass of logic
  - Modular reusable code (PID, alarm, sequence blocks)
  - Fast-loop scan time optimization for turbine and compressor control
  - Safety zone isolation (MCR)
- **Common Exam Buzzwords:** "LIFO stack", "retentive vs. non-retentive", "IEC 61131-3", "scan time optimization", "selective inhibit", "modular programming", "emergency zone isolation".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Handling Instruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Handling Instruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Data Handling Instructions?

**Data Handling Instructions** are a fundamental category of **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** instructions that operate on, transfer, compare, convert, and manipulate data words or words/files stored in the PLC's data tables (such as input, output, binary, integer, timer, counter, and control files). Unlike relay-logic (bit) instructions, data handling instructions work on **16-bit or 32-bit data words**, enabling the PLC to perform arithmetic, comparisons, data moves, and complex decision-making processes essential in modern process control applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Data Handling Instructions* in a PLC are software-level commands used to transfer, compare, convert, and manipulate numerical/word data between memory locations. They are the backbone of supervisory control, data acquisition (SCADA) interfacing, batch process control, and PID loop tuning routines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this topic important in Process Control?**
> In a chemical plant or oil refinery, field sensors (temperature, pressure, flow) return **analog signals** (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) which the PLC's analog input module converts into **integer/word values** (e.g., 0–4095 for a 12-bit ADC). Data handling instructions allow the PLC to scale these raw values, compare them against setpoints, and drive the final control element (valve, VFD) — all in real-time.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy 🏭

Imagine you are the **supervisor of a water treatment plant**. You have 50 workers (analog sensors) who bring you **report slips** (data words) every hour. As a supervisor, you need to:

1. **Collect** these report slips from workers and put them on your desk → **Data Transfer (MOV)**
2. **Read and compare** each slip with a target value (e.g., "pH must be between 6.5–7.5") → **Data Comparison (GRT, LES, LIM)**
3. **Convert** the units (°C to °F) before forwarding to the head office → **Data Conversion (TOD, FRD)**
4. **Calculate** the average of last 10 readings → **Math Instructions (ADD, DIV)**
5. **Decide** if an alarm should be triggered → **Logical Instructions (AND, OR)**

**The PLC is YOU**, and **Data Handling Instructions are your mental processes** to make smart decisions!

> [!TIP]
> 🔑 **Memory Tip**: Think **M–C–C–M–L** → **M**ove, **C**ompare, **C**onvert, **M**ath, **L**ogical — the five pillars of PLC data handling.

---

## 1.3 Standard PLC Data File Organization (Allen-Bradley SLC-500 Convention)

| File Number | File Type | Symbol | Word Width | Typical Use |
|-------------|-----------|--------|------------|-------------|
| **O0** | Output | `O` | 16-bit | Field actuators |
| **I1** | Input | `I` | 16-bit | Field sensors |
| **S2** | Status | `S` | 16-bit | PLC health flags |
| **B3** | Binary | `B` | 16-bit | Bit-level storage |
| **T4** | Timer | `T` | 16-bit | Time-based control |
| **C5** | Counter | `C` | 16-bit | Event counting |
| **R6** | Control | `R` | 16-bit | Sequencer control |
| **N7** | Integer | `N` | 16-bit | Numerical data |
| **F8** | Float | `F` | 32-bit | Floating-point math |

> [!NOTE]
> File **N7** is the *workhorse* for data handling operations — most arithmetic and comparison instructions operate on integer words stored here.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Data Handling — A Structured Breakdown

### 🔹 Pillar 1: DATA TRANSFER INSTRUCTIONS

These instructions copy/move data between memory locations **without altering the source**.

| Instruction | Mnemonic | Operation | Source Unchanged? |
|-------------|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Move** | `MOV` | Source → Destination | ✅ Yes |
| **Masked Move** | `MVM` | (Source AND Mask) → Destination | ✅ Yes |
| **Block Transfer Write** | `BTW` | PLC rack → I/O module | ✅ Yes |
| **Copy File** | `COP` | File A → File B (full copy) | ✅ Yes |
| **Fill File** | `FLL` | Constant → All words of file | ✅ Yes |

**Ladder Logic Anatomy of MOV:**

```
    [   ]              [        ]
   |--|   |------------|  MOV   |
   |  XIC |            |  Source|
   |      |            |  Dest  |
    ------              --------
```

**Ladder Logic Anatomy of MVM:**

$$D_{result} = (S \cdot M) + (D \cdot \overline{M})$$

where $S$ = Source, $M$ = Mask (16-bit), $D$ = Destination, $\overline{M}$ = bitwise NOT of Mask.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Masked Move (MVM)** is critical in process control where you want to update *only specific bits* of a word — e.g., changing just the alarm bits without touching the process value bits.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 2: DATA COMPARISON INSTRUCTIONS

These instructions perform **conditional logic** based on numerical relationships.

| Instruction | Mnemonic | Condition | Output (Rung) |
|-------------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Equal** | `EQU` | A = B | Energized if true |
| **Not Equal** | `NEQ` | A ≠ B | Energized if true |
| **Greater Than** | `GRT` | A > B | Energized if true |
| **Less Than** | `LES` | A < B | Energized if true |
| **Greater or Equal** | `GEQ` | A ≥ B | Energized if true |
| **Less or Equal** | `LEQ` | A ≤ B | Energized if true |
| **Limit Test** | `LIM` | LOW ≤ A ≤ HIGH | Energized if true |
| **Masked Equal** | `MEQ` | (A AND Mask) = (B AND Mask) | Energized if true |

> [!NOTE]
> Comparison instructions are **input-style** instructions — they appear on the **left side** of the rung and produce a boolean true/false output. This makes them perfect for **interlock** and **alarm** logic in a process plant.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 3: DATA CONVERSION INSTRUCTIONS

Conversion between **numbering systems and units** — indispensable when interfacing with BCD-based panel meters, encoders, or analog modules.

| Instruction | Mnemonic | From → To | Typical Use |
|-------------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Convert to BCD** | `TOD` | Binary/Integer → 4-digit BCD | 7-segment display |
| **Convert from BCD** | `FRD` | 4-digit BCD → Binary/Integer | Thumbwheel switch input |
| **Degrees** | `DEG` | Radians → Degrees | Trigonometric PID output |
| **Radians** | `RAD` | Degrees → Radians | Inverse trig functions |

**The BCD ↔ Binary Relationship:**

$$D_{decimal} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 10^i \quad \text{(where each } b_i \in [0,9] \text{)}$$

$$B_{binary} = \sum_{i=0}^{15} a_i \cdot 2^i \quad \text{(where each } a_i \in \{0,1\} \text{)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> ❗ A 16-bit binary word can represent **0 to 65,535**, but the same 16 bits in BCD form can only represent **0 to 9,999** (since each nibble is capped at 1001). KTU students frequently lose marks for confusing these ranges!

---

### 🔹 Pillar 4: MATHEMATICAL INSTRUCTIONS

These enable **arithmetic operations** on word-level data — the heart of any PID loop or rate calculation.

| Instruction | Mnemonic | Operation | Equation Form |
|-------------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Add** | `ADD` | A + B → Dest | $D = A + B$ |
| **Subtract** | `SUB` | A − B → Dest | $D = A - B$ |
| **Multiply** | `MUL` | A × B → Dest | $D = A \times B$ |
| **Divide** | `DIV` | A ÷ B → Math & Remainder | $D_{math} = \lfloor A/B \rfloor$, $D_{rem} = A \mod B$ |
| **Double Divide** | `DDV` | (A·65536 + B) ÷ C | 32-bit division |
| **Clear** | `CLR` | 0 → Dest | $D = 0$ |
| **Square Root** | `SQR` | √A → Dest | $D = \sqrt{A}$ |
| **Scale** | `SCL` | Linear rescale | $D = [(A - S_{min}) / (S_{max} - S_{min})] \cdot (D_{max} - D_{min}) + D_{min}$ |
| **Absolute** | `ABS` | \|A\| → Dest | $D = \vert A \vert$ |

**The SCALE (SCL) Equation — Most Important in Process Control:**

$$D = \left(\frac{A - S_{min}}{S_{max} - S_{min}}\right) \times (D_{max} - D_{min}) + D_{min}$$

where:
- $A$ = Input value (raw ADC count, e.g., 0–4095)
- $S_{min}, S_{max}$ = Source range bounds
- $D_{min}, D_{max}$ = Destination (scaled engineering) range bounds
- $D$ = Resulting engineering value (e.g., 0–100 °C)

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Process Industry Real-World Use:** A 4–20 mA pressure transmitter outputs 4 mA at 0 bar and 20 mA at 10 bar. The PLC ADC reads **0** at 4 mA and **4095** at 20 mA. The **SCL instruction** converts this raw 0–4095 into a meaningful 0–10 bar engineering value for the HMI display. This single instruction is the **most frequently tested data handling concept** in KTU university exams.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 5: LOGICAL & SHIFT INSTRUCTIONS

**Logical (Bitwise) Instructions:**

| Instruction | Mnemonic | Equation |
|-------------|----------|----------|
| **AND** | `AND` | $D = A \cdot B$ (bitwise) |
| **OR** | `OR` | $D = A + B$ (bitwise) |
| **Exclusive OR** | `XOR` | $D = A \oplus B$ (bitwise) |
| **NOT** | `NOT` | $D = \overline{A}$ (bitwise) |

**Shift Register Instructions:**

| Instruction | Mnemonic | Operation |
|-------------|----------|-----------|
| **Bit Shift Left** | `BSL` | Shifts all bits in a word/file left by one position |
| **Bit Shift Right** | `BSR` | Shifts all bits in a word/file right by one position |
| **FIFO Load** | `FFL` | Loads data into a FIFO stack |
| **FIFO Unload** | `FFU` | Unloads oldest data from FIFO |
| **LIFO Load** | `LFL` | Loads data into a LIFO stack |
| **LIFO Unload** | `LFU` | Unloads most recent data from LIFO |

**Bitwise Operation Example:**

For $A = 1100\ 1010\ 0101\ 1100_2$ and $B = 1010\ 0101\ 1111\ 0000_2$:

$$A \text{ AND } B = 1000\ 0000\ 0101\ 0000_2$$

$$A \text{ OR } B = 1110\ 1111\ 1111\ 1100_2$$

$$A \text{ XOR } B = 0110\ 1111\ 1010\ 1100_2$$

---

## 2.2 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Application |
|---|-------------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Move data | $D \leftarrow S$ | Transfer sensor reading to display register |
| 2 | Masked Move | $D \leftarrow (S \cdot M) + (D \cdot \overline{M})$ | Update specific bits in alarm word |
| 3 | SCL (Scale) | $D = \dfrac{(A - S_{min})(D_{max} - D_{min})}{S_{max} - S_{min}} + D_{min}$ | Convert 4–20 mA to engineering units |
| 4 | DIV | $D_{math} = \lfloor A/B \rfloor$ and $D_{rem} = A \bmod B$ | Average calculation, batch counting |
| 5 | SQR | $D = \sqrt{A}$ | Flow rate from differential pressure (√ΔP) |
| 6 | LIM | $R = 1$ if $L \leq A \leq H$ else $0$ | High/Low alarm limit test |
| 7 | MEQ | $R = 1$ if $(A \cdot M) = (B \cdot M)$ | Compare specific bits of two words |
| 8 | BCD Range (16-bit) | $0 \leq N_{BCD} \leq 9999$ | Panel meter display |
| 9 | Binary Range (16-bit) | $0 \leq N_{bin} \leq 65535$ | Internal data processing |
| 10 | Bitwise AND/OR/XOR | $D = f(A,B)$ bit-by-bit | Status word manipulation |
| 11 | FIFO/LIFO Stack | First-In-First-Out / Last-In-First-Out | Batch recipe sequencing |
| 12 | ABS | $D = \vert A \vert$ | Error magnitude in PID controller |

> [!TIP]
> ⭐ **MUST-REMEMBER for KTU Exam:** The SCL equation (#3) and the DIV quotient/remainder pair (#4) together account for **over 60% of marks** in Module 4 data handling problems.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Data Handling |
|-----------------|------------------------------|
| **Oil & Gas** | Scaling wellhead pressure/temperature; ESD logic |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Batch recipe management via FIFO stacks |
| **Power Plants** | Boiler drum level alarming via LIM instruction |
| **Water Treatment** | pH/conductivity monitoring with GRT/LES |
| **Food & Beverage** | Fill-level computation using DIV remainder |
| **Automotive (Paint Shop)** | Conveyor sequencing using BSL/BSR |
| **HVAC** | PID loop setpoint comparison with GEQ/LEQ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Engineering Unit Scaling using SCL

> **Problem:** A differential pressure transmitter for steam flow has a calibrated range of **0–100 inches of H₂O**. The 4–20 mA signal is converted by the PLC's analog input module to a raw integer count of **0–10,000** (not 0–4095 — assume 14-bit resolution). Find the engineering flow in inches of H₂O when the raw input is **6,500 counts**.

### Step 1: Identify the SCL Parameters

| Parameter | Value | Description |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| $A$ | $6500$ | Input (raw ADC count) |
| $S_{min}$ | $0$ | Source minimum |
| $S_{max}$ | $10000$ | Source maximum |
| $D_{min}$ | $0$ | Destination minimum (in H₂O) |
| $D_{max}$ | $100$ | Destination maximum (in H₂O) |
| $D$ | $?$ | Scaled engineering value |

### Step 2: Apply the SCL Equation

$$D = \left(\frac{A - S_{min}}{S_{max} - S_{min}}\right) \times (D_{max} - D_{min}) + D_{min}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$D = \left(\frac{6500 - 0}{10000 - 0}\right) \times (100 - 0) + 0$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Numerator and Denominator

$$D = \left(\frac{6500}{10000}\right) \times 100$$

### Step 5: Divide the Fraction

$$D = 0.65 \times 100$$

### Step 6: Final Multiplication

$$D = 65 \text{ inches of H}_2\text{O}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** The steam flow corresponds to **65 inches of H₂O**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: DIV — Average Calculation with Remainder

> **Problem:** A PLC batch counter accumulates the total number of bottles filled in register **N7:10**. After 8 hours, **N7:10 = 2534** bottles. Use the DIV instruction to find the **average per hour** and the **remaining bottles** that will form the start of the next batch.

### Step 1: Identify DIV Parameters

| Parameter | Value | Description |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| Source A | `N7:10` (2534) | Total bottles |
| Source B | 8 | Number of hours |
| Dest (Math) | `N7:11` | Quotient (avg) |
| Dest (Remainder) | `N7:12` | Remainder |

### Step 2: Apply the DIV Operation

$$D_{math} = \left\lfloor \frac{A}{B} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2534}{8} \right\rfloor$$

$$D_{rem} = A \bmod B = 2534 - 8 \cdot D_{math}$$

### Step 3: Perform the Long Division

$$2534 \div 8 = 316.75$$

### Step 4: Take the Integer Part (Floor)

$$D_{math} = \lfloor 316.75 \rfloor = 316$$

### Step 5: Compute the Remainder

$$D_{rem} = 2534 - (8 \times 316)$$

$$D_{rem} = 2534 - 2528$$

$$D_{rem} = 6$$

### Step 6: Verification

$$316 \times 8 + 6 = 2528 + 6 = 2534 \quad \checkmark$$

> ✅ **Answer:** Average = **316 bottles/hour**, Remainder = **6 bottles** (start of next batch).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: MVM (Masked Move) — Bit-Level Update

> **Problem:** A status word `N7:50` holds process flags. The current value is `0000 0000 1111 1111` (255). We want to **set only Bit 7 and Bit 12** to 1 (for new alarm conditions) using a source value `0001 0000 1000 0000` (4224) and a mask `0001 0000 1000 0000`. Find the new value of `N7:50`.

### Step 1: Apply the MVM Equation

$$D_{new} = (S \cdot M) + (D_{old} \cdot \overline{M})$$

### Step 2: Compute S · M (Bitwise AND of Source and Mask)

$$S = 0001\ 0000\ 1000\ 0000_2$$
$$M = 0001\ 0000\ 1000\ 0000_2$$
$$S \cdot M = 0001\ 0000\ 1000\ 0000_2 = 4224$$

> Bit 7 and Bit 12 of the new contribution are 1; all others are 0.

### Step 3: Compute M̄ (Bitwise NOT of Mask)

$$\overline{M} = 1110\ 1111\ 0111\ 1111_2 = 61439$$

### Step 4: Compute D_old · M̄ (Bitwise AND)

$$D_{old} = 0000\ 0000\ 1111\ 1111_2$$
$$\overline{M} = 1110\ 1111\ 0111\ 1111_2$$
$$D_{old} \cdot \overline{M} = 0000\ 0000\ 0111\ 1111_2 = 127$$

### Step 5: Add the Two Components (Bitwise OR is equivalent for non-overlapping masks)

$$D_{new} = 4224 + 127 = 4351$$

In binary:

$$D_{new} = 0001\ 0000\ 1111\ 1111_2 = 4351$$

> ✅ **Answer:** The new status word is **4351** — only bits 7 and 12 are forced to 1, while all other original bits are preserved.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: MUL + ADD Cascade — Flow Compensation

> **Problem:** In a steam flow measurement, the compensated (density-corrected) flow is computed as:

$$Q_{comp} = Q_{raw} \times \left( \frac{\rho_{op}}{\rho_{cal}} \right)$$

> where $Q_{raw} = 350$ (scaled differential pressure in inches H₂O), $\rho_{op} = 0.85$ (operating density), and $\rho_{cal} = 1.00$ (calibration density). Find $Q_{comp}$.

### Step 1: Set Up the Ratio (using MUL then DIV)

Since PLCs work in integer arithmetic, we scale the densities by 1000 to retain precision:
- $\rho_{op} = 850$
- $\rho_{cal} = 1000$

### Step 2: Compute Numerator (MUL)

$$\text{Numerator} = Q_{raw} \times \rho_{op} = 350 \times 850 = 297500$$

### Step 3: Compute Final Result (DIV)

$$Q_{comp} = \frac{\text{Numerator}}{\rho_{cal}} = \frac{297500}{1000} = 297.5$$

Since integer division is used:

$$\text{Math} = 297, \quad \text{Remainder} = 500$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

$$Q_{comp} \approx 297.5 \text{ inches of H}_2\text{O (compensated)}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** The compensated flow is **297.5 in H₂O** — a reduction from the uncompensated 350, reflecting lower operating density (e.g., higher temperature steam).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: LIM — Multi-Level Alarm Logic

> **Problem:** A reactor temperature sensor returns a scaled value in `N7:20`. Define alarms:
> - **LOW alarm** energize if `N7:20 < 50` °C
> - **HIGH alarm** energize if `N7:20 > 150` °C
> - **NORMAL** if `50 ≤ N7:20 ≤ 150` °C
>
> Implement this using a single `LIM` instruction.

### Step 1: LIM Instruction Setup

| Parameter | Value | Description |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| Lower Limit (L) | 50 | Low bound |
| Test Value (A) | `N7:20` | Current temperature |
| Upper Limit (H) | 150 | High bound |
| Output | `B3:0/5` (NORMAL flag) | TRUE if within range |

### Step 2: Logic

$$\text{NORMAL} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } 50 \leq N7:20 \leq 150 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 3: Invert for Alarms

- `LOW_ALARM = NOT NORMAL AND (N7:20 < 50)` (use LES for direct check)
- `HIGH_ALARM = NOT NORMAL AND (N7:20 > 150)` (use GRT for direct check)

### Step 4: Test with Three Values

| `N7:20` (°C) | LIM Output (NORMAL) | LOW_ALARM | HIGH_ALARM |
|--------------|---------------------|-----------|------------|
| 40 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 100 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 175 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

> ✅ **Result:** The `LIM` instruction provides a single boolean condition for the "in-range" state, which simplifies the rung logic significantly.

---

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Instruction

| Goal | Best Instruction | Why? |
|------|------------------|------|
| Copy a sensor reading to a display register | `MOV` | Simplest, no transformation needed |
| Update only certain bits in a status word | `MVM` | Mask preserves untouched bits |
| Convert raw ADC count to °C | `SCL` | Linear interpolation built-in |
| Find average of multiple values | `ADD` → `DIV` | Standard integer math |
| Check if a value is in a safe range | `LIM` | Single instruction, both bounds |
| Compare only the high byte of two words | `MEQ` | Mask isolates the relevant bits |
| Drive a 7-segment BCD display | `TOD` | Direct BCD output |
| Read thumbwheel BCD input | `FRD` | Direct BCD to binary |
| Find the flow from a ΔP sensor | `SQR` | $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$ |
| Magnitude of error for PID | `ABS` | $|SP - PV|$ for absolute deviation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of PLC Data Handling System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Sensor 4-20mA] --> B[Analog Input Module]
    B --> C[Raw Integer Word N7x]
    C --> D1[MOV Transfer]
    C --> D2[SCL Scale]
    C --> D3[Math Block ADD/SUB/MUL/DIV]
    C --> D4[Comparison Block GRT/LES/EQU]
    C --> D5[Conversion Block TOD/FRD]
    D1 --> E1[Output Register for HMI]
    D2 --> E2[Engineering Value Register]
    D3 --> E3[Computed Result]
    D4 --> E4[Boolean Flags for Alarm/Interlock]
    D5 --> E5[BCD Output to 7-Segment Display]
    E1 --> F[Supervisory SCADA]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> G[Actuator Relay Ladder]
    E5 --> H[Operator Panel Display]
    G --> I[Final Control Element Valve / VFD]
    H --> J[Process Operator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Field Sensor** generates an analog signal → **Analog Input Module** digitizes it → **Data Handling Instructions** (the heart of this module) process it → **Output** drives displays, SCADA, or actuators. This is the canonical data flow in any process control loop.

---

## 4.2 Instruction Category Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Data Handling Instructions] --> B1[Transfer]
    A --> B2[Comparison]
    A --> B3[Conversion]
    A --> B4[Mathematical]
    A --> B5[Logical Shift]

    B1 --> C1[MOV]
    B1 --> C2[MVM]
    B1 --> C3[COP FLL]

    B2 --> D1[EQU NEQ]
    B2 --> D2[GRT LES]
    B2 --> D3[GEQ LEQ]
    B2 --> D4[LIM MEQ]

    B3 --> E1[TOD]
    B3 --> E2[FRD]
    B3 --> E3[DEG RAD]

    B4 --> F1[ADD SUB]
    B4 --> F2[MUL DIV DDV]
    B4 --> F3[SQR SCL ABS CLR]

    B5 --> G1[AND OR XOR NOT]
    B5 --> G2[BSL BSR]
    B5 --> G3[FFL FFU LFL LFU]
```

---

## 4.3 SCL Instruction — Data Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Input A] --> P1[Subtract Smin]
    P1 --> P2[Divide by Smax - Smin]
    P2 --> P3[Multiply by Dmax - Dmin]
    P3 --> P4[Add Dmin]
    P4 --> R[Scaled Output D]
    SMIN[Smin Constant] --> P1
    SMAX[Smax Constant] --> P2
    DMIN[Dmin Constant] --> P4
    DMAX[Dmax Constant] --> P3
```

---

## 4.4 MVM — Bitwise Update Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source S 16-bit] --> J1[Bitwise AND with Mask M]
    B[Mask M 16-bit] --> J1
    J1 --> X[New Bits NewMasked]

    C[Destination Dold] --> J2[Bitwise AND with NOT M]
    B --> J3[Bitwise NOT]
    J3 --> J2
    J2 --> Y[Preserved Old Bits]

    X --> O[Bitwise OR Equivalent Addition]
    Y --> O
    O --> Z[New Destination Dnew]
```

---

## 4.5 PID Loop Showing Data Handling Instruction Usage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> X1[Subtract SUB]
    PV[Process Variable PV] --> X1
    X1 --> ERR[Error e t]
    ERR --> ABS1[ABS Magnitude]
    ERR --> PID[PID Controller Block]
    PID --> OP[Controller Output OP]
    OP --> SCL1[SCL to mA 0-4095 to 4-20mA]
    SCL1 --> VLV[Control Valve Positioner]
    VLV --> PROC[Process Plant]
    PROC --> TRANSMITTER[Sensor Transmitter]
    TRANSMITTER --> SCL2[SCL 4-20mA to Engineering Units]
    SCL2 --> PV
    ABS1 --> ALARM[LIM Limit Test]
    ALARM --> HMI[Alarm Display HMI]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Observe how **SCL** is used **twice** — once at the input (to convert 4–20 mA to engineering units) and once at the output (to convert 0–4095 to 4–20 mA). This dual use is **the most common exam scenario** in KTU Module 4 questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🅰️ PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the function of the MVM (Masked Move) instruction in a PLC. How does it differ from a simple MOV instruction?**

**Model Answer:**

The **MOV (Move)** instruction copies the entire 16-bit source word into the destination word verbatim. It is used when you need to transfer a complete data word, e.g., a sensor reading from `N7:0` to a display register `N7:50`.

The **MVM (Masked Move)** instruction, on the other hand, provides **bit-level control** by using a 16-bit mask. The destination is updated as per the equation:

$$D_{new} = (S \cdot M) + (D_{old} \cdot \overline{M})$$

where $S$ = Source, $M$ = Mask, and $\overline{M}$ = bitwise NOT of Mask. Bits where the mask is 1 are **overwritten** by the source value, while bits where the mask is 0 are **preserved** unchanged from the old destination.

**Key Difference:** MOV is a **word-level** operation (all 16 bits), whereas MVM is a **selective bit-level** operation (only masked bits change). In process control, MVM is invaluable for **updating alarm flags** in a status word without disturbing the live process data bits.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**List any three data comparison instructions used in a PLC and explain the working of the LIM (Limit Test) instruction with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

Three data comparison instructions are:
1. **GRT (Greater Than)** — output is true if `A > B`
2. **LES (Less Than)** — output is true if `A < B`
3. **EQU (Equal)** — output is true if `A = B`

**LIM (Limit Test) Instruction:**

The LIM instruction tests whether a value lies **within a specified range**. It has three operands: Lower Limit (L), Test Value (A), and Upper Limit (H).

$$\text{LIM Output} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } L \leq A \leq H \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Example:** If `L = 50`, `A = N7:20` (reactor temperature), `H = 150`, the LIM output is true (1) whenever the temperature is between 50 °C and 150 °C. This is widely used in **boiler drum level control** and **reactor safety interlock** systems.

---

## 5.2 🅱️ PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🎯 Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the SCL (Scale) instruction in a PLC. Derive its mathematical equation and explain its application in converting a 4–20 mA signal to a 0–100 °C temperature reading. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the data transfer instructions (MOV and MVM) used in PLCs with suitable examples. Show how MVM can be used to selectively update alarm bits in a status word. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q. (a):

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The **SCL (Scale)** instruction performs **linear interpolation** to rescale a raw input value into an engineering unit. It is essential whenever an analog signal must be converted to a meaningful physical quantity.

**Step 2 — Equation Derivation:**

A linear relationship between input $A$ and output $D$ is assumed:

$$D = mA + c$$

where $m$ = slope and $c$ = intercept.

Applying boundary conditions:
- When $A = S_{min}$, then $D = D_{min}$: $\quad D_{min} = m \cdot S_{min} + c$
- When $A = S_{max}$, then $D = D_{max}$: $\quad D_{max} = m \cdot S_{max} + c$

Subtracting:

$$D_{max} - D_{min} = m \cdot (S_{max} - S_{min})$$

$$m = \frac{D_{max} - D_{min}}{S_{max} - S_{min}}$$

Substituting back:

$$c = D_{min} - m \cdot S_{min} = D_{min} - \frac{(D_{max} - D_{min}) \cdot S_{min}}{S_{max} - S_{min}}$$

Final equation:

$$\boxed{D = \left(\frac{A - S_{min}}{S_{max} - S_{min}}\right) \times (D_{max} - D_{min}) + D_{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Application (4–20 mA to 0–100 °C):**

For a temperature transmitter:
- 4 mA → 0 °C → ADC count 0 → $S_{min} = 0$
- 20 mA → 100 °C → ADC count 4095 → $S_{max} = 4095$
- $D_{min} = 0$, $D_{max} = 100$

Suppose ADC reads $A = 2048$ counts:

$$D = \left(\frac{2048 - 0}{4095 - 0}\right) \times (100 - 0) + 0$$

$$D = \frac{2048}{4095} \times 100 = 0.5001 \times 100 \approx 50 \text{ °C}$$

> ✅ The SCL instruction converts raw count 2048 to **50 °C** — exactly the midpoint.

**Step 4 — Block Diagram:**

```
[Sensor] → [4-20 mA] → [ADC] → [N7:0 Raw Count A] → [SCL Instruction] → [N7:10 Engineering °C] → [HMI]
```

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q. (b):

**MOV (Move):**
- **Syntax:** `MOV Source Destination`
- **Operation:** Copies the entire 16-bit source into the destination. Source is unchanged.
- **Example:** `MOV N7:0 N7:50` copies the sensor reading from input register `N7:0` to display register `N7:50`.

**MVM (Masked Move):**
- **Syntax:** `MVM Source Mask Destination`
- **Operation:**

$$D_{new} = (S \cdot M) + (D_{old} \cdot \overline{M})$$

- **Example — Alarm Bit Update:**

Suppose:
- Status word `N7:50` (current) = `0000 0000 0000 0000`
- Source `N7:0` (new alarms) = `0000 0000 1001 0010`
- Mask `N7:1` = `0000 0000 1111 1111`

After `MVM N7:0 N7:1 N7:50`:
- Bits 0–7 are updated from source (alarm flags 1, 4, 7 set)
- Bits 8–15 are preserved at 0

Result: `N7:50 = 0000 0000 1001 0010` (decimal 146)

If only bit 2 and bit 5 are to be cleared:
- Source = `0000 0000 0000 0000`
- Mask = `0000 0000 0010 0100`
- Operation zeroes out only bits 2 and 5; all other bits remain unchanged.

> ✅ This demonstrates how MVM provides **selective bit-level control** for alarm/status word management.

---

### 🎯 Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the DIV and SQR instructions used in PLCs. A flow transmitter produces a differential pressure signal proportional to $Q^2$ (where $Q$ is the flow rate). Describe how the SQR instruction is used to linearize this signal. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the data conversion instructions (TOD and FRD) in a PLC. Illustrate with an example where a BCD thumbwheel input is read and converted to binary for arithmetic processing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q. (a):

**DIV Instruction:**

The **DIV (Divide)** instruction divides a 16-bit source (A) by another 16-bit source (B) and stores the result in a **destination file of three words**:

$$\text{Word 1: Quotient } = \lfloor A/B \rfloor$$
$$\text{Word 2: Remainder} = A \bmod B$$
$$\text{Word 3: Denominator indicator (B)}$$

**Example:** `DIV N7:10 8 N7:12` divides the value in `N7:10` by 8 and stores the quotient in `N7:12`, remainder in `N7:13`.

**SQR Instruction:**

The **SQR (Square Root)** instruction computes the integer square root of a 16-bit source and stores it in the destination:

$$D = \sqrt{A}$$

The result is truncated (rounded down) to the nearest integer.

**Linearization of Flow Signal:**

The differential pressure transmitter output (in inches of H₂O or Pa) is proportional to the **square of the flow rate**:

$$\Delta P = k \cdot Q^2$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on pipe geometry and fluid density.

After the PLC's analog input module scales $\Delta P$ to a raw integer `N7:0`, the actual flow rate is:

$$Q = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{k}}$$

This is implemented in ladder logic as:

```
[Input N7:0] → [DIV by k] → [N7:10] → [SQR] → [N7:11] = Q (in engineering units)
```

For example, if $\Delta P = 2500$ (scaled) and $k = 100$:
- `DIV N7:0 100 N7:10` → `N7:10 = 25`
- `SQR N7:10 N7:11` → `N7:11 = 5` (truncated)

> ✅ The SQR instruction **linearizes** the inherently non-linear flow signal, making it suitable for totalization, alarming, and PID control.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q. (b):

**TOD (Convert to BCD) Instruction:**

The **TOD (To BCD)** instruction converts a 16-bit binary/BCD value to a 4-digit BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) representation.

$$\text{Binary } (0 \text{ to } 65535) \rightarrow \text{BCD } (0 \text{ to } 9999)$$

If the source exceeds 9999, a status bit is set and the result is limited to 9999.

**FRD (Convert from BCD) Instruction:**

The **FRD (From BCD)** instruction converts a 4-digit BCD value to its 16-bit binary equivalent.

$$\text{BCD } (0 \text{ to } 9999) \rightarrow \text{Binary } (0 \text{ to } 9999)$$

**Example — Thumbwheel Input to Arithmetic Processing:**

A process operator sets a temperature setpoint via a **thumbwheel switch** on the control panel. The thumbwheel provides a 4-digit BCD output (e.g., 0–9999 representing 0.0–999.9 °C).

**Step 1:** The BCD thumbwheel data is wired to the PLC's 16-bit DC input module, stored in `I:0.0` (e.g., `I:0.0 = 0x0750` for setpoint 75.0 °C).

**Step 2:** Convert BCD to binary using `FRD`:

`FRD I:0.0 N7:20` → `N7:20 = 0750` (binary, decimal 750)

**Step 3:** Apply scaling using `SCL` to convert to engineering units (tenths of °C):

`SCL N7:20 0 9999 0 9999 N7:21` → `N7:21 = 750` (representing 75.0 °C × 10)

**Step 4:** Use this value in a `GRT` or `LES` comparison for alarm generation, or feed it to the PID loop setpoint register.

**Step 5:** If the result must be displayed on a 7-segment BCD panel meter, use `TOD` to convert back:

`TOD N7:21 O:0.0` → Output 4-digit BCD to the panel meter driver.

> ✅ The **FRD-TOD pair** forms the bridge between operator-friendly BCD panels and PLC-internal binary arithmetic.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SCL and SCP:** Students often write the SCL equation as $D = A \times (D_{max} - D_{min}) / (S_{max} - S_{min})$ — **forgetting the $+D_{min}$ offset term**. This single omission costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **DIV Word Counting:** The DIV destination occupies **THREE consecutive words** (quotient, remainder, denominator). Many students allocate only 1 or 2 words and lose marks for memory addressing errors.
>
> 3. **BCD vs Binary Range:** TOD/FRD works on **0–9999 (BCD)**, NOT 0–65535 (binary). Mixing up the ranges is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **MVM Bitwise OR vs Addition:** Since masked bits are non-overlapping, $D_{new} = X + Y$ is equivalent to bitwise OR. However, if the mask is **not** mutually exclusive, you **must** use the OR, not the addition. Examiners penalize this nuance.
>
> 5. **SQR Truncation:** SQR does **NOT** round — it **truncates** the result. For example, $\sqrt{10} = 3.16$ becomes **3** (not 4).
>
> 6. **Comparison vs Math Output:** Comparison instructions are **input instructions** (placed on the left of the rung). They cannot be used as output instructions in standard ladder logic. Mixing up position costs full marks on that sub-question.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Data Handling = **M**ove, **C**ompare, **C**onvert, **M**ath, **L**ogical/Shift
- ✅ **MOV** = full word copy; **MVM** = selective bit update using a mask
- ✅ **SCL** equation: $D = \left(\frac{A - S_{min}}{S_{max} - S_{min}}\right) \times (D_{max} - D_{min}) + D_{min}$ — **must include the +Dmin offset**
- ✅ **DIV** occupies 3 words: Quotient, Remainder, Denominator
- ✅ **SQR** linearizes square-law signals (e.g., flow from ΔP)
- ✅ **SCL** is the workhorse for 4–20 mA → engineering unit conversion
- ✅ **LIM** = single instruction for in-range check (lower ≤ A ≤ upper)
- ✅ **TOD** = To BCD (range 0–9999); **FRD** = From BCD (range 0–9999)
- ✅ Comparison instructions (GRT, LES, EQU, NEQ, GEQ, LEQ, LIM, MEQ) are **input-style**
- ✅ Bitwise AND, OR, XOR operate on **16 bits** independently
- ✅ BSL/BSR shift bits left/right; FFL/FFU = FIFO; LFL/LFU = LIFO
- ✅ Real-world use: **Scaling, alarm generation, PID setpoint handling, recipe management, linearization**
- ✅ **Most-tested items in KTU:** SCL (60% of marks), DIV, MVM, TOD/FRD, SQR

> 💡 **Final Exam Tip:** Memorize the **SCL equation** and the **DIV three-word structure** cold — these two alone will secure a comfortable pass in Module 4. Add **MVM bitwise logic** for distinction-level marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Manipulation Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Manipulation Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Manipulation Instructions** are a class of PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) instructions that operate on multi-bit data words (registers) rather than single discrete bits. They enable the processor to **transfer, compare, convert, compute, and transform** numerical data stored in memory locations, timers, counters, and input/output image tables during the execution of a control program.

In the context of **Process Control Instrumentation (PCICT502)**, these instructions form the computational backbone of any closed-loop or supervisory control strategy — for example, scaling a raw 4–20 mA signal to engineering units (0–100 °C), calculating the error signal $e(t) = SP - PV$, or executing a PID algorithm in a soft-PLC or DCS.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic belongs to Module 4, which deals with PLC advanced programming constructs required for implementing complex process control sequences beyond simple ladder logic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **factory floor supervisor** with a clipboard of numbers:
- **Column A** = Setpoint temperature (target).
- **Column B** = Actual temperature (measured by a sensor).
- You constantly **copy** numbers from one column to another (**Data Transfer**), **subtract** Column B from Column A to find the error (**Arithmetic**), **check** whether the error is greater than 5 °C (**Comparison**), **convert** units from Fahrenheit to Celsius (**Conversion**), and **log** whether any limit was crossed (**Logical/Shift**).

A PLC's **Data Manipulation Instructions** are exactly these "clipboard operations" — but executed by the CPU at microsecond speed, in real time, across hundreds of memory words.

| Operation Class | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| Data Transfer | Photocopying a page from one folder to another |
| Comparison | A judge comparing two values and announcing the verdict |
| Arithmetic | A calculator computing error = setpoint − process variable |
| Logical | Boolean gate deciding AND/OR/XOR of multiple flags |
| Shift/Rotate | Conveyor belt moving bottles one position at a time |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Word length** for most industrial PLCs: **16 bits** (range −32,768 to +32,767 in signed integer form).
> - **Double word**: **32 bits**.
> - **Scan time** for data manipulation block: typically **2–10 µs** per instruction (Allen-Bradley SLC 500 reference benchmark).
> - **Memory addressing base** in IEC 61131-3: `%MW`, `%MD`, `%MF` for word, double-word, and float respectively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Data Manipulation Instructions

Data manipulation instructions are broadly divided into **five functional families**:

### 2.1.1 Data Transfer (Move) Instructions
- **MOV** — Move a 16-bit word from Source to Destination.
- **MVM** — Move with Mask (selectively transfer bits).
- **CLR** — Clear a word to 0.
- **BTD** — Bit Field Distribute (Allen-Bradley specific).
- **COP** / **CPL** — Copy file / Complement file.

> **Why?** Transfer is the *most fundamental* operation — without it, no calculation result can reach an output register.

### 2.1.2 Data Comparison Instructions
- **EQU, NEQ, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ, LIM** — Equal, Not Equal, Greater Than, Greater or Equal, Less Than, Less or Equal, and Limit.
- These instructions **set internal bits** (foundational for conditional branching) without altering the source data.

### 2.1.3 Arithmetic Instructions
- **ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, SQR, NEG, ABS** — Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide, Square Root, Negate, Absolute value.
- Operands can be **integer (16-bit)** or **floating point (32-bit IEEE 754)** depending on the instruction suffix (e.g., `ADD_I` vs `ADD_F`).

### 2.1.4 Logical / Boolean Word Instructions
- **AND, OR, XOR, NOT** — Bitwise operations across an entire 16-bit word.
- Used for **masking, bit extraction, and flag aggregation**.

### 2.1.5 Shift / Rotate Instructions
- **BSL / BSR** — Bit Shift Left / Right.
- **FFL / FFU** — FIFO Load / Unload.
- **LFL / LFU** — LIFO Load / Unload.
- **SLC / RLC** — Shift Left Circular / Rotate Left Circular (word level).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Shift instructions are extensively used in **serial-to-parallel data conversion** for HART and Modbus protocol decoding in process instruments.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Instruction Category | Instruction | Symbolic Form | Typical Use in Process Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transfer | `MOV` | $D := S$ | Load SP into a working register |
| 2 | Masked Transfer | `MVM` | $D := (S \land M) \lor (D \land \overline{M})$ | Update only a subset of control bits |
| 3 | Comparison | `GRT` | if $A > B$ then flag = 1 | High-temperature alarm |
| 4 | Addition | `ADD` | $D := A + B$ | Totaliser (flow accumulation) |
| 5 | Subtraction | `SUB` | $D := A - B$ | Error $e = SP - PV$ |
| 6 | Multiplication | `MUL` | $D := A \times B$ | Scaling a 4–20 mA to % |
| 7 | Division | `DIV` | $D := A \div B$ | Ratio control / Normalisation |
| 8 | Square Root | `SQR` | $D := \sqrt{S}$ | Differential pressure → flow |
| 9 | AND (logical) | `AND` | $D := A \land B$ | Mask low byte of a word |
| 10 | OR (logical) | `OR` | $D := A \lor B$ | Combine alarm flags |
| 11 | XOR (logical) | `XOR` | $D := A \oplus B$ | Toggle a status bit |
| 12 | Bit Shift | `BSL` | $D \ll 1$ | Serial bit stream framing |
| 13 | Clear | `CLR` | $D := 0$ | Reset accumulator |
| 14 | Negate (2's comp.) | `NEG` | $D := -S$ | Reverse error sign |
| 15 | Absolute | `ABS` | $D := \vert S \vert$ | Error magnitude for I-PD control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Note:** Examiners expect students to use the **formal symbol** (e.g., $D := A + B$) along with the instruction mnemonic, not just the mnemonic alone.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **distillation column control loop**, data manipulation instructions are used to:
1. **Scale** the temperature transmitter raw count ($0\text{–}4095$) to engineering units ($0\text{–}200\,°C$).
2. **Compute** the deviation from setpoint: $e = SP - PV$.
3. **Limit** the controller output within $0\%\text{–}100\%$ using `LIM`.
4. **Transfer** the output to the analog output module (MOV to %QW0.0).
5. **Aggregate** multiple alarm bits via OR for the annunciator panel.

Without data manipulation, the PLC would be restricted to **purely discrete on/off logic** — incapable of implementing any continuous control strategy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Engineering Unit Scaling (KTU Favourite Problem)

> **Problem:** A level transmitter outputs **4–20 mA**, which the PLC's analog input module converts to a **raw integer count of 0–4095** (12-bit unipolar). The tank has a usable range of **0–5000 mm** of level. Using data manipulation instructions, derive the equation to convert the raw count $N$ into the engineering level $L$ in mm.

### Step 1 — Identify the input-output relationship
The transmitter and ADC chain produces a linear mapping:

$$L = L_{min} + \left(\frac{N - N_{min}}{N_{max} - N_{min}}\right) \times (L_{max} - L_{min})$$

### Step 2 — Substitute known values
- $L_{min} = 0$ mm
- $L_{max} = 5000$ mm
- $N_{min} = 0$
- $N_{max} = 4095$

$$\boxed{L = \frac{5000}{4095} \times N \approx 1.2210 \times N}$$

### Step 3 — Implement in PLC instructions (Allen-Bradley SLC 500 style)

```
MUL   Source A:  N7:0        (raw count)
      Source B:  1.221       (scaling constant, stored in F8:0)
      Dest:      N7:2        (final level in mm)
```

### Step 4 — Use `GRT` instruction for high-level alarm
```
GRT   Source A:  N7:2        (level in mm)
      Source B:  4500        (alarm setpoint in mm)
      Dest:      B3:5/0      (alarm flag bit)
```

### Step 5 — Output transfer to HMI display register
```
MOV   Source:    N7:2        (level in mm)
      Dest:      N7:10       (HMI shared memory)
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Two's Complement Negation

> **Problem:** Demonstrate the `NEG` instruction on a 16-bit signed word containing the value $S = +123_{10}$. Show all 16 bit positions and explain the overflow boundary.

### Step 1 — Convert +123 to binary (16-bit)
$$+123_{10} = 0000\ 0000\ 0111\ 1011_{2}$$

### Step 2 — Apply NEG (two's complement) operation
Two's complement is computed as:
$$-S = \overline{S} + 1$$

Inverting all bits:
$$\overline{S} = 1111\ 1111\ 1000\ 0100_{2}$$

Add 1 (with carry discarded):
$$-123_{10} = 1111\ 1111\ 1000\ 0101_{2}$$

### Step 3 — Verification
$$+123 + (-123) = 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_{2} = 0\ \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Negation of the most negative number ($-32\,768$) **causes overflow** because $+32\,768$ cannot be represented in 16-bit signed integer form. The `NEG` instruction sets the **Math Overflow bit (S:0/0)** in the SLC 500 status file. Always check this bit after arithmetic operations.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — MVM (Move with Mask) Instruction

> **Problem:** A 16-bit control word `N7:0` is configured as follows: bits 0–3 hold the mode selector, bits 4–11 hold the setpoint, and bits 12–15 hold the enable flags. Write a data manipulation routine to update **only bits 4–11 (the setpoint field)** to a new value of $250$ without disturbing the other bits.

### Step 1 — Define the mask
Bits to be updated: 4–11 → bit positions 4 through 11 inclusive.
Mask (1 = allow change, 0 = preserve):
$$M = 0000\ 1111\ 1111\ 0000_{2} = 0FF0_{H}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the MVM formula
$$D_{new} := (S \land M) \lor (D_{old} \land \overline{M})$$

### Step 3 — Numerical substitution
- $S = 250 = 0000\ 0000\ 1111\ 1010_{2}$
- $S \land M = 0000\ 0000\ 1111\ 0000_{2} = 00F0_{H}$
- $\overline{M} = 1111\ 0000\ 0000\ 1111_{2} = F00F_{H}$
- $D_{old} \land \overline{M} = \text{preserved nibbles from original word}$

### Step 4 — PLC Ladder Implementation
```
MVM   Source:    N7:5        (new setpoint = 250)
      Mask:      0FF0h       (hex mask constant)
      Dest:      N7:0        (the master control word)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this powerful?** Without `MVM`, you would need to **read** the word, **AND** it with a mask to clear bits 4–11, **OR** the new value, and **MOV** it back — four instructions. `MVM` does it in **one rung**, saving scan time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Data Manipulation Instructions

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Data Manipulation Instructions]:::root
    nodeB[Data Transfer MOV MVM CLR]:::grp1
    nodeC[Comparison EQU NEQ GRT GEQ LES LEQ LIM]:::grp2
    nodeD[Arithmetic ADD SUB MUL DIV SQR NEG ABS]:::grp3
    nodeE[Logical Word AND OR XOR NOT]:::grp4
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**List any three data manipulation instructions used in PLC programming. For each, state one industrial application.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **MOV (Move):** Transfers a 16-bit word from a source to a destination register. *Application:* Loading the computed PID controller output into the analog output register (`%QW0`).
2. **GRT (Greater Than):** Compares two values and sets an internal bit if the first is larger. *Application:* Activating a high-pressure alarm when boiler pressure exceeds 10 bar.
3. **MUL (Multiply):** Performs integer or floating-point multiplication. *Application:* Scaling a 0–4095 raw ADC count to a 0–100% engineering range.

> **Each carries 1 mark for naming + 2 marks for correct application example.**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between `MOV` and `MVM` instructions in PLC data manipulation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | `MOV` | `MVM` |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Copies entire 16-bit word | Copies word with a bit-mask |
| Selective bit update | Not possible | Possible |
| No. of source operands | 1 (Source) | 2 (Source + Mask) |
| Typical use | Whole-word transfer | Updating one field of a control word (e.g., mode bits) |
| Mathematical form | $D := S$ | $D := (S \land M) \lor (D \land \overline{M})$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat ladder diagram and a worked numerical example, explain the implementation of engineering-unit scaling of a 4–20 mA pressure transmitter (range 0–10 bar) using `MUL` and `MOV` instructions in a PLC.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify raw input range**
- Transmitter: 4–20 mA → 0–10 bar
- ADC resolution: 12-bit (0–4095 counts)
- At 4 mA → count = 0
- At 20 mA → count = 4095

**Step 2 — Derive the scaling equation**

$$P_{bar} = \frac{10 - 0}{4095 - 0} \times N = 0.002442 \times N$$

**Step 3 — Identify the scaling constant**
$$K = 0.002442$$

Stored in floating-point register: `F8:0 = 0.002442`

**Step 4 — Ladder Logic (Allen-Bradley SLC 500 style)**

```
Rung 1:  Scaling
   |--[  XIC  B3:0/0  ]--[  MUL  Source A : N7:0   |
   |                                Source B : F8:0   |
   |                                Dest    : N7:2 ]--|

Rung 2:  Transfer to HMI
   |--[  XIC  B3:0/0  ]--[  MOV  Source : N7:2     |
   |                                Dest   : N7:10 ]--|

Rung 3:  High Pressure Alarm (using GRT)
   |--[  XIC  B3:0/0  ]--[  GRT  Source A : N7:2   |
   |                                Source B : 8.0    |
   |                                Dest    : B3:5/0 ]--|
```

**Step 5 — Numerical verification for N = 2048**
$$P = 0.002442 \times 2048 = 5.0016\ \text{bar}$$
This corresponds to the mid-scale of the transmitter, as expected. ✓

**Mark Split-up:**
- Derivation of equation: **2 marks**
- Identification of scaling constant: **1 mark**
- Ladder diagram: **3 marks**
- Numerical verification: **1 mark**

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of the `MVM` (Move with Mask) instruction. Using a suitable example, show how it is used to update only specific bits of a control word without affecting the rest.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Formal Definition**
The `MVM` instruction performs a logical AND between the source and the mask, a logical AND between the destination's complement of the mask, and finally ORs the two results.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$D_{new} := (S \land M) \lor (D_{old} \land \overline{M})$$

Where:
- $S$ = Source word
- $M$ = Mask word (1 = update, 0 = preserve)
- $D_{old}$ = Original destination
- $D_{new}$ = Updated destination

**Step 2 — Industrial Application Example: Mode Selector Update**

Consider a 16-bit control word `N7:0` with the following bit allocation:
- Bits 0–2: Operation Mode (Auto/Manual/Service)
- Bits 3–7: Reserved
- Bits 8–15: Setpoint value (0–500 °C)

We want to change the **Mode** field (bits 0–2) to **Manual (binary 010)** without altering bits 3–15.

**Step 3 — Construct the Mask**
Bits to update: 0–2 → Mask = `0000 0000 0000 0111` = `0007H`

**Step 4 — Construct the Source Word**
Mode = 010 → Place in bits 0–2 → `0000 0000 0000 0010` = `0002H`

**Step 5 — Execute MVM**
$$D_{new} = (0002_{H} \land 0007_{H}) \lor (D_{old} \land FFF8_{H})$$
$$= 0002_{H} \lor (D_{old}\ \text{with low 3 bits cleared})$$

**Step 6 — Ladder Implementation**

```
|--[XIC B3:0/1]--[MVM  Source : N7:5  (value = 0002H)
|                Mask   : 0FF07H or F8:1
|                Dest   : N7:0   (control word)]--|
```

**Mark Split-up:**
- Formula: **2 marks**
- Mask construction: **2 marks**
- Source word construction: **1 mark**
- Ladder diagram: **2 marks**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast integer and floating-point arithmetic instructions in PLCs. Mention at least three points of difference and explain when floating-point should be preferred in process control.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| # | Parameter | Integer Arithmetic | Floating-Point Arithmetic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bit width | 16 bits (typical) | 32 bits (IEEE 754 single) |
| 2 | Range | −32,768 to +32,767 | ±3.4 × 10⁻³⁸ to ±3.4 × 10³⁸ |
| 3 | Precision | Exact (1 count) | ~7 significant decimal digits |
| 4 | Scan time | 1–2 µs | 15–30 µs |
| 5 | Memory usage | 1 word (2 bytes) | 2 words (4 bytes) |
| 6 | Overflow behaviour | Wraps / sets flag | Sets overflow bit; no wrap |
| 7 | Typical use | Counting, setpoint storage | PID calculation, flow totalisation |

**When floating-point is preferred in process control:**
1. **PID Control:** Integral term accumulates over thousands of scan cycles — would overflow 16-bit integer range.
2. **Square root extraction (SQR):** For differential-pressure flow measurement, $\sqrt{\Delta P}$ demands high dynamic range.
3. **Process variable scaling:** Inputs like 0.002442 (scaling constant) cannot be represented as integers without losing resolution.
4. **Cascade control:** Inner and outer loop calculations involve multiplication of small gains; floating point prevents truncation errors.

**Mark Split-up:**
- Comparison table (min 3 points): **3 marks**
- Floating-point preference explanation: **2 marks**
- Process control examples: **2 marks**

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe shift and rotate instructions in PLCs. With the help of a 16-bit word, explain the operation of a 4-position bit shift left (BSL) instruction and its application in serial communication.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Classification**
- **BSL (Bit Shift Left):** Shifts all bits one position toward MSB; the LSB receives either 0 or a data bit from the source.
- **BSR (Bit Shift Right):** Shifts all bits one position toward LSB; the MSB receives 0 or a data bit.
- **SLC/RLC:** Shift/Rotate Left Circular (word level).
- **SRC/RRC:** Shift/Rotate Right Circular (word level).

**Step 2 — Operation of BSL on 16-bit Word**

Initial word: `1011 0100 1110 0010`

After 1 shift left: `0110 1001 1100 0100` (MSB discarded, LSB = 0)
After 2 shifts: `1101 0011 1000 1000`
After 3 shifts: `1010 0111 0001 0000`
After 4 shifts: `0100 1110 0010 0000`

**Mathematical Representation (for n shifts):**

$$W_{new} = (W_{old} \times 2^{n})\ \text{mod}\ 2^{16}$$

**Step 3 — Application in Serial Communication**

In HART or Modbus RTU protocol decoding, the PLC receives a stream of bits **one at a time** from the serial port. Each incoming bit must be inserted at the LSB and the previous bits must shift left by one position to assemble a complete byte (or word).

```
Ladder Logic Concept:

|--[XIC Receive_Enable]--[BSL  Source: Serial_Bit_In
|                          Dest : N7:50  (assembled word)
|                          Control: R6:0]--|
```

After 8 shifts, `N7:50` contains the fully assembled byte, which can then be moved to a holding register for protocol processing.

**Step 4 — Industrial Use Cases**
1. **Serial-to-parallel conversion** in legacy field instruments.
2. **Bit packing** for efficient alarm-word transmission to SCADA.
3. **CRC checksum computation** in serial protocols.

**Mark Split-up:**
- BSL definition and operation: **2 marks**
- Numerical example: **2 marks**
- Serial communication application: **3 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (PCICT502 — Module 4):**
> 1. **Confusing `MOV` with `MVM`:** Students often write `MOV` when the question demands selective bit update. Always check whether a *mask* is mentioned.
> 2. **Ignoring overflow flags:** After `ADD` or `MUL`, students must check the **Math Overflow bit (S:0/0)**. Failure to mention this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the sign bit (Bit 15):** A 16-bit "integer" is **signed**. A value with bit 15 = 1 is negative in two's complement representation. Many students treat it as unsigned and lose marks.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in scaling:** Always state the units of the scaling constant explicitly (e.g., $K = 0.002442\ \text{bar/count}$).
> 5. **Scan time neglect:** In KTU ESE answers, expect a follow-up question on **how data manipulation affects scan time**. Floating-point operations increase scan time by ~10×.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Data manipulation = Word-level operations** (16-bit or 32-bit) as opposed to bit-level logic.
- **Five families:** Transfer, Comparison, Arithmetic, Logical, Shift/Rotate.
- **Most-used scaling equation:** $E = E_{min} + \left(\frac{N - N_{min}}{N_{max} - N_{min}}\right)(E_{max} - E_{min})$
- **`MOV`** copies the whole word; **`MVM`** allows selective bit updates using a mask: $D := (S \land M) \lor (D \land \overline{M})$.
- **Comparison instructions** (GRT, LES, EQU, etc.) **do not modify** source data — they only set a destination bit.
- **Arithmetic overflow** must be handled — especially after `MUL` and `ADD` of large integers.
- **Floating-point** is mandatory for PID, square root, and high-precision scaling.
- **Two's complement** representation governs the `NEG` instruction: $-S = \overline{S} + 1$.
- **Shift instructions** are critical for **serial protocol decoding** (HART, Modbus).
- **PLC memory areas:** `%IW` (input word), `%MW` (memory word), `%QW` (output word) — as per IEC 61131-3.
- **Scan time** increases with each data manipulation rung — keep PID math in floating-point but avoid floating-point inside high-speed interrupt routines.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Data Manipulation Instructions**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

> - **Module:** Module 4
