(iv) **Application server** — Hosts higher-level software such as MPC (model-predictive control), real-time optimisers (RTO), batch-recipe management (ISA-88), and production reporting. Typically resides at Level 3.

(v) **Marshalling cabinet** — A physical cabinet where field cables are *terminated, identified, and cross-wired* to the I/O cards of the controller. Includes fuses, terminals, cable glands, and signal-conditioning devices. Provides isolation between harsh field wiring and sensitive controller electronics.

(vi) **Redundant controller pair** — Two identical controllers running in **synchronised primary/backup** mode. Both receive the same I/O and execute the same logic; the backup takes over within one cycle (< 100 ms) if the primary fails. Eliminates the controller as a single point of failure.

(vii) **Operator console (OWS)** — A PC-based HMI station running the vendor's graphics software. Displays live plant mimics, trends, and alarm lists. The only Level 2 device from which an operator can change set-points, switch control modes, and acknowledge alarms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DCS** = Distributed Control System; a *layered, redundant, networked* control architecture for continuous and batch processes.
- **Purdue Reference Model** has **five levels** (0 → 4): Field, Direct Control, Supervisory, Operations, Enterprise. KTU's most-frequently-tested diagram.
- **Level 1 (Direct Control)** is the *only* level that physically *closes* the loop. Higher levels only *supervise*.
- **Redundancy** exists at every level: power, controller, I/O, network, historian, workstation. Always mention in exam answers.
- **Determinism** of the bus is a non-negotiable design requirement. Fieldbus cycle time must be **< control loop execution time**.
- **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$. For 1oo2 redundancy, $A_{sys} = 1 - (U)^2$.
- **Communication protocols** to remember: Foundation Fieldbus H1, Profibus PA/DP, HART, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA.
- **Standardised configuration** is enabled by **IEC 61131-3** programming languages (FBD, LD, ST, SFC, IL).
- **DCS ≠ SCADA ≠ PLC:** DCS = tight geographic distribution, time-deterministic, control-centric; SCADA = wide geographic distribution, event-driven, telemetry-centric; PLC = single-machine, fast discrete / motion control, no plant-wide supervisory layer.
- **Major DCS vendors** to mention in answers: **Honeywell (Experion), Emerson (DeltaV), Siemens (PCS 7), ABB (System 800xA), Yokogawa (CENTUM VP), Schneider (Foxboro)**.
- **Common 14-mark question pattern:** "Draw and explain the architecture of a DCS" — always include the **five levels**, **functional blocks at each level**, **redundancy**, and **communication backbone** for full marks.
- **Common 3-mark question pattern:** "Differentiate DCS and SCADA" or "Define Level 1 of DCS" — answer in 3–4 sharp lines with a key term in **bold** (e.g., **time-deterministic**, **plant-wide**, **redundant**).
- **Numericals expected:** availability, MTBF/MTTR, annual downtime, and bus cycle-time calculations — practice the squares and unit conversions carefully.
- **Mention ISA-95 / IEC 62264** as the international standard for DCS–ERP integration when discussing Level 3–4 communication.
- **Mention IEC 61131-3** as the programming-language standard for DCS configuration when discussing control strategies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SCADA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized, computer-based industrial control architecture that monitors, gathers, and processes real-time data from geographically distributed field instruments, sensors, and control devices (PLCs, RTUs, IEDs), and directly issues supervisory commands to those devices to regulate large-scale industrial processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim for Exams):**
> *“SCADA is a system operating with coded signals over communication channels so as to provide control of remote equipment (using typically one communication channel per remote station). The control system may be combined with data acquisition, hence the term SCADA.”* — As defined by **IEC 60870-5** and adopted in the **KTU PCICT502 (2024 Scheme)** Module 4 syllabus.

In the context of **Process Control Instrumentation**, SCADA sits at the top of the **CIM (Computer Integrated Manufacturing)** hierarchy — the **Level 3 (Production Control / Operations Management)** layer — above the **Level 2 (Supervisory / HMI)** and **Level 1 (Direct Digital Control / PLC-RTU)** layers of the **ISA-95 / Purdue Model**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Air Traffic Control Tower"**
> Think of SCADA exactly like an **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Tower** at a busy international airport.
> - The **radar screens and headsets** in the tower = the **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)** and **HMI (Human Machine Interface)**.
> - The **pilots in the cockpit** = the **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** or **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** on the plant floor.
> - The **radio signals** between tower and pilots = the **communication protocol (Modbus, DNP3, Profibus, IEC 60870)**.
> - The **aircraft themselves** = the field **sensors, valves, motors, transmitters** in the plant.
> 
> The pilot (PLC) *flies* the aircraft (controls the process) autonomously, but the tower (SCADA) *monitors everything from above* and gives high-level commands such as "change altitude" or "land now" — **SCADA supervises, it does not directly control the loop.**

This is a **critical exam distinction**: SCADA is *supervisory* (Level 2/3) and does *not* replace the basic regulatory control PID loop — it sits above it. The lowest level of automation is **field instrumentation (Level 0)**, then **DDC/PLC (Level 1)**, then **SCADA/HMI (Level 2)**, then **MES (Level 3)**, and finally **ERP (Level 4)**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

| KTU Term | Expansion | Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **MTU** | Master Terminal Unit | Central SCADA server; collects & processes all data |
| **RTU** | Remote Terminal Unit | Field-hardened, telemetry-focused controller (geographically remote sites) |
| **PLC** | Programmable Logic Controller | Factory-floor controller; used in SCADA as a substation |
| **HMI** | Human Machine Interface | The graphical screen an operator uses to interact with SCADA |
| **IED** | Intelligent Electronic Device | Smart sensor/breaker used in power SCADA |
| **DAS** | Data Acquisition System | Read-only monitoring branch of SCADA |
| **DCS** | Distributed Control System | Localized, high-speed peer-to-peer; *competitor* of SCADA |
| **Purdue Model** | ISA-95 reference model | Standard 5-level architecture SCADA sits within |
| **Telemetry** | Remote measurement transmission | Wireless/wired data transport from field → MTU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the SCADA mnemonic "S-C-A-D-A":**
> **S**upervisory (it watches, not directly loops)
> **C**ontrol (sends setpoints/commands to PLCs/RTUs)
> **A**cquisition (collects tag values from thousands of I/O points)
> **D**ata (the data is time-stamped, logged, archived)
> **A**larming (alerts operators when values cross thresholds)

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

The following standards/quasi-constants are essential to cite in **KTU 2024 Scheme** answers:

- **IEC 60870-5**: International protocol for SCADA in power & water utilities
- **DNP3 (IEEE 1815)**: Distributed Network Protocol v3 — dominant in **North American SCADA**
- **Modbus RTU/TCP**: De-facto industrial standard; supports up to **247 slave devices per master**
- **OPC UA (IEC 62541)**: Modern object-oriented protocol replacing legacy OPC DA
- **Profibus / Foundation Fieldbus**: Used in **DCS/SCADA hybrid** systems
- **Reference model**: **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model (Levels 0 → 4)**
- **Typical SCADA scan rate**: **1 to 10 seconds** (vs. DCS at 100 ms to 1 s)

---

### 1.5 Syllabus-Mandated Scope (KTU 2024 Module 4)

The KTU 2024 PCICT502 syllabus defines the following expected outcomes for this topic:

- **CO4 (Module 4):** *Describe the architecture, components, and functions of SCADA and DCS in industrial automation.*
- Students must be able to **(i)** draw a labeled block diagram, **(ii)** distinguish SCADA vs. DCS, **(iii)** list protocols, **(iv)** explain RTU vs. PLC, and **(v)** describe typical applications in water, power, and process plants.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Architecture: Hierarchical Block Breakdown

A SCADA system is *not* a single box — it is a **distributed, layered, networked control hierarchy**. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires the student to know the **functional blocks** and the **information flow** between them.

#### 2.1.1 The Four Primary Subsystems

1. **Field Instrumentation Layer (Level 0 — The "Senses")**
   - **Sensors/Transmitters**: Pressure (PT), Temperature (TT), Flow (FT), Level (LT), Analytical (AT, pH, conductivity)
   - **Final Control Elements**: Pneumatic/electric **Control Valves, Variable Speed Drives, Solenoids, Motor Starters**
   - **Function**: Converts the physical process variable into a standardized signal (typically **4–20 mA** current loop, or digital **HART** over 4–20 mA).

2. **Direct Control Layer (Level 1 — The "Reflexes")**
   - **PLCs and RTUs** — these perform the *fast, deterministic* closed-loop control (e.g., a PID running at 100 ms).
   - They are *autonomous* — the SCADA system going down does not stop them.

3. **Supervisory Layer (Level 2 — The "Brain / Eyes")**
   - **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)**: The SCADA server. Runs the SCADA software (e.g., **Wonderware InTouch, Siemens WinCC, GE iFIX, Ignition by Inductive Automation**).
   - **HMI Workstations**: Operator-facing screens with **mimics, trends, alarms, recipe managers**.
   - **Historian Database**: Time-series database that stores every tag value (e.g., **PI Server, Wonderware Historian**).

4. **Operations Management Layer (Level 3 — The "Memory / Strategy")**
   - **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** and **production reporting**.
   - **ERP integration** for orders, materials, and quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this layered design?** The **Why** is **fault containment** — if SCADA crashes, the plant keeps running on PLCs/RTUs. The **How** is **defense in depth** — each layer has its own scan time, redundancy, and fail-safe state.

---

#### 2.1.2 Communication Infrastructure

The communication subsystem in a modern SCADA is **layered** (mirroring the **OSI 7-layer model**, but with **deterministic real-time industrial protocols**):

| Layer | OSI Equivalent | SCADA Realization |
|---|---|---|
| **Fieldbus** | Layer 1–2 | Modbus RTU, Profibus PA, Foundation Fieldbus H1, HART |
| **Control LAN** | Layer 3 | Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, DNP3 over TCP |
| **Plant Backbone** | Layer 4 | TCP/IP over industrial Ethernet, VLANs |
| **WAN / Telemetry** | Layers 5–7 | Cellular, radio, satellite, leased lines, IEC 60870-5-104 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common question is *"Why does SCADA use Modbus rather than HTTP?"* The answer is **determinism + small payload + error checking for noisy plant floors**. Modbus frames are typically just **8 bytes**; an HTTP header alone is 100+ bytes.

---

### 2.2 The Four Generations of SCADA (KTU Mandatory History)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the **evolution of SCADA**, so the student *must* be able to list and characterize the four generations:

| Generation | Era | Hardware | Communication | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen — Monolithic** | 1960s–70s | Single mainframe, proprietary RTUs | Serial (RS-232/485) | No redundancy; no networking |
| **2nd Gen — LAN / Distributed** | 1980s–90s | LAN-connected stations; task-specific PCs | Proprietary LAN (e.g., Conitel) | Still vendor-locked |
| **3rd Gen — Open / Networked** | 1990s–2000s | COTS hardware, Ethernet, WAN | **TCP/IP, DNP3, IEC 60870, Modbus TCP** | Open but vulnerable to cyber-attack |
| **4th Gen — IoT / Cloud SCADA** | 2010s–present | Virtualized servers, web HMIs, OPC UA | **OPC UA, MQTT, REST/JSON, cloud** | Cyber-security risk; latency in cloud |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hook:** "Explain 4th generation SCADA in 4 lines."
> 4th Gen = **IoT, Cloud, Web-based HMI, OPC UA, IIoT, predictive analytics, and AI-based alarming.**

---

### 2.3 SCADA vs. DCS vs. PLC — The Triad Distinction

This is the **single most-asked comparison** in KTU University Exams. The KTU 2024 module-4 question bank consistently tests this.

| Parameter | **SCADA** | **DCS** | **PLC** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geographic scope** | Wide-area (km to 1000s of km) | Plant-local (single plant) | Machine-local (a single line) |
| **Primary function** | Supervisory + data acquisition | Tight closed-loop control | Sequential logic, interlocking |
| **Latency tolerance** | **1 to 10 s** | **100 ms to 1 s** | **1 to 100 ms** |
| **Architecture** | Central MTU + remote RTUs | Distributed controllers, peer-to-peer | Standalone, networked to SCADA |
| **Redundancy** | Often hot-standby MTU | Built-in controller & I/O redundancy | Optional |
| **Communication** | Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870, OPC UA | Proprietary (Honeywell CDA, ABB 800xA) | Fieldbus (Profibus, EtherNet/IP) |
| **Typical use** | Power grid, water network, pipeline | Refinery, petrochem, power plant boiler | Assembly line, packaging |
| **Tag count typical** | 10,000 – 1,000,000+ | 500 – 50,000 | 50 – 2,000 |
| **Cost per tag** | Low | High | Very low |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** "SCADA replaces DCS." ❌ — They are *complementary* in modern plants. A refinery has a **DCS for the process unit** and a **SCADA for tank-farm and pipeline** monitoring.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-grade formula reference** for SCADA calculations. There are very few *pure math* equations in SCADA, but the **performance and network equations** are testable.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of slaves} &= 2^n - 1 \quad \text{where } n = \text{bits in address field} \\
\text{For Modbus: } n &= 8 \Rightarrow 2^8 - 1 = 255 \text{ addressed}, \text{ 247 usable} \\
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{\text{Frame payload (bits)}}{\text{Frame time (s)}} \quad \text{[bits/s = baud]} \\
\text{SCADA scan time} &= N \cdot t_{\text{polling}} \quad \text{where } N = \text{number of RTUs} \\
\text{Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)}_{\text{system}} &= \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_i} \right)^{-1} \\
\text{Availability} \; A &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \quad \text{(target } A \geq 0.999 \text{ for SCADA)} \\
\text{Data Storage} &= (\text{Tags}) \times (\text{Scan rate}) \times (\text{Retention days}) \times (\text{Bits per value}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Markdown Table — Boundary Conditions and Units (pipe-free):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of addressable slaves per bus | $N$ | 247 (Modbus) | devices | $\le 2^n - 1$ |
| Polling interval per RTU | $t_p$ | 1 – 10 | s | must be $>$ field scan time |
| Standard analog loop current | $I$ | **4 – 20 mA** | mA | live-zero protects against wire break |
| HART bandwidth (FSK over 4–20 mA) | $B$ | 1200 | bps | non-interfering with analog |
| Scan time of MTU | $T_{scan}$ | 1 – 10 | s | longer than DCS loop |
| Latency over WAN | $\tau$ | 100 – 1000 | ms | tolerable for SCADA, not for DCS |
| OPC UA message size | $L$ | $\le 65\,535$ | bytes | TCP segment |
| Availability target | $A$ | $\ge 0.999$ | — | 8.7 h/yr downtime allowed |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

SCADA is the **silent backbone** of all modern critical infrastructure:

- **Power grid**: Every electricity substation uses **DNP3 over IEC 61850** SCADA; operators in the National Load Despatch Centre (India — **NLDC, POSOCO**) see and re-route gigawatts in real time.
- **Water treatment**: SCADA monitors reservoir levels, pump stations, and chemical dosing across a city.
- **Oil & Gas pipelines**: SCADA handles **leak detection, batch tracking, and pump control** across thousands of km (e.g., Reliance Jamnagar, Indian Oil pipelines).
- **Smart manufacturing (Industry 4.0)**: SCADA is being absorbed into **IIoT platforms** with OPC UA tags visible in the cloud.
- **Railways**: Signalling and traction power SCADA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala-specific reference (for context only, not exam):** The **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** Load Despatch Centre uses a **SCADA-EMS (Energy Management System)** to monitor the entire 400 kV/220 kV/110 kV grid in real time.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> SCADA is a *systems* topic — pure mathematical derivations are minimal. This section instead gives an **exhaustive logical derivation of the SCADA scan time, MTBF availability, and the polling-cycle math**, which are the most common *calculation* items in KTU University Exams.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — SCADA Total Scan Time (Polling Cycle)

In a polled SCADA network, the MTU cycles through **N** RTUs sequentially. The total time to complete **one full scan** of the plant is the **sum of the per-RTU polling times**.

**Given:**

- $N$ = number of RTUs on the link
- $t_q$ = query/response round-trip time per RTU (depends on baud, frame size, and propagation delay)
- $t_{\text{frame}}$ = time to transmit one Modbus/RTU frame

**Step 1 — Define frame time.** 
The frame time is the number of bits in a Modbus RTU frame divided by the serial baud rate. A typical Modbus RTU frame is **10 bits per byte** (8 data + 1 start + 1 stop) and a request/response pair is **16 bytes minimum**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{frame}} &= \frac{16 \text{ bytes} \times 10 \text{ bits/byte}}{B \text{ bps}} \\
&= \frac{160}{B} \text{ seconds per RTU}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 9600 bps link, this is $\tfrac{160}{9600} = 16.67$ ms per RTU (plus propagation, typically < 1 ms over copper).

**Step 2 — Add per-RTU overhead.** 
The round-trip time $t_q$ also includes the slave's processing time $t_{\text{slave}}$ (typically 5–50 ms for an RTU to scan its I/O and respond).

$$
t_q = t_{\text{frame}} + t_{\text{propagation}} + t_{\text{slave}}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum over all RTUs.** 
The total SCADA scan time $T_{\text{scan}}$ is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{q,i} \\
&\approx N \cdot \bar{t_q} \quad \text{(assuming uniform } t_q \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add communication turnaround / master overhead.** 
In practice, the master also needs a small inter-character delay (3.5 character times in Modbus). We add $t_{\text{turn}}$:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot \bar{t_q} + N \cdot t_{\text{turn}}
$$

**Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**

*An MTU polls 50 RTUs over a 19200 bps Modbus link. Each RTU's round-trip is 12 bytes (120 bits). Slave processing takes 20 ms. Inter-frame turnaround is 5 ms. Calculate the total scan time.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{frame}} &= \frac{120 \text{ bits}}{19200 \text{ bps}} = 0.00625 \text{ s} = 6.25 \text{ ms} \\
t_q &= 6.25 + 20 = 26.25 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{scan}} &= 50 \times 26.25 + 50 \times 5 \\
&= 1312.5 + 250 \\
&= 1562.5 \text{ ms} \approx 1.56 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 50-RTU SCADA system updates in **1.56 s** — perfectly within the 1–10 s typical SCADA band.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — System Availability (Redundancy Math)

A SCADA MTU is **dual-redundant** (primary + hot-standby). Compute system availability given component reliabilities.

**Given:**

- Single MTU: $\text{MTBF} = 50\,000$ h, $\text{MTTR} = 4$ h
- Communication network: $\text{MTBF} = 30\,000$ h, $\text{MTTR} = 8$ h
- Configuration: **parallel redundant** (one works, system works)

**Step 1 — Single-component availability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{MTU}} &= \frac{50\,000}{50\,000 + 4} = 0.99992 \\
A_{\text{Net}} &= \frac{30\,000}{30\,000 + 8} = 0.99973
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Parallel (1-out-of-2) redundancy availability.**

For 1-out-of-2 redundancy (one fails, the other takes over), the system *fails* only if **both** components fail:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{parallel}} &= 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.99992)(1 - 0.99992) \\
&= 1 - (0.00008)^2 \\
&= 1 - 6.4 \times 10^{-9} \\
&\approx 0.999999994
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Series combination with network.** 
The redundant MTU pair is in **series** with the network. Series availability is the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{SCADA}} &= A_{\text{MTU-pair}} \times A_{\text{Net}} \\
&= 0.999999994 \times 0.99973 \\
&\approx 0.99973
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **network (serial link)** is now the *bottleneck* — redundancy of the MTU alone is not enough; the WAN and RTU-side radio links must also be redundant (e.g., dual SIM, dual fibre).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Data Storage Estimation for the Historian

The KTU 2024 exam may ask: *“Estimate the daily storage requirement of the historian.”*

**Given:**

- 5,000 tags
- Average scan rate: once per **2 s**
- Each value stored as a 32-bit float (4 bytes) + 8-byte time-stamp
- 1 year of retention

**Step 1 — Samples per tag per day.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Samples/day} &= \frac{86\,400 \text{ s/day}}{2 \text{ s/sample}} = 43\,200 \text{ samples/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Bytes per sample.**

$$
\text{Bytes/sample} = 4 \text{ (float)} + 8 \text{ (timestamp)} = 12 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Total daily volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{day}} &= 5\,000 \times 43\,200 \times 12 \\
&= 2.592 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} = 2.59 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{year}} &= 2.59 \times 365 \approx 946 \text{ GB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> With typical 2× lossless compression of time-series data, a real historian needs **~ 500 GB/year** for this plant.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SCADA vs. DCS Decision Logic

The KTU 2024 exam often frames this as *"A 200 km water pipeline needs an automation system. Justify SCADA or DCS."* The decision matrix below is the answer template.

| Selection Criterion | SCADA-Winning Condition | DCS-Winning Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Geographic spread | **Wide area (>10 km)** | Plant-local (< 1 km) |
| Loop speed requirement | **Slow loops (minutes)** | **Fast loops (< 1 s)** |
| Number of I/O | 10,000+ tags, mostly monitoring | 1,000–10,000 tightly integrated |
| Regulatory control? | No (only setpoint supervisory) | Yes (PID, cascade, ratio) |
| Number of operator consoles | Many, distributed | Few, central control room |
| Redundancy | WAN redundancy (dual radio, dual SIM) | Controller & I/O redundancy |
| Cost sensitivity | High (low per-tag cost) | Lower (high per-tag cost) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision rule of thumb (KTU exam answer):**
> *Choose SCADA when geography and telemetry dominate; choose DCS when tight continuous control dominates; in modern plants, the two are integrated (DCS as the Level 1 controller, SCADA as the Level 2 supervisory layer).*

---

### 3.5 Security / Cybersecurity Vulnerability Derivation (Attack Surface Logic)

A KTU 2024 question may ask: *“List three cyber-attack vectors in modern SCADA and give one mitigation for each.”*

| Attack Vector | Mechanism | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping on Modbus TCP** | Plain-text protocol; no encryption | Wrap in **TLS** or migrate to **OPC UA** with built-in security |
| **Man-in-the-Middle on DNP3** | DNP3-Sec adds authentication | Use **DNP3-SA (Secure Authentication) per IEEE 1815-2012** |
| **Unauthorized setpoint change** | Open TCP port 502 to internet | **Firewall + DMZ + VPN** for remote access |
| **Stuxnet-class USB infection** | Field engineer laptop | **Endpoint AV + removable-media control** |
| **Ransomware on HMI** | Phishing → OT network | **Network segmentation (Purdue zones & conduits)** + offline backup |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hook:** *“Why is patching a SCADA MTU harder than patching a corporate server?”*
> Answer: SCADA uptime requirements are **24 × 7 × 365**; an unplanned restart can interrupt a chemical batch costing crores, and the SCADA vendor must *qualify* every patch against the process. Hence the well-known **"patch lag"** of years in OT (Operational Technology) networks.

### 4.2 Information Flow — Data Acquisition Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant F as Field Sensor
    participant P as PLC / RTU
    participant M as MTU SCADA Server
    participant H as HMI / Operator
    participant D as Historian
    participant A as Alarm Engine

    F->>P: 4-20 mA analog OR HART digital
    P->>P: Read inputs, run PID, update outputs
    P-->>M: Modbus TCP poll response [tag values]
    M->>M: Time-stamp, validate, scale to engineering units
    M->>D: Archive every value to historian
    M->>A: Check against limits
    alt Limit exceeded
        A->>H: Trigger alarm with priority, beep
        H->>M: Operator acknowledges
        M->>P: Supervisory command (e.g., new setpoint)
    else Normal
        M->>H: Refresh mimic display
    end
```

### 4.3 SCADA Network Topology — Polled vs. Report-by-Exception

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Polled[Polled Architecture - Legacy]
        m1[MTU]:::sup
        r1[RTU 1]:::ctrl
        r2[RTU 2]:::ctrl
        r3[RTU 3]:::ctrl
        m1 -- Query 1 --> r1
        m1 -- Query 2 --> r2
        m1 -- Query 3 --> r3
        r1 -- Response --> m1
        r2 -- Response --> m1
        r3 -- Response --> m1
    end

    subgraph RBE[Report by Exception - Modern]
        m2[MTU]:::sup
        s1[RTU 1]:::ctrl
        s2[RTU 2]:::ctrl
        s3[RTU 3]:::ctrl
        s1 -- Change detected --> m2
        s2 -- Change detected --> m2
        s3 -- Change detected --> m2
        m2 -- Periodic heartbeat 60 s --> s1
        m2 -- Periodic heartbeat 60 s --> s2
        m2 -- Periodic heartbeat 60 s --> s3
    end

    classDef sup fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#06c,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#060,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Report-by-Exception (RBE) is **bandwidth-efficient** for slow-changing process tags (e.g., tank level), reducing WAN costs over cellular/satellite links. Polled systems are simpler to debug, which is why legacy SCADA still uses polling.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU 2024 — Part A (3-Mark Short-Answer Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme regulations**, Part A questions test *Remembering* and *Understanding* cognitive levels (Bloom Levels 1 & 2). Each carries **3 marks** and expects a **3–4 sentence answer with a labeled diagram where applicable**.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define SCADA. List its four main components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a computer-based industrial control system that monitors and controls geographically dispersed plant assets from a central location using coded signals over communication channels.
>
> The four main components are:
> 1. **Field Instrumentation** — sensors, transmitters, and final control elements.
> 2. **RTUs / PLCs** — field-level data concentrators and controllers.
> 3. **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)** — the central SCADA server with HMI and historian.
> 4. **Communication infrastructure** — WAN/LAN, fieldbus, telemetry (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870, OPC UA).

*(Allocation: Definition 1 m, List 1 m, Brief on each 1 m = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between SCADA and DCS in any three aspects.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **SCADA** | **DCS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Wide-area, geographically distributed (km to 1000s km) | Plant-local, single-site |
| **Function** | Supervisory, data acquisition, slow setpoint changes | Tight closed-loop regulatory control (PID, cascade) |
| **Latency** | Tolerates 1–10 s scan time | Requires < 1 s response |
| **Architecture** | Central MTU + remote RTUs | Distributed, peer-to-peer controllers |
| **Typical use** | Power grid, water network, pipeline | Refinery, petrochem, power plant boiler |

*(Allocation: 1 mark per row × 3 rows = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 KTU 2024 — Part B (14-Mark Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The cognitive levels are *Applying* and *Analyzing* (Bloom Levels 3 & 4).

---

#### ✅ CHOICE 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a SCADA system. List the functions of the MTU and HMI. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the four generations of SCADA with their key features. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

A **SCADA system** has a hierarchical architecture with the following blocks:

**1. Field Instrumentation Layer** *(0.5 m)* — Sensors (PT, TT, FT, LT) and final control elements (valves, drives) communicate over **4–20 mA** or **HART** signals.

**2. Direct Control Layer — RTUs and PLCs** *(1 m)* — **RTUs** are installed at remote sites (e.g., a wellhead, a substation) and provide telemetry. **PLCs** are installed in plants for fast discrete/continuous control. They autonomously perform PID, scan I/O, and execute logic.

**3. Supervisory Layer — MTU and HMI** *(2 m)*
- The **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)** is the central SCADA server. Functions:
  - Polls RTUs/PLCs at configured intervals
  - Stores tag values in a real-time database
  - Generates trends, reports, and alarm events
  - Provides API to MES/ERP via OPC UA
- The **HMI (Human Machine Interface)** is the operator's screen. Functions:
  - Displays **mimics** (graphical plant overview)
  - Allows operator to **acknowledge alarms** and issue **supervisory commands** (setpoint changes, start/stop)
  - Provides **trend curves** for analysis

**4. Communication Layer** *(1 m)* — Provides the data highway. Protocols: **Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870, OPC UA**. Communication can be **wired (fibre, copper)**, **radio (VHF/UHF), cellular (4G/5G)**, or **satellite**.

**5. Operations Management Layer** *(0.5 m)* — MES and ERP consume SCADA data for production scheduling, inventory, and quality.

**Block Diagram:** *(0.5 m — student must draw the standard 4-level pyramid or block diagram and label all arrows.)*

**Sample labeled ASCII block diagram to include in answer sheet:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&[\text{ERP / MES}] \\
&\quad \downarrow \uparrow \text{OPC UA, SQL} \\
&[\text{MTU + HMI + Historian + Alarm Server}] \\
&\quad \downarrow \uparrow \text{Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870, OPC UA} \\
&[\text{PLCs and RTUs}] \\
&\quad \downarrow \uparrow \text{4-20 mA, HART, Fieldbus} \\
&[\text{Sensors and Control Valves}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion line** *(0.5 m)*: SCADA's hierarchical design enables **centralised monitoring with distributed control** — if SCADA fails, the PLCs continue to run the plant.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Four generations of SCADA:**

**1. First Generation — Monolithic (1960s–70s) (1.5 m)**
- Architecture: Single mainframe computer + proprietary RTUs.
- Communication: Dedicated serial lines, no networking.
- Limitation: No redundancy; vendor-locked; expensive to scale.
- Example: Early Ebasco, Bristol Babcock systems.

**2. Second Generation — LAN-based Distributed (1980s–90s) (1.5 m)**
- Architecture: Multiple tasks split across LAN-connected PCs (e.g., one PC for HMI, one for alarms, one for historian).
- Communication: Proprietary LAN protocols (e.g., Conitel, Modbus over RS-485).
- Limitation: Still vendor-specific; limited open connectivity.
- Example: Telvent OASyS, Foxboro I/A.

**3. Third Generation — Open / Networked (1990s–2010s) (2 m)**
- Architecture: COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) servers, standard Ethernet, WAN.
- Communication: **TCP/IP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, Modbus TCP, OPC DA.**
- Key feature: Open architecture, easy third-party integration, but exposed to **cyber-attacks** (Stuxnet 2010).
- Example: Wonderware, Siemens WinCC, GE iFIX.

**4. Fourth Generation — IIoT / Cloud SCADA (2010s–present) (2 m)**
- Architecture: Virtualized MTUs, web-based HMIs, edge gateways, cloud historians.
- Communication: **OPC UA, MQTT, REST/JSON over HTTPS, AMQP.**
- Key features: **AI-based predictive alarming, mobile dashboards, digital-twin integration, IIoT analytics.**
- Challenge: Cyber-security and cloud latency for real-time control.

**Conclusion:** *(0.5 m)* SCADA has evolved from monolithic mainframes to **cloud-native IIoT platforms**; the modern trend is convergence with **DCS and IoT** under the **OPC UA umbrella**.

---

#### ✅ CHOICE 2 — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) Compare PLC and RTU in detail. State where each is preferred in a SCADA architecture. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss the major SCADA communication protocols (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870, OPC UA) with their key features. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**PLC vs. RTU Comparison Table** *(4 marks — full table required in exam)*

| Parameter | **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** | **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Designed for** | Factory-floor machine control | Geographically remote, harsh-environment telemetry |
| **Operating environment** | Indoor, controlled, plant floor | Outdoor, **–40 °C to +70 °C**, humidity, vibration |
| **Power** | 24 V DC or 120/230 V AC, mains available | Often **solar + battery**, low-power, sleep modes |
| **Communication ports** | Ethernet, Profibus, Modbus TCP | Serial, radio, cellular, satellite, dial-up |
| **Scan time** | Fast — **1–100 ms** for discrete logic | Slower — **1–10 s** for telemetry |
| **I/O count** | 50 – 2,000 | 10 – 200 (lower density) |
| **Programming** | Ladder logic, FBD, ST (IEC 61131-3) | Often proprietary or IEC 61131-3 subset |
| **Cost** | Low to medium | Higher per I/O, but ruggedized |
| **Redundancy** | Optional | Optional, often dual-radio |
| **Typical use** | Car assembly, packaging line, batch reactor | Wellhead, substation, water pump station, pipeline valve |

**Where each is preferred in SCADA** *(3 marks)*

- **PLC is preferred** in SCADA **sub-systems** where **factory-floor machinery** must be controlled — for example, a **bottling line HMI** showing PLC tag values, or a **boiler control panel** in a power plant. The PLC is the "muscle" of the SCADA system.
- **RTU is preferred** in SCADA **remote assets** with no mains power, no air-conditioning, and no permanent operator — e.g., a **gas wellhead 30 km away from the control room** transmitting via cellular. The RTU wakes up periodically, scans its few I/O, transmits a small frame, and sleeps.
- In a **substation SCADA**, the substation may contain **both** — a PLC for switchgear interlocking and an RTU for talking to the central control centre.

**Conclusion:** *(0.5 m)* — PLCs are *computational powerhouses on the shop floor*; RTUs are *lean, rugged messengers in the field*. SCADA uses both, picking the right tool for the geography.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Major SCADA Communication Protocols:**

**1. Modbus (Modicon, 1979) — 1.5 m**
- Originally a **serial protocol (Modbus RTU)** on RS-232/485; later **Modbus TCP** over Ethernet.
- Open standard, royalty-free; supports up to **247 slaves** on one bus.
- Frame = **function code + register address + data + CRC**.
- Limitation: **No security** (plain text), **no standard object dictionary**.
- Use: Factory SCADA, building automation, small water networks.

**2. DNP3 (Distributed Network Protocol, IEEE 1815) — 2 m**
- Designed for **SCADA in power utilities** (substation automation).
- Supports **report-by-exception** (bandwidth-efficient) and **time-stamped events**.
- Layered: DNP3 frame → transport → application.
- Modern add-on: **DNP3-SA (Secure Authentication)** per IEEE 1815-2012.
- Use: **National grid, transmission & distribution substations, hydro plants.**

**3. IEC 60870-5 (especially IEC 60870-5-104) — 1.5 m**
- International standard for **SCADA in power, water, and gas**.
- IEC 60870-5-104 = **TCP/IP variant** widely used in Europe and India.
- Companion: **IEC 61850** for substation configuration and GOOSE messaging.
- Use: KSEB substations, ISRO, NTPC, municipal water.

**4. OPC UA (IEC 62541, Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture) — 2 m**
- Modern **object-oriented, secure, cross-platform** protocol.
- Built-in **encryption (TLS 1.2/1.3), authentication, and authorisation**.
- Carries both **data values and semantic information** (engineering units, quality, alarms).
- Replaces older **OPC DA / OPC HDA / OPC AE** that depended on Windows COM/DCOM.
- Use: **Industry 4.0, IIoT, cross-vendor integration between SCADA and MES/ERP**.

**Summary Table (must include in answer) — 0.5 m:**

| Protocol | Domain | Security | Transport |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modbus | Generic factory | None (plain) | Serial / TCP |
| DNP3 | Power utility | Optional SA | Serial / TCP |
| IEC 60870-5 | Power & water | None (optional) | Serial / TCP |
| OPC UA | Industry 4.0, IIoT | **Built-in TLS** | TCP / Websockets |

**Conclusion line:** Modern SCADA is migrating from **plain-text serial protocols to encrypted, semantic OPC UA** to meet Industry 4.0 and IEC 62443 cyber-security requirements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Before Writing!**
> 
> 1. **Never confuse "SCADA" with "DCS".** A 2-mark deduction is common for students who say "SCADA controls the PID loop directly." The PID loop lives in the **PLC/RTU/DCS controller**; SCADA only **monitors and supervises** — it can change *setpoints*, not close the loop at 100 ms.
> 2. **Always draw the block diagram when asked.** A textual description without a diagram loses 1–2 marks. Even a clean ASCII pyramid earns full credit.
> 3. **In the comparison table, give at least 4 rows.** A 2-row table gets only 1–2 marks; 5+ rows get full marks.
> 4. **Cite protocol standards with year/issuing body.** Saying just "Modbus" = 0.5 marks; saying "Modbus RTU/TCP — Modicon, 1979, open royalty-free standard" = full credit.
> 5. **Do not write "SCADA = computer"**. SCADA is a *system* — a combination of hardware, software, network, and procedures. The KTU marking scheme allocates marks for showing this system view.
> 6. **In numerical questions, show the unit conversion explicitly.** (e.g., $\tfrac{120 \text{ bits}}{19200 \text{ bps}}$ = 0.00625 s). Skipping a unit costs a half-mark.
> 7. **Avoid "fake" abbreviations** like "HMI = Human Machine Interaction" — it is **"Human Machine Interface"** (not "interaction"). Examiners flag this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **rapid-revision checklist** for the KTU 2024 PCICT502 Module 4 SCADA topic. Memorize before the exam.

- ✅ **Full form of SCADA** = **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition**.
- ✅ **SCADA is a Level 2 / Level 3 system** in the **Purdue / ISA-95 model** (Level 0 = field, Level 1 = control, Level 2 = supervisory, Level 3 = operations, Level 4 = enterprise).
- ✅ **Four core components**: **Field instruments, RTUs/PLCs, MTU/HMI, Communication network**.
- ✅ **Three levels of hierarchy** in SCADA: **Field, Control, Supervisory** (with optional MES/ERP above).
- ✅ **MTU** = the central SCADA server (Master Terminal Unit). **HMI** = operator's graphical screen.
- ✅ **RTU vs PLC**: RTU = **remote, rugged, low-power, telemetry**; PLC = **factory-floor, fast, computation-heavy, IEC 61131-3**.
- ✅ **Four generations of SCADA**: **Monolithic → LAN-based → Open/Networked → IIoT/Cloud**.
- ✅ **Standard current loop**: **4–20 mA** (live-zero, fault-detectable). **HART** overlays digital data on this 4–20 mA at 1200 bps.
- ✅ **Modbus** supports up to **247 slaves** per master over RS-485.
- ✅ **DNP3** is dominant in **North American power utility SCADA**.
- ✅ **IEC 60870-5-104** is dominant in **European & Indian power and water SCADA**.
- ✅ **OPC UA (IEC 62541)** is the modern **secure, semantic, cross-platform** standard for SCADA–MES–ERP integration.
- ✅ **Scan time** of SCADA is **1 to 10 seconds**; DCS is **100 ms to 1 s**; PLC logic is **1 to 100 ms**.
- ✅ **SCADA is for wide-area supervisory**; **DCS is for tight local control**; they **complement**, not replace, each other.
- ✅ **Cyber-security** is now built-in via **IEC 62443** (zones and conduits), **DNP3-SA**, and **OPC UA security**.
- ✅ **4th-generation SCADA** features: **IoT, cloud historian, web HMI, OPC UA, MQTT, AI-based alarming, digital twin, mobile dashboards**.
- ✅ **Availability target** of a SCADA MTU = $\geq 0.999$ (i.e., **"three nines"** or better).
- ✅ **Standard 4–20 mA fault detection**: 4 mA = 0 %, 20 mA = 100 %, < 3.6 mA or > 21 mA = **open-loop / short-circuit alarm**.
- ✅ **Time-stamping** is a key SCADA responsibility — every tag value is stamped with **millisecond UTC time** for post-event forensic analysis.
- ✅ **SCADA is not a controller** — it is a *supervisor*. Saying otherwise will cost marks in the KTU exam.

---

**END OF NOTES — KTU 2024 SCHEME | PCICT502 | MODULE 4 | SCADA**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Supervisory Computer Tasks and DCS Configuration: Supervisory Computer Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT502 - PROCESS CONTROL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Supervisory Computer Tasks and DCS Configuration: Supervisory Computer Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supervisory Computer (SC):** A supervisory computer is a higher-level digital computing system positioned above the regulatory (direct digital) control layer in the ISA-95 / Purdue reference hierarchy of an industrial process automation system. It performs **information, supervisory, optimization, and management functions** by communicating bidirectionally with Distributed Control System (DCS) controllers, Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), and Smart field instruments.

In a **Distributed Control System (DCS)**, the supervisory computer (often a SCADA host, a Process Server, or a Station Server) does *not* directly close the regulatory loop on the field. Instead, it **supervises** the dedicated single-loop and multi-loop controllers (Level 1) by:
- Receiving process variables (PV), setpoints (SP), controller modes, and alarm status.
- Transmitting revised setpoints, remote mode commands, and recipe parameters.
- Logging, trending, alarming, optimizing, and reporting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU definition to memorize:** "The supervisory computer is a digital system that performs data acquisition, monitoring, alarm handling, setpoint adjustment, optimization, and historical recording of a process by communicating with lower-level controllers in a hierarchical automation architecture."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower

Imagine a modern international airport:

| Airport Element | Industrial Automation Equivalent |
|----------------|----------------------------------|
| **Pilots & Aircraft** | Field instruments (sensors, transmitters, valves) |
| **Tower Controllers (ground radar room)** | DCS / PLC controllers (Level 1) — they handle the *actual* real-time control of each aircraft's taxi, takeoff, and landing commands |
| **Air Traffic Control Tower (supervisory view)** | **Supervisory Computer** — looks at the *whole* sky, all runways, all aircraft simultaneously, issues high-level rerouting, balance traffic, and raises alarms about conflicts |
| **Airport Operations Center** | MES / ERP (Level 3–4) |
| **Radio communication** | Fieldbus / Industrial Ethernet / OPC-UA links |

The pilots do the actual flying (controllers do the actual PID regulation). The tower **supervises** many controllers at once, makes strategic decisions (e.g., "Change runway 09 to 27 due to wind shift"), and warns everyone when something is wrong. That is *exactly* the role of the supervisory computer.

## 1.3 Position in the Standard DCS Hierarchy

The supervisory computer occupies **Level 2** in the **Purdue / ISA-95 reference model**, sitting *above* regulatory control and *below* manufacturing execution:

$$
\boxed{\text{Level 4 (ERP)} \; \rightarrow \; \text{Level 3 (MES)} \; \rightarrow \; \underbrace{\text{Level 2 (Supervisory)}}_{\text{Topic of this module}} \; \rightarrow \; \text{Level 1 (Regulatory)} \; \rightarrow \; \text{Level 0 (Field)}}
$$

## 1.4 Why a Separate Supervisory Computer Exists

A regulatory controller is **fast, deterministic, and dedicated** (typically a single PID loop running every 100–500 ms). It cannot:
- Render colourful trend graphics.
- Hold terabytes of historical data.
- Run complex real-time optimization.
- Coordinate hundreds of loops simultaneously.
- Talk to corporate enterprise software.

The supervisory computer is a **general-purpose, high-throughput computing platform** that complements the controllers and provides the *visibility*, *coordination*, and *intelligence* the controllers cannot.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Note:** Examiners often ask: *"Why can't the regulatory controller itself do the supervisory job?"* The answer is **determinism vs. flexibility** — controllers must respond in real time to process disturbances; overloading them with non-critical tasks would jeopardize control stability. A supervisory computer separates the *fast regulatory* function from the *slower coordination/information* function.

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Values)

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value |
|--------|-------------------------|
| Scan / polling period of supervisory computer | **1 s to 10 s** |
| Data update rate to HMI | **1 s to 2 s** |
| Historical data storage (modern systems) | **≥ 6 months online, ≥ 5 years archived** |
| Communication medium | **Industrial Ethernet (100 Mbps–1 Gbps), Fieldbus, OPC-UA** |
| Reliability (MTBF) | **≥ 50,000 hours** for a redundant station |
| Availability target | **99.95 %** for critical plants |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Supervisory Computer Functions

The functions of a supervisory computer are conventionally grouped into **three functional categories**. KTU examiners frequently test this classification.

### 2.1.1 Information / Data-Handling Functions
These are the *passive* functions where the computer only **observes, stores, and presents** data without influencing the loop.

1. **Data Acquisition (DA):** Cyclic polling of process variables, setpoints, controller outputs, valve positions, and status flags from Level 1 controllers through fieldbus / Ethernet.
2. **Data Processing / Validation:** Engineering unit conversion, linearisation (e.g., square-root for differential-pressure flow), cold-junction compensation, digital filtering, and gross-error detection.
3. **Display / Human-Machine Interface (HMI):** Mimics, faceplates, trend charts, alarm summaries, and overview pages rendered on operator workstations.
4. **Logging:** Periodic and event-triggered printing of operating parameters, shift logs, and batch records.
5. **Alarm Management:** Detection, prioritisation (e.g., low, high, high-high, low-low, critical), annunciation, acknowledgement, and historical archival of alarm events.
6. **Historical Data Storage / Trending:** Time-stamped archival of process variables in a Process Historian database, retrievable for later analysis.

### 2.1.2 Supervisory Control Functions
These are the *active* functions where the computer **sends commands back** to the controllers.

1. **Setpoint Adjustment (Remote Setpoint — RSP):** The computer calculates a new setpoint for a regulatory controller and downloads it, leaving the controller to do the actual PID computation. This is the **most common** supervisory-control mode.
2. **Mode Changing:** Remote switching of a controller between *Auto, Manual, Cascade, Remote* modes.
3. **Sequence Control / Batch Management:** Coordination of multi-step procedures (e.g., start-up, shut-down, recipe execution) by issuing discrete commands to several controllers in a defined order.
4. **Loop Tuning Assistance:** Software-based automatic tuning of PID parameters using relay-feedback, step-test, or model-based methods.
5. **Override & Adaptive Control:** Temporary setpoint overriding in safety-critical situations; adaptation of tuning constants based on operating regime.

### 2.1.3 Optimisation & Management Functions
These are the *strategic* functions that improve the long-term economic and operational performance of the plant.

1. **Steady-State Optimisation (Real-Time Optimisation — RTO):** Repeated solution of a constrained nonlinear program that finds the setpoints maximising profit / minimising cost, subject to equipment and safety constraints.
2. **Production Scheduling & Coordination:** Allocation of shared resources (e.g., common header, steam header) among multiple competing units.
3. **Energy Management & Load Shedding:** Monitoring energy use and automatically shedding non-critical loads during peak tariff or shortage.
4. **Report Generation:** Daily, shift, batch, regulatory, and management reports (production totals, downtime, alarm counts, KPI dashboards).
5. **Communication Gateway:** Acting as a bridge between Level 1 (DCS field network) and Level 3–4 (corporate Ethernet / ERP).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asked:** *"Differentiate between DDC and Supervisory Control."* See Section 5 question bank for the complete model answer.

## 2.2 Setpoint Control Mode — The Most Common Supervisory Action

In the **Setpoint Control Mode**, the supervisory computer (Level 2) does not interfere with the inner PID loop. The Level-1 controller continues to execute its PID algorithm at high speed (say, every 250 ms), but it accepts its setpoint from the supervisory computer via digital communication rather than from a local operator faceplate.

$$
\boxed{e(t) = SP_{\text{from SC}}(t) - PV(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}}
$$

Here, $SP_{\text{from SC}}$ is computed by the supervisory computer based on the optimisation objective and is **downloaded at a much slower rate** (typically 1–60 s) than the controller scan period.

## 2.3 Real-Time Optimisation (RTO) — The Economic Layer

The supervisory computer periodically (e.g., every 1–6 hours, or whenever a disturbance is detected) solves an optimisation problem:

$$
\max_{SP_1,\,SP_2,\,\dots,\,SP_n} \; J = \sum_{i=1}^{m} c_i \, P_i(SP_1,\dots,SP_n) - \sum_{j=1}^{n} u_j \, SP_j
$$

subject to the process model and the operating constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_k(SP_1,\dots,SP_n) &\le 0 \quad \text{(inequality constraints, e.g., equipment limits)} \\
h_k(SP_1,\dots,SP_n) &= 0 \quad \text{(equality constraints, e.g., mass balance)} \\
SP_{j,\min} &\le SP_j \le SP_{j,\max}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $P_i$ is the production rate of product $i$, $c_i$ is its price, and $u_j$ is the unit cost of input $j$. The solution — a vector of *optimal setpoints* — is then sent to the regulatory controllers.

## 2.4 Communication Cycle — Data Refresh Time

The supervisory computer polls $N$ controllers in cyclic fashion. If each transaction takes $T_t$ seconds, the **total scan time** of one complete sweep is:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot T_t
$$

For a guaranteed freshness, the supervisory computer must update every HMI display before the operator's perception threshold (about **1 second**). Therefore the design rule is:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot T_t \;\le\; T_{\text{update,max}}
$$

For example, polling 200 controllers with 5 ms each yields $T_{\text{scan}} = 1.0$ s — exactly at the acceptable limit.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Controller scan period | $T_s$ | s | Typically 0.1 – 0.5 s for regulatory |
| 2 | Supervisory refresh period | $T_r$ | s | Typically 1 – 10 s |
| 3 | Total scan time | $T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot T_t$ | s | $N$ = number of polled nodes |
| 4 | PID output | $u = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | % | Computed at Level 1 |
| 5 | Setpoint from SC | $SP = f(\text{optimiser output})$ | eng. units | Downloaded at $T_r$ |
| 6 | Optimisation objective | $J = \sum c_i P_i - \sum u_j SP_j$ | ₹/h, $/h | Maximised by RTO |
| 7 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | fraction | Target $\ge 0.9995$ |
| 8 | Mean Down Time | $MDT = \dfrac{\text{Total downtime}}{\text{Number of failures}}$ | h | Used in availability calc. |
| 9 | Alarm rate (acceptable) | $\le 1$ alarm / 10 min / operator | — | EEMUA 191 guideline |
| 10 | Effective data rate required | $R = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$ | bps | $b$ = bits per sample |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam tip:** Question 5 above is the *defining equation* of a Real-Time Optimisation supervisory function. Memorise the form $J = \sum c_i P_i - \sum u_j SP_j$. Many students forget the *minus* sign and lose marks.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical Use of Supervisory Computer |
|----------|-------------------------------------|
| **Petrochemical refinery** | RTO on crude distillation unit; product blending optimisation |
| **Power plant** | Boiler-turbine coordinated control; load dispatch; emissions monitoring |
| **Pharmaceutical batch** | Recipe management, batch reporting, regulatory compliance (FDA 21 CFR Part 11) |
| **Steel mill** | Furnace temperature setpoint optimisation; rolling-mill sequencing |
| **Water treatment** | Distributed monitoring of pH, chlorine, flow across a city; SCADA-based alarming |
| **Building automation** | HVAC supervisory scheduling across multiple zones |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section provides *exhaustive, line-by-line derivations* of the key analytical relationships used by supervisory computers. **No step is skipped.**

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Scan Time and Refresh-Rate Constraint

We want to derive the maximum allowable number of controllers that a single supervisory computer can poll within a fixed refresh interval.

**Given:**

- Number of Level-1 controllers to be polled: $N$
- Per-transaction communication latency: $T_t$ (seconds)
- Maximum allowed HMI refresh time: $T_{\text{update,max}}$

**Step 1 — Single transaction time:**
The time for one round-trip request/response handshake between the supervisory computer and a single controller is $T_t$. This includes request, propagation, controller processing, and response transmission.

$$
T_{\text{single}} = T_t
$$

**Step 2 — Polling N controllers sequentially:**
The supervisory computer uses a cyclic poll, completing one transaction before starting the next. Therefore the total scan time is the sum of the individual transaction times:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scan}} &= T_{\text{single},1} + T_{\text{single},2} + \dots + T_{\text{single},N} \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{N} T_t \\
&= N \cdot T_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Operator-freshness constraint:**
For the operator to perceive a continuous, flicker-free update, the entire sweep must complete in less than the maximum refresh interval:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} \le T_{\text{update,max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting and solving for N:**

$$
N \cdot T_t \le T_{\text{update,max}}
$$

$$
N \le \dfrac{T_{\text{update,max}}}{T_t}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_{\text{update,max}}}{T_t} \right\rfloor}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration (KTU-style):**
Suppose $T_{\text{update,max}} = 2.0$ s (refresh every 2 s) and $T_t = 8$ ms per controller.

$$
N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{2.0}{0.008} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 250 \rfloor = 250 \text{ controllers}
$$

If the plant has 400 controllers, **one** supervisory computer is insufficient. Either the refresh time must rise to $T_{\text{update,max}} = 0.008 \times 400 = 3.2$ s, or a **second** redundant supervisory server must be added and the poll load split.

## 3.2 Derivation: Setpoint Update Rate vs. Process Time Constant

The supervisory computer must not download setpoints faster than the process can *meaningfully* respond. Otherwise, the controller will chase an over-aggressive target and oscillate.

**Given:** First-order process with time constant $\tau$, gain $K_p$.

**Step 1 — Process settling time:** A first-order process reaches ~98 % of its final value in approximately $4\tau$. The *useful* setpoint update rate is therefore:

$$
T_{\text{update,process}} \ge 4\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Communication constraint:** From the previous derivation:

$$
T_{\text{update,comm}} = N \cdot T_t
$$

**Step 3 — Design rule (the supervisory computer must satisfy BOTH):**

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{update,comm}} = N \cdot T_t \;\ge\; 4\tau \quad \text{(and)}\quad T_{\text{update,comm}} \;\le\; T_{\text{update,max,operator}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:**
A thermal process has $\tau = 300$ s. The minimum update period is $4 \times 300 = 1200$ s = **20 minutes**. A faster download wastes bandwidth and offers no benefit, because the process cannot follow.

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimiser Marginal-Cost Condition (KKT)

For the linear optimisation problem

$$
\max_{SP} \; J = \sum_{i=1}^{m} c_i P_i - \sum_{j=1}^{n} u_j SP_j \quad \text{s.t.} \quad SP_{\min} \le SP \le SP_{\max}
$$

the optimal setpoint is found by the **Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT)** first-order condition. The unconstrained optimum satisfies:

$$
\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial SP_j} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial SP_j}\!\left[\sum_i c_i P_i - \sum_j u_j SP_j\right] = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{m} c_i \dfrac{\partial P_i}{\partial SP_j} = u_j}
$$

**Interpretation:** The supervisor sets each input $SP_j$ so that the *marginal revenue gain* across all affected products exactly equals the *marginal cost* of that input. This is the **economic balance condition** at the optimum.

If the unconstrained optimum violates $SP_{j,\min} \le SP_j \le SP_{j,\max}$, the optimum lies on the corresponding active bound, and the KKT multiplier enforces the limit.

## 3.4 Derivation: Availability of a Redundant Supervisory Server

Let:

- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures (h)
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair (h)

**Step 1 — Steady-state availability of a single server:**

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 2 — Unavailability:**

$$
U_1 = 1 - A_1 = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 3 — For two parallel servers (1-out-of-2 redundancy):**
The probability of *both* servers failing simultaneously is:

$$
A_2 = 1 - U_1^2 = 1 - (1 - A_1)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:**
Let $MTBF = 50{,}000$ h, $MTTR = 4$ h.

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 + 4} = 0.999920
$$

$$
A_2 = 1 - (1 - 0.999920)^2 = 1 - (0.000080)^2 = 1 - 6.4 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999994
$$

The redundant pair achieves **five nines (99.9999 %)** availability — necessary for critical plants.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Functions to Industrial Scenarios

| Supervisory Function | Required Software Module | Industry-Specific Application | Real-Time Criticality |
|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Data acquisition | I/O driver / OPC server | Telemetry of 5,000 RTUs in a pipeline | High |
| Alarm management | Alarm historian | Boiler drum-level high-high in a power plant | Very high |
| Setpoint download | Recipe manager | RTO of distillation column reflux ratio | Medium |
| Sequence control | SFC / ISA-88 engine | Pharmaceutical batch campaign | High |
| Historical trending | Process historian | Trend of reactor temperature for 90 days | Low |
| Optimisation | RTO solver (NLP) | Crude unit yield maximisation | Low (per hour) |
| Report generation | Reporting engine | Daily production report to plant manager | Low (per shift) |
| Energy management | Load scheduler | Peak-shaving in a steel rolling mill | Medium |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Purdue / ISA-95 Hierarchy Showing Supervisory Computer (Level 2)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LEVEL 4 ENTERPRISE ERP BUSINESS PLANNING"]
    L3["LEVEL 3 MANUFACTURING EXECUTION MES BATCH AND RECIPE"]
    L2["LEVEL 2 SUPERVISORY COMPUTER SCADA HMI HISTORIAN RTO"]
    L1A["LEVEL 1A CONTINUOUS CONTROL DCS PID LOOPS"]
    L1B["LEVEL 1B DISCRETE AND SEQUENCE CONTROL PLC SFC"]
    L0["LEVEL 0 FIELD SENSORS TRANSMITTERS VALVES MOTORS"]
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1A
    L2 --> L1B
    L1A --> L0
    L1B --> L0
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Single Supervisory Computer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PROCESS DATABASE] --> B[DATA ACQUISITION]
    B --> C[DATA VALIDATION]
    C --> D[ALARM MANAGER]
    C --> E[HISTORICAL TRENDING]
    C --> F[REPORT GENERATOR]
    D --> G[OPERATOR HMI]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    A --> H[REAL TIME OPTIMISER]
    H --> I[SETPOINT DOWNLOAD]
    I --> J[LEVEL 1 CONTROLLERS]
    J --> B
    G --> K[OPERATOR COMMANDS]
    K --> I
    K --> L[MODE CHANGER]
    L --> J
```

## 4.3 Information Flow in Setpoint Control Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OPERATOR OR RTO] --> B[SUPERVISORY COMPUTER]
    B --> C[DOWNLOAD NEW SP]
    C --> D[LEVEL 1 PID CONTROLLER]
    D --> E[FINAL CONTROL ELEMENT VALVE]
    E --> F[PROCESS]
    F --> G[SENSOR TRANSMITTER]
    G --> D
    D --> H[UPLOAD PV SP MODE STATUS]
    H --> B
    B --> I[DISPLAY ON HMI]
    I --> A
```

## 4.4 Task Scheduling inside the Supervisory Computer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SCHED[RATE MONOTONIC SCHEDULER] --> T1[HIGH PRIORITY ALARM POLLING 100 MS]
    SCHED --> T2[MEDIUM PRIORITY DATA ACQUISITION 1 S]
    SCHED --> T3[LOW PRIORITY TREND STORAGE 5 S]
    SCHED --> T4[BACKGROUND RTO SOLVE 1 H]
    SCHED --> T5[BACKGROUND REPORT GEN 24 H]
    T1 --> OUT[OPERATOR HMI]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT
    T4 --> SETP[SUPERVISED SETPOINT DOWNLOAD]
    T5 --> OUT
    SETP --> CTRL[LEVEL 1 CONTROLLER]
```

## 4.5 Distinction between DDC and Supervisory Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DDC["DIRECT DIGITAL CONTROL"]
        D1[DDC COMPUTER] -->|PID OUTPUT 100 MS| D2[VALVE]
        D2 --> D3[PROCESS]
        D3 --> D4[SENSOR]
        D4 --> D1
    end
    subgraph SUP["SUPERVISORY CONTROL"]
        S1[SUPERVISORY COMPUTER] -->|SETPOINT 1 10 S| S2[PID CONTROLLER LEVEL 1]
        S2 -->|VALVE SIGNAL 250 MS| S3[VALVE]
        S3 --> S4[PROCESS]
        S4 --> S5[SENSOR]
        S5 --> S2
        S2 -->|PV SP STATUS| S1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:**
> - In **DDC**, the digital computer itself computes the PID algorithm every fast cycle. There is no separate controller.
> - In **Supervisory Control**, the PID algorithm is computed by a **dedicated Level-1 controller** at high speed. The supervisory computer only sends *setpoints* and receives *data* at a slower rate. **This is the architecture used in modern DCS.**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define a supervisory computer. List any **four** of its information functions.

**Model Answer:**

> A supervisory computer is a higher-level digital computing system in the DCS hierarchy (Level 2 of the Purdue model) that performs information, supervisory, and optimisation functions by communicating bidirectionally with Level-1 controllers.

The four information functions are:
1. **Data acquisition** — cyclic polling of PVs, SPs, and status.
2. **Data validation** — engineering-unit conversion, linearisation, filtering.
3. **Logging** — periodic and event-triggered records.
4. **Alarm management** — detection, prioritisation, annunciation, and archival of alarm events.

*(Any 4 of: DA, validation, display, logging, alarming, trending.)*

**Examiner's note (3 marks breakdown):** Definition = 1 mark; listing 4 functions = 2 marks (0.5 each).

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **DDC (Direct Digital Control)** and **Supervisory Control**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | DDC | Supervisory Control |
|--------|-----|---------------------|
| Where the PID is computed | By the digital computer itself | By a separate Level-1 controller |
| Loop speed | Computer scans every ~1 s | Controller scans every 100–500 ms; computer refresh every 1–10 s |
| Reliability of loop | Lost if computer fails | Maintained even if SC fails (controllers continue in Auto) |
| What the computer does | Replaces the controller | Supervises the controller via setpoints |
| Typical architecture | Old, centralised | Modern DCS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** Students write *"DDC is digital, SC is analogue."* This is WRONG. Both are digital. The real difference is **who computes the PID** and **what happens if the computer fails**.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Distributed Control System** and clearly indicate the **position and functions of the supervisory computer**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of DCS [1 mark]:**
A Distributed Control System is a digital automation architecture in which control functions are distributed among multiple dedicated microprocessor-based controllers, each handling a small subset of process loops, while a higher-level computer supervises the entire system.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam) [3 marks]:**

```
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|             LEVEL 4 - ENTERPRISE (ERP)                 |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
                       |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|       LEVEL 3 - MANUFACTURING EXECUTION (MES)          |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
                       |
+--------------------------------------------------------+
|  LEVEL 2 - SUPERVISORY COMPUTER (HMI, HISTORIAN, RTO)  |  <-- TOPIC
+--------------------------------------------------------+
        |                       |               |
+-------+-------+      +--------+-----+  +-----+-----+
| LEVEL 1A      |      | LEVEL 1B     |  | OTHER LOOPS|
| PID CONTROL 1 |      | PLC / SFC    |  |            |
+---------------+      +--------------+  +------------+
        |                       |               |
+-------+-----------------------+---------------+----+
|             LEVEL 0 - FIELD INSTRUMENTS            |
|  Sensors, Transmitters, Valves, Motors, Actuators  |
+---------------------------------------------------+
```

**Step 3 — Position of Supervisory Computer [1 mark]:**
The supervisory computer is at **Level 2** in the Purdue/ISA-95 hierarchy, sitting between the MES (Level 3) and the regulatory controllers (Level 1).

**Step 4 — Six key functions of the SC [2 marks]:**
1. Data acquisition from controllers via fieldbus / Ethernet.
2. Display and HMI (mimics, trends, alarm summary).
3. Setpoint download (remote setpoint control).
4. Sequence / batch coordination.
5. Historical archiving and reporting.
6. Real-time optimisation (RTO).

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A DCS supervisory computer must poll **320 controllers** in a refinery. Each transaction takes **6 ms**. The maximum acceptable HMI refresh time is **2.5 s**.
   (i) Calculate the total scan time.
   (ii) Verify whether the design is acceptable. If not, propose a remedy.
   (iii) If the refinery requires the supervisor to download setpoints to all controllers within a settling-time budget of $4\tau = 800$ s for a thermal process, is the current architecture appropriate?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total scan time [2 marks]:**

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot T_t = 320 \times 0.006 = 1.92 \; \text{s}
$$

**(ii) Design check [2 marks]:**

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = 1.92 \text{ s} \;\le\; T_{\text{update,max}} = 2.5 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark
$$

The design is **acceptable**; the supervisory computer can refresh the HMI within the operator-perception limit. A **safety margin** of $2.5 - 1.92 = 0.58$ s is available for alarm and optimisation tasks.

**Remedy if it had been unacceptable:** Add a **second supervisory server** and split the 320 controllers 160 + 160. Each server would scan in $160 \times 0.006 = 0.96$ s, comfortably below 2.5 s.

**(iii) Setpoint-update vs. process time-constant check [3 marks]:**

Process settling time budget:

$$
4\tau = 800 \text{ s} \approx 13.3 \text{ minutes}
$$

The current supervisory scan time of $T_{\text{scan}} = 1.92$ s is *much faster* than $4\tau$. This is **technically wasteful** but not harmful — the supervisory computer simply repeats the same setpoint because the process cannot yet respond. There is no stability issue; only a small bandwidth inefficiency. **The architecture is appropriate** for this slow thermal process, and the supervisor could even be slowed (e.g., polled once every 30 s) to free CPU for RTO.

---

### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with examples the **three categories** of supervisory computer functions. Give **two examples** from each category as it applies to a petroleum refinery.

**Model Solution:**

The supervisory computer functions are grouped into three categories:

**Category 1 — Information / Data-Handling Functions [2 marks + 1 mark for example]:**
These are *passive* functions where the SC only observes, stores, and displays.

*Examples in a refinery:*
- **Data acquisition** of feed temperature, reflux flow, and tray temperatures from the crude distillation column controllers.
- **Alarm management** for the *high-high* level in the reflux drum, with priority routing to the panel operator.
- **Historical trending** of diesel-95 yield vs. time, kept for one year in the process historian.

**Category 2 — Supervisory Control Functions [2 marks + 1 mark for example]:**
The SC actively issues commands back to Level-1 controllers.

*Examples in a refinery:*
- **Setpoint adjustment** of the furnace-outlet temperature setpoint from an RTO solver seeking maximum diesel yield.
- **Sequence control** of the start-up sequence: pump → pre-heat → circulation → feed introduction, with safety interlocks.
- **Loop tuning** using relay-feedback to re-tune a sluggish temperature controller.

**Category 3 — Optimisation & Management Functions [2 marks + 1 mark for example]:**
Long-horizon, strategic decisions based on economics and plant-wide coordination.

*Examples in a refinery:*
- **Real-time optimisation** of the crude unit — choosing preheat temperature, pump-around rate, and reflux ratio to maximise the daily gross margin, solved every hour as feedstock assays change.
- **Production scheduling** across two crude units to balance the naphtha header demand.
- **Energy management** by load-shedding non-critical steam consumers during the daily peak-tariff window.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** Students often *conflate* the categories — e.g., placing "setpoint adjustment" under "optimisation." Setpoint adjustment is **supervisory control**; optimisation is a *separate* category that *decides* which setpoint to send. The decision-making hierarchy is: Optimiser → decides target SP → Supervisory Control → downloads SP → Controllers → execute PID.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A supervisory computer in a fertiliser plant polls 180 controllers, each transaction taking 7 ms. The system uses a 1-out-of-2 redundant server pair. Each server has $MTBF = 40{,}000$ h and $MTTR = 3$ h.
   (i) Compute the scan time and the availability of a single server.
   (ii) Compute the availability of the redundant pair.
   (iii) Comment on whether the design meets the typical industrial target of 99.95 % availability.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Scan time and single-server availability [3 marks]:**

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = 180 \times 0.007 = 1.26 \text{ s}
$$

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{40{,}000 + 3} = 0.999925
$$

**(ii) Redundant pair availability [2 marks]:**

$$
A_2 = 1 - (1 - A_1)^2 = 1 - (0.000075)^2 = 1 - 5.625 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999994
$$

**(iii) Comparison with industrial target [2 marks]:**

The single-server availability $A_1 = 99.9925\% \; > \; 99.95\%$ target. **However**, in a critical fertiliser plant (e.g., ammonia, where an unscheduled shutdown can cost ₹10 lakh/hour in lost production and catalyst damage), the redundant pair with $A_2 \approx 99.9999994\%$ (≈ seven nines) provides a vastly superior safety margin and is **strongly recommended** for a continuous process. The redundant design **comfortably exceeds** the 99.95 % target.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall callout:** When computing $A_2 = 1 - (1 - A_1)^2$, many students mistakenly write $A_2 = 2A_1 - 1$ (the *linear* approximation valid only for very small $U_1$). The correct form is the **square of the unavailability**. Full marks are awarded only for the exact expression.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Supervisory Computer (SC)** is the **Level-2** device in the ISA-95 / Purdue model, sitting *above* Level-1 controllers and *below* MES.
- **It does NOT compute the PID**; the Level-1 controller does. The SC only *supervises* via setpoints, mode changes, and sequences.
- The **three categories** of SC functions are:
  1. **Information** — DA, validation, display, logging, alarm, trending.
  2. **Supervisory Control** — setpoint download, mode change, sequence, loop tuning.
  3. **Optimisation & Management** — RTO, scheduling, energy, reports, ERP gateway.
- **Key timing relationships:**
  - $T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot T_t \le T_{\text{update,max}}$
  - $T_{\text{update,comm}} \ge 4\tau$ (process settling rule)
- **Availability formulas** — single: $A_1 = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$; redundant: $A_2 = 1 - (1 - A_1)^2$.
- **Optimisation objective:** $J = \sum c_i P_i - \sum u_j SP_j$ (revenue minus cost), maximised subject to constraints.
- **DDC vs. SC:** DDC = computer computes PID directly (centralised). SC = computer supervises separate PID controllers (modern DCS). The SC's failure does NOT interrupt the regulatory loop.
- **Real-time criticality order** (highest to lowest): Alarm > Setpoint Download > Data Acquisition > Trending > RTO > Reports.
- **Communication protocols** in modern systems: OPC-UA, Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, Foundation Fieldbus HSE.
- **Number to memorise:** Typical supervisory scan period is **1–10 s** (not 100 ms — that is the regulatory controller's job).
- **Examination mantra:** "SC **supervises**, controllers **control**, field **senses and actuates**." Memorise this hierarchy statement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 advice:** For any 14-mark question, always (a) **state the category/definition**, (b) **draw or describe the block diagram**, (c) **list functions in tabular form**, and (d) **solve numerical sub-parts with explicit step-by-step arithmetic**. Examiners reward structure and punish vague prose.
